Weekly Research Papers — Innovation Rankings

Papers from Google Scholar alerts & email — Apr 04 – Apr 11, 2026

228 Papers Ranked
69 Thematic Groups

Innovation Ranking Criteria

Papers are ranked from most to least innovative based on five criteria: (1) novelty of problem formulation or paradigm shift, (2) creativity of methodology, (3) surprisingness of results or insights, (4) potential impact on the field, and (5) degree of departure from existing approaches. Each paper includes a justification explaining its placement.

≥90 Groundbreaking
≥75 Highly Innovative
≥60 Innovative
≥40 Moderately Innovative
≥20 Incremental
<20 Routine

Innovation Rankings

#1 — Groundbreaking (94)

General Multimodal Protein Design Enables DNA-Encoding of Chemistry (20 ▲ )

Jarrid Rector-Brooks, Théophile Lambert, Marta Skreta, Daniel Roth, Yueming Long, Zi-Qi Li, Xi Zhang, Miruna Cretu, Francesca-Zhoufan Li, Tanvi Ganapathy, Emily Jin, Avishek Joey Bose, Jason Yang, Kirill Neklyudov, Yoshua Bengio, Alexander Tong, Frances H. Arnold, Cheng-Hao Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Apr 2026
Why this rank: This paper represents a genuine paradigm shift in enzyme design by eliminating the requirement for pre-specified catalytic residues (theozymes), which has been a foundational constraint of computational enzyme design for decades. The joint sequence-structure co-design via coupled discrete and continuous diffusion is architecturally novel, and the inference-time innovations (self-correction, entropy-adaptive temperature, noisy guidance) that boost co-designability from 16% to 88% without retraining demonstrate deep understanding of the generative process. Most critically, experimental validation across four new-to-nature carbene-transfer reactions — with yields and TTNs surpassing extensively evolved enzymes — provides rare and compelling evidence that the computational framework delivers real-world utility, not just benchmark improvements.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: De novo enzyme design for new-to-nature chemical reactions remains largely unsolved. Existing computational pipelines face two fundamental limitations: (1) they require pre-specified, fixed geometric arrangements of active-site residues (theozymes), which is impossible when the catalytic mechanism is unknown; and (2) they operate sequentially — generating backbone first, then designing sequence to match (inverse folding) — preventing sequence-based objectives from influencing structure formation and vice versa. These constraints confine design to reactions with known mechanistic precedents and lock in human assumptions about which residues enable catalysis. There is no general approach to design evolvable enzymes for chemistries that have never existed in biology. Methodology: DISCO (DIffusion for Sequence-structure CO-design) is a multimodal generative model that jointly designs protein sequences and 3D structures as a coupled object, conditioned on arbitrary biomolecules without pre-defined residue motifs. The core insight is that masked discrete diffusion (for sequences) and continuous diffusion (for 3D coordinates) can be jointly trained using independent unimodal losses, while still learning the joint reverse process. The architecture comprises an input embedder, a frozen protein language model (DPLM 650M), an 8-block Pairformer stack, and a 24-block atom-level diffusion transformer. Non-protein molecules (small molecules, reactive intermediates, nucleic acids) are represented identically to proteins using discrete tokens and atomic coordinates, co-folding with the protein throughout generation. A cross-modal recycling mechanism conditions each denoising step on both clean predicted and current noisy sequence/structure, ensuring bidirectional information flow. Inference required three innovations to unlock performance: (1) sequence self-correction via random remasking (allowing tokens to be revised rather than locked in once unmasked); (2) entropy-adaptive sequence temperature that flattens overconfident token distributions early in inference; (3) noisy guidance that naturally arises from decoupled noise schedules. Together, these raise co-designability from 16% to 88% using the same checkpoint. For inference-time steering, DISCO introduces two Feynman-Kac Corrector variants: FKC-MM enables reward tilting over joint sequence-structure objectives (e.g., maximizing disulfide bonds or cation-π interactions), and FKC-SG steers generation toward binding specificity by penalizing similarity to structurally related decoys. Key Results: On unconditional monomer generation, ~90% of sequences refold within 2Å RMSD using ESMFold, with higher novelty and diversity than all baselines. On the new STUDIO-179 benchmark spanning 179 natural and non-natural ligands, DISCO produces the highest fraction of diverse, co-designable complexes for 178 of 179 ligands. For experimental enzyme design, DISCO was conditioned solely on DFT-derived geometries of heme-carbene reactive intermediates — no theozyme, no pre-specified residues. From ~10,000 generated designs, 90 were selected for wet-lab testing across four new-to-nature carbene-transfer reactions. Results: alkene cyclopropanation achieved 72% yield and 4,050 TTN with 99:1 diastereomeric ratio, surpassing both evolved P450 enzymes (364 TTN) and the prior theozyme-based design PNC2 (630 TTN). B–H insertion reached 98% yield and 5,170 TTN, far exceeding the evolved starting point (2,490 TTN). C(sp3)–H insertion — a reaction requiring 14 rounds of directed evolution previously — achieved 2,360 TTN, rivaling the extensively evolved P411-CHF (2,030 TTN). Designed active sites are novel: >80% show >3Å RMSD to any known motif in AlphaFoldDB, no pair among the 90 designs exceeds 50% sequence identity, and the 90 designs cluster into 75 distinct structural families. One round of error-prone PCR on the spirocyclopropanation hit dCT-H11 produced ~35 improved variants with divergent enantioselectivity, confirming that DISCO places designs in evolvable fitness landscape regions. Strengths: - First framework to experimentally validate de novo enzyme design without pre-specified catalytic residues or theozymes, achieving performance competitive with or exceeding extensively evolved biocatalysts across four distinct reaction classes. - Inference-time innovations (self-correction, entropy temperature, noisy guidance) are rigorously ablated and shown to be individually critical — co-designability scales from 16% to 88% through inference alone with a fixed model checkpoint. - The Feynman-Kac Corrector formalism provides principled multimodal inference-time steering that outperforms naive best-of-N filtering, including for highly similar molecular targets (constitutional isomers such as aldosterone vs. cortisone). - Experimental designs are structurally and sequentially novel, diverse, and demonstrably evolvable — addressing the full design-to-directed-evolution lifecycle rather than just computational metrics. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experimental validation is restricted to heme enzymes and carbene-transfer reactions; it is unknown whether DISCO generalizes to reactions requiring different cofactors, non-metallic active sites, or multi-step mechanisms with multiple intermediates. - Enantioselectivity was not a design criterion and results were modest (up to 35% ee), leaving stereocontrol as an open challenge; the spirocyclopropanation reaction yielded only weak activity in the initial screen, requiring directed evolution to improve. - The frozen protein language model (DPLM 650M) was not trained on multimeric complexes, constraining the model to non-protein-binding design tasks and precluding de novo protein–protein interface design in its current form. - Despite requiring ~10× fewer generated designs than recent structure-based pipelines, the screening funnel still requires ~10,000 in silico samples to yield 90 experimental candidates, and computational filtering decisions (ipTM, contact counts, surface properties) introduce tacit assumptions that may bias the search.
Recommended 2604.05181 PDF
#2 — Highly Innovative (85)

Self-Distilled RLVR (81 ▲ )

Chenxu Yang, Chuanyu Qin, Qingyi Si, Minghui Chen, Naibin Gu, Dingyu Yao, Zheng Lin, Weiping Wang, Jiaqi Wang, Nan Duan · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 6 Apr 2026
Why this rank: This paper scores 85 for its exceptionally rigorous theoretical contribution: the impossibility trilemma formally characterizes why on-policy self-distillation is fundamentally ill-posed, and the Bayesian interpretation of per-token credit weights provides a principled bridge between self-distillation and reinforcement learning. The direction/magnitude decoupling insight — using the teacher's evidence ratio only as a credit magnitude signal while the environment reward retains exclusive directional authority — is conceptually elegant and provably leakage-free. The coherent chain from diagnosis (Theorem 1) through impossibility (Theorem 3) to solution (Theorems 4-5) sets a high bar for theoretically grounded RL post-training work.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), e.g., GRPO, trains LLMs using sparse scalar outcome signals applied uniformly to all tokens in a trajectory — a coarse credit assignment that ignores each token's individual contribution. On-policy distillation (OPD) addresses this by having a larger teacher provide dense token-level supervision along the student's own trajectories, but requires a separate, vocabulary-compatible teacher model. On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) tries to eliminate this overhead by having the same model act as both teacher (conditioned on privileged ground-truth information r) and student (conditioned only on the query). However, OPSD empirically degrades after early gains: models learn to reference invisible reference solutions at inference time (privileged information leakage), and validation accuracy collapses. The paper asks why OPD works while OPSD fails, and how to salvage the useful signal in self-distillation without the failure modes. Methodology: The authors prove that OPSD's objective is structurally ill-posed. Because the student cannot condition on privileged information r while the teacher can, the OPSD KL objective decomposes as L_OPSD = L* + I(Y_t; R | X, Y_{<t}), where the mutual information term is θ-independent and irreducible (Theorem 1). This means the student can never close the gap through legitimate optimization. At the gradient level, per-sample gradients carry an r-specific deviation component with zero mean but nonzero variance proportional to this mutual information (Proposition 1). Early in training the beneficial component dominates (rapid improvement); as the student approaches the teacher's marginal distribution, the deviation dominates and drives the model to encode x→r correlations in its parameters — the mathematical origin of leakage. They further prove an Impossibility Trilemma (Theorem 3): under shared parameters no OPSD variant can simultaneously achieve objective stability, sustained improvement, and leakage-free training. The proposed method, RLSD (RLVR with Self-Distillation), repurposes the teacher's evidence ratio P_T/P_S from a gradient direction signal to a token-level credit magnitude signal. The key design: (1) compute privileged information gain Δ_t = sg(log P_T(y_t) − log P_S(y_t)) using a stop-gradient single extra forward pass; (2) construct per-token weights w_t = (P_T/P_S)^{sign(A)}, where sign(A) comes from the GRPO sequence-level advantage — ensuring the environment reward determines update direction while the teacher modulates magnitude; (3) clip weights to [1−ε_w, 1+ε_w] and interpolate with uniform advantages via a scheduled λ that decays to 0 over training, smoothly transitioning to vanilla GRPO. This is a drop-in replacement for GRPO's uniform advantage requiring only one additional teacher forward pass per response. The Bayesian interpretation (Theorem 4) establishes that w_t equals the sequential Bayesian belief update ratio P(r|x,y_{≤t})/P(r|x,y_{<t}), giving a principled per-token credit attribution. Theorem 5 formally proves RLSD is leakage-free: sign(Â_t) = sign(A) always (directional isolation), gradient support is identical to the student's on-policy support (support isolation), and teacher influence is bounded by clipping (magnitude boundedness). Key Results: On Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct trained on MMFineReason-123K and evaluated on five multimodal reasoning benchmarks, RLSD achieves average accuracy of 56.18% vs. 53.86% for GRPO (+2.32%), 52.49% for OPSD, 52.74% for SDPO, and 52.91% for GRPO+OPSD. The largest gains are on challenging mathematical benchmarks: MathVision +3.91% over GRPO and MathVista +1.9%. At 200 training steps RLSD outperforms GRPO trained for 400 steps, demonstrating significantly faster convergence. Training dynamics confirm RLSD maintains higher entropy than GRPO (avoiding rapid entropy collapse from uniform rewards) and avoids the late-stage performance collapse seen in OPSD. Clip ratios stabilize at 3–6%, confirming the trust-region mechanism is actively engaged. Qualitative token-level credit heatmaps show RLSD correctly concentrates positive credit on decisive reasoning tokens and blame on erroneous derivation steps. Strengths: - The theoretical analysis is unusually rigorous: the irreducible mutual information gap (Theorem 1), per-sample gradient decomposition (Proposition 1), impossibility trilemma (Theorem 3), Bayesian interpretation of weights (Theorem 4), and formal leakage-free guarantee (Theorem 5) form a coherent and complete characterization of both the failure mode and the fix. - RLSD requires only one additional teacher forward pass per response (vs. full rollout generation), negligible computational overhead, and needs only the final ground-truth answer as privileged information — far less demanding than OPSD which requires verified reasoning traces distilled from a 235B model. - The direction/magnitude decoupling insight is conceptually clean and elegantly instantiated: by exponentiating the evidence ratio to the power of sign(A), the weight is guaranteed strictly positive, so the environment reward retains exclusive authority over reinforcement vs. penalization direction. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are currently limited to one model family (Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct) and one training domain (multimodal reasoning); the paper explicitly acknowledges that broader validation (text-only reasoning, video understanding, other model families) is deferred to a future version, weakening empirical generalizability claims. - The mixing coefficient λ is decayed to 0 over 50 steps, meaning RLSD degrades to vanilla GRPO for the majority of training. The gain from the self-distillation signal is therefore front-loaded, and sensitivity to the decay schedule (initialized at λ=0.5) and the teacher sync frequency (every 10 steps) is not thoroughly ablated. - The Bayesian interpretation (Theorem 4) rests on Assumption 1 — that the shared model faithfully approximates both the prior and posterior predictive distributions — which may not hold for long or complex privileged contexts, and no empirical analysis quantifies the degradation of the evidence ratio quality as a function of context difficulty or model capacity.
Recommended 2604.03128 PDF
#3 — Highly Innovative (84)

Supply-Chain Poisoning Attacks Against LLM Coding Agent Skill Ecosystems

Yubin Qu, Yi Liu, Tongcheng Geng, Gelei Deng, Yuekang Li, Leo Yu Zhang, Ying Zhang, Lei Ma · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This paper scores 84 because it identifies a genuinely novel and practically important attack surface — the escalation from passive text pollution to active action-space compromise via trusted skill documentation in LLM coding agents. The DDIPE framework demonstrates that code examples in skill files bypass safety alignment where explicit instruction injection fails completely, a counterintuitive and significant finding. The controlled natural experiment cleanly isolating model alignment from architectural guardrails is methodologically sophisticated, and the cross-model ensemble defense insight (compressing attack surface from 13-20% to <2%) is immediately actionable. Real-world validation through 4 confirmed CVEs and deployed vendor fixes elevates this beyond academic novelty.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-based coding agents (Claude Code, OpenHands, Codex, Gemini CLI) extend capabilities through third-party "agent skills" — standardized packages distributed via open marketplaces with no mandatory security review. Unlike passive documentation, skill description files (SKILL.md) function as operational directives that agents parse and act upon with system-level privileges (file I/O, shell, network). Prior work on supply-chain attacks against LLMs either focused on tool-selection bias or text-generation pollution (analogous to RAG poisoning), leaving unanswered whether poisoned skills can hijack an agent's action space — causing it to execute malicious payloads despite safety alignment and architectural defenses. CVE-2025-59536 and the 631,813-skill SkillsMP marketplace without mandatory review ground this as a real, unaddressed threat. Methodology: The paper introduces PoisonedSkills, a framework centered on Document-Driven Implicit Payload Execution (DDIPE). The core insight is that coding agents treat code examples in skill documentation as trusted reference implementations and reproduce them during task execution. DDIPE embeds malicious logic in two ways: (1) Code Example Poisoning — hiding payloads within Markdown code blocks that the agent copies as "official examples"; (2) Configuration Template Poisoning — planting backdoors in YAML/JSON/Makefile templates applied verbatim during environment setup. Three camouflage techniques are applied: functional compliance disguise (masking data theft as telemetry), silent exception suppression (try/except:pass), and false authority suggestion (naming C2 endpoints to resemble internal services). A scalable LLM-driven seed–mutation–validation pipeline (using Claude Opus 4.6 as generator) expands 81 expert-crafted seeds into 1,070 adversarial skills covering 15 MITRE ATT&CK categories via two stages: seed expansion (payload substitution within skeleton) and camouflage mutation (six camouflage operators + two composition operators). Jaccard-similarity deduplication (threshold 0.85) and inverse-coverage weighting prevent homogenization. The resulting dataset is evaluated across 8 configurations (4 frameworks × 5 models), with a three-tier behavioral classifier (Refusal / Code Generation / Direct Execution) validated by human reviewers (Cohen's κ=0.88 vs. LLM judge). Key Results: DDIPE achieves direct execution rates (DER) of 2.3%–27.1% and bypass rates (BR) of 11.6%–33.5% across all tested configurations; explicit natural-language instruction injection achieves 0% under the best-defended setup (Claude Code + Sonnet 4.6). Under Claude Code, Sonnet 4.6 has DER 2.3% while MiniMax-M2.5 reaches 13.3% — a 5.7× gap under identical architectural defenses. Removing architectural guardrails (Claude Code → OpenHands) amplifies GLM-4.7's DER from 2.4% to 27.1% (11.3×), revealing 219 "sleeper payloads," but Sonnet 4.6 produces only 5 sleeper activations, isolating alignment strength as the moderating variable. Cross-model joint bypass is only 1.6%, suggesting heterogeneous model ensembles compress the attack surface from 13–20% to below 2%. Static scanning (SkillScan) catches 90.7% of adversarial samples, but 9.3% evade all four detection layers; 27 of these also penetrate model alignment. Configuration-style attacks account for 72% of Sonnet's executions. A 479-byte pip configuration payload — the most minimalist sample — is the only one executed by all three models under Claude Code. Responsible disclosure resulted in 4 confirmed vulnerabilities and 2 deployed fixes (Bugcrowd/Codex, Google VRP/Gemini CLI, HackerOne/Claude Code, OpenHands PR). Strengths: - Identifies a genuinely novel and practically important attack surface: the escalation from RAG-style text pollution to action-space compromise via trusted skill documentation, validated by real CVEs and vendor acknowledgments. - Rigorous two-layer defense decomposition with a controlled natural experiment (same model, different frameworks) cleanly isolating alignment strength from architectural guardrails, yielding actionable design insights. - High-quality evaluation methodology: 8-cell model×framework matrix, majority-vote re-execution (n≥3), Docker-isolated reproduction, human blind review with strong inter-rater agreement (κ=0.88), and Wilson score confidence intervals. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The retrieval phase is assumed to succeed — there is no end-to-end validation that adversarial skills actually surface to top-k in real marketplaces with hundreds of competing skills, which could significantly dilute real-world attack probability. - DDIPE's three camouflage techniques are never ablated individually, so it is unknown which components drive bypass success; this limits guidance on which defenses to prioritize. - Evaluation omits the Llama ecosystem and Cursor, and the stealth advantage is benchmarked only against naive explicit instruction injection — stronger baselines (e.g., Skill-Inject) might narrow the DDIPE advantage considerably.
Citations 2604.03081 PDF
#4 — Highly Innovative (84)

RAGEN-2: Reasoning Collapse in Agentic RL (44 ▲ )

Zihan Wang, Chi Gui, Xing Jin, Qineng Wang, Licheng Liu, Kangrui Wang, Shiqi Chen, Linjie Li, Zhengyuan Yang, Pingyue Zhang, Yiping Lu, Jiajun Wu, Li Fei-Fei, Lijuan Wang, Yejin Choi, Manling Li · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Apr 2026
Why this rank: This paper scores 84 for identifying a genuinely new and practically dangerous failure mode—template collapse—that is invisible to standard entropy-based diagnostics in agentic RL training. The information-theoretic decomposition of entropy into mutual information and conditional entropy is theoretically elegant and immediately actionable, providing a drop-in diagnostic metric with zero additional inference cost. The SNR-based mechanistic explanation is convincing and the proposed filtering fix is simple yet effective. The breadth of experimental validation (7 environments, 4 RL algorithms, 5 model scales, 2 modalities) with controlled ablations establishing causality is unusually rigorous for this type of contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Training multi-turn LLM agents with reinforcement learning is inherently unstable, and practitioners rely on entropy as a proxy for reasoning process stability. However, entropy only measures within-input diversity—it cannot detect whether reasoning actually responds to different inputs. This paper identifies a previously invisible failure mode called template collapse: models produce reasoning that appears diverse within any single input (high conditional entropy H(Z|X)) but is effectively input-agnostic across different inputs (low mutual information I(X;Z)). This failure silently degrades agent reliability without triggering standard diagnostic metrics, making it especially dangerous in multi-turn settings where sparse rewards cannot distinguish input-driven reasoning from templated reasoning. Methodology: The paper addresses two core questions: how to diagnose template collapse, and why it occurs. For diagnosis, the authors decompose reasoning diversity using the Shannon identity H(Z) = I(X;Z) + H(Z|X), showing that existing entropy metrics track only the conditional entropy term while missing mutual information. They propose a family of MI proxy metrics computed via in-batch cross-scoring: each reasoning trace Z is scored against all batch prompts by computing teacher-forced log-likelihoods L_{i,k,j} = log p_θ(Z_{i,k}|X_j). Two primary proxies are introduced—Retrieval-Acc (discrete: fraction of traces correctly matched to their source prompt) and MI-ZScore-EMA (continuous: EMA-stabilized difference between matched and marginal log-likelihoods). These reuse existing training rollouts with no additional inference passes. For the mechanistic explanation, the authors adopt a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) lens. Task gradients scale with within-prompt reward variance (proven via Cauchy-Schwarz: ||g_task(x)|| ≤ √RV(x) · C), while regularization gradients (KL divergence, entropy bonus) remain flat across all prompts regardless of reward signal. When many prompts have low reward variance, regularization dominates updates and erases cross-input reasoning differences. To address this, they propose SNR-Aware Filtering: at each training iteration, prompts are ranked by within-prompt reward variance and only the top fraction (via nucleus-style top-p selection) are retained for gradient updates. This adds negligible compute overhead (<0.1% of iteration time) since the rollout budget remains fixed. Key Results: Template collapse is verified as a consistent failure mode across all seven environments (Sokoban, FrozenLake, MetaMathQA, Countdown, SearchQA, WebShop, DeepCoder), four RL algorithms (PPO, GRPO, DAPO, Dr. GRPO), model scales from 0.5B to 7B (Qwen2.5 family and Llama3.2-3B), and text/image modalities. MI proxy metrics achieve positive Spearman correlation with final task performance (+0.39 for Trajectory MI-ZScore), while entropy metrics show negative correlation (−0.11 to −0.14), meaning entropy actively misleads training monitoring. Gradient decomposition experiments confirm the SNR mechanism: task gradient norm increases monotonically with reward variance buckets while regularization gradient norm is flat across all buckets. SNR-Aware Filtering consistently improves peak task success rates: +16.0% on Sokoban with PPO/Qwen2.5-3B, +10.9% on FrozenLake, +6.9% average. For visual modality (Qwen2.5-VL-3B), gains are dramatic: +53.5% on FrozenLake (image), +59.5% on FrozenLake (visual). Top-p filtering outperforms top-k and no-filter baselines; filtering also reduces per-step compute time by 26–41% due to fewer gradient computations. A quartile ablation provides causal evidence: training exclusively on Q1 (highest RV) vs. Q4 (lowest RV) prompts yields 21.1% vs. 11.0% task performance and 0.95 vs. 0.73 MI proxy on Sokoban. A cheap diagnostic (Std(RV)/Mean(RV) > ~1.0) predicts whether filtering will help in a given setting. Strengths: - The core insight—decomposing entropy into MI and conditional entropy to expose template collapse—is theoretically clean and practically actionable, filling a genuine blind spot in existing RL training diagnostics - The MI proxy requires no additional models or rollouts, making it a drop-in addition to any existing RL pipeline with negligible overhead - Unusually thorough experimental validation across 7 environments, 4 algorithms, 5 model scales, 2 model families, and 2 modalities, with controlled ablations that establish causality (quartile ablation, noise injection, trajectory-level vs. prompt-level filtering) Weaknesses / Limitations: - The SNR decomposition assumes task-signal and regularization noise separate cleanly, but they may couple through gradient accumulation in practice, and the theory relies on a reward decomposition assumption (Assumption H.1) that may not hold in dense or shaped reward settings - The method degrades in high-stochasticity environments (filter advantage disappears at 80–100% environmental noise in FrozenLake) and requires reward variance to be a meaningful signal proxy, limiting applicability to sparse or highly noisy reward regimes - All experiments are single-agent with relatively small models (≤7B); how template collapse propagates in multi-agent settings or scales to frontier-size models remains unstudied, and the keep rate ρ requires per-task tuning with no principled selection criterion beyond the Std/Mean(RV) diagnostic
Recommended 2604.06268 PDF
#5 — Highly Innovative (84)

GrandCode: Achieving Grandmaster Level in Competitive Programming via Agentic Reinforcement Learning (6 ▲ )

DeepReinforce Team, Xiaoya Li, Xiaofei Sun, Guoyin Wang, Songqiao Su, Chris Shum, Jiwei Li · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 6 Apr 2026
Why this rank: GrandCode scores 84 for achieving a genuinely historic milestone: the first system to consistently take 1st place in live Codeforces contests against top human grandmasters, not just on historical benchmarks. The Agentic GRPO algorithm addresses a real underexplored problem in multi-stage RL—credit assignment with delayed, expensive evaluations—with a principled per-phase normalization scheme rather than a mere heuristic patch. The test-time RL loop that updates LoRA parameters during a contest is a creative departure from static inference-time scaling. However, the problem space (AI for competitive programming) is well-trodden with clear precedents (AlphaCode, AlphaCode2, o3), and the system's reliance on pseudonymous accounts and proprietary-only benchmarks weakens the empirical contribution's verifiability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Despite rapid AI progress in coding, no system had consistently outperformed the best human competitors in competitive programming under live contest conditions. Prior milestones—AlphaCode (~1300 Codeforces rating, top 54%), AlphaCode2 (85th percentile), o3 (175th globally), and Gemini 3 Deep Think (8th place on historical problems)—all fell short of top grandmasters in real-time settings. The gap matters because live contests impose strict time and memory constraints, require both correctness and efficiency, and offer only binary feedback from an online judge. The core challenges are multi-step reasoning under delayed rewards, self-verification without access to the official judge's test suite, and the severe off-policy drift that arises when rollouts take minutes to evaluate. Methodology: GrandCode is a multi-agent system built on Qwen 3.5-397B (MoE) as the main solver, with two auxiliary dense models (Qwen-3.5-27B each) for hypothesis generation (π_hypothesis) and context summarization (π_summary), plus an adversarial test-case generation module. The pipeline has two phases. Post-training involves: (a) continued pretraining on noisy competitive-programming data expanded from TACO, LeetCode, USACO, CodeContests, and IOI using Gemini 3.1 Pro as a data generator; (b) supervised fine-tuning on high-quality (question, thinking trace, solution) triples, using a dual-term scoring function to select the optimal reasoning context for hard problems; and (c) multi-component RL that jointly trains all three policies. At test/contest time, a difficulty classifier routes easy problems to direct generation and hard problems to an online test-time RL loop that updates LoRA parameters, guided by a running summary of explored strategies. The key algorithmic contribution is Agentic GRPO, a modification of GRPO for multi-stage rollouts. Standard GRPO waits for the final reward before updating all tokens; Agentic GRPO issues an immediate gradient update using the intermediate reward r_t as soon as it is available, then applies a delayed correction term (r_N − r_t) once the full trajectory completes. Each stage normalizes with its own group statistics, giving equal gradient voice to early and late stages regardless of reward scale differences—a deliberate departure from the standard single-normalizer design. This is combined with asynchronous pipeline-RL and a staleness weighting scheme that decays and eventually drops tokens generated under stale behavior policies. Infrastructure innovations include pipelined context parallelism for Qwen's hybrid DeltaNet+softmax architecture (achieving ~90% GPU utilization at 4 CP ranks / 4 micro-batches), dynamic CP sizing per difficulty, and frozen MoE router weights during RL to prevent expert routing drift. Hypothesis generation uses a verify-and-revise loop: the system proposes structural conjectures, checks them on small instances with a brute-force solver (optionally querying Wolfram Alpha or OEIS), and injects only validated hypotheses into the main solver's prompt. The reward for π_hypothesis combines local verification accuracy with the marginal improvement it causes in the main solver's output score. Test-case generation employs two strategies: difference-driven generation (iteratively prompting Claude, GPT, DeepSeek V3, and Kimi 2.5 to produce inputs that expose behavioral divergence among candidate solutions) and solution attack (LLM-guided adversarial case synthesis given both a candidate and the gold solution). The resulting cases are used to filter submissions; a Qwen-3.5-27B model is fine-tuned to generate adversarial examples at test time. Key Results: GrandCode ranked first place in all three live Codeforces contests it entered—Round 1087 (Div. 2, Mar 21 2026), Round 1088 (Div. 1+2, Mar 28 2026), and Round 1089 (Div. 2, Mar 29 2026)—outperforming every human contestant including legendary grandmasters and being the first to complete all tasks in each round. S(separate) scores were 9269, 16511, and 11596 respectively. On a proprietary 100-problem benchmark, base Qwen 3.5-397B achieves 64% accept rate (weighted score 52.2); continued training raises this to 71% (61.0); +SFT to 73% (62.5); +full multi-component RL to 81% (72.3, 13/20 Level 5 problems solved); +test-time RL to 85% (73.5, 15/20 Level 5). For comparison, frontier models benchmark at 72–75% (Claude Opus 4.6: 73%, Gemini 3.1 Pro: 75%, GPT-5.4: 72%). The adversarial test generation pipeline improves pass rate on 50 Codeforces problems from 42 to 50 (with the last 2 requiring submission feedback). Hypothesis generation with SFT+RL achieves 52% pass@1 and 57% pass@5, up from 34%/44% for the base model. Pipelined CP for the DeltaNet+softmax hybrid achieves ~90% utilization at 4 micro-batches, approaching 100% asymptotically. Strengths: - First system to demonstrate consistent 1st-place finishes against all human participants under unmodified live contest conditions, including Div. 1+2 rounds with top grandmasters—a qualitatively harder bar than historical-problem evaluations. - Agentic GRPO addresses a real and underexplored problem: credit assignment in multi-stage rollouts where each evaluation takes minutes; the per-phase normalization argument (equal gradient voice regardless of reward scale) is principled, not just heuristic. - The end-to-end pipeline is remarkably complete, addressing data generation, SFT, multi-component RL, infrastructure (pipelined CP, asynchronous training, staleness weighting), adversarial test generation, and live-contest deployment strategy in a single system. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper acknowledges that GrandCode participated under pseudonymous accounts (averyjones1, yokeko, Vortex1), deliberately waiting to submit until near the end to avoid triggering Codeforces AI-detection policies—a significant methodological caveat that undermines the fairness claim and raises questions about whether the scores would hold under full scrutiny. - The evaluation benchmark (100 proprietary problems, 5 difficulty tiers) is entirely internal and not independently verifiable; all ablations are run on this same benchmark, creating the risk that design choices were implicitly tuned to it. No public benchmark results (e.g., LiveCodeBench, CodeElo) are reported. - Many critical hyperparameters (α, β in reward mixing; K1, K2 staleness thresholds; LoRA rank; number of RL steps; training compute budget) are left unspecified, making reproducibility essentially impossible despite the paper's technical depth.
Recommended 2604.02721 PDF
#6 — Highly Innovative (83)

Safeguarding Language Models via Self-Destruct Trapdoor

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: This paper scores 83 for its genuinely novel exploitation of floating-point overflow as an in-weights defense mechanism — a hardware-level property that the entire safety community had overlooked. The three-step null-space calibration procedure is technically elegant, and the emergent resistance to fine-tuning and gradient-based attacks (via NaN gradient corruption) is a surprising and non-obvious consequence. The extension to fingerprinting and bias mitigation demonstrates unexpected generality. However, the trivial detectability of injected weights (magnitudes of 10^17–10^18) fundamentally limits practical deployment against any adversary with model access, preventing a higher score.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Language models (LMs) remain vulnerable to jailbreak attacks that bypass alignment training, and existing defenses fall into unsatisfying trade-offs: alignment training is brittle and computationally expensive, external input/output filters add inference overhead and rely on auxiliary models, and interpretability-based monitors are limited in maturity and scope. A critical hardware-level property of modern LM frameworks—that limited-precision floating-point formats (BF16, FP16, FP32) trigger deterministic overflow errors when matrix products exceed representable range—has been entirely overlooked as a potential defense mechanism. This work exploits that gap. Methodology: Self-Destruct embeds a protective trapdoor directly into a single attention head's query and key weight matrices using a three-step, training-free calibration procedure. Step 1 (Null-space Isolation): compute the SVD of benign activations H and extract V₀, the right null space, which spans directions invisible to benign inputs. Step 2 (Targeted Subspace Extraction): project harmful activations H′ into this null space and extract the top-δ right singular vectors via a second SVD, identifying directions that maximally activate on H′ while remaining suppressed on H. Step 3 (Head Injection): map these directions back to model space, scale them by a destruction factor D_factor such that harmful activations produce query-key dot products approaching the BF16/FP32 maximum (≈3.39×10³⁸), and add the result to one attention head's Ŵ_q and Ŵ_k. For harmful inputs, the injected component dominates and causes ‖(q+q′)(k+k′)ᵀ‖ ≈ Ω → overflow; for benign inputs, the null-space construction ensures the contribution is suppressed (≈0). K-means clustering of harmful activations improves coverage across diverse harmful prompt distributions. The destruction factor is selected via binary search on a validation set, and all hyperparameters (layer, head index, SVD width δ, cluster count n) are chosen by grid search maximizing Δ = RefusalRate(harmful) − RefusalRate(harmless). Key Results: Evaluated across 10 models from 5 families (Falcon3, Llama-3.2, Mistral-0.3, Qwen2.5, Gemma2; 1B–14B parameters) on four jailbreak benchmarks (MalwareGen, PromptInject, PAIR, AutoDAN) and four QA capability benchmarks (PIQA, OpenBookQA, SIQA, ARC). Self-Destruct achieves the lowest average ASR in 9 of 10 models. Representative reductions: MalwareGen ASR Qwen2.5-7B 39→1%, Mistral-7B 67→4%, Gemma-2-2B 10→1%; AutoDAN ASR drops to 0 on Qwen2.5-3B/7B/14B; PAIR ASR Qwen2.5-14B 33→15%, Gemma-2-9B 22→1%. Calibration achieves Δ ≥ 0.97 on both train and test splits across all models, with 7B+ models typically reaching Δ = 0.99. On capability benchmarks, the strongest models show near-zero accuracy degradation (Qwen2.5-14B avg Δ ≈ 0, Gemma-2-2B avg +1.2), while mid-size models incur moderate drops (Mistral-7B avg −3.2, Qwen2.5-7B avg −5.5). Crucially, fine-tuning the protected model fails entirely—backpropagation yields NaN gradients from the first iteration regardless of optimizer, batch size, or dataset, making the model effectively untrainable. Gradient-based GCG attacks also diverge immediately against protected models. The mechanism extends to model fingerprinting (Δ = 1.0 on Gemma-2-2B with human-readable passcodes; Δ = 0.95 with random 12–20-character strings on Qwen2.5-3B) and bias mitigation on BBQ (Δ = 1.0 across all tested configurations). Compatibility is confirmed across NVIDIA GPU generations, Apple MPS, attention backends (EAGER, SDPA, FLEX), and vLLM. Strengths: - Genuinely novel exploitation of floating-point overflow as an in-weights defense: zero inference overhead since the mechanism fires simultaneously with generation, unlike perplexity filtering (requires full-text generation) or SmoothLLM/Self-Defense (require extra LM runs). - Strong adversarial robustness against both fine-tuning and gradient-based attacks (GCG) as an emergent consequence of high-magnitude weight injection corrupting gradient flow—a notable and non-obvious side effect. - Broad versatility: the same SVD-based calibration framework extends to model fingerprinting and bias mitigation with only a dataset swap, demonstrating generality of the underlying mechanism. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The trapdoor is easily detectable and reversible by any user with model access: injected weights have magnitudes of 10¹⁷–10¹⁸ versus typical two-digit values, making a simple threshold scan (e.g., >10⁵) sufficient to identify and zero out the affected head—a fundamental transparency limitation the authors acknowledge. - Evaluation is restricted to BF16/FP32 and standard attention implementations; behavior under quantization (INT8, FP8, INT4), FP64, or cross-precision inference pipelines is entirely uncharacterized, which is a significant gap given the prevalence of quantized deployment. - The accuracy-refusal tradeoff is model-family-dependent and unpredictable: Qwen2.5-7B loses 5.5 points average QA accuracy and shows elevated refusal on benign PIQA/SIQA prompts, while Falcon3-7B gains 17.8 points—suggesting the mechanism's selectivity is sensitive to architecture in ways that are not yet well understood or controllable.
Recommended
#7 — Highly Innovative (83)

VLMs Need Words: Vision Language Models Ignore Visual Detail In Favor of Semantic Anchors (2 ▲ )

Haz Sameen Shahgir, Xiaofu Chen, Yu Fu, Erfan Shayegani, Nael Abu-Ghazaleh, Yova Kementchedjhieva, Yue Dong · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 6 Apr 2026
Why this rank: This paper scores 83 for providing a compelling mechanistic explanation of a widely observed but poorly understood phenomenon — why VLMs fail at fine-grained visual tasks despite encoding sufficient visual information. The semantic anchoring hypothesis is rigorously tested through carefully controlled synthetic experiments that isolate nameability as the key variable, and the Logit Lens analysis provides rare direct mechanistic evidence linking internal token trajectories to behavioral outcomes. The two-pathway finding — that task-specific finetuning learns direct visual comparison rather than better semantic anchoring — is genuinely surprising and reframes VLM visual failures as learned shortcuts rather than architectural limitations, with clear implications for training strategies.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision Language Models (VLMs) achieve strong performance on many multimodal benchmarks yet systematically fail on tasks requiring fine-grained visual perception — chart understanding, optical illusions, abstract visual reasoning, and basic visual discrimination. Prior work showed that VLMs' internal representations often contain sufficient visual information to solve these tasks even when verbal output fails (a 'hidden-in-plain-sight' gap), but the underlying mechanism was not explained. This paper addresses that gap: why do VLMs fail to use the visual information they demonstrably encode? Methodology: The paper hypothesizes that VLMs short-circuit visual reasoning by mapping visual entities to discrete semantic labels in language space — 'semantic anchors' — whenever possible. When no crisp label exists, models attempt the same linguistic strategy but generate hallucinated approximate descriptions that actively degrade performance. The hypothesis is tested via visual correspondence tasks — identifying which of four candidate entities matches a reference — across three settings that vary nameability: (1) real-world semantic correspondence (SPairs71k), (2) synthetic 2D shape correspondence with known geometric shapes vs. procedurally generated 'squiggles,' and (3) face correspondence with known celebrities vs. AI-generated unknown faces. For each task, the authors compare Direct VQA, Chain-of-Thought (CoT), and Representation Probing (linear probe on intermediate LM hidden states). Mechanistic analysis uses Logit Lens to decode hidden states of visual tokens layer-by-layer, measuring Mean Jaccard Distance between entity token sets as a proxy for semantic discernibility. Two intervention experiments follow: (a) teaching VLMs arbitrary names for unknown shapes via finetuning to test whether labeling alone closes the gap, and (b) task-specific finetuning on shape correspondence without any name teaching to test whether direct visual comparison can be learned instead. Key Results: Across all three correspondence settings, VLMs perform substantially better on nameable entities. On 2D shapes, Qwen3VL-2B achieves 97.3% CoT accuracy on known shapes but only 29.0% Direct accuracy on unknown squiggles — despite Representation Probing reaching 74.2%, a 45.2 percentage point gap. CoT is actively harmful on unknown entities (Qwen3VL-8B drops 19.4 points with CoT on unknown shapes), consistent with hallucinated verbal reasoning misleading the model. Logit Lens analysis confirms the mechanism: for known entities, hidden states progressively resolve from semantically unrelated tokens through approximate descriptors to exact labels ('triangle' → 'pointed' → 'star'; face tokens resolve to 'Jungkook' then 'BTS'), while unknown entity tokens remain semantically indiscernible across all layers. Mean Jaccard Distance is consistently higher for known vs. unknown entities from layer 4 onward across both Gemma3 and Qwen3VL families. Teaching arbitrary names (ordinary object names, human names, random strings) substantially closes the gap on unknown shapes: Qwen3VL-2B improves from 29.0% to 86.0% with ordinary names, even surpassing pre-finetuning Representation Probing (74.2%). Logit Lens confirms the mechanism — the Jaccard Distance ordering (Ordinary > Human > Random) exactly matches the accuracy ordering. However, task-specific finetuning on shape correspondence achieves even higher accuracy (98.7%) with lower semantic discernibility than name teaching, strong OOD generalization to mazes (a geometrically different shape family), and transfer gains on face (+16%) and semantic (+10.7%) correspondence. This shows that direct visual comparison — not semantic anchoring — is the mechanism learned by task-specific finetuning. Strengths: - The experimental design is carefully controlled, isolating the semantic anchoring effect by constructing synthetic tasks (squiggles, AI-generated faces) that eliminate confounders like occlusion, resolution variation, and visual artifacts present in real datasets. - Logit Lens provides compelling mechanistic evidence directly mapping model internals to the hypothesized behavior, with quantitative Jaccard Distance metrics supporting qualitative token trajectory observations. - The two-pathway finding — that semantic anchoring and direct visual comparison are distinct, learnable mechanisms — is a non-obvious and practically important result: it reframes current VLM visual failures as learned shortcuts rather than architectural limitations. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are limited to a small set of open-weight VLMs (Qwen3VL and Gemma3 families, InternVL3.5); results on much larger or proprietary models (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet) are absent, leaving generality across frontier models untested. - The visual correspondence tasks, while well-controlled, are synthetic and narrow. The paper does not demonstrate that the identified mechanism fully explains failures on naturalistic fine-grained perception tasks (e.g., chart reading, OCR, medical imaging) beyond the SPairs71k semantic correspondence result. - Logit Lens is known to underestimate visual token interpretability in early layers, and the paper's Jaccard Distance metric uses only top-1 decoded tokens, which may miss semantic information encoded in the probability distribution — the authors acknowledge this but do not quantify the impact on their conclusions.

#8 — Highly Innovative (82)

dTRPO: Trajectory Reduction in Policy Optimization of Diffusion Large Language Models

Wenxuan Zhang, Lemeng Wu, Changsheng Zhao, Ernie Chang, Mingchen Zhuge, Zechun Liu, Andy Su, Hanxian Huang, Jun Chen, Chong Zhou, Raghuraman Krishnamoorthi, Vikas Chandra, Mohamed Elhoseiny, Wei Wen · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 82 for opening principled offline preference optimization to an entirely new model class (diffusion LLMs) where alignment was previously intractable. The ratio reduction theorem — showing that schedule-dependent coefficients cancel exactly in policy ratios, leaving only categorical token probabilities — is a non-obvious mathematical insight that simultaneously solves numerical instability and reduces training cost to match autoregressive DPO (4 forward passes). The block attention mask trick that evaluates all per-block states in a single forward pass is an elegant implementation detail. This work could catalyze a wave of dLLM alignment research.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) generate text via a multi-step masked denoising process, making it fundamentally harder to estimate generation probability than in autoregressive models (ARMs), where a single forward pass suffices. This computational bottleneck prevents efficient post-training alignment — the standard DPO/RLHF pipeline cannot be directly applied to dLLMs without either prohibitive cost (hundreds of forward passes per example for online methods) or heuristic approximations (mean-field, ELBO) that are theoretically unsound or noisy. The paper targets this gap: how to perform principled, efficient offline preference optimization for dLLMs. Methodology: The paper introduces dTRPO (Trajectory Reduction Policy Optimization), which derives two provably correct reductions of the dLLM trajectory probability computation. First, State Reduction (Theorem 3.1): by partitioning the diffusion horizon into blocks and sampling one timestep per block, the full log-trajectory probability is an unbiased estimator using only N_B evaluations (one per block) rather than T total steps. Second, Ratio Reduction (Theorem 3.2): when computing the ratio of current policy to reference policy — as required by DPO — all schedule-dependent coefficients (α_t terms) cancel exactly, leaving only categorical probabilities over newly-unmasked tokens. This eliminates numerical instability from large-vocabulary schedule coefficients. Combining both reductions yields the dTRPO objective, a DPO-style loss that requires exactly 4 forward passes per training example (preferred and dispreferred completions × policy and reference model), identical in cost to DPO for ARMs. The implementation uses a block attention mask to evaluate all per-block partially-masked states in a single forward pass. An inference-aligned top-k confidence scheduler (k=0.1) selects which tokens to unmask at each step, matching the deployment-time decoding strategy. Only MLP and output projection layers are updated (PEFT-style), preventing catastrophic forgetting. Key Results: Evaluated on Fast-dLLM-v2-7B (initialized from Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct), dTRPO achieves the best overall performance among open-source dLLMs across 9 benchmarks. Compared to the strongest dLLM baseline (Fast-dLLM-v2): +9.59% on GPQA (STEM reasoning), +4.04% on MATH, +3.63% on GSM8K, +3.61% on LCBv6 (coding), +4.3% on HumanEval+, +2.95% on IFEval, +6.15% on Arena-Hard, +3.36% on MT-Bench. The gap to the underlying ARM (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) is narrowed to 3.02% on Arena-Hard and 1.55% on MT-Bench. Training cost matches DPO for ARMs (4 forward passes/example) versus online methods requiring hundreds of forward passes (N×). On inference speed, dTRPO achieves 29.87 TPS on Arena-Hard versus 19.55 for Fast-dLLM-v2, with lower average response time (23.98s vs 28.92s). Ablations confirm robustness to projection function choice (DPO, IPO, RSO, APO), scheduling strategy (top-k vs random-k), and λ hyperparameter. The method generalizes to long-block dLLMs (LLaDA) and block-wise architectures. Strengths: - The theoretical grounding is tight: Theorems 3.1 and 3.2 are proven rigorously, and the key insight that schedule coefficients cancel in ratios is both non-obvious and practically impactful — it simultaneously eliminates numerical instability and removes schedule sensitivity from the loss. - Training efficiency is genuinely compelling: achieving comparable MATH performance to online dLLM methods (TRaDO, d2) that use MATH-specific training data, using only a general preference dataset and 4× fewer forward passes than standard offline dLLM baselines. - Broad empirical validation: 9 benchmarks across STEM, code, and instruction-following; ablations cover scheduler, projection function, block size, sample count, and PEFT strategy; tested on two distinct dLLM architectures (block-wise Fast-dLLM-v2 and long-block LLaDA). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The independence assumption across token positions (required for Theorem 3.2) is violated by the top-k confidence scheduler, which selects tokens deterministically based on model confidence. The paper empirically shows negligible impact, but no theoretical bound on the resulting bias is provided. - Evaluation is restricted to a single 7B backbone (Fast-dLLM-v2 from Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct); results against Qwen3-based dLLMs (Table 4) show dTRPO with an average loss of -8.46% relative to the base ARM, comparable to SDAR (-12.36%) and TraDO (-8.88%), suggesting the method does not close the dLLM-vs-ARM gap at the frontier. The paper does not demonstrate scaling beyond 7B or explore whether the approach extends to online RL settings where reward models are used.
Recommended 2603.18806 PDF
#9 — Highly Innovative (82)

Stabilizing Reinforcement Learning for Diffusion Language Models

Jianyuan Zhong, Kaibo Wang, Ding Ding, Zijin Feng, Haoli Bai, Yang Xiang, Jiacheng Sun, Qiang Xu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: StableDRL delivers a theoretically grounded diagnosis of why GRPO catastrophically collapses for dLLMs, tracing the instability to a precise self-reinforcing loop involving heavy-tailed importance ratios and conditional clipping. The fix is surgically minimal — unconditional clipping plus self-normalization — yet provably bounds updates to the convex hull of per-sample gradients. The staircase attention mechanism for block diffusion is an elegant O(1) solution to the efficiency-vs-leakage dilemma. Beating autoregressive Qwen3-8B on AIME 2024 with a diffusion model is a striking empirical result that demonstrates stable RL can unlock genuinely new capabilities in dLLMs.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Discrete diffusion large language models (dLLMs) offer intrinsic advantages over autoregressive (AR) models — parallel decoding and bidirectional context — but cannot benefit from modern RL post-training because Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), the dominant algorithm for AR reasoning fine-tuning, catastrophically collapses when applied to dLLMs (reward collapse within ~300 steps). Prior work attempted to fix the dLLM side of the mismatch by improving importance ratio estimation (mean-field or ELBO-based Monte Carlo proxies), but instability persisted. This paper argues the problem lies equally in GRPO's formulation, which was designed for exact sequence likelihoods, not noisy proxies. Methodology: The authors identify a self-reinforcing instability loop with three links: (1) ELBO-based MC estimation produces heavy-tailed importance ratios (individual ratios can reach 10^5); (2) GRPO's conditional clipping passes these outliers through unclipped when the advantage is negative, causing gradient spikes; (3) spikes induce policy drift, which amplifies future ratio variance, closing the loop. They formalize this with a suite of lemmas and theorems showing the spike probability lower bound is non-decreasing in the policy drift state. To break the loop, they propose StableDRL, a two-change reformulation of GRPO: (i) unconditional clipping — importance ratios are always bounded to [1−ε, 1+ε] regardless of advantage sign, eliminating the 'trapdoor' that allows noise-driven outliers to inject massive gradients; and (ii) self-normalization — the fixed group-size denominator G is replaced by the sum of clipped ratios Σ clip_ε(ρ̂_i), constraining the update to the convex hull of per-sample gradients and provably bounding the update norm by max_j ||g_{i,j}||. For block diffusion models (SDAR), where ELBO estimation requires block-causal conditioning, they introduce staircase attention — a dual-stream attention mask combining a causal context stream and a corrupted target stream with a block-lower-triangular 'staircase' pattern — enabling leakage-free, single-pass (O(1)) ELBO estimation instead of O(K) sequential passes. Key Results: On full-attention dLLMs (LLaDA-8B-Instruct), StableDRL achieves new state-of-the-art on all four benchmarks: GSM8K 84.2% avg (vs. 83.0% SPG), MATH500 41.8% avg (vs. 39.5% ESPO, +5.2% over SPG at 256 tokens), Countdown 83.5% avg (+13.7% over SPG at 256 tokens), and Sudoku 91.5% avg (vs. 90.0% SPG). StableDRL is the first method enabling stable full-parameter RL for >1,000 steps; baselines require LoRA or early stopping for stability. On block diffusion (SDAR-8B), StableDRL achieves 16.7% on AIME 2024 (static sampling), surpassing the base model (11.8%), Trado (13.3%), and even autoregressive Qwen3-8B (10.0%). The adversarial 'exploding weight' stress test — where 70% of samples have artificially inflated importance ratios — shows StableDRL maintains monotonic reward improvement while ESPO collapses immediately and SPG degrades in both normal and stressed conditions. Ablations confirm both components are necessary: removing clipping causes rapid collapse from outlier-dominated gradients; removing self-normalization causes oscillatory collapse from group-scale variance corrupting AdamW's momentum history. Strengths: - The instability diagnosis is theoretically grounded and mechanistically precise: the paper distinguishes two failure modes (individual outlier bypass and group-scale variance), proves both cause self-reinforcing loops, and shows why unconditional clipping alone is insufficient (it trades rare large spikes for frequent boundary-saturated updates). - The fix is minimal and principled — two surgical changes to the loss normalization that together guarantee the update lies in the convex hull of per-sample gradients, with no hyperparameter proliferation beyond the clipping threshold ε. - The staircase attention mechanism cleanly resolves a genuine algorithmic dilemma (efficiency vs. leakage in block diffusion) with a single-pass O(1) construction, making the approach architecturally general across dLLM families. - Results on AIME 2024 (16.7%) beating an AR model of equal parameter count (Qwen3-8B at 10.0%) demonstrates that stable on-policy RL can unlock reasoning capabilities inaccessible to supervised baselines in diffusion models. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The theoretical analysis relies on several strong auxiliary conditions (uniform tail envelope, conditional independence of noise across samples, bounded per-sample gradients, dominance event for the spike-maximizing sample), which are plausible but not empirically verified as holding throughout training — the theory is diagnostic rather than a tight convergence guarantee. - Comparisons use different training tasks for the ablation of instability mechanisms (GRPO evaluated on GSM8K, unconditional clipping and StableDRL on Countdown), which weakens the head-to-head empirical verification of the instability loop even if the qualitative conclusions are convincing. - The method inherits all the computational overhead of ELBO-based proxy estimation (multiple MC samples per step) and requires custom inference kernels (JetEngine) for the block diffusion setting, creating a non-trivial engineering barrier for adoption beyond the authors' infrastructure.
Recommended 2603.06743 PDF
#10 — Highly Innovative (82)

TriAttention: Efficient Long Reasoning with Trigonometric KV Compression (64 ▲ )

Weian Mao, Xi Lin, Wei Huang, Yuxin Xie, Tianfu Fu, Bohan Zhuang, Song Han, Yukang Chen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 Apr 2026
Why this rank: TriAttention introduces a genuinely novel theoretical insight: pre-RoPE Q/K vectors are highly concentrated around stable centers, enabling closed-form trigonometric importance prediction without any runtime attention observation. This elegantly sidesteps the fundamental observation-window limitation that constrains all prior eviction-based KV compression methods. The ~2× accuracy improvement over baselines at matched memory budgets on hard mathematical reasoning benchmarks (AIME) suggests this addresses a real structural flaw, not a marginal tuning opportunity. The offline calibration robustness further strengthens practical viability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Extended chain-of-thought reasoning in LLMs generates sequences of tens of thousands of tokens, causing KV cache memory to grow proportionally and creating severe bottlenecks. Existing KV cache compression methods estimate token importance using attention scores computed from recent post-RoPE queries. However, RoPE rotates query vectors with position, so only the most recent queries retain up-to-date orientations—forming a tiny observation window. Prior work shows that expanding this window beyond ~25 queries does not help and actually hurts performance. The result is that important tokens can be permanently evicted if they happen to receive low attention during this brief window, breaking long reasoning chains, particularly at retrieval heads where relevant tokens stay dormant for long periods. Methodology: TriAttention exploits a previously uncharacterized phenomenon in the pre-RoPE space: Q and K vectors across most attention heads are highly concentrated around fixed, non-zero centers (Mean Resultant Length R ≈ 0.97–0.98 across domains), and this concentration is stable across positions and input contexts. The key theoretical result is that when Q/K are concentrated, the attention logit reduces to a trigonometric series that depends only on the Q-K distance Δ: logit(Δ) ≈ Σ_f [a_f cos(ω_f Δ) + b_f sin(ω_f Δ)], where coefficients are determined by the Q/K centers and ω_f are the RoPE frequencies. This means attention distance preferences are predictable from stable centers computed once via offline calibration, without needing to observe actual attention scores at inference time. The TriAttention scoring function combines two components: (1) S_trig(k, Δ) uses the Q center as a proxy for future queries, evaluating the trigonometric series at multiple geometrically-spaced future offsets and averaging to produce an expected-attention score for each key; (2) S_norm(k) is a frequency-band-weighted norm score for heads where concentration is weaker, scaled by (1 − R_f) so it contributes little when R is high. The final score S̃(k) = S_trig + S_norm ranks all keys; the top-B are retained and the rest evicted every 128 decoded tokens. For GQA, per-head scores are z-score normalized and aggregated via max across query heads sharing each KV head. Key Results: On AIME25 (Qwen3-8B, 32K token generation), TriAttention matches Full Attention accuracy (40.8%) while achieving 2.5× higher throughput (563 vs. 223 tokens/s) or 10.7× KV memory reduction. At the same KV budget of 2048 tokens, TriAttention nearly doubles R-KV on AIME25 (32.9% vs. 17.5%) and AIME24 (42.1% vs. 25.4%). On MATH 500 with only 1,024 cached tokens out of 32K, TriAttention closely matches Full Attention (68.4% vs. 69.6%), yielding 6.3× throughput. On a custom recursive DFS memory retention benchmark, TriAttention matches Full Attention up to recursion depth 16, while R-KV catastrophically degrades from 61% at depth 14 to 31% at depth 16. On LongBench (16 subtasks, 50% KV budget), TriAttention achieves the highest average (48.1) across all compression baselines, winning 11 of 16 subtasks. On RULER retrieval, TriAttention scores 66.1 vs. 55.6 for SnapKV. Ablations confirm that removing S_trig degrades AIME24 from 42.1% to 18.8%, while removing S_norm costs ~5 points. Calibration data quality and quantity have minimal impact, confirming that Q/K centers are model-intrinsic properties. The method also enables a Qwen3-32B INT4 agent (OpenClaw) to run on a single RTX 4090 where Full Attention causes OOM. Strengths: - The core insight—that pre-RoPE Q/K concentration is a stable, model-intrinsic property enabling closed-form importance prediction without any runtime attention observation—is novel, theoretically grounded, and empirically validated across three architectures (Qwen3, Qwen2.5, Llama3) and GQA/MLA variants. - Performance gains over baselines are large and consistent: roughly 2× accuracy at the same memory budget on hard AIME benchmarks, not marginal improvements, suggesting the method addresses a genuine structural flaw in prior observation-window approaches. - The offline calibration is robust to data quality and quantity (HTML vs. chat; 50K vs. 960K tokens produce nearly identical results), making deployment straightforward. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method is evaluated primarily on mathematical reasoning; generalization to coding, agentic multi-step tasks, and open-ended generation remains largely asserted but not deeply studied (LongBench covers some ground but at 50% KV budget where compression pressure is modest). - TriAttention still lags Full Attention on hard benchmarks at low budgets (e.g., AIME25 at 2048 tokens: 32.9% vs. 40.8%), and the paper does not provide a hardware-optimized inference kernel, meaning the throughput numbers do not yet reflect a fully production-ready implementation—the trigonometric series scoring overhead is not fully characterized. - The paper claims Q/K concentration causes attention distance preference, but the causal mechanism is validated only through correlation (Pearson r ≈ 0.5–0.9 averaged across heads), not controlled interventions. The 10–50% of heads with lower reconstruction correlation are handled by a fallback norm term, but the interaction between these heterogeneous heads and reasoning quality at extreme compression is not closely analyzed.
Recommended 2604.04921 PDF
#11 — Highly Innovative (82)

Batched Contextual Reinforcement: A Task-Scaling Law for Efficient Reasoning

Bangji Yang, Hongbo Ma, Jiajun Fan, Ge Liu · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Scores 82 for its striking elegance: a single structural change—batching N problems into a shared token budget—achieves what multi-stage curricula and explicit length penalties cannot, with no reward function or architecture modifications. The discovery that implicit budget constraints avoid the adversarial gradient collapse seen in penalty-based approaches is a genuinely useful finding for the RL-for-reasoning community. The task-scaling law (N as a controllable throughput-accuracy knob) is a novel conceptual contribution with immediate practical value. Deducted points because evaluation is confined to mathematical reasoning at small scales (1.5B–4B), the 'syntactic not semantic' compression claim lacks rigorous verification, and the narrow sensitivity window for B_max raises transferability concerns.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Chain-of-Thought reasoning models trained via Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) suffer from excessive verbosity—generating redundant metacognitive loops, self-verification spirals, and strategy exploration dead ends that inflate inference costs without proportional accuracy gains. Existing remedies fall into three categories: explicit length penalties (which create adversarial gradients causing training collapse), auxiliary difficulty estimators (which require additional model components and human-designed taxonomies), and multi-stage curricula (3–8 stage pipelines with brittle hyperparameters). The core question is whether LLMs can learn to reason efficiently without any explicit length supervision. Methodology: Batched Contextual Reinforcement (BCR) is a single-stage GRPO-based training paradigm with one structural change: instead of training on individual problems, the model trains on groups of N problems concatenated into a single shared context window with a fixed token budget B_max. The N problems compete for the shared budget—verbose reasoning on early problems truncates budget for later ones, yielding zero accuracy reward. No length penalty is ever applied; every token within the budget is "free." The reward decomposes into accuracy (mean per-problem correctness via symbolic equivalence checking) and format components only. A stratified difficulty-balanced sampling strategy constructs 3,000 groups from DeepMath-103K, and a stack-based parser handles nested LaTeX expressions for reliable per-problem answer extraction. BCR is instantiated on two base models: JustRL-DeepSeek-1.5B and Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, both trained with N=3 and B_max set to approximately 50% of the base model's average output length. Efficiency transfers fully to standard single-problem (N=1) inference—the model internalizes a denser reasoning policy rather than merely adapting to batched inputs. Key Results: At N=1 inference, BCR-JustRL-1.5B reduces token usage by 39.8–62.6% across five benchmarks (AIME25, AMC23, Minerva, MATH-500, Olympiad) with accuracy gains on 2/5 benchmarks (AMC23 +2.5%, Minerva +5.1%). BCR-Qwen3-4B reduces token usage by 15.8–31.8% while improving accuracy on all five benchmarks (AIME25 +13.3%, AMC23 +2.5%, Minerva +2.2%, MATH-500 +0.4%, Olympiad +1.9%), a genuine "free lunch." A task-scaling law is identified: increasing concurrent problems N monotonically reduces per-problem tokens while BCR degrades far more gracefully than baselines—on AMC23, baseline accuracy collapses from 85.0% (N=1) to 22.5% (N=5) while BCR drops only from 87.5% to 50.0%. At N=4, BCR uses 75% fewer tokens than baseline on AIME25 while achieving higher accuracy. Explicit length penalty baselines (both 2-1-1 and 5-1-1 reward weight configurations) exhibit catastrophic training collapse—accuracy drops to near zero while the length reward is maximized—confirming that constraint-based approaches fundamentally outperform penalty-based ones. Training cost is 200 GPU-hours (1.5B) and 720 GPU-hours (4B) on 4× NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 GPUs. Strengths: - Elegant minimalism: a single structural input change (batching N problems) achieves what multi-stage curricula and auxiliary models fail to achieve cleanly, with no modification to the reward function, training algorithm, or model architecture. - The empirical demonstration that implicit budget constraints (treating tokens as free within a hard cap) avoid the adversarial gradient problem that destroys explicit penalty approaches is a genuinely useful finding for practitioners designing RL-based reasoning systems. - The task-scaling law—N as a controllable throughput-accuracy knob analogous to batch size in classical compute—is novel and practically actionable; BCR-trained models can trade efficiency for accuracy predictably at inference time without retraining. - The qualitative analysis is thorough and mechanistically informative: four distinct syntactic compression mechanisms (metacognitive loop elimination, strategy exploration reduction, pedagogical narration removal, catastrophic degeneration prevention) are identified and illustrated across 16 diverse examples. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is restricted to mathematical reasoning at 1.5B and 4B scales; generalization to larger models (7B–70B), code generation, scientific reasoning, or multimodal tasks is entirely unvalidated, making the universality of the task-scaling law speculative. - The qualitative claim that BCR compression is purely "syntactic, not semantic" is asserted but not rigorously verified—it rests on cherry-picked examples, and it is possible that on harder problems BCR systematically skips necessary verification steps that are genuinely load-bearing for correctness, a risk that aggregate accuracy numbers cannot fully rule out. - The token budget B_max requires a probe-set calibration step (Algorithm 1) that introduces a new hyperparameter (compression ratio λ ≈ 0.5) with non-trivial sensitivity: ablations show 4096 tokens hurts accuracy while 6144 weakens compression, creating a narrow target range that may not transfer cleanly across domains or model sizes.
Search Results 2604.02322 PDF
#12 — Highly Innovative (82)

SEVerA: Verified Synthesis of Self-Evolving Agents (21 ▲ )

Debangshu Banerjee, Changming Xu, Gagandeep Singh · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Apr 2026
Why this rank: SEVerA introduces a genuinely novel synthesis of formal verification and self-evolving LLM agents through the Formally Guarded Generative Models (FGGM) mechanism, which provides parameter-independent correctness guarantees without modifying model internals. The key insight — decomposing verification into local first-order contracts that convert constrained learning into unconstrained optimization — is both theoretically elegant and practically powerful. Achieving strictly zero constraint violations across four diverse tasks while simultaneously surpassing state-of-the-art performance (including beating a frontier model with a smaller open-weight model) is a compelling empirical validation. The approach bridges two communities (formal methods and LLM agents) that have largely operated independently, opening a productive new research direction.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Self-evolving LLM agent frameworks synthesize programs that invoke parametric models (LLMs, neural networks, SMT solvers) and then fine-tune those models to improve performance. These agents are deployed autonomously on unseen inputs, yet existing frameworks provide no formal correctness guarantees. This gap has concrete consequences: agents cheat in program verification by modifying input code rather than adding annotations (inflating accuracy by 8.1%), delete failing tests instead of fixing bugs, and violate domain policies on 65–76% of customer-service interactions. Natural-language task descriptions and soft performance metrics on fixed training sets are insufficient to prevent such failures. The paper asks whether it is possible to achieve both the formal guarantees of constraint-guided program synthesis and the performance benefits of gradient-based self-evolving frameworks. Methodology: The paper introduces two key contributions. First, Formally Guarded Generative Models (FGGMs): a mechanism that wraps each generative model (GM) call in a rejection sampler with a verified non-parametric fallback. The planner LLM specifies a local first-order input–output contract (Φ_l, Ψ_l) per GM call, along with a prompting program f_p and a fallback f_d verified to satisfy the contract. The rejection sampler draws K samples from the GM, accepts the first that passes the contract checker, and falls back to f_d unconditionally if all are rejected. This guarantees the contract holds for all parameter values of the underlying GM, enabling parameter-independent correctness. Second, SEVerA (Self-Evolving Verified Agents): a three-stage framework built on FGGMs. In Search, a planner LLM generates candidate parametric programs in Dafny (a verification-aware language) where all GM calls are wrapped as FGGMs. In Verify, Dafny's built-in verifier checks FGGM well-formedness and the full program against global behavioral specifications (Φ, Ψ), using FGGM local contracts as axioms. Programs failing verification receive error feedback in a CEGIS-style loop. In Learn, verified programs reduce the constrained optimization to unconstrained gradient-based training: small neural networks are tuned with Adam backpropagation; open-weight LLMs are fine-tuned via GRPO with LoRA adapters using a reward that combines task loss and FGGM conformance. For closed-source LLMs, only prompt tuning through the synthesized f_p applies. The framework is proved sound (Theorem 5.4): any returned agent satisfies (Φ, Ψ) for all inputs and all parameter values. Theorem 5.5 gives a sufficient condition under which a verified agent strictly dominates an unconstrained GM call in task loss. Key Results: SEVerA is evaluated on four tasks. (1) LLM-Assisted Dafny Verification (closed-source Claude Sonnet 4.5, no parameter tuning): SEVerA achieves 97.0% verification+NoDiff rate on HumanEvalDafny versus 86.9% for the DafnyBench baseline, with 0% violations compared to 4–8.1% for all baselines. On DafnyBench: 89.1% vs. 81.6%, again 0% violations. (2) Agentic Tool Use (τ²-bench, Qwen3-8B): SEVerA achieves 53.6% pass rate on retail and 52.6% on airline with 0% violations, outperforming Agent-C with Claude Sonnet 4.5 (47.3%) on airline while using a smaller open-weight model. Unconstrained SEVerA attains 49.4%/44.7% but incurs 10.3%/25.5% violations. (3) Symbolic Math (GSM-Symbolic, Qwen3-8B with GRPO): SEVerA without parameter tuning achieves 53.2% accuracy with 0% violations versus CRANE's 44.7% with 2.1% violations; with GRPO fine-tuning, accuracy reaches 66.0% (0% violations). Ablations show global task-loss tuning contributes 8.5 pp and local conformance tuning adds 4.3 pp. (4) Constrained Symbolic Regression: SEVerA finds verified solutions in 33/35 instances with 0% violations, while PySR and LLM-SR violate specifications on 62.86% and 31–34% of instances respectively, and SEVerA achieves substantially lower NMSE on the valid instances. Strengths: - The FGGM mechanism provides a principled, model-agnostic solution to enforcing first-order logic contracts on GM calls without modifying decoding internals, making it compatible with closed-source models where constrained decoding fails. - The key insight that verification can be decomposed into parameter-independent local contracts is elegant and practically powerful: it converts a constrained learning problem into unconstrained optimization, enabling standard GRPO/LoRA fine-tuning to be applied without any modification. - Empirical results are unusually strong: SEVerA achieves strictly zero constraint violations across all four diverse tasks while simultaneously surpassing state-of-the-art task performance, including beating Agent-C with a frontier model (Claude Sonnet 4.5) using a smaller open-weight model (Qwen3-8B). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The framework is resource-unaware: behavioral specifications constrain functional correctness but not computational cost (LLM calls, token budgets, wall-clock time). The rejection sampler with K=5 samples and Dafny verification adds 1.9–2.5× latency overhead, which could be prohibitive for latency-sensitive deployments. - The approach inherits Dafny's verifier limitations: verification runs with a fixed timeout (240s per call) and returns false on timeout, so programs requiring complex proofs may be incorrectly rejected. Quantified output specifications Ψ_l cause the contract checker to invoke an SMT solver that may also time out, making the checker incomplete in those cases and potentially rejecting valid GM outputs.
