#13 — Highly Innovative (81)

Act Wisely: Cultivating Meta-Cognitive Tool Use in Agentic Multimodal Models (28 ▲ )

Shilin Yan, Jintao Tong, Hongwei Xue, Xiaojun Tang, Yangyang Wang, Kunyu Shi, Guannan Zhang, Ruixuan Li, Yixiong Zou · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 81 for its rigorous mathematical diagnosis of the coupled-reward failure mode in tool-use RL and the elegant HDPO solution with its emergent implicit curriculum. The Taylor expansion showing efficiency signals are washed out during advantage normalization is a precise theoretical contribution, and the conditional advantage mechanism that restricts efficiency optimization to correct rollouts is technically sound. Reducing tool invocation from 98% to 2% while simultaneously improving accuracy across perception and math benchmarks is a striking empirical result. The decoupled optimization paradigm could broadly influence multi-objective RL training for agents beyond tool use.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Agentic multimodal large language models (MLLMs) equipped with external tools suffer from "blind tool invocation"—reflexively calling tools even when queries are resolvable from the raw visual context. Existing open-source agents invoke tools 80–100% of the time yet fail to translate this computational expenditure into better reasoning. Beyond latency and cost, redundant tool calls inject environmental noise that degrades reasoning accuracy. Existing RL mitigation strategies scalarize accuracy and efficiency into a single reward, creating an irreconcilable optimization dilemma: an aggressive efficiency penalty suppresses necessary tool use, while a mild penalty is washed out by the variance of the accuracy reward during advantage normalization (e.g., in GRPO), rendering it mathematically impotent against tool overuse. Methodology: The paper proposes Hierarchical Decoupled Policy Optimization (HDPO), which reframes tool efficiency from a competing scalar objective into a strictly conditional one. HDPO maintains two independent optimization channels: (1) an accuracy channel applying standard GRPO over all rollouts, and (2) an efficiency channel that computes tool-parsimony rewards—defined as 1/(T+1) where T is the number of tool calls—exclusively over the subset of correct rollouts (the "qualifying set" Q). When |Q| < 2, the efficiency advantage is zeroed to prevent semantically invalid cross-prompt comparisons. The two channels produce independently normalized advantages that are combined only at the final loss: L_HDPO = w_acc·L_GRPO(A_acc) + w_tool·L_GRPO(A_tool). A key emergent property is an implicit cognitive curriculum: early in training when Q is mostly empty, only accuracy is optimized; as the model matures and more rollouts are correct, the efficiency signal smoothly scales up—first learn correctness, then learn parsimony, without any manual scheduling. The approach is complemented by a rigorous three-stage SFT data curation pipeline: (i) executing all code in a sandbox to eliminate hallucinated environmental feedback, (ii) filtering out samples the base model can solve without tools (pass@8=1) to enforce genuine tool necessity, and (iii) applying Gemini-3.1-Pro as a multidimensional judge to penalize blind tool invocation in training demonstrations. The resulting agent, Metis, is trained on Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct with SFT cold-start followed by HDPO RL, using G=16 rollouts per prompt, w_acc=1.0, w_tool=0.15, and KL=0. Key Results: Metis reduces tool invocation from 98% (standard GRPO) to 2% on VBench and from 80% to 1% on HRBench, while simultaneously improving accuracy. On perception benchmarks: VBench 91.1% (vs. 88.7% GRPO, 86.4% base), HRBench-4K 83.5%, HRBench-8K 82.0% (outperforming the 30B-parameter Skywork-R1V4-30B-A3B at 79.8%), CharXiv reasoning questions 54.1% (vs. previous best 48.9%). On mathematical reasoning, Metis averages 66.9% across five benchmarks vs. 53.7% for DeepEyesV2 and 59.4% for the base model, with a striking +26.4% absolute gain on WeMath (65.2% vs. 38.8% base). Ablations confirm an inverted-U in performance vs. w_tool, with w_tool=0.15 optimal; w_tool=0.20 degrades performance by over-suppressing necessary tool use. HDPO consistently outperforms standard GRPO (accuracy-only) by +2.4–3.1% across benchmarks, validating that eliminating gradient entanglement is the mechanism. Strengths: - Rigorous mathematical diagnosis of the coupled-reward failure mode: the Taylor expansion showing the efficiency signal is O(α) relative to accuracy variance is a precise and useful theoretical contribution. - The conditional advantage mechanism is elegant and technically sound—restricting efficiency optimization to correct rollouts prevents the degenerate case of rewarding wrong answers for tool abstention. - The implicit curriculum induction is a genuine emergent property of the design, not bolted-on reward scheduling, making the approach robust and hyperparameter-light. - The SFT data curation pipeline is notably principled: filtering by pass@8=1 specifically addresses dataset staleness (weak base models over-annotating tool necessity), which is a real and underappreciated problem in the field. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The efficiency reward function 1/(T+1) treats all tool calls as equivalent cost, ignoring that different tools (web search vs. image crop) have vastly different latency and noise profiles; a latency-aware reward would be more principled. - Evaluation is conducted on static benchmarks; the claim that tool parsimony generalizes to long-horizon, open-ended agentic tasks (the authors' stated future work) is untested, and reflexive tool use may be genuinely necessary in environments with less well-defined visual context. - The w_tool hyperparameter still requires task-distribution-specific tuning (the inverted-U shape from w_tool=0.10 to 0.20 shows meaningful sensitivity), somewhat undermining the claim of eliminating hyperparameter fragility relative to coupled reward approaches.
Recommended 2604.08545 PDF
#14 — Highly Innovative (81)

A foundation model for multivariate time series forecasting

researchsquare.com · New articles in my profile
Why this rank: Scores 81 for the principled group attention mechanism that cleanly extends the TSFM paradigm to multivariate forecasting via in-context learning, and for the creative synthetic multivariate data generation pipeline covering instantaneous and sequential interaction types. The breadth of evaluation across three benchmarks and four rigorous scientific case studies with domain-appropriate metrics is exceptional and makes the generalization claims unusually credible. While it builds on the established Chronos/T5 foundation rather than proposing an entirely new paradigm, the architectural contribution is elegant and the zero-shot performance across epidemiology, macroeconomics, carbon flux, and hydrology demonstrates genuine cross-domain transfer that prior TSFMs could not achieve.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Most time series foundation models (TSFMs) treat each series independently, preventing them from capturing the multivariate interactions that characterize real-world systems — epidemics spreading across populations, economic indicators co-evolving through causal relationships, ecosystems exchanging carbon and energy through linked feedbacks. Task-specific models require bespoke training for each dataset. Existing TSFMs (Chronos, TimesFM, Moirai) that enable zero-shot forecasting still operate on individual time series, losing the predictive signal embedded in covariates and cross-series correlations. Chronos-2 addresses this gap by extending the TSFM paradigm to the multivariate setting with heterogeneous covariates. Methodology: Chronos-2 is a 120M-parameter encoder-only Transformer (T5 architecture) that infers multivariate dependencies at inference time via in-context learning (ICL). The central innovation is a group attention mechanism that alternates with standard temporal self-attention in each Transformer block: temporal attention aggregates information along the time axis within a single series, while group attention aggregates across all series assigned to the same group (targets, past-only covariates, known future covariates) at each patch index. Groups are flexible and can represent variates of a single multivariate series, series from the same source, or target+covariate bundles — allowing seamless switching between univariate and multivariate modes at inference. Inputs are tokenized as non-overlapping patches of length 16 with robust scaling (standardization + sinh⁻¹ transform) and binary masks for missing values. A quantile head predicts 21 quantiles (0.01–0.99) in a single forward pass over multiple future patches, avoiding expensive autoregression. The model handles contexts up to 8,192 timesteps and horizons up to 1,024 steps. Training uses a two-stage curriculum (context 2,048 then 8,192) with a pinball/quantile regression loss. The pretraining corpus combines real univariate data (23 datasets spanning energy, transport, cloud ops, weather) with a novel synthetic multivariate data generator that constructs instantaneous interactions (random linear mixing matrices and MLP transformations applied at the same timestep) and sequential interactions (Temporal Causal Models simulating VAR processes, and random RNNs for nonlinear lagged dynamics). This synthetic pipeline covers the combinatorial space of interaction types without requiring curated real multivariate datasets. Key Results: On fev-bench (100 tasks, 68 multivariate), Chronos-2 achieves an average win rate of 87.5% (MASE) and skill score of 35.5%, surpassing the next-best TSFM (TiRex: 77.5% / 30.0%) by 10 and 5.2 percentage points respectively — all without any task-specific training. Enabling group attention (ICL) over univariate mode yields +10.7 percentage points in win rate and +4.1 in skill score, directly quantifying the value of cross-series context. Chronos-2 processes 100 time series in 0.8 seconds on an A10G GPU, 50× faster than the best point forecaster (TFT at 827s) and 1000× faster than DeepAR (1113s). On Chronos-Bench-II and GIFT-Eval it leads or ties state-of-the-art, with ICL improving even univariate tasks by cross-learning from related series. In four scientific case studies: (1) Epidemiology — surpasses the operational RespiCast Hub-Ensemble at horizons >1 week for ARI/ILI across 31 European countries (p<0.001); (2) Macroeconomics — outperforms AutoARIMA and AutoETS on German CPI components at 3- and 12-month horizons (p<0.001) using natural COICOP groupings; (3) Carbon flux — achieves lowest RMSE across NEE, LE, H on 65 FLUXNET sites in zero-shot mode, beating supervised XGBoost (p<0.01); (4) Hydrology — zero-shot median NSE of 0.68 on CAMELS-DE (1,582 catchments), rising to match the state-of-the-art LSTM ensemble (NSE≈0.90) after lightweight fine-tuning, without static catchment attributes. Strengths: - The group attention mechanism is a clean, principled design that enables in-context learning of multivariate dependencies without storing task-specific parameters, and is fully composable with standard Transformer blocks. - The synthetic multivariate data generation pipeline — covering instantaneous (linear mixing, MLP) and sequential (VAR/TCM, RNN) interactions — is a scalable and creative solution to the scarcity of curated multivariate pretraining data. - Empirical breadth is exceptional: three standard benchmarks plus four scientifically rigorous domain case studies with domain-appropriate metrics (WIS, NSE, RMSE), making the generalization claims credible rather than benchmark-optimized. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The pretraining data is predominantly synthetic for multivariate relationships, which may not capture the distributional complexity of real joint dynamics (e.g., sparse or asymmetric causal structures not well-represented by random VAR/RNN); zero-shot performance on hydrology (NSE=0.68 vs 0.90 for supervised LSTM) illustrates the remaining gap for physically complex domains. - The architecture currently lacks support for static metadata covariates (e.g., catchment soil properties, demographic profiles), a significant limitation for cold-start generalization to ungauged basins or new markets where temporal history is absent but rich static features exist.
Recommended
#15 — Highly Innovative (81)

PLUME: Latent Reasoning Based Universal Multimodal Embedding

Chenwei He, Xiangzhao Hao, Tianyu Yang, Yuxiang Ma, Yuheng Jia, Lingxiang Wu, Chaoyang Zhao, Haiyun Guo, Jinqiao Wang · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: PLUME introduces a genuinely novel paradigm for multimodal retrieval by replacing explicit chain-of-thought token generation with continuous latent state rollout, achieving a 30× inference speedup over CoT-based methods while improving accuracy. The semantic-anchor-guided MoE adapter and progressive explicit-to-latent curriculum are creative architectural contributions with strong ablation support. The core insight — that retrieval reasoning need not be verbalized — challenges a growing assumption in the field and delivers a compelling Pareto improvement on the MMEB-v2 benchmark. The 15-point gap on knowledge-intensive tasks like ChartQA reveals meaningful limitations of continuous compression for symbolic precision.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Universal multimodal embedding (UME) maps heterogeneous inputs—text, images, videos, visual documents—into a shared retrieval space using a single model. Complex queries require compositional spatial understanding, knowledge-intensive inference, or aggregation of temporally dispersed evidence that surface-level similarity cannot handle. Recent approaches address this by prepending explicit chain-of-thought (CoT) rationales before extracting embeddings (e.g., UME-R1, TTE, TRACE), but this creates a dual bottleneck: (1) computational—generating hundreds of tokens per query crushes throughput; (2) representational—routing rich multimodal evidence through discrete text tokens loses fine-grained continuous information. The paper asks whether retrieval needs verbalized intermediate text at all, or whether the underlying multi-step computation can unfold directly in continuous hidden space. Methodology: PLUME replaces explicit CoT with a short autoregressive rollout of K continuous latent states (K=8 by default) inside a frozen-backbone MLLM (Qwen2-VL-2B). The architecture has three core components. First, the latent rollout: after encoding the multimodal prefix, a special `<slt>` token opens a latent block; at each of K steps, the previous hidden state is refined and fed back into the backbone as a continuous input embedding, with KV-cache growing incrementally so each step attends to the full multimodal prefix and all prior latent states. No token is ever decoded during rollout—only continuous vectors are passed. Second, a semantic-anchor-guided transition adapter (MoE-style): between adjacent latent steps, a lightweight routed adapter with one shared expert and Me=4 specialized experts refines the hidden state. Routing is conditioned on a fixed "semantic anchor" extracted from a dedicated `<anchor>` token in the prefix, plus a learnable per-step position embedding. This allows different modalities (video, image, document) to follow different reasoning trajectories under the same K-step budget. Expert balance is regularized with a variance penalty. Third, a progressive explicit-to-latent curriculum: training starts with full explicit CoT (Stage 0), then sentence-level segments of the rationale are progressively replaced left-to-right with latent blocks across Stages 1–4, until Stage 4 uses no explicit tokens at all. This prevents the training instability that occurs with an abrupt switch. The retrieval embedding is extracted from the hidden state at a `<gen>` token placed immediately after the latent block; an auxiliary anchor embedding provides additional contrastive supervision during training but is discarded at inference. The total loss combines InfoNCE (bidirectional), causal LM loss on suffixes, anchor contrastive loss, and MoE balance loss. Key Results: On the 78-task MMEB-v2 benchmark (covering image, video, and visual document retrieval), PLUME with K=8 achieves 61.6 overall, versus 60.1 for UME-R1 (+1.5) and 58.0 for the best single-pass baseline VLM2Vec-V2 (+3.6). All methods use the same Qwen2-VL-2B backbone and training data. The gains are modality-differentiated: Image 66.3 (vs. UME-R1 66.6, −0.3), Video 44.1 (vs. 42.2, +1.9), VisDoc 67.5 (vs. 63.9, +3.6). The largest single gain is Video Multi-modal Retrieval: PLUME 46.7 vs. UME-R1 39.7 (+7.0). On inference efficiency (single H20 GPU), PLUME achieves 3.3 samples/s vs. UME-R1's 0.11 samples/s—a 30.3× speedup—while adding only 1.9× overhead over the no-reasoning baseline VLM2Vec-V2 (6.4 samples/s). At K=6, PLUME already exceeds UME-R1's accuracy while running 30× faster, and at K=4 it still beats all single-pass baselines. Ablations confirm that removing the progressive curriculum causes the largest drop (−6.8 overall), removing the latent transition entirely costs −2.8, replacing MoE with a single MLP costs −2.4, and removing the semantic anchor costs −1.5. Expert activation patterns are non-uniform and emerge without explicit task labels: Expert 2 activates strongly on video/classification tasks, Expert 1 on QA, Expert 0 on image grounding and retrieval. Strengths: - The latent reasoning mechanism eliminates the fundamental tension between reasoning quality and inference throughput in UME—8 hidden steps replace ~400 generated tokens with a 30× speedup and better accuracy, a genuinely strong Pareto improvement. - The semantic-anchor-guided MoE adapter is a well-motivated design choice: routing from a fixed global anchor (rather than the rapidly evolving latent state) stabilizes expert selection across modalities, and the emergent task-level specialization (verified via activation heatmaps) validates the design without explicit task labels. - The progressive explicit-to-latent curriculum is principled and empirically critical: the ablation shows a −6.8 drop without it, making it the single most important component, and the curriculum granularity ablation (2/4/6/8 stages) is thorough and reveals a meaningful non-monotonic optimum. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Performance on knowledge-intensive image QA tasks (ChartQA: 49.8 vs. UME-R1's 64.9; InfographicsVQA: 59.6 vs. 67.7) lags substantially—a 15-point gap on ChartQA—suggesting that compressing fine-grained numerical and textual reasoning into K=8 continuous steps is insufficient when exact symbolic precision is required, and this tradeoff is not fully resolved. - The training cost is substantial (2,562–3,119 H20 GPU-hours depending on K), and the multi-stage curriculum requires careful scheduling; the method's accessibility is therefore limited, and the paper does not explore whether the curriculum could be simplified or whether latent rollout could be initialized more effectively to reduce this cost. - Interpretability of the continuous latent trajectories remains an open problem acknowledged by the authors: while routing activation patterns provide indirect evidence of specialization, there is no mechanistic understanding of what information is encoded in each latent step, making it difficult to diagnose failure modes or predict when the approach will work.
Search Results 2604.02073 PDF
#16 — Highly Innovative (79)

Reaching Beyond the Mode: RL for Distributional Reasoning in Language Models

Isha Puri, Mehul Damani, Idan Shenfeld, Marzyeh Ghassemi, Jacob Andreas, Yoon Kim · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 79 for reframing RL post-training away from mode-seeking toward distributional reasoning — a genuine paradigm shift in how we think about LLM alignment objectives. The unified multi-answer RLVR reward elegantly subsumes standard RLVR, pass@K, and set recovery as special cases, and the Multi-Brier score reward for calibrated confidence is a natural extension. The substantial token efficiency gains (43-50% fewer tokens than independent sampling) demonstrate that single-pass set generation is not just theoretically appealing but practically superior. The coverage-precision tradeoff is honestly presented but unresolved.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard reinforcement learning post-training for language models optimizes for a single high-reward output, collapsing the model's implicit distribution over answers onto a dominant mode. This is a fundamental mismatch for real-world tasks involving epistemic uncertainty, ambiguous information, or inherently multi-label outputs — such as medical differential diagnosis, ambiguous QA, and code generation with multiple valid implementations. Existing inference-time fixes (repeated sampling, prompting for uncertainty) are computationally redundant and suffer train-test mismatch: models trained to commit to one answer resist hedging at inference time, and independently sampled chains exhibit high n-gram overlap even when they produce different final answers. Methodology: The paper proposes Multi-Answer Reinforcement Learning, which modifies the RL training objective to generate a structured set of K distinct candidate answers within a single chain-of-thought pass. Two variants are introduced. Multi-Answer RLVR uses a set-level correctness reward that counts how many of the K generated answers belong to the ground-truth set: R = Σ 1[aᵢ ∈ Y*]. This subsumes standard RLVR (K=N=1), pass@K (N=1, K>1), and partial/full set recovery (N>1) as special cases. Multi-Answer RLCR additionally requires the model to output a calibrated confidence score per answer, trained with a Multi-Brier score reward (mean squared error between verbalized confidence and binary per-answer correctness) combined with the set-level correctness reward. A format reward enforces answer uniqueness (rewards zeroed if answers are not distinct), and in the N=1 setting, confidences must sum to ≤1. Training uses GRPO on Qwen3-8B with 32 rollouts per prompt, BNPO objective, and 1 epoch. Evaluation spans three datasets: DDXPlus (medical differential diagnosis, N>1), HotPotQA-Modified (ambiguous single-gold-answer QA, N=1), and MBPP (code generation, low-ambiguity multi-solution). Key Results: On DDXPlus (k=3), Multi-Answer RLVR achieves coverage of 0.79 vs. 0.62 for single-answer RLVR, with perfect diversity (1.00 vs. 0.62) and 43% fewer tokens (677 vs. 1191). Multi-Answer RLCR reaches 0.77 coverage with 510 tokens vs. 1378 for RLCR Single. On MBPP, RLVR Multi achieves 1.35 average correct answers per set vs. 0.98 for RLVR Single, 49% top-1 accuracy vs. 29%, and uses only 235 tokens vs. 512 — a >50% reduction. Prompting single-answer models with a multi-answer prompt is consistently inferior to trained multi-answer models, demonstrating that set generation requires explicit training rather than just prompting. Under a matched budget of 30 total answers, RLVR-Multi produces nearly twice as many unique diagnoses on average (8 vs. 4). Calibration results show RLCR-Multi substantially improves over RLVR-Multi: on DDXPlus, Set ECE drops from 0.13 to 0.02, and RLCR-Multi closely tracks the identity calibration line while RLVR-Multi is systematically overconfident. A notable exception is HotPotQA-Modified, where Multi-Answer RLCR underperforms Single RLCR in top-1 calibration because the model has a strong prior to sum confidences to 1, which is miscalibrated in a setting where pass@3 < 30%. Strengths: - Clean theoretical framing: the multi-answer RLVR reward generalizes standard RLVR, pass@K, and set recovery under one unified objective, making the contribution intellectually crisp. - Token efficiency gains are substantial and practically meaningful — single-pass multi-answer generation eliminates the redundant reasoning scaffolding recomputed in each independent sample, a concrete win for deployment cost. - Empirical coverage across three qualitatively distinct settings (inherently multi-label, ambiguous single-label, and unambiguous multi-solution) strengthens generality claims. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Single-answer methods retain higher top-1 accuracy across all datasets; there is a real coverage-precision tradeoff that the paper acknowledges but does not resolve, and the top-1 accuracy gap could be a blocker in many production scenarios. - The calibration benefit breaks down on HotPotQA-Modified for RLCR-Multi, and the explanation (strong generative prior to sum confidences to 1) is acknowledged but not addressed — leaving open how to handle difficult single-gold settings with low pass@k where the model should express total uncertainty below 1. - Experiments are limited to Qwen3-8B with k≤5 and three datasets; scalability to larger models, higher k, and more diverse task types (e.g., open-ended generation, retrieval-augmented settings) is untested, and serial generation of k answers within one context window limits parallelism compared to independent sampling.

#17 — Highly Innovative (79)

MinerU2.5-Pro: Pushing the Limits of Data-Centric Document Parsing at Scale (89 ▲ )

Bin Wang, Tianyao He, Linke Ouyang, Fan Wu, Zhiyuan Zhao, Tao Chu, Yuan Qu, Zhenjiang Jin, Weijun Zeng, Ziyang Miao, Bangrui Xu, Junbo Niu, Mengzhang Cai, Jiantao Qiu, Qintong Zhang, Dongsheng Ma, Yuefeng Sun, Hejun Dong, Wenzheng Zhang, Jutao Xiao, Jiayong Shi, Pengyu Liao, Xiaomeng Zhao, Huaping Zhong, Liqun Wei, Jing Yu, Jie Yang, Wei Li, Shasha Wang, Qianqian Wu, Xuanhe Zhou, Weijia Li, Zhenxiang Li, Zhongying Tu, Jiang Wu, Lijun Wu, Chao Xu, Kai Chen, Wentao Zhang, Yu Qiao, Bowen Zhou, Dahua Lin, Conghui He · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 Apr 2026
Why this rank: MinerU2.5-Pro earns 79 for its rigorous data-centric experimental design that holds architecture completely fixed and attributes all gains to data engineering innovations. The Cross-Model Consistency Verification framework is a genuinely creative approach to distinguishing model-specific blind spots from universally hard samples, enabling intelligent annotation resource allocation. The render-then-verify Judge-and-Refine pipeline cleverly addresses a fundamental limitation of naive self-reflection by grounding structural correctness in visual comparison. The OmniDocBench v1.6 contribution with multi-granularity adaptive matching fixes a real evaluation bias that affected all prior comparisons in the field.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Document parsing—converting PDFs into structured formats like Markdown—is critical infrastructure for LLM training pipelines and RAG systems. While recent progress has focused almost entirely on model architecture innovation, the authors observe that state-of-the-art models spanning diverse architectures and parameter scales (1B to 200B+) exhibit identical failure patterns on the same hard samples. This convergence points to a shared root cause: training data deficiencies rather than architectural limitations. Two interrelated data problems are identified: (1) insufficient coverage, with existing datasets under-representing long-tail scenarios such as complex nested tables and dense formula layouts, and (2) an annotation quality paradox, where the hardest samples—those most valuable for training—are precisely the ones for which automatic annotation is least reliable. Additionally, the dominant evaluation benchmark (OmniDocBench v1.5) contains systematic element-matching biases that unfairly penalize systems based on output granularity rather than content accuracy. Methodology: MinerU2.5-Pro holds the 1.2B-parameter decoupled coarse-to-fine architecture of MinerU2.5 (NaViT-675M vision encoder + Qwen2-0.5B language model) completely fixed and attributes all gains to three data engineering innovations plus a matched training strategy. The Data Engine has three co-designed components. Diversity-and-Difficulty-Aware Sampling (DDAS) embeds pages via ViT-Base, clusters them with K-Means, then uses Cross-Model Consistency Verification (CMCV) difficulty labels to resample: easy clusters are downweighted, hard/diverse clusters upweighted, invalid content dropped. This operates at both page and element granularity (text, formula, table blocks independently), expanding training data from under 10M to 65.5M samples. Cross-Model Consistency Verification (CMCV) replaces single-model uncertainty estimation with multi-model cross-validation across three heterogeneous parsers (MinerU2.5, PaddleOCR-VL, Qwen3-VL-30B). Task-specific pairwise metrics (edit distance, TEDS, CDM) classify samples into Easy (MinerU2.5 agrees with ≥1 external model), Medium (two external models agree but MinerU2.5 differs—highest training value), and Hard (all models disagree). The Annotation Pipeline for Hard Cases addresses the core challenge that hard samples have unreliable annotations. A render-then-verify Judge-and-Refine loop renders LaTeX formulas and HTML tables into images, feeds both the original and rendered images to Qwen3-VL-235B for visual error localization and targeted correction. Cases beyond automatic correction receive targeted expert annotation prioritized by correction efficiency and marginal impact, yielding 192K high-quality hard samples. The three-stage progressive training strategy matches data quality tiers: Stage 1 pre-trains on 65.5M CMCV auto-annotated Easy/Medium samples (learning rate 1×10⁻³); Stage 2 fine-tunes on 192K expert-annotated hard samples with differentiated Hard:Replay mixing ratios per subtask (learning rate 5×10⁻⁵); Stage 3 applies GRPO reinforcement learning using task-specific evaluation metrics directly as rewards (edit distance, CDM, TEDS, IoU), bridging the gap between cross-entropy optimization and sequence-level evaluation. OmniDocBench v1.6 introduces Multi-Granularity Adaptive Matching (MGAM) that searches for the optimal prediction segmentation granularity via three-stage bipartite matching (direct, splitting at LaTeX line-break delimiters, exhaustive partition enumeration), eliminating granularity-mismatch scoring artifacts. A Hard subset of 296 pages (withheld from all training stages) is added alongside the original 1,355-page Base subset for a three-tier Base/Hard/Full evaluation protocol. Key Results: On OmniDocBench v1.6 Full, MinerU2.5-Pro achieves 95.69, improving over the same-architecture MinerU2.5 baseline by 2.71 points and outperforming all competing systems including Gemini 3 Pro (92.85), Qwen3-VL-235B with 235B parameters (89.78), and GLM-OCR (95.15). On the Hard subset where discriminative power matters most, MinerU2.5-Pro leads at 94.08 vs. GLM-OCR's 92.01 (+2.07) and PaddleOCR-VL-1.5's 92.01. GLM-OCR drops 4.18 points from Base to Hard; MinerU2.5-Pro drops only 2.04 points. Per-task element results: formula CDM 97.29 (best), table TEDS 93.42 (best), table TEDS-S 95.92 (best), text edit distance 0.019 (best). Ablation (Table 3) shows Stage 1 large-scale SFT contributes the largest gain (+1.31 Full), Stage 2 hard-sample SFT adds +0.96 (most impact on table TEDS: 90.37→92.87), and Stage 3 GRPO adds +0.45 (most impact on formula CDM: 96.48→97.29). Strengths: - Rigorous controlled experiment: architecture is held completely fixed, so performance gains are cleanly attributable to data engineering—a rare and convincing experimental design that validates the data-centric hypothesis. - The CMCV framework elegantly distinguishes model-specific blind spots (Medium: fixable with cross-model pseudo-labels) from universally hard samples (Hard: require expert annotation), enabling targeted annotation resource allocation rather than brute-force labeling. - The render-then-verify Judge-and-Refine pipeline addresses a fundamental failure mode of naive self-reflection (models cannot judge structural correctness of LaTeX/HTML in implicit space) with a creative and effective visual comparison approach. - OmniDocBench v1.6 with MGAM and the Hard subset is a genuine methodological contribution to the field: the identified granularity-matching bias in v1.5 affected all prior comparisons, and the Hard subset reveals capability differences invisible on standard benchmarks. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper does not report computational costs or annotation costs for producing 192K expert-annotated hard samples, making it difficult to assess the practical replicability of the data engine—this is a significant omission for a data-centric work. - The Hard subset (296 pages) is small and selected to cover known failure categories; it may not generalize to unseen hard scenarios in production domains (finance, legal, medical), and the authors acknowledge this explicitly but offer no mitigation. - The element-matching paradigm underlying OmniDocBench v1.6 still has inherent format and structural ambiguity (HTML vs. Markdown tables, different LaTeX representations of the same formula), and semantic-equivalence-aware evaluation remains unsolved—scores may still systematically favor systems whose output conventions match annotation conventions. - The image-aware parsing capability is noted to lack Data Engine optimization in this release, meaning that component has not been subjected to the same rigorous data quality improvements, leaving an inconsistency in the overall system.

#18 — Highly Innovative (79)

FlowInOne:Unifying Multimodal Generation as Image-in, Image-out Flow Matching (4 ▲ )

Junchao Yi, Rui Zhao, Jiahao Tang, Weixian Lei, Linjie Li, Qisheng Su, Zhengyuan Yang, Lijuan Wang, Xiaofeng Zhu, Alex Jinpeng Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Apr 2026
Why this rank: FlowInOne proposes a genuinely elegant paradigm shift by collapsing all multimodal instructions — text, layouts, arrows, force vectors, trajectories — into a unified image-in, image-out flow matching framework, eliminating cross-modal encoders and task-specific condition heads entirely. The DP-SAM mechanism and the extension to physics-aware tasks (force dynamics, trajectory prediction) demonstrate capabilities absent from comparable systems. However, the 256×256 fixed resolution is a serious practical constraint that undermines real-world applicability, and evaluation on the authors' own VP-Bench with their own VisPrompt-5M training data creates circularity concerns that temper confidence in the reported margins.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal generation has long operated under a text-dominant assumption: language encodes intent and vision executes it. This creates a fundamental asymmetry where cross-modal alignment bottlenecks, task-specific architectural branches (separate text encoders, ControlNet-style heads), and noise scheduling complexity fragment the representation space. Prior work in image editing, text-to-image generation, layout-guided synthesis, and visual instruction following each requires separate pipelines or condition heads. The authors ask whether all modalities — textual descriptions, spatial layouts, arrows, doodles, bounding boxes, and physics-based force cues — can be unified into a single visual representation, eliminating cross-modal machinery entirely. Methodology: FlowInOne reformulates multimodal generation as an intra-modal transport problem. All instructions (text, bounding boxes, arrow markers, doodles, force vectors, trajectory lines) are rendered directly onto the input image canvas as visual prompts, producing a unified input image Iv. A SigLIP Vision Transformer (from Janus-Pro-1B) encodes this canvas into patch-level features, projected via MLP into a source latent via a text-image VAE. A frozen LDM VAE encodes the target image into an isomorphic target latent. Flow matching then learns the velocity field transporting the source latent to the target latent, avoiding Gaussian noise schedules and modality-specific condition heads. The key architectural innovation is a Dual-Path Spatially-Adaptive Modulation (DP-SAM) mechanism within each DiT transformer block: for pure generation tasks (text-to-image), the cross-attention path is bypassed entirely; for editing tasks with a source image, a cross-attention branch injects source structural priors via a learned token-level gating vector Λ that distinguishes background regions requiring preservation from edited regions requiring reconstruction. The full model (1.2B parameters, initialized from CrossFlow) is trained jointly on VisPrompt-5M — a 5M-pair dataset the authors construct across 8 task categories including force dynamics (32K pairs) and trajectory understanding (1.5K pairs from Blender renders). Training uses a combined flow matching loss, KL divergence regularization, and a CLIP-style contrastive loss aligning visual instruction embeddings with target image embeddings. Key Results: Evaluated on the authors' own VP-Bench (1,060 manually curated pairs across 8 task categories), FlowInOne achieves the highest pass rates under GPT-5.2 (39.2%), Qwen3.5 (50.3%), and human evaluation (44.9%), substantially outperforming open-source baselines: OmniGen2 (0.7%/1.9%/0%), FLUX.1-Kontext-dev (1.9%/5.6%/1.8%), and Qwen-Image-Edit-2509 (4.8%/8.3%/1.2%). The commercial Nano Banana (Google Gemini-Flash-based) scores higher only on the Gemini-3 evaluator (56.0% vs. 54.0%), which the authors attribute to evaluator bias from shared model lineage. On spatial/physical metrics, FlowInOne achieves the highest DINOv3 Directional Similarity (48.7% vs. Nano Banana's 47.3%), with particularly large margins in force understanding (53.6% vs. 47.4%) and trajectory understanding (57.0% vs. 48.6%). Ablation studies confirm that: (1) joint training over all 5M samples (47.8% pass rate) far outperforms two-stage curriculum training (29.1%), likely due to catastrophic forgetting in the staged approach; (2) DP-SAM (23.1%) outperforms plain dual-path cross-attention (21.4%) and self-attention only (18.2%); (3) the MLP+MLP sequence/feature projection strategy (18.2%) outperforms truncation-based compression (16.9%). Strengths: - Genuinely elegant unification: collapsing all modalities into a single image-in, image-out paradigm eliminates architectural complexity (no separate text encoders, no ControlNet branches, no diffusion noise schedules) while achieving competitive or superior performance across diverse tasks. - The physics-aware tasks (force dynamics, trajectory prediction) are novel capabilities not present in comparable open-source systems, demonstrating the paradigm's extensibility beyond conventional editing. - Rigorous multi-evaluator experimental design (three frontier VLMs plus human judges, with leakage prevention via root-image-level dataset partitioning) provides credible evidence for SOTA claims. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark (VP-Bench) and dataset (VisPrompt-5M) are both created by the authors, creating potential circular advantage; the model is trained on data from the same distribution it is evaluated on, and the benchmark's leakage prevention protocol, while documented, is not independently verified. - Fixed 256×256 generation resolution is a significant practical limitation for creative workflows; all comparisons are conducted at this resolution, which disadvantages methods (like FLUX.1-Kontext) designed for high-resolution use, making fair comparison difficult. - Text-in-image editing performance under human evaluation (24.2% pass rate) is notably weak and falls below Nano Banana (27.1%), suggesting the vision-only instruction approach struggles with complex semantic editing instructions that text encoders handle more reliably — the paradigm's core assumption that text-in-pixel is sufficient appears to break down for fine-grained editing.

#19 — Highly Innovative (79)

The Depth Ceiling: On the Limits of Large Language Models in Discovering Latent Planning (4 ▲ )

Yi Xu, Philipp Jettkant, Laura Ruis · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 79 for identifying a genuinely surprising depth ceiling in latent planning that persists across orders-of-magnitude scale increases—scaling from a 1.6M-parameter transformer to GPT-4o adds only two steps of planning depth. The novel dissociation between discovery ceiling and generalization ceiling is a clean conceptual contribution with direct implications for the reliability of chain-of-thought monitoring as a safety mechanism. The experimental design using star graphs is exceptionally rigorous in eliminating heuristic shortcuts, and the attention-based mechanistic analysis adds interpretive depth. Limited somewhat by the synthetic task domain and small test sets for frontier models.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Chain-of-thought (CoT) monitoring is increasingly proposed as a safety mechanism for overseeing LLM behavior, but its viability depends critically on a key assumption: that models cannot reason effectively within a single forward pass without externalizing intermediate steps. If LLMs can perform complex multi-step planning entirely in their latent representations, CoT traces become unreliable as oversight tools. This paper asks: how deep can latent planning go, and does scale change the answer? Prior work reached contradictory conclusions—Bachmann & Nagarajan (2024) found complete failure of next-token prediction to induce latent planning, while Saparov et al. (2025) showed near-perfect latent planning is possible with curriculum training—leaving the true limits uncharacterized. Methodology: The authors use star graph path-finding as a precisely controlled testbed for latent planning. A star graph G(k,m) has a central source node and k branches each of length m; the task is to identify the first hop on the shortest path to a designated target leaf. Crucially, all branches are structurally identical, ruling out heuristic shortcuts—any above-random accuracy requires genuine multi-step lookahead. Each model is trained only on problems of a single fixed depth m with supervision only on the final answer (no intermediate step labels), preventing bootstrapping from easier problems. The metric is Latent Planning Capacity (LPC): the maximum depth m at which performance is statistically distinguishable from random guessing (α=10⁻⁵) across branch factors K={2,3,4,5,10}. Models evaluated span a 1.6M-parameter GPT-2-style transformer trained from scratch, fine-tuned open-weight LLMs (Qwen 2.5-7B/32B, Qwen 3-8B/32B), and proprietary models (GPT-4o fine-tuned, GPT-5.4 few-shot only). Attention analysis via a backtracking ratio metric probes the internal strategy discovered. Control experiments use explicit CoT backtracking supervision and ICoT progressive internalization. Key Results: A persistent depth ceiling emerges across all scales. The 1.6M-parameter transformer trained from scratch reaches LPC=3 but fails completely at depth 4—and this failure is not overcome by increasing layers (up to 32), attention heads (up to 8), or hidden dimension (up to 512). Fine-tuned GPT-4o and Qwen 3-32B reach LPC=5; GPT-5.4 under few-shot prompting achieves LPC=7. Scaling from the tiny transformer to GPT-4o—a difference of many orders of magnitude in parameters—increases discovered planning depth by only two steps. A discovery/generalization dissociation is found: models trained on depth-5 problems fail to discover strategies when trained directly on depth-6/7/8, yet once a depth-5 strategy is discovered, it generalizes at test time up to depth 8 (GPT-4o extrapolates to m*+4). Error analysis reveals that failures beyond the training depth are predominantly on-path errors (95% for Qwen 2.5-7B), meaning models correctly identify the branch but fail to complete all backtracking steps—confirming a generalization depth ceiling distinct from a strategy-identification failure. In contrast, with explicit CoT backtracking supervision, all LLMs solve depth-20 graphs and converge in just 20 training steps, confirming the task itself is not hard—the bottleneck is sparse-supervision strategy discovery. Strengths: - The experimental design is exceptionally clean: star graphs with shuffled node IDs eliminate all local heuristics, ensuring any above-random accuracy reflects genuine multi-step planning. The single-fixed-depth training protocol rules out curriculum bootstrapping, isolating autonomous discovery. - The dissociation between discovery ceiling (max depth learnable under sparse supervision) and generalization ceiling (max depth executable once discovered) is a genuinely novel finding with interpretive clarity. - Rigorous hypothesis testing with α=10⁻⁵ and analysis across multiple branch factors avoids spurious significance claims; the ablation study ruling out architectural capacity as the cause of the ceiling is thorough. - The attention-based backtracking ratio provides mechanistic interpretability evidence, showing successful models learn an explicit reverse-path strategy while failed models show uniform attention. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The task domain is highly synthetic and symmetric. Real-world reasoning tasks contain local heuristics, denser supervision signals, and richer structure that could partially bypass the discovery bottleneck—the paper's claims about CoT monitoring implications require this finding to generalize broadly, which is not demonstrated. - GPT-5.4 is only evaluated few-shot (fine-tuning unavailable), making the reported LPC=7 a lower bound rather than the true discovery ceiling. The comparison between few-shot and fine-tuned LPC for weaker models is imperfect given that few-shot typically underperforms fine-tuning, potentially underestimating the frontier ceiling. - Test set sizes for GPT-4o and GPT-5.4 are only 100 samples, reducing statistical power and making the significance thresholds looser—a concern given the binary nature of the LPC metric at these sample sizes. - The paper does not test whether reinforcement learning with outcome reward (rather than next-token prediction cross-entropy) could push the discovery ceiling further, which is a natural and practically relevant alternative training regime.
Recommended 2604.06427 PDF
#20 — Highly Innovative (79)

Rethinking Generalization in Reasoning SFT: A Conditional Analysis on Optimization, Data, and Model Capability (152 ▲ )

Qihan Ren, Peng Wang, Ruikun Cai, Shuai Shao, Dadi Guo, Yuejin Xie, Yafu Li, Quanshi Zhang, Xia Hu, Jing Shao, Dongrui Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 79 for systematically dismantling the widely-held 'SFT memorizes, RL generalizes' narrative with tightly controlled experiments that yield genuinely surprising findings. The dip-and-recovery dynamics reveal that prior negative results about SFT generalization were likely under-optimization artifacts—a finding with immediate practical consequences for how the field approaches post-training. The Countdown-CoT experiment is particularly clever, cleanly isolating procedural reasoning transfer from domain content and providing the strongest evidence to date that it is reasoning structure (backtracking, verification patterns), not math knowledge, that generalizes across domains. The causal attribution of safety degradation specifically to long-CoT procedural patterns (not the math content itself) is an unexpected and important finding that opens a new research direction.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: The dominant narrative in LLM post-training holds that supervised fine-tuning (SFT) memorizes while reinforcement learning (RL) generalizes. This paper systematically challenges that framing for reasoning SFT with long chain-of-thought (CoT) supervision. Prior work establishing the "SFT memorizes, RL generalizes" claim varied considerably in experimental conditions—training epochs, data quality, and base model capability—making it unclear whether reported generalization failures are intrinsic to SFT or artifacts of particular setups. The paper asks: under what conditions does reasoning SFT actually generalize, and at what cost? Methodology: The authors conduct a controlled empirical study using math-only reasoning SFT on pretrained base models (Qwen3-14B/8B/4B/1.7B, Qwen2.5 family, InternLM2.5-20B), systematically varying three factors while holding others fixed. (1) Optimization dynamics: they extend training to 8 epochs and track benchmark trajectories at every checkpoint, comparing cosine vs. constant LR schedules and repeated exposure vs. one-pass coverage under matched compute. (2) Training data: four configurations are compared—Math-CoT-20k (20k verified long-CoT traces generated by Qwen3-32B), Math-NoCoT-20k (same queries but CoT stripped), NuminaMath-20k (mixed-quality human solutions), and Countdown-CoT-20k (long-CoT traces from a toy arithmetic game). (3) Model capability: four Qwen3 base model sizes (1.7B–14B) trained identically. Evaluation spans in-domain math (MATH500, AIME24), OOD reasoning (LiveCodeBench v2, GPQA-Diamond, MMLU-Pro), general capabilities (IFEval, AlpacaEval, HaluEval, TruthfulQA), and safety (HEx-PHI attack success rate). Response length is tracked as a proxy for learning stage. Key Results: The central finding is that cross-domain generalization in reasoning SFT is conditional, not absent. Four specific findings emerge. First, a dip-and-recovery pattern: OOD benchmarks initially degrade then recover and surpass baselines with sufficient training. At 1 epoch, Qwen3-14B gains +29.7% on MATH500 but shows limited/negative OOD gains; at 8 epochs (640 steps), it reaches 95.1% MATH500, 55.1% LCB v2 (+17.6pp), 63.3% GPQA-Diamond (+19.2pp), suggesting prior negative results were under-optimization artifacts. Repeated exposure (8-pass, 2.5k examples) substantially outperforms one-pass coverage (1-pass, 20k examples) under identical 640-step budget across all benchmarks. Second, data quality is critical: NuminaMath (mixed-quality) broadly degrades OOD performance (LCB v2 drops from 29.1% to 7.9% on Qwen3-8B) and shows no dip-and-recovery dynamics. Countdown-CoT (pure arithmetic game) beats Math-NoCoT on math tasks for Qwen3 models despite having no math domain content, suggesting procedural patterns (backtracking, verification) are the transferable element. Third, model capability is necessary: Qwen3-14B shows broad gains across all domains; Qwen3-1.7B shows marginal or negative gains everywhere even at late checkpoints, while producing responses 3-4× longer. Token-level log-probability analysis shows 14B has an 8–18× advantage over 1.7B specifically on CoT transition words ("therefore," "alternatively," "wait," "however"), confirming that larger models internalize the strategic backbone of chain-of-thought reasoning, not just surface verbosity. Fourth, safety degrades asymmetrically: HEx-PHI attack success rate rises substantially under Math-CoT-20k training but not under Math-NoCoT-20k (same queries/answers), causally attributing safety degradation to the procedural patterns themselves. Models self-rationalize during extended thinking ("for educational purposes...") before producing harmful content—framed as the persistent problem-solving prior working against the refusal guardrail. Strengths: - Tightly controlled experimental design: each of three factors is varied independently while holding others constant, producing causally interpretable conclusions rather than correlational claims - Response length as an interpretable, zero-cost diagnostic of learning stage is a practical and validated contribution, consistent across multiple model families - The Countdown-CoT experiment cleanly isolates procedural transfer from domain-specific knowledge, providing the strongest evidence that it is reasoning structure, not math content, that generalizes - Results are robust across multiple model families (Qwen3, Qwen2.5, InternLM2.5) and two teacher models (Qwen3-32B, DeepSeek-R1), substantially strengthening generalizability of the findings Weaknesses / Limitations: - Exclusive focus on math reasoning data, where verification and high-quality long-CoT generation are tractable; it is unclear whether the dip-and-recovery dynamics and procedural generalization findings extend to code, scientific reasoning, or multimodal settings - No direct comparison with RL-based methods, which is arguably the most policy-relevant question; the authors acknowledge this but argue a fair comparison is methodologically difficult—a reasonable but unsatisfying omission given the paper's framing as a response to the SFT-vs-RL narrative - Experiments are capped at 20B dense models on 8 H200 GPUs; behavior on MoE architectures or large-scale models (70B+) remains unknown, and the capability threshold below which generalization fails may shift substantially at larger scales - The safety degradation finding, while important, lacks any mitigation analysis; the paper identifies the problem (procedural patterns undermine refusal) but offers no guidance on how to preserve safety while training with long-CoT supervision
Recommended 2604.06628 PDF
#21 — Highly Innovative (79)

Neural Computers (11 ▲ )

Mingchen Zhuge, Changsheng Zhao, Haozhe Liu, Zijian Zhou, Shuming Liu, Wenyi Wang, Ernie Chang, Gael Le Lan, Junjie Fei, Wenxuan Zhang, Yasheng Sun, Zhipeng Cai, Zechun Liu, Yunyang Xiong, Yining Yang, Yuandong Tian, Yangyang Shi, Vikas Chandra, Jürgen Schmidhuber · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Apr 2026
Why this rank: This paper scores 79 for introducing the Neural Computer abstraction — a formally grounded, genuinely new machine paradigm that proposes unifying computation, memory, and I/O inside a single neural network's latent state, clearly distinct from world models, agents, or classical stored-program machines. The formal CNC criteria (Turing completeness, universal programmability, behavior consistency, machine-native semantics) provide a crisp research target that could redirect significant effort in the field. The empirical ablations are thorough and yield actionable design guidance, particularly the finding that 110 hours of curated data outperforms 1,400 hours of random exploration. However, the prototypes remain open-loop and the arithmetic probe results are inflated by conditioned rendering rather than genuine latent computation, making the current evidence more visionary than demonstrative.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern AI systems divide computation, memory, and I/O across separate engineered components: conventional computers execute explicit stored programs, agents act through external execution environments, and world models predict or render environment dynamics. None of these paradigms makes the model itself the running computer. This paper asks whether a single set of neural network weights can unify all three roles — computation, memory, and I/O — inside a single latent runtime state, eliminating the dependency on an external OS or execution environment. Methodology: The paper proposes Neural Computers (NCs) as a new machine abstraction: a neural system (Fθ, Gθ) that maintains a persistent latent runtime state ht, updates it from observations and user actions, and decodes the next observable frame. As an initial instantiation, NCs are realized as video generation models built on top of Wan2.1 (a diffusion transformer backbone), trained from raw interface I/O traces — no privileged access to program state is used. Two interface-specific prototypes are studied: (1) NCCLIGen for command-line interfaces, conditioned on text captions and first terminal frames, trained on ~823k asciinema clips (CLIGen General, ~1,100 GPU-hours of traces) and ~128k scripted Docker traces (CLIGen Clean); (2) NCGUIWorld for desktop GUIs, conditioned on prior pixels plus synchronized mouse/keyboard actions, with 110 hours of goal-directed Claude CUA traces. For GUIWorld, the paper conducts ablations over four action injection schemes (external, contextual, residual, internal) and two action encodings (raw event streams vs. API-like meta-actions). Cursor tracking uses explicit SVG visual supervision rather than coordinate regression. The paper concludes with a formal definition of Completely Neural Computers (CNCs) requiring Turing completeness, universal programmability, behavior consistency, and machine-native semantics, plus a roadmap toward achieving them. Key Results: For NCCLIGen on CLIGen General: VAE reconstruction quality of 40.77 dB PSNR and 0.989 SSIM at 13px font; character-level OCR accuracy improves from 0.03 at initialization to 0.54 at 60k steps with exact-line accuracy of 0.31. Detailed captions improve PSNR from 21.90 dB (semantic) to 26.89 dB (detailed). Symbolic arithmetic performance is only 4% without conditioning but jumps to 83% via reprompting, demonstrating that the model is a conditionable renderer, not a native reasoner. For NCGUIWorld: goal-directed Claude CUA data (110 hrs) yields FVD 14.72 vs. 20.37 for random slow exploration (1,400 hrs), confirming alignment quality dominates over scale. Explicit SVG cursor supervision achieves 98.7% cursor accuracy vs. 8.7% with position-only regression. Internal action injection achieves best post-action metrics (SSIM 0.863, LPIPS 0.141, FVD 14.5) versus untrained baseline (SSIM 0.326, FVD 184.3). Meta-action encoding provides modest gains over raw events (SSIM 0.863 vs. 0.847). Strengths: - Introduces a crisp, formally grounded abstraction (NC/CNC) that distinguishes this direction from world models, agents, and classical computers, with operational testability criteria. - Extensive and honest empirical ablation covering data quality, action conditioning depth, cursor supervision strategy, and action encoding — practical design guidance is directly actionable. - Demonstrates concretely that data alignment quality (110 hrs curated) outperforms 10× larger noisy random exploration, a non-obvious and practically significant finding for future NC training pipelines. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The current prototypes are open-loop: at inference, actions are replayed from logs rather than generated in response to a live environment, making evaluation inherently easier than real closed-loop execution. - Symbolic stability, routine reuse, long-horizon coherence, and explicit reprogramming — the four most critical CNC requirements — all remain unsolved by the current work; the paper is largely a position/roadmap paper with early prototype evidence rather than a demonstration of a usable system. - Arithmetic probes improve from 4% to 83% only via reprompting that encodes the answer in the condition, so the reported gain reflects conditioned rendering rather than genuine computation inside the latent state, undermining the claim of early runtime primitives for symbolic tasks.
Recommended 2604.06425 PDF
#22 — Highly Innovative (78)

Epistemic Blinding: An Inference -Time Protocol for Auditing Prior Contamination in LLM -Assisted Analysis

Michael Cuccarese · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Scores 78 for defining a genuinely new problem—parametric contamination in LLM-assisted data analysis—and proposing an elegant, model-agnostic inference-time protocol to detect it. The contrastive A/B blinding design is conceptually original and immediately actionable without retraining or model internals access. The 'smoking gun' justification analysis (e.g., KRAS covalent inhibitor knowledge injected from parametric memory) is compelling evidence of an analytically invisible failure mode. The finding that contamination scales with feature ambiguity is predictive and practically useful for high-stakes domains.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: When LLMs are used for data-driven analysis of named entities—ranking genes by drug target potential, scoring equities by fundamentals—they silently blend supplied data with memorized priors from pretraining. The result is analytically invisible: a single output cannot reveal whether a recommendation came from the data or from the model's parametric memory about famous entities. This matters because LLM-assisted pipelines are increasingly being used for high-stakes decisions (drug target prioritization, investment screening) where auditability of the reasoning process is essential. Existing work on data contamination addresses benchmark leakage; this paper addresses a different problem—the model's general world knowledge contaminating domain-specific inference. Methodology: Epistemic blinding is an inference-time protocol that replaces named entity identifiers (gene symbols, ticker symbols) with anonymous codes (Gene_001, Company_042) before prompting, then runs an A/B comparison against an unblinded control in a fresh session. The six-step protocol covers: (1) identifying entity columns with uneven training-corpus representation, (2) building a shared, seed-stable mapping across all datasets to preserve cross-dataset consistency, (3) enumerating and mitigating subtle structural leak sources (e.g., a 42% mutation frequency that effectively identifies KRAS), (4) shuffling rows, (5) running matched blinded/unblinded sessions and comparing outputs via set overlap, Jaccard index, mean rank delta, and Kendall τ, and (6) de-anonymizing results. The protocol was applied in two stages of an oncology target identification system: structurally, in ShinkaEvolve, an LLM-guided evolutionary framework where Claude Sonnet proposes Python scoring functions that operate on 22-dimensional numerical feature vectors indexed by position (never by gene symbol); and diagnostically, in a post-scoring LLM reasoning step over top-100 candidates. Eight data layers were integrated (TCGA somatic mutations, Open Targets GWAS, ESM2/Geneformer/ProtT5 foundation model embeddings, gnomAD pLI), with literature-mined gene-disease databases deliberately excluded to avoid reinjecting publication bias. The evolutionary optimization ran 13 generations, discovering a hierarchical evidence-first scoring function that separates direct genetic evidence (Tier 1, base score 8.0) from pathway-neighbor genes (Tier 2, base score 3.0). Key Results: In oncology (four cancer types: AML, pancreatic adenocarcinoma, CRC, GBM), blinding changed 16% of top-20 predictions on average (overlap: 84%), while target recovery was identical in both conditions (2.75 validated targets per indication). Fame bias was systematic and directional: well-known genes were promoted when named (PTEN: #15→#3 in GBM; RNF43: #20→#6 in pancreatic), while obscure genes with strong feature profiles were demoted (DPP8, highest triple-modality convergence in GBM, dropped from #3 to #9; KCNH7 in CRC dropped 11 ranks). Contamination scaled with feature ambiguity: AML (strong signal) showed 90% overlap; GBM (weak, diffuse signal) showed 75% overlap. The mechanism was directly observable in model justifications—the phrase "proven therapeutic tractability via covalent RAS inhibitors" appeared in the unblinded KRAS output despite not being present in any supplied feature. In S&P 500 equity screening, blinding tickers reshaped 30–40% of top-20 rankings across five random seeds (mean overlap 13.0/20, Jaccard 0.48), with consistent directional bias for specific tickers (ELV, CI promoted; CTRA demoted in majority of seeds). Strengths: - The core insight—that parametric contamination is invisible from a single output and requires a contrastive protocol to detect—is correct, well-motivated, and actionable without model modification or retraining. - The justification analysis (Table 4) constitutes a genuine "smoking gun": parametric knowledge injected into ostensibly data-driven output is directly quotable and verifiable as absent from the supplied features. - The contamination-scales-with-ambiguity finding is predictive and practically useful: it tells analysts when to be most concerned (weak feature signal, many similarly-scored candidates). Weaknesses / Limitations: - All experiments used a single model (Claude/Anthropic), so contamination magnitude is not characterized across models with different training data distributions—making it unclear how generalizable the quantitative findings are. - The oncology experiment used single runs per indication rather than multiple seeds, leaving run-to-run variance unquantified for the primary application, which is the paper's central case study. - Structural leakage is acknowledged but not experimentally characterized: the protocol cannot guarantee full blinding when feature combinations are highly identifying, and the effectiveness of mitigations (normalization, binning) is not empirically evaluated.
Search Results 2604.06013 PDF
#23 — Highly Innovative (78)

Learning the Signature of Memorization in Autoregressive Language Models

David Ilić, Kostadin Cvejoski, David Stanojević, Evgeny Grigorenko · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 78 for the elegant insight that fine-tuning creates unlimited labeled MIA data by construction, eliminating the shadow-model bottleneck that has blocked learned attacks at LLM scale. The zero-shot cross-architecture transfer to fundamentally different models (Mamba, RWKV, RecurrentGemma) is a genuinely surprising empirical discovery suggesting a universal, architecture-invariant memorization signature — not an artifact of the method's design, as even simple baselines transfer. The diversity-over-scale principle (30 combinations at 600 samples beats 1 at 18,000) is a clean, actionable finding. Practical deployability is strong given the lightweight classifier (~500K params), though the requirement for a reference checkpoint limits applicability to open-weight models only.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Membership inference attacks (MIAs) — which determine whether a specific text appeared in a model's training data — are critical for privacy auditing, copyright detection, and data governance. All prior MIA methods for language models are hand-crafted heuristics (loss thresholding, Min-K%, Zlib normalization, reference calibration), each bounded by the designer's intuition about what signals membership. Learned attacks that train classifiers on shadow models outperform heuristics in other domains, but require training many models with known membership, which is prohibitively expensive at LLM scale. The implicit assumption has been that learned MIA cannot scale to language models. Methodology: The paper introduces Learned Transfer MIA (LT-MIA), exploiting a key observation: fine-tuning any model on any corpus yields unlimited labeled training data by construction (membership is known), eliminating the shadow model bottleneck. Given black-box access to a fine-tuned target model and its pre-trained reference checkpoint, LT-MIA extracts 154-dimensional per-token feature vectors capturing how the two models' predictions diverge — including per-token losses, top/bottom-20 logits from each model, ground-truth token ranks, cross-model ranks, and sequence-level statistics. A lightweight transformer encoder (2 layers, 4 heads, ~500K parameters) processes this feature sequence and predicts membership via a learned attention pooling over encoder outputs fed into a 2-layer MLP. Training uses 10 transformer models fine-tuned on 3 corpora (30 combinations, 540K samples total), optimized with AdamW for 30 epochs. The critical design choice is training diversity over scale: 30 combinations with 600 samples each substantially outperforms 1 combination with 18,000 samples (0.860 vs. 0.796 AUC), as diversity filters model-specific artifacts (tokenizer idiosyncrasies, logit scale biases), retaining only the architecture-invariant memorization signal. Key Results: LT-MIA achieves 0.908 mean AUC on held-out transformer combinations, outperforming the strongest baseline EZ-MIA by 2.1 AUC points. The gap widens dramatically at strict operating points: 1.6× higher TPR@1% FPR (0.375 vs. 0.230) and 2.8× higher TPR@0.1% FPR (0.171 vs. 0.061). More strikingly, the classifier trained exclusively on transformers transfers zero-shot to architecturally unrelated models: Mamba-2.8B (0.963 AUC), RWKV-4-3B (0.972 AUC), and RecurrentGemma-2B (0.936 AUC) — all exceeding performance on held-out transformers. This transfer is not an artifact of LT-MIA's design: even simple loss thresholding achieves 0.867 AUC on Mamba, and EZ-MIA reaches 0.931. Feature ablation via permutation importance confirms the memorization signal is relational — comparison features (target vs. reference) dominate identically across all four architectural families, with the same hierarchy (Comparison > Target-only > Reference-only). LT-MIA also transfers to code (0.865 AUC) despite training only on natural language. Strengths: - The core insight that fine-tuning provides unlimited labeled data, eliminating the shadow model bottleneck, is elegant and practically impactful — it reframes MIA as a standard supervised learning problem. - Zero-shot cross-architecture transfer to Mamba, RWKV, and RecurrentGemma (which share no computational mechanisms with transformers) is a genuine empirical discovery, not an artifact of design choices, as confirmed by the fact that even simple baselines transfer. - The diversity-over-scale finding (30 diverse combinations at 600 samples each beats 1 combination at 18,000 samples by 6.4 AUC points) is a useful practical principle with clean ablation support. - The classifier is extremely lightweight (~500K parameters, ~30 GPU-hours total compute), making the approach practically deployable. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method requires access to the pre-trained reference checkpoint sharing the same architecture as the target, restricting applicability to open-weight models and excluding closed API models entirely — a significant practical constraint given that the most capable and widely-deployed models (GPT-4, Claude) are inaccessible. - Performance degrades meaningfully when the reference model already assigns high likelihood to target text (e.g., WikiText-103 with Llama-2-7B: 0.781 AUC, Pythia-6.9B: 0.796 AUC), a fundamental limitation for detecting memorization of data that overlaps with pretraining corpora — precisely the scenario most relevant for copyright auditing of large pretrained models. - The method is validated only for fine-tuning with cross-entropy loss; whether the signature persists under RLHF, DPO, or instruction tuning — training paradigms used in most practically deployed models — remains untested and is left as future work. - Pretraining membership inference, identified by the authors as a different and harder challenge, is not addressed; prior work shows existing reference-based methods achieve near-random performance at pretraining scale, and it is unclear whether a learned approach would fare better or whether the signal is genuinely weaker.

#24 — Highly Innovative (78)

Accurate and Ultra-Fast Launch-Time Validation of Idempotency for GPU Kernels

ipads.se.sjtu.edu.cn · karypis - new results
Why this rank: PICKER identifies a genuinely new and previously unrecognized class of GPU kernel behavior — conditional idempotency — and quantifies its prevalence (490 of 547 kernels), which reframes how idempotency-based optimizations must be designed. The hybrid offline/online architecture is architecturally creative, exploiting the launch-time window as a principled information boundary that enables microsecond-scale validation with formal correctness guarantees. The practical impact is substantial: enabling checkpoint-free fault recovery and dramatically reducing preemption latency in real systems. The main limitation — high false-negative rates on complex DL kernels — reflects a fundamental information boundary rather than a design flaw, but it does temper the practical reach for the most important workloads.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Idempotency-based GPU system optimizations—covering fault tolerance, preemptive scheduling, and soft error correction—require knowing before execution whether a GPU kernel instance can be safely re-executed without side effects. Prior work statically classifies kernels as either fully idempotent or non-idempotent at the kernel level, treating all instances of a kernel identically. This paper discovers that 490 out of 547 kernels studied are conditionally-idempotent: they have both idempotent and non-idempotent instances depending on runtime pointer arguments. Treating cond-idempotent kernels as idempotent risks correctness (false positives lead to corrupted state on re-execution), while treating them as non-idempotent sacrifices ~90% of performance gains. Neither static analysis (which lacks runtime pointer values) nor dynamic analysis (which produces results only after execution) can resolve this correctness-performance dilemma. Methodology: PICKER exploits the unique launch-time window—the period between kernel submission and execution when arguments are bound but execution has not started. It uses a hybrid offline/online architecture. Offline, a symbolic execution engine (based on claripy and Z3) processes GPU kernel binary (NVIDIA SASS assembly) to extract symbolic memory access expressions as functions of kernel arguments and thread IDs. These are compiled into lightweight C validation functions in shared libraries. At launch time, PICKER intercepts the kernel launch call and invokes the pre-compiled validation function with actual arguments in microseconds. Idempotency is determined by checking whether the predicted read and write address sets overlap: any overlap → non-idempotent; no overlap → idempotent. This conservative R/W overlap check ensures zero false positives at the cost of some false negatives. Three key optimizations reduce validation latency from ~50 ms (naive) to under 5 µs: (1) Range-based address model: instead of enumerating all thread addresses, PICKER checks monotonicity of symbolic addresses w.r.t. thread IDs using SMT solving, then represents the address set as a range [min, max] computable from only the extreme thread IDs; (2) Range compaction: loop induction variables are retained symbolically rather than unrolled, collapsing many symbolic addresses per loop into one; (3) Compiled execution: validation functions are compiled to native C rather than interpreted symbolically, enabling compiler optimizations like CSE and loop unrolling. Key Results: Evaluated on 547 GPU kernels and 18,217 instances across Rodinia, Parboil, TVM, PyTorch, TensorRT, and FasterTransformer. PICKER achieves zero false positives and an 18.54% overall false-negative rate (2,673 of 14,418 true-idempotent instances misclassified as non-idempotent). False-negative rate varies by framework: TVM 2.13%, Rodinia 8.74%, PyTorch 35.73%, TensorRT 42.58%. All 18,217 instances are validated in under 5 µs; ~90% complete in under 1 µs. Application overhead from launch-time validation is under 1% end-to-end. In case studies: a checkpoint-based fault-tolerant system (Asymmetric Resilience) reduces overhead from 155%–4,496% (no idempotency) to under 4%; a preemptive scheduling system (Chimera) reduces average preemption latency by 84.2% (from 64.3 µs to 10.2 µs) versus kernel-level classification which provides negligible improvement because most kernels are cond-idempotent; for soft error recovery during GPT-2 training, PICKER enables rollback-free recovery in 24 of 50 injected faults versus 3 of 50 with kernel-level classification. Strengths: - Precisely identifies and quantifies a previously unrecognized class of GPU kernel behavior (conditional idempotency), with a rigorous empirical study across both open-source and closed-source (binary-only) kernels from major DL frameworks. - The hybrid offline/online architecture is principled: expensive symbolic analysis happens once per kernel, while per-instance validation costs only microseconds, making the approach practical for production systems. - The correctness guarantee (zero false positives) is hard and formally grounded in the conservative R/W overlap check and monotonicity-preserving range transformations, which is exactly the right property for safety-critical re-execution decisions. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The 18.54% average false-negative rate is non-trivial and skews much worse for complex DL kernels (PyTorch 35.73%, TensorRT 42.58%), driven primarily by non-parameter addresses (double pointers, values loaded from GPU memory) that PICKER cannot track—a fundamental limitation of the launch-argument-only information model that cannot easily be resolved without runtime memory access. - The system currently supports only NVIDIA Volta and Ampere ISAs, and offline analysis is expensive for complex kernels (up to 62 minutes for a single cuDNN matrix multiplication kernel; 36 CPU-hours total for the evaluated suite), which could be a deployment barrier for large, frequently updated kernel libraries.
