#25 — Highly Innovative (76)

LightThinker++: From Reasoning Compression to Memory Management (24 ▲ )

Yuqi Zhu, Jintian Zhang, Zhenjie Wan, Yujie Luo, Shuofei Qiao, Zhengke Gui, Da Zheng, Lei Liang, Huajun Chen, Ningyu Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 Apr 2026
Why this rank: The explicit commit/expand/fold memory primitive design is a creative contribution that cleanly decouples reasoning depth from context growth while preserving lossless backtracking — a genuine limitation of prior implicit compression approaches. The environment-aware trajectory synthesis pipeline, where compression state is enforced during teacher generation, is methodologically rigorous and often neglected in similar work. Strong results across both reasoning benchmarks (+5.73 on GPQA) and long-horizon agentic tasks (3.08× Pass@1 on hard instances) validate the approach's breadth. The 44.5% trajectory filtering rate and dependence on a strong proprietary teacher temper the novelty somewhat.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large language models employing chain-of-thought and o1-style slow thinking achieve strong reasoning performance but at severe computational cost: Transformer attention complexity grows quadratically with context length, and KV cache memory grows linearly. For Qwen-32B, context lengths of 10^4 tokens require KV cache storage comparable to the model itself. Existing mitigation approaches either require inference-time intervention with high latency (H2O, SepLLM) or use training-based compression that irreversibly discards intermediate details, creating logical bottlenecks in complex tasks. Long-horizon agentic tasks compound the problem further as context continuously accumulates across dozens or hundreds of interaction rounds. The paper addresses how to dynamically manage reasoning context to reduce memory and compute overhead without sacrificing reasoning fidelity. Methodology: The paper presents two complementary frameworks. LightThinker achieves implicit, representation-level compression: after each thought segment, the model condenses it into a small number of gist tokens (cache tokens) via a custom thought-based attention mask that lets gist tokens attend to the question, prior compressed content, and the current thought—but subsequent generation attends only to the compressed history, discarding the original tokens. This is trained with standard SFT on reconstructed data where compression triggers and gist tokens are inserted, with a causal attention mask enforcing the desired dependency structure. Two variants are explored: thought-level (compress per paragraph boundary) and token-level (compress every 6 tokens into 2). LightThinker++ replaces implicit compression with explicit adaptive memory management using three behavioral primitives: commit (archive a reasoning step as a summary, discarding the raw derivation), expand (restore a specific archived step's raw content on demand), and fold (re-archive it after use). Each reasoning entity is maintained as a dual-form container (raw derivation R_k, semantic summary Z_k) with a dynamic visibility state. Training uses an environment-aware trajectory synthesis pipeline: a teacher model (DeepSeek-V3.2-Thinking) generates expert trajectories that interleave reasoning with explicit memory operations; when commit is issued, the environment actually hides the raw content before the next generation step, forcing the teacher to reason under true compression. Behavioral pruning enforces lifecycle completeness (all primitives must be used), a symmetry constraint (fold must follow expand on the same step), and anti-jitter heuristics (operational density limits, no back-to-back identical operations). For long-horizon agentic tasks (DeepResearch), the framework maps to the Thought-Action-Observation cycle with commit/expand/fold applied to full TAO turns, and trajectory synthesis uses a two-agent architecture (interaction agent + contextual governor). Key Results: LightThinker (thought-level, Qwen2.5-7B): reduces peak token usage by 70%, inference time by 26%, with only 1% accuracy drop across GSM8K, MMLU, GPQA, BBH. Under an equal memory budget, inference time is reduced 2.5× versus vanilla. Compression ratio is approximately 4.5× by the Dependency metric. LightThinker++ in Throughput mode: slashes peak tokens by 69.9% (3120→940) while matching vanilla accuracy (60.02 vs. 59.62 avg.). In Budget mode: achieves +2.42% average accuracy gain over vanilla while reducing peak by 45.0%; on the reasoning-intensive GPQA benchmark alone, +5.73 accuracy points with 40.5% less peak memory. Visible context per step is reduced 82.9% on Qwen and 80.4% on Llama. Compression ratio on GPQA reaches 15×. In long-horizon agentic tasks (Qwen3-30B-A3B): vanilla context inflates to ~100k tokens within 60 rounds; LightThinker++ maintains 30k–40k tokens even past 80 rounds (60–70% reduction). Pass@1 improves +5.7% on xBench-DeepSearch, with consistent gains on BrowseComp-ZH and BrowseComp-EN. On hard instances (vanilla succeeds ≤1/3 runs), Pass@1 improvements reach 3.08× on xBench. LightThinker++ reaches vanilla's peak performance in 24 actions vs. 60 (2.5× action efficiency gain). Ablation confirms that removing expand/fold (commit-only) causes a 6.5% accuracy drop (60.1%→53.6%), validating that bidirectional memory access is essential. Strengths: - The explicit memory primitive design (commit/expand/fold) elegantly decouples reasoning depth from context length growth, preserving lossless backtracking capability that implicit compression lacks, addressing a genuine failure mode of prior methods. - The environment-aware trajectory synthesis pipeline—where the environment actually enforces memory state during teacher generation—produces high-fidelity training data that closely mirrors inference-time conditions, a methodologically sound approach often neglected in similar work. - Breadth of evaluation is commendable: two model families (Llama, Qwen), four reasoning benchmarks, two serving configurations (throughput vs. budget), three agentic benchmarks spanning English and Chinese, plus careful ablations isolating each component contribution. Weaknesses / Limitations: - LightThinker's implicit compression is brittle on high-density reasoning (structured mathematical traces), losing critical anchors (specific numerical values) irreversibly—the paper acknowledges this via a failure case analysis and the degradation observed when applying implicit compression to the denser LightThinker++ synthetic traces. The proposed fix (adaptive latent capacity allocation) is left as future work. - The behavioral pruning pipeline is stringent (44.5% of trajectories filtered out for the agentic setting) and relies on a strong proprietary teacher model (DeepSeek-V3.2-Thinking, GPT-5 for evaluation), making the approach potentially expensive to replicate and sensitive to teacher model capability—a dependency not fully discussed in the paper.

#26 — Highly Innovative (76)

Reasoning Diffusion for Unpaired Test Time Out-of-distribution Text-Image to Video Generation

mn.cs.tsinghua.edu.cn · karypis - new results
Why this rank: This paper earns a high score for identifying and formalizing a genuinely novel problem — unpaired text-image-to-video generation — that all prior work sidesteps by assumption. The decomposition into a reasoning module (VisionNarrator) that infers temporal placement and narrative coherence, followed by latent-level injection via AlignFormer's multi-stage temporal anchor attention, is architecturally principled and more expressive than naive prompt rewriting. The pragmatic training strategy of simulating unpaired conditions from standard video data via wide-interval frame sampling avoids the need for a new curated dataset. However, evaluation on a self-constructed synthetic benchmark and the uncharacterized robustness of the MLLM reasoning component temper the impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing text-image-to-video generation models assume the input text and image are semantically paired and temporally aligned — i.e., both describe the same event occurring simultaneously, with the image serving as the first frame. This assumption breaks down in realistic scenarios where the two modalities are "unpaired": the image and text may describe events at different timestamps, and the image may belong at an arbitrary position in the video. For example, given a broken vase (image) and "a cat plays in the room" (text), the model must infer the cat caused the damage and place the vase-breaking event logically in the video. No prior work addresses this out-of-distribution (OOD) unpaired setting explicitly; existing models either latch onto the image and ignore the text, superficially blend both, or produce static juxtapositions. Methodology: ReasonDiff is built on top of Wan2.1 (a DiT-based flow-matching video model) and introduces two novel modules. The VisionNarrator uses a frozen multimodal LLM (MLLM) to reason over the unpaired text-image pair: it infers the most plausible temporal position of the condition image within the target video and generates a per-frame narrative (a "video script") that coherently bridges both modalities. In-context learning stabilizes the structured JSON output format. The AlignFormer then translates this high-level reasoning into frame-wise latent representations. It takes the condition image feature as an anchor, its inferred position, and per-frame narrative embeddings, then applies a Multi-stage Temporal Anchor Attention (MTAA) mechanism — two cascaded cross-attention stages (coarse then fine) — where the anchor image feature acts as Query and narrative embeddings form Keys/Values. The resulting reasoning-enhanced latents are fused with the condition frame and injected as guidance throughout denoising. Training is two-stage: (1) joint warm-up of the base model and AlignFormer with standard flow-matching denoising loss; (2) fine-tuning of AlignFormer alone with an auxiliary reconstruction loss (β=0.2) against ground-truth latents to stabilize predicted representations. Training data is WebVid with wide-interval frame sampling and LLaMA-3.2-11B-generated per-frame captions to simulate unpaired conditions. Key Results: On a self-constructed ActivityNet benchmark (500 videos, unpaired conditions formed by pairing first/last frame with a caption of the opposite end), ReasonDiff achieves top performance on 5 of 6 metrics: Imaging Quality (0.528 vs. 0.512 for best baseline Wan2.1), Motion Smooth (0.986 vs. 0.980), Dynamic Degree (0.936 vs. 0.872 for CogVideoX), CLIP Score Text (0.261 vs. 0.224, a +16.5% improvement over Wan2.1), and User Rank (1.743 vs. 2.692 for Wan2.1, lower is better). CLIP Score Image is comparable across methods (~0.5), which the authors explain is because baselines over-rely on the image condition, inflating that metric. On MSR-VTT (paired inputs), ReasonDiff also ranks first in User Rank (1.769) and Imaging Quality (0.571), outperforming even its base model Wan2.1, suggesting cross-modal reasoning generalizes to the paired setting. Ablations confirm each component is necessary: removing the auxiliary loss hurts Motion Smooth; removing multi-frame prompts reduces Dynamic Degree; removing enhanced latents substantially degrades Imaging Quality; and naively rewriting the prompt for a base model yields poor CLIP-Text alignment. Strengths: - Identifies and formalizes a genuinely novel and practically important problem — unpaired text-image video generation — that prior work ignores by assumption. - The VisionNarrator + AlignFormer decomposition is principled: reasoning is decoupled from generation, and latent-level injection (via MTAA) is more expressive than simply prepending a rewritten text prompt. - The two-stage training avoids the need for a new dataset of unpaired text-image pairs by reformulating training on standard video data with wide-interval frame sampling — a pragmatic and scalable approach. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation dataset is self-constructed and synthetic: unpaired conditions are created by pairing opposite-end frames and auto-generated captions, which may not capture the full diversity and difficulty of real-world unpaired inputs. No human-curated unpaired benchmark exists to validate generalization. - The method introduces a frozen MLLM (VisionNarrator) as a mandatory inference-time component, adding latency and a dependency on external model quality. If the MLLM misreasins the connection between modalities, errors propagate without correction into AlignFormer and the generator. - The paper does not report failure modes or quantify how often VisionNarrator produces plausible vs. incorrect narratives, leaving the robustness of the reasoning component uncharacterized.
Search Results
#27 — Highly Innovative (76)

Can Heterogeneous Language Models Be Fused?

Shilian Chen, Jie Zhou, Qin Chen, Wen Wu, Xin Li, Qi Feng, Liang He · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This paper scores 76 for cleanly formulating the heterogeneous model merging problem—a genuine gap given the proliferation of diverse open-weight architectures—and proposing a principled solution with the frozen-A/predict-ΔB design that constrains cross-architecture transfer to bounded, basis-preserving perturbations. The SVD-guided spectral gating and noise robustness results (maintaining 70.5% F1 where baselines collapse to ~20%) demonstrate meaningful methodological creativity. However, the narrow evaluation scope (only IE and GLUE tasks on 7-8B models with rank-8 LoRA) and reliance on a replay dataset temper the impact, leaving generalization to generative tasks and larger models unvalidated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Model merging—combining multiple fine-tuned expert models into a single model without inference-time ensembling costs—has proven effective when all source models share the same pretrained backbone (homogeneous setting). However, real open-model ecosystems increasingly contain useful experts built on different architectures (Llama, Qwen, Mistral), making direct weight-space fusion ill-posed. Three fundamental obstacles block naive extension: (1) topology mismatch across layer depths, hidden dimensions, and attention parameterizations; (2) latent basis misalignment, where even functionally analogous modules encode adaptations in incompatible coordinate systems; and (3) cross-source conflict amplification, where architecture-specific artifacts compound rather than cancel. Existing heterogeneous approaches sidestep these issues via distillation (requiring source-model inference and training data pipelines) or inference-time ensembling (keeping all models active at deployment). Neither achieves direct white-box weight-space fusion. Methodology: HeteroFusion operates in LoRA adapter space and has two core components. First, topology-based alignment avoids raw tensor-to-tensor matching by instead aligning modules at the level of functional role (q/k/v/o projections, MLP up/down/gate) using tail-alignment layer mapping. For each compatible module group, complementary row-space and column-space views of the LoRA factors are extracted—preserving algebraic structure across variable hidden dimensions. A HyperNet with target-as-query cross-attention (analogous to the Perceiver architecture) then predicts structured corrections ΔB for the target's B matrices, while the A matrices are kept frozen. This is the key design decision: fixing A preserves the target adapter basis (the latent subspace geometry), so the transfer becomes a bounded perturbation ‖ΔW′−ΔW‖_F ≤ |α|‖ΔB‖_F‖A‖_2, which is explicitly controlled by a learnable group-wise scaling factor α. Second, conflict-aware denoising applies an SVD-guided sparse gate that suppresses spectral channels dominated by architecture-specific variation before cross-source interaction, followed by a Rectified Distribution Matching (RDM) regularizer—a sliced Wasserstein surrogate—that prevents distribution fragmentation in the shared latent space. Optimization uses a small mixed replay set (300 instances per task) with dynamic patching: target weights are temporarily updated during forward-backward, then restored, so the transfer network is trained without permanently corrupting the target adapter. Key Results: On the InstructUIE benchmark (NER, RE, ET tasks across biomedical, social media, news domains) for single-source Qwen→Llama transfer, HeteroFusion achieves 71.38 average F1, compared to 67.60 for the homogeneous Llama merge baseline and 63.15 for FuseLLM (the strongest distillation baseline), an improvement of +8.23 F1. For multi-source fusion (Qwen + Mistral → Llama), HeteroFusion achieves 71.31 average F1 vs. FuseLLM at 60.32 (+10.99 F1). Inverting the target to Qwen (Llama + Mistral → Qwen) yields 69.84 F1 vs. FuseLLM's 58.66. Under noisy-source conditions (adding 4 task-irrelevant Llama experts), traditional merging methods catastrophically degrade to ~20-25% F1, while HeteroFusion maintains 70.55% F1 vs. FuseLLM's 58.77%. On GLUE, HeteroFusion achieves the best average of 82.58 vs. 81.36 for FuseLLM and 81.04 for Breadcrumbs. Ablations confirm both components are necessary: removing topology-based alignment drops average F1 from 72.20 to 36.49; removing conflict-aware denoising drops it to 66.28. Hyperparameter sensitivity is low, with F1 varying only from 69.40 to 70.89 across α ∈ [0.05, 0.9]. Strengths: - Clean problem formulation that precisely identifies three distinct failure modes (topology mismatch, basis misalignment, conflict amplification) and addresses each with a dedicated mechanism, with formal perturbation bounds supporting the design. - The frozen-A / predict-ΔB design is principled: it constrains heterogeneous transfer to a basis-preserving perturbation, avoiding representation collapse while remaining interpretable. - Compelling noise robustness results—maintaining 70.55% F1 when traditional merging collapses to 20-25%—demonstrate that the SVD-guided gating genuinely filters architecture-specific spectral artifacts rather than simply averaging. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method still requires a small replay dataset (300 instances per task), meaning it is not truly data-free; unlike task-arithmetic-style merging, it needs optimization, which adds complexity and dependency on task-representative samples. - Evaluation is narrow in scope: only information extraction (NER/RE/ET) and GLUE classification tasks are tested, all using 7-8B parameter models with LoRA rank 8. Generalization to generative tasks, instruction following, reasoning benchmarks, or larger/smaller models is unvalidated. - The theoretical justification (Eq. 11) only bounds the magnitude of the perturbation; it says nothing about whether the injected ΔB actually encodes useful cross-architecture knowledge, so the theoretical contribution is more of a stability argument than a characterization of what is transferred.
Citations 2604.01674 PDF
#28 — Highly Innovative (76)

Generalizable Foundation Models for Calorimetry via Mixtures-of-Experts and Parameter Efficient Fine Tuning

Carlos Cardona-Giraldo, Cristiano Fanelli, James Giroux, Cole Granger, Benjamin Nachman, Gerald Sabin · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Scores 76 for its architecturally elegant fixed-routing MoE scheme that provably prevents catastrophic forgetting when extending to new detector configurations, combined with the practically significant few-shot material adaptation result (1k samples matching full-dataset fidelity — a ~1000× data reduction). The three-tier modular adaptation stack (MoE + LoRA + particle-specific heads) is a creative architectural contribution that maps cleanly onto the physics of the problem. However, validation is limited to electromagnetic showers in a single detector geometry, and the ad-hoc post-hoc longitudinal calibration for cross-species transfer undermines the claim of principled generalization.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Particle physics experiments at future colliders (e.g., ILC/HL-LHC) face a simulation crisis: Geant4-based Monte Carlo detector simulation is projected to exceed available CPU resources as luminosities rise. Deep generative surrogates have emerged as a solution, but existing models are single-purpose — trained per material, per particle species, and per detector configuration. Retraining from scratch for each new configuration is expensive and wasteful. The gap this paper fills is the lack of a generalizable foundation model for calorimetry that can be incrementally extended to new materials and particle types without catastrophic forgetting of previously learned distributions, while remaining computationally competitive with faster specialized approaches. Methodology: The authors build a next-token autoregressive transformer backbone (dual-sequence cross+self-attention, embedding dimension 256) that generates electromagnetic calorimeter showers hit-by-hit on a 30×30×30 voxel grid. Sparse shower sequences are tokenized with ~27k spatial tokens and ~25k energy tokens; sequences are sorted by energy in descending order. Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE, θ=1000) with Partial RoPE (50% of dimensions rotated) replace learned positional encodings to handle variable sequence lengths across materials and particle types. The core architectural innovation is a three-tier modular adaptation stack: (1) Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) with fixed routing — each expert maps to a unique (particle, material) combination; adding a new material requires training only one new expert while all backbone weights remain frozen, preventing catastrophic forgetting by construction; (2) LoRA (rank r=128) applied to Q, K, V, and output projections for cross-species transfer — enabling structural attention reconfiguration when adapting to new particle types (e.g., photon→electron) whose shower dynamics differ fundamentally; (3) Particle-specific output vocabulary heads — each particle species has its own projection head for pixel and energy prediction, sidestepping the high-rank corrections that would otherwise be needed for large vocabulary shifts. The base model is pre-trained jointly on photons in tungsten and tantalum (760k samples each, 10–100 GeV). New materials are added by fine-tuning a single new expert; new particle species are added via LoRA + new vocabulary heads; subsequently extending a particle-adapted model to new materials again requires only adding one expert. LLM inference optimizations (KV-caching, memory preallocation, CUDA graph execution) are applied to bring autoregressive throughput to practical levels. Key Results: (1) Baseline fidelity: The pre-trained MoE model matches Geant4 distributions for photons in both tungsten and tantalum across all shower observables (visible cell energy, energy sum, hit multiplicity, longitudinal center of gravity, layer-wise energy, radial profile), with agreement within 3σ statistical uncertainty except in low-density distribution tails. Performance is comparable to the Omnijet-αc autoregressive baseline on tungsten, while simultaneously handling tantalum — which Omnijet-αc cannot do without retraining. (2) Few-shot material adaptation: Fine-tuning with only 1k photon samples in lead (a new absorber material) yields distributions consistent within uncertainty with a model trained on the full dataset (~950k samples). This ~1000× reduction in required simulation data is the headline claim for detector design workflows. (3) Cross-species transfer: Adapting to electrons in tungsten via LoRA + new vocabulary heads achieves high-fidelity generation with ~50k samples, though 10k samples shows degradation in center-of-gravity and hit multiplicity. Secondary material extension for electrons (tantalum, lead) requires only adding one expert and converges at 1k–10k samples, but requires a post-hoc calibration step (shifting top-k=1–3 most energetic deposits deeper) to correct systematic longitudinal shower profile bias. (4) Inference speed: With KV-caching, CUDA graphs, and memory preallocation, the model achieves 10.46 ms/shower on an A100 GPU — a 392× speedup over Geant4 (4100 ms on CPU). This is slower than CaloClouds II (6.12 ms, 670×) and L2LFlows (3.24 ms, 1260×) but over 1000× faster than the comparable autoregressive baseline OmniJet-αc (3000 ms). Strengths: - The MoE fixed-routing scheme is architecturally elegant for the problem: inference FLOPS remain constant as the model expands to cover more configurations, and catastrophic forgetting is provably prevented since base weights are never modified. - The few-shot material adaptation result (1k samples matching full-dataset performance) is a genuinely useful result for the target application — detector optimization workflows where simulating a new candidate material from scratch is the bottleneck. - Applying standard LLM inference optimizations (KV-cache, CUDA graphs) to achieve a ~1000× speedup over the prior autoregressive baseline is a practically important engineering contribution that applies broadly to any transformer-based physics simulator. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The post-hoc longitudinal calibration step (manually shifting top-k energy deposits deeper) for electron adaptation to new materials is a red flag: it signals that the LoRA rank-128 constraint is insufficient to fully encode the photon→electron→new-material transformation chain, and the "fix" is ad hoc rather than model-based. The authors acknowledge this but leave self-correction for future work. - The pre-training dataset is intentionally small (~950k samples per material) to match prior work for fair comparison, which by the authors' own admission means the transformer backbone is operating well below its representational capacity. Claims about foundation model scalability therefore rest on extrapolation rather than demonstrated scaling behavior. - Only electromagnetic showers (photons and electrons) in one detector geometry (ILD Si-W ECAL, 30×30×30 voxels) are studied. Generalization to hadronic showers, different calorimeter geometries, or non-ILD detectors — the scenarios where a true foundation model would matter most — is entirely undemonstrated.
Search Results 2603.28804 PDF
#29 — Highly Innovative (76)

ACES: Who Tests the Tests? Leave-One-Out AUC Consistency for Code Generation (45 ▲ )

Hui Sun, Yun-Ji Zhang, Zheng Xie, Ren-Biao Liu, Yali Du, Xin-Ye Li, Ming Li · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 76 for its genuinely creative theoretical contribution: the LOO-AUC identity elegantly breaks the circular dependency between code correctness and test quality using only the binary pass matrix, providing the first provable criterion for identifying informative tests without ground truth. The reframing of test value as discriminative power rather than correctness is a surprising and clean insight rooted in item response theory. The two complementary algorithms (closed-form ACES-C and iterative ACES-O) offer practical utility with near-negligible overhead, and the empirical gains over majority voting are consistent across benchmarks. The main limitation—reliance on conditional independence and degradation with small test budgets—keeps this from the highest tier.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: When selecting the best LLM-generated code candidate from a large pool, a natural strategy is to use LLM-generated tests for evaluation. However, this creates a circular dependency: reliable tests require knowing which code is correct, and reliable code quality assessments require correct tests. Existing methods either treat all tests equally (majority voting) or rely on ad-hoc heuristics. No prior method offers a formal, provable criterion for identifying informative tests using only the binary pass matrix, without knowing ground-truth code correctness. Methodology: The key insight is to reframe code selection as a ranking problem where a test's value lies not in its correctness but in its ability to distinguish correct from incorrect code — its discriminative power δⱼ = αⱼ − βⱼ (difference in class-conditional pass rates). ACES breaks the circular dependency via leave-one-out (LOO) evaluation: hold out one test, rank codes by aggregate scores on the remaining tests, and measure whether the held-out test's pass/fail pattern agrees with that ranking via the area under the ROC curve (LOO-AUC). The central theoretical result (Theorem 3, LOO-AUC Identity) proves that E[LOO-AUCⱼ(w)] − 1/2 = cⱼ(w) · δⱼ, where cⱼ(w) > 0 under mild conditions — meaning LOO-AUC excess is proportional to discriminative power without requiring knowledge of code correctness. Two algorithms build on this: ACES-C computes closed-form test weights as wⱼ = max(0, LOO-AUCⱼ − 1/2) · pⱼ(1−pⱼ), applying a pass-rate correction that recovers δⱼ in expectation (Theorem 6), under Assumption 4 (positive average test quality); ACES-O drops this assumption and iteratively maximizes a differentiable surrogate LOO-AUC objective via gradient ascent with logistic approximation, using softmax-parameterized weights on a pre-filtered candidate pool. Both operate solely on the binary pass matrix with negligible overhead (ACES-C: 9ms, ACES-O: ~850ms per task for n=200, m=500). Key Results: On HumanEval/HumanEval+/MBPP with ~200 GPT-3.5-Turbo candidates and ~500 tests per problem: ACES-O achieves 84.15%/74.39%/72.37% Pass@1, outperforming Majority Voting (80.49%/69.51%/68.62%) and CodeT (78.05%/67.87%/71.90%). ACES-O surpasses even DS³ (static analysis method) on HumanEval and HumanEval+ (84.15% vs. 81.71%; 74.39% vs. 72.56%) using only the binary pass matrix. Combined with DS³ pre-filtering, ACES-C+DS³ achieves 85.37%/77.44%/76.11% Pass@1 — the best overall results. Test quality detection is accurate: ACES-C correctly identifies ≥94.8% of informative tests across benchmarks. On harder LeetCode problems with multiple models, ACES-C consistently recovers performance where MV degrades below zero-shot (e.g., ACES-C: 25.44% vs. MV: 13.16% on LeetCodeDataset with Qwen2.5-Coder-14B Pass@1). Assumption 4 is satisfied for 71–83% of non-trivial tasks across benchmarks. Strengths: - Strong theoretical foundation: the LOO-AUC identity is the first provable criterion for identifying informative vs. misleading tests from the pass matrix alone, with a clean connection to discriminative power via item response theory. - Two complementary algorithms with clearly delineated regimes: ACES-C is parameter-free, near-instant, and near-oracle when Assumption 4 holds; ACES-O handles adversarial cases through iterative co-evolution of weights and ranking. - Empirically robust: ACES-O improves over Majority Voting even when Assumption 4 is violated (16/52 vs. 9/52 tasks on MBPP); sensitive to neither the number of tests nor candidate codes compared to MV, which plateaus or degrades. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The LOO-AUC objective is non-convex, so ACES-O converges to a stationary point rather than a global optimum; the theoretical guarantees for ACES-O are primarily motivational rather than convergence guarantees. - The framework assumes conditional independence of test outcomes given code correctness, which may not hold when tests are generated with correlated prompts or share structural dependencies — a gap the authors acknowledge but do not address. - Experiments rely on a single generation setup (GPT-3.5-Turbo with ~200 candidates and ~500 tests from Huang et al. 2024); performance on smaller test budgets (m' < 50) or fewer candidates degrades notably for ACES-O, and the Hard regime (δ̄ ≤ 0) remains unsolved by either variant.
Recommended 2604.03922 PDF
#30 — Innovative (74)

Pseudo-Likelihood Training for Reasoning Diffusion Language Models

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: PADRE's reformulation of KL-regularized RL as pseudo-likelihood matching via the Hammersley-Clifford theorem is theoretically elegant and provides a principled alternative to biased ELBO approximations. The augmented-sequence trick for efficient multi-conditional evaluation is clever. However, the practical approximations partially undermine the theoretical bias-free guarantees, and evaluation is limited to a single model (LLaDA-8B), narrowing the demonstrated generality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Policy-gradient reinforcement learning (PGRL) methods like GRPO have become the dominant approach for post-training LLMs on reasoning tasks, but they fundamentally require efficient computation of token-level log-likelihoods. Diffusion-based language models (dLLMs), such as LLaDA, generate text through iterative masked denoising rather than autoregressive factorization, making exact likelihood computation intractable. Existing attempts to adapt PGRL to dLLMs (e.g., diffu-GRPO/d1) rely on biased Monte-Carlo ELBO approximations or mean-field approximations of the joint density, leading to gradient bias and training instability. The gap is the lack of a principled, scalable RL alignment objective native to the dLLM parameterization. Methodology: PADRE reframes KL-regularized RL as a distribution-matching problem and replaces the standard joint KL divergence with a pseudo-KL (PKL) objective over per-token conditional distributions. The key theoretical insight is that for strictly positive distributions, matching all single-token conditionals p(y^i | y^{-i}) is sufficient to match the full joint distribution (Hammersley-Clifford). The resulting tractable objective is J_PKL = E_{y~π_θ} Σ_i [log π_θ(y^i | y^{-i}) - log q̃(y^i | y^{-i})], where q̃ is the Boltzmann target distribution. This avoids computing the partition function and requires only conditional token probabilities, which dLLMs can approximate efficiently by masking one position at a time and reading the denoiser output. To compute all L conditionals in roughly one forward pass, PADRE constructs an augmented sequence x ⊕ y_{1:L} ⊕ m_{L+1:2L} with shared positional embeddings and a custom attention mask, so each mask row L+i attends to y_{-i} without cross-contamination. Two efficient approximation variants are proposed: PKL-U (block mean-field, interpolates between full PKL and d1) and PKL-T (trajectory-based, uses the denoising unmasking order to restrict attention). Training follows the GRPO recipe with group-normalized advantages, clipped importance weights, KL regularization, and optional prompt token perturbation as dropout regularization. Key Results: On math reasoning benchmarks with LLaDA-8B as the base model, PKL+ (with prompt perturbation) achieves 87.3% on GSM8K (+5.2% over base, +5.2% over d1-LLaDA), 42.4% on MATH500 (+6.2% over base, +2.2% over d1-LLaDA), and 44.3% on MATH (+5.4% over base). The full PKL variant achieves 85.6/40.9/41.6 on the three benchmarks, consistently outperforming diffu-GRPO, d1, VRPO, and wd1. On coding benchmarks (HumanEval, MBPP), PKL achieves the best results at all tested sequence lengths (128/256/512 tokens), e.g., 36.2% on HumanEval-256 vs. 32.9% for d1 and 47.4% on MBPP-256 vs. 44.7% for d1. A KL divergence diagnostic confirms that PKL's conditional approximation error (avg KL < 1) is substantially lower than d1's mean-field approximation error (KL = 2.87–3.15 on GSM8K/MATH500). FLOP-equalized comparisons show that at sufficient compute budgets, PKL outperforms d1 on GSM8K, though d1 has an edge at very low FLOP counts. Strengths: - Theoretically principled: the pseudo-KL objective shares the same global optima as KL-regularized PGRL, unlike the biased approximations used in competing dLLM methods, and this is backed by a formal theorem (Hammersley-Clifford) and derivation. - Practical efficiency: the augmented-sequence trick with shared positional embeddings and custom attention masks enables approximate computation of all L conditionals in near-constant forward passes, making the method scalable. - Consistent empirical gains: PADRE outperforms all dLLM alignment baselines across math and coding benchmarks by meaningful margins, with the PKL-U and PKL-T variants providing a compute-accuracy tradeoff spectrum. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The conditional likelihood approximation used in practice (masking one token, reading denoiser output) involves the same Markovian approximation as prior work; the paper acknowledges that PKL's per-token conditional KL is still non-zero (avg < 1 but not 0), so the theoretical guarantee of bias-free optimization does not fully carry through to the implemented algorithm. - Experimental scope is narrow: only LLaDA-8B is evaluated, no larger model scales are tested, and tasks like Sudoku (where dLLMs already struggle) are excluded—making it unclear how well the method generalizes across model families, scales, or longer-horizon reasoning tasks where the conditioning approximations may degrade. - The efficient multi-conditional evaluation via attention mask sharing is only exact for a single transformer layer; across multiple layers the attended context for y^j bleeds through to y^i, requiring the PKL-U or PKL-T heuristics whose theoretical properties revert to those of prior approximations.
Recommended
#31 — Innovative (74)

Swift-SVD: Theoretical Optimality Meets Practical Efficiency in Low-Rank LLM Compression (3 ▲ )

Ruoling Qi, Yirui Liu, Xuaner Wu, Xiangyu Wang, Ming Li, Chen Chen, Jian Chen, Yin Chen, Qizhen Weng · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 6 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Swift-SVD delivers a clean theoretical contribution by proving the closed-form eigendecomposition solution for activation-aware low-rank compression, achieving exact numerical optimality where prior methods accumulate floating-point error. The incremental covariance aggregation decoupling compression cost from dataset size is practically significant, yielding 19-77x speedups over competitors. The discovery and formal exploitation of the negative correlation between layer importance and effective rank adds a principled dimension to rank allocation. However, the core mathematical insight (Eckart-Young-Mirsky on output activations) is a natural application of well-known theory, and the evaluation is limited to 7-8B models, leaving the strongest motivation — frontier-scale deployment — unvalidated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying large language models is constrained by the memory footprint of static weights and the growing KV cache during autoregressive decoding. SVD-based low-rank compression offers a hardware-compatible path to reduce both, but existing methods suffer from a frustrating dichotomy: activation-unaware methods (e.g., FWSVD, PALU) are suboptimal in reconstruction error, while activation-aware methods that achieve theoretical optimality (e.g., SVD-LLM, Dobi-SVD) require Cholesky decompositions or repeated full SVDs per sample, causing numerical instability and prohibitive compression times at scale. Non-uniform rank allocation across layers has been explored but remains heuristic due to the cost of per-layer loss estimation. Swift-SVD targets all three gaps simultaneously. Methodology: Swift-SVD frames activation-aware low-rank compression as minimizing ||XW - XW_k||_F over rank-k matrices W_k. The key theoretical contribution (Theorem 3.1) shows that the optimal solution is W_k = W V_k V_k^T, where V_k are the top-k right singular vectors of the output activation Y = XW, provably via the Eckart-Young-Mirsky theorem. Rather than computing SVD of Y directly (which scales with sequence length and dataset size), Swift-SVD incrementally aggregates the n×n covariance matrix C = Y^T Y over calibration samples and performs a single eigenvalue decomposition of C to recover V and Σ. This requires only O(n²) extra memory, avoids Cholesky factorization entirely, and is agnostic to variable sequence lengths. Because the full spectral decomposition is computed once, any target rank can be applied by simple truncation without re-running compression. For dynamic rank allocation, Swift-SVD introduces a compressibility score s_i = (β_i)^α · (log(e + ε_{k̄,i}))^{1-α} combining layer importance β (end-to-end impact) and local Frobenius loss ε* (reconstruction difficulty). A guaranteed minimum rank (δ × k̄) prevents over-compression of critical layers; the remaining budget is distributed proportionally to s_i. A grid search over 11 values of α selects the best configuration on a validation set, exploiting the observation—empirically validated across six LLMs—that local compressibility (effective rank) and layer importance are negatively correlated. Key Results: On LLaMA-7B at compression ratio 0.8, Swift-SVD achieves WikiText-2 PPL of 7.91 vs. 7.94 for SVD-LLM(W) and 8.54 for Dobi-SVD(w); the dynamic variant Swift-SVD reaches 7.84. At the aggressive 0.6 ratio, Swift-SVD obtains 13.42 PPL (WikiText-2) vs. 13.54 for Dobi-SVD(w), with consistent improvements in average zero-shot accuracy (0.43 vs. 0.41). Compression speedups are the headline result: with 512 calibration samples, Swift-SVD achieves 76.9× end-to-end speedup over Dobi-SVD and 19.8× over SVD-LLM(W), by eliminating repeated SVDs per sample. Numerical stability experiments on random matrices show Swift-SVD achieves exactly zero absolute error versus the theoretical minimum across matrix sizes from 128×128 to 4096×4096, while SVD-LLM and Dobi-SVD accumulate errors of +13 to +134 units. Cross-model generalization at ratio 0.8 shows Swift-SVD achieving PPL of 11.65/13.68 (Wiki2/C4) on OPT-6.7B versus 16.04/21.27 for SVD-LLM, and 6.63/11.08 on Mistral-7B versus 10.21/13.17. Inference throughput on LLaMA-7B scales from 154 to 243 tokens/sec as compression ratio decreases from 1.0 to 0.2 (batch 16, PL=32). Strengths: - The closed-form eigenvalue decomposition solution is theoretically clean and achieves exact numerical optimality (zero reconstruction error above minimum), unlike prior methods that claim theoretical optimality but accumulate floating-point error through Cholesky or repeated SVD. - The incremental covariance aggregation decouples compression cost from dataset size: once C is formed, switching compression ratios requires no additional forward passes, explaining the dramatic 3-77× speedups. - The empirical discovery and formal exploitation of the negative correlation between layer importance and effective rank is a genuinely useful insight that motivates a principled dynamic rank allocation beyond heuristics. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The O(n²) covariance matrix storage assumes n (output dimension) is tractable; for very wide layers in frontier models this could become a memory bottleneck not analyzed in the paper. - Cross-dataset calibration experiments confirm that Swift-SVD is strongly activation-aware to the point of domain sensitivity: calibrating on C4 and evaluating on WikiText-2 at ratio 0.6 nearly triples PPL (23.17 → 37.00), raising questions about deployment robustness when calibration data is mismatched. - Experiments are limited to 7-8B parameter models; scalability to 70B+ models—where the practical deployment motivation is strongest—is not demonstrated, and the interaction between the n×n covariance storage and very large hidden dimensions at that scale is unaddressed.
Recommended 2604.01609 PDF
#32 — Innovative (74)

FileGram: Grounding Agent Personalization in File-System Behavioral Traces (25 ▲ )

Shuai Liu, Shulin Tian, Kairui Hu, Yuhao Dong, Zhe Yang, Bo Li, Jingkang Yang, Chen Change Loy, Ziwei Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 Apr 2026
Why this rank: FileGram defines a genuinely new problem space—grounding agent personalization in file-system behavioral traces rather than dialogue—and delivers the first benchmark and memory architecture for this domain. The three-channel Engram design with a deterministic 17-D procedural fingerprint is architecturally creative, and the deferred-abstraction principle (semantic interpretation at query time, not ingest) is well-validated by ablation. However, the sharp sim-to-real accuracy drop (<10% on human recordings) significantly undermines practical impact, and the most important capability (shift analysis at 37.8%) remains essentially unsolved, tempering what is otherwise a strong conceptual contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Personalized AI agents that act as file-system coworkers need to adapt to individual user workflows, organizational habits, and execution styles. However, existing memory architectures are interaction-centric—they learn from dialogue summaries—and miss the rich behavioral signals embedded in file-system operations: atomic actions (reads, writes, moves, deletes) and content deltas (what users actually produce and edit). Collecting real-world file-system traces at scale is impractical due to privacy constraints, and no benchmark exists that evaluates memory systems on file-system-grounded personalization. The gap is threefold: lack of training data, lack of evaluation infrastructure, and lack of a memory architecture suited to this domain. Methodology: The paper proposes FileGram, a three-component framework. (1) FileGramEngine is a persona-driven synthetic data engine. It defines 20 user profiles across 19 attributes, with six behavioral dimensions (consumption pattern, production style, organization preference, iteration strategy, curation, cross-modal behavior), each discretized into L/M/R tiers. Each profile is paired with 32 tasks (16 text-centric, 16 multimodal), yielding 640 sandboxed trajectories. Raw tool traces are filtered to retain only 12 atomic action types (20,028 total) and paired with content deltas (file snapshots and edit diffs), then rendered into 10K+ multimodal outputs. Deliberate behavioral perturbation injects controlled single-tier shifts in 5 trajectories per profile to simulate real-world drift. (2) FileGramBench is a 4.6K-question diagnostic benchmark with four tracks: Understanding (attribute recognition, behavioral fingerprinting, profile reconstruction), Reasoning (behavioral inference, trace disentanglement), Detection (anomaly detection, shift analysis), and Multimodal Grounding (file grounding, visual grounding on human screen recordings). (3) FileGramOS is a bottom-up, three-stage memory architecture. Stage 1 encodes each trajectory into an Engram via three parallel streams: procedural extraction (computing a 17-D fingerprint from action statistics), semantic parsing (VLM captioning of file snapshots and diffs plus a behavioral descriptor), and action merge (LLM-based episode boundary detection). Stage 2 consolidates N Engrams across sessions into three channels: procedural (aggregated fingerprint statistics), semantic (chunked embeddings + cross-session LLM summary), and episodic (trajectory clustering with z-score anomaly detection and LLM disambiguation of variation vs. genuine shift). Stage 3 performs query-adaptive retrieval that pulls channel-specific clues before composing the final answer. Key Results: On FileGramBench (text setting), FileGramOS achieves 59.6% overall accuracy, outperforming the strongest baseline EverMemOS at 49.9% and all other memory methods (Mem0: 33.2%, Zep: 40.2%, MMA: 44.7%). On anomaly detection, aggregation-capable methods (FileGramOS, EverMemOS) exceed 70%, while flat stores (Mem0, SimpleMem) remain near random at 21–26%. However, shift analysis—attributing detected changes to specific behavioral dimensions—remains hard across all methods (FileGramOS: 37.8%). Full Context achieves competitive 48.0% overall by preserving raw evidence but fails on cross-session comparison tasks where structured aggregation is required. In the multimodal and real-world settings, all methods drop sharply; accuracy on human screen recordings falls to single digits (<10%) across all systems, exposing a large sim-to-real gap. Channel ablation confirms the procedural channel is most critical (removing it costs −11.1 pp overall and −27.8 pp on trace disentanglement), while semantic and episodic channels contribute −5.5 pp and −4.2 pp respectively. Cross-backbone validation shows FileGramOS's signal is model-agnostic (<2 pp variance across Gemini 2.5-Flash, GPT-4.1, Claude Sonnet 4). Strengths: - The core architectural insight—defer semantic abstraction to query time rather than collapsing trajectories into narratives at ingest—is well-motivated and empirically validated; narrative-first methods systematically flatten distinct profiles into identical generic descriptors. - The benchmark is the first to jointly require multimodal content, persistent memory, file-system behavioral traces, and controlled user profiles, filling a genuine gap relative to all prior conversational and GUI benchmarks (Table 1). - The 17-D procedural fingerprint is deterministic (no LLM calls), interpretable, and covers all six behavioral dimensions; ablation confirms it provides the strongest signal for procedural tasks while remaining modality-invariant. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All 640 training trajectories are generated by a single LLM (Claude Haiku 4.5), which imposes stylistic uniformity and may overstate profile separability; the sharp real-world accuracy drop (<10%) confirms that synthetic traces do not transfer to authentic user behavior, undermining the benchmark's practical validity. - The benchmark scale is modest (20 profiles, 32 tasks), behavioral shifts are limited to single-tier, single-dimension perturbations, and entire task categories (code development, real-time collaboration, system administration) are excluded, limiting coverage of real OS-agent workloads. - Shift analysis remains unsolved by all methods including FileGramOS (best: 37.8%), and the paper offers limited analysis of why the episodic channel's LLM-based anomaly judge fails to attribute changes correctly—leaving the most practically important capability (detecting and explaining behavioral drift) as an open problem without a concrete path forward.
Recommended 2604.04901 PDF
#33 — Innovative (74)

Communication-free Sampling and 4D Hybrid Parallelism for Scalable Mini-batch GNN Training

Cunyang Wei, Siddharth Singh, Aishwarya Sarkar, Daniel Nichols, Tisha Patel, Aditya K. Ranjan, Sayan Ghosh, Ali Jannesari, Nathan R. Tallent, Abhinav Bhatele · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: ScaleGNN introduces an elegant communication-free uniform vertex sampling algorithm with a principled unbiased rescaling correction that requires only global constants, eliminating a fundamental bottleneck in distributed GNN training. The 4D hybrid parallelism design correctly separates tensor and data parallelism axes, and the distributed full-graph evaluation via 3D PMM is a genuine practical advance. The evaluation is unusually rigorous—measuring end-to-end time to target accuracy across three HPC platforms and five datasets, with scaling demonstrated to 2048 GPUs. The scope limitation to GCN architectures and lack of convergence-rate analysis temper the contribution, but the combination of algorithmic insight and systems engineering is strong.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Distributed mini-batch GNN training at scale faces two fundamental bottlenecks: (1) popular sampling algorithms (GraphSAGE, GraphSAINT) require cross-device communication to fetch multi-hop neighbors or compute global normalization coefficients, making sampling a critical latency bottleneck; and (2) vanilla data parallelism—the default scaling strategy—often fails to reduce end-to-end training time because increasing the number of data-parallel replicas raises the number of epochs needed to converge, offsetting throughput gains. No prior system combines tensor parallelism (3D parallel matrix multiplication) with data parallelism for mini-batch GNN training. Methodology: ScaleGNN organizes GPUs into a 4D virtual grid (Gd × Gx × Gy × Gz), combining data parallelism with 3D parallel matrix multiplication (3D PMM). The key algorithmic contribution is uniform vertex sampling: at each step, B vertices are drawn uniformly without replacement using a shared random seed; each GPU independently derives the identical sample and extracts its local CSR shard of the induced subgraph, with no inter-device communication. Bias from dropped cross-boundary edges is corrected via importance-weight rescaling by factor p = (B-1)/(N-1), which depends only on global constants and requires no communication. Within each data-parallel group, 3D PMM distributes both SpMM (graph aggregation) and GEMM (feature transformation) across a 3D GPU grid using all-reduce collectives on orthogonal process groups, with a layer-rotation scheme to handle changing feature distribution planes across GCN layers. Four cumulative optimizations are applied: (1) pipelined sampling-training overlap (sampling step t+1 runs concurrently with training step t on a separate CUDA stream), (2) selective BF16 communication for 3D PMM all-reduces while keeping computation in FP32, (3) kernel fusion of RMSNorm/ReLU/Dropout via torch.compile, and (4) overlapping backward-pass all-reduces across orthogonal communicators. Key Results: On accuracy, ScaleGNN's uniform sampling achieves 81.3% on ogbn-products and 96.3% on Reddit, exceeding GraphSAINT (80.2%, 96.2%) and GraphSAGE (79.6%, 95.4%). On end-to-end training time to target accuracy on Perlmutter, ScaleGNN achieves 3.5× speedup over SALIENT++ and 10.6× over BNS-GCN at 64 GPUs on ogbn-products. On Frontier, speedups over MassiveGNN reach 162× on ogbn-products (10.2s vs. 1651.7s at 32 GCDs). Evaluation is also dramatically faster: 0.19s per round vs. 6.89s for BNS-GCN and 20.82s for DistDGL/MassiveGNN on ogbn-products at 8 GPUs, because ScaleGNN performs full-graph distributed evaluation via a single 3D PMM forward pass with no sampling. Strong scaling on Perlmutter reaches 21.7× speedup from 64→2048 GPUs on ogbn-papers100M (111M vertices, 1.6B edges), 19.8× on Products-14M (14M vertices), and scales to 2048 GCDs on Frontier and 1024 GPUs on Tuolumne. The four optimizations collectively yield 1.75× epoch-time reduction at 8 GPUs. Strengths: - The communication-free sampling algorithm is elegant and principled: the unbiased rescaling trick (dividing off-diagonal edge weights by p) corrects for induced-subgraph bias using only global constants, enabling fully independent per-GPU subgraph construction. It also outperforms GraphSAINT and GraphSAGE on accuracy, refuting the assumption that simpler sampling sacrifices quality. - The 4D parallelism design correctly identifies and separates the two orthogonal scaling axes (tensor parallelism for per-group efficiency, data parallelism for throughput), and the distributed full-graph evaluation—enabled by 3D PMM holding the entire graph within a group—is a genuine practical advantage over baselines that must re-run sampling pipelines at evaluation time. - The evaluation is unusually rigorous: end-to-end time to target accuracy (not per-epoch time) is used for cross-framework comparison, with correct reasoning that epochs are incommensurable across different sampling strategies. Testing spans five datasets and three HPC platforms (Perlmutter A100, Frontier MI250X, Tuolumne MI300A). Weaknesses / Limitations: - Uniform vertex sampling induces very sparse induced subgraphs for power-law graphs (most sampled pairs have no edge), which is why the rescaling factor 1/p can be large and the effective neighborhood coverage per vertex can be poor. The paper does not analyze how accuracy degrades with graph density, degree distribution skew, or increasing depth L, nor does it compare convergence rate (epochs to target) against neighbor-sampling baselines—only end-to-end wall-clock time. - The system is evaluated only on GCN (with augmentations), a relatively simple message-passing architecture. Generalization to attention-based GNNs (GAT), heterogeneous graphs, or models requiring multi-hop neighbor features (which are absent under induced-subgraph sampling) is not addressed, limiting the scope of the contribution for practitioners using more expressive architectures.
Citations 2604.02651 PDF
#34 — Innovative (74)

Think in Strokes, Not Pixels: Process-Driven Image Generation via Interleaved Reasoning (47 ▲ )

Lei Zhang, Junjiao Tian, Zhipeng Fan, Kunpeng Li, Jialiang Wang, Weifeng Chen, Markos Georgopoulos, Felix Juefei-Xu, Yuxiang Bao, Julian McAuley, Manling Li, Zecheng He · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Apr 2026
Why this rank: This paper introduces a genuinely novel paradigm for text-to-image generation by tightly coupling textual reasoning and visual generation in an iterative Plan-Sketch-Inspect-Refine loop, a departure from both single-pass and decoupled approaches. The self-sampling critique strategy is a principled insight that yields clear empirical gains over symbolic corrections. The 11× training data reduction and 8× inference speedup over PARM while achieving higher accuracy on GenEval demonstrates strong efficiency, though evaluation is limited to two benchmarks and the reliance on Flux-Kontext and GPT-4o for data construction raises reproducibility concerns.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Single-pass text-to-image generation forces models to commit to an entire scene in one forward pass, making it brittle on spatial relations, object counting, attribute binding, and compositional prompts. Existing chain-of-thought approaches are text-only and visually blind; multimodal CoT and tool-augmented sketching decouple reasoning from generation; post-hoc refinement repairs after the fact rather than reasoning during generation. No prior method achieves a tightly coupled loop where textual reasoning and visual generation mutually inform each other at each step. Methodology: The paper introduces process-driven image generation, which decomposes synthesis into a recurring four-stage cycle—Plan, Sketch, Inspect, Refine—applied iteratively until a final image is produced. At each iteration: (1) Plan generates a step-level instruction <ins> and global scene description <des>; (2) Sketch synthesizes a partial visual draft conditioned on prior context; (3) Inspect checks the draft against both the step instruction and the overall prompt to detect misalignments; (4) Refine emits a corrective instruction <refine> and updates the image if conflicts are found. The model is a fine-tuned BAGEL-7B unified multimodal model trained to autoregressively emit interleaved text and vision tokens, with a combined cross-entropy loss on text tokens and rectified-flow MSE loss on image tokens. Training data is constructed from three complementary subsets: (a) a Multi-Turn Generation subset (32K samples) built via scene-graph subsampling to yield contradiction-free incremental prompts, synthesized with Flux-Kontext and filtered with GPT; (b) an Instruction-Intermediate Conflict subset (15K samples) built by self-sampling intermediate trajectories from the fine-tuned model and using GPT to label and explain alignment failures; and (c) an Image-Instruction Alignment subset (15K samples) extending the Gen-Ref dataset with positive/negative image-instruction pairs. A key design choice is supervising concrete semantic visual states (semantic partitioning) rather than blurry diffusion latent noise as in prior work. Key Results: On GenEval (compositional text-to-image), the method lifts BAGEL-7B from 0.77 to 0.83 (+6% absolute), with particularly large gains on Position (0.51→0.72) and Color Attributes (0.56→0.69)—categories requiring precise spatial and cross-modal reasoning. This matches or exceeds the 12B-parameter FLUX.1-dev (0.82) using a 7B model. On the WISE world-knowledge benchmark, it improves BAGEL from 0.70 to 0.76 (+8.5% absolute), with nearly +15% on Time and Chemistry sub-categories. Compared to the process-level baseline PARM (RL+TTS), which scores 0.77 on GenEval using 688K training samples and 1000 cumulative inference steps, this method achieves 0.83 with only 62K training samples (11× reduction) and 131 inference steps (≈8× speedup). Ablations show self-sampled critiques outperform scene-graph-derived symbolic corrections by +6% on attributes, and combining instruction-intermediate conflict supervision with image-instruction alignment supervision is strictly better than either alone, with Counting improving by +12% from the visual alignment component. Strengths: - The process-driven paradigm is well-motivated and cleanly executed: the Plan→Sketch→Inspect→Refine loop is tightly coupled, with each visual state grounding the next textual step, unlike prior decoupled or post-hoc approaches. - The self-sampling strategy for critique data is a principled insight—supervising the model on its own failure distribution rather than symbolic corrections yields substantially better generalization, with a clear ablation validating this. - Efficiency gains over PARM are striking: 11× less training data and 8× fewer inference steps with higher accuracy, driven by the semantic partitioning insight over latent-noise supervision. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The approach relies heavily on Flux-Kontext for generating intermediate visual ground truth and GPT-4o for filtering and labeling; the quality and cost of the data pipeline are not deeply analyzed, making reproducibility uncertain. - Evaluation is limited to two benchmarks (GenEval, WISE); there is no assessment on human perceptual quality, photorealism, or prompt-following for open-ended, non-compositional prompts where single-pass models already perform well. - The number of reasoning iterations is not systematically studied—the paper reports an average of 2.62 steps but does not analyze failure cases where the inspect-refine loop diverges, oscillates, or fails to converge on hard prompts.
Recommended 2604.04746 PDF
#35 — Innovative (74)

Beyond Accuracy: Unveiling Inefficiency Patterns in Tool-Integrated Reasoning (29 ▲ )

Qisheng Su, Shiting Huang, Zhen Fang, Ziyan Chen, Zehui Chen, Feng Zhao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 74 for introducing PTE, a principled hardware-aware efficiency metric grounded in the roofline model that achieves r=0.925 correlation with wall-clock latency versus r=-0.375 for naive token counts — a stark and practically important gap. The identification and quantification of four distinct inefficiency patterns (confirmatory tool usage, tool-mixing, lack of tool priors, format collapse) with detection heuristics provides actionable diagnostic value. However, the metric ignores real tool latency and batching effects, and the causal claims linking efficiency to accuracy remain underspecified.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Tool-Integrated Reasoning (TIR) — where LLMs interleave chain-of-thought with external tool calls — introduces hardware costs that existing efficiency metrics fundamentally misrepresent. Token counts and tool-call counts treat all tokens as equal, ignoring two critical real-world phenomena: (1) KV-Cache eviction during tool-execution pauses forces expensive context recomputation at the next LLM turn; and (2) long, unfiltered tool responses inflate context length, making every subsequent memory-bound decode step progressively more expensive. The net result is that SOTA models with near-identical task accuracy can differ by orders of magnitude in actual wall-clock latency, yet this gap is invisible to current benchmarks. Methodology: The paper introduces PTE (Prefill Token Equivalents), a hardware-aware efficiency metric grounded in the roofline model of transformer inference. PTE decomposes each reasoning turn into a compute-bound prefill cost (proportional to context tokens) and a memory-bound decode cost (proportional to generated tokens times cumulative context length), unified by a dimensionless coefficient γ = (2·n_layers·d_model·HOI) / N_params, where HOI is the hardware's peak FLOPs-to-bandwidth ratio. γ is refined for GQA (scaled by KV/query head ratio) and MLA architectures (substituting compressed dimensions). The total trajectory cost is PTE = Σ_i (D_prefill_i + γ·L_seq_i·D_decode_i), which explicitly penalizes growing context from both tool responses and prior reasoning. PTE is validated against wall-clock latency on a DeepSeek-V3.2 deployment (8×H200, 256 concurrent requests) and tested for rank-order robustness across H100, H200, A100, RTX 4090, and V100. Twelve open-source models (Qwen 2.5/3, LLaMA 3.1, GLM-4.5, DeepSeek-V3.1, GPT-OSS-120B) are evaluated on five benchmarks: MATH500, AIME 2024/2025, SimpleQA, and WebInstruct-Verified, using Search, Visit, and Python tools. Key Results: PTE achieves r=0.925 Pearson correlation with per-step wall-clock latency (N=100), versus r=−0.375 for raw token counts and r≤0.758 for commercial API pricing metrics. Model efficiency rankings are stable across hardware (Spearman ρ>0.95 on all tested GPUs despite γ scaling from 0.18× to 1.00× relative to H100). Four inefficiency patterns are identified and quantified: (1) Confirmatory Tool Usage — models compute answers internally then verify with tools; occurs in 81% of Qwen3-235B-Instruct MATH500 trajectories at 1.77× PTE overhead; (2) Tool-Mixing — alternating between toolsets in a single trajectory (only DeepSeek-V3.1-Terminus does this consistently); 59% frequency, 2.42× PTE overhead with no accuracy gain; (3) Lack of Tool Priors — Qwen 2.5 series loses accuracy and incurs 2.15× higher PTE on AIME when Python tool is provided, because models forget to print results; (4) Tool Format Collapse — Tongyi-Deepresearch fails on 100% of SimpleQA trajectories due to tool name/schema mismatch from training. Critically, incorrect trajectories exhibit consistently higher PTE than correct ones across all five benchmarks and all models. Partial correlation analysis (controlling for difficulty level) confirms a significant negative PTE–accuracy association (r=−0.040, p=0.002). Thinking-mode models (Qwen3-235B-Thinking) show +16.7% accuracy on AIME25 at 1.8× PTE versus the non-thinking variant, but −3.4% accuracy at 4.2× PTE on the easier SimpleQA, demonstrating task-dependent value of extended reasoning. Strengths: - The PTE formulation is principled and hardware-grounded, deriving γ analytically from model architecture and hardware specs rather than fitting empirical curves, making it interpretable and portable across deployments. - The industrial validation (256-concurrent-request, 8×H200, real tool calls) is unusually rigorous for a metrics paper; the r=0.925 vs r=−0.375 comparison is stark and credible. - Identification and quantification of four distinct inefficiency patterns with detection heuristics, frequency statistics, and PTE cost multipliers provides actionable diagnostic value for training and inference optimization. - The finding that higher PTE correlates with lower accuracy (not just higher cost) reshapes how tool-use RL reward shaping should be framed. Weaknesses / Limitations: - PTE models transformer computation only; it ignores real-world tool latency (API round-trips, Python sandbox startup), network jitter, and batching effects, all of which can dominate wall-clock time in practice and weaken the metric's operational utility. - The γ coefficient assumes a single static Hardware Operational Intensity for the entire inference session, missing dynamic effects such as prefill/decode disaggregation (as in DistServe), speculative decoding, or KV-cache compression, which increasingly characterize production deployments. - The negative correlation between PTE and accuracy is correlational, and the paper explicitly acknowledges it cannot establish causality; confounders (e.g., harder problems naturally trigger more tool calls) are controlled only partially via difficulty stratification on MATH500/AIME25, leaving the causal mechanism underspecified.
Recommended 2604.05404 PDF
#36 — Innovative (74)

ThinkTwice: Jointly Optimizing Large Language Models for Reasoning and Self-Refinement (29 ▲ )

Difan Jiao, Qianfeng Wen, Blair Yang, Zhenwei Tang, Ashton Anderson · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Apr 2026
Why this rank: ThinkTwice presents an elegantly simple framework—two sequential GRPO steps per batch with a single generic prompt and binary reward—that jointly improves both base reasoning and self-refinement without any process supervision, critique models, or correctness hints. The emergent 'rectify-then-fortify' curriculum, where training naturally transitions from error correction to answer preservation, is a genuine mechanistic insight that elevates this beyond a trick. The cross-model refinement generalization is compelling. However, evaluation is limited to math reasoning, base reasoning gains without refinement are modest, and multi-step refinement behavior is unexplored.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs trained with reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) can still produce correctable errors—incomplete derivations, algebraic mistakes, unproductive solution paths. Self-refinement (giving the model a second pass at its own output) is a natural remedy, but existing approaches are unsatisfying: training-free prompting methods do not learn a reusable refinement policy and can even hurt frontier models on hard benchmarks (GPT-4.5 loses points on AIME24 after self-refinement prompting). Training-based methods that do learn refinement invariably rely on process supervision, critique annotations, or explicit signals indicating whether the initial answer is correct—supervision that is unavailable at the frontier. The paper asks: can a single RLVR framework jointly improve both first-pass reasoning and self-refinement using nothing beyond the same sparse binary correctness reward? Methodology: ThinkTwice wraps standard GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) in a two-phase training loop that processes each batch of problems twice. Phase 1 is ordinary RLVR: the shared policy generates G candidate solutions per problem, computes binary correctness rewards, and updates via the standard clipped surrogate objective. Phase 2 immediately follows, using the same batch: one base solution per problem is randomly sampled from Phase 1's rollouts, a multi-turn refinement prompt is constructed (original problem + base solution + a generic task-agnostic review instruction containing no correctness signal), and the now-updated policy generates G refinement attempts, receiving correctness rewards that drive a second GRPO update. The random sampling of base solutions over training creates an implicit curriculum: early in training when most solutions are wrong, Phase 2 predominantly trains error correction; as the base policy improves, Phase 2 naturally shifts toward preserving and polishing already-correct solutions. No verifier, critic, correctness hint, or separate teacher model is involved at any point. The approach adds a standard multi-turn conversation to native GRPO with minimal implementation complexity. Key Results: Evaluated across five mathematical reasoning benchmarks (AIME, AMC, MATH500, Minerva Math, OlympiadBench) with Qwen3-4B and OLMo3-7B, ThinkTwice consistently leads all baselines (GRPO, Dr.GRPO, DAPO) on both direct reasoning (pass@4 before any refinement) and self-refinement (pass@4 after one refinement step). For Qwen3-4B, ThinkTwice achieves 65.57% average reasoning pass@4 vs. 64.53% for DAPO and 62.22% for GRPO. On the hardest benchmark (AIME), ThinkTwice reaches 44.11% reasoning vs. 39.06% for GRPO (+5 points) and 60.43% after one refinement vs. 48.91% for GRPO (+11.5 points). In cross-model refinement experiments—where each model is used as a refiner applied to solutions from every other model—ThinkTwice as the refinement model achieves the highest score regardless of whose base solution it refines, demonstrating generalizable refinement capability. Training cost is minimal: ThinkTwice adds only 3% wall-clock overhead at the same step count and actually converges to its best checkpoint 16% faster (7.2h vs. 8.6h) due to an enriched reward signal. Training dynamics reveal a 'rectify-then-fortify' pattern: ThinkTwice maintains a higher fix-wrong rate than GRPO throughout training, while its damage-correct rate drops near zero in the second half, and refined responses grow progressively shorter (indicating genuine compression, not padding). Strengths: - Elegantly simple: the entire method is two sequential GRPO steps per batch using one generic prompt and the same binary correctness signal; no new loss terms, verifiers, or annotations. - Simultaneous improvement of both reasoning and refinement within a single shared policy is non-obvious; naively one might expect that refinement training degrades base reasoning or vice versa—the paper shows the opposite. - The emergent 'rectify-then-fortify' curriculum is a genuine scientific contribution: it explains mechanistically why the method works and is supported by quantitative transition metrics and detailed qualitative case studies across training checkpoints. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is confined to mathematical reasoning with symbolic, exact-match verifiable answers; the authors acknowledge the domain-agnostic design but provide no experiments on code generation, scientific reasoning, or other verifiable domains where the method's universality claims would need confirmation. - The improvement in base reasoning (Phase 1 only, no refinement applied) is real but modest—ThinkTwice beats GRPO by ~5 points on AIME but trails DAPO on several easier benchmarks (AMC, MATH500); the large gains materialize primarily in the refinement setting, so the method's value for users not running a refinement step at inference is less clear. - Only one refinement step is evaluated at test time; the training framework natively supports multi-turn refinement, but the paper does not study whether iterating refinement further continues to help or whether it degrades, leaving the method's behavior under multi-step deployment unexplored.
Recommended 2604.01591 PDF
#37 — Innovative (74)

Predicting Dynamics of Ultra-Large Complex Systems by Inferring Governing Equations

Qi Shao, Duxin Chen, Jiawen Chen, Yujie Zeng, Athen Ma, Wenwu Yu, Vito Latora, Wei Lin · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: SIGN's core architectural insight — decoupling symbolic support identification from coefficient estimation to make parameter count independent of network size — is a genuinely creative methodological contribution that enables simultaneous interpretability and scalability to 10^5 nodes, something no prior framework achieved. The two-phase design (DBSCAN consensus mask + mask-constrained GNN) elegantly reframes per-node sparsity inconsistency as a clustering problem. However, the load-bearing node-invariant assumption limits applicability to heterogeneous real-world systems, and the SST demonstration's modest R²=0.687 despite favorable MAPE reveals limitations the paper underemphasizes.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Predicting the collective dynamics of ultra-large networked systems (O(10^4–10^5) nodes) is a fundamental open challenge in domains ranging from climate science to neuroscience and epidemiology. Existing approaches face a hard trade-off: sparse identification methods like SINDy recover interpretable governing equations but require per-node regressions that scale linearly with network size and yield inconsistent sparsity patterns across nodes; neural predictors (GNNs, physics-informed networks) scale to large systems but produce black-box representations that generalize poorly under noise, partial observability, or distributional shift. No prior framework simultaneously achieves interpretable equation discovery and reliable long-horizon prediction at the scale of 10^5 nodes. Methodology: The paper introduces SIGN (Sparse Identification Graph Neural Network), a two-phase framework that decouples symbolic structure discovery from network scale by exploiting the node-invariant structure of networked dynamics — the assumption that all nodes share the same intrinsic function F(x_i) and pairwise coupling function C(x_i, x_j). Phase I samples a small subset of k=50 nodes, solves Lasso-type sparse regressions at each sampled node to obtain binary support masks, then applies DBSCAN clustering to extract a consensus global sparsity mask that filters spurious terms. Phase II trains a GNN with parameters constrained to the identified support mask, propagating dynamical information across the full graph topology to learn a fixed-size, node-count-independent set of shared coefficients. Because the parameter count depends only on the number of active basis terms (not n), inference on 10^5 nodes is computationally tractable. Training uses a multi-step rollout loss over trajectory segments. Prediction is then obtained by numerically integrating the inferred ODEs forward from initial conditions — no separate predictor is needed. For climate data lacking known connectivity, spatial proximity graphs are constructed (edges within 0.3° distance), and time-dependent Fourier basis functions are added to F to capture seasonal forcing. Key Results: SIGN is validated on six benchmark dynamical systems: Kuramoto synchronization, SIS epidemic spreading, Michaelis-Menten gene regulation, coupled Rössler oscillators, FitzHugh-Nagumo (FHN) neuronal dynamics, and Hindmarsh-Rose (HR) neurons. Across all benchmarks on networks up to 10^5 nodes, coefficient recovery errors (sMAPE) remain below 1%. In robustness studies on 10^5-node networks: errors degrade gracefully down to SNR=60 dB noise; accurate recovery is maintained with as few as 200 temporal samples; up to 20% node removal and 40% edge deletion are tolerated. SIGN achieves sMAPE ≤1% vs. ~10% for TP-SINDy and LaGNA baselines, while completing inference on 10^5 nodes in a few hundred seconds versus substantially longer for baselines. For large-scale FHN neural prediction (22,000+ nodes, 2,000 time steps) under SNR=50 dB noise, SIGN achieves R²=0.9999 and outperforms five GNN baselines (MTGNN, ASTGCN, MSTGCN, STSGCN, STGCN) on both RMSE and MAPE. For real-world sea surface temperature prediction across 71,987 spatial nodes over a decade (2002–2012), SIGN achieves mean training MAPE=1.93% and test MAPE=3.55% over a 2-year forecast horizon, recovering a compact interpretable SST equation with diffusive spatial coupling and Fourier-mode periodic forcing. Strengths: - The decoupling of symbolic support identification from coefficient estimation is the key architectural insight: it makes the parameter count independent of network size, enabling genuine scalability to 10^5 nodes while retaining full equation interpretability — something no prior method achieves simultaneously. - The two-phase design (DBSCAN consensus mask + mask-constrained GNN) is technically clean and elegantly handles the inconsistency problem of per-node SINDy by treating sparsity patterns as a clustering problem over noisy estimates of a shared ground truth. - The real-world SST demonstration on 71,987 nodes with monthly observations over 10 years is a compelling proof of concept, recovering a physically meaningful equation (diffusive coupling + Fourier seasonal forcing) and sustaining 2-year out-of-sample predictions. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The node-invariant assumption (shared F and C across all nodes) is the load-bearing structural prior and is violated by many real systems — heterogeneous biological networks, power grids with varied node types, and multi-layer or directed networks.
