The paper acknowledges this but does not evaluate settings with significant functional heterogeneity beyond varying Kuramoto natural frequencies. - The SST results show a modest test-set R²=0.687 despite low absolute MAPE, indicating the model captures mean trends but misses local variance and fine-scale fluctuations — the gap between smooth ODE integration and the true stochastic, externally-forced climate system is papered over by the MAPE metric, which can look favorable even when variance is poorly captured. - The framework requires the network topology (adjacency matrix A) to be known or constructable from spatial proximity; the connectivity inference problem for systems without natural geometric structure is out of scope and unaddressed, limiting applicability to domains where topology must itself be inferred from data.
Search Results 2604.00599 PDF
#38 — Innovative (74)

Scheduling LLM Inference with Uncertainty-Aware Output Length Predictions

Haoyu Zheng, Yongqiang Zhang, Fangcheng Fu, Xiaokai Zhou, Hao Luo, Hongchao Zhu, Yuanyuan Zhu, Hao Wang, Xiao Yan, Jiawei Jiang · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: This paper scores 74 for its theoretically grounded insight that LLM output lengths follow heavy-tailed log-t distributions, supported by both a formal proof from EOS sampling dynamics and systematic empirical validation across 6 distribution families. The TIE scheduling metric is elegantly simple — a drop-in scalar replacement for SJF — yet delivers consistent 2-3× latency improvements across 8 model families, MoE architectures, and multiple datasets. The adaptive CVaR component that responds to queue pressure is a principled design choice. The main limitation is the expensive training data requirement (20 repeated generations per prompt), which creates a practical deployment barrier.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM inference schedulers based on the Shortest-Job-First (SJF) principle require predicting output lengths to prioritize short requests and avoid head-of-line (HOL) blocking. Existing methods (SSJF, LTR, TRAIL, ELIS) produce a single point estimate per request, which fundamentally mismatches the stochastic nature of autoregressive decoding — the same prompt can produce very different output lengths across runs depending on which tokens are sampled. Point estimates also struggle with the heavy-tailed output length distributions observed empirically, where a small fraction of very long requests disproportionately degrades throughput and latency for all other requests. Methodology: The paper makes two core contributions. First, it establishes that LLM output lengths follow a heavy-tailed distribution well-fit by the log-t distribution (a log-transformed Student's t). This is supported both empirically (KS test pass rate of 90.6% with fixed ν=3.5 on 1K prompts × 100 generations each, versus 60.3% for log-normal) and theoretically via a formal proof that shows power-law tail decay under a mild assumption on the distribution of per-trajectory EOS termination rates. Distribution parameters (μ, σ) for each incoming prompt are predicted by a fine-tuned DeBERTa-v3-base encoder with multi-pooling feature fusion and separate MLP heads, achieving R² of 0.82 and 0.76 respectively. Second, it introduces the Tail Inflated Expectation (TIE) scheduling metric: Score = E[X̃] + β·CVaR_α[X̃], where X̃ is the max-tokens-censored predicted distribution, α=0.9 captures the top-10% tail, and β is adaptively scaled by queue pressure (β = min(0.5, max(0.1, 0.1·L_q/B))). This replaces the point estimate in SJF ordering. The system is implemented on vLLM with asynchronous batched prediction (3ms timeout, batch size 32) and a min-heap waiting queue to decouple prediction latency from scheduling decisions. Starvation prevention uses exponential waiting-time score decay (γ=0.9, τ=30s). Key Results: On online chatbot serving (LMSYS-Chat-1M, 8B model, 100 RPS), TIE reduces average per-token latency by 4.73× vs. FCFS, 2.91× vs. SSJF, and 2.31× vs. LTR. P90 latency improvements are similar. For offline synthetic data generation (Alpaca dataset), TIE reduces time-to-3K-samples by 2.34× vs. FCFS and 1.42× vs. LTR, and improves 3-minute throughput to 4,762 vs. 3,672 (LTR) and 2,292 (FCFS). Generalization across the 70B Llama model and cross-dataset transfer (trained on LMSYS-Chat-1M, tested on ShareGPT and Alpaca) remains strong — TIE achieves 3.77× PTLA speedup over FCFS on the 70B model. Results hold across 8 additional model families including MoE architectures (Qwen3, Mixtral) and reasoning models (DeepSeek-R1). Scheduling overhead is negligible: 4.26 ms per request vs. average TTFT of 48.23 s. Ablations confirm that log-t outperforms log-normal significantly, and that adaptive CVaR provides meaningful gains over pure expectation (SEPT) scheduling. Strengths: - The distributional framing is well-motivated both theoretically (formal heavy-tail proof from EOS sampling dynamics) and empirically (systematic KS test comparison across 6 distribution families). - The TIE metric is simple and principled — it reduces to a single scalar replacement in SJF with no architectural changes to the serving system, and the asynchronous prediction design avoids the latency penalty of synchronous approaches. - Evaluation breadth is strong: 2 primary models + 6 additional families including MoE, 3 datasets, both online and offline scenarios, with consistent wins throughout. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Training data collection requires generating 20 repeated responses per prompt to fit distribution parameters — this is expensive (45K prompts × 20 generations = 900K samples) and cannot leverage existing single-response serving logs directly, creating a barrier to deployment on new workloads. - The theoretical heavy-tail derivation relies on Assumption 3.1 (termination rate follows a power-law near zero), which is reasonable but is assumed rather than verified from real model internals; the mapping from trajectory-level termination rates to the per-prompt log-t distribution involves a simplifying independence assumption (fixed effective termination rate p̃) that may not hold in practice. - The paper only compares against non-preemptive baselines; preemptive methods (TRAIL, ELIS) are discussed qualitatively but not experimentally benchmarked, making it unclear whether TIE's gains over the best preemptive approaches justify the offline training cost.
Search Results 2604.00499 PDF
#39 — Innovative (74)

Watch Before You Answer: Learning from Visually Grounded Post-Training (24 ▲ )

Yuxuan Zhang, EunJeong Hwang, Huaisong Zhang, Penghui Du, Yiming Jia, Dongfu Jiang, Xuan He, Shenhui Zhang, Ping Nie, Peter West, Kelsey R. Allen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Apr 2026
Why this rank: This paper makes a valuable diagnostic contribution by rigorously quantifying that 40–60% of long video understanding benchmarks are answerable from text alone, reframing reported progress as linguistic bias amplification rather than visual grounding. The VidGround intervention — filtering training data via text-only answerability — is elegantly simple yet outperforms more complex RL methods, establishing data quality as the primary bottleneck. The frame-scaling monotonicity analysis provides a practical and interpretable signal for grounding quality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-language models (VLMs) have shown persistent gaps in video understanding relative to text-based reasoning, yet the community has assumed steady progress. This paper reveals the situation is worse than previously recognized: 40–60% of questions in commonly used long video understanding benchmarks (VideoMME, VideoMMMU, MMVU) can be answered correctly using question text alone, without accessing any video. The same linguistic bias infects post-training datasets — nearly 31% of Video-R1-260K is text-only answerable. As frontier models scale, their benchmark gains stem increasingly from stronger language priors rather than improved visual grounding, while visual gain often stagnates or regresses. This benchmark contamination makes reported progress misleading and post-training on contaminated data actively harmful. Methodology: The authors define two question categories: text-only answerable (TA) — questions correctly answered by a frontier LLM given only question text and answer choices, no video — and visually grounded (VG) — questions that require visual input. Four TA categories are identified: textual/linguistic cues, external commonsense knowledge, inferential elimination strategies, and hallucinated video content. VidGround is a post-training recipe combining: (1) data curation — filtering Video-R1-260K using GPT-5-mini in text-only mode, retaining only the 181,710 (69.1%) questions it cannot answer correctly; (2) RL-based post-training — GRPO augmented with DAPO-style token-level policy gradient loss and asymmetric clipping, plus temporal-aware rewards from Video-R1, applied to Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct for 700 steps on 8×H100 GPUs. Robustness of the data curation is validated via multi-model agreement (Jaccard index 68.7% across GPT-5-mini, Qwen2.5-VL-7B, Gemini-3.1-Pro) and circular evaluation to rule out positional bias. Multi-model consensus variants (VidGround-M1 at 161K samples, VidGround-M2 at 148K) are also tested. Key Results: VidGround outperforms all five 7B-scale post-training baselines (Video-R1, TW-GRPO, Video-RTS, LongVILA-R1, Qwen2.5-VL-7B-SFT) across VideoMME, VideoMMMU, and MMVU at 16, 32, and 64 frames. Compared to Video-R1 (trained on unfiltered 263K samples), VidGround improves Full Avg by 4.8, 4.6, and 6.2 points at 16/32/64 frames respectively, using only 69.1% of the data. Relative to the Qwen2.5-VL-7B base, gains are +2.2–+2.4 on Full Avg. VidGround exhibits consistent frame-scaling: accuracy monotonically increases from 16→32→64 frames (56.8→58.5→59.5 Full Avg), while baselines trained on full data plateau or regress (52.0→53.9→53.3 for GRPO-Full). Ablations confirm data composition dominates: the asymmetric clipping variant adds minimal value on top of VG data alone. Cross-task evaluation shows VidGround also improves image QA benchmarks (MME: 648.9 vs. 624.3; MMMU: 58.7 vs. 56.7), ruling out distribution narrowing. Multi-model curation (VidGround-M1) slightly outperforms the single-model variant on some splits but is substantially more expensive; overly strict curation (VidGround-M2) degrades performance. Qualitative analysis shows VidGround reasoning chains ground claims in video observations, while Video-R1 defaults to text-based elimination — even when both reach the correct answer. Strengths: - Compelling diagnostic: systematically quantifies TA contamination across 17 frontier models spanning VLMs and text-only LLMs, demonstrating the problem is universal and growing as models scale. - Elegant intervention: data curation via text-only answerability filtering is simple, cheap (one LLM inference pass over training data), and broadly applicable to any VLM post-training pipeline. - Strong empirical evidence: VidGround beats more complex RL algorithms (TW-GRPO, Video-RTS) that use specialized token weighting or adaptive frame selection, establishing data quality as the primary bottleneck. - Frame-scaling behavior provides a mechanistically interpretable signal: VG-trained models improve monotonically with frames, while linguistically biased models plateau — this is a practical diagnostic for whether post-training truly improves visual grounding. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The TA/VG binary label depends on a snapshot capability of the filtering model (GPT-5-mini); as models improve, questions currently classified VG will become TA, requiring periodic re-filtering. No mechanism for self-refreshing curation is proposed. - Evaluation benchmarks themselves are contaminated by the same linguistic bias problem, so reported gains on VideoMME/VideoMMMU/MMVU are partly measuring improved text reasoning on the VG subset — the true improvement in visual understanding may be smaller or larger than reported numbers suggest. - Experiments are limited to Qwen2.5-VL-7B and a single post-training dataset (Video-R1-260K); generalizability to other base models, architectures, or data sources is not demonstrated. - Only 16 frames are sampled during training despite evaluating at 16/32/64 frames; the effect of training frame count on visual grounding quality is not ablated.
Recommended 2604.05117 PDF
#40 — Innovative (73)

AdaRubric: Task-Adaptive Rubrics for LLM Agent Evaluation

Liang Ding · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: AdaRubric's core insight — that evaluation dimensions should be derived from the task description rather than fixed globally — is a meaningful conceptual advance over static LLM-as-Judge frameworks. The formal proof that no scalar threshold can prevent dimension-level masking (Proposition J.2) grounds an empirical design choice in theory, and the end-to-end demonstration from improved evaluation correlation through DPO/PPO training gains across five diverse benchmarks is convincing. However, the approach is ultimately a well-engineered application of LLMs generating structured rubrics, and the 3–5× latency overhead and reliance on capable backbone LLMs limit practical novelty.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Evaluating LLM agent trajectories is fundamentally broken by static rubrics. Standard LLM-as-Judge frameworks apply fixed dimensions—Helpfulness, Fluency, Safety—originally designed for chat assistants, to goal-directed agents operating through tool use and multi-step planning. A ToolBench API-chaining task requires assessing API Selection Accuracy, Parameter Correctness, and Error Recovery; none of these appear in a standard helpfulness rubric. Conversely, a fluency-focused rubric penalizes agents that correctly call APIs but produce compact, machine-readable output. Reference-based metrics (ROUGE-L, BERTScore) measure surface overlap and are blind to goal-directed reasoning. The paper argues that evaluation dimensions must be a function of the task itself, not a fixed property of the evaluator. Methodology: ADARUBRIC is a three-stage pipeline. Stage 1 (Adaptive Rubric Generation): given a task description T, an LLM generates a dynamic rubric R(T) comprising N=5 task-specific, orthogonal evaluation dimensions, each with a weight w_j and verbalized 5-point scoring criteria. Rubrics pass automated validation (cosine distance between dimension names, weight sum, score level completeness) and are cached per task type, reducing API cost by >95%. Stage 2 (Confidence-Weighted Trajectory Evaluation): for each step k and dimension j, the evaluator LLM produces a score s_{k,j} ∈ {1..5} and a confidence c_{k,j} ∈ [0,1], where confidence is low when the step does not directly engage that dimension. Three pluggable aggregation strategies are provided—Weighted Mean (default, with recency decay λ), Geometric Mean (enforces balance), and Min Score (for safety-critical tasks). Stage 3 (DimensionAwareFilter): a composable filter that requires every dimension to clear a per-dimension threshold θ_j, preventing a trajectory with high scores on most dimensions from masking a catastrophic failure on one. This filter is formally proven to be the minimal additional constraint that closes the masking gap that any scalar threshold leaves open (Proposition J.2, via Gauss-Markov and Cauchy-Schwarz). Filtered trajectories are paired by score margin δ_min to produce DPO preference pairs, which can also serve directly as PPO reward signals. Key Results: On WebArena (812 tasks) and ToolBench (500 tasks), ADARUBRIC-DA (DimensionAwareFilter variant) achieves Pearson r=0.79 and r=0.74 human correlation respectively, versus r=0.64/0.60 for GPT-4 Direct (the best static baseline)—a +0.15 average improvement. Reliability (Krippendorff's α) reaches 0.85/0.82, comfortably above the proposed deployment threshold of α≥0.80. For downstream DPO training on Qwen2.5-7B, ADARUBRIC-DA yields 27.8% WebArena task success rate, +6.8 pp over Prometheus and +15.5 pp over the zero-shot base. Across all three benchmarks (WebArena, ToolBench, AgentBench), gains over Prometheus are +6.8/+8.5/+7.7 pp. On SWE-bench Lite—an unseen task type requiring real GitHub issue repair—ADARUBRIC-DA achieves r=0.77 and a 14.7% resolve rate (+4.9 pp over GPT-4 Direct) with zero rubric engineering. In PPO-based online RL, ADARUBRIC-DA rewards reach 30.2% WebArena SR at 5K steps, +6.6 pp above Prometheus, with faster early convergence (+4.3 pp at 1K steps). An ablation confirms adaptive dimension generation is the dominant driver (+0.14 r over generic fixed rubric); confidence weighting adds +0.03; DimensionAwareFilter adds +0.04. Human study of 60 tasks rates ADARUBRIC-generated rubrics at 4.3/4.2/4.1 on relevance/orthogonality/completeness—only 0.3 below expert-designed rubrics. Cross-domain transfer experiments show ADARUBRIC preference pairs trained on WebArena achieve 31.2% ToolBench TCR, exceeding Prometheus in-domain performance (29.3%). Strengths: - The core insight—that evaluation criteria should be derived from the task description, not fixed globally—is well-motivated and the empirical case is convincing across five diverse benchmarks spanning web automation, API orchestration, code repair, and multimodal tasks. - The DimensionAwareFilter is accompanied by a formal proof (Proposition J.2) that no scalar threshold can prevent dimension-level masking, grounding an empirical design choice in theory; similarly, Proposition J.1 provides Gauss-Markov justification for confidence-weighted aggregation. - End-to-end utility is demonstrated rigorously: improvements propagate from better evaluation correlation all the way through DPO training and PPO convergence on held-out benchmarks, with consistent cross-domain transfer gains that static rubric methods cannot match. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is a single-author arXiv preprint that self-cites a concurrent work (AgentHER, also by the same author, also under review), raising questions about independent corroboration and whether the experimental comparisons were conducted under fully controlled conditions. - Computational cost is non-trivial—ADARUBRIC runs K×N LLM calls per trajectory (40 calls for WebArena vs. 1 for GPT-4 Direct); while rubric caching mitigates this, the 3–5× latency overhead and requirement for a capable backbone LLM (performance degrades ~0.11 Pearson r going from GPT-4o to Llama-3.1-8B) may limit practical deployment for large-scale trajectory datasets or weaker evaluator models. - Confidence scores are LLM-predicted and rest on an idealized Gaussian noise model (Eq. 10) that the paper itself acknowledges may not be well-calibrated for out-of-distribution tasks; error analysis reveals 32% of disagreement cases involve long-horizon binary goals where ADARUBRIC systematically over-credits partial completion, a structural flaw not fully addressed.
Recommended 2603.21362 PDF
#41 — Innovative (73)

Asymmetric Actor-Critic for Multi-turn LLM Agents

Shuli Jiang, Zhaoyang Zhang, Yi Zhang, Shuo Yang, Wei Xia, Stefano Soatto · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This paper introduces a genuinely novel asymmetric actor-critic framework that exploits the generation-verification gap between large proprietary and small open-source models. The empirical finding that an 8B-parameter critic outperforms Claude Sonnet as a critic supervising the same actor is surprising and counterintuitive, validating a non-obvious hypothesis with practical significance. The self-play data generation pipeline for critic training fills a real gap in existing methodology. However, the modest evaluation scale (50-115 test instances), domain-specific critic design, and hand-engineered gating function temper the generalizability claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-turn LLM agents deployed in real-world customer-facing settings must often succeed in a single interaction—retries are impossible and failures are costly. Existing approaches fall into two unsatisfying camps: (1) reflection/self-correction methods that require multiple attempts, and (2) symmetric actor–critic frameworks from RL that assume both actor and critic are open-source and jointly trainable, with structured (enumerable) action spaces. Neither addresses the practical regime where the best actor is a proprietary, fixed LLM operating over open-ended conversational language. The paper asks whether a system can leverage proprietary LLM capabilities while retaining adaptability and controllability of open-source models, without retries or actor fine-tuning. Methodology: The paper proposes an Asymmetric Actor–Critic (Asym-AC) framework with two heterogeneous components: a fixed proprietary actor (Claude-4 Sonnet) that generates responses and tool calls, and a lightweight trainable open-source critic (Qwen3 family) that monitors the actor at runtime and intervenes within the same trajectory. At each turn, the actor proposes a candidate action; a gating function SHOULDINTERVENE decides whether to invoke the critic. If invoked, the critic produces verbal feedback (approve, reject, or corrective guidance), which is appended to the context, and the actor generates a revised action. Only the critic is trained; the actor is never updated. The design exploits a generation–verification asymmetry: high-quality generation requires large models, but effective oversight does not. To train the critic, the paper introduces a self-play, critic-in-the-loop data generation pipeline. Existing conversational datasets are repurposed: tasks are inferred from trajectories, replayed with the target actor to identify hard tasks (where the actor fails above a threshold ψ across K runs), and then executed with the actor–critic system. Trajectories where the critic intervenes non-trivially and the task succeeds are segmented into (context + actor proposal, critic feedback) training pairs for supervised fine-tuning of the critic via LoRA on Qwen3-8B. Evaluation covers τ-bench (customer service with explicit policy compliance) and UserBench (travel planning with implicit user preference satisfaction), both emphasizing one-shot success. Key Results: Asym-AC with an off-the-shelf Qwen3-8B critic improves over single-actor Claude-4 Sonnet on τ-bench (0.5998 → 0.6318 average pass@1) and on UserBench (0.3277 → 0.3798 average score). Critic capacity experiments show that using Claude-4 Sonnet as the critic (0.6095) underperforms even the much smaller Qwen3-8B (0.6318) and Qwen3-32B (0.6526), across both benchmarks—demonstrating the generation–verification gap empirically. After critic fine-tuning (Asym-AC-SFT), performance improves further to 0.6546 on τ-bench and 0.4176 on UserBench, outperforming CRITIC (0.6131 / 0.3619), ReAct (0.6085), APIGen-MT's xLAM-2-70b (0.562), and IRMA (0.5275). Qualitative examples show the fine-tuned critic correcting policy violations (incorrect refund payment method), hallucinated constraints (wrongly claiming items cannot be removed from orders), and suboptimal travel recommendations. Strengths: - The generation–verification asymmetry hypothesis is validated concretely: a Qwen3-8B critic (8B parameters) outperforms Claude-4 Sonnet as a critic supervising the same actor, which is a non-obvious and practically significant finding. - The data generation pipeline is critic-oriented rather than actor-oriented, filling a genuine gap—existing datasets provide actor demonstrations but no supervision for intervention decisions; the self-play approach is sample-efficient and does not touch the proprietary actor. - The framework is applicable to real deployment constraints (proprietary APIs, no actor fine-tuning, one-shot execution), making it more practically relevant than prior symmetric actor–critic work. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation scale is modest: τ-bench has only 50–115 test instances per domain, and UserBench 67–101; results are averaged over 5 runs, but the small test sets limit statistical confidence and generalizability claims. - The critic is domain-specific (separate critics for airline vs. retail, a single aggregated critic for UserBench environments), and the intervention gating function SHOULDINTERVENE is hand-engineered per benchmark rather than learned—undermining claims of general applicability. - Runtime latency from the critic evaluation step is acknowledged but not measured or quantified, which is a significant practical concern for interactive deployments the paper explicitly targets. - The comparison to symmetric actor–critic baselines is absent; no experiment shows what a jointly-trained open-source actor–critic achieves on these benchmarks, making it unclear how much performance is left on the table by fixing the actor.
Citations 2604.00304 PDF
#42 — Innovative (73)

Self-Execution Simulation Improves Coding Models (21 ▲ )

Gallil Maimon, Ori Yoran, Felix Kreuk, Michael Hassid, Gal Cohen, Pierre Chambon, Yossi Adi · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 Apr 2026
Why this rank: This paper introduces a genuinely creative approach to a real problem: training LLMs to internally simulate code execution via natural language traces, eliminating the need for external interpreters during self-verification. The NLEX data pipeline (80M automated traces translated to natural language) is a scalable and novel training methodology, and the Self-RLEF context-switching design is technically well-motivated. However, the approach is restricted to single-file competitive programming with simple I/O, the pass@1 gains from Self-RLEF are modest (~2 points), and the fundamental task conflict between solving and simulation remains unresolved.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs generate code but cannot reliably predict how that code will execute — they are essentially blind to their own bugs. Existing approaches use actual code execution as feedback (RLEF), but real execution at scale is problematic: it requires environment setup, dependency management, sandboxing, and can be prohibitively slow (e.g., MLE-Bench runs take up to 9 hours). The gap motivates training models to simulate execution internally, enabling self-verification and self-fixing without any external interpreter. Methodology: The paper presents a two-stage post-training recipe applied to Qwen2.5 (3B, 7B) and CWM base models. Stage 1 is supervised fine-tuning on Natural Language Execution Traces (NLEX): ~80M Python function executions are recorded with a tracer, then translated from structured JSON-like traces into free-form natural language explanations using Qwen3-32B-FP8, yielding a rich SFT dataset grounded in real execution. Stage 2 applies multi-task Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) jointly for (a) output prediction — given code and stdin, predict stdout, with binary ±1 reward — and (b) competitive programming solving. Two inference-time applications are introduced. Best@k simulate: generate k candidate solutions, simulate each on public tests, select the candidate predicted to pass the most tests. Self-RLEF: a multi-turn pipeline where the model iteratively solves, simulates execution, and fixes its code based on predicted (not real) execution feedback, using explicit context-switching between turns to isolate simulation from solution reasoning and avoid long-context degradation. A key training detail: Self-RLEF is trained with ground-truth execution feedback but evaluated with self-predicted feedback, as preliminary results showed no gap between the two. Key Results: On CruxEval-O output prediction, NLEX-trained Qwen2.5-3B improves from 37.5 to 68.0 pass@1 (+81%) and Qwen2.5-7B from 48.5 to 75.5 (+56%), matching or exceeding models many times larger (e.g., Qwen3-14B, Gemma-3-27B). After RLVR, Qwen-7B reaches 79.7/77.3 pass@1 on LCB-IO/DMC output prediction for competitive programming solutions. For self-execution (same model solves and simulates), CWM joint-trained reaches 86.5/80.2 pass@1 vs. official CWM at 68.6/57.7. In the best@k setting, self-verification improves competitive programming solve rates by 2–8 absolute points over standard solving for k≥2, with a modest simulation gap vs. oracle execution. Self-RLEF achieves 63.2/62.3 pass@1 on DMC/LCB-IO vs. 60.8/61.0 for CWM-RL and 65.3/63.8 for the true-execution oracle — the simulation gap on pass@1 is roughly 2 points. CWM also effectively verifies Qwen3-32B solutions, achieving near-oracle best@k performance on external code. Ablations confirm that (1) NLEX data is essential — RL alone without NLEX provides significantly weaker output prediction; (2) the self-RLEF scaffold at inference on an untrained model yields degraded performance, confirming that training (not just the multi-turn structure) drives gains. Strengths: - The NLEX data construction pipeline is practical and scalable: automated tracing + LLM translation produces 80M+ grounded natural language traces without human annotation, and the quality filter (discard where predicted output doesn't match ground truth) is simple and effective. - The multi-turn Self-RLEF context-switching design is technically well-motivated: isolating simulation from solution reasoning prevents contamination by the correct answer and enables clean turn-by-turn reward assignment. - Thorough empirical coverage across two models, two sizes, two benchmarks, and multiple baselines (pass@k, best@k oracle, short1@k) with an explicit simulation gap measurement that honestly quantifies the cost of replacing real execution. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The approach is currently restricted to single-file competitive programming problems with simple stdio interfaces. Applicability to real-world SWE tasks (multi-file repos, external APIs, stateful environments) is entirely unaddressed and left as future work. - Execution simulation inherently fails on computationally intensive operations (large-number arithmetic, transcendental functions, bitwise ops over large integers), introducing systematic noise that is difficult to mitigate with more training data. The method cannot replace true execution for adversarial or precision-sensitive scenarios. - Joint training for both solving and execution prediction degrades output prediction accuracy compared to training for output prediction alone (e.g., CWM joint 80.2 vs. out-pred-only 85.0 pass@1 on DMC), suggesting a fundamental task conflict that the paper does not resolve — users must choose a tradeoff point rather than getting both capabilities for free. - The reported pass@1 gains from Self-RLEF (63.2 vs. 60.8) are modest, and pass@1 under Self-RLEF is actually lower than best@10 from the same model, indicating limited exploration diversity in the iterative fixing regime.
Recommended 2604.03253 PDF
#43 — Innovative (73)

Learning to Retrieve from Agent Trajectories (55 ▲ )

Yuqi Zhou, Sunhao Dai, Changle Qu, Liang Pang, Jun Xu, Ji-Rong Wen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Apr 2026
Why this rank: This paper introduces a genuinely novel paradigm by reconceptualizing agent execution trajectories as a supervision source for retrieval training, analogous to human click logs but with fundamentally different properties. The intensity-aware weighting via reasoning-trace length is a creative signal that has no direct precedent. Results are strong and consistent across a wide range of agent sizes (4B–358B) and retriever architectures, with the self-improving data flywheel demonstrating practical scalability. The core limitation is validation only on closed-corpus Wikipedia settings.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern information retrieval (IR) systems are trained on human interaction logs (clicks, dwell time), but LLM-powered search agents are increasingly the primary consumers of retrieval systems. Agents issue queries not to satisfy immediate informational needs but to advance intermediate reasoning objectives, resulting in fundamentally different relevance patterns than human users. Existing retrievers trained under human-centric assumptions exhibit a mismatch when deployed in agentic settings, causing retrieval quality to emerge as the primary performance bottleneck for search agents. No prior work has addressed how to train retrievers specifically from agent interaction data. Methodology: The authors propose LRAT (Learning to Retrieve from Agent Trajectories), a framework that mines retrieval supervision directly from multi-step agent execution trajectories. The approach begins with an empirical analysis of 34,619 trajectories generated by Tongyi-DeepResearch-30B on InfoSeekQA using four retrievers (BM25, Qwen3-Embedding 0.6B/4B/8B) over a 11.2M-chunk Wikipedia corpus. This analysis surfaces three key findings: (1) browsing actions are a necessary condition for task success (not merely correlated), making browsed documents natural positive candidates; (2) agent browsing exhibits weak position bias—unlike human clicks, browsing is distributed relatively uniformly across rank positions—making unbrowsed documents reliable negatives without debiasing; and (3) post-browse reasoning length is strongly correlated with document utility, distinguishing truly useful documents from superficially browsed ones. LRAT operationalizes these insights via a three-stage pipeline: naive relevance mining from Search→Browse transitions, LLM-based filtering of false positives using post-browse reasoning traces (Qwen3-30B-A3B-Thinking as judge), and intensity-aware training using a weighted InfoNCE loss where weights are derived from a saturating exponential function of reasoning-trace length (analogous to time-aware click models). Negatives combine trajectory-derived unbrowsed documents with standard in-batch negatives. Key Results: LRAT is evaluated on two benchmarks—in-domain InfoSeek-Eval (300 multi-hop queries) and out-of-domain BrowseComp-Plus (830 complex questions)—across six agent backbones ranging from 4B to 358B parameters and two retriever architectures (Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B and Multilingual-E5-Large-Instruct). LRAT yields an average +28.6% gain in success rate on InfoSeek-Eval and +27.5% gain in evidence recall on BrowseComp-Plus. Specific gains include: AgentCPM-4B success rate 40.3%→55.7% (+38.2%), GLM-4.7-358B success rate 67.7%→82.0% (+21.1%), MiniMax-M2.1-229B recall 57.2%→69.2% (+21.0%). Average agent step counts decrease by up to ~30%, indicating more efficient evidence acquisition. Ablation confirms all three components contribute: naive mining alone yields substantial gains, LLM filtering provides further improvement (at the cost of reducing training pairs from 120K to 92K while improving quality), and intensity reweighting adds additional benefit. Training scales log-linearly with data size (no saturation at 30K trajectories). A data flywheel simulation shows steady iterative improvement across 5 loop steps, and even incorrect trajectories provide useful signal (+15-19% over baseline). Strengths: - Conceptually clean paradigm shift: the analogy between agent trajectories and human click logs as supervision sources is well-motivated and empirically grounded with a rigorous trajectory analysis - Strong and consistent empirical results across a wide range of agent architectures (4B–358B), retriever types (sparse BM25, encoder-based E5, decoder-based Qwen3-Embedding), and evaluation settings (in-domain and OOD) - No human annotation required; the method exploits naturally occurring byproducts of agent execution, making it practically scalable and enabling a self-improving data flywheel Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method is validated primarily on Wikipedia-based closed-corpus settings; generalization to open-web retrieval (where document sets are dynamic and unbounded) is not demonstrated and may face challenges from very different browsing distributions - Trajectory generation itself is expensive (26K+ trajectories from a 30B agent on 10K queries), and the quality of mined supervision is inherently limited by the quality of the trajectory-generating agent—weak agents producing few browse actions would yield sparse or noisy training data - The LLM-based filtering step introduces a dependency on a powerful judge model (Qwen3-30B-A3B-Thinking), adding inference cost and a potential bottleneck; the 74.8% retention of browsed non-evidence documents suggests the filter is imprecise, retaining substantial noise among positives
Recommended 2604.04949 PDF
#44 — Innovative (73)

Towards Position-Robust Talent Recommendation via Large Language Models

Silin Du, Hongyan Liu · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: This paper scores 73 for its technically coherent suite of architectural innovations—block attention, local positional encoding, implicit scoring, and ID sampling—each precisely targeted at a well-characterized failure mode (position bias, token bias, quadratic attention cost) in listwise LLM ranking. The theoretical analysis via Abel's transform grounding the local positional encoding design is a notable contribution. Beating >100B parameter models with a 6B fine-tuned model demonstrates that task-specific architectural design can outweigh brute-force scaling, a valuable empirical lesson.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Talent recruitment is costly ($4,700+ average cost per hire) and existing LLM-based talent recommendation systems predominantly use pointwise paradigms — independently scoring each resume against a job posting. This wastes tokens by processing the job description N times, fails to capture comparative relationships among candidates, and is vulnerable to position bias (favoring candidates placed at certain list positions) and token bias (preferring certain ID tokens like 'A' or 'D' due to pretraining priors). These biases are particularly severe in listwise talent recommendation because it combines two bias-prone formats: multi-document processing (like RAG) and ID-based selection (like multiple-choice questions), causing MRR to swing from 0.85 to below 0.10 as a matched resume moves from position 1 to position 15. Methodology: The paper proposes L3TR (Listwise LLM-based Talent Recommendation), which fine-tunes open-source LLMs for listwise candidate ranking via four technical contributions. First, an implicit recommendation strategy extracts matching scores from the output probability of the first generated token rather than parsing text output, enabling differentiable training with a cross-entropy loss over all candidates simultaneously. Second, a block attention mechanism partitions the prompt into structured blocks (task description, job posting, per-resume blocks) and restricts token-level attention: normal resume tokens attend only within their own block, while S=10 learnable special tokens prepended to each resume can attend globally, enabling inter-resume comparison without quadratic cross-resume token costs. Third, a local positional encoding method replaces each resume token's global RoPE position with a local position computed as if the resume immediately followed the job posting, reducing the effective position distance and mitigating RoPE's long-term decay that weakens job-resume attention for later candidates. The paper provides theoretical analysis via Abel's transform showing local positions increase attention dot products. Fourth, an ID sampling method randomly draws candidate IDs from a large pool during training (rather than sequential 1..N), allowing the model to generalize to unseen IDs at inference when N_test > N_train. For debiasing at inference, two training-free methods are proposed: a pre-ranker that uses cosine similarity of average word embeddings to push likely matches toward the front, and a probability-based debiasing method that estimates prior ID token preferences on a small dev set and divides observed probabilities by these priors. Key Results: On HRT (29K resumes, 1.2K jobs, Chinese HR platform dataset), L3TR (6B parameters) achieves MRR=0.8531, R@1=0.8102, ND@5=0.8518, outperforming TALLRec (the strongest fine-tuned baseline) by 13.11% on R@1, and outperforming List-Exp (GLM-4, >100B parameters) by 9.62% on MRR. On JobRec (public CareerBuilder dataset), L3TR (20B) achieves MRR=0.9231, R@1=0.8794, surpassing TALLRec by 3.76% on R@1. On bias evaluation, L3TR reduces position bias range from 0.2081 (L3TR−) to 0.0140, and standard deviation from 0.0780 to 0.0054. Prior bias range over 15 ID tokens drops from 0.4735 (List-Imp) to 0.0290 (L3TR). Ablation shows block attention is the most critical component (removing it drops ND@5 by ~15%), while ID sampling and local positional encoding each contribute moderately. L3TR consistently outperforms at N=10, 15, 20, 30, though all models degrade as N grows. Strengths: - The combination of implicit scoring, block attention, and local positional encoding is technically coherent and each component is motivated by a clear failure mode in prior approaches - Bias decomposition methodology is carefully designed: the reference ID set B and controlled permutation protocol isolate token bias from position bias in a way that applies to both open- and closed-source models - Comprehensive experiments span two datasets, multiple candidate list sizes (N=10 to 30), multiple training set sizes, and ablations of all components, providing strong empirical grounding - L3TR with 6B parameters beats GLM-4 (>100B) and specialized reasoning rankers (Rank1, ReasonRank-32B), demonstrating that task-specific fine-tuning with proper architectural design matters more than scale Weaknesses / Limitations: - Both datasets are recruitment-domain specific (one private, one from 2012); generalizability to other listwise recommendation settings with long-text items is asserted but not demonstrated - The training/inference candidate count mismatch (N_train=4 vs N_test=15) is a practical constraint that limits how well the model learns comparative ranking at the actual inference scale; the paper acknowledges L3TR− underperforms TALLRec without the special designs, suggesting the baseline listwise fine-tuning is quite weak - The pre-ranker debiasing method deliberately exploits position bias (pushing good candidates to the front) rather than eliminating it, which is a pragmatic workaround but means the model is not truly position-invariant — this is partially masked by the probability-based debiasing but the combination still shows residual sensitivity in prior bias tables - Fairness implications of automated talent ranking (e.g., demographic bias in resume-job matching) are noted as future work but not addressed, which is a significant gap given the high-stakes deployment context
Search Results 2604.02200 PDF
#45 — Innovative (73)

MARS: Enabling Autoregressive Models Multi-Token Generation (23 ▲ )

Ziqi Jin, Lei Wang, Ziwei Luo, Aixin Sun · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Apr 2026
Why this rank: MARS delivers a technically clean contribution by enabling multi-token generation from existing AR models without architectural modification, extra parameters, or quality degradation in single-token mode. The gap analysis between AR and block-masked diffusion is insightful and well-validated through ablations, and the dual-stream training formulation that provably maintains AR signal above 50% is a non-obvious design choice. The continuous, per-request speed-quality tradeoff via confidence thresholding is practically valuable for serving. However, multi-token generation is a crowded research area, and evaluation is limited to small-scale models.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Autoregressive (AR) language models spend equal compute on every token — one forward pass per token — regardless of how predictable that token is given prior context. Existing acceleration approaches all require modifications to the deployment stack: speculative decoding needs a separate draft model (doubling memory), while multi-head methods like Medusa and EAGLE attach extra prediction heads (extra parameters, architectural changes). The gap in the literature is a method that adds multi-token generation as an optional capability to an existing AR model without any architectural modification, extra parameters, or quality degradation in single-token mode. Methodology: MARS (Mask AutoRegresSion) is a lightweight fine-tuning approach that starts from an instruction-tuned AR SFT checkpoint and continues training on the same SFT data with a masked prediction objective. The key insight is a careful gap analysis identifying four differences between standard AR and block-masked diffusion: (1) token masking (inherent to multi-token prediction), (2) attention pattern (bidirectional vs. causal), (3) logits alignment (right-shifted vs. not), and (4) generation order (left-to-right vs. confidence-based). MARS closes gaps 2–4 by keeping strictly causal attention everywhere, preserving right-shifted logits, and enforcing left-to-right acceptance. Training concatenates a clean stream (standard AR loss) and a noisy stream (blocks of B tokens replaced with [MASK] placeholders) into a single forward pass of length 2L using a structured attention mask. The combined loss L = L_mask + L_AR ensures the AR training signal fraction stays above 50% regardless of block size B, preventing the 1/B signal decay that degrades quality in mask-only training. At inference, a sliding-window procedure appends B [MASK] tokens to the prefix, runs one forward pass, and greedily accepts consecutive tokens left-to-right while max probability ≥ τ (at least one token always accepted). The threshold τ is tunable per-request at serving time with no model reload. A block-level KV caching strategy amortizes the prefix recomputation cost for batch inference. Key Results: In single-token mode (τ=1.0), MARS matches or exceeds the AR SFT baseline across six benchmarks (IFEval, BBH, MMLU-Pro, GPQA, GSM8K, HumanEval). At 0.5B, MARS averages 30.4 vs. AR SFT's 28.7 (+1.7), with HumanEval improving 4.8 points (40.2 vs. 35.4). At 7B, MARS averages 58.1 vs. 56.6 (+1.5), with GSM8K gaining +4.5 (73.2 vs. 68.7). A compute-matched 10-epoch AR SFT baseline scores 26.4 average (worse than 5-epoch), ruling out that gains come from extra training. Block Diffusion, which uses bidirectional intra-block attention, collapses to near-base-model quality (22.8 average at 0.5B), validating the gap analysis. In multi-token mode (τ=0.95), MARS achieves 1.46–1.68 tokens per forward pass with only ~1.1–1.3 average accuracy loss; the 7B model at τ=0.95 still exceeds the AR SFT baseline (56.8 vs. 56.6). Wall-clock speedup with block-level KV caching reaches 1.71× over AR with KV cache on Qwen2.5-7B at batch size 4 (161.2s vs. 276.2s), 1.60× at batch size 8, and 1.34× at batch size 16. Compared to Jacobi decoding (a training-free baseline), MARS achieves 1.46× tokens/forward vs. Jacobi's 1.07×. Strengths: - The dual-stream training formulation is technically clean: the structured attention mask enforces exactly the right visibility, and the combined loss provably keeps the AR signal above 50% regardless of block size — a non-obvious insight that enables large blocks without degradation. - The resulting model is a true strict superset of AR: it can be served identically to the original with no quality cost, and confidence thresholding enables continuous, on-the-fly speed-quality adjustment per request without model swapping — a practically valuable serving property. - The gap analysis provides a principled explanation for why prior AR-to-diffusion conversions degrade quality, and the ablations cleanly isolate the contribution of each design choice (SFT loss, attention pattern, logits alignment). Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is restricted to Qwen2.5 at 0.5B and 7B, with B=4 only at 7B due to compute constraints; generalization of larger block sizes and the claimed Pareto stability to frontier-scale models (70B+) is unverified. - Training doubles effective sequence length, incurring ~2× training compute (33 vs. 15 H200-hours at 0.5B; 202 vs. 100 at 7B) — a non-trivial cost that may limit adoption for teams already at capacity, and is not discussed against the amortized benefit per deployment. - The block-level KV cache requires batch synchronization at block boundaries (all samples must fill a block before the cache advances), which causes faster samples to idle and limits throughput gains at large batch sizes — the speedup drops from 1.71× at batch 4 to 1.34× at batch 16, suggesting the strategy may not scale well to production serving loads.
Recommended 2604.07023 PDF
#46 — Innovative (73)

Explainable and Fine-Grained Safeguarding of LLM Multi-Agent Systems via Bi-Level Graph Anomaly Detection

openreview.net · karypis - new results
Why this rank: XG-Guard introduces a well-motivated bi-level graph anomaly detection architecture that directly addresses the known failure mode of sentence-level-only defenses missing malicious tokens camouflaged in benign responses. The covariance-guided fusion mechanism is a principled and self-correcting contribution, and the theme-based prototype approach elegantly handles dialogue diversity. Strong empirical results consistently outperforming unsupervised baselines with large margins validate the design.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS) are vulnerable to adversarial attacks—prompt injection, memory poisoning, tool exploitation—where a single compromised agent can propagate malicious behavior across the entire system. Existing graph anomaly detection (GAD) defenses model agent outputs as attributed graphs but rely solely on sentence-level (BERT) embeddings, missing fine-grained lexical cues (e.g., a manipulative instruction buried in an otherwise benign response). They also produce opaque binary verdicts with no explanation of why an agent was flagged, limiting trust and deployability in safety-critical applications. Methodology: XG-Guard is an unsupervised GAD framework with three core components. First, a bi-level agent encoder constructs both sentence-level node attributes (via frozen SentenceBERT on the full response) and token-level attributes (SentenceBERT applied per-token). Token attributes are enriched with the corresponding sentence embedding before being mean-pooled to a fixed-size representation, enabling a GNN with skip connections to operate at both levels while avoiding the homophily over-smoothing trap. Second, a theme-based anomaly detector computes per-dialogue prototype vectors (mean of all agent representations at each level) and scores each agent by its distance to the prototype, making the normal-class reference adaptive to each dialogue's topic rather than fixed across all inputs. Third, a covariance-guided bi-level score fusion normalizes sentence- and token-level anomaly scores, then weights the token-level scores by their covariance with the sentence-level scores. Negative covariance (indicating semantic mismatch, where anomalous tokens contaminate the token prototype) automatically down-weights the noisy token contribution. The covariance-weighted per-token distances also serve directly as token-level explanation scores. The model is trained with contrastive learning on clean MAS graphs, using within-dialogue agent–prototype pairs as positives and cross-dialogue prototype pairs as negatives, requiring no attack labels. Key Results: Evaluated on six datasets spanning three attack types (prompt injection on CSQA/MMLU/GSM8K, tool attack on InjecAgent, memory attack on PoisonRAG/CSQA) and four graph topologies (chain, tree, star, random) with GPT-4o-mini as the backbone LLM. XG-Guard consistently achieves the highest AUC and lowest ASR@3 among unsupervised methods, exceeding 90% AUC across all settings. Against the prior unsupervised SOTA (BlindGuard), XG-Guard achieves large margins—e.g., on TA-InjecAgent/Chain: 99.56 vs. 84.89 AUC, and MA-CSQA/Star: 96.00 vs. 74.67 AUC. On several datasets (PI-GSM8K, TA-InjecAgent, MA-PoisonRAG, MA-CSQA), XG-Guard is competitive with the supervised upper bound G-Safeguard despite requiring no labeled attack instances. The method generalizes across DeepSeek-V3 and Qwen3-30B-A3B backbones with stable performance. Ablation confirms that removing the token view (–Token) drops AUC substantially (e.g., 99.56→90.67 on TA-InjecAgent/Tree), and that naïve score averaging (–Fusion) performs even worse than no token view at all (e.g., 48.27 AUC on TA-InjecAgent/Tree), validating the necessity of the covariance-guided fusion. Strengths: - The bi-level architecture directly addresses the core failure mode of prior work: malicious tokens camouflaged within benign responses are missed by sentence-level compression alone, and the token-level stream recovers these signals effectively. - The covariance-guided fusion is principled and self-correcting: when token-level prototypes are contaminated by anomalous content, negative covariance automatically reduces the token stream's influence, preventing degradation rather than requiring a manual threshold. - The theme-based prototype approach elegantly handles the diversity of MAS dialogue topics—normal behavior is defined relative to the current conversation, not globally, which is a genuine improvement over context-agnostic GAD methods. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The explainability output has a known failure mode acknowledged by the authors: punctuation and stop words receive spurious high anomaly scores because SentenceBERT blends contextual information into all token embeddings, including non-semantic tokens. The model treats the encoder as a black box and cannot disentangle this. - Evaluation is confined to simulated MAS environments with synthetic attacks and a fixed defense budget (top-3 agents flagged); performance on real-world deployments with unknown attack rates, evolving agent populations, or API-level model updates remains untested and is flagged as a limitation.
Search Results
#47 — Innovative (73)

Implicit Turn-Wise Policy Optimization for Proactive User-LLM Interaction

Haoyu Wang, Yuxin Chen, Liang Luo, Buyun Zhang, Ellie Dingqiao Wen, Pan Li · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: ITPO introduces a clean and theoretically grounded approach to multi-turn RL credit assignment by aggregating token-level implicit rewards to turn granularity, with a compelling Bayesian interpretation of the normalization as posterior credit attribution. The method elegantly eliminates the need for labeled process rewards or costly rollouts while demonstrably improving reward stability and interpretability. However, it builds directly on existing implicit PRM work (PRIME) and standard RL algorithms, and all evaluation relies on LLM-simulated users rather than real humans, leaving the practical impact uncertain.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-turn human-AI collaboration—spanning tutoring, document writing, and medical consultation—is critical for deploying useful AI systems, yet aligning LLMs for these tasks via reinforcement learning is fundamentally difficult. The core challenge is reward sparsity: outcome signals arrive only at conversation's end, making credit assignment across turns hard. Existing remedies all fall short in this setting. Human-annotated process reward models (PRMs) require hundreds of thousands of labels; Monte Carlo rollout-based PRMs multiply sample complexity 8-10x; value function estimation diverges under high-variance user dynamics; and LLM-as-a-Judge approaches are too slow and biased for online RL. Token-level implicit PRMs avoid these costs but produce noisy, semantically uninterpretable credit signals that overfit and exhibit high variance. The paper fills the gap between the scalability of implicit PRMs and the semantic coherence needed for multi-turn interactions. Methodology: ITPO (Implicit Turn-wise Policy Optimization) reformulates multi-turn collaboration as a POMDP and introduces turn-level aggregation of token-wise implicit rewards. Each turn's reward is the sum of per-token log-likelihood ratios between an online-updated implicit PRM and a fixed reference model, collapsing the noisy token granularity to the natural semantic unit of a conversational turn. The normalized variant, Norm-ITPO, redistributes the scalar outcome reward across turns via a softmax over turn-level implicit scores, enforcing scale consistency between implicit and outcome rewards. This is given a Bayesian interpretation: the softmax weights equal the posterior probability of each turn being the decisive one under a Boltzmann likelihood. The resulting turn-wise rewards are integrated with standard advantage estimators—PPO (with GAE), GRPO, and RLOO—via a turn-level PPO clipping objective that preserves semantic coherence of entire responses. The implicit PRM is updated online via binary cross-entropy against trajectory-level outcomes, requiring no labels or extra rollouts. Experiments use Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct as the policy, Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct as the user simulator, and are run with VeRL/vLLM on 2-4 H100 GPUs across three tasks: math tutoring (500 MATH problems, accuracy metric), document writing (500 Medium articles, BLEU metric), and medical recommendation (550 MTMedDialog samples, hierarchical diagnosis score). Key Results: Norm-ITPO consistently outperforms all baselines across all three tasks and all three advantage estimators. With RLOO, Norm-ITPO improves the trajectory-level sparse baseline by 34.4% on math tutoring, 12.0% on document writing, and 8.0% on medical recommendation. Compared to PRIME (the strongest prior implicit PRM baseline), Norm-ITPO gains +3.44 accuracy points on math tutoring and +10.24 score on medical recommendation with RLOO. With GRPO, Norm-ITPO achieves 71.74% diagnosis accuracy on medical recommendation vs. 66.41% for the vanilla trajectory baseline. LLM-as-a-Judge provides modest gains but consistently trails online-updated reward models. Training an extra value model on Norm-ITPO rewards (PPO setting) further improves performance: +16.7%, +10.3%, +8.5% over pure outcome-supervised PPO across the three tasks. Turn-wise reward stability is quantified via Spearman correlation with converged values, reaching ~0.8+ within 50-100 steps, compared to token-level rewards that remain noisy throughout. In a human interpretability study on 64 best/worst turn decisions, Norm-ITPO matched 48/64 (75%) and ITPO matched 47/64 (73.4%), far above the 16/64 random baseline and approaching Gemini-3.0-Pro's 58/64 reference. Generalization to larger backbones (Qwen3-4B, Qwen2.5-7B) confirms the method's model-agnostic effectiveness. Strengths: - The insight of aggregating token-level implicit rewards to turn granularity is simple, theoretically grounded (Bayesian credit attribution), and eliminates the need for any labeled process rewards or extra rollouts while being provably more stable than token-level supervision. - The normalization mechanism (Norm-ITPO) cleanly addresses the scale instability between implicit and outcome rewards that plagues raw ITPO, with the clearest gains observed in the PPO setting where a value model must regress on these signals. - Thorough empirical analysis covering stability, consistency, trajectory-level prediction quality, and semantic interpretability, including a carefully controlled human annotation study, gives unusually strong evidence that the learned rewards are meaningful rather than artifacts. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All experiments use an LLM-based user simulator (Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct) rather than real humans; the reliability of simulator-trained policies on real users is untested, and the gap between simulated and human interaction variance is a significant unverified assumption. - The policy backbone is small (3B parameters), and while 7B results are shown on one task, it is unclear whether the method's advantages persist at scales where the policy is itself a more capable implicit reasoner—potentially shrinking the benefit of explicit turn-wise credit assignment. - The method adds an online-updated implicit PRM that must be co-trained with the policy, increasing system complexity and memory footprint relative to simple GRPO/RLOO baselines; sensitivity to the temperature hyperparameter η and β is not thoroughly ablated across tasks.
Recommended 2603.23550 PDF
#48 — Innovative (72)

REAL: Regression-Aware Reinforcement Learning for LLM-as-a-Judge

Yasi Zhang, Tianyu Chen, Mingyuan Zhou, Oscar Leong, Ying Nian Wu, Michal Lukasik · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 72 for cleanly combining regression-aware objectives with RL exploration in LLM-as-a-Judge, filling a genuine methodological gap. The generalized policy gradient derivation that decomposes into CoT exploration and score prediction terms is technically sound, and the proof that MSE minimization implies optimal Pearson correlation provides principled justification. However, the contribution is more a well-executed synthesis of existing ideas than a fundamental departure.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-as-a-Judge systems assign numeric scores to model outputs, but this is fundamentally a regression task. Standard RL post-training (e.g., GRPO, REINFORCE) uses binary 0/1 accuracy rewards, treating a prediction of 1 and a prediction of 4 equivalently when the ground truth is 5 — ignoring ordinal structure entirely. Regression-aware SFT methods like RAFT and TRACT address the ordinal issue but remain confined to static, ground-truth trajectory supervision without active exploration of reasoning pathways. No prior work combined regression-aware objectives with RL exploration. Empirically, standard RL applied to LLM-as-a-Judge causes correlation metric collapse during training. Methodology: REAL (REgression-Aware Reinforcement Learning) introduces a policy-dependent regression reward: r_REAL(θ, x, c) = -(ŷ_θ(x,c) - y)² + λ log π_θ(y|c,x), where ŷ_θ is the RAIL expected-value predictor (weighted sum over digit token probabilities). Because this reward explicitly depends on policy parameters θ, the standard policy gradient — which assumes ∇_θr = 0 — is invalid. The authors invoke the generalized policy gradient (Schulman et al., 2015), deriving a gradient that decomposes into two terms: (1) a CoT policy-gradient term that explores reasoning trajectories weighted by the regression reward, and (2) a prediction refinement term that backpropagates directly through the differentiable reward. Training uses RLOO variance reduction with K sampled CoT trajectories per prompt and a dynamic sampling strategy that filters to only partially-correct prompts (0 < acc < 1). The authors also prove (Lemma 3.1) that minimizing squared error loss is a sufficient condition for maximizing Pearson correlation — closing a theoretical gap in prior regression-aware LLM work. Experiments use Mistral2-7B, Qwen3-8B, and Qwen3-32B on the Feedback Collection training set, evaluated on Feedback Bench (in-domain), FLASK, Vicuna Bench, and MT Bench (out-of-domain). Key Results: REAL consistently outperforms zero-shot, RAFT, TRACT, standard binary-reward RL, and JEPO across all three model scales and all four benchmarks. On Qwen3-32B, REAL achieves average Pearson/Spearman of 71.0/70.0 versus RAFT's 62.6/62.8 — gains of +8.4/+7.2 over the SFT baseline and +18.3/+11.2 over the base model. On Mistral2-7B, REAL reaches average Pearson 67.9 vs. TRACT's 63.2 and standard RL's 64.1. The out-of-domain improvement is particularly pronounced: on MT Bench (Mistral2-7B), REAL achieves Pearson 59.3 vs. TRACT 52.1 and standard RL 52.9. Performance at N=1 generation is nearly identical to averaging N=10 generations, indicating a robust learned policy. Ablations confirm: larger group size K improves performance (K=16 > K=8 > K=4); regression-only reward (λ=0) already substantially beats TRACT; and initializing from a strong SFT checkpoint further boosts REAL's gains. Strengths: - The theoretical grounding is non-trivial and closes a real gap: the proof that MSE minimization implies optimal Pearson correlation provides principled justification for the regression reward, not just empirical motivation. - The generalized policy gradient derivation cleanly resolves the policy-dependent reward problem, producing an interpretable two-term decomposition that distinguishes reasoning exploration (CoT update) from score prediction (backprop update) — a conceptually meaningful separation. - Out-of-domain generalization gains are substantially larger than in-domain gains, which is the harder and more practically important regime for a judge model; this validates that REAL learns transferable evaluation skills rather than dataset-specific patterns. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method is restricted to pointwise scalar scoring and does not address pairwise preference learning, which is a dominant paradigm in RLHF alignment and LLM evaluation. - The theoretical optimality result (Lemma 3.1) relies on the conditional independence assumption c ⊥ y | x — i.e., that the chain of thought carries no additional information about the label beyond the input. This is questionable: a good CoT that correctly analyzes the response should be informative about y, potentially undermining the clean theoretical story. - Evaluation is limited to a single training dataset (Feedback Collection) and a narrow benchmark suite focused on instruction-following quality scoring; generalization to other judge tasks (e.g., mathematical correctness, safety) is undemonstrated.
Recommended 2603.17145 PDF
#49 — Innovative (72)

Robust Safety Monitoring of Language Models via Activation Watermarking

Toluwani Aremu, Daniil Ognev, Samuele Poppi, Nils Lukas · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Activation watermarking introduces a genuinely clever defense primitive: embedding a secret-keyed direction that creates unresolvable uncertainty for adaptive attackers who know the algorithm but not the key. The security argument via key mismatch is well-motivated and the empirical evaluation across four jailbreak families is thorough. The approach falls short of highly innovative because security guarantees remain entirely empirical with no provable bounds, and the math-reasoning degradation suggests the activation-regularization objective has fundamental trade-offs that are not yet understood.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM providers deploy safety monitors (guard models, classifiers) to detect policy-violating outputs, but these monitors are vulnerable to adaptive attackers who know the monitoring algorithm and iteratively craft jailbreak prompts to simultaneously elicit harmful outputs and evade detection. Since guard models are typically static and open-source variants are publicly accessible, an attacker can surrogate-optimize attacks that transfer to production deployments. Existing activation-based probing approaches use publicly discoverable directions that adaptive attackers can also bypass. The gap is a monitoring mechanism that is robust against adversaries who fully know the detection algorithm but not a secret key. Methodology: The paper proposes activation watermarking, which fine-tunes the base LLM to embed a secret, keyed direction into its hidden-state activations whenever it produces policy-violating outputs, while keeping benign activations orthogonal to this direction. Key generation samples a Gaussian vector per target layer from a seeded PRNG, defining the secret key. Training adds a loss term to a standard KL-regularized fine-tuning objective: for harmful examples, cosine similarity between activations and the watermark direction is maximized; for benign examples, it is minimized. A linear token-weighting ramp concentrates the signal on later, more explicitly harmful tokens (post-onset), reducing false positives from ambiguous preamble tokens. Detection at inference is a single cosine-similarity computation averaged over assistant tokens, with a threshold calibrated on held-out benign data at a target FPR. Adaptive attackers are modeled as having full algorithm knowledge but no access to the secret key, forcing them to optimize on a surrogate with a mismatched key; the key mismatch prevents clean transfer. Experiments use Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and 14B as base models, BeaverTails for training/evaluation, XSTest seeds with EasyJailbreak for jailbreak suites (Jailbroken, DeepInception, Multilingual, AutoDAN), and compare against LlamaGuard-3-8B and Qwen3Guard-Gen-8B. Key Results: On standard BeaverTails, activation watermarking (ActWM) achieves AUROC 0.878, competitive with QwenGuard (0.849) and above LlamaGuard (0.745). The advantage widens substantially under adaptive jailbreaks: ActWM reaches AUROC 0.929 (Jailbroken), 0.923 (DeepInception), 0.962 (Multilingual), and 0.905 (AutoDAN), outperforming the best guard baseline by up to 52 percentage points on AUROC and consistently achieving lower attack success rates (ASR) at 1% FPR. Cross-model transfer attacks (developed on Mistral-7B-Instruct, evaluated on watermarked Qwen2.5-7B) still yield high AUROC (0.90–0.95), confirming cross-architecture robustness. In a 20-entity secret extraction game, attribution accuracy is ~80% versus 5% random chance, with TPR ~80% at FPR = 10^-4. Utility impact is modest on most benchmarks (<2 pp on BBH, IFEval, MMLU-pro, TruthfulQA) but reaches −7.1 pp on GSM8K and −2.6 pp on MATH-Hard. Training requires ~4 GPU hours on a single RTX A6000 with negligible inference overhead (O(Kd) additional cost vs. a full guard forward pass). Strengths: - The core insight of using a secret-keyed direction to introduce unresolvable uncertainty for the attacker is well-motivated and clearly distinguishes the method from prior activation-probe work, which relies on discoverable public directions. - The empirical evaluation is thorough: four jailbreak families (two template-based, one optimization-based, one multilingual), cross-key and cross-model transfer experiments, multi-entity attribution, and 72-configuration ablations covering layer depth, learning rate, watermark strength, and token weighting. - Inference overhead is negligible compared to guard-model pipelines, making the approach practical for high-throughput deployment; the single-pass architecture and streaming compatibility are genuine engineering advantages. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The threat model excludes white-box adversaries with gradient access to the model's activations; an attacker who can query hidden states directly could potentially identify and suppress the watermark direction, yet this scenario is not evaluated or bounded theoretically. - Security guarantees are entirely empirical—there are no provable lower bounds on the attacker's difficulty, so a stronger adaptive strategy (e.g., directly optimizing against the cosine statistic using activation access or model distillation) could reduce detection rates in ways not captured by the current attack suite. - The math-reasoning utility degradation (−7.1 pp on GSM8K) is non-trivial, and the paper does not investigate whether this is fundamental to the activation-regularization objective or addressable with better training recipes; at deployment scale this could be a significant quality regression.
Recommended 2603.23171 PDF
#50 — Innovative (72)

Rethinking Model Efficiency: Multi-Agent Inference with Large Models

Sixun Dong, Juhua Hu, Steven Li, Wei Wen, Qi Qian · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: This paper challenges a genuinely underexplored assumption — that smaller models are inherently more inference-efficient — by demonstrating that output token count dominates latency more than model size. The multi-agent inference framework with mutual verification and reasoning transfer is a creative, training-free approach that reframes efficiency as a system-level coordination problem rather than a model compression problem. The theoretical grounding via attention sparsity analysis adds rigor, though the evaluation is limited to two architecturally similar model families and the prototype implementation artifacts weaken the quantitative claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Prior work on VLM inference efficiency has focused almost exclusively on reducing model size (parameter count) and pruning input visual tokens. The output token dimension has been largely ignored. This paper challenges the conventional assumption that smaller models are more inference-efficient by demonstrating that end-to-end latency is dominated by the number of output tokens, not just model size. Because smaller models require significantly more reasoning tokens to reach comparable accuracy, a larger model answering concisely can be both faster and more accurate than a smaller model with extended chain-of-thought. Methodology: The paper first profiles 2B/4B/8B Qwen3-VL models on an H100, decomposing latency into input preprocessing, vision encoding, prefilling (TTFT), and decoding. The key empirical finding is that decoding throughput differs only mildly across model sizes (61.8 vs. 47.3 tok/s for 2B vs. 8B), while smaller models emit 2–10× more tokens to match the accuracy of larger models on benchmarks like MMMU and ChartQA. Prompt engineering (Simple/Explain/Reasoning variants) is used to control output length; a 2-stage decoding strategy (generate reasoning, then force-format the answer) corrects instruction-following failures observed in small models after long reasoning chains. Building on this analysis, the paper proposes Multi-Agent Inference (MAI): (1) Mutual Verification — both a large (8B) and small (2B) model answer with simple prompts; if they agree, return immediately with minimal tokens; (2) if they disagree, the small model generates full reasoning tokens; if the small model's re-answer now matches the large model's simple answer, return; (3) if still disagreeing, Reasoning Transfer is invoked — the small model's reasoning chain is prepended as input context for the large model, which then answers with a simple prompt, converting expensive sequential decoding into cheap prefilling. A theoretical bound (Corollary 1) is derived showing that the large model's self-attention can suppress noisy reasoning tokens from the small model, since the top 20% of reasoning tokens account for ~80% of attention weight. Sparse reasoning transfer, selecting only the dominant-attention tokens (δ=0.8), further reduces the transferred context without degrading accuracy. Key Results: Evaluated across POPE, MMMU, MMBench, ChartQA, InfoVQA, and RealWorldQA using Qwen3-VL (2/4/8B) and InternVL3.5 (1/2/4/8/14B). Full reasoning transfer (8B+2B-R) improves over the 8B-S baseline on 4 of 6 tasks (e.g., MMMU: 55.6→62.9, +13.1%). The complete MAI framework with mutual verification further reduces expensive large-model calls: on POPE, 94.5% of queries are resolved at Stage 1; only 5% require reasoning transfer. MAI achieves 1.76×–8.05× speedup over full reasoning transfer in total wall-clock time across benchmarks (Table 11), while maintaining or improving accuracy (e.g., ChartQA 86.8% vs. 85.3% for full RT). Compared with token-level speculative decoding (2B draft / 8B target), which is actually 16% slower than standard 8B decoding on HuggingFace (due to ~40% rejection rate and context-switching overhead), MAI on vLLM achieves a 2.40× speedup over the 8B reasoning baseline with better accuracy (86.52% vs. 86.32%). Sparse reasoning transfer with layer L10–L14 attention weights achieves comparable or better POPE F1 (89.3–89.6) than full transfer (89.2) using only ~85–90 tokens instead of 266. Strengths: - Identifies a genuinely underexplored bottleneck (output token count) with careful empirical profiling, making a convincing case that the conventional small-model efficiency narrative is incomplete. - The multi-agent design is training-free and operates at the response level, making it natively compatible with optimized serving engines like vLLM and amenable to multi-GPU deployment with negligible inter-model communication. - The theoretical analysis (Corollary 1) provides a principled justification for why noisy small-model reasoning can still benefit large models via attention sparsity, with supporting layer-wise empirical validation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is limited to two model families (Qwen3-VL and InternVL3.5) within a narrow size range (1B–14B), all from the same architectural lineage; it is unclear whether the efficiency crossover holds for architecturally dissimilar or much larger models. - GPU memory constraints are explicitly excluded from the analysis — running a 2B+8B configuration simultaneously requires ~10B parameters, which fits a single H100 but is non-trivial in resource-constrained settings; the paper defers memory-aware scheduling to future work. - The current MAI implementation restarts a fresh vLLM engine per stage, incurring cold-start overhead that artificially inflates reported latency for the baseline comparisons while undercounting real MAI overhead in production; the claimed speedups are framed as lower bounds but the gap between prototype and production deployment is significant and unquantified.
