#51 — Innovative (72)

FlatAttention: Dataflow and Fabric Collectives Co- Optimization for Large Attention-Based Model Inference on Tile-Based Accelerators

Chi Zhang, Luca Colagrande, Renzo Andri, Luca Benini · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: FlatAttention introduces a genuinely novel co-design of attention dataflow with hardware NoC collectives, fundamentally rethinking how attention parallelism maps to tile-based architectures by replacing per-tile HBM access with group-level multicast and reduction. The insight that FlashAttention's embarrassingly parallel design becomes a liability on scratchpad-based mesh architectures is non-obvious, and the unified treatment across MHA/GQA/MLA is practically valuable. However, results are entirely simulation-based on non-existent silicon, limiting immediate impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: As MoE-based LLMs like DeepSeek-v3 shift compute cost away from FFN layers, attention accounts for up to 71% of total FLOPs during decoding — yet FlashAttention-3 and FlashMLA on NVIDIA GH200 achieve only 26–64% of roofline performance. Emerging tile-based and wafer-scale accelerators (mesh-of-tiles with scratchpad L1 memory) promise better efficiency, but existing attention dataflows (FlashAttention variants) map each attention head independently to a single tile, preventing cross-tile data reuse and leaving HBM bandwidth as the dominant bottleneck. No prior work co-designs the attention dataflow with hardware-accelerated collective primitives (multicast, reduction) in the on-chip NoC fabric. Methodology: FlatAttention redefines MHA parallelization by treating a rectangular group of N×N tiles as a unified compute unit (a "group") that collectively processes a single FlashAttention block. Instead of each tile independently accessing HBM for its own head slice, only diagonal tiles fetch Q and K/V from HBM and multicast them row- and column-wise within the group. Each tile computes a slice of the attention score matrix, then participates in row-wise reductions for the Softmax row-max and denominator, followed by row-wise multicasts to redistribute global statistics, and finally a row-wise reduction of partial O outputs — with only diagonal tiles writing back to HBM. This replaces costly global HBM traffic with cheaper on-chip collective communication. The key enabling mechanism is hardware-accelerated NoC collectives (flit-level replication in routers), which outperform software tree-based multicasts by 5.1× and sequential unicasts by 30.7× for row-wise multicast, and 10.9×/67.3× for row-wise reduction. An asynchronous schedule (FlatAsync) overlaps matrix multiplications for one head with DMA and Softmax operations for another, keeping the matrix engine nearly continuously active. The approach generalizes to GQA (grouped queries viewed as extended Q length) and MLA (weight-absorption to convert to MQA form). A general tiling strategy prioritizes per-tile compute efficiency (targeting Br/Gy = Bc/Gx = 128 for ≥95% RedMulE utilization) before scaling the group size. End-to-end DeepSeek-v3-671B decoding is evaluated on a 64-accelerator wafer-scale system using a SUMMA GEMM dataflow and EP32–PP2 parallelism, modeled via GVSoC with calibrated RTL components (RedMulE, FlooNoC, DRAMSys). Key Results: On a 32×32 tile configuration matching GH200 peak FP16 performance (988 TFLOPS, 2 TB/s HBM), FlatAttention achieves up to 92.3% matrix engine utilization for prefill MHA (S=4096, D=128), a 4.1× speedup over FlashAttention-3, and 16× lower HBM traffic. Across all attention variants and input shapes, FlatAttention averages 86% utilization in compute-bound regimes and 78% HBM bandwidth utilization in memory-bound regimes, yielding an average 1.9× speedup over GH200 implementations. For end-to-end DeepSeek-v3-671B FP8 decoding on 64 tile-based accelerators (wafer-scale), FlatAttention reduces the attention fraction of decoding runtime from 71% (FlashMLA) to 42%, achieving a 4.5× speedup on the attention kernel and 2.1× end-to-end decoding speedup at batch size 256. Compared to the state-of-the-art DS-Prof system (96 NVIDIA H800 GPUs), the 64-accelerator wafer-scale system achieves 1.9× higher overall throughput and 1.4× lower TPOT (35.8 ms vs. 50.2 ms) despite 1.5× lower peak system FLOPS. Strengths: - Hardware–software co-design insight is crisp and well-motivated: the paper clearly shows that FlashAttention's embarrassingly parallel dataflow is fundamentally bottlenecked by HBM on tile-based architectures, and that hardware NoC collectives are the critical enabler to trade off HBM traffic for cheap on-chip communication. - Generalization across all major modern attention variants (MHA, GQA, MLA with weight-absorption) under a unified tiling framework is non-trivial and practically valuable given the diversity of deployed LLMs. - The evaluation methodology is rigorous: models are calibrated against cycle-accurate RTL simulations with sub-1% deviation for compute and ~6–12% for NoC, and end-to-end results are compared against real production deployment figures (DS-Prof on H800 GPUs). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The wafer-scale evaluation assumes a naive barrier-separated execution model (no kernel–communication overlap across chips), which likely underestimates achievable performance and makes the 1.9× comparison against H800 GPUs somewhat conservative or hard to interpret fairly. - The proposed tile-based accelerator and its FlooNoC with hardware collective support do not yet exist as fabricated silicon; all results are simulation-based, and real hardware costs (area, power, yield for wafer-scale) are not quantified, limiting direct cost-efficiency comparison with commercial GPUs. - The "over-flattening" phenomenon (where large group sizes hurt short-sequence performance) is identified but the proposed tiling heuristic is fixed and conservative — the framework lacks an automatic or adaptive runtime selection of group scale based on input shape, leaving performance on the table for dynamic workloads.

#52 — Innovative (72)

ClawArena: Benchmarking AI Agents in Evolving Information Environments (26 ▲ )

Haonian Ji, Kaiwen Xiong, Siwei Han, Peng Xia, Shi Qiu, Yiyang Zhou, Jiaqi Liu, Jinlong Li, Bingzhou Li, Zeyu Zheng, Cihang Xie, Huaxiu Yao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 Apr 2026
Why this rank: ClawArena defines a genuinely novel evaluation regime by jointly testing multi-source conflict resolution, dynamic belief revision, and implicit personalization under a unified six-layer scenario architecture—a combination no prior benchmark addresses. The 14-category taxonomy forcing evaluation across all dimension interactions is methodologically creative, and the finding that executable-check performance is orthogonal to multi-choice performance validates the multi-format design. However, the current scale (64 scenarios, 12 used for most experiments) limits statistical power, and the benchmark remains confined to staged updates rather than live environments, tempering its practical impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Persistent AI assistants deployed in real workflows must maintain correct beliefs as their information environment evolves: evidence is scattered across heterogeneous sources that contradict each other, new facts can invalidate earlier conclusions, and user preferences surface implicitly through corrections rather than explicit instructions. Existing benchmarks — SWE-bench, AgentBench, WebArena, HotpotQA, LoCoMo — test fragments of this problem in static, single-authority settings. None jointly evaluate source-conflict resolution, dynamic belief revision, and silent preference retention under a common evaluation regime. ClawArena fills this gap. Methodology: ClawArena structures each scenario as a six-layer artifact. Layer 0 is a hidden ground truth (objective timeline, contradiction map, answer provenance); Layers 1–4 are agent-visible: workspace files (4–8 documents), multi-channel session histories (5–7 channels, 200–400 messages with 30–50% noise), staged update packages, and evaluation questions. A four-stage personalization protocol culminates in silent-exam rounds where no preference reminders are provided. Evaluation is organized around three coupled dimensions — multi-source conflict reasoning (MS), dynamic belief revision (DU), and implicit personalization (P) — and their pairwise and three-way interactions, yielding a 14-category question taxonomy. Two question formats are used: multi-choice set-selection (per-option and exact-match scoring) and sandboxed shell-based executable checks that verify workspace-level claims. The current release covers 64 scenarios in 8 professional domains, totaling 1,879 rounds and 365 dynamic updates. Scenarios are constructed via a three-stage pipeline (hand-authored seeds → meta-specification induction → batch generation constrained by 200+ empirical distributions for message timing, contact frequency, and document conventions), validated at structural, semantic, and control levels. Key Results: Cross-framework comparison under GPT-5.1 shows MetaClaw (self-evolving skills) leads with an overall score of 0.603, followed by OpenClaw (0.579), Claude Code (0.566), PicoClaw (0.515), and NanoBot (0.511); framework design induces a 9.2% performance range. Cross-model comparison under OpenClaw shows Opus 4.6 (0.735) > Sonnet 4.6 (0.708) > Haiku 4.5 (0.614) > GPT-5.1 (0.581); model capability induces a 15.4% range, substantially larger than framework design's contribution. Executable-check performance is orthogonal to multi-choice performance: Haiku scores 95.2% on multi-choice but 0.0% on executable checks in one scenario, validating the two-format design. Belief revision difficulty is determined by update design strategy (concentrated targeted updates cause drops of 0.28–0.36) rather than update volume. Performance varies by more than 60% across domains for every model, and GPT-5.2 outperforms Haiku by 26.7% on a Chinese-language scenario despite trailing overall, implicating language-specific training data. Strengths: - The six-layer scenario architecture with a hidden ground truth cleanly separates evaluation reliability from the noisy observable evidence, ensuring failures reflect agent behavior rather than benchmark inconsistency. - The 14-category taxonomy forces evaluation across all combinations of the three dimensions (MS, DU, P) and their interactions, preventing gaming by partial competence; executable checks add a workspace-grounding dimension orthogonal to reasoning. - Construction is grounded in 200+ empirical distributions (diurnal messaging curves, Dunbar-layer contact weighting, domain-specific document conventions), making scenarios realistic rather than stylized. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark currently contains only 64 scenarios and a subset of 12 is used for most cross-model experiments, raising concerns about statistical power; the 3.5pp gap between subset and full-set scores on the one full-coverage model is reassuring but not conclusive. - Coverage is limited to static files and staged updates; the authors acknowledge the natural extension to live, unconstrained environments with real-time information sources is left as future work, limiting ecological validity for the most dynamic real deployments. - Personalization scoring relies on automated scripts checking five preference dimensions, but the quality and coverage of those scripts is not analyzed; silent-exam compliance may be under- or over-credited depending on script fidelity.

#53 — Innovative (72)

DISCO-TAB: A Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning Framework for Privacy-Preserving Synthesis of Complex Clinical Data

Arshia Ilaty, Hossein Shirazi, Amir Rahmani, Hajar Homayouni · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: DISCO-TAB introduces a genuinely creative architectural idea: decomposing clinical validity into four semantic granularities and training each as a separate discriminator within a hierarchical RL framework. The automated constraint discovery module and IFRS reward shaping are well-motivated technical contributions. The ablation cleanly demonstrates non-additive synergy (+37% combined vs. +18.4% and +22.1% individually), validating the design. However, the reliance on pairwise-only constraints and the unresolved privacy concerns (low Integrity score, no DP guarantees) temper the impact, and the GPT-2 backbone is dated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Electronic Health Records (EHRs) are scarce due to privacy regulations (HIPAA, GDPR), limiting the training and validation of clinical AI models. Existing synthetic tabular data generators — GANs (CTGAN, MedGAN), diffusion models (TabDDPM, TabSyn), and LLM-based generators (GReaT, RealTabFormer) — each fail in distinct ways: GANs suffer mode collapse on rare classes, diffusion models inject noise that corrupts clinically meaningful thresholds, and unconstrained LLMs hallucinate medically implausible records (e.g., assigning pregnancy to male patients) without any interpretable signal of what went wrong. A shared failure mode is that all prior methods optimize a single scalar or likelihood objective that cannot localize violations across the multiple semantic granularities (syntactic, semantic, statistical, holistic) that define clinical validity. Methodology: DISCO-TAB frames tabular synthesis as a constrained sequential decision-making problem and trains a GPT-2-based autoregressive generator via Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) guided by a four-level discriminator ensemble. Each level targets a distinct granularity: (1) Token-level (Dtoken) — a bidirectional LSTM checks syntactic validity of individual generated tokens (e.g., valid numeric formats, ICD code prefixes); (2) Sentence-level (Dsent) — a BERT-base encoder evaluates individual feature–value clauses for local semantic plausibility (e.g., detecting "Gender: Male, Pregnancy: Yes"); (3) Feature-level (Dfeat) — MLP projection heads operating on mean-pooled generator hidden states penalize deviation from statistically significant pairwise dependencies automatically discovered via Pearson/Cramér-V/Correlation-Ratio analysis of the real data; (4) Row-level (Drow) — a mean-pooled MLP assesses global clinical coherence of the full patient record. Discriminator scores are aggregated into a terminal reward for the trajectory: R_raw = Σ λ_k D_k(S). To address class imbalance, Inverse-Frequency Reward Shaping (IFRS) multiplies R_raw by a rarity weight W_y = 1 + α·max(0, log(N_total / (N_y·|Y|))), dynamically amplifying rewards for rare cohorts. KL regularization against the pretrained reference policy prevents reward exploitation. The constraint discovery module is entirely automated, requiring no clinician-provided rules. Key Results: Evaluated on nine datasets (six medical, three non-medical) using Train-on-Synthetic, Test-on-Real (TSTR) F1 with Random Forest classifiers. DISCO-TAB achieves positive utility gains over all baselines on five of six medical benchmarks: Heart Failure (F1=1.000, Δ=+0.123 over TabSyn), Liver Disorders (F1=0.990, Δ=+0.382 over TAGAL), Parkinsons (F1=0.966, Δ=+0.080), Breast Cancer (F1=0.994, Δ=+0.004), Obesity (F1=0.929, Δ=+0.027). The only medical exception is Diabetes (large-scale, Δ=−0.104 vs. MostlyAI). Statistical fidelity is strong: JSD < 0.01 and KS < 0.035 across all datasets, outperforming diffusion models that catastrophically fail on Diabetes (TabDDPM KS=0.553). Under the FAITH hallucination framework, DISCO-TAB achieves the best Alignment score (0.9839) and near-perfect Tracking (0.9920), though its Integrity score (0.2303) is low — a deliberate consequence of tight adherence to the real data manifold rather than noise injection. Memory footprint averages 5.0 GB VRAM vs. ~10.7 GB for diffusion baselines. Ablation confirms that hierarchical discriminators provide +13.1% over a single row-level discriminator, RL alone contributes +18.4%, discriminators alone +22.1%, and their combination yields a non-additive +37.0%, demonstrating strong synergy. Strengths: - The hierarchical four-level discriminator design is well-motivated and empirically validated: each level targets a distinct failure mode, and ablation quantifies each contribution cleanly, with row-level Drow being the single most important component (−8.9% when removed). - IFRS is a simple but effective mechanism for minority-class coverage, outperforming SMOTE by 12.3% balanced accuracy on Diabetes without post-hoc resampling artifacts. - Automated Constraint Discovery eliminates the need for clinician-specified rules while still surfacing clinically meaningful pairwise dependencies, making the framework scalable across datasets. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The low FAITH Integrity score (0.2303) — framed as a design choice — is functionally a privacy liability: generated samples cluster tightly around real records (DCR=1.27 on Heart Failure), raising genuine membership inference risk that is not addressed by formal differential privacy guarantees, which the paper explicitly lacks. - Constraint discovery is limited to pairwise correlation statistics (Pearson, Cramér-V, Correlation Ratio), missing higher-order and causal dependencies (e.g., multi-way treatment–comorbidity–outcome interactions) that are clinically critical. - The GPT-2 backbone is a weak language model by current standards; the framework's dependence on its pre-trained priors introduces uncontrolled inductive biases from general web text that may not align with clinical semantics, yet no domain-adaptive pretraining is performed.
Citations 2604.01481 PDF
#54 — Innovative (72)

Logic-Gated Time-Shared Feedforward Networks for Alternating Finite Automata: Exact Simulation and Learnability

Sahil Rajesh Dhayalkar · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: This paper introduces a theoretically elegant single-scalar modification (learnable bias) that provably upgrades a neural architecture from NFA-equivalent to AFA-equivalent, with a clean bidirectional isomorphism proof and empirical validation of exponential succinctness up to 1,000 states. The continuous OR-to-AND slider via bias is a genuinely clever construction. However, the evaluation is entirely on synthetic random AFAs with no connection to real-world tasks, and no convergence or sample complexity guarantees are provided, keeping it from the highest tier.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Prior work on neural automata simulation (specifically Time-Shared Feedforward Networks, TS-FFNs) could only represent Nondeterministic Finite Automata (NFAs) and their existential (OR) reachability semantics. Alternating Finite Automata (AFAs), which add Universal (AND) branching, are exponentially more succinct than NFAs and doubly exponentially more succinct than DFAs — making them ideal for modeling synchronization constraints, safety properties, and logical entailment. No neural architecture had been shown to simulate AFAs with matching succinctness. This paper bridges that gap. Methodology: The paper introduces the Logic-Gated Time-Shared Feedforward Network (LG-TS-FFN), which extends the TS-FFN framework by adding a learnable, state-dependent bias vector β to each layer's linear transformation. The key insight is that the bias acts as a configurable firing threshold: setting β ≈ 0.5 implements an OR gate (existential: fire if any predecessor is active), while setting β ≈ d_in − 0.5 implements an AND gate (universal: fire only if all d_in predecessors are active). The forward pass unrolls shared transition matrices T^(x) and bias vectors β^(x) per input symbol, interleaved with an ε-closure operator C_ε that resolves instantaneous state propagation. The paper proves (Theorem 4.3) that this forward pass exactly simulates AFA reachability dynamics, and (Theorem 5.1) that the mapping is a structural isomorphism — every LG-TS-FFN corresponds exactly to an AFA and vice versa. For learning, the binary step activation is relaxed to a logistic sigmoid with temperature annealing, and a three-phase curriculum strategy gradually increases sequence length and sigmoid sharpness over 20 epochs, allowing gradient descent to simultaneously learn transition topology (T) and logical type (β). Key Results: On synthetic AFAs, the paper reports 100% simulation accuracy in both a baseline configuration (n=20 states, |Σ|=6, strings up to length 50) and a high-complexity configuration (n=1,000 states, |Σ|=62, strings up to length 2,000), across all theoretical claims. The parameter count matches the derived O(kn²) bound exactly (2,960 for Config 1; 2,532,800 for Config 2). The succinctness ratio — network width n versus equivalent NFA size 2^n — reaches 5.24×10⁴ for Config 1 and 8.03×10⁵⁷ for Config 2, empirically confirming exponential compression. For learnability (Proposition 5.2), Config 1 achieves 100% test accuracy and Config 2 achieves 99.93% (95% CI: 0.99909–0.99934) — near-perfect recovery of unknown target AFAs from binary labels alone. Strengths: - The theoretical contribution is clean and rigorous: a single scalar modification (the bias term β) provably upgrades the computational class of an existing neural architecture from NFA-equivalent to AFA-equivalent, with a constructive, bidirectional isomorphism proof. - The exponential succinctness claim is not merely theoretical — it is directly operationalized: the network uses exactly n neurons while representing languages that require 2^n NFA states, with this ratio empirically validated at scales up to n=1,000. - The differentiable relaxation is elegant: β functions as a continuous slider between OR and AND logic, avoiding discrete structure search while remaining interpretable after training (the recovered β values directly decode to AND/OR types). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The experimental evaluation is entirely on synthetic, randomly generated AFAs with no connection to real-world language recognition tasks. It is unclear whether the curriculum learning strategy generalizes beyond this controlled setting, or whether gradient-based recovery of logic gates degrades on adversarially structured AFAs (e.g., deep chains of AND states). - The paper does not provide convergence guarantees or sample complexity bounds for the learning setup — realizability is proven but the optimization landscape analysis is informal, leaving open whether the approach scales reliably beyond the tested configurations or gets trapped in local minima for more structured automata.
Search Results 2604.01228 PDF
#55 — Innovative (72)

Test-Time Scaling Makes Overtraining Compute-Optimal (15 ▲ )

Nicholas Roberts, Sungjun Cho, Zhiqi Gao, Tzu-Heng Huang, Albert Wu, Gabriel Orlanski, Avi Trost, Kelly Buchanan, Aws Albarghouthi, Frederic Sala · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 6 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Closes a real gap by jointly optimizing model size, training tokens, and inference samples under a unified compute budget, yielding the surprising prescription that extreme overtraining (orders of magnitude beyond Chinchilla) is optimal when test-time scaling is accounted for. The two complementary modeling approaches (NLL power-law and Beta-distribution pass@k) agreeing on the same conclusion strengthens the finding. Held back by the sub-billion parameter scale and reliance on simple benchmarks where the overtraining advantage may not transfer to complex reasoning tasks.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Pretraining scaling laws (e.g., Chinchilla) optimize model size and training tokens under a fixed training budget, but ignore how models will be deployed. Test-time scaling laws (e.g., pass@k via repeated sampling) optimize inference compute allocation, but treat pretrained models as given. These two regimes have developed in isolation despite being fundamentally coupled: model size and training duration jointly determine both per-sample quality and per-sample inference cost. The core tension is that smaller models are cheaper per inference sample but weaker per sample, and the benefit of repeated sampling is a highly nonlinear function of per-sample quality. No existing work jointly optimizes model size, training tokens, and number of inference samples under a unified compute budget that includes both training and inference costs. Methodology: The authors propose Train-to-Test (T²) scaling laws that jointly optimize (N, D, k)—model parameters, training tokens, and number of repeated samples—subject to fixed training budget C_train = 6ND and inference budget C_inf = 2Nk. Two complementary modeling approaches are developed. Approach 1 extends the Chinchilla NLL model with a power-law term in k: L̂(N,D,k) = E + A/N^α + B/D^β + G/k^γ, modeling the negative log pass@k as a smooth surrogate. Approach 2 models pass@k accuracy directly by assuming per-question success probabilities follow a Beta distribution parameterized via Beta regression on the Chinchilla loss estimate, then computing mean pass@k analytically via Beta function properties. Both approaches apply an inference cost correction: for fixed C_inf, smaller models receive k = C_inf/(2N) more samples, linking pretraining decisions directly to test-time compute allocation. Models are fit to 106 checkpoints (5M–901M parameters, 50M–120B tokens) from Porian et al. (2024), extended with 21 overtrained checkpoints trained by the authors. Evaluation spans eight tasks: four real-world benchmarks (LAMBADA, ARC-Easy, SciQ, OpenBookQA) and four synthetic tasks (knowledge recall, arithmetic reasoning, commonsense causal, spatial reasoning). Key Results: Both T² approaches consistently forecast extreme overtraining relative to Chinchilla. Where Chinchilla prescribes ~20 tokens per parameter, T² recommends tokens-per-parameter ratios orders of magnitude higher (Figure 2 shows optimal D/N reaching 10^5–10^7 at large compute budgets). Empirically, the best overtrained checkpoint (typically 37M parameters) outperforms the Chinchilla-optimal checkpoint (901M parameters) on every one of the eight tasks under inference-corrected pass@k at C_train = 2.56×10^19, C_inf = 2×10^9 FLOPs. On LAMBADA the overtrained model achieves 49.90% vs. 27.30% pass@k; on Simple Reasoning 57.90% vs. 18.40%. Approach 1 extrapolates to overtrained checkpoints with 2.8% relative error vs. 8.4% for Approach 2. Post-training (both standard fine-tuning and SFT) preserves the overtraining advantage: overtrained models continue to dominate Chinchilla-optimal checkpoints on all three post-training tasks, though the degree of recommended overtraining is slightly reduced, consistent with overtrained models being harder to fine-tune (Springer et al., 2025). Strengths: - Closes a genuine gap by formalizing a joint optimization over training and inference compute rather than treating them in isolation, with two methodologically distinct formulations that agree on the main conclusion. - Theoretical grounding is solid: the Beta-distribution model for per-question difficulty is empirically motivated and yields a closed-form pass@k estimator; the NLL surrogate is justified via Jensen's inequality. - Empirical validation is unusually careful: predictions are tested against held-out overtrained checkpoints trained from scratch, not just interpolated, demonstrating true extrapolation rather than curve-fitting. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All experiments use models ≤901M parameters trained on RefinedWeb; the paper explicitly defers validation at frontier scales (billions of parameters, trillions of tokens), where the magnitude of recommended overtraining may differ substantially. - The inference cost model (C_inf = 2Nk) is a simplified FLOP count that ignores transformer-specific costs (KV cache, memory bandwidth, batching effects), which can shift the optimal operating point significantly in practice. - Evaluation tasks are mostly simple benchmarks solvable by small base models with low absolute accuracy; it is unclear whether the overtraining prescription generalizes to complex reasoning tasks where per-sample quality matters more and diminishing returns to k set in earlier.
Recommended 2604.01411 PDF
#56 — Innovative (72)

Survival In-Context: Prior-fitted In-context Learning Tabular Foundation Model for Survival Analysis

Dmitrii Seletkov, Paul Hager, Rickmer Braren, Daniel Rueckert, Raphael Rehms · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: First application of the prior-fitted in-context learning paradigm to survival analysis, with a mathematically principled synthetic prior under the Extended Hazard model that subsumes PH, AFT, and AH as special cases. The practical value is clear — competitive performance with zero tuning and 4,000x speedup — but the core contribution is adapting an existing paradigm (TabPFN/TabICL) to a new task rather than introducing fundamentally new methodology, and the hard scalability ceiling on large datasets limits impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Survival analysis (time-to-event modeling) is critical for clinical decision-making but remains difficult for modern ML due to censored outcomes, limited labeled data, and the heterogeneity of tabular covariates. Classical methods (Cox PH, AFT) impose rigid structural assumptions, while deep learning approaches (DeepSurv, DeepHit) require dataset-specific training and extensive hyperparameter tuning. The prior-fitted paradigm—pre-training on synthetic data and using in-context learning at inference—has succeeded for tabular classification and regression (TabPFN, TabICL), but extending it to survival analysis is non-trivial because censoring and time-dependent risk require modeling distributions over event times rather than scalar labels. Methodology: The paper introduces Survival In-Context (SIC), the first prior-fitted in-context learning model for survival analysis. SIC has two components. First, a synthetic survival data generation framework uses Structural Causal Models (SCMs) to generate covariate structures via DAGs with MLP/tree-based dependencies. Crucially, it generates two continuous target variables (η1, η2) and derives event times under the Extended Hazard (EH) model—h(t|x) = h0(t·e^η1)·e^η2—which subsumes Proportional Hazard, Accelerated Failure Time, and Accelerated Hazard as special cases. The inverse cumulative hazard H0^{-1} is drawn from a mixture of five baseline distributions (Weibull, Gompertz, Lognormal, Log-logistic, Birnbaum-Saunders). Both independent and administrative censoring are simulated. Second, SIC builds on the TabICL transformer encoder, adding a multiplicative Time-event Embedding that combines one-hot event indicator (linear layer) and event time (MLP), plus a DeepHit survival head with 10-bin discretization. Training uses a three-stage curriculum that progressively increases synthetic dataset size from 1,024 to 40,000 samples, initializing from TabICL classification weights and using AdamW with cosine/polynomial decay. Total pretraining: ~254 GPU-hours on one A100 80GB. At inference, SIC requires only a single forward pass with no hyperparameter tuning. Key Results: SIC is evaluated on 13 publicly available survival datasets (137–25,000 samples, 3–40 features) using nested 5-fold cross-validation with time-dependent C-index (C^td) against CoxPH, DeepSurv, DeepHit, and XGBoost—all tuned with 100 Optuna trials. SIC achieves the best mean and median rank across all 13 datasets. It is significantly outperformed by the best baseline on only 2 datasets: FRAMINGHAM (by 0.023 C-index vs. CoxPH) and SYNTHETIC (by 0.006 vs. DeepHit). SIC shows statistically significant improvements on METABRIC (0.660 vs. CoxPH 0.633, DeepSurv 0.627) and SUPPORT (0.694 vs. CoxPH 0.662, DeepSurv 0.672). On inference speed, SIC completes 5-fold cross-validation on SYNTHETIC (n=25,000) in 18.3 seconds vs. 0.056h for XGBoost, 0.674h for CoxPH, 9.6h for DeepSurv, and 19.9h for DeepHit (all with hyperparameter search). SIC underperforms on larger restricted-access datasets UNOS (62K samples, C^td=0.566 vs. DeepHit 0.611) and SEER (281K samples, C^td=0.833 vs. XGB 0.867), which exceed its pretraining prior size. Strengths: - Genuinely novel problem formulation: first application of the prior-fitted ICL paradigm to survival analysis, with a mathematically principled survival prior under the Extended Hazard model that covers PH, AFT, and AH as special cases. - Strong practical value: zero hyperparameter tuning, single forward pass, ~4,000x faster than DeepHit with tuning, competitive or superior performance on 11 of 13 benchmarks—directly actionable for clinical settings with compute constraints. - Rigorous evaluation: compiles what the authors claim is the full set of publicly accessible clinical survival datasets, uses nested 5-fold CV with 100-trial Optuna tuning for all baselines, includes both rank-based and per-dataset statistical significance testing. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Scalability ceiling is hard-coded into the prior: SIC fails to compete on large datasets (UNOS, SEER) where n exceeds the synthetic prior range. Extending the prior increases GPU memory and training time non-trivially, and the paper shows that stages 2 and 3 of curriculum learning provide diminishing returns even within the evaluated range. - Calibration is entirely absent from the evaluation. Calibration matters clinically—a model used for survival prediction in healthcare must produce reliable probability estimates, not just discriminative rankings. The authors acknowledge this gap but leave it completely to future work. - Coarse discretization (10 bins fixed) is heuristically chosen with no principled justification; the ablation shows no consistent trend across bins, suggesting the model's continuous-time behavior is poorly characterized. Alternative heads (DeepSurv, DeepEH) that produce continuous-time predictions were only tested for 2,000 steps and underperformed, leaving the continuous-time regime unexplored.
Citations 2603.29475 PDF
#57 — Innovative (72)

InCoder-32B-Thinking: Industrial Code World Model for Thinking (6 ▲ )

Jian Yang, Wei Zhang, Jiajun Wu, Junhang Cheng, Tuney Zheng, Fanglin Xu, Weicheng Gu, Lin Jing, Yaxin Du, Joseph Li, Yizhi Li, Yan Xing, Chuan Hao, Ran Tao, Ruihao Gong, Aishan Liu, Zhoujun Li, Mingjie Tang, Chenghua Lin, Siheng Chen, Wayne Xin Zhao, Xianglong Liu, Ming Zhou, Bryan Dai, Weifeng Lv · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 6 Apr 2026
Why this rank: InCoder-32B-Thinking introduces a genuinely novel concept in the Industrial Code World Model (ICWM), which learns to predict domain-specific backend outputs to amortize expensive toolchain invocations for scalable training data synthesis. The Error-driven Chain-of-Thought approach that retains failed intermediate turns is a creative methodological contribution. However, the overall paradigm — synthetic data generation via a learned simulator followed by fine-tuning — builds on established distillation and self-play concepts, and the thinking variant's regressions on several task families temper the claimed generality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Industrial software development domains — chip design (Verilog/RTL), GPU kernel optimization (CUDA/Triton), embedded firmware, and 3D CAD modeling — require reasoning about specialized hardware semantics absent from web-scale corpora. Existing LLMs, including frontier models, achieve limited success on benchmarks like KernelBench and VeriScope despite strong general coding performance. The root problem is twofold: there are no expert reasoning traces capturing iterative error-correction patterns specific to these domains, and no scalable way to generate grounded training data without repeated access to expensive hardware toolchains (simulators, compilers, emulators). This paper addresses both gaps with a purpose-built thinking model. Methodology: The paper proposes InCoder-32B-Thinking, trained via two synergistic components. First, Error-driven Chain-of-Thought (ECoT) synthesis generates reasoning traces by running a frontier model (DeepSeek-V3.2) against real domain-specific backends — Triton/CUDA for GPU kernels, Icarus/Verilator for RTL, Renode/STM32 for firmware, CadQuery for CAD — collecting up to K=4 correction rounds per task. Both successful and failed intermediate turns are retained to capture diagnostic patterns. Second, the Industrial Code World Model (ICWM) is a language model trained on these real execution trajectories that learns to predict backend outputs (outcome labels, diagnostic logs, numerical outputs) without invoking real toolchains. A domain-tag routing mechanism allows a single ICWM to serve all four verticals. Once trained, the ICWM replaces real backends in a large-scale synthesis loop, generating the DICWM dataset at low cost with periodic real-execution audits for calibration. The final training corpus D = Dreal ∪ DICWM is used to fine-tune the InCoder-32B base model. A lightweight prompt router injects domain-specific reasoning instructions (e.g., warp divergence and shared memory budgets for GPU tasks; clock domain crossings for RTL tasks) before each generation step. Key Results: On general benchmarks: 95.1% on HumanEval, 81.3% on LiveCodeBench v5 (77.1% on v6), 70.4% on SWE-bench Verified, and 63.9% on BFCL v3 — competitive with models of significantly larger scale or proprietary access. The thinking variant improves on the non-thinking InCoder-32B by 28.0% on LiveCodeBench. On industrial benchmarks, InCoder-32B-Thinking achieves the best open-source results across all domains evaluated: 84.0% compile-pass on CAD-Coder (vs. 79.0% Kimi-K2.5), 38.0% L2 on KernelBench (vs. 28.0% Kimi-K2.5), 75.6% Syn@1 on RealBench module synthesis, 51.0% on EmbedCGen, and 93.0% on SuperCoder. On VeriScope (chip design), it scores 75.4 (vs. 73.1 for Kimi-K2.5), though proprietary Claude-Sonnet-4.6 leads at 87.7. Notably, on some chip design benchmarks the non-thinking InCoder-32B (80.7 VeriScope score) outperforms the thinking version (75.4), suggesting thinking does not uniformly help all industrial sub-tasks. Strengths: - The ICWM is the first domain-specific world model for industrial code environments, enabling scalable training data synthesis by amortizing expensive toolchain invocations across many synthetic trajectories, with calibration audits to prevent drift. - ECoT's explicit modeling of the error-correction process — retaining failed intermediate turns, not just final solutions — captures industrial engineering reasoning patterns that single-shot supervision misses. - The approach achieves top open-source results across five fundamentally different industrial domains (chip design, GPU optimization, embedded systems, compiler optimization, 3D modeling) with a single 32B dense model, demonstrating generality without domain-specific model variants. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The thinking capability regresses on several task families relative to the non-thinking InCoder-32B: VeriScope chip design score drops from 80.7 to 75.4, Mercury code efficiency drops, and Text2SQL (Bird/Spider) performance degrades, suggesting extended chain-of-thought reasoning is detrimental for certain structured or constraint-dominated tasks. - ICWM calibration quality is underspecified — the paper does not report prediction accuracy of the world model vs. real backends, leaving open how much hallucinated or miscalibrated synthetic feedback degrades training signal. The periodic audit mechanism is described qualitatively without ablation. - Industrial benchmark coverage omits compiler-level optimization results (e.g., TritonBench G-call) from the main comparison tables, and several baselines have missing entries, making comprehensive cross-model comparison difficult.
Recommended 2604.03144 PDF
#58 — Innovative (71)

CrossHGL: A Text-Free Foundation Model for Cross-Domain Heterogeneous Graph Learning

Xuanze Chen, Jiajun Zhou, Yadong Li, Shanqing Yu, Qi Xuan · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: CrossHGL addresses a genuinely underexplored and practically important niche — text-free, cross-domain transfer on heterogeneous graphs — where LLM-based alignment methods are inapplicable. The data-driven meta-pattern mining via constrained random walks is a creative alternative to brittle expert-defined meta-paths, and the tri-prompt composition mechanism for frozen-backbone adaptation is architecturally clean. However, the evaluation is confined to four small academic benchmarks under a single leave-one-out protocol, with no demonstration on large-scale or truly diverse domain pairs, and the Freebase results reveal an unresolved ceiling under feature degeneration that tempers the generality claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Heterogeneous graphs model complex real-world systems with diverse node and edge types, but existing Heterogeneous Graph Neural Networks (HGNNs) operate under a closed-world assumption — training and testing must share identical schemas and feature spaces. Recent graph foundation models improve transferability but fall into three limited categories: (1) homogeneous-only models that discard multi-relational semantics, (2) heterogeneous models tied to fixed schemas or domain-specific meta-paths, and (3) LLM-enhanced models that require rich textual attributes for semantic alignment. In many safety-critical or privacy-sensitive domains (financial transactions, network anomaly detection, molecular property prediction), features are purely numerical or anonymized — making LLM-based alignment inapplicable. The gap: no method handles text-free, few-shot, cross-domain transfer on heterogeneous graphs while preserving multi-relational semantics. Methodology: CrossHGL is a three-phase foundation framework. Phase 1 (Semantic-Preserving Graph Transformation): heterogeneous graphs are homogenized via SVD-based feature alignment to a unified dimension d, combined with data-driven meta-pattern mining via constrained random walks that automatically discover Top-K high-frequency structural patterns (replacing expert-defined meta-paths). Edges in the resulting homogeneous graph encode aggregated intermediate-node features from all valid meta-pattern path instances, preserving multi-relational context without text. Phase 2 (Multi-Domain Pre-training): a Tri-Prompt mechanism — feature prompts (P_fea), edge prompts (P_edge), and structure prompts (P_str) — applies element-wise modulation at input and layer levels to a shared GNN backbone, decoupling feature, relational, and topological adaptation. Self-supervised training follows the Deep Graph Infomax (DGI) paradigm with local-global mutual information maximization across K source domains, with domain-specific prompts but shared backbone weights. Phase 3 (Cross-Domain Fine-tuning): the backbone is frozen; source-domain prompts are composed via learned softmax attention weighting and combined with target-specific open prompts. A decoupled dual-branch architecture (feature branch + structure branch) generates final representations used with non-parametric prototypical networks for N-way K-shot classification. This parameter-efficient design updates only lightweight prompt parameters during adaptation. Key Results: Evaluated on four heterogeneous benchmarks (ACM, DBLP, IMDB, Freebase) under a leave-one-out cross-domain protocol with 1-shot supervision, against 10 baselines spanning supervised GNNs, contrastive learning, and graph foundation models. For node classification, CrossHGL achieves best results on 3/4 datasets: on ACM +8.8% Micro-F1 over the strongest baseline (GCN); on DBLP 85.18% Micro-F1 vs. 44.01% for GraphSAGE (the strongest supervised baseline). For graph classification, CrossHGL ranks first on ACM, DBLP, and IMDB: on DBLP +13.4% Micro-F1 over HAN. Average relative improvements are 25.1% and 7.6% in Micro-F1 for node and graph classification respectively. On Freebase (a feature-poor dataset with 36 edge types), CrossHGL does not outperform HAN on Micro-F1 but achieves best Macro-F1, exposing a boundary condition for severely feature-degenerated settings. K-shot analysis on ACM (K=1..10) shows CrossHGL consistently leads across the range, with particularly strong advantages at K≤3. Ablation confirms all three prompt components contribute, with Feature Prompt removal causing the largest drop on ACM (−8.37% Micro-F1). Semantic-preserving transformation consistently outperforms direct flattening across GCN, SAGE, and GAT backbones. Strengths: - Cleanly addresses an underexplored but practically important setting (text-free heterogeneous cross-domain transfer) with a well-motivated three-phase design that preserves multi-relational semantics without LLMs. - Data-driven meta-pattern mining via constrained random walks avoids the brittleness of expert-defined meta-paths while remaining domain-agnostic. - The Tri-Prompt composition mechanism provides a principled way to distill and reweight source-domain priors for target adaptation, with frozen backbone ensuring parameter efficiency and avoiding catastrophic forgetting. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is limited to four relatively small academic benchmark datasets (ACM, DBLP, IMDB, Freebase); there is no demonstration at scale (millions of nodes/edges) or on truly diverse domain pairs (e.g., molecular graphs to social networks). - The Freebase results (36 edge types, feature-poor) expose a fundamental ceiling for the approach under severe feature degeneration — the framework's semantic-preserving transformation degrades to structural identifiers when features are absent, and the paper does not propose a principled solution for this case. - The method introduces multiple interacting hyperparameters (SVD dimension d, meta-pattern count K, walk length L, window size w, branch balancing coefficients α, β, γ) with limited guidance on sensitivity or selection, raising practical deployment concerns.
Recommended 2603.27685 PDF
#59 — Innovative (71)

Structured Causal Video Reasoning via Multi-Objective Alignment

Zinuo Li, Yongxin Guo, Jun Liu, Jiawei Zhan, Xi Jiang, Chengjie Wang, Mohammed Bennamoun, Farid Boussaid, Feng Zheng, Qiuhong Ke · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Introduces a principled 'Structure-First' paradigm grounded in event segmentation theory, with the P-FAB multi-objective RL algorithm providing a theoretically sound solution to gradient ambiguity in GRPO. The empirical observation that unstructured Chain-of-Thought reasoning hurts video understanding is a valuable negative result. However, the system combines many existing techniques (LoRA, GRPO, structured prompting) into a complex pipeline, and the inference overhead and fact-quality robustness are insufficiently analyzed.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) apply Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning in an unstructured manner inherited from text-only settings. This produces verbose, non-linear narratives that bury critical visual cues, exhibit reasoning drift away from the query, and reduce temporal reasoning to isolated frame retrieval rather than causal analysis. The authors empirically observe that reasoning-enhanced "Thinking" models (e.g., Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking) actually underperform their instruction-tuned counterparts on video benchmarks — a key motivating finding. Drawing on cognitive psychology (event segmentation theory), the paper argues that humans form a structured mental sketch of entities, actions, and event boundaries before engaging in abstract reasoning, and proposes a machine analogue of this process. Methodology: The paper introduces a "Structure-First" paradigm built around three components. First, Structured Event Facts (SEF) are generated prior to reasoning: for each 10–30 second temporal segment, the model outputs a fixed schema of time, person, human action, scene, camera position, and a causal event caption within `<facts>` tags. This acts as an explicit grounding constraint on downstream reasoning. Second, structured causal thinking then proceeds in three steps — Global Search & Localization (scanning facts for query-relevant cues), Causal Verification (antecedent-action-consequence validation with mandatory visual re-verification), and Final Alignment (global consistency check). Third, CausalFact-60K is curated from 32,049 videos across ActivityNet, QVHighlights, COIN, and Charades-STA, using Qwen3-VL-235B and Gemini-2.5-Pro in a mutual quality-judging pipeline, with 50% human inspection. Training proceeds in four progressive stages: facts alignment (Stage 1, SFT), format warm-start (Stage 1.5, SFT to stabilize XML structure), thinking warm-start (Stage 2, SFT with causal reasoning), and RL post-training (Stage 3). For Stage 3, the paper identifies that balancing four competing rewards (Format, Linear IoU, Multi-choice Accuracy, Length) with standard GRPO produces ambiguous gradient directions — identical advantage values for groups with the same average reward. To resolve this, the authors propose Pareto-Frontier guided Advantage Balancing (P-FAB): rewards are centered per group, standardized to remove scale dominance, and combined via the Frank-Wolfe algorithm applied to the MGDA minimum-norm problem over the probability simplex, yielding Pareto-optimal weights that dynamically amplify rare, hard-to-satisfy objectives. The base model throughout is Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct with LoRA for supervised stages and full-parameter updates for RL, at 1 fps and max 256 frames. Key Results: Factum-4B sets new open-source state-of-the-art on temporal grounding despite operating at 1 fps versus competitors at 2 fps with up to 2048 frames. On ActivityNet-Captions it achieves R1@0.5 = 48.4% and R1@0.7 = 28.1%, substantially surpassing Time-R1-7B (39.0% / 21.4%). On Charades-TimeLens it reaches R1@0.7 = 21.6%, the highest among open-source models. On general understanding benchmarks, it attains 64.7% on VideoMME and 73.6% on NExT-GQA (a 7% absolute gain over the Thinking base model). On ETBench it leads 6 of 8 sub-tasks and exceeds GPT-4o on TVG (66.1%) and TEM (26.8%). Ablations confirm that both facts and structured thinking are individually necessary: removing thinking causes the largest collapse (VideoMME drops to 58.5%), while removing facts causes logical drift. P-FAB consistently outperforms standard GRPO, with the performance gap widening at group size G=8 (ActivityNet R1@0.3 margin grows from 1.2% to 2.5%). Strengths: - The core empirical observation — that unstructured thinking hurts video understanding relative to instruction tuning — is credible, reproducible under controlled settings, and provides a genuinely useful negative result for the field. - P-FAB is a principled multi-objective RL solution grounded in MGDA theory, addressing a real and underappreciated failure mode of standard GRPO (directional ambiguity from averaged advantages); the algorithm is clearly specified and convergence-guaranteed. - The four-stage curriculum is carefully motivated: each stage addresses a specific failure mode (hallucinated facts, structural collapse, reasoning drift), and the intermediate Format Warm-Start stage is a pragmatic insight that prevents mode collapse. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Inference overhead of generating structured facts before every reasoning step is not analyzed — in practice this could double or triple output token count, which matters for deployment and benchmark fairness. - The frame-rate asymmetry in comparisons (Factum-4B at 1 fps / 256 frames vs. most baselines at 2 fps / 2048 frames) cuts both ways and is underexplored: superior performance under this constraint is impressive, but the paper does not report what happens at matched settings, leaving headroom questions open. - The quality of the structured facts themselves is never stress-tested: there is no analysis of how often the facts are incorrect, hallucinated, or insufficiently detailed, and no study of downstream reasoning failures that propagate from bad facts — a single point of failure in the pipeline.
Search Results 2604.04415 PDF
#60 — Innovative (71)

AURA: Always-On Understanding and Real-Time Assistance via Video Streams (35 ▲ )

Xudong Lu, Yang Bo, Jinpeng Chen, Shuhan Li, Xintong Guo, Huankang Guan, Fang Liu, Dunyuan Xu, Peiwen Sun, Heyang Sun, Rui Liu, Hongsheng Li · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 Apr 2026
Why this rank: AURA makes a meaningful contribution by formalizing the streaming video understanding problem into three interaction modes and introducing the Silent-Speech Balanced Loss, whose ablation results are striking (Proactive Alerting from 0% to 37.5%). The dual sliding-window context management with floating-window KV-cache reuse is a practical and principled engineering solution, though the overall framework is more of a well-executed systems integration than a conceptual breakthrough.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Most Video Large Language Models (VideoLLMs) are designed for offline, batch-mode processing—they require a complete video segment before analysis, making them unsuitable for live streaming applications such as real-time AI assistants, robotic systems, and continuous surveillance. Prior attempts at streaming VideoLLMs fall into two unsatisfying camps: decoupled architectures (a small trigger model decides when to invoke the main model, creating context inconsistency) and unified architectures that are limited to captioning-style narration and cannot handle open-ended question answering. A third class of full-duplex models lacks robustness for long-duration streaming, suffering memory overflow and performance degradation. The core challenge is enabling a single model to (1) autonomously decide when to remain silent vs. respond and (2) manage an unbounded, growing context without latency blowup. Methodology: AURA (Always-On Understanding and Real-Time Assistance) is an end-to-end streaming framework built on top of Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, co-designed across data, training, and deployment. Context Management: Video is chunked at 1-second intervals. A dual sliding-window strategy maintains the most recent N=30 seconds of visual tokens and the M=10 most recent QA interaction groups outside that window. This bounds context length while preserving the textual history that carries user intent. A floating-window variant (extending to N+N' before batch-truncating) enables KV-cache prefix reuse, avoiding the continuous prefix invalidation that kills inference efficiency in naive FIFO designs. QA Taxonomy and Data Engine: Three interaction modes are defined—Real-Time QA (immediate response), Proactive QA (model waits for sufficient visual evidence), and Multi-Response QA (ongoing tracking with multiple asynchronous answers). A five-stage Coarse-to-Fine Data Engine constructs ~115k streaming QA samples: scene segmentation → QA synthesis → difficulty/phrasing refinement → streaming structuring (unrolling sequences into per-answer training instances) → hallucination-aware quality verification. About 59k offline QA samples are mixed in to prevent catastrophic forgetting. Silent-Speech Balanced Loss: The class imbalance between silent tokens (dominant) and spoken responses is addressed by a weighted cross-entropy: silent tokens are down-weighted by 1/N_silent while response tokens receive weight 1. Additionally, only the last non-silent assistant message per training sample is supervised (earlier ones may lack sufficient visual evidence after window truncation), preventing hallucination-inducing gradients. Inference: The system is implemented on vLLM with sliding-window attention and prefix caching. ASR (Qwen3-ASR-1.7B) and TTS (Qwen3-TTS-12Hz-1.7B) operate asynchronously on a separate accelerator, enabling pipelined audio-visual interaction at 2 FPS on two 80G accelerators. Key Results: AURA achieves state-of-the-art on all three streaming benchmarks. On StreamingBench it scores 73.1% overall, outperforming the best open-source baseline MiniCPM-o-4.5 by 10.4% and surpassing proprietary Gemini-1.5-Pro by 6.0% (73.1% vs. 67.1%), leading on 14/18 fine-grained sub-tasks. On OVO-Bench it scores 65.3%, +4.2% over the prior best open-source (ViSpeak) and +2.3% over Gemini-1.5-Pro. On OmniMMI it scores 25.4%, first overall including on Proactive Alerting (37.5%) where non-streaming models score 0. Offline capability is largely preserved: AURA scores 58.8/68.1/65.1% on LongVideoBench/MVBench/Video-MME vs. its base model's 61.9/69.0/68.6%—modest drops showing streaming-oriented fine-tuning does not catastrophically hurt offline understanding. Latency breakdown on two 80G GPUs: ASR 84.2ms, TTFT 75ms (p90=87.8ms), first-sentence decode ~60ms, TTS first chunk 93ms, totaling ~312ms end-to-end. The ablation of the training objective is decisive: replacing the Silent-Speech Balanced Loss with standard cross-entropy drops OmniMMI overall from 25.4% to 16.4%, and collapses Proactive Alerting from 37.5% to 0.0%. Strengths: - The dual sliding-window context management is a principled and practical solution to the unbounded context problem, with the floating-window KV-cache reuse trick providing a concrete engineering win (bounded TTFT vs. growing latency in ablations). - The three-way QA taxonomy (Real-Time / Proactive / Multi-Response) is a useful formalization that drives the entire data pipeline and enables training for response timing, not just response content—a genuine gap in prior work. - The Silent-Speech Balanced Loss is well-motivated and ablation results are striking (9-point overall gain, Proactive Alerting goes from dead to 37.5%), confirming that naive cross-entropy training collapses to always-silent behavior. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The system requires two 80G accelerators at 2 FPS, which is a substantial compute footprint for a "real-time" assistant; scalability to lower-resource or edge settings is not addressed. - The training data pipeline is entirely synthetic (LLM-generated QA verified by another LLM), and the quality of proactive/multi-response supervision depends on MLLM judgment—systematic failure modes (e.g., biases in scene segmentation or timestamp verification) are not quantified. - Offline benchmark regressions (−3.1% on LongVideoBench, −3.5% on Video-MME) are described as modest but are non-trivial for a deployment replacing an offline model; the paper does not explore joint training strategies that might close this gap more fully.
Recommended 2604.04184 PDF
#61 — Innovative (71)

TC-AE: Unlocking Token Capacity for Deep Compression Autoencoders (9 ▲ )

Teng Li, Ziyuan Huang, Cong Chen, Yangfu Li, Yuanhuiyi Lyu, Dandan Zheng, Chunhua Shen, Jun Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Apr 2026
Why this rank: TC-AE delivers a clean mechanistic diagnosis — that the single abrupt token-to-latent bottleneck, not token count alone, causes latent collapse in deep compression autoencoders — supported by rigorous linear probing measurements. The staged token compression combined with joint iBOT self-supervised training achieves the rare feat of simultaneously improving generative quality (gFID 2.57 vs. 17.31 for prior art) while reducing compute by 3.7×, making it strictly Pareto-dominant on both axes. The restriction to ImageNet 256×256 and the reconstruction-generation tradeoff from SSL are notable limitations.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Latent diffusion models rely on tokenizers to compress images into compact latent representations. Recent work pushes toward deep compression (high spatial downsampling, e.g., 32×) compensated by expanding the latent channel dimension. This combination causes latent representation collapse — the latent space loses semantic structure — which degrades downstream generative quality even when reconstruction quality is maintained. Existing ViT-based tokenizers exacerbate this by applying a single abrupt bottleneck that compresses all token-level information at once. The key gap is that the intermediate token space — the bridge between pixels and latents — has been largely ignored as a design axis for improving generatability. Methodology: TC-AE addresses latent collapse from the perspective of the token space via two complementary mechanisms. First, staged token compression (STC): the ViT encoder is split into two stages. Stage 1 processes high-resolution image tokens (small patch size p, yielding up to N = HW/p² tokens) to build rich semantic structure. An intermediate convolutional compression module then reduces the token sequence to 1/4 its length. Stage 2 processes the compressed tokens, and a final pixel-shuffle + MLP bottleneck produces the compact latent. This decomposition distributes the total token-to-latent compression ratio across two steps, reducing the fraction lost at any single step — validated by showing the semantic preservation ratio A₂/A₁ improves from 0.08 (single bottleneck, p=8) to 0.31 (staged). Second, joint self-supervised training via iBOT: the tokenizer encoder is jointly trained with a student-teacher iBOT objective (masked image modeling + class-token distillation), structuring the token space semantically without relying on external pretrained foundation model weights. The full training loss is α·L_rec + L_iBOT, with α=0.1 found optimal. Experiments use ImageNet-1K at 256×256; the downstream generative model is LightningDiT trained for up to 800 epochs. Key Results: In system-level comparison on ImageNet 256×256 with 64 latent tokens (f=32 compression), TC-AE achieves gFID of 7.16 (w/o CFG) and 2.57 (w/ CFG) paired with a 675M DiT, versus 26.44 and 17.31 for prior deep compression tokenizers DC-AE and DC-AE-1.5 respectively. TC-AE requires only 164 GFLOPs versus 607 for DC-AE — a 3.7× efficiency improvement alongside drastically better generation. TC-AE with 64 tokens matches or outperforms 256-token low-compression tokenizers such as ViTok (gFID 2.45) and VAR (2.95). Ablations confirm the contributions are orthogonal: STC alone reduces gFID from 44.72→32.92 (IS 33.62→43.58); adding SSL further reduces to 16.39 (IS 71.33). TC-AE accelerates diffusion model convergence by 4.7× (gFID metric) and 3.5× (IS) versus the baseline. Token number scaling outperforms parameter scaling at matched compute, and the two are complementary when combined. Strengths: - The core diagnosis — that token-to-latent compression bottleneck, not lack of tokens per se, is the root cause of latent collapse — is empirically grounded via linear probing before and after the bottleneck, providing a clean mechanistic explanation rather than just an empirical fix. - TC-AE achieves significant generative improvements while reducing FLOPs by 3.7× compared to DC-AE, making it strictly better on both axes simultaneously. - The self-supervised training via iBOT is lightweight and avoids dependency on large pretrained teacher models (e.g., DINOv2), making the approach practical under limited compute budgets and decoupled from fixed teacher resolutions. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All experiments are conducted solely on ImageNet 256×256; generalization to higher resolutions, diverse domains, or video remains undemonstrated, and the optimal M (stage split) may shift with resolution or compression ratio. - There is an inherent tension between reconstruction and generation quality when SSL is applied: adding iBOT consistently degrades PSNR/SSIM (e.g., at p=8, PSNR drops from 25.13 to 24.84), meaning users must accept a reconstruction-generation tradeoff rather than Pareto-improving both simultaneously.
Recommended 2604.07340 PDF
#62 — Innovative (71)

Audio Hallucination Attacks: Probing the Reliability of Large Audio Language Models

Ashish Seth, Sonal Kumar, Ramaneswaran Selvakumar, Nishit Anand, Utkarsh Tyagi, Prem Seetharaman, Ramani Duraiswami, Dinesh Manocha · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: This paper identifies a genuinely useful and under-explored vulnerability axis — the explicit/implicit query distinction — revealing that LALMs that correctly reject explicit presence queries still hallucinate confidently on implicit questions presupposing the same absent sounds. The finding that Chain-of-Thought prompting backfires on implicit attacks (increasing ASR from 68.7% to 82.9%) is surprising and practically important for the safety community. The dual attack surface design and DPO mitigation with rejection-balanced negatives are well-conceived, though the work is limited by its short format, single-model mitigation evaluation, and artificial TTS-concatenation attack vector.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large Audio Language Models (LALMs) achieve impressive scores on standard audio reasoning benchmarks (MMAU, MMAR, MMAU-Pro), yet their reliability in real-world settings is poorly understood. The core vulnerability identified is that LALMs frequently skip the grounding step of verifying whether a sound actually exists in the audio before reasoning about it. Prior hallucination work focused exclusively on explicit queries ("Is X present?"), which the authors show is insufficient to reveal systematic failures — models that correctly reject explicit queries about absent sounds will still confidently hallucinate answers to implicit queries that presuppose those same sounds. Methodology: The authors introduce AHA (Audio Hallucination Attacks), a two-pronged attack framework targeting LALMs along two surfaces: (1) query-based attacks, subdivided into explicit queries (binary presence checks) and implicit queries (how/when/where/why questions that presuppose a sound's existence); and (2) audio-based attacks, which prepend TTS-synthesized speech referencing non-existent sounds to the original audio clip. The pipeline curates 8K audio clips from AudioCaps, Clotho, and MusicCaps, filtered via LLM-based caption consistency checking. For each clip, Gemini 3 Pro generates four counterfactual sound events: two adversarial (contextually plausible co-occurrences) and two random (out-of-context). This yields AHA-Eval (6.5K QA attack pairs) evaluated via Attack Success Rate (ASR) judged by GPT-5.2 (92.4% human agreement). For mitigation, the authors construct AHA-Guard (120K DPO preference pairs) including factual questions with rejection-balanced negatives to prevent over-refusal, and fine-tune Qwen2.5-Omni with LoRA on 8×A100s. Chain-of-Thought prompting is also evaluated as a test-time baseline. Key Results: Across six evaluated LALMs (Qwen2.5-Omni, Qwen3-Omni, R1-AQA, Audio Flamingo 3, Gemini 3 Pro, GPT-4 Audio), implicit attacks dramatically outperform explicit attacks — for Gemini 3 Pro, random-sound ASR jumps from 10.88% (explicit) to 59.67% (implicit), a 48.79% gap. Audio-based attacks are even more potent: Audio Flamingo 3's random explicit ASR rises from 1.90% (text) to 53.40% (audio). The best-performing model (Audio Flamingo 3) still reaches 95.35% ASR under adversarial audio implicit attacks, and Gemini 3 Pro reaches 79.65%. CoT prompting backfires on implicit attacks, increasing random implicit ASR from 68.74% to 82.90% for Qwen2.5-Omni. DPO fine-tuning on AHA-Guard reduces implicit ASR substantially: random implicit drops from 68.74% to 39.01% (text) and adversarial implicit from 79.19% to 40.24% (text), a ~49% reduction. Attention analysis confirms implicit attacks cause models to assign less attention to audio tokens during generation, and adversarial sounds produce high-confidence hallucinations (76.3% high-confidence "yes" vs. 23.7% for random). Strengths: - The explicit/implicit query distinction is a genuinely useful and under-explored axis; the finding that CoT worsens implicit hallucinations is surprising and practically important. - The dual attack surface (query manipulation + audio stream injection via TTS) is creative and reflects realistic adversarial conditions. - AHA-Guard's inclusion of balanced factual negatives to prevent rejection bias is a thoughtful design choice that addresses a common pitfall in safety alignment. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is short (4 pages + references) and lacks ablations on key design choices: the number of DPO epochs, LoRA rank sensitivity, the effect of AHA-Guard dataset size, and whether gains generalize beyond Qwen2.5-Omni. - Evaluation relies on LLM-as-Judge (GPT-5.2) for ASR, which introduces a dependency on a closed model that may itself have biases; the 92.4% human agreement on only 200 samples is a thin validation for 6.5K attack pairs. - The audio-based attack uses TTS speech prepended to audio, which is an artificial and detectable manipulation — robustness to more subtle or realistic audio injection (e.g., mixed rather than concatenated) is not explored.
