#63 — Innovative (71)

AgentSocialBench: Evaluating Privacy Risks in Human-Centered Agentic Social Networks (4 ▲ )

Prince Zizhuang Wang, Shuli Jiang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 6 Apr 2026
Why this rank: AgentSocialBench earns 71 for its identification of the abstraction paradox—a genuinely counterintuitive finding that substitutive privacy defenses expand partial disclosure surface in contexts where silence was the natural default. This is a non-obvious, practically important insight for privacy system designers. The benchmark is impressively comprehensive in scope (352 scenarios, seven topologies, eight LLMs). However, it remains a benchmark paper built entirely on synthetic data without architectural solutions, and the core methodology (scenario generation, LLM-as-judge evaluation) follows established patterns.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: As personalized LLM agent frameworks (e.g., OpenClaw) enable teams of AI agents to act on behalf of individual humans within shared social platforms, a new class of privacy risks emerges that has no analogue in prior agent-only or single-agent settings. Agents must simultaneously coordinate across domain boundaries (health ↔ finance ↔ social), mediate between humans, and interact with other users' agents—all while protecting sensitive personal information. Prior benchmarks (ConfAIde, PrivLM-Bench, MAGPIE, MAMA, AgentLeak) each cover at most a subset of these dimensions: none jointly handle multi-agent coordination, cross-domain flows, agent mediation, cross-user interaction, multi-party topologies, and social graph structure. The paper asks whether current LLM agents can preserve contextual privacy under these compound conditions, and whether prompt-based defenses help. Methodology: The authors introduce AgentSocialBench, a benchmark of 352 scenarios across seven categories: Cross-Domain (CD, 100), Mediated Communication (MC, 100), Cross-User (CU, 50), Group Chat (GC, 28), Hub-and-Spoke (HS, 23), Competitive (CM, 23), and Affinity-Modulated (AM, 28). Each scenario is grounded in synthetic multi-domain user profiles (health, finance, social, schedule, professional, lifestyle) with hierarchical sensitivity labels (1–5) and directed social graphs with asymmetric affinity tiers. Privacy violations are defined via contextual integrity theory across four boundary types: domain, user, mediation, and affinity-modulated. Three privacy instruction levels are evaluated: L0 (unconstrained), L1 (explicit hard rules + acceptable abstractions), and L2 (L1 + Domain Boundary Prompting + Information Abstraction Templates + Minimal Information Principle). Evaluation uses LLM-as-judge (Claude Opus 4.6) computing per-category leakage rates (CDLR, MLR, CULR, MPLR, HALR, CSLR/CER, ACS), Information Abstraction Score (IAS, 0/0.5/1.0), and Task Completion Quality (TCQ, 0–1.0). Eight LLM backbones are tested: GPT-5 Mini, Claude Haiku 4.5, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Kimi K2.5, MiniMax M2.1, DeepSeek V3.2, Qwen3-235B. Key Results: Cross-domain (intra-team) coordination creates the strongest leakage pressure: CDLR of 0.40–0.67 across models, roughly 2–3× the rates in mediated communication (~0.21–0.30) and cross-user (~0.14–0.29) settings. Multi-party settings do not uniformly amplify dyadic risks: competitive scenarios suppress self-disclosure (CSLR ~0.08–0.10) as adversarial pressure makes agents guarded, while affinity-modulated scenarios achieve near-perfect compliance (ACS ≈ 1.0 across all models). Hub-and-spoke shows wide model variance, indicating coordinator-role design matters greatly. Privacy instruction escalation from L0 to L2 improves Information Abstraction Score substantially (+0.16 dyadic, +0.13 multi-party) but reduces aggregate leakage only modestly for dyadic scenarios (−0.04) and actually increases it slightly for multi-party (+0.01). The key finding is the abstraction paradox: in mediated communication and cross-user scenarios where agents would otherwise remain silent, providing abstraction templates creates a sanctioned vocabulary for referencing sensitive topics, converting 10–20% of previously zero-leakage items into partial leakage. Full leakage drops sharply (77–93% of full-leakage items at L0 transition to no/partial at L2), confirming defenses prevent direct disclosure—but partial leakage expands. Task completion quality is stable across defense levels (TCQ ~0.77–0.80), indicating no privacy-utility tradeoff in aggregate. No model Pareto-dominates: Claude Sonnet 4.6 achieves best TCQ (94.1% task completion) but not lowest leakage; GPT-5 Mini has lowest CDLR (0.40) but worst task completion (68.2%). The lifestyle→finance and finance→schedule domain pairs exhibit the highest cross-domain leakage; social→schedule the lowest. Strengths: - The abstraction paradox is a genuine, non-obvious finding with clear practical implications: substitutive defenses ("replace X with Y") expand partial disclosure surface in categories where silence was the natural default, while suppressive defenses ("avoid topic entirely") would avoid this at potential utility cost—a novel insight for privacy system designers. - The benchmark is comprehensive in scope: 352 scenarios, seven topologically distinct interaction types, six user profile domains, directed social graphs with asymmetric affinity tiers, three defense levels, and eight backbone LLMs, covering ground no prior benchmark addressed jointly. - Evaluation methodology is carefully designed to avoid confounds: the privacy judge is template-blind (receives only conversation history and privacy boundary, not defense level), and item-level transition analysis (L0→L2 leakage classification changes) validates that measured increases in partial leakage reflect genuine behavioral change rather than measurement artifact. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark uses only synthetic user profiles and scenarios generated by GPT-5.2 and Claude Opus 4.6, not real agentic social network deployment traces; ecological validity is unconfirmed, and the distributional properties of synthetic scenarios may not match real-world privacy failure modes. - The paper evaluates only prompt-based defenses and explicitly defers architectural solutions (information-flow control, learned privacy policies, formal verification) to future work, leaving the core finding—that prompt engineering is insufficient—without a concrete alternative path forward. - The LLM-as-judge evaluation, while validated by human experts for metric definitions, may itself have systematic biases toward its own abstraction patterns; the evaluator (Claude Opus 4.6) shares model family with two of the agent backbones tested, and inter-rater reliability statistics between human judges and LLM judge at the item level are not reported in the main paper.
Recommended 2604.01487 PDF
#64 — Innovative (71)

MFS: An Efficient Model Family Serving System for LLMs

cse.hkust.edu.hk · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: MFS introduces the clean and non-obvious insight of restructuring an entire model family into a single nested model via Knowledge Precipitation, unlocking cross-tier batching and KV-cache sharing that are architecturally impossible with independent model serving. The approach is practical — only 24 hours of fine-tuning on a small dataset — and the 47.8% KV-cache memory reduction and 56.1% latency improvement are meaningful. However, the lack of validation at commercially relevant scales (70B) and missing comparisons against vLLM with PagedAttention limit confidence in real-world competitiveness.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM service providers routinely deploy entire model families (e.g., Llama 2 at 7B, 13B, and 70B) to satisfy diverse quality-of-service requirements. Existing serving systems such as Orca and vLLM optimize inference for a single model in isolation, leaving two major inefficiencies unaddressed when serving a family simultaneously: (1) GPU memory is wasted because each model must store its own parameters and KV-caches that cannot be shared across sizes, and (2) cross-model batching is impossible because different architectures violate the homogeneity requirement for efficient GPU parallelism. No prior system exploits the structural redundancy and layer uniformity shared by models within the same family. Methodology: MFS introduces a multi-tiered serving architecture built around a single restructured model that encapsulates all smaller members of a family. The core technique, Knowledge Precipitation (adapted from knowledge distillation), fine-tunes the largest model in the family end-to-end with a weighted multi-tier loss L = L₀ + λ₁L₁ + … + λᵢLᵢ, where each Lᵢ is a language-modeling loss computed at an intermediate layer exit point. Fine-tuning proceeds step-by-step (n−1 passes for an n-tier structure), propagating gradients from higher tiers to lower ones. A system-aware optimization divides tiers by layer count only (not attention heads), matching latency of smaller standalone models rather than parameter counts—for instance, the first 24 layers of Llama2-13B are found to match the inference latency of standalone Llama2-7B at prompt length 1024. On top of the unified model, MFS implements three serving optimizations: (1) group batching, which co-batches requests targeting different tiers when they share the same model prefix layers; (2) attention fusion, which concatenates QKV matrices across requests to enable parallel attention computation even when sequence lengths differ; and (3) shareable KV-cache management, which allows the KV-cache computed by a lower tier to be reused directly by the higher tier, avoiding recomputation when a request escalates from a small to a large model. The system also supports pluggable scheduling policies including speculative sampling (lower tier as draft, upper tier as verifier with shared KV-cache) and heuristic query routing. Key Results: Quality preservation: across ten standard benchmarks (MMLU, PIQA, HellaSWAG, BoolQ, ARC, ANLI), MFS tiers match or exceed the corresponding standalone baselines on the majority of metrics. For the Llama2-13B two-tier model, MFS-13B outperforms vanilla Llama2-13B on 8/10 metrics and MFS-7B outperforms Llama2-7B on 7/10. The Qwen-14B two-tier results are similarly competitive. System performance vs. Orca: MFS reduces end-to-end token generation latency by up to 56.1% and improves job completion time (JCT) by up to 31.2% in batching scenarios. KV-cache sharing reduces GPU memory footprint by 47.8%. GPU utilization improves from 23.9% to 59.8% (35.9 percentage-point absolute gain) in the speculative sampling scenario, where Orca's KV-cache becomes invalid on model switches while MFS reuses it seamlessly. Fine-tuning required only ~24 hours on 16× H800 GPUs with a 9.85k-dialogue dataset, far cheaper than training from scratch. Strengths: - The core insight—restructuring a model family into a single nested model rather than serving independent models—is clean and well-motivated, unlocking cross-tier batching and KV-cache sharing that are architecturally impossible otherwise. - The step-by-step Knowledge Precipitation procedure is practical: it requires only a small fine-tuning dataset and 24 hours of compute, making it accessible to providers without trillion-token corpora or massive training budgets. - Validated across two distinct model families (Llama2 and Qwen), multiple tier counts (2-tier and 3-tier), and diverse benchmark tasks, giving reasonable confidence in generality. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experimental scope is limited to relatively small models (up to Llama2-13B and Qwen-14B); the 70B case is discussed theoretically but not demonstrated due to GPU constraints, leaving the most commercially relevant scale unvalidated. - The attention fusion approach introduces cross-request attention computations (attention between tokens of different requests) that are mathematically incorrect for standard self-attention semantics; the paper dismisses this as not increasing wall-clock time due to GPU parallelism but does not assess whether this spurious cross-request interaction degrades output quality. - The evaluation lacks comparison against strong modern baselines such as vLLM with PagedAttention or DistServe; the paper justifies this by claiming different optimization targets, but the omission makes it difficult to assess the real-world competitiveness of MFS in production deployments.

#65 — Innovative (71)

OpenVLThinkerV2: A Generalist Multimodal Reasoning Model for Multi-domain Visual Tasks (29 ▲ )

Wenbo Hu, Xin Chen, Yan Gao-Tian, Yihe Deng, Nanyun Peng, Kai-Wei Chang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Apr 2026
Why this rank: G²RPO's use of 1D optimal transport to normalize reward distributions across heterogeneous tasks is a theoretically principled and computationally elegant solution to a real multi-task RL problem. The comprehensive 18-benchmark evaluation across perception and reasoning tasks, with SOTA results surpassing GPT-4o and specialized models on several benchmarks, validates the approach. However, the per-task hyperparameters for length and entropy shaping limit generalizability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Applying Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to multimodal LLMs across diverse visual tasks is destabilized by two compounding problems. First, reward topologies vary wildly across task types—binary signals in math VQA versus dense IoU scores in grounding—causing severe inter-task gradient imbalance. Standard GRPO's sample-wise standard deviation normalization suppresses low-variance tasks; Dr.GRPO removes normalization but amplifies high-variance tasks; EMA-GRPO uses moving averages but remains a linear transformation that preserves distributional shape, leaving the optimizer vulnerable to heavy-tail outliers and bimodal reward distributions. Second, jointly training on vision-centric tasks (OCR, grounding) and reasoning-centric tasks (math, science VQA) induces divergent response length and entropy dynamics—over-exploration in reasoning tasks causes entropy explosion while perception tasks collapse into degenerate low-entropy outputs. Methodology: The paper proposes three complementary mechanisms integrated into OpenVLThinkerV2, built on Qwen3-VL-Instruct-8B via RL post-training on the OneThinker-600k dataset. Gaussian GRPO (G²RPO) replaces scalar standardization with non-linear distributional matching via 1D Optimal Transport. The key insight is that the W₂ distance between the empirical reward distribution and N(0,1) admits a closed-form solution in 1D: advantages are assigned by mapping each reward's empirical CDF rank to the inverse CDF (quantile function) of the standard normal. Specifically, Â_i = Φ⁻¹(F_{R_τ}(R_i)), where ties receive the mean of the corresponding quantiles. This guarantees that every task's advantage distribution converges exactly to N(0,1), providing inter-task gradient equity without requiring momentum hyperparameters and mathematically capping outliers at extreme quantiles rather than allowing them to dominate gradient updates. Task-level response length shaping applies a trapezoidal reward envelope parameterized per task category, rewarding outputs in an optimal length range [L_low, L_high] and softly penalizing outputs outside [L_min, L_max]. This explicitly elicits long reasoning chains for math/science VQA while enforcing concise direct outputs for grounding and OCR. Task-level entropy shaping adds a margin-based regularization loss L_ent_reg = max(0, H_task − H_max) + max(0, H_min − H_task) that fires only when task entropy drifts outside a per-task target band, preventing both entropy explosion in reasoning tasks and entropy collapse in vision-centric tasks. Key Results: Evaluated across 18 benchmarks spanning 6 task categories, OpenVLThinkerV2 establishes SOTA among open-source 8B-scale models and outperforms leading proprietary models on several benchmarks. On general VQA: 71.6% MMMU (vs. 70.7% GPT-4o, 60.2% baseline), 88.2% MMBench, 73.8% MMStar. On math VQA: 79.5% MathVista, 65.8% MathVerse, 53.4% MathVision. On chart understanding: 87.4% ChartQA (exceeding Gemini 2.5 Pro's 83.3%). On document understanding: 96.7% DocVQA, 911 OCRBench (surpassing specialized DeepEyesV2 at 882 and GPT-5 at 810). On spatial reasoning: 83.1% EmbSpatial (surpassing GPT-5's 82.9%). On grounding: 93.4%/88.2%/90.4% on RefCOCO/RefCOCO+/RefCOCOg, outperforming specialized Grounding DINO. Ablation confirms additive gains: G²RPO alone gives the largest jump, with entropy shaping and length shaping each contributing further incremental improvements across all six task categories. Strengths: - The G²RPO formulation is theoretically principled—Wasserstein-2 optimal transport in 1D has a closed-form solution, making the approach computationally cheap (rank-sort + erfinv) while providing strict distributional guarantees no linear method can offer. - The paper addresses a genuinely hard multi-task RL challenge (not just math reasoning) and validates across a comprehensive 18-benchmark suite spanning perception-heavy and reasoning-heavy tasks, including comparisons against task-specific expert models. - The analysis of per-task response length and entropy dynamics during training is empirically grounded and motivates the shaping mechanisms from observed failure modes rather than ad-hoc design. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The length and entropy shaping mechanisms introduce per-task hyperparameters (L_min, L_low, L_high, L_max, H_min, H_max, λ_ent) that are set by empirical observation rather than principled search; the paper acknowledges that automated hyperparameter search is left to future work, which limits reproducibility and portability to new task distributions. - All comparisons use an 8B-parameter model trained for a single epoch on a filtered 600k dataset with ~3 days of AWS Trainium compute; scalability of G²RPO to larger models or longer training has not been demonstrated, and the advantage normalization behavior under very large group sizes or highly non-stationary reward distributions during extended training remains uncharacterized.

#66 — Innovative (71)

PhyGeo-KG: Physics-Regularized Distant Supervision for Multimodal Geometric Knowledge Graph Construction in Catenary Maintenance

mdpi.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: PhyGeo-KG introduces a genuinely novel problem formulation — long-distance semantic bias in knowledge graph construction — and addresses it with the creative Physics Critic mechanism that operationalizes physical plausibility as a differentiable soft gate grounded in domain physics. The IFace abstraction that bridges 3D B-Rep geometry and unstructured text without labeled training data is a principled contribution. The white-box design is well-suited to safety-critical infrastructure. Evaluation scale (83 core edges, single 2km section) and absence of neural baselines limit the strength of empirical claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: High-speed railway catenary maintenance generates heterogeneous data from two irreconcilable sources: unstructured maintenance logs (describing what happened) and BIM/IFC geometric models (specifying where components are). Existing knowledge graph (KG) construction methods, particularly distant supervision (DS) pipelines, produce relations that are linguistically plausible but geometrically or physically infeasible — a phenomenon the authors term "long-distance semantic bias." Pure NLP-based models assign high confidence to entity co-mentions even when the entities are physically far apart and cannot be structurally connected. This semantic-physical disconnect, combined with severe label sparsity in failure records, makes reliable KG construction for safety-critical infrastructure extremely difficult. No prior framework embeds explicit geometric and topological feasibility constraints as first-class computable regularizers throughout the extraction loop. Methodology: PhyGeo-KG is a three-phase white-box (interpretable, non-neural) framework. Phase 1 constructs a four-dimensional Semantic-Geometric-Physical-Procedural (S-G-P-P) ontology by fusing the Function-Behavior-Structure (FBS) design theory with the Product-Process-Resource (PPR) engineering paradigm. A key novelty is the Geometric Interface (IFace) concept — physically meaningful connection regions (BoltHole, ClampSurface, ArticulatedJoint) extracted from IFC B-Rep geometry and parameterized as (centroid, normal, type, scale) tuples. Phase 2 generates a high-recall candidate graph via deterministic BIM-text alignment: IFC topology is preserved as a native graph, location cues induce Regions of Interest (ROI) for spatial pruning, and mentions are scored against IFC anchors using a weighted combination of ontology-type matching, IFace Jaccard similarity, and k-hop topological cosine similarity. Phase 3 refines the candidate graph via physics-regularized distant supervision: 15 labeling functions produce probabilistic weak labels, a linear evidence aggregator (logistic regression on keyword co-occurrence, syntactic patterns, alignment confidence) computes semantic scores s_e, and a Physics Critic computes physical plausibility c_e = exp(-d_geo/d_0) where d_0=20m is the characteristic interaction scale derived from standard catenary span half-wavelength. A dual gating rule (s_e > τ_sem AND c_e > τ_phy) produces the refined core graph, followed by ontology-driven evolution using inference templates to expand from high-confidence seeds while requiring newly inferred edges to also pass the Physics Critic. Key Results: The Catenary-KG-2K dataset covers a 2km section with 6,861 IFC entities and 350 maintenance documents producing 3,238 initial candidate edges. The progressive filtering pipeline reduces these to 120 geometrically grounded edges (Geo-aware) and finally 83 physics-verified core edges. Precision@50 improves from 0.56 (text-only) to 0.82 (geo-aware) to 0.90 (PhyGeo-KG core), with F1 on the annotated 120-edge subset reaching 0.92 (vs. 0.80 for geo-aware), at the cost of a modest recall drop from 1.00 to 0.94. Physical consistency rate (PCR, fraction of edges with d_geo ≤ 5m) rises from 34% to 86%, and average inter-entity distance compresses from 19.65m to 3.37m. The Physics Critic achieves a hallucination removal rate of 79.4% against semantically confident but physically distant edges. A Pearson correlation of r=0.23 (p=0.013) between semantic confidence and physical distance confirms the long-distance semantic bias empirically. Ontology-driven evolution expands the core graph 23.6-fold (83 → 2,041 relations) via 24 structural templates, with inferred relations showing physically compact distributions (σ=0.09m for rigid cantilever assemblies). A case study demonstrates instance-level fault localization from ambiguous inspection text to a unique BIM GUID. Strengths: - The white-box design (lexicon matching + logistic regression + exponential distance decay) is well-suited to the cold-start, label-scarce setting and is fully auditable for safety-critical validation — an important engineering advantage over opaque neural encoders. - The Physics Critic elegantly operationalizes "physical plausibility" as a differentiable soft gate that continuously penalizes long-distance semantic hallucinations, rather than applying a hard post-hoc cutoff, and this design is justified by domain physics (catenary span geometry). - The IFace abstraction is a principled contribution: it converts 3D B-Rep geometry into discrete, semantically typed interaction anchors that bridge unstructured text and structured BIM without requiring labeled training data. - The dual-safeguard on ontology-driven evolution (requiring newly inferred edges to pass the Physics Critic) prevents logical inference from generating physically impossible relations, maintaining graph fidelity during expansion. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation scale is extremely small: 83 core edges and a 120-edge annotated subset from a single 2km railway section provide insufficient statistical power to claim generality, and comparisons against competitive neural DS baselines (PCNN, BERT-based RE, Snorkel+transformers) are entirely absent, making it impossible to assess whether the white-box approach sacrifices meaningful accuracy. - The Physics Critic's instantiation — a single exponential decay of Euclidean IFace centroid distance — is a very coarse proxy for physical plausibility; it cannot distinguish between components that are geometrically close but functionally disconnected (e.g., components from different subsystems that happen to be co-located), and the framework explicitly defers richer PDE-based mechanism validation to future work.
Search Results
#67 — Innovative (71)

CogBias: Measuring and Mitigating Cognitive Bias in Large Language Models

Fan Huang, Songheng Zhang, Haewoon Kwak, Jisun An · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: CogBias makes a meaningful methodological contribution with its contrastive probing design that correctly diagnoses and fixes the instruction-detection confound plaguing prior interpretability work. The coherent Behavior–Representation–Intervention progression and the independently emergent two-cluster structure (D1/D2 vs. D3/D4) across behavioral, representational, and steering analyses suggest a real organizational principle rather than an artifact. The 25-benchmark capability evaluation is thorough and yields actionable asymmetric cost findings. However, the paper's central activation steering claim is seriously undermined by the fact that learned steering directions are statistically indistinguishable from random perturbations at moderate strength, suggesting the mechanism may be general activation disruption rather than targeted bias suppression.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large Language Models are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains (medical diagnosis, legal analysis, financial forecasting), yet prior work on their cognitive biases has been limited to behavioral observation — measuring outputs without examining internal representations or applying targeted interventions. This gap matters because behavioral observations alone cannot distinguish whether biases arise from stable, structured internal features or from surface-level prompt sensitivities, a distinction that determines whether principled mitigation is possible. The paper asks three questions in a Behavior–Representation–Intervention progression: do LLMs exhibit systematic cognitive biases across four families; are these biases encoded as linearly separable directions in activation space; and can activation steering reduce biased behavior while preserving capability? Methodology: The authors introduce LLM CogBias, a benchmark organized around four bias families — Judgment & Decision (anchoring, base-rate neglect, sunk cost, status quo), Information Processing & Salience (framing, availability, illusory truth, randomness misperception), Social & Belief-Related (confirmation bias, overconfidence), and Response & Interaction-Induced (sycophancy, position preference) — drawing on four existing datasets: Malberg30k, CoBBLEr, BBQ, and BiasMonkey (5,300 unified instances). Every instance appears in paired conditions (bias-inducing vs. control) with computable ground-truth baselines, enabling objective bias measurement. Behavioral profiling (RQ1) uses vanilla prompts across GPT-4o-mini, Llama-3.3-70B, and Qwen2.5-72B. For probing (RQ2), the authors carefully introduce a contrastive prompt design — both conditions acknowledge the same heuristic mechanism but differ only in whether it is endorsed — to avoid the instruction-detection confound that inflated non-contrastive probe accuracy to >99% even at layer 0. Residual stream activations from all 80 transformer layers are extracted and linear probes trained with 5-fold cross-validation. For activation steering (RQ3), a mean-difference direction is computed and subtracted from hidden states during inference (h' = h − α·v), with grid search over α ∈ [0,3] and three target layers per model, evaluated against 25 downstream benchmarks spanning medical QA, reasoning, NLU, safety, and code. Key Results: All three LLMs exhibit systematic biases across all four families. Response bias is most pronounced (78.1% first-option selection vs. 36.8% chance baseline), followed by Information Processing (50.7% order-dependent choices), Judgment (2.7–7.7pp anchor-induced shifts), and Social (near-zero stereotype rate, but heavily driven by alignment-induced refusals rather than genuine debiasing). Prompt-level debiasing is strongly family-dependent: it reduces Response biases dramatically (+40.5% position independence) but backfires for Judgment biases (−4.4% accuracy), with the worst individual cases being Hyperbolic Discounting (−21.0pp) and Disposition Effect (−19.6pp). Contrastive probing achieves 97.8% mean classification accuracy across four families (D1/Judgment: 99.8%, D2/Info Processing: 98.2%, D4/Response: 99.2%, D3/Social: 93.9%), with linear probes outperforming MLPs, confirming that bias representations are linearly separable. Cross-model transfer fails (49.7% accuracy), indicating architecture-specific representations despite near-orthogonal steering directions (mean cosine similarity 0.01). Activation steering achieves 26–32% bias score reduction at Pareto-optimal configurations. A two-cluster structure emerges: D1/D2 (Judgment, Information Processing) require strong early-layer steering (α=2.6–3.0, layers 0–15), while D3/D4 (Social, Response) respond to gentle mid-layer steering (α=0.3–1.7, layers 10–15). Llama preserves near-baseline capability (<1pp mean degradation across all 25 benchmarks); Qwen shows asymmetric costs — D3/D4 steering is benign (−0.3pp, −1.1pp) but D1/D2 causes substantial degradation (−19.0pp, −10.8pp) due to the higher α required. Despite near-orthogonal bias directions, steering response curves are correlated across models (r(246)=0.621, p<0.001), suggesting shared functional organization. Strengths: - The contrastive probing design is a genuine methodological contribution: the paper correctly identifies and empirically diagnoses the instruction-detection confound in non-contrastive probing and implements a principled fix, avoiding a common pitfall that would have inflated all interpretability claims. - The Behavior–Representation–Intervention progression yields a coherent narrative: the two-cluster finding (D1/D2 vs. D3/D4) emerges independently across behavioral debiasing effectiveness, optimal steering layer/strength, and capability cost, which substantially strengthens confidence that this is a real organizational principle. - The 25-benchmark capability evaluation is appropriately comprehensive and reveals the asymmetric cost structure between Llama and Qwen, providing practically actionable guidance rather than just bias reduction numbers. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The robustness controls (random directions, orthogonal directions, cross-family transfer) show that learned steering directions are statistically indistinguishable from random perturbations at α=1.5 (all p > 0.05) and provide only 1.6% greater bias reduction than controls. The paper attributes this to a broad representational basin, but this seriously undercuts the claim that activation steering is "targeted" — the mechanism may simply be activation disruption rather than directional suppression of bias-encoding subspaces. - The benchmark covers only four bias families from one representative dataset each (one dataset per family, not per category), so coverage of the 11 operational categories is uneven and several important biases (confirmation bias, hindsight bias, availability heuristic as a distinct phenomenon) are absent or conflated. The generalizability of the two-cluster finding to a broader bias taxonomy remains untested. - Activation steering requires open-weight model access with internal activation hooks, which excludes prominent proprietary models (GPT-4, Claude) and limits deployment relevance. The steering approach also applies uniform perturbation at all token positions, leaving open whether position-selective or layer-adaptive methods would yield a better capability–bias tradeoff, especially for Qwen's D1/D2 families where the current approach causes severe degradation.
Recommended 2604.01366 PDF
#68 — Innovative (69)

KnowU-Bench: Towards Interactive, Proactive, and Personalized Mobile Agent Evaluation (34 ▲ )

Tongbo Chen, Zhengxi Lu, Zhan Xu, Guocheng Shao, Shaohan Zhao, Fei Tang, Yong Du, Kaitao Song, Yizhou Liu, Yuchen Yan, Wenqi Zhang, Xu Tan, Weiming Lu, Jun Xiao, Yueting Zhuang, Yongliang Shen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Apr 2026
Why this rank: The information asymmetry design — hidden user profile accessible only to the simulator while the agent sees only behavioral logs — is the correct formulation for realistic personalization evaluation and represents a genuine methodological advance over benchmarks that hand preferences to agents as context. The three-way proactive decomposition (Act/Silent/Stop) reveals a fundamental calibration tradeoff invisible to single-metric evaluation, and the finding that 80% of proactive failures stem from intervention calibration rather than GUI execution reframes the bottleneck for mobile agents. Limited persona diversity (four profiles) and reliance on GPT-4o as user simulator temper the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Current mobile GUI agent benchmarks evaluate explicit instruction-following in isolation, but real deployment demands far more: agents must infer unstated user preferences from behavioral history, acquire missing preferences through multi-turn dialogue, decide autonomously when to intervene, and respect user rejection. Existing personalization benchmarks either treat preferences as static context available to the agent (bypassing genuine inference) or evaluate offline trajectory matching rather than grounded GUI execution. No prior benchmark jointly tests interactive preference elicitation, full proactive decision chains (intervene vs. seek consent vs. remain silent), and verifiable live execution. This gap motivates KnowU-Bench. Methodology: KnowU-Bench is an online, reproducible benchmark built on a containerized, rooted Pixel 8 Android emulator (8 parallel AVDs) covering 23 apps, 192 tasks split into 42 general, 86 personalized, and 64 proactive tasks. The key design choice is an information asymmetry: the GUI agent receives only timestamped behavioral logs (time, location, action entries), while a structured hidden profile (identity, locations, habits, preferences, decision criteria, social graph) is accessible exclusively to an LLM-driven user simulator (GPT-4o). This forces genuine preference inference rather than context lookup. The GUI agent can issue `ask_user` actions to elicit clarifications; the simulator responds in-character based on the hidden profile. Proactive tasks omit explicit instructions entirely — the agent must choose among direct execution, confirmation-seeking, or silence given only current system state and logs. Evaluation uses a hybrid protocol: rule-based checks for verifiable states (order creation, alarm configuration, recipient correctness, post-rejection restraint) combined with LLM-as-a-Judge scoring on a task-specific rubric (preference alignment, trade-off quality, clarification quality, communication style). The final score is a convex combination Si = λi·Srule + (1−λi)·Sllm, with λi set per task proportional to the share of deterministic requirements. Memory access is evaluated under four conditions: full vs. RAG-retrieved logs × clean vs. noisy history. Key Results: Across 11 evaluated models (GUI-specific 8B models, general open-source up to 397B MoE, and closed-source frontier models), a clear difficulty progression emerges. On easy general tasks, MAI-UI-8B and Seed 2.0 Pro both reach 100% SR, confirming that explicit GUI execution is no longer the primary bottleneck. However, performance degrades sharply under personalization and proactivity: on hard personalized tasks, Claude Sonnet 4.6 achieves only 44.2% SR (best among all models), while all open-source models fall below 12%. Claude Sonnet 4.6 leads overall with 60.4% SR, followed by Seed 2.0 Pro at 51.6% and Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview at 44.3%. For proactive tasks, Claude Sonnet 4.6 achieves the best Act rate (70.8%) while Qwen3.5-397B-A17B leads on Silent (73.7%) and Qwen3.5-122B-A10B on Stop (83.3%), exposing a fundamental calibration tradeoff with no single model dominating all three dimensions. Error analysis on Claude Sonnet 4.6 failures reveals that 66.7% of personalized failures stem from insufficient clarification (Clarify errors) and 27.1% from partial preference satisfaction, while GUI errors account for only 4.2%. For proactive failures, 60.0% are unwarranted interventions and 20.0% are false passivity, together accounting for 80.0% — confirming intervention calibration as the bottleneck rather than downstream execution. The hybrid judge achieves MAE=0.035 against human expert mean ratings vs. MAE=0.120 for pure rule-based scoring. RAG log retrieval improves Qwen3-VL-8B (13.6%→20.4%) but hurts UI-Venus-1.5-8B, showing model-dependent optimal memory access strategies. Strengths: - The information asymmetry design (hidden profile / exposed logs) is the right formulation for realistic personalization evaluation; it prevents trivial context lookup and forces genuine behavioral inference, which prior benchmarks did not enforce. - The three-way proactive evaluation (Act / Silent / Stop) correctly decomposes intervention calibration into orthogonal failure modes and reveals the over-action bias in current models, a nuance that a single success rate would obscure. - The hybrid evaluation protocol achieves strong alignment with human judgment (MAE=0.035), substantially outperforming pure rule-based scoring, and is validated on 26 trajectories against four human experts. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark covers only four user profiles (Researcher, Developer, Student, Grandma), all synthetically constructed with LLM assistance; this limited persona diversity may not capture the long tail of real user variation and could introduce biases from the generating LLM. - Using GPT-4o as the user simulator introduces a dependency on a specific closed model whose own preference consistency and role-grounding fidelity are not formally validated; simulator quality directly caps the reliability of preference elicitation evaluation. - The task set (192 tasks, primarily Chinese-language apps like Taodian, Tuantuan, chilemei) has limited geographic and linguistic coverage, and the mock-backend apps may not fully capture the complexity and brittleness of production commercial applications.
Recommended 2604.08455 PDF
#69 — Innovative (69)

DP-OPD: Differentially Private On-Policy Distillation for Language Models

Fatemeh Khadem, Sajad Mousavi, Yi Fang, Yuhong Liu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: DP-OPD introduces a clean and well-motivated technical insight: using on-policy rollouts to directly address the exposure bias that DP noise exacerbates in autoregressive generation, while confining privacy accounting to the student alone. The pipeline simplification from a multi-stage 8×A100 setup to a single-GPU training loop is a genuine practical contribution. However, the building blocks (DP-SGD, GKD, on-policy training) are individually well-known, and the evaluation is narrow—perplexity-only on two datasets with a single GPT-2 teacher-student pair, leaving open whether the gains translate to downstream quality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying LLMs fine-tuned on sensitive domain-specific corpora requires reconciling formal differential privacy (DP) guarantees with efficient model compression. The dominant DP mechanism, DP-SGD, injects gradient noise that exacerbates exposure bias in autoregressive generation: the model is trained on teacher-forced (off-policy) prefixes but must condition on its own imperfect outputs at inference, causing compounding errors that worsen under DP noise. Existing private distillation approaches either apply DP-SGD to both teacher and student (dual-DP), worsening the privacy-utility tradeoff, or use synthesis-based pipelines (e.g., DISTILDP) that require privately training a large teacher, generating a synthetic corpus, and then distilling a student—a multi-stage, compute-heavy pipeline with its own distribution shift artifacts. Methodology: DP-OPD (Differentially Private On-Policy Distillation) is a synthesis-free framework that enforces privacy exclusively via DP-SGD on the student while using a frozen, non-private teacher for supervision. At each training step, with probability λ the student samples its own continuations (on-policy rollouts); with probability 1−λ, teacher-forced sequences are used. The teacher then scores the student's rollouts with dense token-level logits, and the student is updated using a Generalized Knowledge Distillation (GKD) loss parameterized by β ∈ [0,1] that interpolates between forward-KL (β=0), JSD (β=0.5), and reverse-KL (β=1) divergences. Per-example gradients are clipped and Gaussian noise is added via standard DP-SGD; privacy accounting (RDP) applies only to student updates. The teacher's inference is internal to the training pipeline and consumes no additional privacy budget. The setup mirrors DISTILDP's control-code conditioning (textual prefixes encoding class labels/metadata) for fair comparison, using GPT-2 Large (774M) as teacher and DistilGPT-2 (82M) as student—a ~9.5× compression ratio. Key Results: At ε=2.0, δ=1/N on Yelp (short-form reviews, 1.9M examples) and BigPatent (long-form patent abstracts, 200K examples), DP-OPD achieves test perplexity of 41.68 and 30.63 respectively. This outperforms: DP-SGD student-only (48.12 / 41.80), off-policy DPKD (46.16 / 41.20), and synthesis-based DISTILDP (44.15 / 32.43)—the strongest prior baseline. The gain over DISTILDP is notable because DISTILDP uses a large 8×A100 multi-GPU setup across multiple pipeline stages, while DP-OPD runs on a single RTX A6000 (40GB). Best configuration: λ=0.5 (half on-policy), β=0.5 (JSD-like); ablations show β=0 (forward-KL) achieves the best perplexity (30.63) when λ=1.0, with perplexity monotonically increasing as β→1. Strengths: - The core insight is well-motivated: on-policy rollouts directly address the train-test mismatch that DP noise exacerbates, and the theoretical framing (teacher targets states the student actually visits) is clean and principled. - Significant pipeline simplification: collapses three separate stages (DP teacher training, synthetic corpus generation, student training) into one DP student training loop, reducing compute from 8×A100 multi-day runs to a single 40GB GPU. - Outperforms DISTILDP on both short- and long-form domains while using strictly less compute and no DP teacher training, making the result credible rather than an artifact of additional resources. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is narrow: only perplexity is reported on two datasets with one teacher-student pair (GPT-2 family). No downstream task accuracy, generation quality metrics (MAUVE, diversity), or human evaluation—perplexity improvements of ~2-6 points may not translate to meaningfully better outputs in practice. - The comparison against DISTILDP relies on their reported numbers rather than a controlled reimplementation; hardware, hyperparameter tuning effort, and training duration differ substantially, making the compute comparison informal rather than FLOP-normalized. - On-policy rollout overhead is uncharacterized at scale: for longer continuations or larger student models, per-step wall-clock time could exceed that of synthesis-based pipelines that amortize teacher cost. The ablation on β is useful but there is no ablation on λ (on-policy mixing ratio) or rollout length L, which are equally critical hyperparameters.
Recommended 2604.04461 PDF
#70 — Innovative (69)

MegaTrain: Full Precision Training of 100B+ Parameter Large Language Models on a Single GPU (24 ▲ )

Zhengqing Yuan, Hanchi Sun, Lichao Sun, Yanfang Ye · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Apr 2026
Why this rank: MegaTrain presents a clean architectural inversion—treating CPU memory as the authoritative store and GPU as a transient compute cache—that is principled and well-engineered, with impressive throughput results across diverse hardware tiers. However, the core contribution is a systems-level optimization of memory offloading rather than a conceptual breakthrough; the individual techniques (double-buffered streaming, gradient checkpointing, CPU-side optimizer updates) are known, and the novelty lies in their careful composition and the stateless template binding mechanism.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Fine-tuning and post-training of large language models (100B+ parameters) requires loading full model parameters, gradients, and optimizer states into memory—demanding hundreds of gigabytes of GPU memory that most researchers lack. Existing offloading systems (ZeRO-Offload, ZeRO-Infinity, FSDP) treat GPU memory as the primary store and use CPU/NVMe only as spill buffers, leading to fragmented transfers, redundant staging, and near-exponential host memory growth that makes them unusable beyond ~30B parameters on practical hardware. The paper identifies a fundamental mismatch: post-training workloads are compute-light but memory-bound, yet the community lacks tools to exploit the abundant, cheap host memory sitting idle alongside scarce GPUs. Methodology: MegaTrain inverts the conventional GPU-centric paradigm by making host (CPU) memory the authoritative parameter store and treating the GPU as a transient compute cache. All model parameters, gradients, and FP32 Adam optimizer moments live in host DDR/LPDDR; the GPU holds only the active layer's weights at any moment. The system streams one layer at a time via three concurrent CUDA streams (compute, H2D weight transfer, D2H gradient offload) with double-buffered ping-pong staging: while the GPU executes layer i using Buffer 0, the CPU packs and DMA-transfers layer i+1 into Buffer 1. This overlapping hides PCIe latency behind compute. To avoid the mismatch with PyTorch's autograd (which assumes parameters persist on GPU throughout backward), MegaTrain introduces stateless layer templates—CUDA kernel wrappers with no persistent weight pointers—that are dynamically bound to streamed weights via a Bind primitive, completely eliminating the autograd graph and its stored metadata. Backward passes use block-wise gradient checkpointing: activations are checkpointed every K layers, and each block is recomputed forward before backpropagating, keeping activation memory bounded to one block regardless of depth. CPU-side Adam updates run with AVX-512 vector instructions, which the paper argues matches GPU Adam throughput while eliminating 4× the round-trip data movement. Memory layout is layer-contiguous (all states for one layer packed into a single aligned slab) to enable single-burst DMA transfers that saturate PCIe bandwidth (~26 GB/s on Gen4). Key Results: On a single H200 with 1.5 TB host memory, MegaTrain trains models up to 120B parameters (including a 120B MoE) where all baselines OOM. On GH200 (NVLink-C2C, 900 GB/s interconnect), it sustains 284 TFLOPS at 7B, 264 TFLOPS at 14B (1.84× over ZeRO-3 Offload), and >250 TFLOPS at 32B. In depth-scaling experiments with fixed GPU allocation (3.83 GB), MegaTrain degrades only 20% (284→227 TFLOPS) from 28 to 180 layers, while ZeRO-3 and FSDP collapse catastrophically and OOM at 84+ layers—MegaTrain is 5.93× faster at 84 layers. On a commodity A100 PCIe, speedups of 2.42× (7B), 8.13× (14B), and 12.20× (14B) over ColossalAI-Gemini and ZeRO-3 are reported. Consumer-grade RTX 3090 (24 GB) trains 14B models at 30 TFLOPS while ZeRO-3 OOMs at 14B. For long-context training, TFLOPS actually improves with context length (284→407 at 512K tokens) due to higher attention arithmetic intensity, with GPU memory remaining stable (~82 GB). Training accuracy on MetaMathQA matches PyTorch Native and ZeRO baselines within <0.1% at 7B and 14B, confirming no numerical drift. Strengths: - The architectural inversion—CPU as primary store, GPU as cache—is a clean, principled design that directly addresses the root cause (persistent GPU residency of cold parameters), not just a heuristic workaround. - Double-buffered triple-stream pipelining with stateless template binding effectively hides PCIe latency, achieving surprisingly high sustained TFLOPS even on PCIe-bottlenecked systems. - Demonstrated breadth across hardware tiers (GH200, H200, A100, A6000, RTX 3090) and model families (dense Qwen2.5 up to 72B, MoE GPT-OSS-120B) makes the practical utility clear. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The 120B training claim requires 1.5 TB host memory (an H200 SXM node), which is not commodity hardware; the democratization narrative is somewhat overstated given this requirement. - The Ratel comparison is dismissed in one paragraph (Appendix B) citing SSD bottlenecks in the official code without providing a fair controlled comparison—a notable gap given Ratel targets the same use case. - Evaluation uses only MetaMathQA (a narrow math benchmark) to validate correctness; there is no convergence comparison across diverse tasks or training steps to demonstrate that throughput parity translates to end-to-end training quality at scale.
Recommended 2604.05091 PDF
#71 — Innovative (68)

SNEAK: Evaluating Strategic Communication and Information Leakage in Large Language Models

Adar Avsian, Larry Heck · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: SNEAK defines a genuinely new evaluation axis — strategic information control under asymmetric knowledge — that is well-grounded in signaling game theory and fills a real gap in LLM benchmarking. The dual-metric framework (utility vs. leakage) is principled, and the 4× human advantage over LLMs is a striking empirical finding. The limitation to single-turn, structured-category tasks and the moderate human-LLM evaluator correlation on leakage (r=0.39) constrain how much this benchmark tells us about real-world strategic communication.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks evaluate reasoning, factual knowledge, and instruction following, but none directly measure whether models can control how much information they reveal when communicating under asymmetric information states. In multi-agent settings — tutoring, consulting, investor communication, collaborative AI — an agent must signal knowledge to an informed collaborator while remaining ambiguous to an uninformed adversary. This utility–leakage trade-off is well-studied in economics (Crawford & Sobel signaling games) and pragmatics (Gricean maxims, Rational Speech Acts) but has no dedicated LLM benchmark. SNEAK fills this gap. Methodology: SNEAK is inspired by the party game The Chameleon. Each benchmark instance consists of a semantic category, a candidate word set (12 prototypical members drawn from human category production norms across 117 categories), and a secret word. The message-generating model observes all three and must produce a ≤5-word natural language message. The message is then evaluated by two simulated agents: an ally (knows the secret, must identify which of 6 messages — 1 generated + 5 WordNet-derived decoys — corresponds to the secret) and a chameleon (does not know the secret, must infer it from the message and the candidate set). Both agents are implemented via constrained decoding over answer-label logits, averaged across three independent evaluator models (Mixtral-8x7B, Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct, Qwen-2.5-72B-Instruct) to reduce dependence on any single judge. Metrics are Utility and Leakage (each normalized relative to a random-guess baseline and clipped to [0,1]), a composite SoftScore = Utility × (1 − Leakage), and a stricter BinaryScore requiring the ally to rank the generated message first and the chameleon to not rank the secret first. The benchmark contains 1,394 instances. Three reference baselines (random word, category synonym, secret synonym) span the utility–leakage space. Three inference-time scaling methods are also tested: Chain-of-Thought (CoT), SETS (self-verification + self-correction), and a novel Recursive Message Refinement (RMR) that generates a message from the secret then generates a second message conditioned on the first to encourage indirection. Key Results: Modern LLMs achieve very high utility (58–99) but at the cost of high leakage (50–75), yielding SoftScores of only 18–35. Humans, by contrast, achieve utility 91.3, leakage 33.8, and SoftScore 59.2 — roughly 2–4× higher SoftScore than the best LLMs. Gemma-3-27B achieves the best SoftScore among LLMs (34.9), while DeepSeek-V3.2 achieves near-ceiling utility (98.5) but correspondingly high leakage (75.4, SoftScore 23.8). Inference-time scaling shows mixed results: CoT and SETS generally increase both utility and leakage together, leaving SoftScore largely unchanged. RMR is the most promising: for Gemma-3-27B, RMR reduces utility from 92.2 to 63.3 but also cuts leakage from 59.2 to 26.4, raising SoftScore from 34.9 to 40.8 and BinaryScore from 33.8 to 40.9 — the best single result in the benchmark. Abstract semantic categories are easier than concrete ones (SoftScore 28.4 vs. 25.2). Higher within-category semantic similarity strongly predicts lower utility (r = −0.43). Semantic analysis shows entity-based (intrinsic property) messages outperform taxonomic messages; temporal and action-based associations achieve strong performance with relatively low leakage. LLM evaluators agree with human plurality judgments on 88.9% of ally items and 66.7% of chameleon items, with evaluator-to-human probability correlations of r = 0.61 (ally) and r = 0.39 (chameleon). Evaluator Spearman rank correlations across the three judges range from 0.90 to 0.99, confirming robustness. Strengths: - The dual-metric framework (utility + leakage → SoftScore/BinaryScore) is principled, directly grounded in signaling game theory, and produces objective, reproducible scores without subjective LLM-as-judge ratings. - Thorough robustness analysis: three independent evaluators, same-model bias checks, decoy ablation (scores change <6 utility, <1 leakage under LLM-generated decoys), and sensitivity sweeps over candidate/decoy set sizes all confirm stability. - The human study (AMT, 117 instances, 5 annotators per item) provides a credible upper-bound reference and validates the LLM evaluator pipeline; the 4× human advantage over several LLMs is a striking, concrete finding. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The task is single-turn and one-shot: it does not capture multi-turn negotiation, deception, or refinement dynamics present in real asymmetric communication, limiting ecological validity. - Evaluation relies on LLM-based ally and chameleon agents whose correlation with human leakage judgments is only moderate (r = 0.39), meaning leakage scores may be noisy proxies for what a real adversary would infer. - The dataset is drawn entirely from structured semantic category norms, which favors short, concrete associative messages; the benchmark may not generalize to richer communicative contexts involving background knowledge, pragmatic inference over discourse, or domain-specific jargon.
Recommended 2603.29846 PDF
#72 — Innovative (68)

Token Warping Helps MLLMs Look from Nearby Viewpoints (23 ▲ )

Phillip Y. Lee, Chanho Park, Mingue Park, Seungwoo Yoo, Juil Koo, Minhyuk Sung · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 6 Apr 2026
Why this rank: The conceptual leap from pixel-space to token-space warping is creative and well-motivated by mental imagery theory, offering a training-free method applicable to any ViT-based MLLM. The systematic comparison of forward vs. backward warping in token space is methodologically clean. Limited by its dependence on depth maps and camera parameters at inference time, and the coarse token grid constrains fine-grained geometric fidelity.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) excel at visual reasoning but remain brittle under viewpoint changes. Existing workarounds rely on pixel-wise image warping — transforming the image before feeding it to the model — but this approach is highly sensitive to depth estimation errors and introduces geometric distortions that compound through the rendering pipeline. The core question is whether there is a more semantically stable substrate for viewpoint transformation, one that operates inside the model's own representation space rather than at the raw pixel level. Methodology: The paper proposes operating on image tokens produced by the ViT-based visual encoder of an MLLM rather than on pixels. The key insight, grounded in theories of mental imagery, is that part-level structural representations are the natural basis for perspective transformation in humans, and that image tokens in ViTs may approximate such representations. The method, called token warping, adapts classical warping geometry to the token grid: given a source image, its depth map, camera intrinsics, and a target camera pose, a dense grid is defined on the target token space and each grid point retrieves the corresponding source-view token via bilinear interpolation. The paper systematically compares forward warping (pushing source tokens to target positions) versus backward warping (pulling source tokens to fill target positions), finding backward warping significantly more stable because it avoids holes and preserves dense coverage of the token grid. Warped token sequences are then passed directly to the MLLM's language model in place of the normal visual tokens, requiring no fine-tuning of the base model. To evaluate the approach, the authors introduce ViewBench, a new benchmark testing viewpoint-aware visual question answering across multiple object categories and viewpoint offsets. Key Results: On ViewBench, backward token warping consistently outperforms all evaluated baselines: pixel-wise warping approaches (which suffer from depth-error sensitivity and hole artifacts), spatially fine-tuned MLLMs (which adapt the model but not its visual input), and a generative warping method (which introduces hallucinated content). The margin over pixel-warping is especially pronounced under larger viewpoint shifts, where depth errors accumulate. Qualitative results show that token warping preserves semantic coherence — object identity, part structure, and spatial layout — better than pixel warping, which can distort or occlude key features. The method is training-free with respect to the MLLM, making it directly applicable to any ViT-based MLLM without retraining. Strengths: - Training-free: plugs into any ViT-based MLLM without fine-tuning, making it broadly applicable and easy to deploy - Strong theoretical grounding: motivation from mental imagery theory connects human perspective transformation to token-level representations in a principled way - Rigorous comparison: evaluates forward vs. backward warping and compares against a diverse set of baselines including fine-tuned models and generative approaches Weaknesses / Limitations: - Requires a depth map and known camera intrinsics/extrinsics at inference time, which limits applicability in monocular or uncalibrated settings where these must be estimated and errors will propagate - Token grids are coarse spatial abstractions (e.g., 16×16 or 24×24 patches), so fine-grained geometric structure within each token is lost during warping, potentially degrading performance for detail-sensitive queries or large viewpoint offsets - ViewBench is a new, self-constructed benchmark whose difficulty calibration and coverage of real-world diversity have not yet been validated against established 3D scene understanding benchmarks
Recommended 2604.02870 PDF
#73 — Innovative (67)

Learning to Hint for Reinforcement Learning

Yu Xia, Canwen Xu, Zhewei Yao, Julian McAuley, Yuxiong He · arxiv.org · Julian McAuley - new articles
Why this rank: Scores 67 for the creative idea of co-training an adaptive hinter alongside the reasoner to address GRPO's advantage collapse on hard problems. The hint reliance metric and transfer bound give the framework principled grounding beyond heuristic scaffolding. However, the requirement for reference solutions at training time limits applicability, and the 2.6-3.8x computational overhead relative to accuracy gains raises cost-effectiveness questions.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), the dominant RL algorithm for training LLM reasoning with verifiable rewards, suffers from "advantage collapse": when all rollouts in a group receive the same reward (all correct or all incorrect), relative advantages vanish and no gradient update occurs. Hard questions — precisely those most important for expanding the model's capability — are most likely to produce all-incorrect groups and thus yield no learning signal. Prior work adds static hints or scaffolds to hard questions to induce mixed-outcome groups, but these hints are fixed rather than adapted to the model's current failure modes, and a hint that creates a GRPO signal does not necessarily improve the no-hint policy used at test time. Methodology: The paper proposes HiLL (Hint Learning for Reinforcement Learning), a co-training framework that jointly trains a hinter policy alongside the reasoner via GRPO. When a question produces an all-incorrect group, the hinter generates M candidate hints conditioned on the question, a sampled incorrect rollout from the current reasoner (exposing its specific failure mode), and the reference solution (available only during training). For each candidate hint, the reasoner re-samples G rollouts under the hinted input, and hints are scored along two axes. First, signal creation measures the non-degenerate probability s(p_hat; G) = 1 - p_hat^G - (1-p_hat)^G, which peaks at p_hat = 0.5 and vanishes for all-correct or all-incorrect groups. Second, signal transfer is quantified via "hint reliance" — the average log-probability ratio of correct hinted trajectories under the hinted vs. original input. A formal transfer bound (Proposition 1) shows that lower hint reliance implies a tighter lower bound on no-hint success probability: p >= p_h * exp(-rho_c). The hinter reward multiplies signal creation by exp(-rho_c / T), penalizing hints whose induced correct trajectories are unlikely under the original question. The best-scoring hint replaces the degenerate group for the reasoner update, while all M candidate hints form a GRPO group for the hinter update. Both policies are updated jointly each step using standard GRPO objectives. Key Results: Evaluated on Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct across six math benchmarks (AIME24/25, AMC23, MATH-500, Minerva, OlympiadBench) and two non-math benchmarks (GPQA, MMLU-Pro). On Llama-3B, HiLL achieves an in-distribution average accuracy of 24.6 vs. 21.9 for GRPO and 23.9 for the best prior hint baseline (SAGE), and 35.3 out-of-distribution vs. 33.1 for GRPO and 34.0 for SAGE. On Qwen2.5-7B, HiLL reaches 44.2 in-distribution average vs. 41.1 for GRPO and 42.3 for SAGE, and 51.0 out-of-distribution vs. 47.4 for GRPO. Ablation shows that removing the transfer weight (HiLL w/o TW) causes hint reliance to climb steadily during training, and accuracy drops consistently vs. full HiLL, confirming the transfer weight is load-bearing. Transfer temperature T=0.3 yields the best accuracy balance; all T values outperform the no-transfer-weight variant. HiLL with transfer weighting produces shorter, more conceptual hints (avg. 29 words vs. 79 for HiLL w/o TW on Llama) with fewer math expressions, emerging naturally from the reliance penalty. Computational overhead is approximately 3.8x GRPO wall-clock per step for Llama-3B (high degenerate-group rate) and 2.6x for Qwen-7B. Strengths: - The hint reliance metric and accompanying transfer bound (Proposition 1) are theoretically grounded via a clean KL-divergence decomposition, giving the hinter reward a principled justification rather than being heuristic. - The online, failure-conditioned hint generation adapts to the reasoner's evolving capability frontier — as the reasoner improves, previously hard questions leave the intervention set and hints are generated for the current capability boundary, a dynamic that static hinting cannot replicate. - The emergent behavioral difference between HiLL and HiLL w/o TW (shorter, conceptual hints vs. step-by-step algebraic scaffolds) provides interpretable qualitative evidence that the transfer penalty shapes hint style in the intended direction, not just accuracy metrics. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The framework requires a reference solution at training time (used as hinter input), which restricts applicability to domains with ground-truth solutions and excludes open-ended or subjective reasoning tasks. - Computational overhead is substantial (2.6-3.8x GRPO), and the hinter is a separate model (Qwen3-4B) that must be co-located and co-trained, adding engineering complexity and memory cost that is not fully analyzed against the accuracy gains per FLOP. - Experiments are limited to mathematical reasoning; generalization claims to GPQA/MMLU-Pro are tested only at evaluation time on a model trained entirely on math data, so it remains unclear whether HiLL's advantages persist when applied directly to non-math RLVR settings.
Recommended 2604.00698 PDF
#74 — Innovative (67)

DMax: Aggressive Parallel Decoding for dLLMs (28 ▲ )

Zigeng Chen, Gongfan Fang, Xinyin Ma, Ruonan Yu, Xinchao Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Apr 2026
Why this rank: DMax precisely identifies the root cause of parallel decoding failure in masked diffusion LLMs — irrevocable token commitment — and proposes a clean two-part fix combining on-policy self-distillation with soft hybrid embeddings. The finding that self-correction also improves accuracy at conservative parallelism settings is a nice bonus. Limited to a single base model and task-specific variants rather than a unified model.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Diffusion language models (dLLMs) promise inference efficiency through parallel decoding, but in practice their performance degrades sharply under aggressive parallelism. The root cause is error accumulation: masked diffusion LLMs (MDLMs) use a binary, one-way mask-to-token transition — once a masked position is decoded into a token, that prediction is irrevocably committed as context for all future steps. Erroneous early predictions thus propagate and cascade, causing semantic collapse. Prior work addresses this via improved decoding strategies or distillation but never targets this fundamental architectural limitation. The paper identifies that uniform diffusion LLMs (UDLMs), which denoise from arbitrary vocabulary tokens, can in principle self-correct, but suffer from instability because training uses uniformly sampled noise that diverges sharply from the model's own inference-time prediction distribution. Methodology: DMax introduces two tightly coupled components. First, On-Policy Uniform Training (OPUT) converts a pretrained MDLM into a self-corrective model by constructing training inputs on-policy: at each training iteration, the model first predicts tokens for all masked positions (a no-gradient rollout), producing a noisy sequence drawn from the model's own output distribution rather than from a uniform vocabulary. Two forward passes are then performed — one on the original masked input and one on the on-policy predicted sequence — with cross-entropy loss applied at all positions for both, jointly training mask denoising and error-correction capability. The final loss is L_on-policy = L_mask + L_pred. Second, Soft Parallel Decoding (SPD) operates at inference using block-wise semi-autoregressive decoding with block size 32. Instead of committing decoded tokens discretely, each intermediate token position is represented as a hybrid embedding: a convex combination of the predicted token embedding and the mask embedding, weighted by the model's confidence at that position (and its complement). The hybrid embedding is renormalized to preserve magnitude. A contiguous-prefix promotion rule ensures that low-confidence tokens on the right cannot corrupt mask predictions on the left. Blocks converge when either predictions are stable across two steps or all positions exceed a confidence threshold τ_acc = 0.9. Critically, SPD requires OPUT-trained models; applying it to a vanilla MDLM causes catastrophic collapse because the interpolation between mask and token embeddings is only meaningful when the model has been trained to recover from both. Key Results: Built on LLaDA-2.0-mini, DMax achieves average tokens-per-forward (TPF) of 6.2 vs. 2.8 for the base model across six benchmarks, while preserving near-identical accuracy. On GSM8K, TPF improves from 2.04 to 5.48 with accuracy dropping only 0.5% (92.6% → 92.1%). On MATH500, TPF improves from 2.58 to 5.94 with a 0.4% accuracy drop (75.8% → 75.4%). On HumanEval-Instruct, TPF goes from 4.38 to 7.36 with a −0.7% accuracy change. DMax consistently dominates baselines (Hierarchical Decoding, dParallel SFT) on AUP Score by large margins. Conventional Uniform Diffusion Training — the naive alternative — fails entirely, degrading accuracy to 15–69% range across tasks due to train-inference mismatch. On two H200 GPUs, DMax achieves 1,338 tokens/second at batch size 1. At low parallelism (conservative thresholds), DMax also improves accuracy by 0.8–3.0% over the base model, suggesting that self-revision benefits quality even beyond throughput gains. Training data is generated entirely via self-distillation from LLaDA-2.0-mini with no external supervision. Strengths: - Precisely diagnoses the root cause of parallel decoding failure in MDLMs (one-way irrevocable commitment) and proposes a principled fix that addresses train-inference mismatch directly. - On-policy rollout for constructing training data is a simple, elegant idea that bridges the distribution gap without requiring external data or a separate teacher model — the 1.7M training samples are all self-generated. - Soft embeddings as uncertainty carriers during decoding are a clean mechanism; the ablation rigorously confirms that each component (on-policy rollout, contiguous-prefix rule, hybrid embedding) contributes independently, with OPUT being prerequisite to SPD. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are restricted to a single base model (LLaDA-2.0-mini); generalizability to other MDLM architectures or scales is unverified. - The paper trains separate DMax-Math and DMax-Coder models rather than a single unified model, raising questions about how the approach scales to general-purpose instruction following across diverse task distributions. - The AUP Score metric used for parallel decoding comparison is not yet widely adopted, and throughput numbers (TPS) depend heavily on specific hardware (H200) and framework (dInFer with tensor parallelism), limiting direct comparability to other reported results.
Recommended 2604.08302 PDF
#75 — Innovative (67)

Read More, Think More: Revisiting Observation Reduction for Web Agents

Masafumi Enomoto, Ryoma Obara, Haochen Zhang, Masafumi Oyamada · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This paper delivers a genuinely surprising and practically actionable finding: observation reduction—a near-universal design choice in web agents—actually hurts performance for capable models with sufficient thinking budget. The mechanistic error analysis (CSS z-index exploitation vs. ID hallucination) elevates this beyond a simple benchmark comparison. However, the study is confined to a single benchmark domain (WorkArena L1) and the key CSS layout hypothesis lacks ablation confirmation, limiting the strength of the causal claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-based web agents rely on web page observations (typically HTML) to plan and execute actions. A longstanding assumption in the field is that verbose HTML hurts performance, leading to near-universal adoption of observation reduction—converting pages to compact accessibility trees (a11y) or filtering to task-relevant elements. This paper challenges that assumption: with modern LLMs that have large context windows and extended chain-of-thought reasoning budgets, is observation reduction still beneficial? The gap being filled is the absence of a systematic study of how model capability and inference-time thinking tokens interact with observation representation choice. Methodology: The authors conduct controlled experiments on WorkArena L1 (330 tasks across 33 task types on ServiceNow), varying two orthogonal factors: (1) observation representation—accessibility tree (a11y, ~6.7K tokens), raw HTML (~57K tokens), or a11y augmented with screenshots; and (2) observation history length—no history, past 4 steps (hist4), past 9 steps (hist9), with full or character-level diff variants. These are crossed with a diverse model suite: higher-capability proprietary models (Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.1, Gemini 2.5 Flash, o3-mini) and lower-capability open-source models (GPT-OSS-120b/20b, Qwen3-VL, Llama-3.1), with varying reasoning effort / thinking budget parameters. Grounding errors are categorized by type (not-found, not-visible, intercepted, etc.) using Playwright execution exceptions to diagnose mechanistic reasons behind performance differences. Key Results: The central finding is that observation reduction is not universally beneficial. For higher-capability proprietary models, raw HTML consistently outperforms a11y—by up to +17.5 percentage points for GPT-5.1 (high) and +14.6 points for Claude Sonnet 4.6. Increasing thinking tokens amplifies this gap: GPT-5.1 high vs. low effort shows +17.5 vs. +8.8 from HTML; Gemini 2.5 Flash at budget=16384 vs. 128 shows +11.2 vs. +6.0. In contrast, lower-capability open-source models degrade with HTML: GPT-OSS-20b drops -18.8 points, Llama-3.1-70B drops -14.6 points. This pattern is robust to formatting choices (XML vs. indented), ruling out format artifacts as an explanation. Error analysis explains the mechanism: stronger models reduce "intercepted" click errors by exploiting CSS z-index layout cues in HTML, while weaker models show large increases in "not-found" errors (hallucinated DOM element IDs) under longer inputs. Observation history consistently improves performance across nearly all models (+2–11 points), and diff-based history achieves comparable gains to full history at ~1/3 the token cost (13.7K vs. 39K tokens for hist9). Strengths: - Clean, well-controlled experimental design that systematically decouples model capability, thinking budget, observation format, and history—producing actionable, non-obvious insights - Grounding error analysis provides genuine mechanistic evidence (CSS z-index exploitation vs. hallucination) rather than just correlational performance numbers - Diff-based history is a practically useful contribution: matching full-history performance at 1/3 the token cost Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are confined to WorkArena L1 (ServiceNow platform), a narrow, single-domain benchmark; generalization to other websites, domains, or grounding schemes (coordinate-based) is untested - The CSS layout cue hypothesis for HTML benefit is not verified through ablation—it remains plausible but unconfirmed, and other contributing factors are unexamined - History experiments fix observation representation to a11y, leaving the potentially important combination of HTML observations with history unexplored; the interaction between richer observations and longer task horizons is an open question
Citations 2604.01535 PDF
#76 — Innovative (67)

GBQA: A Game Benchmark for Evaluating LLMs as Quality Assurance Engineers (37 ▲ )

Shufan Jiang, Chios Chen, Zhiyang Chen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Apr 2026
Why this rank: GBQA addresses a genuinely understudied and important problem — autonomous bug discovery rather than bug repair — that is orthogonal to existing benchmarks like SWE-Bench. The benchmark construction is rigorous with strong inter-annotator agreement, and the finding that reasoning capability outweighs raw scale is actionable. However, the benchmark is small (30 LLM-generated games, 124 bugs), raising ecological validity concerns, and the workshop-paper scope limits depth of analysis.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: While LLMs have achieved strong performance on code generation and bug-fixing benchmarks like SWE-Bench, the complementary task of autonomous bug discovery remains largely unstudied. Existing benchmarks assume bugs are already identified and described by humans; agents are only asked to localize and patch. Bug discovery is fundamentally harder: the objective is ill-defined (no explicit bug description is given), it requires long-horizon exploration of a dynamic state space, and it demands reasoning about gaps between expected and actual runtime behavior. This paper addresses this gap by introducing GBQA, a benchmark targeting the quality assurance phase of the software development cycle. Methodology: GBQA consists of 30 interactive game environments (spanning Action, Adventure, RPG, Strategy, Simulation, and Puzzle genres) with 124 human-verified bugs categorized into three difficulty levels: Easy (surface-level single-observation bugs, 72 total), Medium (short-horizon rule violations, 31 total), and Hard (long-horizon consistency bugs requiring state tracking across many steps, 21 total). Games are constructed using a hierarchical multi-agent builder that simulates a professional game studio, with a Producer Agent coordinating Design, Programming, and Art teams; each team uses dynamically instantiated worker agents and a shared skill platform. An iterative complexity-scaling mechanism ensures environments are non-trivial. Ground truth was curated by three professional QA engineers using majority voting (Krippendorff's α = 0.901). For evaluation, a baseline QA agent uses a ReAct loop augmented with step-level reflection and a verification phase before filing bug reports, combined with a hierarchical memory module comprising in-session sliding-window summarization and cross-session persistent memory. Evaluation uses recall as the primary metric, computed by a Critic Agent (validated against human judgments, Pearson ρ = 0.903 for GPT-5.2). Two modes are evaluated: Player Exploring Mode (no source code provided) and Quality Assurance Mode (design specs and source code provided). Step budgets T ∈ {50, 100, 200, 500} are swept for each model. Key Results: Autonomous bug discovery is substantially harder than code repair. The best model, Claude-4.6-Opus in thinking mode with QA Mode at 500 steps, achieves only 48.39% recall—compared to 81.4% on SWE-Bench Verified for the same model. Instruct-mode Claude-4.6-Opus peaks at 37.90%, while smaller open models like Llama-3.1-8B reach only 8.87%. A key finding is that reasoning capability (inference-time compute) is more parameter-efficient than raw scale: Qwen3-32B-Thinking (33.87%) substantially outperforms Qwen3-235B-A22B in instruct mode (18.55%).
