QA Mode consistently outperforms Player Exploring Mode across all models and budgets, but even with full source code access, performance remains far below human-level QA. Ablations show that Hard bugs exhibit no saturation at 500 steps, while Easy bugs plateau around 300 steps. Full hierarchical memory (IS+CS) consistently dominates ablated memory configurations across all sessions and step budgets. A closed-loop case study on the CASTLE environment demonstrates 100% discovery and fixing rates across three autonomous sessions using Claude-4.6-Opus-Thinking paired with Claude Code for patching. Strengths: - Addresses a well-motivated and genuinely understudied problem—autonomous bug discovery in dynamic runtime environments—that is orthogonal to existing code-repair benchmarks. - Rigorous benchmark construction: three professional QA annotators, strong inter-annotator agreement (α = 0.901), automated critic validation against human judgments (ρ ≥ 0.807), and a scalable multi-agent game builder enabling future expansion. - Comprehensive experimental analysis covering 20+ frontier models, two evaluation modes, four step budgets, and ablations over memory components, providing actionable insights into failure modes (hallucination, state-tracking limitations, lack of QA-specific exploration heuristics). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is limited to 30 games and 124 bugs, all text/web-based with structured API interfaces; whether findings generalize to GUIs, real codebases, or multimodal software environments is unaddressed. - The benchmark is published as a workshop paper, and the game environments are LLM-generated rather than drawn from real-world software projects, raising questions about ecological validity and whether the injected bugs reflect the distribution of defects in production systems. - The baseline agent architecture and evaluation protocol are fixed; precision (false positive rate) is not reported as a primary metric, which could mask models that flood the system with spurious reports to maximize recall.
Recommended 2604.02648 PDF
#77 — Innovative (67)

A1: A Fully Transparent Open-Source, Adaptive and Efficient Truncated Vision-Language-Action Model

Kaidong Zhang, Jian Zhang, Rongtao Xu, Yu Sun, Shuoshuo Xue, Youpeng Wen, Xiaoyu Guo, Minghao Guo, Weijia Liufu, Liu Zihou, Kangyi Ji, Yangsong Zhang, Jiarun Zhu, Jingzhi Liu, Zihang Li, Ruiyi Chen, Meng Cao, Jingming Zhang, Shen Zhao, Xiaojun Chang, Feng Zheng, Ivan Laptev, Xiaodan Liang · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: A1's main novelty lies in the joint acceleration of VLM backbone and flow-matching action head via Inter-Layer Truncated Flow Matching with warm-start initialization across layers — an elegant idea that addresses a real bottleneck prior early-exit methods merely shifted. The full open-source release across multiple embodiments is valuable, but the core approach builds heavily on established VLA architectures and imitation learning paradigms.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for robot manipulation face a severe deployment cost problem: billion-parameter VLM backbones combined with iterative diffusion/flow-matching action heads (typically 10–20 denoising steps) make real-time control expensive on commodity hardware. Prior efficiency work targets either the VLM backbone (quantization, sparsity, early-exit) or the action head, but not both jointly, meaning that reducing backbone cost merely shifts the bottleneck to the action head. There is also a gap in fully open, reproducible VLA systems — most state-of-the-art models rely on proprietary data or closed training stacks. Methodology: A1 is built on three empirical observations: (1) flow-matching trajectories converge to the correct mode within ~3 denoising steps; (2) actions change smoothly across consecutive control steps; and (3) intermediate VLM hidden states already encode sufficient spatial features to seed action prediction. These motivate a budget-aware adaptive inference scheme that jointly accelerates both components. The VLM backbone (Molmo-7B / Qwen2-7B, 28 layers) is trained with multi-exit supervision: at each layer i, a single shared action head (either a 400M-parameter flow-matching Qwen3 expert or a lightweight MLP) is applied and supervised simultaneously. At inference, an action-consistency test compares consecutive layers' predicted action chunks; early exit is triggered when discrepancy falls below a layer-specific threshold η_i calibrated from training data using an exponential exit-probability distribution parameterized by criterion c. The key novelty for the flow-matching variant is Inter-Layer Truncated Flow Matching: instead of running full δ=10 denoising steps from random noise at each layer, only δ=2 steps are run, and the output of layer i is used as warm-start initialization for layer i+1, propagating the denoising trajectory across layers rather than restarting it. Training uses a two-stage pipeline (large-scale pretraining on DROID, AgiBot, RoboCOIN, RoboMind, GM-100, RoboChallenge plus 15,951 in-house trajectories, followed by task-specific finetuning) with hierarchical dataset-level and embodiment-level balanced sampling. Key Results: On LIBERO simulation, A1 achieves 96.6% average success rate, competitive with π0.5 (96.9%) and above π0 (94.2%). On VLABench, A1 achieves 53.5% vs. π0.5's 49.5% and π0's 42%. In zero-shot transfer to LIBERO-Plus (distribution shift in objects, textures, instructions), A1-FM achieves 75.3%, outperforming π0 (53.6%) and π0-FAST (61.6%). On the real-robot RoboChallenge Table30 benchmark (30 tasks, multiple embodiments), A1 achieves 29.00% mean success as the top fully open-source system, surpassing π0 (28.33%), X-VLA (21.33%), and RDT-1B (15.00%), ranking 6th overall including closed-source systems. In multi-platform real-world evaluation across Franka, AgiBot, OpenArm, and Dobot-Arm, A1 achieves 56.7% mean success vs. π0.5 (47.5%) and π0 (40.8%). On inference efficiency: with exit criterion c=0.1, backbone computation is reduced by 76.6% with only 1.7% success rate drop (92.3% vs. 94.0% baseline). The Inter-Layer Truncated Flow Matching with warm-start reduces per-episode latency from 37.8s to 10.5s (72.3% reduction) while maintaining 96.4% accuracy on LIBERO. Strengths: - The joint acceleration of both VLM backbone and flow-matching action head is technically novel and practically significant — prior early-exit work shifted cost rather than eliminating it; the warm-start denoising across layers elegantly addresses this. - Strong empirical validation across simulation (LIBERO, VLABench), four real-robot platforms, and a competitive multi-embodiment benchmark (RoboChallenge), with ablations isolating contributions of each component. - Full open-source release (weights, training code, data pipelines, evaluation scripts) is a genuine contribution to reproducibility in a field where most top performers rely on closed infrastructure. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The multi-exit training requires running the full training set an additional time to calibrate per-layer thresholds η_i, and the early-exit consistency test adds overhead at inference time (when early exit reaches the final layer, A1-FM with δ=10 actually increases wall-clock time from 37.8s to 40.9s due to the extra per-layer action head calls — requiring the truncated δ=2 variant to realize actual speedups). - The gap between A1 and top closed-source systems (DM0: 62%, Spirit-v1.5: 51%, GigaBrain: 51.67% on RoboChallenge) is substantial, suggesting that proprietary data scale and training infrastructure remain critical advantages not overcome by algorithmic efficiency alone. - Reliance on imitation learning accumulates compounding errors in long-horizon tasks; the paper acknowledges this but offers no solution, and the LIBERO-Long subtask (58.0% for A1-FM zero-shot) shows this is a real weakness.
Search Results 2604.05672 PDF
#78 — Innovative (67)

MegaStyle: Constructing Diverse and Scalable Style Dataset via Consistent Text-to-Image Style Mapping (79 ▲ )

Junyao Gao, Sibo Liu, Jiaxing Li, Yanan Sun, Yuanpeng Tu, Fei Shen, Weidong Zhang, Cairong Zhao, Jun Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Apr 2026
Why this rank: MegaStyle's core insight — exploiting T2I models' consistent text-to-style mapping to break the circular dependency on imperfect style transfer models for training data — is conceptually clean and well-validated through ablations showing the dataset is the primary performance driver. The scale (170K fine-grained styles, 1.4M images) substantially exceeds prior work. However, the encoder and transfer model architectures rely on standard techniques (contrastive learning, LoRA fine-tuning of FLUX), and the self-referential evaluation loop where MegaStyle-Encoder scores the outputs of MegaStyle-FLUX limits the independence of the results.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Image style transfer requires paired training data — images sharing the same style but differing in content — yet collecting such pairs is extremely difficult. Real-world artwork collections (WikiArt) suffer from intra-style inconsistency because even a single artist's works vary noticeably in color, texture, and brushwork. Prior synthetic datasets (IMAGStyle, OmniStyle-150K) generate style pairs by running existing style transfer models on reference images, but this bootstraps from flawed methods: the generated pairs inherit the artifacts, limited color diversity, and style inconsistencies of those methods, capping the quality and diversity of any downstream model trained on them. A reliable style similarity metric is also lacking, further hindering automated dataset scaling. Methodology: MegaStyle proposes a three-stage data curation pipeline that sidesteps the circular dependency on imperfect style transfer models. The key insight is that modern text-to-image models (Qwen-Image) establish a consistent mapping from a style description to a visual style: given the same textual style prompt, the model reliably produces images with coherent style across arbitrary content. Stage 1 builds content (2M LAION-Aesthetics images) and style (1M JourneyDB + 80K WikiArt + 1M LAION-Aesthetics) image pools. Stage 2 uses Qwen3-VL-30B to caption style prompts along six fine-grained axes (overall artistic style, color composition, light distribution, artistic medium, texture, brushwork) and content prompts describing only objects and relationships. Exact, fuzzy, and semantic deduplication plus hierarchical k-means balance sampling (four levels: 50K→10K→5K→1K clusters) distills 2M raw prompts into 170K style + 400K content prompts spanning 8,355 distinct overall styles. Stage 3 generates 1.4M images (N=8 content images per style prompt) via Qwen-Image, forming the MegaStyle-1.4M dataset. Using this dataset, the authors train MegaStyle-Encoder via style-supervised contrastive learning (SSCL): a supervised contrastive loss over 170K fine-grained style labels at batch size 8,192, augmented by a SigLIP image–text contrastive regularizer to prevent collapse. MegaStyle-FLUX fine-tunes FLUX (MM-DiT backbone) by concatenating VAE-encoded reference style tokens with noisy target tokens, using shifted RoPE on reference tokens to prevent positional collision and content leakage, for 30K steps with LoRA rank 128. Key Results: On the custom StyleRetrieval benchmark (2,400 unseen fine-grained styles, 32 images each), MegaStyle-Encoder (SoViT backbone) achieves mAP@1=88.46 and Recall@1=88.46, compared to SigLIP at 10.43 and CSD at 45.60 — roughly doubling CSD's performance. On out-of-distribution benchmarks (StyleBench with 275 real artworks, FLUX-Retrieval with 76,800 FLUX-generated images, OmniStyle-150K), MegaStyle-Encoder consistently outperforms CLIP and CSD across all metrics. For style transfer on StyleBench (50 real-world artworks, 20 text prompts), MegaStyle-FLUX achieves Style=76.16 (second only to Attention-Distillation's 85.59, which achieves this by copying the reference image and consequently scores lowest on text alignment at 20.29), Text=23.20 (highest among all methods), Human Style=31.37 (highest, vs. second-best StyleShot at 15.21), and Human Text=28.72 (highest, vs. second-best CSGO at 16.18). Ablation replacing MegaStyle-1.4M with OmniStyle-150K drops Style from 76.16 to 51.49; replacing with JourneyDB drops it to 34.56, confirming the dataset is the primary driver of performance. Strengths: - The core insight — use T2I models' consistent style mapping to generate training pairs rather than imperfect style transfer models — is conceptually clean and breaks a circular dependency that has plagued prior work; the idea is well-validated by comparative ablations across three datasets. - The scale and diversity of MegaStyle-1.4M (170K fine-grained styles, 8,355 overall style descriptors) substantially exceeds all prior datasets (OmniStyle-150K covers only 1K styles), and each component of the pipeline is independently scalable. - The human preference study (30+ volunteers, 20 tasks) reinforces the automated metrics with consistent conclusions; MegaStyle-FLUX wins decisively on both style and text alignment in human judgment. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation benchmark StyleRetrieval is constructed using the same generative model (Qwen-Image) used to produce the training data, introducing source-model bias; while the authors present additional benchmarks to partially address this, the primary benchmark cannot be considered fully independent. - The method inherits biases from Qwen-Image's training: the paper acknowledges association bias (e.g., "Japanese painting" systematically produces Edo/Meiji-era imagery) and reduced coverage of uncommon styles, but no quantitative analysis of how much this skews the style distribution is provided. - Style similarity scores are measured using MegaStyle-Encoder itself, creating a self-referential evaluation loop for the transfer model; using the encoder to score outputs of a model trained on the encoder's training distribution conflates quality with in-distribution recall.
Recommended 2604.08364 PDF
#79 — Innovative (67)

Crystalite: A Lightweight Transformer for Efficient Crystal Modeling

Tin Hadži Veljković, Joshua Rosenthal, Ivor Lončarić, Jan-Willem van de Meent · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Demonstrates that a standard Transformer with lightweight geometry-aware attention biases can match or exceed equivariant GNNs on crystal structure prediction and generation at 70x lower sampling cost. The subatomic tokenization providing a chemically meaningful continuous diffusion space and the intensive/extensive metric taxonomy are genuine contributions. However, the work is primarily a well-executed architectural simplification rather than a conceptual departure, and the lack of space-group symmetry enforcement limits its reach for complex crystallographic tasks.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Generative models for crystalline materials have largely relied on equivariant graph neural networks (GNNs) that capture geometric and symmetry structure well but are architecturally complex, computationally expensive, and slow to sample. The central question is whether a simpler Transformer backbone can remain competitive on crystal structure prediction (CSP) and de novo generation (DNG) without full equivariant message passing, provided that periodic geometry and chemical structure are incorporated appropriately. Methodology: Crystalite is a diffusion Transformer built on two core inductive biases injected into an otherwise standard EDM-style denoising framework. The first is Subatomic Tokenization: instead of high-dimensional one-hot atom identity vectors (e.g., 89-dimensional for MP-20), each element is assigned a fixed 34-dimensional descriptor encoding period, group, block, and ground-state valence-shell occupancies. These are standardized, group-rebalanced, PCA-compressed to 16 dimensions, and ℓ₂-normalized, yielding a continuous token space where chemically similar elements are geometrically proximate. During DNG, atom types are treated as continuous diffusion variables; at sampling time, discrete elements are recovered by nearest-prototype cosine-similarity decoding. The second is the Geometry Enhancement Module (GEM): at each denoising step, GEM computes pairwise minimum-image geometry under periodic boundary conditions using the lattice metric G = LL⊤, producing two additive head-wise attention biases — a distance penalty (Bdist) with a learned monotonically non-positive slope, and an edge-aware bias (Bedge) from an MLP acting on Fourier features of fractional displacements and RBF-encoded distances. These are gated by a learned noise-dependent scalar and injected additively into the attention logits, modulating atom-atom attention scores without altering the canonical QKᵀ formulation. The diffusion state (H, F, y) covers atom tokens, fractional coordinates, and a 6-dimensional lower-triangular lattice parameterization (y). Training uses separate EDM-style denoising losses for each channel with torus-aware wrapped fractional residuals for coordinates. An optional channel-wise anti-annealing heuristic during sampling rescales the reverse-time update independently per channel to improve geometric refinement without retraining. The backbone is a 14-layer Transformer with width 512 and 16 heads (~67M parameters), trained in bfloat16 with AdaLN noise conditioning. Key Results: On CSP across three benchmarks, Crystalite achieves state-of-the-art results on both match rate (MR) and RMSE. On MP-20: MR 66.05% vs. 65.83% (KLDM, prior best) and RMSE 0.0329 vs. 0.0517 (KLDM) — a ~36% RMSE reduction. On MPTS-52: MR 31.49% (vs. 25.15% OMatG) and RMSE 0.0701 (vs. 0.1276 KLDM). On Alex-MP-20: MR 67.52% and RMSE 0.0335 vs. 64.71%/0.1251 (OMatG). Ablations show GEM has limited impact on MR but reduces RMSE by ~20% consistently. On DNG (MP-20, 10,000 crystals), Crystalite achieves the highest SUN score (48.55%) among compared baselines — combining stability (70.97%), uniqueness (95.33%), and novelty (79.15%) — while sampling at 22.36s/1k crystals (5.14s† with FlashAttention), versus MatterGen at 2,639s/1k and DiffCSP at 237s/1k. Crystalite achieves the best wdist-ρ (0.046) for density distribution matching. On the LeMat-GenBench leaderboard (pre-relaxed category), Crystalite leads with valid 97.20%, MSUN 22.60%, outperforming MatterGen (15.00%) and OMatG (18.00%). At scale (10⁶ samples), Crystalite maintains higher uniqueness and UN rate than ADiT. Strengths: - The GEM design is modular and computationally cheap — it adds periodic geometry to attention biases rather than requiring equivariant message passing, yielding ~70× speedup over MatterGen with better CSP accuracy and SUN. - Subatomic Tokenization provides a chemically meaningful continuous diffusion space that reduces memorization of frequent compositional patterns and aligns training and decoding geometries through normalized cosine-similarity decoding. - The paper performs thorough and fair evaluation: baselines are re-evaluated under a uniform NequIP-based relaxation pipeline, and results are cross-validated on the independent LeMat-GenBench and MatterGen evaluation pipelines. - The intensive/extensive metric taxonomy is a genuine methodological contribution, clarifying that uniqueness-based metrics (UN, SUN) are budget-dependent and cannot be compared across different generation budgets. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The model does not enforce or leverage space group symmetry at generation time, leaving structural diversity and symmetry-completeness as open questions compared to methods like SymmCD or WyFormer. - Despite best SUN score, Crystalite's novelty (79.15%) and uniqueness (95.33%) are lower than several baselines (e.g., MatterGen 91.14%/98.10%), suggesting higher compositional memorization consistent with the stability-diversity trade-off the paper itself identifies. - The lattice parameterization is basis-dependent and only partially addresses representation non-uniqueness; Niggli reduction is applied but training augments only over global translations, not lattice-basis permutations or rotations, leaving residual ambiguity. - Evaluation is confined to small-cell datasets (≤20 or ≤50 atoms); scaling behavior for larger unit cells with more complex topologies is unexplored.
Search Results 2604.02270 PDF
#80 — Innovative (67)

Economics of Prompting AI'CentER Discussion

repository.tilburguniversity.edu · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: This paper makes a clean and well-executed theoretical contribution by applying Carroll's robust contracting framework to AI prompting, yielding an interpretable closed-form proportional framing rule. The counterintuitive prediction that more capable models warrant more aggressive importance inflation, confirmed experimentally, is a genuinely surprising and practically relevant finding. However, the core move is transferring an existing economic framework to a new domain rather than creating fundamentally new theory, and the narrow experimental validation (math problems only, small sample) limits confidence in generalizability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: How should a user communicate with an LLM whose internal response function is unknown? Standard contract theory fails here: LLMs have no salary, no outside option, and no coherent utility function that can be aligned via payment schemes. Yet practitioners routinely observe that prompt framing—how stakes are described, how urgency is conveyed—measurably affects output quality. This paper asks what the optimal framing rule is when the principal does not know precisely how the AI maps importance signals to effort. It fills a gap in both contract theory (which assumes strategic agents) and the prompting engineering literature (which treats framing as a collection of ad-hoc heuristics rather than a continuous design variable). Methodology: The authors model human-AI interaction as a robust delegation problem. A principal faces tasks of varying true importance θ and sends an importance signal s embedded in a prompt. The AI follows a trained policy e(s) (effort increases monotonically with s), and effort translates to output quality q = f(e, θ). The principal bears a quadratic cost C(s,θ) = γ/2(s−θ)² for mis-calibrated framing. Crucially, the principal does not know the AI's effort policy or quality function precisely—she knows only a verified productivity floor µ₀ from calibration tasks. Following Carroll (2015), the principal adopts a maxmin criterion, maximizing guaranteed payoff against an adversarial worst-case response function. This yields a closed-form optimal framing rule. The theoretical predictions are then tested experimentally: GPT-4o-mini and GPT-4o are used to solve 864 math problems (AMC 10 for easy, AIME/Putnam for hard) in a 2×2 factorial design crossing task difficulty with importance framing (low vs. high), measuring accuracy and token output as proxies for quality and effort. Key Results: The main theoretical result (Proposition 2) is that the optimal framing rule is proportional: s(θ) = (1 + µ₀/γ)θ. The principal should inflate stated importance by a constant factor β = 1 + µ₀/γ above the task's true importance, and this inflation should scale linearly with θ—more important tasks receive strictly more inflation in absolute terms. A surprising corollary (Proposition 4) is that as AI capability µ₀ increases, the optimal inflation factor also increases: more capable models warrant more aggressive framing, not less. Experiments confirm these predictions. Effort (token count) increases monotonically with importance framing (209 → 473 tokens on easy tasks, 768 → 927 on hard tasks). The cost of over-inflation is empirically sharp: high framing on easy (low-θ) tasks reduces accuracy by 16.7 percentage points (z = 2.43, p = 0.015) despite generating longer responses—the AI over-verifies and second-guesses correct intermediate steps. Proportional framing (low signal for easy, high for hard) achieves 63.1% accuracy versus 62.0% flat-low and 54.7% flat-high, and uses 132 fewer tokens than flat-high. In the cross-model experiment, GPT-4o gains +21.7pp accuracy on hard tasks from high framing (p = 0.018) while GPT-4o-mini gains only +5.0pp (p = 0.58), directly confirming that gains from framing are larger for more capable models. Strengths: - Clean theoretical contribution: applying Carroll (2015)'s robust contracting framework to AI delegation is a natural and well-executed move, yielding a closed-form proportional rule that is more interpretable than generic prompt engineering advice. - The experiments are tightly coupled to the theory—they test specific structural assumptions (effort monotonicity, cost of over-inflation, asymmetric cost by difficulty) before testing predictions, providing a principled validation sequence rather than a post-hoc fit. - The capability proposition (β* increases with µ₀) is counterintuitive but experimentally supported, and has direct practical relevance as frontier models improve. - Extensions to multi-task delegation with compute budgets (Proposition 6) and interpretability valuation (Proposition 5) are compact yet meaningful, showing that budget constraints make the optimal rule superproportional and that interpretability value scales quadratically with task importance. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The experimental sample is small (24 problems, 8 replications per cell) and restricted to a narrow domain (math problems with binary correctness), making it unclear whether the proportionality result generalizes to open-ended tasks like code generation, legal drafting, or clinical summarization where 'effort' and 'quality' are harder to operationalize. - The model assumes a one-shot interaction and a static AI policy, explicitly setting aside the credibility degradation that arises in repeated settings where persistent importance inflation could erode signal informativeness—a significant omission for enterprise use cases where models may be fine-tuned on interaction histories. - The linear-quadratic specification imposes that effort equals the signal exactly (e(s) = s) and quality is linear in effort (f(e,θ) = µe), which are strong functional form assumptions; the paper acknowledges that relaxing these breaks proportionality, but provides no empirical evidence that the LQ approximation is adequate in non-math domains.
Search Results
#81 — Innovative (67)

FluxMoE: Decoupling Expert Residency for High-Performance MoE Serving

Qingxiu Liu, Cyril Y. He, Hanser Jiang, Zion Wang, Alan Zhao, Patrick P. C. Lee · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: FluxMoE's reconceptualization of expert weights as transient, streamed resources via OS-inspired virtual memory paging is an elegant abstraction that addresses a real production pain point for large MoE serving. The bandwidth-proportional storage placement is a principled closed-form solution, and the 3× throughput gain over vLLM with only 20 LoC modification demonstrates practical impact. The evaluation on PCIe-only L40 GPUs rather than NVLink production hardware, and the ~20% compression ceiling from Huffman coding on exponent bits, temper the generalizability of the results.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large MoE models such as DeepSeek-R1 (671B parameters) and Qwen3-Next-80B occupy hundreds of gigabytes of GPU memory, yet at any given decoding step only the experts of the currently executing layer are active—all others sit idle consuming GPU memory. This directly crowds out the KV cache, whose capacity is the primary determinant of inference throughput (larger KV cache → larger batches → higher tokens/second). Existing inference systems (e.g., vLLM) treat model parameters as persistent, GPU-resident state throughout the serving session, leaving KV cache to compete for whatever memory remains. For large MoE models deployed under memory pressure this tension causes aggressive KV cache swapping and collapsed throughput. FluxMoE addresses this by reconceptualizing expert weights as transient, streamed resources rather than persistent residents. Methodology: FluxMoE introduces expert paging—an OS-inspired abstraction that decouples the logical identity of expert tensors from their physical GPU residency—implemented via three tightly integrated mechanisms. First, PagedTensor virtualizes tensor addresses using CUDA VMM APIs (cuMemMap/cuMemUnmap), reserving stable virtual addresses for all experts across all layers while dynamically binding small pools of physical memory blocks to those virtual addresses. A two-layer sliding window (Ri ⊆ Ei ∪ Ei+1) guarantees only 2/N of the full model's experts must reside in GPU memory simultaneously. CUDA event barriers enforce WAR (block cannot be reused until compute completes) and RAW (compute cannot launch until parameters are loaded) ordering constraints, enabling fully pipelined compute/IO overlap with zero kernel modification. Second, the expert storage hierarchy distributes expert parameters across two backends—a compressed GPU backend that stores expert weights using selective Huffman coding on exponent bits only (exploiting observed low entropy in BFloat16 exponents while leaving mantissa bits raw), achieving ~20% compression and decompressing on-the-fly at hundreds of GB/s; and a CPU offload backend using pinned host DRAM with async PCIe DMA. Parameters are allocated across backends in proportion to their bandwidth (xk = Bk / ΣBl) so all backends finish loading each layer simultaneously, maximizing aggregate throughput. Third, a budget-aware residency planner continuously monitors the compute-to-load ratio ρ = τcomp(theory)/τload and adjusts the expert residency fraction α between GPU-compressed and CPU-offloaded storage; it enforces a hard constraint Cexp(α) ≤ Cgpu − Ckv to ensure expert residency never evicts KV cache, and uses an asymmetric dead-zone [0.9, 1.0] to avoid oscillation from PCIe burstiness. The entire system is implemented atop vLLM v0.10.2 with only 20 LoC of vLLM modification. Key Results: On Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct (80B parameters, 4 NVIDIA L40 GPUs), FluxMoE achieves 3.0× throughput over vLLM and 3.7× over vLLM-O (a PCIe-offloading variant) at batch size 256 and context length 4096 tokens, where vLLM collapses due to KV cache swapping. When scaling context from 1024 to 4096 tokens at batch 256, vLLM degrades 45.3% versus only 20.5% for FluxMoE. In a capacity-constrained regime (Mixtral-8×7B on 2 GPUs where vLLM OOMs), FluxMoE outperforms FluxMoE-H (an ablation with coarse whole-layer placement) by 28.5% at batch 256 and context 1024. The overhead of PagedTensor itself is at most 3.0% versus unmodified vLLM when all experts are uncompressed and GPU-resident. The dynamic residency planner reclaims 5.3 GB of GPU memory over a long inference session through 7 incremental α adjustments without throughput dropping below the fixed α=1.0 baseline. At small batch sizes (32) in a memory-abundant setting, FluxMoE achieves only 63.9% of vLLM's throughput due to decompression overhead, a tradeoff that reverses as memory pressure grows. Strengths: - Elegant OS-inspired abstraction (virtual memory paging applied to expert tensors) that requires no modification to compute kernels—existing PyTorch/Triton kernels work transparently, enabling easy adoption. - The bandwidth-proportional storage placement policy is a principled closed-form solution that maximizes aggregate loading rate across heterogeneous backends without heuristics. - Lossless compression preserves model accuracy entirely, which matters critically for mathematical reasoning workloads where lossy quantization is known to cause degradation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is conducted exclusively on NVIDIA L40 PCIe GPUs; L40 lacks NVLink, so the PCIe-constrained analysis may overstate benefits relative to production H100 NVLink clusters where the GPU-resident weight assumption is cheaper to maintain. - The Huffman compression of exponent bits yields only ~20% storage reduction for BFloat16 models, and effectiveness depends on the exponent entropy distribution observed in the evaluated models—it is unclear how well this generalizes to future model families with different quantization formats (e.g., FP8 weights already have fewer exponent bits). - The current prototype restricts the tri-state residency model to x=0 (no uncompressed GPU-resident fraction), leaving the full design with simultaneous compressed-resident and uncompressed-resident expert tiers unimplemented and unevaluated.
Citations 2604.02715 PDF
#82 — Innovative (66)

Claw-Eval: Toward Trustworthy Evaluation of Autonomous Agents (96 ▲ )

Bowen Ye, Rang Li, Qibin Yang, Yuanxin Liu, Linli Yao, Hanglong Lv, Zhihui Xie, Chenxin An, Lei Li, Lingpeng Kong, Qi Liu, Zhifang Sui, Tong Yang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Apr 2026
Why this rank: The three-evidence-channel architecture collecting execution traces, audit logs, and environment snapshots invisibly during execution is a principled solution to reward hacking that creates an evaluation surface agents cannot game. The Pass@k/Pass^k/Average Score trifecta cleanly separates peak capability from deployment reliability, and the empirical finding that resilience does not track baseline performance establishes robustness as an independent capability axis. The benchmark is well-architected but relatively small (300 tasks), and the use of Claude Opus 4.6 as both top-performing model and simulated user introduces circular evaluation concerns.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing agent benchmarks suffer from three critical gaps that undermine their diagnostic power. First, trajectory-opaque grading checks only final artifacts, making faithful execution indistinguishable from hallucinated steps and enabling reward hacking where models find shortcuts that satisfy output checks without completing the intended workflow. Second, safety and robustness are underspecified: safety evaluations are divorced from genuine task pressure, and no benchmark systematically stress-tests agents under realistic perturbations like API timeouts or rate limiting. Third, existing benchmarks target narrow modalities or interaction paradigms, failing to jointly evaluate text-based tool use, multimodal perception, and multi-turn dialogue under a unified methodology. These gaps collectively prevent trustworthy conclusions about whether frontier agents are reliably deployable. Methodology: Claw-Eval is an end-to-end evaluation suite built around three design principles addressing these gaps. First, full-trajectory auditing: each evaluation run proceeds through a three-phase lifecycle (Setup, Execution, Judge) inside an isolated Docker sandbox. Three independent evidence channels are collected without the agent's knowledge — execution traces, server-side audit logs from mock services (CRM, email, scheduling APIs), and post-execution environment snapshots. A strict temporal firewall ensures no grading artifacts exist in the container during execution. Second, integrated multi-dimensional scoring: each task is scored along Completion, Safety, and Robustness dimensions. Safety acts as a multiplicative gate (a policy violation zeros the score regardless of completion quality). Robustness is measured via controlled error injection (configurable per-call failure rates simulating HTTP 429, HTTP 500, and latency spikes), scored as the fraction of errored tool types for which the agent achieved a successful recovery. Completion is decomposed into fine-grained rubric items (mean 7.2 per task), combining deterministic checks on audit log evidence with LLM-judge assessments for open-ended criteria. Three metrics are reported across k=3 independent runs: Average Score (overall capability), Pass@k (capability ceiling — passed at least once), and Pass^k (reliability floor — passed every trial). Third, unified cross-modal coverage: 300 human-verified tasks across 9 categories span General service orchestration (161 tasks, Easy/Medium/Hard difficulty), Multimodal perception and generation (101 tasks: Video, Doc & Image, Code), and Multi-turn professional dialogue (38 tasks: STEM, Social Science, Business). Multi-turn tasks use a simulated user persona with progressive information revelation, testing whether agents ask the right clarifying questions rather than the most questions. Key Results: Experiments on 14 frontier models yield four main findings. (1) Trajectory-opaque grading is systematically unreliable: a vanilla LLM judge given full conversation transcripts and grader source code misses 44% of safety violations and 13% of robustness failures that the hybrid pipeline catches, because deterministic substring matching on tool-call parameters cannot be reliably performed mentally by an LLM judge. (2) Error injection primarily degrades consistency, not peak capability: Pass@3 is nearly stable across error rates 0.0–0.6 (Claude Opus 4.6 drops only 3.7%), while Pass^3 drops sharply (Gemini 3.1 Pro loses 24.2 percentage points, Claude Opus 4.6 loses 14.3%). Crucially, resilience does not track baseline performance — GLM-5-Turbo (lower baseline) degrades less than Gemini 3.1 Pro (higher baseline), establishing robustness as an independent capability axis. (3) No single model dominates across task groups or modalities: Claude Opus 4.6 leads General Pass^3 (70.8%) but ranks second in Multimodal (24.8%) behind GPT-5.4 (25.7%); each multimodal domain (Video, Doc & Image, Code) has a different leader; Gemini 3.1 Pro ranks 2nd in Multi-turn but 7th in General. (4) In multi-turn dialogue, question precision explains 76% of Pass^3 variance (r=0.87) while conversation length explains under 1% (r=0.07), confirming that information-gathering strategy quality, not quantity, drives performance. Even the strongest overall model achieves only 70.4% Overall Pass^3, leaving substantial headroom. Strengths: - The three-evidence-channel architecture (execution traces + audit logs + environment snapshots) is a principled and technically sound solution to reward hacking, creating an evaluation surface the agent cannot game because grading evidence is collected outside the container, invisibly, during execution. - The Pass@k / Pass^k / Average Score trifecta cleanly separates peak capability from deployment-grade reliability, providing actionable distinctions that single-metric evaluation cannot reveal; the empirical demonstration that this gap widens monotonically under error injection is a concrete and generalizable finding. - The unified cross-modal framework covering three radically different capability dimensions under identical scaffolding and scoring methodology is a genuine contribution over siloed benchmarks; the finding that no single model dominates across modalities would be invisible without this unified view. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is small (300 tasks total, 38 for multi-turn dialogue), which limits statistical power for fine-grained cross-model comparisons, especially in subcategories. The claim that question precision explains 76% of variance across only 13 models warrants caution. - The scoring formula parameters (α=0.8, β=0.2; safety as hard gate) are chosen by the authors without ablation or sensitivity analysis, and the fixed pass threshold (τ=0.75) is similarly unjustified. Different parameter choices could substantially alter model rankings. - The evaluation relies on mock services rather than live production APIs, and the simulated user in multi-turn dialogue is itself an LLM (Claude Opus 4.6 at temperature 0.7), introducing circular dependency and potential evaluation bias — the same model family that tops the leaderboard also acts as the judge and simulated user for multi-turn tasks.
Recommended 2604.06132 PDF
#83 — Innovative (66)

Adam's Law: Textual Frequency Law on Large Language Models (23 ▲ )

Hongyuan Adam Lu, Z. L., Victor Wei, Zefan Zhang, Zhao Hong, Qiqi Xiang, Bowen Cao, Wai Lam · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Adam's Law formalizes an intuitive but previously unquantified relationship between textual frequency and LLM performance, backed by unusually broad empirical coverage across four tasks, 100 translation languages, and multiple model families. The practical contribution — a training-data-agnostic frequency estimation method using public wordfreq tools — is genuinely useful for closed-source LLMs. However, the core insight that LLMs perform better on more common expressions is somewhat expected, and the unigram frequency proxy ignores structural and n-gram dependencies.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs exhibit markedly different performance on semantically equivalent inputs depending on how a sentence is phrased. While data quality and quantity effects are well-studied, the role of textual frequency — how commonly a given expression appears in pre-training data — is essentially unexplored. Since LLM training corpora are mostly closed-source, there is no direct way to measure this. The paper investigates whether preferring higher-frequency paraphrases of the same meaning, for both prompting and fine-tuning, yields systematic performance gains. Methodology: The framework has three components. First, the Textual Frequency Law (TFL) posits that among meaning-equivalent inputs, the one with the highest sentence-level frequency should be preferred. Sentence-level frequency is estimated as the geometric mean of word-level frequencies using an off-the-shelf Zipf-based wordfreq tool, since ground-truth pre-training frequencies are unavailable. An LLM-based paraphraser generates candidate paraphrases and the highest-frequency one is selected. Second, Textual Frequency Distillation (TFD) improves the frequency estimate by prompting target LLMs to complete stories drawn from training data, then estimating frequency over the resulting generated corpus and combining it with the web-based estimate via a weighted formula. Third, Curriculum Textual Frequency Training (CTFT) applies the frequency signal during fine-tuning by sorting training examples from low to high sentence-level frequency within each epoch, analogous to easy-to-hard curriculum learning but along the frequency axis rather than difficulty. To enable evaluation, the authors construct the Textual Frequency Paired Dataset (TFPD): GPT-4o-mini generates high- and low-frequency paraphrases of GSM8K, FLORES-200, and CommonsenseQA examples, with three human annotators validating semantic equivalence, yielding 738/526/575/114 pairs for math reasoning, translation, commonsense reasoning, and tool calling respectively. Experiments use GPT-4o-mini, DeepSeek-V3, LLaMA-3.3-70B, and Qwen2.5 models, with LoRA fine-tuning for the supervised setting. The paper also provides a formal theoretical proof grounding TFL in Zipf's law and cross-entropy training, showing that under appropriate assumptions, higher-frequency sentences incur lower NLL loss. Key Results: For prompting on math reasoning, accuracy consistently improves: DeepSeek-V3 63.55%→71.54%, GPT-4o-mini 60.70%→68.70%, LLaMA-3.3-70B 80.49%→88.75%. Crucially, when a low-frequency sample is already answered correctly, its high-frequency paraphrase also remains correct — gains come purely from recovering previously incorrect cases. For machine translation across 100 languages, 99/100 language pairs improve in BLEU for DeepSeek-V3, with 31 improving by >3 points and 12 by >5 points; when degradation occurs it is always <1 point. Results are consistent across BLEU, chrF, and COMET. Commonsense reasoning and tool-calling tasks show analogous gains. For fine-tuning, high-frequency training data outperforms both low-frequency and mixed-frequency data; CTFT further adds 8/8 best metrics across languages and metrics in translation, with BLEU improvements up to +29.96% (pag_Latn). TFD ablation shows removing it reduces winning percentage by 3–17 percentage points across metrics and models. Table 18 shows that high-frequency prompting helps uniformly across all Qwen-2.5 model sizes (0.5B–72B). Strengths: - Broad empirical coverage: four distinct tasks, 100 translation languages, multiple closed- and open-source LLMs, and both prompting and fine-tuning regimes, all consistently supporting the law. - Practical and training-data-agnostic: frequency estimation using publicly available wordfreq tools means the method applies to any closed-source LLM without access to its training corpus. - Non-trivial theoretical grounding: the Zipf-based formal proof cleanly separates assumptions (with explicit error bounds) and distinguishes TFL from standard curriculum learning by showing frequency is nearly orthogonal to syntactic difficulty (Table 5 Pearson correlations are all weak, ≤0.28). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The theoretical proof's key Assumption 2 — that model log-probabilities closely track true token frequencies pointwise — is not derivable from the cross-entropy training objective; it is an empirical hypothesis that has never been directly measured, and its validity for rare tokens is uncertain. - Dataset construction requires human annotation to verify semantic equivalence, limiting scalability; 44% of GSM8K and 48% of FLORES-200 instances were discarded due to failed equivalence checks, suggesting the paraphrasing pipeline is noisy and the effective dataset is relatively small. - Sentence-level frequency is approximated as a position-unaware product of unigram frequencies, ignoring word order and n-gram dependencies; this may conflate frequency with lexical simplicity and may not generalize to paraphrases that differ structurally rather than at the word level.
Recommended 2604.02176 PDF
#84 — Innovative (66)

FP4 Explore, BF16 Train: Diffusion Reinforcement Learning via Efficient Rollout Scaling (10 ▲ )

Yitong Li, Junsong Chen, Shuchen Xue, Pengcuo Zeren, Siyuan Fu, Dinghao Yang, Yangyang Tang, Junjie Bai, Ping Luo, Song Han, Enze Xie · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Sol-RL presents an elegant systems-level insight: FP4 quantization preserves reward ranking fidelity (Spearman ρ=0.927) even when absolute pixel values drift, making it suitable for exploration but not regression targets. The EVT-based theoretical guarantee that contrastive reward range grows as O(√log N) while quantization error is constant provides principled grounding beyond empiricism. Broad validation across three architecturally diverse models strengthens the contribution, though the hard dependency on NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs limits near-term reproducibility.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Reinforcement learning post-training (via GRPO) has proven effective for aligning text-to-image diffusion models with human preferences, and scaling the rollout group size consistently improves alignment quality. However, on large foundation models like FLUX.1-12B, generating massive candidate pools in BF16 precision is prohibitively expensive — the rollout phase becomes the dominant bottleneck, not gradient updates. Naive FP4 quantization of rollouts cuts compute but degrades training quality, since quantized samples used as regression targets force the high-precision policy to mimic distorted outputs, capping alignment. The gap between needing large rollout pools and affording them is the core problem. Methodology: Sol-RL (Speed-of-light RL) resolves the efficiency-fidelity dilemma through a two-stage decoupled framework. Stage 1 uses NVFP4-quantized inference (1 sign, 2 exponent, 1 mantissa bit; 4× TFLOPs over BF16) with reduced denoising steps (6 instead of 10) to rapidly generate N=96 candidate images per prompt and rank them by proxy reward. The key insight is that FP4 preserves coarse semantic layout and relative reward ordering even when absolute pixel values drift — empirically confirmed via Spearman ρ=0.927 and Kendall τ=0.798 between FP4 and BF16 rankings, with >96% top-4 match precision. Stage 2 takes only the top-K/2 and bottom-K/2 seed noise vectors identified in Stage 1 (K=24 of 96), regenerates them at full BF16 precision with 10 denoising steps, and runs policy optimization (DiffusionNFT objective with LoRA r=32) exclusively on these high-fidelity, high-contrast samples. After each gradient update, weights are re-quantized in-place into the NVFP4 inference engine without recompilation. An Extreme Value Theory analysis proves that the quantization error is a static constant penalty (−4Δ) while the contrastive reward range grows as 2σ√(2 log N), so larger pools inevitably overpower quantization noise. The method is evaluated across SANA-1.5B, FLUX.1-dev, and SD3.5-Large on 8 NVIDIA B200 GPUs. Key Results: On FLUX.1 under a fixed GPU-hour budget, Sol-RL achieves ImageReward=1.7636 (+1.31 over base), CLIPScore=0.3089, PickScore=0.8932, and HPSv2=0.3688, outperforming all baselines including DiffusionNFT (1.6707), AWM (1.6693), FlowGRPO (1.5331), and DanceGRPO (1.4937). Convergence speedup to reach DiffusionNFT's final score ranges from 1.91× (FLUX.1 CLIPScore) to 4.64× (SD3.5-L HPSv2), with Sol-RL also converging to a higher final alignment ceiling. In rollout time, Sol-RL achieves 2.33–2.41× speedup for large models (FLUX.1, SD3.5-Large) and 1.41× for SANA; end-to-end training iteration speedup is 1.25–1.62×. Alignment fidelity versus naive BF16 scaling (brute-force 96 BF16 samples) shows at most −1.08% HPSv2 degradation on SD3.5-Large and +0.11% improvement on SANA — effectively matching full-precision quality at a fraction of cost. NVFP4 rollouts achieve on-par IS and CLIP scores versus BF16 across all three base models. Strengths: - Elegant decoupling of exploration and optimization: the core insight that FP4 is suitable for ranking but not regression targets is well-motivated, theoretically justified via EVT, and empirically validated with rigorous rank-correlation metrics. - Broad empirical validation across three architecturally diverse foundation models (SANA linear DiT, FLUX.1 flow transformer, SD3.5-L rectified flow) and four reward metrics, with consistent gains. - The EVT-based theoretical analysis provides a principled guarantee that rollout scaling asymptotically dominates the constant FP4 quantization penalty, grounding the method beyond empirical observation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Hardware dependency: NVFP4 requires NVIDIA Blackwell (B200) GPUs; the method is inapplicable on older or non-NVIDIA hardware, severely limiting reproducibility and generalizability outside NVIDIA's ecosystem. - The two-stage pipeline introduces additional engineering complexity (re-quantization, dual precision inference engines) and the 2% overhead from BF16 regeneration, though modest, is non-trivial to implement correctly in distributed settings. - Ablations are limited to exploration steps T and pool size N; the sensitivity of Top-K selection ratio (K/N), the choice of K=24, and interactions between LoRA rank and quantization precision are not explored, leaving key design decisions underspecified.
Recommended 2604.06916 PDF
#85 — Innovative (65)

ENEC: A Lossless AI Model Compression Method Enabling Fast Inference on Ascend NPUs

Jinwu Yang, Jiaan Wu, Zedong Liu, Xinyang Ma, Hairui Zhao, Yida Gu, Yuanhong Huang, Xingchen Liu, Wenjing Huang, Zheng Wei, Jing Xing, Yili Ma, Qingyi Zhang, Baoyi An, Zhongzhe Hu, Shaoteng Liu, Xia Zhu, Jiaxun Lu, Guangming Tan, Dingwen Tao · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: ENEC's hardware-software co-design for Ascend NPUs is a meaningful systems contribution, with each algorithmic choice grounded in specific DaVinci SIMD constraints. The IDD-Scan technique — transforming intra-segment dependencies into inter-segment operations via transposition — is a non-obvious and elegant solution to a fundamental alignment constraint with broader applicability. Strong empirical validation across 10 models and thorough ablation strengthen the work, though the compression ratio trails CPU-based methods and the hardware-specific focus limits generalizability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying large language models on Huawei Ascend NPUs is bottlenecked by weight data transfer, not computation. For Qwen3-32B on a single 910B2, memory access accounts for 78–85% of inference latency in both prefill and decode stages, and models like Llama-3.1-405B vastly exceed the 512 GB HBM capacity of an 8-NPU server, necessitating CPU offloading with frequent CPU-NPU transfers. While lossless compression could reduce this bottleneck without sacrificing model accuracy, porting existing algorithms (ANS, LZ77) to Ascend NPUs produces extremely low throughput — as low as 20–40 GB/s — because the DaVinci SIMD architecture lacks conditional branching, scatter/gather instructions, efficient variable-length memory handling, and fast inter-core synchronization. No open-source lossless compressor for NPU model weights existed prior to this work. Methodology: ENEC (Efficient NPU-Enhanced Compression) is a hardware-software co-designed lossless compressor targeting Ascend NPUs that works entirely within the constraints of the SIMD-based AIV units. The design starts from five empirical observations about BF16 model weight statistics: exponents are far more compressible than mantissas (2.58 bits entropy vs. 7.97); exponent values are concentrated in a narrow continuous range; and exponent frequency follows a strong negative linear relationship with value. Building on these, ENEC applies three key optimizations over a baseline block-based (8,192–16,384 elements/block) HCLOG-style scheme. First, bit-width quantization with hierarchical halving bit-packing replaces expensive reduction-max operations with bitwise OR, using a two-level scheme (store in m bits if below threshold, else n bits) and an iterative lane-folding algorithm to produce byte-aligned output without division. Second, a vectorized branch-free integer transformation f(x) = b − x replaces slow gather table lookups with arithmetic operations (vector add + multiply + shift + modulo), exploiting the linear frequency–value relationship to map high-frequency exponents to small integers without branching. Third, the Intra-Segment Dependency Decoupled Scan (IDD-Scan) handles prefix sum computation by working around the 32-byte alignment constraint that prohibits direct intra-row SIMD operations: it transposes the local tensor, computes column-wise prefix sums (converting intra-row to inter-row dependencies), transposes back, then propagates row cumulative offsets hierarchically. Parameters (b, n, m, L) are selected offline via a cost-model search that minimizes expected compressed bit-length. The full pipeline separates exponent from sign/mantissa, applies the integer transform, groups data, bit-masks anomalous groups, and hierarchically bit-packs, with sign and mantissa stored directly. Key Results: On Ascend 910B2 with 10 real-world models (BF16, FP16, FP32), ENEC achieves compression throughput of 263–523 GB/s and decompression throughput of 188–336 GB/s, with all reconstructions verified bit-identical. For BF16 models, average compression throughput is 372 GB/s — 987× faster than ZipNN (CPU) and 1.36× faster than HANS (the only prior NPU compressor, which is closed-source). Compared to GPU methods, ENEC achieves 3.43× higher throughput than DietGPU's ANS mode and a 1.12× better compression ratio than nvCOMP's best variant (Bitcomp). Compression ratios for BF16 range from 1.35–1.37×, exceeding HANS (1.34–1.35×) and all nvCOMP variants but trailing CPU-based ZipNN (1.51×) and DietGPU::Float (1.48×), which use more expensive tail-exponent separation. End-to-end inference on Qwen3-32B shows up to 4.1× TTFT and 3.9× TPOT speedup over uncompressed CPU-offloading baseline, and on Falcon-40B up to 6.3× TTFT and 4.9× TPOT. Ablation confirms each optimization contributes substantially: IDD-Scan alone nearly doubles decompression throughput over V2; the branch-free integer transform nearly doubles compression throughput over V1; hierarchical bit-packing improves both by ~30% over V0. Parameter transferability across models is strong, with near-zero degradation for most architectures. Strengths: - The hardware-software co-design is principled and systematic: each algorithmic choice is grounded in a concrete architectural constraint of the DaVinci NPU, with performance bottlenecks quantified (35–45% overhead per identified bottleneck before optimization). - IDD-Scan is a non-obvious and practically effective solution to a fundamental SIMD alignment constraint — transforming an intra-segment dependency into inter-segment operations via transposition is elegant and broadly applicable. - Strong empirical validation across 10 diverse models, three float formats, multiple batch sizes, and ablation at each optimization stage; parameter robustness study reduces per-deployment tuning burden. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Compression ratio (1.35–1.37× BF16) lags meaningfully behind ZipNN (1.51×) and DietGPU::Float (1.48×); the trade-off is explicitly acknowledged but the practical impact on applications where ratio matters more than throughput is not quantified. - Support is limited to BF16, FP16, and FP32; INT8 and FP8 are excluded due to missing AIV API primitives, which is a significant gap given the growing importance of quantized inference on NPU hardware.
Search Results 2604.03298 PDF
#86 — Innovative (65)

Detecting Miscitation on the Scholarly Web through LLM-Augmented Text-Rich Graph Learning

Huidong Wu, Haojia Xiang, Jingtong Gao, Xiangyu Zhao, Dengsheng Wu, Jianping Li · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The structural correspondence between LLM multi-hop reasoning steps and GNN message-passing layers is a principled and creative design choice that elevates this above standard LLM-GNN integration work. Strong empirical results across three benchmarks with meaningful ablations. However, the core components (knowledge distillation, GNNs, chain-of-thought prompting) are individually well-established, and the synthetic miscitation injection in S2ORC weakens the real-world validity claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Miscitation — where a cited source fails to support or contradicts the claim it is cited for — affects an estimated 25% of citations in the scientific literature. It propagates misinformation, distorts academic search engines, and undermines scientific integrity. Existing detection methods fall into two inadequate camps: network topology approaches that identify structural anomalies but ignore semantic content, and text-similarity approaches that operate on surface-level lexical features without deep semantic reasoning. LLMs offer stronger semantic understanding but suffer from hallucinations when reasoning without global graph context and are computationally intractable at web scale (billions of citation edges). The paper addresses the gap between these extremes: combining LLM semantic depth with GNN structural efficiency. Methodology: LAGMiD (LLM-Augmented Graph Learning-based Miscitation Detector) integrates three tightly coupled components over a text-rich citation graph G = (P, E, X^P, X^E). First, an evidence-chain reasoning mechanism uses chain-of-thought prompting to perform multi-hop verification: for a citation edge e_ij, a K-hop subgraph is extracted rooted at the cited paper, top-m semantically relevant nodes are selected via cosine similarity at each hop, and the LLM progressively accumulates verification states R_h across hops, finally outputting a miscitation level O ∈ [0,1], confidence C, and natural-language explanation U. Second, a layer-wise knowledge distillation transfers the LLM's intermediate reasoning states T_h (token embeddings from the final transformer layer at hop h) to the GNN's edge representations e^(h)_{h-1,h} via InfoNCE contrastive loss, creating a structural correspondence between GNN layers and LLM reasoning hops. Third, an iterative collaborative learning strategy selects the top w% most uncertain edges (by prediction entropy) for targeted LLM refinement, filtering distillation samples to those where LLM confidence C > τ_conf and LLM prediction matches ground truth. The total training objective combines task-specific binary cross-entropy with the targeted distillation loss. Implementation uses Qwen3-8B as the LLM backbone, a 2-layer GCN with hidden dim 1024, SciBERT for text encoding, K=2 hops, m=10 nodes/hop, w=20%, τ_conf=0.7. Key Results: LAGMiD achieves state-of-the-art on three benchmarks — RED, SciFact, and S2ORC — across AUC, F1, and Precision, with statistically significant margins (p < 0.05) over all baselines in every case. On RED: AUC 0.9615 vs. second-best GuARD at 0.9100 (+5.1%), F1 0.9167 vs. 0.8571 (+7.0%). On SciFact: AUC 0.8208 vs. AnomalyLLM at 0.8024 (+2.3%), F1 0.8205 vs. 0.7958 (+3.1%). On S2ORC: AUC 0.8100 vs. GLAD at 0.7883 (+2.8%), F1 0.8256 vs. GuARD at 0.7687 (+7.4%). Efficiency gains are substantial: LAGMiD delivers 10× inference speedup over LLM (Directed) and 100× over LLM (EC) on S2ORC. Ablation confirms all components are necessary: removing evidence-chain reasoning causes the largest performance drop (AUC −3.2% on RED, −6.2% on S2ORC), while removing knowledge distillation, layer-wise distillation, and targeted distillation each degrade results progressively. K=2 hops is the sweet spot — K=1 is insufficient for multi-hop dependencies and K>2 introduces noise. Strengths: - The structural correspondence between LLM reasoning hops and GNN message-passing layers is a principled and elegant design choice that enables meaningful knowledge transfer rather than generic feature alignment. - The targeted distillation strategy (routing only high-uncertainty, high-confidence samples) is computationally practical and avoids noise propagation from LLM hallucinations — an important safeguard given the task. - Comprehensive evaluation across diverse benchmarks (biomedical RED, scientific claim verification SciFact, large-scale CS S2ORC) with 8 baselines spanning GNN, PLM, LLM, and text-rich graph methods provides strong empirical grounding. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The S2ORC evaluation relies on synthetically injected miscitations (3 heuristic types: self-promotional, collaborator, cross-domain) rather than human-annotated ones, raising questions about whether the model learns to detect real-world subtle semantic distortions or just engineered statistical artifacts. - Evaluation datasets are quite small by graph standards (max 3,561 edges for classification), and the citation graphs themselves are sparse (413–5,844 nodes). Scalability claims of 10–100× speedup and suitability for 'billions of edges' are argued theoretically but not demonstrated empirically at scale. - The distillation requires access to LLM internal token embeddings (final transformer layer), which is not possible with black-box API-only models — limiting the approach to open-weight LLMs and constraining practical deployment options as frontier models increasingly move toward API-only access.
Recommended 2603.12290 PDF
#87 — Innovative (65)

MedLayBench-V: A Large-Scale Benchmark for Expert-Lay Semantic Alignment in Medical Vision Language Models

Han Jang, Junhyeok Lee, Heeseong Eum, Kyu Sung Choi · arxiv.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: MedLayBench-V fills a genuine gap — no prior multimodal benchmark existed for expert-lay medical communication — and the three-stage SCGR pipeline's explicit decoupling of semantic extraction from fluency refinement is a sound design insight validated by a strong ablation (LLM-only simplification collapses retrieval by 83%). The scale (79K pairs, 7 modalities) is commendable. The main limitation is that the retrieval evaluation fails to convincingly demonstrate the expert-lay gap it claims to address, and all lay captions are synthetic.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Medical Vision-Language Models (Med-VLMs) have reached expert-level proficiency in interpreting diagnostic images, but are exclusively trained on professional literature and cannot communicate findings in patient-accessible language. While text-centric NLP has developed benchmarks for medical lay language generation (MLLG), no large-scale multimodal benchmark pairs medical images with lay-register descriptions. Without such a resource, it is impossible to train or rigorously evaluate whether Med-VLMs can bridge the expert-lay communication gap. Naive LLM-based simplification fails this need — it introduces hallucinations or loses critical quantitative detail, as documented in clinical trials where ~6–7% of LLM-simplified reports contained factual errors. Methodology: The authors introduce MedLayBench-V, a 79,793 image-caption pair benchmark derived from ROCOv2 (7 imaging modalities: CT, X-Ray, MRI, Ultrasound, Angiography, PET, Combined) augmented with layman-accessible captions via a three-stage Structured Concept-Grounded Refinement (SCGR) pipeline. Stage 1 (Concept-Knowledge Alignment) extracts UMLS Concept Unique Identifiers (CUIs) via MedCAT and fine-grained named entities (spatial descriptors, lesion sizes) via SciSpacy, forming a semantic constraint set C = C_onto ∪ C_ent. Stage 2 (Knowledge-Constrained Refinement) replaces each CUI with its MedlinePlus patient-friendly definition via deterministic dictionary substitution, producing a grammatically noisy draft T_draft that preserves factual anchors. Stage 3 (LLM Refinement) uses Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct within a constrained generation framework, with the LLM's role strictly limited to grammar and fluency correction while the structured constraints enforce semantic fidelity. The key design insight is the explicit decoupling of semantic extraction from stylistic refinement, preventing hallucination. Evaluation spans four dimensions: n-gram relevance (BLEU-4, ROUGE-L, METEOR), readability (FKGL, CLI, FRE, LENS), radiological factuality (RaTEScore, GREEN), and downstream zero-shot image-text retrieval (Recall@K) across eight CLIP-family models. Key Results: The SCGR pipeline reduces reading grade level from 13.10 to 10.35 (FKGL) and from 15.82 to 9.88 (Coleman-Liau Index), reaching recommended patient-education readability. Flesch Reading Ease more than doubles (26.14 → 55.88). Vocabulary size shrinks 46.1% (44,673 → 24,085 unique terms), removing long-tail jargon. Clinical correctness is preserved: RaTEScore = 64.70 and GREEN = 69.29 on the overall set, stable across train/val/test splits. Human evaluation by two board-certified radiologists and one lay reader rates Factual Correctness and Completeness at 4.86/5.0 and Fluency at 4.93/5.0 on 100 sampled pairs. Zero-shot retrieval baselines show BiomedCLIP achieving best performance (I2T R@1: 31.06% Expert, 30.70% Layman), with negligible degradation (<1.03% across all models, all statistically significant but practically negligible). Critically, an ablation isolates the MedlinePlus lexical substitution step as the key component: LLM-only simplification collapses average R@1 to 1.96 (83% drop from Expert baseline of 11.44); adding CUI extraction alone yields 2.08; full SCGR recovers 11.26, restoring 98.4% of Expert-level performance. Zero-shot captioning reveals that LLaVA-Med exhibits severe expert-register bias (BERTScore gap = +22.93 between expert and layman prompts), while general-domain models (Qwen2-VL, LLaVA-v1.5) and MedGemma show near-zero gaps. Strengths: - The three-stage pipeline design that decouples semantic extraction from fluency refinement is technically sound and directly addresses the hallucination problem that plagues naive LLM simplification approaches. - The ablation study is rigorous and informative: it cleanly isolates MedlinePlus lexical substitution as the load-bearing component, not LLM refinement or ontology mapping alone, which is a non-obvious finding. - Scale and modality diversity (79,793 pairs across 7 imaging modalities) make this substantially more comprehensive than prior work like Layman's RRG, which was limited to chest X-rays. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The retrieval benchmark used to demonstrate expert-lay alignment gap is arguably too easy: all performance differences between Expert and Layman are below 1.03%, which the authors themselves acknowledge may be masking the true alignment gap. The paper claims to expose a problem but the primary evaluation task does not convincingly demonstrate it exists at scale. - The dataset is entirely synthetic — no human-authored lay descriptions are included — and the layman captions inherit the ROCOv2 figure captions' style (terse, figure-reference-heavy), which is not representative of actual patient-facing reports. Validation with real patient comprehension studies is absent. - The paper is restricted to English and inherits ROCOv2's modality imbalance (CT + X-Ray dominate at ~62% of samples), limiting generalizability. The claim of being a 'large-scale' benchmark for patient-centered AI is somewhat overstated given these construction constraints.
Search Results 2604.05738 PDF
#88 — Innovative (65)

INSPATIO-WORLD: A Real-Time 4D World Simulator via Spatiotemporal Autoregressive Modeling (19 ▲ )

InSpatio Team, Donghui Shen, Guofeng Zhang, Haomin Liu, Haoyu Ji, Hujun Bao, Hongjia Zhai, Jialin Liu, Jing Guo, Nan Wang, Siji Pan, Weihong Pan, Weijian Xie, Xianbin Liu, Xiaojun Xiang, Xiaoyu Zhang, Xinyu Chen, Yifu Wang, Yipeng Chen, Zhenzhou Fan, Zhewen Le, Zhichao Ye, Ziqiang Zhao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Apr 2026
Why this rank: INSPATIO-WORLD delivers a well-engineered combination of implicit spatiotemporal caching and explicit depth-based geometric warping for real-time interactive world simulation, achieving strong results on WorldScore-Dynamic and RE10K-Long. The JDMD dual-teacher distillation elegantly bridges the synthetic-to-real gap without paired multi-view data. However, the individual technical components — autoregressive chunk generation, monocular depth warping, distillation — are established techniques; the primary contribution is their effective integration and engineering toward real-time performance rather than a fundamentally new approach.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Building interactive world models that support long-horizon spatial navigation from monocular video remains an unsolved challenge. Existing video diffusion models generate compelling short clips but fail along three axes when used for interactive roaming: (1) spatial persistence degradation — autoregressive frameworks lack memory mechanisms and geometric guidance, causing scene drift and structural collapse over long sequences; (2) synthetic-to-real gap — models trained on synthetic data exhibit texture smoothing and plastic-like artifacts compared to real-world visual statistics; and (3) insufficient control precision — models cannot reliably execute user-defined 6-DoF camera trajectories. The paper targets the intersection of real-time performance, geometric consistency, and high visual fidelity, a combination no prior open-source system had achieved simultaneously. Methodology: INSPATIO-WORLD builds on Wan2.1 (an open-source video DiT backbone) and introduces two main technical contributions. The first is the Spatiotemporal Autoregressive (STAR) framework, which formulates generation as chunk-wise conditional autoregression. It maintains an Implicit Spatiotemporal Cache that injects reference-video latents as a global spatial anchor and keeps a sliding-window of recent generated latents as short-term memory — both fed into the DiT via KV cache. An Explicit Spatial Constraint Module uses feedforward monocular depth reconstruction (FFR) to lift reference frames to 3D, then reprojects them to the target camera pose via a 6-DoF transformation, yielding a warped guidance feature and a binary occlusion mask concatenated as additional input channels. A position-index fixing strategy for RoPE prevents positional extrapolation drift during long sequences. Training uses Chunk-wise Backpropagation that decouples forward inference (gradient-free, full sequence) from backward passes (chunk-by-chunk), keeping peak memory at single-chunk scale while retaining full differentiability. The second contribution is Joint Distribution Matching Distillation (JDMD), a dual-teacher DMD variant. A motion teacher (fine-tuned on synthetic data with camera control) supervises a V2V distillation loss for precise geometric adherence; a perceptual teacher (the original Wan-T2V foundation model trained on real-world data) supervises a T2V distillation loss for visual fidelity. The two tasks alternate during training with shared model weights, so real-world distribution gradients calibrate the feature space without interfering with motion-control learning. Inference is further accelerated with a Tiny-VAE and torch.compile graph compilation, achieving 24 FPS on an H-series GPU and 10 FPS on a consumer RTX 4090. Key Results: On the WorldScore-Dynamic benchmark (the primary world-model leaderboard), INSPATIO-WORLD (1.3B parameters) ranks first among all real-time/interactive methods with an overall dynamic score of 68.72 — above TeleWorld (66.73) and close to FantasyWorld-1.0 (71.39, non-real-time, much larger compute). It achieves the highest camera control score (81.51 vs. 76.58 for TeleWorld) and the highest photometric quality score (93.00 vs. 88.82 for TeleWorld). On RE10K-Long (100 sequences >150 frames), INSPATIO-WORLD substantially outperforms all baselines: FID 42.68 vs. 64.84 (LingBot-World), FVD 100.55 vs. 173.02 (LingBot-World), rotation error 2.88 vs. 11.98 (LingBot-World), translation error 0.14 vs. 0.21 (LingBot-World) — a roughly 4× improvement in trajectory accuracy. On camera-controlled video rerendering (OpenVid + Blender datasets), INSPATIO-WORLD achieves SOTA FID (44.46 vs. 103.23 for NeoVerse) and FVD (110.11 vs. 230.87), with comparable camera control accuracy to NeoVerse, and is claimed to be the only open-source real-time solution for this task. Strengths: - The combination of implicit KV-cache memory and explicit depth-based geometric warping is technically well-motivated and empirically effective: trajectory errors drop by ~4× over the prior best on long-horizon evaluation, which is a qualitatively different level of control precision. - JDMD's dual-teacher formulation elegantly resolves the synthetic-to-real fidelity gap without requiring real multi-view paired data, achieving the highest photometric score (93.00) on WorldScore — a concrete validation of the distillation design. - Real-time operation (24 FPS at 1.3B parameters) is practically significant and demonstrated on consumer hardware (10 FPS on RTX 4090), making the system deployable beyond research settings; code and models are open-sourced. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The explicit geometric constraint relies on monocular depth estimation (FFR), which is unreliable for dynamic objects; the paper acknowledges that multi-view consistency of dynamic elements during wide-angle or 360° view transitions remains unsolved, limiting applicability to scenes with significant moving content. - The implicit spatiotemporal cache encodes only structural geometry, not fine-grained texture of autonomously generated regions — the model cannot faithfully recall detailed appearance of areas it hallucinated earlier, creating a persistent identity problem for long-range exploration beyond the reference video's field of view.
Recommended 2604.07209 PDF
#89 — Innovative (65)

Bias Inheritance in Neural - Symbolic Discovery of Constitutive Closures Under Function-Class Mismatch

Hanbing Liang, Ze Tao, Fujun Liu · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: Identifies and rigorously formalizes an important and underappreciated failure mode in neural-symbolic scientific discovery: that symbolic compression preserves rather than repairs neural surrogate bias, with the BIR metric providing clean empirical confirmation. The PySR control experiment ruling out symbolic expressivity as the bottleneck is a strong methodological contribution. Narrow 1D benchmarks and the lack of a proposed solution to the diagnosed problem limit impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Data-driven discovery of constitutive laws (diffusion D(u) and reaction R(u)) in nonlinear reaction-diffusion PDEs is an ill-posed inverse problem. A critical and underappreciated pitfall in scientific machine learning is that low training residuals or small short-horizon rollout errors do not guarantee correct physical law recovery — different closure pairs can compensate for each other in trajectory space while being individually wrong. The paper targets this gap specifically for the neural-symbolic pipeline, where a neural surrogate is first fit to data and then compressed into an interpretable symbolic form. The central question is whether symbolic compression repairs or merely inherits the bias already present in the neural surrogate. Methodology: The authors propose a three-stage framework: (1) weak-form-driven numerical surrogate learning, where a two-branch residual MLP (one branch for D(u), one for R(u)) is trained via a hybrid objective combining a space-weak PDE residual, auxiliary rollout, mass-balance, strong-form, anchor, and regularization terms — avoiding brittle strong-form differentiation of noisy data; (2) restricted symbolic compression, where the learned surrogate is sampled on a dense support grid and fit to a small interpretable family (polynomials, rationals, exponential-decay, saturation forms), selecting the lowest-complexity candidate within 5% tolerance of the best fit; and (3) forward re-simulation validation, where both neural and symbolic closures are reinserted into the PDE and rolled out on unseen initial conditions. The framework is evaluated on three 1D benchmark cases: two matched-library polynomial cases (Cases A, B) and one function-class-mismatch case with exponential closures (Case Exp). The theory section provides five propositions formalizing: weak-form consistency, matched-library identifiability under sufficient excitation, non-identifiability under limited excitation, approximation bias under mismatch, and — crucially — that symbolic compression is bias-preserving by the reverse triangle inequality. Key Results: Across all settings (clean, 5% Gaussian noise, sparse spatial/temporal observations), the bias inheritance ratio BIR = ErrD_sym / ErrD_neural stays persistently near 1.0 (e.g., 0.989–1.000 for diffusion, 0.994–1.002 for reaction across all cases and conditions). In matched-library settings, weak polynomial baselines substantially outperform neural surrogates on closure error (e.g., Case A clean: strong_poly ErrD = 4.1e-4 vs. neural ErrD = 6.9e-2), demonstrating that neural surrogates do not uniformly win. Under 5% observation noise, neural closure errors deteriorate sharply (ErrD jumping from ~0.07 to ~0.87 between clean and noisy for Case A), and the symbolic stage inherits this degradation entirely. Replacing the restricted symbolic library with PySR leaves BIR unchanged (BIR_D ≈ 0.9985, BIR_R ≈ 0.9958 for Case Exp clean), confirming the bottleneck is not symbolic expressivity. An excitation benchmark shows that low-excitation training sets achieve smaller weak losses yet produce significantly worse closure recovery — empirically validating the non-identifiability proposition.
