Spatial downsampling is more damaging than temporal downsampling. A preliminary 2D check (32×32 grids) confirms the same mechanism. Strengths: - The central theoretical claim (Proposition 5, bias preservation via reverse triangle inequality) is clean, correct, and directly explains the empirical results — the paper's theory and experiments are unusually well-aligned. - The PySR replacement check is a critical control experiment: it rules out the alternative hypothesis that bias inheritance is an artifact of a weak symbolic library, substantially strengthening the main claim. - The excitation benchmark (Table 2) makes explicit and quantitative the dangerous failure mode where lower loss coexists with worse physics recovery, providing a practically actionable warning for practitioners. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark suite is narrow: 1D synthetic periodic systems with smooth, scalar closures. The 2D check is explicitly preliminary (32×32 grids, 4 trajectories, less tuning), so the generalization to realistic 2D/3D, anisotropic, or multivariate closure settings is undemonstrated. - The hybrid Stage 1 objective degrades sharply even at 1%–3% noise (reaction errors jumping an order of magnitude), yet no improved noise-robust Stage 1 design is proposed — the paper diagnoses the bottleneck without solving it. - The neural surrogate uses a small residual MLP with explicit polynomial features and only 150 epochs of training, which may not represent the full expressive potential of neural approaches; it is unclear whether more capable Stage 1 models would shift the BIR conclusions.
Search Results 2604.01335 PDF
#90 — Innovative (65)

Evaluating the Formal Reasoning Capabilities of Large Language Models through Chomsky Hierarchy

Yihong Dong, Xiaoha Jian, Xue Jiang, Xuyuan Guo, Zhiyuan Fan, Jiaru Qian, Kechi Zhang, Jia Li, Zhi Jin, Ge Li · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: ChomskyBench makes a meaningful contribution by being the first benchmark to span all four Chomsky Hierarchy levels with deterministic symbolic verification and process-trace evaluation, enabling precise asymptotic analysis of where LLM reasoning breaks down. The parameterized complexity design and rigorous contamination controls (CR=0) represent solid methodological innovation. However, the core finding — that LLM performance degrades monotonically with formal language complexity — is largely expected from prior work, and the benchmark's abstract symbolic tasks lack validated mapping to real-world software engineering scenarios. It is ultimately a well-executed measurement tool rather than a new paradigm.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks (HumanEval, SWE-bench, LiveCodeBench) measure functional correctness on high-level programming tasks, but conflate reasoning with API memorization and provide only binary pass/fail feedback. They lack a principled, complexity-theoretic framework for probing whether LLMs possess genuine formal reasoning capabilities — i.e., the ability to simulate abstract automata — or are merely sophisticated pattern matchers. Prior benchmarks for formal languages (NNCH, FLaRe) are incomplete: they exclude recursively enumerable tasks, use vectorized I/O suited for neural classifiers rather than LLMs, and evaluate only final-state correctness without process traces. This gap is consequential for safety-critical software engineering tasks such as compiler design, protocol verification, and static analysis. Methodology: The authors introduce ChomskyBench, a benchmark of 115 tasks organized along all four levels of the Chomsky Hierarchy: Type-3 (Regular, FSA), Type-2 (Deterministic and Non-deterministic Context-Free, PDA), Type-1 (Context-Sensitive, LBA), and Type-0 (Recursively Enumerable, Turing Machine). Each level contains both recognition tasks (is a given string in the language?) and generation tasks (produce a valid string). Task difficulty is controlled via a complexity parameter n (string length, recursion depth), enabling asymptotic analysis of where model reasoning breaks down. All tasks use minimalist alphabets and unambiguous grammars to isolate structural reasoning from semantic knowledge. Correctness is verified by deterministic algorithmic verifiers (FSA simulators, PDA parsers, LBA/TM simulators) rather than unit tests, ensuring mathematical certainty and resistance to data contamination. The benchmark was constructed over ~860 person-hours with adversarial cross-validation between a generator team and a separate verifier team. Twelve SOTA LLMs (o3, gpt-5, gemini-2.5-pro, sonnet-4, deepseek v3.1, qwen3-235b, kimi-k2, llama-4-maverick, and others) were evaluated zero-shot via OpenRouter API. Five research questions were addressed: hierarchical stratification, impact of input complexity, efficiency vs. traditional programs, test-time scaling efficacy, and root-cause failure analysis. Key Results: Performance stratifies sharply and monotonically with Chomsky Hierarchy level. The best model (o3) achieves Acc=0.333 on Regular, 0.286 on NCF, 0.250 on CS, and 0.217 on RE tasks, with average Acc=0.278. A decisive performance cliff exists at the Type-2 to Type-1 transition: sonnet-4 drops from 0.238 (NCF) to 0.071 (CS); gemini-2.5-pro from 0.286 to 0.143; deepseek v3.1 from 0.219 to 0.091. Performance also degrades sharply with input complexity n within each level. LLMs are 10,000–350,000× slower than deterministic programs (which run in <0.001s), and Pearson correlation between task complexity and LLM PassRatio is predominantly negative, indicating that longer reasoning chains increase failure rather than success. Test-time scaling (Best-of-N) improves accuracy consistently (~0.05 per doubling of N) with no hard ceiling, but achieving >90% accuracy is estimated to require N>10,000 samples — prohibitive in practice and dependent on an unrealistic oracle verifier. Extended reasoning (thinking mode) offers modest gains at lower cost than Best-of-N. Root-cause analysis (LLM-as-a-Judge) finds high scores on automata terminology (>4.0/5) and output format, but notably lower scores on transition rule execution (2.96–3.98), confirming that failure is in execution, not comprehension. Three failure modes dominate: state tracking collapse during long traces, recursion depth limitations (implicit stack management), and long-range dependency failure across multiple synchronized counters. Strengths: - The first benchmark to span all four Chomsky Hierarchy levels including Type-0, combined with process-trace evaluation (not just final-state) and deterministic symbolic verification, yielding mathematically rigorous, unambiguous ground truth. - The parameterized complexity design enables asymptotic analysis, identifying precisely where each model's reasoning breaks down rather than providing a single aggregate score. - Rigorous contamination controls: 85% novel expert-designed tasks with abstract symbolic alphabets, validated via CDD analysis showing CR=0 across all levels; performance differences are genuine capability signals rather than memorization artifacts. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark deliberately uses abstract symbolic tasks (e.g., {a,b,c} alphabets) that do not directly correspond to realistic software engineering inputs; the mapping from Chomsky level to real-world SE tasks (type checking, AST parsing, etc.) remains qualitative and unvalidated empirically. - The Best-of-N oracle analysis assumes access to a perfect verifier, which is unavailable in practice for open-ended formal tasks; the practical scaling conclusions therefore rest on an idealized setup that overstates the potential of test-time compute. - Evaluation is zero-shot only; few-shot prompting, fine-tuning on formal language tasks, or hybrid neuro-symbolic architectures (which the paper's own conclusions suggest are the right direction) are not explored, leaving the improvement ceiling incompletely characterized.
Search Results 2604.02709 PDF
#91 — Innovative (65)

Fairness in Opinion-Formation Dynamics

cs.uoi.gr · karypis - new results
Why this rank: This paper opens a genuinely new research direction by formalizing fairness in opinion-formation dynamics — the φ-fairness definition and MSAF optimization are novel constructs with clean theoretical underpinnings (feasibility, monotonicity, convergence guarantees). The Sherman-Morrison rank-one update strategy is technically elegant. However, the core optimization tools (linearized convex relaxation, greedy selection) are standard, and the severe scalability limitations (22+ hours on ~10K nodes) constrain practical relevance to the large social networks where the problem actually matters.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Online social networks shape public opinion through opinion-formation dynamics, yet the fairness of these processes has been largely ignored. In the Friedkin–Johnsen (FJ) model, each node's expressed opinion converges to a weighted combination of its fixed inner opinion (scaled by stubbornness) and its neighbors' opinions. The weight a node's inner opinion carries in the aggregate public opinion — its "influence" — can be highly unequal across demographic groups even when group sizes are balanced, since stubbornness asymmetries systematically amplify the voice of already-dominant groups. No prior work had formalized fairness for opinion dynamics or designed algorithms to achieve it. Methodology: The paper defines φ-fairness as requiring that the total influence Q_R of a protected group R equals a target φ ∈ (0,1), where influence is the column-sum of the FJ influence matrix Q = (I − (I−A)W)⁻¹A. The central optimization problem, MSAF (Minimum Stubbornness Adjustment for Fairness), minimizes the squared-L2 cost of changing stubbornness values subject to Q_R(a') = φ. The objective is convex but the constraint is nonlinear (it involves a matrix inverse). Two algorithm families address this: (1) Selective (Se) algorithms iteratively push individual nodes' stubbornness to extremal values (ε or 1−ε), using closed-form Sherman–Morrison rank-one updates (O(n²) per step) to recompute influence efficiently; three variants differ in node selection — random (Se-Rand), greedy by influence gain (Se-Greedy), and gradient-based (Se-SM). (2) Global Adjustment (GA) algorithms iteratively solve a linearized convex subproblem using first-order Taylor expansion of the constraint; GA-SM uses exact partial derivatives from Lemma 1, while GA-NMA uses a faster first-order Neumann series approximation. Key theoretical results proven include: universal feasibility (any φ ∈ (0,1) is achievable), monotonicity (increasing a node's stubbornness monotonically increases its group's influence), and local quadratic convergence of GA. Key Results: Experiments on 7 real-world networks (34–10,537 nodes; Karate, Residence, Twitter, Blogs, Trinity, Facebook, Northwestern) and synthetic SBM graphs with n=1000 nodes show: (1) Stubbornness strongly dominates group size in determining influence — a 50/50 split with one group at high stubbornness and the other at low yields Q_R ≈ 80% vs. 20%, far exceeding demographic parity. (2) GA-SM achieves the lowest intervention cost across all graphs (e.g., cost 0.175 on Karate vs. 1.760 for Se-Rand), but is the slowest (e.g., 81,088 seconds on Northwestern vs. 1,234 for Se-Greedy). (3) GA-NMA provides a practical cost–runtime tradeoff (second-lowest cost, much faster than GA-SM). (4) Selective algorithms change far fewer nodes but push them to extreme stubbornness, resulting in higher cost despite sparser modifications. (5) High-degree nodes are preferentially modified by the best-performing variants (Se-SM, Se-Greedy, GA-SM), with Pearson correlations between degree and |a_i − a'_i| reaching 0.59 for GA-SM on Northwestern — a consistent emergent pattern not explicitly encoded in any algorithm. Strengths: - The mathematical framework is clean and rigorous: closed-form Sherman–Morrison updates for single-node perturbations enable both an exact characterization of influence change and an O(n²)-per-iteration Selective algorithm, avoiding full matrix recomputation. - The theoretical results (feasibility, monotonicity, convergence) are non-trivial and properly proved, providing strong guarantees for algorithm termination and solution quality. - The empirical analysis goes beyond effectiveness metrics to characterize what the algorithms do structurally (degree correlation, stubbornness distribution shifts), yielding interpretable insights about the intervention strategies. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Runtime scalability is a serious concern: GA-SM requires over 22 hours on the 10,537-node Northwestern graph, limiting practical applicability to large social networks; the paper does not propose approximations or subsampling strategies to address this. - The fairness definition requires a single pre-specified target φ and partitions nodes into exactly two groups; extension to multiple groups or intersectional fairness is noted as future work but would make the optimization problem substantially harder, with no clear path forward provided. - Stubbornness values are randomly sampled from U(0,1) in real-world experiments rather than estimated from real behavioral data, which limits the ecological validity of the results and the interpretability of the cost metric as a real-world intervention effort.

#92 — Innovative (64)

SafeSeek: Universal Attribution of Safety Circuits in Language Models

Miao Yu, Siyuan Fu, Moayad Aloqaily, Zhenhong Zhou, Safa Otoum, Xing fan, Kun Wang, Yufei Guo, Qingsong Wen · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: SafeSeek contributes a well-engineered dual-mask optimization framework that jointly extracts malicious and clean subgraphs, and the STE-based binarization resolves a real technical problem in differentiable circuit discovery. However, the approach builds substantially on existing mechanistic interpretability techniques (masking, circuit discovery, activation probing), and the core idea of learning binary masks over model components is incremental relative to prior differentiable masking work.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Ensuring LLM safety requires understanding where safety-relevant computations live inside the model — not just achieving behavioral alignment. Existing mechanistic interpretability methods for safety rely on heuristic search (e.g., greedy head ablation, activation contrasting) or domain-specific metrics that isolate disjoint components (individual neurons or heads) rather than the complete functional subgraphs responsible for safety. These approaches are computationally unstable, non-generalizable across attack types, and produce incomplete circuits that cannot be directly leveraged for targeted safety interventions. Methodology: SafeSeek frames safety circuit discovery as a gradient-based optimization over learnable binary masks. Each model unit (individual weight, generalized neuron, attention head, or layer) is assigned a latent scalar z_u, from which a sigmoid mask m_u is derived. The Straight-Through Estimator (STE) binarizes masks in the forward pass while allowing gradient flow through the continuous variable in the backward pass, resolving the training-inference inconsistency that plagues prior differentiable masking approaches. The optimization minimizes KL divergence between the full model's and masked model's predictive distributions, plus an L1 sparsity penalty — producing compact, faithful safety subgraphs. For backdoor attribution, SafeSeek introduces Dual-Mask Optimization (DMO), which simultaneously learns a sparse backdoor circuit C_bkd and a complementary dense clean subgraph G_clean via three coordinated loss terms: a clean subgraph loss (preserving benign behavior and neutralizing triggered behavior), a backdoor circuit loss (isolating malicious trigger-response functionality), and a sparsity loss penalizing overlap between the two masks. For safety alignment, the same DMO framework identifies a sparse alignment circuit C_safe and a dense unsafe subgraph G_unsafe. Identified circuits are validated by ablation — measuring Attack Success Rate on C and G\C separately. An optional Safety Circuit Tuning (SaCirT) stage then fine-tunes only the identified circuit parameters for efficient safety enhancement, leaving ~98% of model weights frozen. Key Results: Experiments on LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct and Qwen-3-8B across three backdoor types (Refusal, Jailbreak, Mislabel) and safety alignment scenarios yield strong results. For backdoor attribution: the identified backdoor circuit C_bkd achieves 0.42% sparsity (Refusal, LLaMA) and 100% in-domain ASR_p, while the complementary clean subgraph G_clean reduces ASR_p from 100% → 0.0% in-domain and 100% → 0.4% OOD, retaining >99% general utility (GSM8k drops <1%). This pattern is consistent across all three backdoor types and both model families, with circuit sparsity ranging from 91% to 99%. For safety alignment: removing the sparse alignment circuit C_safe (3.03% heads, 0.79% neurons in LLaMA) causes ASR to spike from 0.8% → 96.9% in-domain and 7.9% → 81.5% OOD, while preserving 92.1% of general capabilities. SaCirT improves safety retention during helpfulness fine-tuning from 89.3% (LoRA baseline) to 94.0% on Advbench for LLaMA, while matching or exceeding baseline utility on GSM8k (72.3% vs. 71.3%). The STE ablation shows that without it, optimization collapses — GSM8k drops from 72.0% to 0.0% even as ASR appears suppressed, confirming the collapse is artifactual. Strengths: - The STE-based differentiable binary masking is technically sound and resolves the forward/backward consistency problem that makes continuous masks poor proxies for discrete circuits; the ablation study quantitatively demonstrates this is non-trivial. - The dual-mask decomposition jointly extracts both the malicious circuit and the sanitized complement, providing a cleaner disentanglement than methods that only identify one or the other. - Empirical generalization across two distinct model families, three backdoor trigger types (phrase, character, semantic), and both ID and OOD evaluation sets is notably thorough for a mechanistic interpretability paper. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are limited to 8B-scale models; it is unclear whether the extreme circuit sparsity (<1%) and structural orthogonality findings hold at larger scales (70B+) where safety behavior may be more distributed. - The clean subgraph for jailbreak and mislabel backdoors does not fully suppress ASR (5.5–30% residual in-domain vs. 0.4% for refusal), yet this asymmetry is not analyzed — suggesting the circuit decomposition quality varies with backdoor type in ways that are unexplained. - The method requires access to labeled safety datasets for mask optimization, and the choice of in-domain dataset directly determines what circuit is found; the sensitivity of circuit identity to dataset composition and size (only 100 samples used) is not thoroughly ablated.

#93 — Innovative (64)

TABQAWORLD: Optimizing Multimodal Reasoning for Multi-Turn Table Question Answering

Tung Sum Thomas Kwok, Xinyu Wang, Xiaofeng Lin, Peng Lu, Chunhe Wang, Changlun Li, Hanwei Wu, Nan Tang, Elisa Kreiss, Guang Cheng · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: TABQAWORLD introduces a meaningful novelty by formalizing modality selection as a decision variable within a POMDP framework, rather than treating table representation as a fixed input. The metadata-guided trajectory optimization is a practical contribution that jointly improves accuracy and efficiency. However, the core ideas of adaptive tool selection and lightweight state tracking are well-established patterns in the agentic reasoning literature, limiting the conceptual departure from prior work.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-turn table question answering (TableQA) suffers from two compounding failure modes. First, fixed text serializations (JSON, Markdown, LaTeX) distort 2D table topology by flattening rows and columns into token sequences, causing perceptual errors that accumulate across reasoning steps. Second, state estimation of full table structure is fragile even for frontier LLMs, producing hallucinated values and trajectory drift. Prior remedies — stepwise reflection, process reward modeling, tabular grounding — improve accuracy at the cost of heavy inference overhead, making real-world deployment impractical. The paper fills the gap of jointly improving accuracy and efficiency in training-free multi-turn TableQA. Methodology: TABQAWORLD is a training-free, plug-and-play framework with two orthogonal components. (1) Action-conditioned multimodal selection policy: at each reasoning step, the agent selects the best table representation modality (text, image, or multimodal) conditioned on the specific action to be performed (e.g., filter, aggregate, sort). This is formalized as a partially observable MDP where modality becomes a decision variable rather than a fixed input format. (2) Metadata-guided trajectory optimization: instead of estimating the full table state (shown experimentally to cause hallucination), the agent maintains a compact low-dimensional metadata summary — row count direction, affected columns, and a key output value. After each action the predicted metadata is compared to the realized metadata; mismatch triggers replanning, while high-confidence low-risk actions are batched together to reduce conversation turns and latency. A cross-step drift guard detects repeated action patterns and forces convergence. The algorithm initializes modality to image and allows the MLLM to switch per step within a budget of 12 tool-use steps. Key Results: Evaluated on seven benchmarks spanning TableQA (WTQ, TABMWP, TAT-QA, HiTab, FeTaQA) and Table Fact Verification (TabFact, InfoTabs), TABQAWORLD with Qwen3-VL-8B achieves an average accuracy of 85.67%, outperforming the best dynamic modality baseline (TableDART) by 10.81%, all training-free proprietary GPT-3.5 agents by at least 6.32%, and trained trajectory optimization baselines (TabTrim-8B) by 4.87% on comparable benchmarks. On WTQ alone it reaches 88.89% accuracy. The ablation isolates each component: adaptive modality selection contributes +5.42% accuracy over the best fixed-modality setting; metadata-guided trajectory optimization reduces end-to-end inference latency by 33.35% (from 41.55s to 27.29s per sample) and average reasoning turns from 3.61 to 2.24, while maintaining comparable token compute. The agent internalizes a policy that defaults to image for most actions but switches to text for within-row column computations, validating that the framework discovers action-appropriate representations without training. Strengths: - Training-free and model-agnostic: plugs into any MLLM backbone and outperforms methods that require fine-tuning on specialized datasets, including trained PRM and table pruning agents. - Jointly addresses accuracy and efficiency, a combination absent from prior work — most methods trade one for the other. - Rigorous ablation design using two 2025 datasets (MMQA, MMTU) that post-date model pre-training, credibly demonstrating zero-shot generalization rather than memorization. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The metadata estimation (row direction, key output) is itself generated by the LLM and can be wrong; the paper shows this degrades full-table estimation but does not quantify how often low-dimensional metadata predictions fail or trigger unnecessary replanning, which could hurt latency in adversarial cases. - Image rendering is capped at 30 rows × 10 columns (200 DPI), so very large tables are truncated; the paper's table-size ablation shows accuracy drops sharply for tables beyond ~1700 tokens, and image modality's advantage becomes less clear as tables grow, leaving open the question of scalability to warehouse-scale tables.
Search Results 2604.03393 PDF
#94 — Innovative (64)

Retrieval Augmented Conversational Recommendation with Reinforcement Learning

Zhenrui Yue, Honglei Zhuang, Zhen Qin, Zhankui He, Huimin Zeng, Julian McAuley, Dong Wang · arxiv.org · Julian McAuley - new articles
Why this rank: RAR's key insight—training only a lightweight retriever using frozen LLM feedback via DPO/GRPO—is an elegant and practical solution to retrieval-generation misalignment. The 4× improvement on unpopular items and near-elimination of hallucinations are meaningful results. However, the individual components (retrieval augmentation, preference optimization) are well-established, and the contribution is more in their principled combination than in fundamental novelty.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Conversational recommender systems (CRS) powered by LLMs rely on pretrained parametric knowledge, making them unable to recommend novel or less-popular items not seen during training. Existing retrieval-augmented approaches either require expensive knowledge graph construction or lack a unified text corpus suitable for embedding-based retrieval. A further critical problem is retrieval–generation misalignment: when the retriever returns poor candidates, the LLM amplifies those errors, degrading accuracy on cold-start and tail items. No prior work has systematically studied embedding-based retrieval augmentation for CRS or proposed a principled method to align the retriever with LLM feedback. Methodology: RAR is a two-stage framework comprising a lightweight sequential retriever and a black-box LLM generator. The retriever (LRURec, a state-space model with O(log t) complexity) encodes item histories via Qwen 3 embeddings against a curated corpus of 337,731 movies with rich metadata (title, year, genre, director, cast, plot) and retrieves top-k candidates. The LLM generator then re-ranks these candidates conditioned on full conversation context. To address retrieval–generation misalignment, the retriever is post-trained via online on-policy preference optimization: multiple candidate sets are sampled per turn from the current retriever policy, each set is scored by the frozen LLM generator (NDCG as reward), and the retriever parameters are updated using either pairwise DPO (preferred vs. dispreferred candidate set) or multi-sample GRPO (group-normalized advantages). Candidate set likelihoods are modeled via the Plackett-Luce ranking distribution. A supervised NLL term is combined with the RL loss to prevent policy collapse. The full training objective is L = L_nll + L_rl. Notably, the LLM generator is never fine-tuned, making RAR compatible with any black-box model. Key Results: RAR is evaluated on Inspired, Redial, and Reddit against traditional CRS (KBRD, KGSF, UniCRS), sequential models (SASRec, FMLPRec, LRURec), and SFT-based retrieval augmentation using Qwen3-8B, GPT-5 mini, and Gemini 3 Flash. RAR with online DPO achieves an average 7.60% improvement over the best baseline across all datasets and metrics. Compared to SFT-only counterparts, RAR gains 11.9% (Qwen), 7.5% (Gemini), and 7.7% (GPT) average improvement. Top-5 precision improvements are larger than top-10 (9.33% vs. 5.86%), indicating the RL training specifically benefits high-precision ranking. GRPO slightly outperforms DPO but requires ~8× more inference, while DPO retains 98.6% of GRPO performance. For unpopular items (unseen in training), RAR achieves nearly 4× improvement in NDCG@10 over the Qwen-only baseline, and hallucination rates drop below 1% across all datasets. Extended reasoning ('thinking' mode) does not uniformly help: it hurts Qwen but helps GPT on two out of three datasets. RAR generalizes to older LLM variants (GPT-4o mini, Gemini 2.0 Flash, Qwen 2.5), consistently outperforming SFT baselines. Strengths: - The RL feedback loop is elegant and practical: training only the small retriever with LLM feedback as reward avoids fine-tuning any large model while still closing the retrieval–generation alignment gap. - The curated 337k-movie corpus with rich structured metadata is a genuine infrastructure contribution that enables embedding-based retrieval at scale where prior work was limited to ~7k titles (REDIAL). - Comprehensive ablations across retriever architectures, RL algorithms (DPO, GRPO, SimPO), hyperparameters (β, candidate set size), and LLM backbones provide strong empirical grounding. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is restricted to the movie domain; generalizability to other recommendation verticals (e.g., e-commerce, music) is assumed but not demonstrated, and corpus construction would need to be repeated for each new domain. - Despite the 4× improvement on unpopular items, absolute NDCG@10 for tail items on Reddit remains extremely low (0.0013 reported), indicating popularity bias is mitigated but far from solved—the retriever still struggles with genuinely cold items not covered by MovieLens pretraining. - The online RL loop requires repeated LLM inference per training step (g candidate sets × LLM forward pass), which may be cost-prohibitive at deployment scale for larger corpora, and the paper does not report wall-clock training costs.
Recommended 2604.04457 PDF
#95 — Innovative (64)

TLC-Calibrator: Latency-Efficient LoRA-Split Inference for Edge LLMs via Two-Tiered Communication Calibration

openreview.net · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: TLC-Calibrator introduces a well-motivated two-tiered gating framework that independently filters both communication directions in LoRA-Split Inference, a meaningful architectural contribution to the edge LLM deployment problem. The use of AOD as a calibration proxy is empirically grounded through systematic correlation analysis rather than ad hoc selection. However, the core idea — lightweight gating to skip unnecessary communication rounds — is a natural extension of existing pruning approaches like DLoRA, and the evaluation on only two QA datasets limits confidence in generality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying personalized LLMs on user devices is constrained by memory and compute. LoRA-Split Inference (LoS-Inference) offers a promising alternative: host the frozen base model on an edge server and keep user-specific LoRA adapters on device. However, each Transformer layer requires two bidirectional communication rounds — edge→device to send hidden states for LoRA projection, and device→edge to return LoRA-adapted Q/K/V values. Profiling shows communication accounts for over 80–87% of per-layer latency at 1 Gbps, making it the dominant bottleneck. Existing approaches (DLoRA) only prune one direction and operate token-level, leaving the bilateral overhead largely unaddressed. Methodology: The paper proposes TLC-Calibrator, a two-tiered framework that independently gates both communication directions at each (layer, token) pair. Tier-1 runs on the edge server and decides whether to transmit the hidden state h^l(t_i) to the device; it uses a shallow MLP over four lightweight features: token-wise ℓ2 norm, mean activation, max activation, and positional standard deviation. Tier-2 runs on the device and decides whether the computed LoRA projections ΔQ/K/V are significant enough to return; it uses energy ratio (LoRA vs. base ℓ2-norm), cross-layer self-similarity of Q/K/V projections, token entropy, and projection diversity. Both calibrators output scalar impact scores compared against thresholds τ1 and τ2. The choice of Attention Output Divergence (AOD) — ||Attn_base − Attn_lora||_2 — as the training signal for the calibrators is motivated empirically: AOD achieves Pearson correlation 0.88–0.91 with the final output-level metric (Final Logit Shift), outperforming RDA (0.72–0.85) and Variance Shift (0.55–0.64). Both tiers are trained offline to predict AOD, adding negligible runtime overhead since they consume only local statistics already computed during the forward pass. Key Results: Evaluated on OPT-1.3B, LLaMA-3B, LLaMA-7B, and OPT-13B across COQA and ELI5. TLC-Calibrator achieves 2.2–2.4× latency speedup over the naive LInfer baseline for larger models (LLaMA-7B: 0.206→0.094 s/token on COQA; OPT-13B: 0.262→0.107 s/token), and 1.37–1.9× over DLoRA, while incurring only 0.3–1.9% F1 degradation. At the extreme (τ1=0.8), accuracy collapses 12–13% on ELI5, demonstrating a hard tradeoff boundary. Ablation confirms Tier-1 contributes more latency reduction than Tier-2 (disabling Tier-1 alone raises latency from ~0.094 to 0.158–0.213 s; disabling Tier-2 raises it to 0.135–0.188 s). Device GPU memory is also reduced vs. LInfer due to fewer activation transfers. The method does not close the gap to edge-only Server inference (0.026–0.045 s), which sacrifices privacy entirely. Strengths: - The empirical grounding is solid: AOD is justified as a calibration proxy through systematic Pearson correlation analysis before being embedded in the calibrators, rather than chosen ad hoc. - The two-tier decomposition is well-motivated — Tier-2 can filter even when Tier-1 transmits, and operates purely on local device statistics requiring no additional communication. - Case study tables (Tables 5–6) provide unusually transparent per-layer, per-token calibration traces, clearly showing that common factual queries are pruned more aggressively than personalized ones. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is limited to two QA datasets (COQA, ELI5) and four models; no results on generation-heavy tasks (summarization, code, instruction following) where LoRA personalization matters most. - The calibrators are trained with fixed feature weights and thresholds; there is no online or adaptive threshold mechanism for varying network conditions, and the sensitivity analysis reveals sharp accuracy cliffs (12–13% F1 drop) at τ1=0.8, making safe deployment threshold selection non-trivial. - The framework assumes low-latency edge infrastructure (microsecond-level) with ≥100 Mbps bandwidth; at 100 Mbps, per-token latency exceeds 0.7 s, making the approach impractical without reliable connectivity — a major constraint for mobile edge scenarios the paper targets.
Search Results
#96 — Innovative (64)

JoyAI- LLM Flash: Advancing Mid-Scale LLMs with Token Efficiency

Aichen Cai, Anmeng Zhang, Anyu Li, Bo Zhang, Bohua Cai, Chang Li, Changjian Jiang, Changkai Lu, Chao Xue, Chaocai Liang, Cheng Zhang, Dongkai Liu, Fei Wang, Guoqiang Huang, Haijian Ke, Han Lin, Hao Wang, Ji Miao, Jiacheng Zhang, Jialong Shi, Jifeng Zhu, Jingjing Qian, Junhui Luo, Junwu Xiong, Lam So, Liang Huang, Ming Ke, Mingyang Li, Panfeng Shi, Peng Hao, Qi Wang, Qian Lai, Qiaoqiao Yuan, Qingyu Yin, Qiong Cao, Qixiang Wang, Rongcheng Bian, Rongduo Han, Shaoqiang Zheng, Shi Hu, Shi Suo, Shijie Ren, Shijin Zhang, Shiying Fan, Shuai Xie, Tianyi Zhang, Wei Liu, Wentao Tan, Xianghan Meng, Xiaodong He, Xing Pan, Xiran Wang, Xuyang Peng, Ya Zhang, Yang Liu, Yangyang Duan, Yanxu Chen, Yicheng Gong, Yidan Huang, Yifei Liu, Yinhao Bai, Yongqiang Liu, Yuesong Zhang, Yuqi Zhang, Zerui Xie, Zhenfang Wang, Zhennan Shen, Zheyuan Liu, Zhuwei Zeng · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: FiberPO's two-scale decomposition via fiber bundle geometry is a genuinely creative theoretical framing that addresses complementary failure modes of GRPO and GSPO, and the empirical token-efficiency results are striking (85% reduction vs. GLM-4.7-Flash-Thinking with higher accuracy). However, the core proofs are deferred to a companion paper, the architecture is largely derivative of DeepSeek-V3/Kimi-K2, and the RL comparison omits important recent baselines like DAPO, limiting the strength of the novelty claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large language models increasingly face two intertwined challenges: excessive token consumption during inference and high computational cost. While scaling test-time compute has historically improved performance, the community lacks models in the sub-50B regime that are simultaneously accurate, fast to serve, and token-efficient. Existing RL alignment algorithms (PPO, GRPO, GSPO) provide only coarse stability controls — GRPO cannot bound trajectory-level drift, leading to entropy collapse; GSPO suppresses within-trajectory variation, preventing fine-grained token-level credit assignment. This paper addresses all three concerns (token efficiency, inference throughput, RL stability) in a single system. Methodology: JoyAI-LLM Flash is a sparse MoE model with 48B total parameters activating only 2.7B per forward pass (8 of 256 routed experts + 1 shared expert), using Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA) and RoPE, drawing its macro-architecture from DeepSeek-V3 and Kimi-K2. Pretraining uses 20.7T tokens across four curriculum stages (foundational → code/math enhancement → mid-training → 128K context extension), with the Muon optimizer (matrix orthogonalization-based, substantially more stable than Adam) and a lightweight single-layer dense MTP head for joint training–inference co-design. Post-training follows an SFT → DPO → RL pipeline: SFT interleaves thinking/non-thinking data and includes four categories of tool-integrated reasoning trajectories (code-centric, search-centric, hybrid, terminal-centric); DPO runs 1,000 steps to suppress hallucinations before RL. The main RL innovation is FiberPO, grounded in fiber bundle theory. FiberPO decomposes each token's gated importance ratio into a trajectory-level base weight (controlling aggregate drift via a piecewise-linear gate g^agg with a restorative rollback regime) and a token-level gated residual (clipping individual tokens relative to their within-trajectory mean rather than to absolute log-ratio). This two-scale decomposition provides independent trust-region budgets at trajectory and token levels, with provable first-order agreement with the true RL objective near on-policy. Multi-domain RL uses a Gaussian curriculum scheduler that progressively shifts from easy to hard prompts. Inference optimization combines QAT (INT4/FP8/W4AFP8), PTQ, and MTP speculative decoding. Key Results: On the base model, JoyAI-LLM Flash substantially outperforms Qwen3-30B-A3B and Qwen3.5-35B-A3B on math (MATH: 78.1 vs. 59.6/56.0; MATH-500: 77.0 vs. 58.0/54.8) and MMLU-Pro (73.1 vs. 61.7/60.7), at the cost of slightly lower scores on CMMLU and LiveCodeBench. On the instruct model, JoyAI-LLM Flash achieves best-in-class LiveCodeBench (65.6% at 7,300 avg tokens vs. GLM-4.7-Flash-Thinking's 64.0% at 53,600 tokens — 85% token reduction with higher accuracy), SWE-bench Verified (62.6%), and AIME'25 (72.9%). Compared to Qwen3.5-35B-A3B, it matches or exceeds accuracy on most benchmarks while consuming 2–8× fewer tokens. MTP achieves 1.87× speculative decoding speedup on SpecBench, best among all compared models (GLM-5: 1.82×, Qwen3.5-35B-A3B: 1.61×). W4AFP8 quantization with MTP next-2 achieves 1.96× throughput over the BF16 non-MTP baseline. In the single-domain FiberPO ablation on DAPO-Math-17k/AIME 2024: GRPO entropy collapses to 0.038 nats (91% drop) with importance ratios exceeding 10^3; GSPO stagnates with response lengths of 7,380–8,870 tokens; FiberPO improves validation accuracy from 0.668 to 0.786 while reducing mean response length from 7,902 to 4,543 tokens, preserving entropy at 0.43 nats throughout. Strengths: - FiberPO's two-scale decomposition is theoretically grounded via fiber bundle geometry and addresses complementary failure modes of GRPO (entropy collapse) and GSPO (token discrimination loss) in a single unified framework, with empirical validation showing stable training where both baselines fail. - The extreme MoE sparsity ratio (2.7B/48B ≈ 5.6% activated) combined with auxiliary-loss-free load balancing is more aggressive than contemporary models of comparable total scale, enabling favorable throughput without sacrificing quality. - The joint training–inference co-design (QAT + dense MTP) yields the highest MTP speedup (1.87×) among compared systems on a single GPU, with the W4AFP8+MTP combination reaching 1.96× — practically meaningful for deployment. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The core theoretical apparatus underlying FiberPO is deferred to a companion paper (arXiv:2603.08239); the mathematical proofs (FBG First-Order Agreement Theorem, APC-Obj-TRPO Equivalence) are not self-contained, making independent verification difficult. - The RL comparison is narrow — only GRPO and GSPO are compared, omitting DAPO and other recent methods. Benchmark results for GLM-4.7-Flash-Thinking are flagged with asterisks indicating they differ substantially from the authors' reproductions, raising concerns about fairness of the token efficiency claims. - The architecture is largely derivative of DeepSeek-V3 and Kimi-K2 (MLA, auxiliary-loss-free balancing, MTP head structure), with limited architectural novelty beyond the MoE sparsity ratio; no ablation isolates the contribution of individual design choices (MLA vs. GQA, Muon vs. Adam, curriculum stages).

#97 — Innovative (63)

Dynamic Linear Coregionalization for Realistic Synthetic Multivariate Time Series

Annita Vapsi, Penghang Liu, Saheed Obitayo, Aakriti, Manoj Cherukumalli, Prathamesh Patil, Amit Varshney, Nicolas Marchesotti, Elizabeth Fons, Vamsi K. Potluru, Manuela Veloso · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: DynLMC introduces three well-motivated, interpretable mechanisms (correlation drift, regime switching, lead-lag) that directly target a genuine gap in synthetic multivariate time series generation for foundation model pretraining. The progressive mixing training strategy is a nice methodological contribution. However, inconsistent gains on Chronos-2 and the workshop-paper scope (missing key ablations and comparisons to other nonstationary generators) temper the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Foundation models for time series (FMTS) require large-scale pretraining data with realistic multivariate structure, but obtaining real labeled multivariate data at scale is difficult. Synthetic data generation is the practical alternative, yet existing generators—including Chronos's KernelSynth (univariate GPs) and TimePFN's LMCSynth (static Linear Model of Coregionalization)—assume fixed, instantaneous inter-channel correlations. Real-world multivariate time series in finance, energy, and climate exhibit correlation drift, regime shifts, and lead-lag effects across channels, none of which static LMC can reproduce. This mismatch between synthetic pretraining distributions and real-world dynamics limits FMTS transferability. Methodology: DynLMC extends the static LMC framework by replacing fixed coregionalization weights αᵢⱼ with time-varying weights αᵢⱼ(t) and adding per-channel lags τᵢⱼ. Each observed channel is generated as a weighted sum of lagged latent GP functions: Cᵢ(t) = Σⱼ αᵢⱼ(t) lⱼ(t − τᵢⱼ). Three dynamic correlation mechanisms are provided: (a) smooth drift via AR(1) evolution of softmax-normalized logits with persistence ρ ∈ (0.9, 0.99); (b) regime switching via a Hidden Markov Model with K latent states, each having a fixed mean weight matrix perturbed by intra-regime noise; (c) lagged dependencies via integer lags τᵢⱼ ∼ Uniform[−τₘₐₓ, τₘₐₓ]. Three primary dataset variants (Drift, Lag, Regime Shift) plus a Combined variant and a Bayesian-optimized mixture (Combined BO using TPE) are constructed. Fine-tuning uses a progressive interleaved mixing schedule where the probability of sampling DynLMC data increases logarithmically over training, avoiding catastrophic forgetting of simpler inductive biases. Experiments fine-tune three architecturally distinct models—TimePFN, iTransformer, and Chronos-2—on DynLMC variants and evaluate zero-shot MAE across nine real benchmarks. Key Results: For iTransformer, the Regime Shift variant wins on all 9 benchmarks over the KS+LMC pretrained baseline, with notable MAE reductions on ECL (0.472→0.376), Traffic (0.676→0.610), and ETTm1 (0.563→0.513). The Combined variant achieves 8/9 wins. For TimePFN, Combined achieves 7/9 wins with improvements on ECL (0.369→0.356) and Weather (0.297→0.294). Gains are strongest on high-dimensional datasets (ECL: 321 channels, Traffic: 862 channels, Solar: 137 channels), consistent with the hypothesis that dynamic correlation modeling matters most when inter-channel structure is rich. Chronos-2 shows more modest results (3 wins at best with Regime Shift), attributed to the model already having been pretrained on real data and synthetic data with some dynamic inter-channel structure, reducing marginal benefit. The Combined BO variant achieves 6/9 wins for TimePFN vs. the naive Combined's 7/9, providing mixed evidence for Bayesian mixture optimization. Strengths: - Principled generalization of static LMC that directly targets the identified gap (correlation nonstationarity) with three well-motivated, interpretable mechanisms rather than a single black-box generator - Evaluation across three architecturally distinct FMTS backbones and nine diverse benchmarks, with results averaged over five seeds, substantially strengthening empirical credibility - Progressive mixing training strategy elegantly avoids curriculum staging while still implementing soft data-centric adaptation Weaknesses / Limitations: - Gains on Chronos-2 are inconsistent and occasionally negative, and the explanation (no access to original pretraining corpus) is a methodological gap that limits conclusions about whether DynLMC helps strong, real-data-pretrained models - The paper is a workshop paper (4 pages main content), so key ablations are absent: no analysis of sensitivity to DynLMC hyperparameters (ρ, K, τₘₐₓ), no comparison of DynLMC against other nonstationary generators, and no investigation of whether gains transfer to few-shot or in-distribution settings beyond zero-shot MAE - The Bayesian optimization mixing approach (Combined BO) does not consistently outperform naive equal-weight mixing, and the evaluation covers only TimePFN, raising questions about whether the added complexity is justified
Search Results 2604.05064 PDF
#98 — Innovative (63)

ClawBench: Can AI Agents Complete Everyday Online Tasks? (40 ▲ )

Yuxuan Zhang, Yubo Wang, Yipeng Zhu, Penghui Du, Junwen Miao, Xuan Lu, Wendong Xu, Yunzhuo Hao, Songcheng Cai, Xiaochen Wang, Huaisong Zhang, Xian Wu, Yi Lu, Minyi Lei, Kai Zou, Huifeng Yin, Ping Nie, Liang Chen, Dongfu Jiang, Wenhu Chen, Kelsey R. Allen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Apr 2026
Why this rank: The final-request HTTP interception approach elegantly solves the realism-versus-safety tradeoff that has confined prior benchmarks to sandboxes, and the stark performance gap it reveals — Claude Sonnet 4.6 drops from 65–75% on sandboxed benchmarks to 33.3% on live websites — is an important empirical finding for the field. The five-layer recording infrastructure enables diagnostic depth beyond binary pass/fail. However, inherent fragility of live-website evaluation limits longitudinal utility, and 153 tasks with some categories containing fewer than 8 tasks limits statistical reliability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing web-agent benchmarks evaluate models in offline sandboxes with static HTML, fixed DOM structures, no authentication, and no dynamic content — conditions that strip out the very challenges that make real-world web interaction hard. Benchmarks that do operate on live websites (WebVoyager, AssistantBench, Online-Mind2Web) are limited to read-only information retrieval. Write-heavy task completion on production websites — purchasing, booking, submitting applications — remains unevaluated. The result is a blind spot: strong benchmark scores (65–75% on OSWorld and WebArena) do not tell us whether agents can actually "get things done" on the real web. Methodology: CLAWBENCH is a benchmark of 153 everyday write-heavy online tasks spanning 144 live production platforms across 15 fine-grained categories (Daily Life, Shopping, Entertainment, Job Search, Travel, Finance, etc.). Tasks are each defined by a natural-language instruction, a starting URL, and a human-annotated HTTP interception signal — the specific endpoint, method, and payload schema that identifies the irreversible terminal submission. A lightweight Chrome extension paired with a CDP instrumentation server intercepts only this final request (blocking it before it reaches the server), while allowing all other traffic to pass unmodified, preserving ecological validity with zero real-world side effects. Every agent run produces five synchronized recording layers: session video (Xvfb + FFmpeg), per-step action screenshots, full HTTP traffic logs, agent reasoning traces/tool calls, and low-level browser actions (clicks, keystrokes, scrolls). Human annotators complete every task under the same infrastructure, yielding ground-truth reference trajectories. Evaluation is performed by an Agentic Evaluator — a Claude Code sub-agent — that explicitly aligns the agent trajectory against the human reference across all five evidence layers under a fixed rubric, producing a binary pass/fail verdict with step-level justification grounded in schema-level payload field checks. Key Results: Seven frontier models are evaluated. Claude Sonnet 4.6 is the top performer at only 33.3% overall success rate; GLM-5 follows at 24.2%, Gemini 3 Flash at 19.0%, and Claude Haiku 4.5 at 18.3%. GPT-5.4 scores a surprisingly low 6.5%, Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite 3.3%, and Kimi K2.5 just 0.7%. In contrast, Claude Sonnet 4.6 and GPT-5.4 achieve 65–75% on OSWorld-Verified and WebArena-Verified, illustrating that benchmark saturation on sandboxed environments does not transfer. Performance varies substantially by category: Claude Sonnet 4.6 leads on Finance (50%) and Academic (50%) but scores only 11.1% on Dev tasks, while Claude Haiku 4.5 leads on Dev (27.8%). No model reaches saturation in any category. The interception mechanism achieved 100% precision on all 153 human ground-truth runs with zero false positives on navigation traffic. Strengths: - The live-website execution with final-request interception is technically elegant: it preserves full real-world complexity (dynamic JS, cookie consent popups, authentication flows, anti-bot defenses) while guaranteeing zero side effects — a clean solution to the realism-vs-safety trade-off that prior work avoided by retreating to sandboxes. - The five-layer recording infrastructure and human-grounded comparative evaluation go well beyond binary pass/fail: failures are traceable to specific steps, specific form fields, and specific divergences from the human reference trajectory, providing actionable diagnostic signal for agent development. - Breadth of coverage (144 distinct live platforms across 15 categories) and the write-heavy task focus fill a concrete gap that no prior benchmark addresses; the benchmark is open-sourced with collection and evaluation tooling. Weaknesses / Limitations: - At only 153 tasks, the benchmark is small; statistical reliability of per-category results (some categories have as few as 3–8 tasks) is limited, and category-level performance rankings could be noisy. - The benchmark is inherently fragile over time: live websites change layouts, add CAPTCHAs, modify APIs, or go offline, making longitudinal comparisons difficult and requiring continuous maintenance effort that the paper acknowledges but does not fully address. - The agentic evaluator itself (a Claude Code sub-agent) introduces evaluator bias — results could systematically favor or penalize certain models, and the reliability of this LLM-based judge on multimodal trajectory alignment is validated only implicitly via the 100% interception precision statistic, not through inter-annotator agreement or evaluator accuracy studies.
Recommended 2604.08523 PDF
#99 — Innovative (63)

MZSGO: multimodal zero-shot protein function annotation via evolutionary signals and textual semantics

academic.oup.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The reformulation of protein function prediction as a pairwise protein–GO matching problem in a shared semantic space is a clean and effective solution to the structural zero-shot limitation of prior classifiers, yielding 8–10× improvements in unseen-label AUPR. However, the core idea of text-anchored zero-shot classification via shared embeddings is well-established in vision and NLP, and the model omits both GO graph structure and 3D structural information, leaving meaningful headroom.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Automated protein function prediction (AFP) faces a fundamental generalization barrier: existing deep learning methods treat Gene Ontology (GO) labels as opaque categorical IDs, suppressing any weight for unseen labels during training and making zero-shot prediction structurally impossible. Even multimodal methods like ProtGO and DPFunc encode modalities independently, failing to model semantic correspondence between sequence features and textual functional descriptions. As GO databases continuously expand with new terms, models that cannot generalize to novel labels are inadequate for real-world annotation workflows. The paper addresses this gap by framing AFP as a protein–GO matching problem in a shared semantic space, enabling inference for GO terms never seen during training. Methodology: MZSGO is a pairwise matching framework with four components. (1) Feature encoding: ESM2-650M extracts 1280-dimensional evolutionary embeddings from protein sequences via mean pooling over residue-level representations. Qwen3-Embedding-4B (a 4B-parameter text embedding model) encodes both protein domain descriptions (two-level mean pooling: token-level then domain-level aggregation) and GO term textual definitions into a shared 2560-dimensional semantic space. (2) Asymmetric modality dropout: During training, sequence and domain features are independently masked with probability p=0.15, with a forced-retention constraint ensuring at least one protein modality survives. GO embeddings are never dropped, reflecting the asymmetry that GO definitions are always available at inference while protein evidence may be incomplete. (3) Adaptive gated fusion: All three projected features (sequence, domain, GO) are concatenated into a global context vector, passed through a two-layer gate network to produce softmax attention weights, then each modality passes through a shared MLP transformation; the weighted sum is projected to a 256-dimensional fused representation. (4) Binary classification: An MLP head outputs a sigmoid compatibility score per protein–GO pair. Three ontology-specific models (BP, MF, CC) are trained separately using BCE loss (BP, MF) or Focal Loss (CC). Inference scores every candidate GO term against the query protein in a single batch operation. The zero-shot test set is constructed via strict temporal partitioning: training uses GO annotations from January 2023; zero-shot labels are GO terms that appear only in an October 2025 GO update, combined with protein sequences filtered to <30% sequence identity with training proteins via Diamond. Key Results: On the standard CAFA5-derived test set, MZSGO achieves best-in-class Fmax across all three ontologies: 0.5045 (BP), 0.7611 (MF), 0.7470 (CC), narrowly surpassing DPFunc (0.4984, 0.7495, 0.7386) and DeepGO-SE (0.5006, 0.7481, 0.7379). The decisive advantage emerges in zero-shot generalization. On Unseen AUPR — the ability to score GO terms absent from training — MZSGO achieves 0.2393 (BP), 0.4806 (MF), and 0.5862 (CC), compared to the best competing baseline ProtNote at 0.0303, 0.0490, and 0.2530 — roughly 8×, 10×, and 2.3× improvements. On the harmonic mean H balancing seen and unseen AUPR, MZSGO scores 0.3241 / 0.5821 / 0.6669 vs. ProtNote's 0.0550 / 0.0897 / 0.3659. On the dedicated true zero-shot test set (temporally novel 2025 GO terms), MZSGO achieves Fmax of 0.4881 / 0.5982 / 0.6610 vs. ProtNote's 0.3763 / 0.3599 / 0.2983. The CC zero-shot gap is most striking: MZSGO more than doubles ProtNote's Fmax while achieving precision of 0.5521 vs. ProtNote's 0.1853, indicating ProtNote suffers severe false positives from imprecise textual matching. Ablations confirm that removing semantic domain descriptions causes the worst degradation (ESM-only Unseen Fmax drops to 0.1793 on CC), gated fusion outperforms simple concatenation (H 0.6669 vs. 0.6000), and feature dropout provides a meaningful regularization benefit (removing it drops H from 0.6669 to 0.5714). Strengths: - The protein–GO pairwise matching formulation with text-anchored GO embeddings elegantly enables zero-shot inference for ontologically novel labels without architectural modification, a clean solution to a structural limitation of prior classifiers. - The temporally-partitioned zero-shot benchmark (GO 2023 train / GO 2025 test, <30% sequence identity filtering) is methodologically rigorous and more realistic than typical unseen-label evaluations that simply withhold rare training terms. - The asymmetric dropout design — dropping protein modalities but never GO definitions — is well-motivated by the actual asymmetry of deployment conditions and demonstrably improves generalization (H drops from 0.6669 to 0.5714 without it). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The model relies entirely on textual GO term definitions as semantic anchors, which are often terse and overlapping; the paper does not incorporate the rich DAG structure of GO (acknowledged as future work), leaving hierarchical consistency to emerge implicitly rather than being enforced. - The zero-shot test set for CC contains only 17 proteins and 6 novel labels, making CC zero-shot statistics statistically fragile despite the large reported gains; the BP zero-shot set (3,687 proteins, 34 labels) is more credible. - No 3D structural information is included, despite AlphaFold2 coverage of most Swiss-Prot proteins; the paper's own discussion notes structure remains a critical functional determinant, and competing methods like Struct2GO and DPFunc already exploit this signal.
Search Results
#100 — Innovative (63)

EmbedPart: Embedding-Driven Graph Partitioning for Scalable Graph Neural Network Training

Nikolai Merkel, Ruben Mayer, Volker Markl, Hans-Arno Jacobsen · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: EmbedPart offers a genuinely creative conceptual shift by repurposing GNN training-time embeddings for graph partitioning, converting an irregular graph problem into GPU-friendly dense clustering. The robust balance enforcement and support for dynamic graphs are practical advantages over streaming baselines. However, the meaningful quality gap versus METIS (59% vs. 75% edge-cut reduction) and the conflation of training cost with partitioning overhead limit the practical impact of the speed claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Distributed GNN training requires partitioning large graphs across machines to minimize inter-machine communication (cut edges) while balancing computational load. Existing approaches face a fundamental trade-off: high-quality in-memory partitioners like METIS are expensive (hours on billion-node graphs), prohibiting fast repartitioning and dynamic scaling; streaming partitioners are faster but produce poor-quality partitions and often violate balance constraints. All existing methods treat partitioning as a preprocessing step on irregular graph structures, which are hard to parallelize on GPUs. Methodology: EmbedPart reframes graph partitioning as a clustering problem in dense embedding space. Rather than partitioning before training, EmbedPart starts GNN training immediately and extracts node embeddings produced as a natural byproduct of the training workload. These embeddings encode both node features and local structural information via message passing. Phase 1 trains any GNN architecture (GraphSAGE, GAT, GCN) and extracts per-node embeddings. Phase 2 runs GPU-accelerated K-Means clustering (via FAISS with sampling-based centroids using 2^9 embeddings per cluster) on the dense embeddings to produce k partitions, followed by a lightweight rebalancing step that migrates low-degree vertices from overloaded to underloaded partitions proportional to free capacity. The same architecture supports graph reordering for single-machine training by assigning consecutive IDs to same-cluster vertices, improving cache locality. For dynamic graphs, GNN inductivity allows computing embeddings for new nodes without full retraining. Key Results: EmbedPart achieves 127–155× speedup over METIS and outperforms streaming partitioners (LDG: 61× over METIS; Cuttana: 1.78×) on partitioning time across four OGB/DGL datasets (arxiv 169K nodes, reddit 233K, products 2.4M, papers100M 111M nodes, up to 1.6B edges). On partitioning quality, EmbedPart-l-3 reduces edge-cut ratio by 67.71% over random partitioning—close to METIS (74.65%) and Spinner (69.81%)—while streaming methods score 56.46% and 42.14%. Critically, EmbedPart reliably enforces vertex balance ≤1.05, whereas Cuttana, LDG, and Spinner frequently violate this constraint (imbalances up to 3.5× on products). In end-to-end distributed training on 4 GPU servers, METIS leads at 2.55× speedup over random, with EmbedPart-v-3 at 2.01× and EmbedPart-v-2 at 1.92×, outperforming Spinner (1.84×) and LDG (1.41×). Even untrained GNNs (0 epochs) reduce edge-cut ratio by 7–53% over random due to inductive architectural biases. For graph reordering on CPU, EmbedPart achieves up to 1.212× training speedup, best among all methods on arxiv, products, and reddit. Strengths: - Elegant conceptual shift: repurposing training-time embeddings for partitioning eliminates a separate expensive preprocessing step, making the overhead negligible relative to training time - Robust balance enforcement: unlike streaming methods, EmbedPart reliably satisfies vertex balance ≤1.05, which is critical for avoiding OOM errors and stragglers in practice - Strong dynamic/iterative support: inductive GNN properties enable partition updates when graphs evolve or system scale changes, and embeddings are reusable across architectures Weaknesses / Limitations: - Partitioning quality gap vs. METIS remains meaningful: EmbedPart-v variants achieve only ~59% edge-cut reduction vs. METIS's 75%, and in distributed training METIS still leads by ~27% speedup (2.55× vs. 2.01×)—the claim of "competitive quality" is accurate but optimistic for quality-sensitive deployments - The evaluation conflates the cost of embedding computation (GNN training epochs) with partitioning overhead: since Phase 1 requires actual training, the claimed 127–155× speedup over METIS is only meaningful when training epochs are already planned; cold-start scenarios where even a few epochs of training are undesirable are not addressed - papers100M results show stagnant partitioning quality regardless of training epochs (edge-cut ratio ~0.49 for node prediction across all epochs), suggesting the method may be ineffective on certain graph structures—this outlier is not adequately analyzed
Citations 2604.01000 PDF
#101 — Innovative (63)

A Simple Baseline for Streaming Video Understanding (52 ▲ )

Yujiao Shen, Shulin Tian, Jingkang Yang, Ziwei Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 6 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Delivers a sharp, falsifiable negative result showing that a zero-memory, zero-training sliding-window baseline outperforms 13 published streaming video methods — a genuine reality check for the field. The perception-memory trade-off analysis is the most valuable methodological contribution, exposing that aggregate scores conflate distinct capabilities. However, the paper is deliberately minimal by design and does not propose or evaluate a solution, leaving its 'recent-first, history-on-demand' conjecture unimplemented.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Streaming video understanding systems must answer queries causally — using only observed video frames up to query time — under bounded memory and compute budgets. The prevailing design trend is to build increasingly complex memory mechanisms (explicit memory banks, KV cache retrieval, token compression, learned latent states) under the assumption that long-range history is necessary for strong performance. This paper challenges that assumption, asking whether such complexity is actually necessary or whether it obscures the true capability ceiling of modern VLMs. Methodology: The authors introduce SIMPLESTREAM, a deliberately minimal inference-time baseline with no architectural modifications, no training, and no memory module. Given a query at time t, SIMPLESTREAM feeds only the last N frames (N ∈ {2, 4, 8}) sampled at 1 fps to an off-the-shelf VLM (Qwen2.5-VL-7B or Qwen3-VL-8B). Frames outside the sliding window are discarded, keeping memory and compute O(N) regardless of stream length. The paper uses this baseline as a controlled reference to isolate how much performance is achievable from recent context alone, and conducts three complementary ablations: (1) recency-window size sweeps across N ∈ {2,4,8,16}; (2) model-scale sweeps across Qwen2.5-VL (3B–72B) and Qwen3-VL (2B–32B); and (3) Visual-RAG augmentation using a CLIP-indexed offline store with top-5 retrieved historical chunks appended at inference time. A perception-memory trade-off metric is defined: ΔP = RT_method − RT_SimpleStream and ΔM = ER_method − ER_SimpleStream (where ER = mean of EPM and ASI backward-tracing tracks). Key Results: On OVO-Bench, SIMPLESTREAM with Qwen3-VL-8B and 4 frames achieves 67.7% average accuracy, surpassing the strongest published streaming method HERMES by 8.5 pp (59.2%). The margin is largest on Real-Time Visual Perception: 81.4% vs. 69.0% for HERMES. On Backward Tracing, SIMPLESTREAM (8f) reaches 54.9% vs. 52.0% for StreamForest and 49.4% for HERMES. On StreamingBench, Qwen3-VL + 4f reaches 80.59%, also topping HERMES (79.44%). SIMPLESTREAM achieves the lowest peak GPU memory among all methods (flat ~15.5 GB regardless of stream length) and second-lowest TTFT (35/33/38 ms at 16/64/256 observed frames vs. HERMES's 27/29/29 ms). The recency-window ablation shows performance peaks at 4 frames and then declines: Real-Time accuracy drops from 81.4% (4f) to 77.9% (16f), revealing non-monotonicity. The model-scaling ablation shows that preferred window size varies by backbone family (e.g., Qwen2.5-VL-72B prefers 16f while Qwen2.5-VL-32B peaks at 4f), disconfirming a clean scaling law. The Visual-RAG ablation shows retrieval improves EPM (+7.1) and ASI (+6.1) but hurts Real-Time tracks OJR (−9.2), OCR (−8.1), ACR (−7.3), with overall accuracy dropping 2.3 pp — a clear perception-memory trade-off. Strengths: - The central empirical finding is sharp and falsifiable: a zero-extra-training, zero-memory-module baseline outperforms 13 published streaming methods, providing a concrete and reproducible reality check for the field. - The perception-memory trade-off analysis (ΔP vs. ΔM) is the paper's most valuable methodological contribution — it exposes that aggregate benchmark scores conflate two distinct capabilities, and that every external baseline incurs a perception penalty that aggregate scores obscure. - The benchmark critique is technically grounded: identifying that HLD (Hallucination Detection) is not a memory task, that OVO-Bench's 6:3 track imbalance structurally favors perception-heavy methods, and that this creates a double advantage for recent-context baselines — both from backbone alignment and evaluation protocol. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is entirely evaluated on two Qwen-family backbones; the authors acknowledge that SIMPLESTREAM's competitive performance may be partly explained by these models' already-exceptional short-range OCR, object recognition, and query-conditioned reasoning. The conclusion that complex memory is unnecessary could weaken substantially on older or architecturally different backbones. - SIMPLESTREAM by design cannot solve tasks requiring genuine long-horizon memory (e.g., questions about events from minutes ago in a long stream). The paper correctly scopes itself as a baseline study, but this means it does not close the problem it raises — it reframes it. The proposed 'recent-first, history-on-demand' direction remains a conjecture with no implementation or evaluation.
Recommended 2604.02317 PDF
#102 — Innovative (63)

From Business Events to Auditable Decisions: Ontology-Governed Graph Simulation for Enterprise AI

preprints.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The illusive accuracy metric is a genuinely novel conceptual contribution that exposes a blind spot in standard evaluation—architecturally correct simulation-grounded reasoning versus coincidentally correct outcomes. The F1=0.00 finding on basic traversal tasks for frontier models, verified by manual inspection, is a striking empirical result. The dual-mode skill/reasoning design with principled context complexity analysis adds further substance. However, the synthetic micro-scale benchmark (20-30 nodes), asymmetric fine-tuned vs. zero-shot comparison, and missing ablation isolating simulation enforcement from domain fine-tuning significantly weaken the generalizability claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: General-purpose LLM-based agent systems answer queries from an unrestricted knowledge space, ignoring the active business scenario conditions (carrier contracts, spending policies, org-scoped access rules) that define which portion of the enterprise knowledge graph is actually valid for any given decision. The result is fluent but ontologically ungrounded decisions with no audit trail. Injecting scenario conditions as prompt instructions fails because models treat them as soft preferences rather than hard constraints. The gap is invisible to standard accuracy metrics—a model can reach correct answers by coincidence while systematically bypassing simulation, producing no compliance evidence. The paper formalizes this as "illusive accuracy" and presents LOM-action to close it. Methodology: LOM-action introduces a strictly ordered three-phase pipeline—scenario parsing → sandbox simulation → decision derivation—backed by a persistent, isolated Neo4j graph sandbox keyed by session UUID. Phase 1 parses incoming business event payloads to extract EO-authorized constraint predicate sets R. Phase 2 applies R deterministically to a working copy of the enterprise ontology graph G, materializing the scenario-valid subgraph G_R (constraint case: node/edge deletion) or G_A (augmentation case: node/edge addition) without touching the persistent EO. Phase 3 executes decision tools (shortest_path, calculate_max_flow, check_graph_connectivity, etc.) exclusively against this evolved sandbox state, writing a fully replayable audit log. A dual-mode execution architecture handles two cases: skill mode (registered 19-function graph API suite invoked without loading raw graph into context) and reasoning mode (G_R fused, attribute-pruned, and loaded into context for novel computations not covered by any registered skill). The base model is Qwen3.5-27B fine-tuned via SFT on 2,200 synthetic Neo4j samples across 11 tasks using a simulation-ordered curriculum. Evaluation introduces tool-chain F1—order-sensitive matching of predicted vs. ground-truth tool-call sequences—and the illusive accuracy index IA(M) = Acc(M) − F1_chain(M) to distinguish simulation-grounded decisions from accidentally correct ones. Key Results: LOM-action achieves 93.82% accuracy and 98.74% tool-chain F1 on the 1,100-sample held-out benchmark. Zero-shot frontier baselines Doubao-1.8 and DeepSeek-V3.2 reach 80.00%/80.18% accuracy but only 24.42%/36.21% F1—a four-fold F1 gap. The illusive accuracy indices are 0.56 (Doubao), 0.44 (DeepSeek), and −0.05 (LOM-action). On basic traversal tasks (CONNECTIVITY, NEIGHBOR, PREDECESSOR, EDGE), both baselines score F1=0.00 despite ≥98% accuracy: manual inspection of 50 Doubao outputs confirms 47/50 produced correct answers with zero tool calls, pure parametric reasoning with no sandbox engagement. On the enterprise-critical scenario-simulation task fc_constraint_connection, LOM-action reaches 1.00 accuracy vs. 0.66/0.64 for baselines—a 34-point gap from Phase 2 bypass. On the hybrid Mode C task (delta_plus_one_coloring), LOM-action achieves F1=1.00 but Acc=0.46, showing skill-mode pipeline correctness and reasoning-mode algorithmic accuracy are separable failure modes. Strengths: - The illusive accuracy metric and simulation-first principle are a genuine and well-argued contribution: the paper cleanly separates architecturally correct behavior (simulation-grounded reasoning chains) from coincidentally correct outcomes, which standard benchmarks cannot distinguish. - The F1=0.00 finding on basic traversal tasks for frontier zero-shot models is empirically verified by manual inspection rather than inferred, giving it credibility as an architectural failure rather than a formatting or scoring artifact. - The dual-mode design (skill mode vs. reasoning mode with graph fusion) and the context complexity analysis (O(t·δ) vs. O(|H_t|)) provide a principled solution to the infinite-context accumulation problem in enterprise multi-turn AI, independently of the simulation contribution. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark uses synthetic Neo4j subgraphs of only 20–30 nodes and 30–60 edges; real enterprise ontologies operate at orders of magnitude greater scale with noise, inheritance hierarchies, and incomplete data. No evidence is provided that Phase 2 simulation accuracy or decision quality holds at that scale. - The comparison is inherently asymmetric (fine-tuned LOM-action vs. zero-shot frontiers), and the critical ablation—Qwen3.5-27B fine-tuned on the same 2,200 samples without the Phase 2 simulation curriculum—is absent. It is therefore impossible to isolate how much of the F1 advantage is architectural (simulation enforcement) vs. simply the result of domain fine-tuning. - The benchmark's 11 tasks are graph-theoretic and domain-narrow (graph connectivity, max flow, bipartite matching); only 2 tasks (fc_constraint_connection, fc_constraint_path) directly exercise enterprise scenario simulation, and the hybrid Task 11 is acknowledged as illustrative rather than derived from a deployed business process. Generalization to financial ledger, HR, or supply chain ontologies is entirely undemonstrated.
Citations
#103 — Innovative (63)

Communication-Efficient Collaborative LLM Inference over LEO Satellite Networks

Songge Zhang, Wen Wu, Liang Li, Ye Wang, Xuemin, Shen · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Scores 63 for applying pipeline-parallel LLM inference to the genuinely novel LEO satellite domain with a technically sound three-stage compression pipeline (Gumbel sparsification → quantization → entropy coding) achieving >20× compression at <1% accuracy loss, and an elegant MINLP-to-DAG reformulation with A* search. However, evaluation is limited to ViT image classification rather than autoregressive LLMs, which fundamentally undermines the paper's titular claim and leaves the hardest activation structure challenges (KV-cache, causal attention) unaddressed.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying large language models (LLMs) on LEO satellites for intelligent Earth observation tasks is constrained by limited onboard memory and excessive inference delay. Single-satellite deployment is often infeasible for billion-parameter models, and existing model compression techniques (pruning, quantization, sparsification) inevitably degrade accuracy. Prior work on model splitting typically addresses only two-node (device-server) splits, leaving the multi-satellite, heterogeneous-resource regime underexplored. In multi-split scenarios, load imbalance across heterogeneous satellites introduces idle/waiting time, and frequent inter-satellite activation transmissions inflate communication overhead substantially. Methodology: The paper proposes a three-component scheme. First, the LLM (specifically Vision Transformers) is partitioned into K sub-models deployed across a chain of computing satellites, with pipeline parallelism overlapping computation at satellite k with transmission of activations to satellite k+1, eliminating serial idle time. Second, an adaptive activation compression pipeline reduces inter-satellite transmission volume: a learnable Gumbel-mask module trained end-to-end with a Straight-Through Estimator generates near-binary sparsity masks that select informative activations; the retained elements are then quantized to low bit-width; finally, entropy-guided coding (Huffman) further compresses the quantized representation. The Gumbel temperature is annealed during training to transition from soft exploration to hard binary selection. Third, the joint optimization of layer assignment {lk} and per-stage compression ratios {qk} is formulated as a MINLP to minimize total pipeline inference delay subject to per-satellite memory budgets and a minimum accuracy constraint. This is reformulated as a shortest-path problem on a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where each path encodes a valid layer partition; an A*-based outer search over layer splits is coupled with an inner grid-search over discrete compression ratios per edge, with an admissible heuristic that lower-bounds remaining delay by assigning all remaining layers to the fastest satellite. Key Results: Experiments use a Walker Delta LEO constellation (12 satellites, 500 km altitude), four NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin devices emulating heterogeneous satellite compute (15W/30W/50W power modes), and ViT-B/L/H/G models on EuroSAT and RESISC45 remote sensing datasets. The proposed scheme reduces end-to-end inference delay by up to 42% compared to ground-only inference (even at 800 Mbit/s S2G rate) and over 100% at low downlink rates (0.2 Gbit/s). Communication overhead is reduced by 71.7% versus ground-only at 240p and 72.2% at 1080p. GumbelMask compression maintains accuracy within 1% of uncompressed baseline across all ViT sizes and datasets, while Top-k sparsification degrades by up to 4–5% on smaller models. The A*-based splitting achieves 2.03× speed-up over a capacity-proportional heuristic baseline on a 48-layer ViT-G across five heterogeneous satellites, converging within 120 iterations. Strengths: - The three-stage compression pipeline (Gumbel sparsification → quantization → entropy coding) achieves >20× overall activation compression while keeping accuracy loss below 1%, which is a practically meaningful constraint for deployment. - Reformulating the MINLP as a DAG shortest-path problem with A* and an admissible heuristic is technically sound and yields a globally optimal layer partition efficiently, avoiding exhaustive enumeration. - Experimental setup is unusually realistic: actual Jetson AGX Orin hardware emulating satellites at different power modes, free-space optical ISL parameters, and Ka-band S2G link modeling grounded in real constellation geometry.
