Weaknesses / Limitations: - The accuracy constraint is modeled by fitting a monotone regression on a uniform compression sweep (q1=…=qK-1=q), which is a coarse approximation that ignores layer-specific sensitivity; per-stage accuracy may diverge from this shared curve in practice, especially when split points vary significantly. - The scheme is evaluated only on image classification (ViT on EuroSAT/RESISC45); generalization to autoregressive LLMs (e.g., GPT-style models with KV-cache and causal attention) is not addressed, yet these are the models most commonly referred to as 'LLMs' and present fundamentally different activation structures. - The compression module requires offline training calibrated to a fixed satellite configuration; dynamic topology changes (satellite handoffs, link failures) or adapting to a new model without retraining are not discussed.
Search Results 2604.04654 PDF
#104 — Innovative (63)

3D-Stacked NMP, LLM Decoding, Systolic Array Microarchitecture, Multi-Core Scheduling

Chenyang Ai, Yixing Zhang, Haoran Wu, Yudong Pan, Lechuan Zhao, Wenhui OU · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: SNAKE makes a sharp and non-obvious diagnostic contribution: identifying that high NMP bandwidth paradoxically creates a compute-area bottleneck, then systematically addressing it through unified systolic-vector substrate co-design. The RTL-level analysis grounded in realistic 7nm packaging constraints and the 2.90× speedup over MAC-tree baselines are substantive. The serpentine reconfiguration mechanism achieving flexibility at only 6% area overhead is architecturally elegant. However, the contribution is highly specialized to 3D-stacked NMP and the non-iso-frequency comparison complicates clean attribution of gains.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM autoregressive decoding is severely memory-bandwidth-bound due to low arithmetic intensity (token-by-token generation with minimal weight reuse). 3D-stacked near-memory processing (NMP) architectures address this by placing compute logic on a logic die beneath stacked DRAM, achieving internal bandwidths of ~24 TB/s. However, the paper identifies a critical and underappreciated secondary bottleneck: this very high bandwidth shifts many decode operators from memory-bound back to compute-bound, yet prior 3D NMP designs (Stratum, Duplex) have compute-to-bandwidth ratios of only 3.7–8 FLOP/Byte—far below what is needed for batch sizes of 16–64. The root cause is the severely constrained logic-die area (~76–121 mm²), which limits deployable compute. Prior work universally used MAC-tree-based compute units, which are area-inefficient at scale due to high-fanout operand delivery paths and hierarchical reduction networks. The paper makes the case that the compute microarchitecture itself—not just DRAM organization—is the key design lever for 3D NMP LLM decoding. Methodology: The authors propose SNAKE, a reconfigurable systolic array (SA) microarchitecture co-designed with a multi-core scheduling framework for 3D-stacked NMP. Three principal insights drive the design. First, systolic arrays are inherently more area-efficient than MAC trees (RTL experiments show 8.23× area advantage at matched compute). Second, LLM decode operators exhibit substantial GEMM shape diversity (M ≪ N, K), requiring reconfigurability in both array shape and dataflow (output-stationary OS vs. input-stationary IS); a fixed-shape array leaves many PEs idle. Third, the high local bandwidth reduces the need for large on-chip SRAM buffers, freeing area for more PEs; a buffer-to-compute reallocation study shows an 8×512 elongated PE configuration (vs. a more balanced 8×128) is optimal. The key architectural innovation is a unified systolic-vector substrate: rather than placing a vector core beside the SA, SNAKE reuses the vector core's multi-ported buffering and fine-grained control logic for SA reconfiguration, dramatically reducing the area overhead of flexibility. The physical 64×64 PE array is logically remapped via a "SNAKE-like" serpentine traversal into elongated shapes (e.g., 8×512 or 16×256) matching the small-M decode operator tiles. A shared 2R/2W output buffer eliminates redundant storage between the SA and vector core. At the multi-core level, the paper proposes an operator-aware scheduling framework combining spatial (IS-S, OS-S) and spatio-temporal (IS-ST, OS-ST) partitioning over the N and K dimensions across 16 processing units (PUs), with per-operator strategy selection chosen by lightweight simulation. The design is implemented in SystemVerilog using the 7 nm ASAP7 PDK, with SRAM modeled via FinCACTI and performance via Scale-Sim v3 extended for 3D NMP. Key Results: Against the MAC-tree baseline (Stratum architecture, 24 TB/s DRAM bandwidth, 8-device TP=8 system on OPT 66B, LLaMA3 70B, Mixtral 8×22B, Qwen3 30B-A3B, DeepSeek 236B): SNAKE achieves an average 2.90× speedup and 2.40× higher energy efficiency across both dense and MoE models. Compute-area efficiency improves 4.00× over the MAC tree. Against a fixed-shape 48×48 SA, SNAKE achieves 2.33× speedup and 1.05× higher energy efficiency; against a fixed-shape 8×288 SA, 3.00× speedup and 1.31× energy efficiency. Against GPU-only decode (H100), SNAKE achieves 11.47× speedup and 5.74× energy efficiency. In serving latency experiments, MAC-tree E2E latency is 1.1–2.3× that of SNAKE; GPU E2E latency is 1.5–3.0×; and fixed-shape SA TBT latency can reach 1.5–4.5× SNAKE's. Per-operator adaptive scheduling vs. best fixed strategy yields up to 6.43× slowdown for MoE models (Qwen3 30B-A3B) when using a single fixed policy, confirming the value of per-operator scheduling. The design operates at 800 MHz (vs. 1 GHz for baselines) within a 62 W thermal budget, with buffering area shrunk from 53.6% (SA + vector core baseline) to 28.1%. Strengths: - The paper makes a sharp and non-obvious diagnosis: that high NMP bandwidth is not purely beneficial—it creates a new compute-area bottleneck—and then systematically addresses it through co-design of microarchitecture and scheduling. - The RTL-level area analysis (ASAP7 7 nm) and thermal modeling (HotSpot) ground the design in realistic packaging constraints, unlike prior work that assumes unrealistically large logic dies. - The unified systolic-vector substrate is architecturally elegant: reusing existing vector-core control and buffering for SA reconfigurability achieves flexibility at low marginal area cost (only 6% overhead from reconfiguration muxes/registers), with the area savings elsewhere more than compensating. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The comparison is not iso-frequency: SNAKE runs at 800 MHz while baselines run at 1 GHz, making the raw speedup numbers somewhat favorable to SNAKE and complicating clean attribution of gains to architectural improvements vs. thermal/power budget redistribution. - The minimum reconfiguration granularity of M=8 means GEMV-like operators with M=1 (batch=1, single-request latency) are poorly matched; the paper acknowledges these remain memory-bound but does not quantify the residual performance penalty for latency-critical single-user scenarios. - MoE expert routing is modeled with uniform distribution, which does not capture the load imbalance and activation sparsity patterns of real MoE serving; results on Qwen3 and DeepSeek may be optimistic.
Search Results 2604.04253 PDF
#105 — Innovative (63)

Communicating about Space: Language-Mediated Spatial Integration Across Partial Views (7 ▲ )

Ankur Sikarwar, Debangan Mishra, Sudarshan Nikhil, Ponnurangam Kumaraguru, Aishwarya Agrawal · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 6 Apr 2026
Why this rank: COSMIC identifies a genuinely underexplored and practically important problem — distributed spatial reasoning through natural language communication between agents with partial views — and the three-level diagnostic task hierarchy cleanly isolates failure modes in a way that generic benchmarks cannot. The human baseline collection and single-agent ablation are methodologically strong. However, as a purely evaluative benchmark with no proposed method or training intervention, its contribution is diagnostic rather than generative.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Humans routinely build shared spatial understanding by communicating partial, egocentric observations — e.g., two people locating each other in an unfamiliar park by describing local landmarks. As MLLMs are increasingly deployed in collaborative, embodied, and multi-user settings (robotics, AR/VR, multi-agent systems), the question of whether they can do the same becomes critical. Prior spatial reasoning benchmarks test single-model perception over one or multiple views but neglect the fundamentally harder distributed case: two agents, each with only a partial view, must coordinate through natural language to build a shared, allocentric spatial model. This gap motivates COSMIC. Methodology: The authors introduce COSMIC (Collaborative Spatial Communication), a diagnostic benchmark with 899 procedurally generated 3D indoor scenes (built on Infinigen) and 1,250 multiple-choice question–answer pairs. Two static MLLM agents — an Answerer and a Helper — each observe a distinct egocentric RGB view of the same room and exchange natural-language messages over multiple turns to jointly answer spatial queries. Five tasks span three levels of difficulty: Object-level (Anchor Recognition, Global Counting), Relation-level (Relative Distance, Relative Direction), and Map-level (Cognitive Mapping). All tasks are posed as 4-choice MCQ (Cognitive Mapping as binary), with carefully constructed distractors. To contextualize model behavior, 250 human–human dialogues are collected on a COSMIC-HUMAN subset. Models evaluated include frontier closed-source models (Gemini-3-Pro-Thinking, GPT-5.2-Thinking) and open-source models (Qwen3-VL-32B, InternVL3.5-38B, Gemma-3-27B) in zero-shot settings. A thinking-vs-no-thinking ablation is included for GPT-5.2 and Gemini-3-Flash. A single-agent baseline (both views given simultaneously, no dialogue) isolates the overhead of communication itself. Key Results: The best model, Gemini-3-Pro-Thinking, reaches 71.82% aggregate accuracy versus 95.22% for human pairs — a gap of over 23 percentage points. The capability hierarchy is stark: models perform reasonably on Anchor Recognition (Gemini-3-Pro-Thinking: 91.99%, GPT-5.2-Thinking: 76.83%) but collapse on Cognitive Mapping, where even frontier models fall near the 50% chance baseline while humans achieve 94%. Closed-source models consistently outperform open-source; among open-source, only Qwen3-VL shows meaningful scale gains (8B: 40.08% → 32B: 52.47%), while Gemma-3 and InternVL3.5 show no significant improvement with scale. Thinking capability yields consistent, statistically significant gains on Anchor Recognition and Relative Distance but provides zero benefit on Relative Direction or Cognitive Mapping, indicating the bottleneck at higher levels is not reasoning depth but geometric understanding. Communication itself is costly: Qwen3-VL-32B drops from 64.62% (single-agent) to 52.62% (two-agent); Gemini-3-Flash-Thinking drops from 78.38% to 68.07%. Human pairs average 199.65 words per conversation; MLLMs average 438.48 words with no correlation between verbosity and accuracy (r=0.37, p=0.26). Dialogue repair rate: humans 79.31%, best MLLM 28.04%. Failure mode analysis on 150 Gemini-3-Pro-Thinking failures identifies cross-view grounding failures as the dominant category (46.09%), followed by geometric/relational failures (dominant on higher-level tasks: 57.33% on Relative Direction, 69.44% on Cognitive Mapping), with perceptual failures the smallest share (19.70%). Strengths: - The benchmark is carefully designed to be diagnostic rather than holistic — the three-level task hierarchy (Object → Relation → Map) isolates where failures arise, enabling actionable analysis rather than just a leaderboard number. - The human baseline collection (250 dialogues) is a methodological strength that enables rigorous qualitative and quantitative comparison of communication strategies, revealing the convergence/verbosity gap and low dialogue repair rates that would otherwise be opaque. - The single-agent vs. two-agent ablation cleanly attributes performance loss to communication overhead, a contribution distinct from prior multi-view benchmarks. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All scenes are procedurally generated (Infinigen), which may not capture the full diversity and clutter of real indoor environments; it is unclear how well findings transfer to real-world deployment. - The benchmark is purely evaluative with no proposed method or training recipe to close the identified gaps; the paper diagnoses the problem thoroughly but offers no concrete intervention. - Cognitive Mapping is framed as binary map verification (correct/incorrect top-down map) rather than free-form map generation, which avoids the evaluation problem but also limits the richness of what can be measured about allocentric representation quality.
Recommended 2603.27183 PDF
#106 — Innovative (63)

Vanast: Virtual Try-On with Human Image Animation via Synthetic Triplet Supervision (30 ▲ )

Hyunsoo Cha, Wonjung Woo, Byungjun Kim, Hanbyul Joo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Vanast introduces a genuinely useful unified framework for video virtual try-on plus animation, with the synthetic triplet data pipeline being the most novel contribution — the insight that reference images must show different clothing is non-obvious and well-executed. The dual-module architecture atop a frozen DiT backbone is clean engineering, though the core diffusion components are standard. Solid results across metrics, but modest dataset scale and small evaluation sets temper the impact claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Generating a video of a person wearing a target garment — given only a single human image, garment images, and a pose guidance video — requires jointly solving virtual try-on and human animation. Existing approaches decompose this into two sequential stages (image-based try-on followed by pose-driven animation), which introduces identity drift, garment distortion, front–back inconsistency across viewpoints, and compounding errors at inference. No unified single-stage framework existed, and no public dataset provided the required triplet structure (human image in different clothes, garment image, and video of the person wearing the garment in motion). Methodology: Vanast is a unified end-to-end video diffusion transformer framework trained on synthetically constructed triplet supervision. The core challenge in building training data is that the human reference image must depict the person in different clothing than the video garment, otherwise the model learns motion reenactment rather than garment transfer. The authors solve this via three data pipelines: (1) FLUX-based diffusion inpainting to generate identity-preserving images of a person wearing alternative garments, using Qwen2.5-VL for frame selection, SDXL to generate auxiliary pose-matched images for mask creation, and LLM-generated garment prompts for diversity; (2) extraction of complete (human, garment, video) triplets from in-the-wild videos without paired catalog images, using VLM-based frame scoring and segmentation; (3) a newly captured studio dataset covering simultaneous upper and lower garment triplets. The model architecture introduces a Dual Module design atop a frozen text-to-video DiT backbone (WAN): a Human Animation Module (HAM) conditioned on the human image and DWPose keypoint video, and a Garment Transfer Module (GTM) conditioned on garment images. HAM and GTM outputs are injected additively into every other backbone block with fixed weights (α=β=0.5). The backbone remains frozen; only HAM and GTM are trained. Zero-shot garment interpolation is enabled by linearly blending GTM block outputs for two garments with a scalar γ. Key Results: On both evaluation sets (80 Internet samples, 50 ViViD samples), Vanast outperforms all 16 two-stage pipeline combinations across nearly all metrics. Against the best subject-to-image+animation baseline (VACE 1-stage): L1 improves from 0.1453 to 0.0719 (Internet) and 0.1733 to 0.1077 (ViViD); PSNR improves from 13.09 to 17.95 dB and 11.62 to 14.67 dB; LPIPS improves from 0.4052 to 0.2370 and 0.5363 to 0.3649; FID improves from 115.40 to 91.05 and 134.96 to 105.89. Against the best virtual try-on+animation baseline (CatVTON+DisPose on SSIM), Vanast is competitive on SSIM but substantially better on L1, PSNR, LPIPS, FID, and VFID. Ablation confirms each component is necessary: removing the dual-module design (single module) degrades pose control, backbone fine-tuning with LoRA fails garment transfer, and training without synthesized alternate-clothing human images (w/o SynthHuman) causes the model to revert to motion reenactment. Strengths: - The data generation pipeline is the most technically novel contribution: the insight that the reference human image must show different clothing is non-obvious yet critical, and the three-pronged synthetic triplet construction (inpainting, in-the-wild extraction, studio capture) is well-engineered and scalable. - The Dual Module architecture cleanly separates pose and garment conditioning into independent pathways rather than competing through a shared module, producing measurably better pose adherence and garment fidelity with fast convergence by freezing the strong T2V backbone prior. - Zero-shot garment interpolation and multi-garment (upper+lower simultaneously) transfer emerge naturally from the modular GTM design without any task-specific fine-tuning, demonstrating genuine generalization. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Training data scale is modest (9,135 videos), and the evaluation sets are small (80 + 50 samples), making it difficult to assess generalization to highly diverse body shapes, complex garments (e.g., structured outerwear, full dresses with intricate patterns), or significant viewpoint changes requiring back-of-garment synthesis. - The fixed equal weighting of HAM and GTM (α=β=0.5) is a heuristic with no ablation; edge cases where pose and garment conditioning conflict (e.g., very loose garments under rapid motion) may require adaptive balancing that the current architecture cannot provide.
Recommended 2604.04934 PDF
#107 — Innovative (62)

TDMM-LM: Bridging Facial Understanding and Animation via Language Models

Luchuan Song, Pinxin Liu, Haiyang Liu, Zhenchao Jin, Yolo Yunlong Tang, Zichong Xu, Susan Liang, Jing Bi, Jason J Corso, Chenliang Xu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: TDMM-LM contributes a useful formulation of 3D facial motion as a language modeling problem and the Open3DFaceVid dataset with 187 emotion labels fills a genuine data gap. However, the geometry tokenization approach follows the increasingly common pattern of discretizing continuous signals for LLM consumption, and evaluation on synthetic test data sharing the training distribution limits confidence in real-world generalization.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Text-guided facial animation lags significantly behind full-body motion generation due to two compounding problems: (1) existing video-language models process frames as hundreds of image tokens, causing temporal sparsity that destroys subtle facial dynamics (micro-expressions, lip twitches, brow raises) when frames are downsampled; and (2) real-world training corpora are overwhelmingly neutral-expression-dominated (YouTube, TikTok, VoxCeleb), leaving models with a narrow, low-variance facial prior. Existing labeled datasets like MEAD offer only 8 coarse one-hot emotion labels and studio-controlled subjects, lacking the open-form text annotations needed for language-driven modeling. This creates a fundamental gap: no large-scale, class-balanced, richly text-annotated corpus of 3D facial motion exists. Methodology: The paper addresses the data gap by introducing Open3DFaceVid, a synthetic corpus of ~80 hours (57.2K clips, 4–6s each) generated by prompting multiple T2V models (Wan2.1/2.2, HuMo, Open-Sora, Veo2/3) with a curated lexicon of ~200 emotion and facial-action descriptors (grin, pout, smirk, squint, etc.), ensuring class balance across 187 labels. Per-frame FLAME 3DMM parameters (expression codes, head yaw/roll/pitch) are extracted via monocular regression, and ~10K wild clips annotated by Gemini are appended to narrow the synthetic-to-real gap. Building on this data, the authors train a Geometry VQ-VAE that discretizes 3DMM sequences into a compact codebook by operating in mesh space (vertex positions) rather than raw coefficient space, avoiding many-to-one mapping ambiguities where similar expressions map to different coefficient patterns. Each video frame is encoded as a single geometry token—replacing 300–500 image tokens—drastically reducing context length while preserving temporal continuity. Two complementary tasks are then trained on the same LLM: (1) Motion2Language fine-tunes a Qwen3 backbone to consume geometry-token sequences and produce free-form natural-language descriptions of emotion, intensity, and head dynamics; (2) Language2Motion uses an autoregressive transformer conditioned on word-level T5 embeddings to predict geometry token sequences from text prompts, enabling single-keyword control over emotional style and intensity. Key Results: For Motion2Language on geometry inputs, the model outperforms HumanOmni and Gemini-2.5 VLM across all GPT-4-judged and human-rated metrics: emotion correctness 4.02 vs. 2.45 (Gemini), motion correctness 3.35 vs. 2.91, intensity 3.63 vs. 3.51, human emotion rating 4.29 vs. 2.88. On natural image inputs (a fairer comparison for VLMs), Gemini-2.5 leads on emotion (4.21 vs. 4.02), but the proposed method uses only 1 token per frame versus hundreds for Gemini, a major efficiency advantage. For Language2Motion, the model outperforms T2M-GPT on all metrics: L2 0.219 vs. 0.226, Fréchet Distance 31.75 vs. 37.04, token accuracy 0.920 vs. 0.895, expression correctness 4.13 vs. 3.57, and human rating 3.95 vs. 3.91. Dataset ablations confirm that Open3DFaceVid (187 labels, well-separated t-SNE clusters) significantly outperforms MEAD (8 labels) and Gemini-annotated YouTube (37 labels, heavy neutral bias) in both tasks. Scaling experiments show Motion2Language saturates at 4B–8B parameters, while Language2Motion continues benefiting from larger backbones. Strengths: - First unified framework to cast 3D facial parameter modeling as a language problem, enabling both semantic understanding and controllable generation with a single LLM backbone. - Geometry tokenization achieves ~300–500× token compression versus image-based VLMs while preserving subtle temporal facial dynamics, making it both efficient and effective for long sequences. - Open3DFaceVid provides by far the richest facial-motion dataset available (187 emotion labels, class-balanced, with both text and 3DMM ground truth), which is likely to have standalone value for the community. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method is evaluated primarily on synthetic test clips that share the same T2V generation distribution as training data; generalization to diverse real-world video (e.g., extreme occlusions, non-frontal poses, in-the-wild lighting) is not demonstrated. - On natural image inputs—the setting most relevant to real deployment—Gemini-2.5 outperforms the model on emotion understanding (4.21 vs. 4.02 CorE), and the paper uses a circular evaluation for Language2Motion correctness (the trained Motion2Language model scores its own sibling task). - Camera constraints during dataset synthesis ("frame remains steady, head and shoulders centered") limit head-motion diversity; the ablation acknowledges this results in narrower CorM scores compared to YouTube, potentially limiting utility for dynamic talking-head applications.
Recommended 2603.16936 PDF
#108 — Innovative (62)

REAM: Merging Improves Pruning of Experts in LLMs

Saurav Jha, Maryam Hashemzadeh, Ali Saheb Pasand, Ali Parviz, Min-Joong Lee, Boris Knyazev · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 62 for the elegant pseudo-pruning concept that recovers pruning's structural benefits while preserving expert knowledge through selective merging, and for the non-obvious empirical finding that math calibration data has near-zero correlation with MoE compression performance. However, the core contribution is a careful engineering combination of existing components (REAP saliency, permutation alignment, sequential recomputation) rather than a fundamentally new approach, and the surfaced MC-GEN trade-off remains unsolved.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs achieve strong performance by routing each token to a small subset of experts, but all experts must reside in memory regardless of activation sparsity, creating significant deployment challenges for models with hundreds of billions of parameters. Prior work split into two camps: pruning (removes low-saliency experts) and merging (combines similar experts). REAP, the state-of-the-art pruning approach, discards experts entirely, potentially losing useful knowledge for tasks outside the calibration domain. Existing merging methods, meanwhile, suffer from poor grouping strategies and a known gate-weight error when logits are summed across merged groups. REAM bridges this gap by preserving all expert knowledge through merging while mimicking pruning's structural properties. Methodology: REAM introduces four components that together outperform both paradigms. (1) Aggregated similarity: expert pair similarity is computed as the sum of gate logit similarity (capturing routing co-activation) and gated expert output similarity (outputs scaled by softmax router scores, making the metric aware of expert specialization). (2) Pseudo-pruning: the top-N′ most salient experts (using REAP's router-weighted activation saliency score) become protected centroids; remaining experts are greedily absorbed into the nearest centroid up to a fixed group size C. Because N−N′ ≪ N′·C in practice, most centroids receive no assignments and remain singletons — behaviorally mimicking pruning while allowing absorption of redundant experts. Gate weight rows for non-centroid experts are removed, avoiding the irreducible gate-summing error of prior merging methods. (3) Combined cost matrix: neuron permutation alignment for weight averaging uses a combined activation-based and weight-based cost, preventing spurious matches that coincidentally share activations but differ in weights. (4) Sequential merging: rather than collecting all layer statistics from the frozen original model, REAM re-runs a forward pass through each MoE layer after it is merged, so subsequent layers see realistic inputs. This adds ~50% wall-clock time (~1 hour → ~1.5 hours for Qwen3-30B) but significantly improves accuracy. Key Results: Experiments cover Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct (128 experts), Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct and Qwen3-Coder-Next (512 experts), and GLM-4.5-Air (128 experts, 106B parameters), under 25% and 50% compression. At 25% compression on Qwen3-30B (128→96 experts), REAM achieves a GEN average of 69.8 vs. REAP's 68.6 and the uncompressed baseline of 70.9, closing ~90% of the gap. On Qwen3-Coder-Next (512→384 experts), REAM exactly matches the original GEN score of 72.9. Across all three larger models, REAM consistently outperforms REAP on GEN. A critical finding is a fundamental MC (multiple-choice) vs. GEN (generative) trade-off driven entirely by calibration data composition: C4 (general text) proportion strongly predicts MC scores (r≈+0.95) but suppresses GEN (r≈−0.82); code data shows the reverse; math proportion has near-zero correlation with both (|r|≤0.19), suggesting mathematical reasoning is diffusely distributed across experts. In Pareto hypervolume analysis over 10 calibration mixtures, REAM achieves the highest hypervolume (HV=920.3, n/10=7/10) at 25% compression, dominating all baselines across most MC floors. Ablations show the REAP saliency score is the single most important component (removing it costs ΔAVG=−8.7), followed by gated expert similarity (ΔAVG=−5.9) and pseudo-pruning (ΔAVG=−3.6). Sequential merging and gate logit similarity each contribute smaller consistent gains (~−1.0 to −1.4). Strengths: - The pseudo-pruning strategy is conceptually elegant: it recovers the structural benefits of pruning (few large groups, many untouched singletons, clean gate weight removal) while still preserving knowledge from redundant experts through weighted merging. - The Pareto hypervolume analysis is a rigorous and practically motivated evaluation framework that goes beyond single-point comparisons, revealing that REAM's advantage is not confined to one lucky calibration ratio but holds broadly across the calibration space. - The empirical discovery that math calibration data has near-zero correlation with benchmark performance is a non-obvious finding with implications for how practitioners should construct calibration sets for MoE compression. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The revealed MC–GEN trade-off is a problem REAM surfaces but does not solve: no single calibration mixture simultaneously maximizes both metrics, and the paper offers no automatic way to find the optimal mixture without exhaustive search across 10+ configurations. - Evaluation is limited to Qwen3 and GLM families; it is unclear whether REAM's gains over REAP generalize to architecturally different MoEs (e.g., Mixtral, DeepSeek-V3) with different expert granularities or routing mechanisms. - The method requires running calibration forward passes through large models (up to 512 experts per layer) and sequential per-layer recomputation, making it computationally heavier at compression time than simple pruning, which may matter for very large-scale or iterative deployment pipelines.
Recommended 2604.04356 PDF
#109 — Innovative (62)

Exploring Silent Data Corruption as a Reliability Challenge in LLM Training

Anton Altenbernd, Philipp Wiesner, Odej Kao · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This paper provides the first systematic, controlled characterization of silent data corruption during LLM training, filling a genuine gap between anecdotal industry reports and actionable understanding. The per-bit, per-kernel sensitivity analysis and the discovery of the gradient-norm-clipping failure mode (infinity norm zeroing all gradients across replicas) are meaningful contributions. However, the detection metric is a relatively straightforward application of optimizer statistics monitoring, and the experimental scope (single-GPU, up to 1.3B parameters) leaves the most operationally critical distributed-training scenario unvalidated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Silent Data Corruption (SDC) — hardware faults that bypass Error-Correction Codes (ECCs) — poses a growing reliability threat to LLM pretraining. Unlike overt hardware failures, SDCs produce incorrect outputs (e.g., bit flips in floating-point values) without triggering system alerts, potentially corrupting gradients, stalling training, or silently degrading model quality over many steps. Industry reports from Google (Gemini: one SDC event every 1–2 weeks), Meta (six incidents in a 54-day LLaMA 3 run), ByteDance, and DeepSeek confirm this is a real operational concern at scale. Despite its importance, SDC during LLM training lacks a systematic characterization and practical detection methodology — this paper addresses that gap. Methodology: The authors use NVBit, a dynamic binary instrumentation framework for NVIDIA GPUs, to inject controlled intermittent faults at the SASS instruction level during LLaMA pretraining (60M, 350M, 1.3B parameters; C4 dataset; AdamW with bfloat16). Faults are injected into Half-Precision Matrix-Multiply Accumulate (HMMA) instructions inside GEMM kernels, which account for 42.8%–96.6% of LLM training compute. The injection framework applies bit-flipping bitmasks to input registers before instruction execution and restores them after, confining corruption to computation without altering memory state. Experiments sweep bit positions (0–31), kernel functions (5 forward + 10 backward GEMM kernels in the 60M model), lanes, Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs), injection rates, and fault durations. The paper then proposes a lightweight detection metric R_t, defined as the maximum RMS of the AdamW adaptive update across parameter groups, and flags anomalies by monitoring |ΔR_t| relative to a warm-up baseline scaled by an auxiliary gradient-norm signal. Upon anomaly detection, the most recent step is recomputed; non-reproducibility between the original and recomputed ΔR_t confirms hardware corruption. Key Results: Only exponent bits in bfloat16 produce measurable impact; mantissa and sign bits do not. High-order exponent bits cause NaN propagation; mid-range exponent bits (positions 10–13) cause loss spikes and parameter divergence. The backward pass is far more sensitive than the forward pass: backward-pass faults produce gradient norm spikes (up to infinity) and persistent parameter shifts, while forward-pass faults are largely transient. Spatial location (lane, SM) has negligible effect on outcome — any lane or SM yields similar corruption magnitude. Fault rate and duration both scale impact: injecting every step yields evaluation loss of 7.38 vs. 4.30 baseline (60M, 1,000 steps); injecting every 100 steps drops the delta to near-zero. Over 10,000 training steps with fault injection every 100 steps (1–5 step duration): the 60M model degrades from 3.50 to 3.61, the 350M from 3.25 to 3.37, and the 1.3B from 3.26 to 3.34 in evaluation loss. With the proposed detection and recomputation (α=0.05), evaluation loss returns to baseline values (3.50, 3.25, 3.26 respectively) across all three model scales. Runtime overhead of detection is ~1% (0.01 s/it for both 60M and 1.3B). Each undetected injected fault costs approximately 30–40 equivalent training steps. The paper also identifies an unintended failure mode: when gradient norm reaches infinity, gradient norm clipping zeros all gradients, causing a momentum-only optimizer step — a risk that is amplified in data-parallel training where a single faulty replica can zero the aggregated gradient across thousands of workers. Strengths: - Rigorous controlled experimental design with fixed seeds, deterministic training, and per-bit, per-kernel sensitivity sweeps — providing clean causal attribution of corruption effects not achievable through production incident analysis alone. - The detection mechanism is lightweight (1% overhead), optimizer-statistics-based, model-architecture-agnostic, and compatible with distributed training — properties that make it practically deployable without changes to model code or ABFT instrumentation of specific operations. - The identification of the gradient-norm-clipping failure mode (∞ norm → zero clipped gradient → momentum-only update discarding all replica work) is a non-obvious and practically important finding with direct implications for distributed training design. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are strictly single-GPU; distributed training effects — gradient aggregation propagating corruption across replicas, and the interaction of per-worker vs. post-aggregation detection — are discussed but not empirically evaluated, leaving the most operationally relevant scenario unvalidated. - Model scale tops out at 1.3B parameters with only 3 seeds, which is orders of magnitude smaller than production LLMs (100B+); whether the detection metric's behavior and the α=0.05 threshold generalize to much larger models with different optimizer dynamics is unknown. - The fault model is restricted to GEMM/HMMA instructions and does not cover faults in attention softmax, layer norm, embedding lookups, or communication primitives, limiting completeness of the SDC characterization.
Citations 2604.00726 PDF
#110 — Innovative (61)

Two-Stage Optimizer-Aware Online Data Selection for Large Language Models

Fangxin Wang, Peyman Baghershahi, Langzhou He, Henry Peng Zou, Sourav Medya, Philip S. Yu · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: A technically thorough unification of optimizer-awareness, redundancy penalization, and online sequential selection into a coherent formulation with solid second-order justification. The factorized gradient representation scaling as O(d₁+d₂) is a practical contribution for long-context LLMs. However, the core ideas—gradient matching, Adam preconditioning, greedy subset selection—are individually well-established, and the experiments are limited to ≤1B models on only two benchmarks, leaving scalability and generality unclear.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Gradient-based data selection provides a principled way to estimate sample utility during LLM fine-tuning, but existing methods were designed for offline settings where the full dataset is available upfront. In online fine-tuning—where data arrives sequentially (e.g., continual instruction tuning)—these methods have three critical gaps: (1) sample utility is step-dependent, so static gradient scores go stale; (2) most methods assume SGD dynamics and ignore the non-linear geometry induced by Adam; and (3) additive, per-sample scoring fails to account for redundancy among jointly selected samples. The paper addresses all three gaps with a unified online framework. Methodology: The core idea is to reframe online data selection as an optimizer-aware update-matching problem: find a weighted subset whose optimizer-transformed gradient best approximates the validation gradient at each step. Formally, they minimize ||∇l_val − P_t(Σ w_i ∇l_i)||² + λ||w||², where P_t is the Adam preconditioner. This non-additive, distance-based objective naturally penalizes redundant gradients via pairwise interaction terms, with a principled second-order justification. Since Adam's preconditioner depends on the current batch selection (making exact optimization intractable), they linearize it using the previous step's second-moment estimate, then transfer the preconditioner to the validation side—so selection operates in the optimizer-induced feature space without changing the training-side computation. The resulting two-stage Filter-then-Weight algorithm (1) greedily identifies geometrically diverse candidates using residual pursuit with unit weights, then (2) globally solves a Non-Negative Least Squares (NNLS) problem over the candidate set to assign continuous weights. Non-negativity is enforced to prevent destructive gradient cancellation. For computational tractability at LLM scale, they combine LoRA fine-tuning with random projection and a factorized outer-product gradient representation (exploiting the rank-1 structure ∇l_i = g_i a_i^T), which reduces effective dimensionality from O(d₁d₂) to O(d₁+d₂) and eliminates quadratic dependence on sequence length T—critical as modern LLMs extend context aggressively. Key Results: Evaluated on MMLU (5-shot accuracy) and TyDiQA (1-shot macro F1) using Llama-3.2-1B and Qwen3-0.6B with a strict 5% data budget from a 270k-sample instruction corpus. Under both best-of-run and fixed-budget settings, the proposed method consistently outperforms all baselines. On TyDiQA with Llama-3.2-1B, the method achieves 48.86 F1 (best-of-run) vs. 47.50 for GREATS, 47.21 for LESS, 47.01 for TracIn, and 42.47 for full-data training—demonstrating that curated 5% subsets outperform training on all available data. On MMLU with Llama-3.2-1B, the method achieves 30.93% vs. 30.07% (GREATS), 29.74% (LESS). The method runs at 6.47 s/step (1.12× SFT cost), making it competitive with GREATS (1.08×) and far cheaper than GRAD-MATCH (2.11×). Ablation studies reveal a non-obvious insight: applying reweighting to raw gradients (without optimizer-aware preconditioning) performs worse than simple greedy selection, confirming that sophisticated weighting is only beneficial when grounded in the correct optimizer geometry. Strengths: - Principled unification of optimizer-awareness, inter-sample redundancy, and online sequential selection into a single coherent formulation with formal second-order justification - The non-negative constraint and asymmetric preconditioning (Adam state on alignment, raw gradients on interaction) are well-motivated and empirically validated through careful ablations - Efficient factorized gradient representation that scales better than ghost dot-product for long-context LLMs (O(d₁+d₂) vs. O(d₁d₂)), enabling practical deployment Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are limited to models ≤1B parameters (Llama-3.2-1B, Qwen3-0.6B); whether the computational overhead and performance gains hold at 7B+ scale is unevaluated - The linearized Adam approximation (freezing the preconditioner from step t−1) introduces approximation error that may accumulate over long training runs or with large learning rates; sensitivity to this assumption is not analyzed - Only two benchmarks (MMLU, TyDiQA) are evaluated, both instruction-following tasks; generalization to other fine-tuning regimes (e.g., preference learning, domain adaptation) is not demonstrated
Citations 2604.00001 PDF
#111 — Moderately Innovative (59)

MolmoWeb: Open Visual Web Agent and Open Data for the Open Web (27 ▲ )

Tanmay Gupta, Piper Wolters, Zixian Ma, Peter Sushko, Rock Yuren Pang, Diego Llanes, Yue Yang, Taira Anderson, Boyuan Zheng, Zhongzheng Ren, Harsh Trivedi, Taylor Blanton, Caleb Ouellette, Winson Han, Ali Farhadi, Ranjay Krishna · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Apr 2026
Why this rank: MolmoWeb's primary contribution is openness—releasing weights, data, training pipeline, and evaluation harness—which is valuable for the field but is an engineering and community contribution rather than a methodological innovation. The multi-source data pipeline is well-designed and the ablation showing synthetic data substantially outperforms human demonstrations is an informative finding, but the core approach (SFT on synthetic trajectories with a VLM) follows established patterns. The vision-only 8B model still trails its AxTree teacher by 5-10+ points, confirming the known cost of discarding structured inputs.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Web agents—autonomous systems that navigate and complete tasks on the web—have significant potential for broadening digital access, but the most capable systems are proprietary, with undisclosed training data and recipes. This opacity limits reproducibility, scientific understanding, and community-driven progress. The authors argue that agents for the open web should be built openly, and address the gap by releasing a fully open data mixture, training pipeline, model weights, and evaluation harness for multimodal web agents. Methodology: The paper introduces two main contributions. First, MolmoWebMix, a large training dataset (278.5K trajectories, 2.2M steps across 2.6K domains) combining: (1) synthetic trajectories from an AxTree single-agent pipeline using Gemini-3-Flash-Preview, (2) higher-quality synthetic trajectories from a multi-agent Planner/Operator/Verifier harness using Gemini-2.5-Flash + GPT-4o + Gemini AxTree, (3) 36K human demonstrations collected via a custom Chrome extension and verified by crowdworkers, (4) atomic skill trajectories isolating individual browser operations, and (5) GUI perception data including 7M+ grounding QA pairs and 2.2M screenshot QA pairs. Second, MolmoWeb, a family of 4B and 8B VLMs fine-tuned on MolmoWebMix via supervised fine-tuning. The models are built on Molmo2 (Qwen3 LM + SigLIP2 vision encoder) and operate purely from screenshot inputs—no HTML, accessibility tree, or specialized APIs—predicting a natural language thought followed by a JSON-encoded browser action (coordinates normalized to [0,100]). Training used 64 H100 GPUs with global batch size 128 for up to 50K steps. At test time, the paper demonstrates best-of-N selection via parallel rollouts evaluated by an LLM judge. Key Results: On WebVoyager, MolmoWeb-8B achieves 78.2% pass@1, surpassing open-weight peers Fara-7B (73.5%), UI-TARS-1.5-7B (66.4%), and Holo1-7B (55.4%). It also outperforms the SoM+GPT-4o agent (65.1%) despite GPT-4o having both AxTree and set-of-marks inputs. MolmoWeb-8B matches SoM+o3 (79.3%) on WebVoyager and trails GPT-5-based agents by only ~12 pts. On Online-Mind2Web, MolmoWeb-8B scores 35.3%, beating Fara-7B (34.1%) and UI-TARS (31.3%). Test-time scaling via parallel rollouts yields dramatic gains: MolmoWeb-8B reaches 94.7% pass@4 on WebVoyager (vs. 78.2% pass@1) and 60.5% pass@4 on Online-Mind2Web (vs. 35.3% pass@1). A key ablation finding is that synthetic AxTree-generated trajectories are substantially more effective training data than human demonstrations on identical tasks (DeepShop: 24.4 vs. 19.8; WebV: 53.0 vs. 35.4; OM2W: 16.8 vs. 9.0), attributed to lower noise and more consistent action patterns in synthetic data. Nucleus sampling (p=0.8, T=0.7) outperforms greedy decoding by 7 pts on WebVoyager. Strengths: - Full openness: model weights, training data (MolmoWebMix), code, and evaluation harness are all released, a rare combination in a competitive field dominated by proprietary systems. - Compelling empirical demonstration that a vision-only 8B model trained on carefully curated data can match or exceed SoM agents built on vastly larger closed models with richer input representations. - Multi-source data pipeline is thoughtfully designed with complementary coverage: synthetic trajectories for consistent signal, human demonstrations for diversity, atomic skill trajectories for compositionality, and GUI perception data for grounding. - Honest and informative ablations clearly quantifying the relative value of human vs. synthetic data, data scale effects, and sampling strategies. Weaknesses / Limitations: - MolmoWeb-8B still trails its own AxTree teacher (Gemini-3-Flash) by 5–10+ points across benchmarks, indicating the vision-only constraint is a meaningful handicap, particularly for tasks requiring precise element identification or reading small text. - Human demonstrations offer limited benchmark gains and can actually hurt performance on some metrics when combined with synthetic data, due to distributional mismatch in action styles (e.g., scroll vs. scroll_at); the authors note this but do not resolve it. - Evaluations rely on LLM-as-a-judge scoring (GPT-4o, o4-mini), which introduces its own variance and potential systematic errors; the inconsistency across judges used for different benchmarks complicates cross-benchmark comparisons.
Recommended 2604.08516 PDF
#112 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Unifying Group-Relative and Self-Distillation Policy Optimization via Sample Routing (23 ▲ )

Gengsheng Li, Tianyu Yang, Junfeng Fang, Mingyang Song, Mao Zheng, Haiyun Guo, Dan Zhang, Jinqiao Wang, Tat-Seng Chua · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 58 as a competent engineering unification of GRPO and SDPO with a clean routing mechanism, but the contribution is primarily integrative rather than conceptually novel. The diagnosis of SDPO's instability modes is well-executed, and the entropy-aware dynamic weighting is sensible. However, the 'work in progress' status, narrow benchmark coverage (four science MCQ tasks), and fallback to pure GRPO on the hardest prompts limit the demonstrated impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Post-training LLMs via reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become standard practice, with GRPO as the dominant method. GRPO's core weakness is coarse credit assignment: it computes a single scalar advantage per rollout and broadcasts it uniformly to every token, meaning failed rollouts receive a uniform penalty regardless of which tokens caused the failure. Self-Distillation Policy Optimization (SDPO) addresses this with dense, token-level logit supervision from a feedback-conditioned self-teacher, achieving faster early convergence—but collapses catastrophically under prolonged training. The paper diagnoses two root causes of SDPO's instability: (1) applying self-distillation to already-correct samples introduces optimization ambiguity by forcing one correct reasoning path to mimic a different, reward-equivalent sibling; and (2) the self-teacher's signal degrades over time as the teacher-student gap narrows and teacher entropy rises. Neither method alone is satisfactory, motivating a principled unification. Methodology: The paper proposes Sample-Routed Policy Optimization (SRPO), a unified on-policy framework with two complementary branches. For each rollout, a binary routing decision is made based on two flags: correctness (c_i) and teacher availability (m_i). Correct rollouts go to the GRPO branch for reward-aligned reinforcement; incorrect rollouts with available teacher information (a correct sibling rollout from the same group) go to the SDPO branch for dense logit-level correction. The SDPO branch is further augmented with entropy-aware dynamic weighting: each token's contribution is reweighted by exp(−β·H_{i,t}), where H_{i,t} is the self-teacher's entropy at that position, normalized across all SDPO tokens to preserve loss scale. This suppresses high-entropy (unreliable) distillation targets and emphasizes confident ones. The combined loss normalizes by total routed tokens, so the mixing ratio adapts automatically—early in training when failures dominate, SDPO gets more weight; as training matures, GRPO increasingly dominates. No separate mixing hyperparameter is required. The routing is mathematically consistent: both branches reduce to policy-gradient updates, differing only in the granularity of the advantage estimator (sequence-level reward-derived vs. logit-level teacher-derived). Experiments use Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B on five benchmarks: Chemistry, Physics, Biology, Materials (from SciKnowEval), and Tool Use (ToolAlpaca). Key Results: On Qwen3-8B at 10h of training, SRPO achieves a five-benchmark average of 77.4%, compared to 74.0% for GRPO and 71.1% for SDPO—gains of +3.4% and +6.3% respectively. On Qwen3-4B, SRPO reaches 74.2% vs. 69.7% (GRPO) and 66.7% (SDPO), gains of +4.5% and +7.5%. SDPO saturates early (identical 5h and 10h averages), while SRPO continues improving. Per-step compute time for SRPO starts 17.4% higher than GRPO at 1h (due to self-teacher forward passes for failed samples) but drops to 17.2% below GRPO at 10h, as fewer samples are routed to SDPO. Response length is intermediate between GRPO (verbose) and SDPO (excessively terse). Ablations confirm sample routing is the dominant contributor: replacing it with advantage-level mixing (λ·A_GRPO + (1-λ)·A_SDPO) is +0.7 at 1h but −3.3 at 10h. Dynamic weighting adds +1.8 at 10h on top of routing alone, with gains widening over time consistent with increasing teacher noise. Strengths: - The diagnosis of SDPO's instability is empirically grounded: the ablation isolating SDPO on correct vs. incorrect samples, and the entropy-rise curve of the self-teacher over training, directly validate the two claimed failure modes. - The routing mechanism is elegant and hyperparameter-free in its mixing ratio—it adapts automatically to training dynamics without manual scheduling, and the math unifies both objectives under the policy-gradient framework. - Compute efficiency improves over long horizons rather than just at peak performance, which is practically significant: 17.2% per-step savings at 10h translates to meaningful total-run cost reduction. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is labeled "Working in Progress" and evaluates only on five narrow benchmarks (four science MCQ and one tool-use task), all following the SDPO evaluation protocol. Generalization to math reasoning (the dominant RLVR benchmark), coding, or instruction following is undemonstrated. - Teacher information is limited to correct sibling rollouts from the same group; when all G rollouts fail (no correct sibling exists), the SDPO branch is entirely inactive and falls back to GRPO. This degrades to pure GRPO on the hardest prompts where dense correction would be most valuable. - Experiments are conducted on Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B only, and hyperparameters for SRPO (notably the learning rate of 5×10^{-6}) were set heuristically rather than via grid search, raising questions about sensitivity and fair comparison at larger scale.
Recommended 2604.02288 PDF
#113 — Moderately Innovative (58)

MSAO: Adaptive Modality Sparsity-Aware Offloading with Edge-Cloud Collaboration for Efficient Multimodal LLM Inference

Zheming Yang, Qi Guo, Jun Wan, Jiarui Ruan, Yunqing Hu, Chang Zhao, Xiangyang Li · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: MSAO presents a reasonable synthesis of modality-aware sparsity analysis with adaptive edge-cloud offloading, and the two-timescale optimization (Bayesian coarse + EMA fine-grained) is well-designed. However, the core ideas — token pruning, speculative decoding, edge-cloud splitting — are individually well-established, and the use of an RTX 3090 as the 'edge' device significantly undermines the practical relevance of the edge-cloud narrative.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) that jointly process image, video, audio, and text impose severe computational and memory demands that neither cloud-only nor edge-only deployment can satisfy. Cloud-only inference incurs excessive transmission latency and bandwidth contention; edge-only inference is bottlenecked by limited compute. Existing edge-cloud collaborative frameworks use uniform offloading policies that treat all modalities identically, ignoring the fact that visual inputs often carry large amounts of spatial/temporal redundancy while text and audio carry semantically dense content that needs high-fidelity handling. The result is wasted bandwidth, suboptimal latency, and poor resource utilization. Methodology: MSAO introduces two coupled components. First, a lightweight heterogeneous modality-aware sparsity module computes a Modality Activation Sparsity (MAS) score for each input modality through three analyses: (i) spatial sparsity — a 1×1 conv head on intermediate vision-encoder feature maps produces a patch-importance map, and low-importance patches are flagged for pruning; (ii) temporal sparsity — locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) estimates inter-frame similarity for video, identifying redundant frames; (iii) modal sparsity — a lightweight cross-modal MLP estimates each modality's conditional relevance to the task prompt via softmax-normalized information-gain scores. These three signals are fused into a single MAS scalar per modality. Second, an adaptive speculative edge-cloud offloading mechanism uses MAS scores to set per-modality compression and retention ratios at coarse granularity (once per request, via Bayesian optimization with a Gaussian process surrogate), then dynamically adjusts a confidence threshold at fine granularity (per decoding step) using entropy of the draft model's output distribution. When confidence is high, the lightweight edge draft model (Qwen2-VL-2B) generates speculative tokens that the cloud model (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) verifies in parallel; when confidence is low, the intermediate state is asynchronously offloaded for cloud-side high-quality generation. The confidence threshold is updated via exponential moving average. The optimization objective minimizes expected end-to-end latency subject to quality degradation, memory, bandwidth, and MAS-based compression constraints. Key Results: Experiments on VQAv2 (5,000 sampled validation images) and MMBench (full test set, 20 capability dimensions) compare MSAO against cloud-only (Qwen2.5-VL-7B), edge-only (Qwen2-VL-2B), and PerLLM across three bandwidth levels (200/300/400 Mbps). Accuracy: MSAO stays within 0.4% of cloud-only (e.g., 76.1% vs. 76.3% on VQAv2 at 200 Mbps; 76.3% vs. 76.5% on MMBench at 400 Mbps), beating edge-only and PerLLM by 4.8%–16.8% absolute. Throughput: MSAO achieves 1.5×–2.3× improvement over cloud-only and over 2× over edge-only; at 400 Mbps on VQAv2 it reaches 128 tokens/s vs. 35 for cloud-only (2.66×). Latency: over 50% reduction vs. cloud-only and 30%+ vs. PerLLM. Resource overhead: 30%–65% computing reduction and up to 64% memory reduction (9.0 GB vs. 25.0 GB at 200 Mbps) compared to cloud-only. The lightweight sparsity module itself adds only 4.2–15.3 ms latency (<2% of end-to-end) and 0.47%–1.23% additional FLOPs. Ablation confirms both the modality-aware module (−6.8%/−7.6% accuracy without it) and collaborative scheduling (48% latency increase without it) are essential. Strengths: - The MAS metric unifies spatial, temporal, and modal redundancy into a single actionable signal with negligible overhead (<2% latency, <1.23% FLOPs), making it practical for real edge devices. - The two-timescale optimization (Bayesian coarse-grained per request + EMA fine-grained per step) cleanly separates the slow modality-selection problem from the fast speculation-triggering problem, and has convergence guarantees for both. - Results are compelling across multiple axes simultaneously — accuracy is essentially preserved while latency, throughput, and memory all improve substantially over the strongest collaborative baseline (PerLLM). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The experimental setup uses an RTX 3090 as the "edge" device — a 24 GB GPU that is far more powerful than typical real-world edge hardware (phones, IoT nodes, embedded systems), undermining the ecological validity of the edge scenario. - The MAS probing module requires training (the cross-modal MLP and spatial probing head), but training details, data requirements, and transferability across different MLLM architectures are not discussed. - Only one edge-cloud baseline (PerLLM) is compared; missing comparisons to other recent adaptive collaborative frameworks cited in the related work (e.g., SLED, SpaceServe, cloud-device collaborative learning) makes it hard to assess the true margin of improvement. - The paper does not address audio modality in experiments despite including it in the formalism, limiting the scope of empirical validation for the full multi-modal claim.
Search Results 2604.02945 PDF
#114 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Fast Spatial Memory with Elastic Test-Time Training (4 ▲ )

Ziqiao Ma, Xueyang Yu, Haoyu Zhen, Yuncong Yang, Joyce Chai, Chuang Gan · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Applying Elastic Weight Consolidation to test-time training for 4D view synthesis is a principled and well-executed transfer of a known continual learning technique to a new problem setting. The streaming-EMA anchor policy and the demonstration that multi-chunk elastic updates dramatically outperform fully plastic updates (29.99 vs. 26.91 PSNR) are solid contributions, and FSM is the first feedforward model supporting arbitrary-length 4D input. However, the core intellectual contribution is recognizing the chunk-task analogy rather than developing fundamentally new machinery.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large Reconstruction Models (LRMs) and Large View Synthesis Models (LVSMs) for 3D/4D novel view synthesis are constrained by activation memory, limiting context length to what fits in a single forward pass. Large Chunk Test-Time Training (LaCT) partially addresses this by enabling chunk-wise fast-weight adaptation during inference, but its fully plastic updates suffer from catastrophic forgetting and overfitting — particularly in 4D settings where illumination, pose, and scene dynamics evolve continuously across time. As a result, LaCT works best with a single large chunk spanning the full input, defeating the purpose of chunk-wise processing for arbitrarily long sequences. The paper asks: how to design a scalable spatial memory architecture that handles genuinely long sequences without unbounded activation memory. Methodology: The paper introduces Elastic Test-Time Training (LaCET — Large Chunk Elastic Test-Time Training), which augments LaCT's fast-weight update with a consolidation step inspired by Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) from continual learning. After each chunk's fast-weight update, a Fisher-weighted penalty softly pulls critical parameters back toward an anchor state, while less important parameters remain free to adapt. The anchor itself evolves as an exponential moving average (Streaming-EMA) of past fast weights, creating a low-pass filter over the fast-weight trajectory that balances stability and plasticity. The importance matrix is maintained via an online EMA over per-chunk Fisher-style statistics; the paper evaluates EWC-, MAS-, and SI-style estimators and finds SI-style performs marginally best. Built on LaCET blocks with a SwiGLU-MLP fast-weight network and window attention, the authors train Fast Spatial Memory (FSM) on 7 large-scale 3D/4D datasets (including RealEstate10K, DL3DV, Stereo4D) — totaling hundreds of millions of frames — with a curriculum that gradually increases resolution (128→256), temporal span, and input view count. Two decoder variants are explored: LVSM-style (direct RGB patch prediction) and LRM-style (pixel-aligned 4D Gaussian Splatting with tile-based rasterization). Key Results: On 4D novel view synthesis, FSM-LVSM achieves 32.16 PSNR / 0.043 LPIPS / 0.931 SSIM on Stereo4D at 256×256, substantially outperforming MoVieS (27.19 / 0.114 / 0.888) and 4DGT (24.62 / 0.102 / 0.785) at comparable or higher resolutions. On the NVIDIA dynamic benchmark, FSM-LVSM achieves 23.90 PSNR / 0.105 LPIPS / 0.747 SSIM at 256×256, surpassing all feedforward methods and approaching optimization-based methods like MoSca (21.45 PSNR at higher resolution). On static 3D NVS (DL3DV-140), FSM-LVSM achieves 26.69 PSNR / 0.091 LPIPS / 0.846 SSIM, competitive with tttLVSM (26.90 / 0.185 / 0.837) despite the latter using 540×960 resolution. Ablations show the streaming-EMA anchor policy is essential — streaming without EMA and global anchoring both degrade significantly. LaCET with 4 chunks (PSNR 29.99) dramatically outperforms LaCT with 4 chunks (26.91) and even LaCT with 1 chunk (26.02), demonstrating that elastic consolidation unlocks the benefits of multi-chunk processing. LaCET also reduces the camera-interpolation shortcut, where LaCT learns to predict frames by interpolating neighbors rather than performing genuine novel view synthesis. Strengths: - The borrowing of EWC from continual learning to address inference-time catastrophic forgetting is principled and well-motivated; the analogy between sequential chunks and sequential tasks is clean and technically sound. - Thorough ablations isolate the contribution of each component (chunking strategy, anchor policy, Fisher estimator) and provide interpretable explanations for why each matters. - FSM is the first feedforward 4D reconstruction model supporting arbitrary-length input sequences with arbitrary novel view-time queries, making a genuine advance over single-pass systems. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The approach requires posed input images; joint camera pose estimation in dynamic scenes is explicitly out of scope, limiting applicability to settings where calibrated cameras or SLAM-derived poses are available. - Despite reducing the camera-interpolation shortcut, the paper acknowledges the problem does not fully disappear under photometric-only supervision, and ghosting artifacts persist for challenging viewpoints with large motion; geometric supervision (depth, flow, multi-view consistency) is deferred to future work. - Evaluation resolution (256×256) is modest compared to several baselines that run at 504×504 or 540×960; the claim of competitive performance relies heavily on resolution-normalization arguments that are reasonable but not universally accepted, and the LPIPS advantage for FSM-LVSM may partly reflect the lower resolution baseline it is compared against.
Recommended 2604.07350 PDF
#115 — Moderately Innovative (58)

EffiPair: Improving the Efficiency of LLM -generated Code with Relative Contrastive Feedback

Samira Hajizadeh, Suman Jana · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The relative contrastive feedback idea is a sensible refinement over absolute profiling signals, and the 100x token reduction versus EffiLearner with comparable efficiency gains demonstrates practical value. However, the absolute improvements are modest (1-5 DPS points), the method still requires substantial infrastructure (profiler, embeddings, AST analysis), and the connection to model compression is tangential — this is really about code optimization rather than weight or architecture compression.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs routinely produce functionally correct code that is nonetheless inefficient in runtime and memory. Prior inference-time refinement methods address this by feeding absolute execution feedback (scalar runtime or memory measurements) back to the model, but this signal is weak: it tells the model how fast a program ran without revealing which structural choices caused the inefficiency or how to fix them. This yields slow convergence, repeated profiling overhead, and high token costs. The paper argues that relative comparisons between candidate programs provide a richer, more actionable signal. Methodology: The authors introduce Relative Contrastive Feedback (RCF), a training-free inference-time mechanism. RCF selects a pair of candidate programs for the same task: an efficient reference p+ (fastest correct solution) and a structurally similar but slower p- (worst performer above a similarity threshold τ). Similarity is measured as a weighted sum (α=0.8) of cosine similarity over Jina code embeddings and over AST bag-of-node-type vectors, with τ=0.85 as the default threshold. The Scalene sampling profiler identifies hot functions and memory hotspots; raw traces are compressed to lines responsible for >1% CPU time or ≥100 allocations, and this compact summary is included alongside both programs in the refinement prompt. The refined program is returned to the candidate pool for subsequent rounds. The full system, EffiPair, runs N=3 initial candidates and T=3 refinement rounds by default, evaluating each candidate in an isolated subprocess with fixed timeouts (30s correctness, 120s profiling). No model fine-tuning or parameter updates are required. Key Results: Evaluated on EvalPerf, Mercury, and ENAMEL benchmarks using GPT-5.4, GPT-5.4 mini, Claude Sonnet 4.6, and DeepSeek-Chat V3.2. On EvalPerf with DeepSeek-Chat V3.2, EffiPair improves DPS from 85.89 (solo summarized profiling) to 87.46 and reduces timing from 0.043s to 0.023s while maintaining Pass@1 at 92.37%. With Claude Sonnet 4.6, timing drops to 0.021s and DPS reaches 92.97. Cross-benchmark comparison against GPT-4o mini shows EffiPair achieves 84.74 DPSnorm on EvalPerf (vs. 83.78 for LLM4EFFI), 77.39 Beyond@1 on Mercury (vs. 74.94), and 50.37 eff@1 on ENAMEL (vs. 49.89), outperforming all prior methods on efficiency across all three benchmarks. Against EffiLearner, EffiPair achieves comparable 1.50x speedup with higher Pass@1 (92.37% vs. 88.14%) while using ~200k prompt tokens vs. EffiLearner's ~23.6M — a >100x reduction. Gains are front-loaded: most improvement occurs in the first efficiency round. Strengths: - Token efficiency is dramatically better than prior work: 100x fewer prompt tokens than EffiLearner with comparable or better efficiency and higher correctness, making the approach practically viable at scale. - The contrastive pairing insight is principled — by controlling for structural similarity, the feedback signal isolates efficiency-relevant design choices rather than conflating unrelated algorithmic differences. - The persistent candidate pool prevents correctness regression: once a correct solution is found it cannot be overwritten, so efficiency refinement never sacrifices correctness — a known failure mode of single-trajectory methods. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Gains are modest in absolute terms (DPS improvements of 1-5 points) and vary across benchmarks, suggesting the method is sensitive to whether inefficiencies are consistently localizable in similar candidate pairs — a condition that may not hold for algorithmic-level gaps. - The method still requires generating multiple candidates, running a profiler, and computing pairwise embeddings and ASTs per task, which adds latency and infrastructure complexity not present in simple single-pass generation; the paper does not report end-to-end wall-clock cost of the full pipeline. - Evaluation is restricted to Python and competitive programming-style tasks; generalizability to systems code, statically typed languages, or real-world codebases with external dependencies is undemonstrated.
Search Results 2604.05137 PDF
#116 — Moderately Innovative (58)

DWDP: Distributed Weight Data Parallelism for High- Performance LLM Inference on NVL72

Wanqian Li, Jintao Peng, Zongfei Jing, Tianyu Zhang, Ze Long, Xianjie Qiao, Xiaoming Chen, Dongxu Yang, Kefeng Duan, June Yang · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: DWDP offers a clean systems insight — replacing synchronous collectives with asynchronous P2P weight fetches to decouple ranks under load imbalance — but the core concept of trading weight replication for communication is well-established in data/model parallelism literature. The engineering contributions (split-weight merge elimination, TDM contention mitigation) are solid but incremental, and evaluation on a single model/platform limits generalizability claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-GPU LLM inference using expert parallelism (EP) or tensor parallelism requires layer-wise inter-rank synchronization via collective operations (all-to-all, all-gather). In production serving, workloads are rarely balanced: requests differ in sequence length and KV-cache hit rates, while MoE routing creates hot/cold expert imbalance across ranks. These imbalances compound at synchronization barriers, making end-to-end throughput bounded by the slowest rank. The paper measures ~12% synchronization overhead at a realistic coefficient of variation of 20% in per-rank sequence lengths — a routine production condition, not a pathological corner case. Prior load-balancing mitigations (cache-aware scheduling, expert load balancing) do not remove the synchronization requirement. Methodology: DWDP (Distributed Weight Data Parallelism) keeps all ranks data-parallel while partitioning MoE expert weights across peers (attention weights are fully replicated). Each rank permanently stores only its local expert shard; before executing each MoE layer, it fetches the remaining experts from peer GPUs on demand via `cudaMemcpyAsync` (copy-engine-based peer-to-peer pulls, not NCCL collectives). This eliminates SM occupancy from communication and removes collective synchronization from the critical path — each rank proceeds independently once its experts arrive. Prefetches for layer l+1 are overlapped with the MoE block of layer l and attention of layer l+1 using double buffering. Two key optimizations address practical overheads: (1) Split-weight merge elimination: rather than merging local and remote expert buffers with a D2D copy before the grouped GEMM, the kernel is extended via CuTeDSL TensorList inputs to consume multiple non-contiguous buffers directly; (2) Time-division multiplexing for contention: remote weight transfers are sliced into fixed-size chunks and scheduled in round-robin across destination ranks at the source copy engine, converting many-to-one serialization into interleaved small DMA requests that exploit copy engine pipelining. A roofline-based analytical model characterizes when prefetch can be hidden behind computation (requires T_compute/T_prefetch ≥ 1, met at ~16K tokens at batch size 1 on GB200, or earlier at larger batch sizes). Key Results: Evaluated on GB200 NVL72 with DeepSeek-R1 (NVFP4 MoE weights, FP8 KV cache) in TensorRT-LLM under disaggregated serving (DWDP applied to context servers only). Context-only results: 1.09–1.11× TPS/GPU speedup and 1.11–1.27× TTFT speedup across ISLs 1K–32K (MNT=32K). Speedup increases with workload imbalance: at ISL std dev = 4096, TPS/GPU improves 1.15× vs. 1.09× at balanced load. Split-weight merge elimination contributes ~3% additional TPS/GPU; contention mitigation adds further gains especially at short compute windows (at ISL ratio 0.5, MNT=16K, DWDP without contention mitigation falls slightly below DEP while full DWDP is 8.1% above). End-to-end (8K input / 1K output, SemiAnalysis dataset, 20–100 TPS/user range): DWDP improves output TPS/GPU by 8.8% at comparable TPS/user, primarily by reducing required context GPU count. TPS/user speedup reaches 1.15–1.16× in the 20–50 TPS/user range. The primary cost is increased TTFT: at 20–30 TPS/user, median TTFT rises from 2538ms to 8314ms due to fewer context GPUs lowering aggregate context throughput and worsening rate matching. Strengths: - Clean architectural insight: replacing collective all-to-all with asynchronous copy-engine P2P pulls eliminates both synchronization semantics and SM resource contention, addressing the fundamental scaling bottleneck under load imbalance rather than patching scheduling heuristics. - Thorough hardware-level interference analysis (Appendix A) identifying power-induced DVFS frequency throttling — not L2/DRAM/NVLink saturation — as the dominant source of compute slowdown during prefetch overlap; attention alone uses 96.7% of TDP, communication adds 30.5%, yielding ~114% TDP and triggering frequency reduction to 0.798× baseline. - Flexible expert placement (no strict divisibility requirement, redundant experts supported) enables non-power-of-two DWDP group sizes and finer-grained resource provisioning in disaggregated serving. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Significant TTFT regression in the low TPS/user regime (e.g., 2538ms → 8314ms at 20–30 TPS/user) due to reduced context GPU count worsening rate matching with generation servers; the paper attributes this to configuration choice rather than per-GPU inefficiency, but no mitigation is demonstrated. - DWDP requires high-bandwidth peer-to-peer GPU interconnect (NVLink at NVL72 scale) to hide prefetch behind computation — the approach is hardware-specific and may not generalize to multi-node or PCIe-connected deployments where P2P bandwidth is far lower. - Evaluation is limited to a single model (DeepSeek-R1) on a single hardware platform (GB200 NVL72) under a single quantization scheme (NVFP4), leaving generalizability to other MoE architectures, hardware generations, or full-precision workloads uncharacterized.
Search Results 2604.01621 PDF
#117 — Moderately Innovative (58)

A neural - symbolic AI agent system for biomedical concept mapping

nature.com · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: The gated neural-symbolic workflow for biomedical concept mapping is well-engineered: invoking LLM expansion only for abbreviated/non-standard mentions preserves deterministic behavior on clean cases and yields strong zero-shot gains (+24.8 pts on abbreviated subsets). The interpretable intermediate outputs are a practical advantage in clinical deployment. However, the individual components (TF-IDF retrieval, zero-shot LLM prompting, RRF fusion) are all established techniques, and the gene/genome regression remains unsolved.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Biomedical concept mapping — linking free-text mentions (e.g., "MI", "RA") to standardized UMLS concept identifiers — is a foundational NLP task underpinning clinical QA, knowledge graph construction, and EHR interoperability. Existing rule-based systems (USAGI, ScispaCy) fail on linguistically variable or ambiguous text, while learning-based models (KrissBERT) collapse on long-tail and zero-shot abbreviated concepts underrepresented in training data. Both suffer interpretability deficits. The core gap is the inability to handle abbreviated, rare, or context-dependent mentions without relying on training-time exposure to those surface forms. Methodology: MCM (Medical Concept Mapping) is a five-module neural-symbolic agentic workflow. The pipeline takes a mention and its surrounding context and returns a ranked list of UMLS CUIs. (1) Concept Retrieval: character-level TF-IDF cosine similarity against labeled mentions from training data, ranking candidates by frequency. (2) Expansion Decision: an LLM prompted to classify whether the mention is non-standard (abbreviated, shorthand) — binary flag. (3) Mention Expansion: if flagged, the LLM rewrites the mention into a canonical form using context. (4) Equivalence Validation: a self-feedback LLM check that the expanded form is semantically equivalent to the original in context; if not, falls back to the original. (5) Symbolic Linking: character-level TF-IDF cosine similarity against the full UMLS concept name index, fused with Concept Retrieval results via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). The LLM modules (steps 2–4) are zero-shot prompted with no fine-tuning; GPT-OSS (20B) is selected as the backbone after comparison of 7 open-source models. Implementation uses scikit-learn TF-IDF, LangChain for prompt orchestration, and MCP for agent communication. Key Results: On three benchmarks (MedMentions, ST21pv, MCN), MCM achieves Recall@1 of 63.3, 60.0, and 67.9, outperforming KrissBERT (58.0, 54.6, 63.1), ScispaCy (55.9, 53.7, 60.1), and USAGI (30.7, 36.4, 53.1). On the hardest zero-shot abbreviated subset, gains are substantially larger: MCM scores 36.3 / 36.7 / 38.6 vs. KrissBERT 11.5 / 12.0 / 28.8 — a +24.8 point improvement on MedMentions. Semantic type analysis shows massive post-expansion gains for Organic Chemicals (+57.6), Pharmacologic Substances (+57.7), Diseases (+46.7), and Procedures (+44.9), with a notable regression for Gene/Genome (-19.6) due to gene-protein name aliasing. Human evaluation of 50 sampled mentions across 7 LLMs rated 79.4% of expansions as reasonable and useful (Likert 4–5); GPT-OSS led at 85.1%. Context window experiments show consistent Recall@1 improvement from 32 to 256 tokens (e.g., GPT-OSS: 36.0 → 44.0) with no statistically significant increase in runtime on NVIDIA L40S hardware, making larger contexts a free lunch. Strengths: - The decomposition into gated, conditional modules (decide → expand → validate → link) is principled: LLMs are invoked only when needed, preserving deterministic symbolic behavior for well-represented concepts and avoiding hallucination-induced noise for clean mentions. - Strong performance on zero-shot abbreviated mentions — the hardest and most practically relevant regime — where the gains (+24.8 pts over KrissBERT) far exceed overall dataset gains (+5.3 pts), precisely validating the design's target use case. - Interpretability is a genuine architectural property: the expansion and validation steps produce human-readable intermediate outputs that enable auditing, a significant advantage over black-box embedding models in clinical settings. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The Symbolic Linking module relies on character-level TF-IDF against UMLS, a deliberately simple retrieval method that the authors acknowledge was not deeply optimized.
