This likely suppresses overall Recall@1 ceiling and makes it hard to disentangle expansion quality from retrieval quality. - Evaluation is on isolated concept mapping only, not end-to-end NER-to-normalization pipelines; NER span errors in real systems would propagate and degrade these numbers in ways not assessed. The gene/genome regression (-19.6 Recall@1 post-expansion) reveals a systematic failure mode — gene symbol aliasing with protein names — that the authors note without solving, leaving a meaningful semantic type unaddressed.
Search Results
#118 — Moderately Innovative (58)

AutoMIA: Improved Baselines for Membership Inference Attack via Agentic Self-Exploration

Ruhao Liu, Weiqi Huang, Qi Li, Xinchao Wang · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The agentic closed-loop formulation for automated MIA strategy discovery is a timely application of LLM-as-optimizer to privacy auditing, and the interpretable code output is a practical advantage over opaque learned approaches. However, the underlying paradigm follows the now-common pattern of using LLM agents to search a design space, and the near-IID results show only marginal gains over the strongest handcrafted baselines (0.723 vs. 0.716 AUC), suggesting the discovered strategies operate within the same signal space as existing heuristics rather than uncovering fundamentally new memorization signatures.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs) determine whether a specific sample was used during model training, serving as a core auditing tool for privacy leakage in machine learning. Existing MIA methods rely on handcrafted statistical heuristics (entropy, perplexity, Min-k% probability) that are tightly coupled to specific model architectures or tasks, degrade when transferred across models, and require substantial expert design effort. No prior work offers a unified, automated mechanism for discovering and refining attack strategies across heterogeneous vision-language models (VLMs). The paper asks whether agentic LLM reasoning can replace manual feature engineering for MIA strategy discovery. Methodology: AutoMIA frames membership inference as an automated strategy generation and refinement problem under a grey-box threat model (adversary observes output logits and tokenizer but not model weights or training algorithm). The system operates as a closed loop with two cooperating agents. The AutoMIA agent iteratively generates K candidate attack strategies per round, each consisting of a semantic description and executable Python/PyTorch code that computes a scalar membership score from per-token logit distributions. The Guidance agent evaluates each batch of strategies using a composite effectiveness score Q = 0.6·AUC + 0.3·Acc + 0.1·TPR@5%FPR, categorizes them as strong/mid/weak, and produces natural-language feedback identifying promising directions and dead ends. A sliding window of size w=5 (3 strong + 2 weak exemplars) is maintained to prevent both forgetting of good strategies and redundant re-exploration of failed ones. Logits are pre-collected once from target VLMs and reused across all iterations, making each round computationally cheap. The agent backbone is DeepSeek-V3.2-Reasoner (ablated against Gemini 3 Flash, Grok 4.1 Fast, Qwen3-Max). Experiments run for 10–20 rounds on three VL-MIA benchmarks (Text, DALL·E, Flickr) against three target models (LLaVA-1.5, MiniGPT-4, LLaMA-Adapter). Key Results: AutoMIA consistently matches or outperforms all handcrafted baselines across text, image, and multimodal MIA settings. On text-based VL-MIA (Table 1), AutoMIA achieves AUC of 0.810/0.994/0.824/0.891/0.828/0.778 across LLaVA, MiniGPT-4, and LLaMA-Adapter at text lengths 32 and 64, versus the best individual baselines (e.g., ModRényi α=1 at 0.808/0.993, but only for LLaVA; perplexity collapses to 0.431 on LLaMA-Adapter TextLen=64). On image-centric DALL·E benchmarks, AutoMIA achieves AUC 0.787 on LLaVA image embeddings versus the best handcrafted baseline of 0.705. The near-IID evaluation on OLMo (Table 4) shows AutoMIA best strategy at AUC 0.723 versus the strongest baseline Max-100% Rényi α=0.5 at 0.716, with notably higher TPR@5%FPR (0.240 vs. 0.216). Ablations confirm: (1) the Guidance agent is critical — removing it drops AUC by ~0.12 on LLaMA-Adapter TextLen=32 (0.709 vs. 0.828); (2) convergence typically occurs around iteration 15; (3) all four tested LLM backbones outperform baselines, confirming the framework rather than the backbone drives performance; (4) held-out test splits show moderate but non-trivial generalization of discovered strategies to unseen data. Strengths: - The closed-loop agentic formulation produces mathematically explicit, interpretable code rather than opaque neural components — discovered metrics can be directly inspected and validated. - Robust across models and modalities: while individual handcrafted metrics can be competitive on specific (model, dataset, text-length) combinations, AutoMIA is consistently near-optimal across all 18+ configurations tested, addressing a key practical failure mode of prior work. - The near-IID and held-out split ablations provide meaningful evidence that discovered strategies capture genuine memorization signals rather than distributional artifacts, with mathematical simulation (logit-boost injection) further supporting this claim. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The grey-box assumption (access to output logits) is more privileged than real-world black-box deployments; the paper does not demonstrate whether discovered strategies can be adapted or distilled to black-box settings, limiting direct applicability to most commercial models. - The evaluation is confined to three relatively dated open-source VLMs (MiniGPT-4, LLaVA-1.5, LLaMA-Adapter); it is unclear whether AutoMIA's discovered strategies transfer to or maintain advantages over stronger, more recent models where memorization dynamics may differ substantially. - The near-IID experiment (Table 4) shows only a marginal advantage over the strongest baseline (AUC 0.723 vs. 0.716), which tempers the headline performance claims made relative to weaker baselines on benchmark datasets that may contain non-IID artifacts.
Search Results 2604.01014 PDF
#119 — Moderately Innovative (58)

When Numbers Speak: Aligning Textual Numerals and Visual Instances in Text-to-Video Diffusion Models (104 ▲ )

Zhengyang Sun, Yu Chen, Xin Zhou, Xiaofan Li, Xiwu Chen, Dingkang Liang, Xiang Bai · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Apr 2026
Why this rank: NUMINA offers a solid diagnostic contribution — identifying semantic weakness and instance ambiguity as root causes of counting failures in T2V models — and the finding that attention-derived layouts outperform GroundingDINO-derived ones is non-obvious. However, the absolute counting accuracy remains modest (best 59.1%), the evaluation relies on a small self-constructed benchmark with a single seed, and the heuristic placement costs with manually tuned hyperparameters limit generalizability. The contribution is a useful but incremental step on a narrow subproblem.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Text-to-video (T2V) diffusion models consistently fail to generate the correct number of objects specified in a prompt. The authors diagnose two root causes: (1) semantic weakness — numeral tokens exhibit diffuse, low-contrast cross-attention responses compared to nouns, verbs, or adjectives, indicating poor grounding of count constraints in the latent space; and (2) instance ambiguity — the heavily downsampled spatiotemporal latent space of Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architectures limits separability of individual object representations. Retraining is impractical due to cost and the scarcity of precisely-annotated numerical video data, motivating a training-free approach. Methodology: NUMINA is a training-free, identify-then-guide framework operating in two phases. In Phase 1 (Numerical Misalignment Identification), a pre-generation pass is run to timestep t=20 in an intermediate DiT layer (ℓ=15). Self-attention heads are scored by a three-component discriminability metric (foreground-background separation, structural richness via block variance, and edge clarity via Sobel gradients), and the single highest-scoring head is selected to form an instance-discriminative spatial scaffold. Per-noun cross-attention heads are selected by peak activation concentration. These maps are fused via clustering (mean-shift for spatial proposals, DBSCAN for focus masks) to construct an explicit countable layout M_T, which is compared against the prompt numeral to detect misalignment. In Phase 2 (Numerically Aligned Video Generation), layout refinement corrects count errors with minimal structural change: object removal erases the smallest instance region; object addition places a copied or circular template at the optimal location by minimizing a three-term cost (overlap penalty, center proximity, temporal stability). The corrected layout then guides regeneration via cross-attention modulation — suppressing attention for removed regions and boosting/overwriting pre-softmax scores for added regions, scaled by a monotonically decreasing intensity function δ(t) that applies stronger guidance early in denoising. Key Results: On the authors' introduced CountBench (210 prompts, counts 1–8, 1–3 object categories), NUMINA improves counting accuracy (CountAcc) by +7.4% on Wan2.1-1.3B (42.3%→49.7%), +4.9% on Wan2.2-5B (47.8%→52.7%), and +5.5% on Wan2.1-14B (53.6%→59.1%), consistently outperforming seed search (+3.2/+1.0/+2.5%) and prompt enhancement (+4.9/+1.2/+3.3%). The 1.3B model with NUMINA surpasses the unaugmented 5B baseline (49.7% vs 47.8%). CLIP scores improve in all cases (e.g., 33.9→35.6 for 1.3B), and temporal consistency is maintained or improved. Per-numeral breakdown shows the largest gains at high counts: for 8 objects, baseline accuracy is 11.3% while NUMINA nearly doubles it to 20.7%. Combined with seed search and prompt enhancement, NUMINA reaches 54.2% on Wan2.1-1.3B. The method also generalizes to CogVideoX-5B (MMDiT architecture), yielding +4.2% CountAcc. Computational overhead is modest — 431s vs 292s wall-clock on 1.3B, with VRAM increasing from 14.3 to 16.3 GB — and is partially recovered via EasyCache integration (355s). A blind user study (10 participants, 100 video pairs) shows 61% preference for NUMINA over baseline. Strengths: - Principled diagnosis of why T2V models fail at counting (semantic weakness + instance ambiguity) directly motivates the design, and the attention head selection ablation clearly validates that instance-separable information is sparse across heads (Top-1 selection: 49.7% vs. all-head average: 43.0%). - Training-free and broadly applicable: works across Wan (1.3B/5B/14B, standard DiT) and CogVideoX-5B (MMDiT) with a simple adaptation, requiring no input masks, segmentation models at inference, or auxiliary networks. - The attention-derived layout outperforms a GroundingDINO-derived layout as a guidance signal (+2.2% CountAcc), suggesting the latent-native layout better captures nascent instance structure during denoising, which is an interesting and non-obvious finding. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is conducted only on the authors' own CountBench benchmark with a fixed single seed for baselines, which limits independent verification; the 210-prompt benchmark is modest in scale and relies on GroundingDINO for automated counting, inheriting its detection errors as evaluation noise. - The method's ceiling remains low in absolute terms (best: 59.1% on 14B model, 54.2% combined), meaning counting errors persist for nearly half of prompts; performance on very high counts (e.g., 7–8 objects) is still poor (20.7%), and dense instance scenarios (tens or hundreds) are entirely unaddressed. - The two-pass generation (pre-generation to t*=20, then full re-generation) adds ~48% wall-clock overhead on the 1.3B model, which scales worse on larger models; the method's reliance on heuristic placement costs (Equations 5–6) with manually tuned hyperparameters (λ, τ, k) may not transfer robustly to novel scene geometries or viewpoints.
Recommended 2604.08546 PDF
#120 — Moderately Innovative (58)

How Well Do Agentic Skills Work in the Wild: Benchmarking LLM Skill Usage in Realistic Settings (24 ▲ )

Yujian Liu, Jiabao Ji, Li An, Tommi Jaakkola, Yang Zhang, Shiyu Chang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Apr 2026
Why this rank: This paper makes a valuable empirical contribution by systematically demonstrating that LLM skill benefits degrade monotonically as evaluation conditions become more realistic, with a well-designed progressive framework that cleanly isolates selection, retrieval, and adaptation challenges. However, the core finding — that idealized benchmark conditions overstate real-world performance — is more confirmatory than surprising, and the query-specific refinement method, while useful, essentially doubles compute for modest gains.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-based agent platforms (Claude Code, Codex, etc.) have broadly adopted "skills" — reusable, filesystem-based knowledge artifacts (SKILL.md files) encoding domain-specific workflows, API patterns, and best practices. Prior benchmarks like SKILLSBENCH evaluate skills under idealized conditions: hand-crafted, task-specific skills are placed directly in the agent's context, bypassing the realistic challenges of (1) selecting which skills to load from a list, (2) retrieving relevant skills from a large noisy repository, and (3) adapting general-purpose skills not authored for the specific task. This paper asks whether skill benefits survive when these idealizations are removed. Methodology: The authors assemble a collection of 34,198 real-world skills sourced from open-source GitHub repositories (filtered for MIT/Apache 2.0 licenses, deduplication, and quality). They build a skill search engine with BM25 sparse retrieval (SQLite FTS5), dense retrieval (Qwen3-Embedding-4B), and hybrid Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF), exposed as HTTP endpoints to agents. Five search strategies are compared: direct semantic search, and four agentic variants (keyword, semantic, hybrid w/o content, hybrid w/ content), where the agent iteratively formulates queries and evaluates candidates. Evaluation is structured as a progressive ladder of six settings on SKILLSBENCH (84 tasks): force-loaded curated skills (upper bound), curated skills (agent decides to load), curated + distractors, retrieved w/ curated skills in pool, retrieved w/o curated skills, and no skills (baseline). Three frontier models are evaluated — Claude Opus 4.6 with Claude Code, Kimi K2.5 with Terminus-2, and Qwen3.5-397B-A17B with Qwen-Code — each running the full pipeline independently. Two skill refinement strategies are then studied: query-agnostic refinement (each retrieved skill improved offline via self-evaluation against synthetic queries using the skill-creator meta-skill) and query-specific refinement (the agent attempts the task using retrieved skills, reflects on what helped or hurt, then synthesizes a new composite skill). Results are additionally validated on TERMINAL-BENCH 2.0 (89 tasks), a general-purpose agent benchmark with no curated skills. Key Results: The headline finding is that skill benefits are fragile and degrade monotonically as settings become more realistic. For Claude Opus 4.6: force-loaded curated skills yield 55.4%, agent-selected curated skills drop to 51.2%, adding distractors drops to 43.5%, retrieval from a pool including curated skills drops to 40.1%, and retrieval without curated skills drops to 38.4% — only 3.0 points above the no-skill baseline of 35.4%. Kimi and Qwen fall below their no-skill baselines in the fully realistic setting (19.8% vs. 21.8% for Kimi; 19.7% vs. 20.5% for Qwen), indicating irrelevant retrieved skills can actively hurt weaker models. Agentic hybrid search with full content achieves Recall@5 of 65.5%, substantially above direct semantic search (47.0%), but the recall ceiling limits downstream task performance. Query-specific refinement recovers much of the gap when initially retrieved skills are relevant (coverage score ≥3.83): Claude rises from 40.1% to 48.2% on SKILLSBENCH w/ curated, and improves by +4.1/+5.6/+4.9 points across all three models on TERMINAL-BENCH 2.0, where Claude reaches 65.5% from a 57.7% no-skill baseline. Query-agnostic refinement yields smaller, inconsistent gains (+1.9 for Claude on SKILLSBENCH w/ curated). Refinement is ineffective when initial skill coverage is low (≤3.49), confirming that it amplifies existing signal rather than generating new knowledge. Strengths: - The progressive evaluation framework is well-designed and cleanly isolates the three distinct challenges (selection, retrieval, adaptation), yielding actionable insight about where the performance losses occur. - Results are consistent across three architecturally diverse frontier models and two benchmarks, strengthening the generalizability of the core claim. - The query-specific refinement approach is practically motivated and technically sound — synthesizing across multiple partially relevant skills is a genuine contribution beyond naive RAG. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The ground-truth for retrieval evaluation (Recall@k) uses SKILLSBENCH's hand-curated skills as the oracle, which are themselves over-fitted to tasks; this may underestimate the practical utility of general-purpose retrieved skills that could solve tasks via alternative approaches. - Query-specific refinement requires a full task exploration pass at inference time, roughly doubling compute, yet the paper does not analyze cost-benefit tradeoffs or compute budgets, making it hard to assess practical viability. - The refinement is limited to a single iteration by design; whether iterative refinement would further close the gap is unexplored, and the single-iteration constraint may artificially suppress the method's ceiling.
Recommended 2604.04323 PDF
#121 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Graph-Based Chain-of-Thought Pruning for Reducing Redundant Reflections in Reasoning LLMs (7 ▲ )

Hongyuan Yuan, Xinran He, Run Shao, Bolei He, Xianwei Xue, Mengke Chen, Qiutong Pan, Haiwei Wang, Haifeng Li · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Apr 2026
Why this rank: The DAG abstraction for reasoning traces is a reasonable structural contribution that moves beyond naive length-based pruning, and the ablation showing graph-pruned vs. truncated CoT validates the approach. However, the method is engineering-heavy—requiring external LLM calls for graph construction, a binary progress/review taxonomy that oversimplifies real reasoning, and a three-stage training pipeline—while gains are modest and confined to math benchmarks.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning via reinforcement learning (e.g., DeepSeek-R1, OpenAI o1) improves LLM performance on complex tasks but induces "overthinking" — models generate redundant intermediate reasoning that adds inference cost without improving correctness. Prior approaches treat this as a length-control or token-importance problem, but this paper argues the core issue is inefficient reflection: models perform unnecessary self-checks (Indiscriminate Reflection) and repeatedly re-verify already-established conclusions (Repetitive Reflection). Existing methods lack a principled structural model of reasoning to distinguish useful from redundant steps. Methodology: The paper proposes converting linear CoT traces into Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) to expose reasoning structure, then applying targeted pruning. Each reasoning step is classified as either a progress node (advancing reasoning) or a review node (verifying/restating). Graph construction uses an external LLM (qwen-turbo) to iteratively process CoT chunks, deciding whether each chunk should insert a new node or merge into an existing one; edges encode logical dependencies. Two pruning criteria are then applied: (1) Branch-level pruning removes review nodes with fewer than k=2 descendants — these form narrow side branches unlikely to influence the main path; (2) Depth-level pruning removes review nodes whose relative depth exceeds m=0.9 of the maximum DAG depth, targeting post-answer re-verification. The pruned DAG is relinearized into a compact CoT. Training uses a three-stage pipeline: (1) cold-start SFT on pruned traces using LoRA; (2) DPO preference alignment using a redundancy score combining review-node fraction and normalized length; (3) GRPO with a length penalty rewarding correctness but penalizing excess length relative to the shortest correct trajectory in each rollout batch. Key Results: Evaluated on five mathematical reasoning benchmarks (AIME24, AIME25, AMC23, OlympiadBench, MATH500) with DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B and 7B base models. On the 7B model, the method improves average accuracy from 59.72% (base) to 60.95% while reducing average reasoning length from 8,134 to 4,660 tokens (42.7% reduction). On AIME25 specifically, accuracy improves from 29.00% to 31.67% with tokens dropping from 12,779 to 6,977. On the 1.5B model, average accuracy improves from 46.68% to 49.91% with a 36% token reduction. The method outperforms all compared baselines (O1-Pruner, TokenSkip, EfficientReasoning, AdaptThink) on the accuracy-efficiency frontier. A key ablation validates that graph-based pruning preserves reasoning integrity: Graph-Pruned CoT achieves 93.70% accuracy and 90.69% consistency versus 73.60%/69.10% for naive length truncation to the same token budget. Behavioral analysis shows reduced frequency of reflection tokens ("wait", "hmm", "check") and increased use of progress tokens ("therefore") after training. Strengths: - The DAG representation is a principled, interpretable abstraction that enables semantically-motivated pruning rather than heuristic length penalties, with human evaluation confirming node-type accuracy (F1: 0.93 for review, 0.87 for progress) and 85.3% atomicity validity. - The three-stage training pipeline (SFT → DPO → GRPO) is well-motivated: each stage addresses a distinct failure mode — cold start for initialization, DPO for redundancy preference, GRPO for joint correctness-efficiency optimization. - The critical ablation comparing graph-pruned vs. length-truncated CoT definitively demonstrates that the gains come from structural intelligence, not simply shorter inputs. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Graph construction requires an external LLM call per reasoning chunk, adding non-trivial preprocessing overhead (reported as $20 for ~3,335 samples) that may limit scalability to large-scale training sets or deployment in lower-resource settings. - The progress/review node dichotomy is coarse: many reasoning steps blend reflection with genuine inference (e.g., checking a result that then informs the next step), and the binary labeling may incorrectly prune borderline-useful review nodes, as evidenced by the accuracy drop from 98.95% (Full-CoT) to 93.70% (Graph-Pruned) in the conditioning experiment. - Evaluation is restricted to mathematical reasoning benchmarks; generalization to open-ended tasks (code generation, multi-hop QA, long-form reasoning) is undemonstrated, and the authors acknowledge this explicitly as a limitation.
Recommended 2604.05643 PDF
#122 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Understand and Accelerate Memory Processing Pipeline for Disaggregated LLM Inference

Zifan He, Rui Ma, Yizhou Sun, Jason Cong · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Scores 58 for its genuinely useful unified four-step pipeline abstraction that subsumes diverse LLM memory techniques under a single framework, and the rigorous arithmetic intensity analysis that motivates the GPU-FPGA heterogeneous design. However, FPGA offloading of irregular, memory-bound workloads is a well-established systems technique, and the evaluation suffers from an unfair technology-node comparison (16nm FPGA vs 6nm GPU) that inflates reported gains.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: As LLMs increasingly rely on long-context techniques—sparse attention, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and compressed contextual memory—the overhead of memory processing during inference has grown substantially. Prior work treats these methods as isolated techniques, lacking a unified characterization of their computational demands or a systematic hardware strategy to accelerate them. Without such a framework, each optimization must be independently re-engineered for efficiency, and common bottlenecks go unexploited. The paper addresses this gap by profiling memory processing latency across diverse LLM inference paradigms and showing it constitutes 22%–97% of total inference time, making it the dominant performance bottleneck at scale. Methodology: The paper makes three coordinated contributions. First, it proposes a unified four-step memory processing pipeline—Prepare Memory, Compute Relevancy, Retrieval, and Apply to Inference—that subsumes sparse attention (DeepSeek Attention, SeerAttention-R, LServe), RAG (DRAGIN, FLARE, fixed-sentence, two-stage), synthesized memory (MemAgent), memory-as-context (Titans, HMT), and test-time training (TTT/LaCT). Second, it performs a systematic arithmetic intensity analysis of each pipeline step, revealing strong computational heterogeneity: Compute Relevancy and Retrieval are memory-bound with irregular access patterns (skinny matmul, top-k selection, BM25 scoring), while Prepare Memory and Apply to Inference involve regular, compute-intensive dense linear algebra. Third, motivated by this heterogeneity, the paper implements a GPU-FPGA heterogeneous system (AMD MI210 + Alveo U55C over PCIe) that offloads irregular, memory-bound steps to the FPGA while retaining compute-intensive operations on the GPU. The FPGA kernel employs a streaming dataflow architecture with a three-level on-chip memory hierarchy (BRAM at 21.8 TB/s, URAM at 10.4 TB/s, HBM at 460 GB/s) for storing compressed key vectors and a fused Compute Relevancy + Retrieval kernel using inner-product engines and parallel-reduction top-k. PCIe P2P data transfer bypasses system DRAM to minimize communication latency. Method-specific mappings handle MemAgent via prefill-decode disaggregation (FPGA decodes, GPU prefills) and Memory-as-Context via FPGA-resident memory with streamed retrieval outputs. Key Results: On AMD MI210 + Alveo U55C vs. GPU-only baseline: sparse attention achieves 1.04–1.49× end-to-end speedup (1.5–5.7× for the memory processing steps alone); single-stage RAG achieves up to 2.2× end-to-end speedup (5.1–7.65× for memory processing); Memory-as-Context achieves 1.3–1.6× end-to-end speedup; MemAgent achieves 1.8× end-to-end speedup. Energy efficiency improves 1.11–4.66× across methods, with MemAgent showing the largest gain (4.66× geomean) due to FPGA's lower operating power (~44W vs. 99W for GPU) and higher effective decoding throughput. FPGA on-chip memory provides ~5× higher bandwidth than GPU SRAM for Compute Relevancy and Retrieval. PCIe overhead is 3 orders of magnitude smaller than kernel computation time across all methods. The system scales well with batch size for sparse attention and RAG (increasing relative advantage) but degrades for MemAgent at batch size > 2, where the system dynamically falls back to GPU-only execution. Similar gains are estimated to hold for NVIDIA A100 configurations. Strengths: - The four-step pipeline abstraction is genuinely unifying—it captures a diverse set of LLM memory optimizations under a single framework, enabling a single hardware solution to benefit all of them simultaneously rather than requiring method-specific accelerators. - The arithmetic intensity analysis is rigorous and quantitative, grounding the heterogeneous system design in measured computational characteristics rather than intuition. - The energy efficiency results are compelling and practically relevant for serving cost, with the FPGA consuming roughly half the power of the GPU for equivalent workloads, and the MemAgent case showing a 4.66× improvement. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation uses an Alveo U55C fabricated in a 16 nm process vs. 6 nm for MI210, creating an unfair technology-node comparison that likely understates how much of the speedup is attributable to architectural design vs. process node gap. The A100 comparison is estimated, not directly measured on co-located hardware. - The MemAgent and large-batch cases reveal a fundamental limitation: the FPGA's throughput advantage collapses when dense matrix operations dominate (batch size > 2 for MemAgent), requiring dynamic fallback logic that adds system complexity and undermines the generality claim. - FPGA kernel development requires substantially more engineering effort than GPU kernels, and the deployment API only supports pre-built combinations; arbitrary new methods require custom HLS kernel design, limiting practical adoption without the design automation the authors acknowledge is future work.
Search Results 2603.29002 PDF
#123 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Xpertbench: Expert Level Tasks with Rubrics-Based Evaluation (2 ▲ )

Xue Liu, Xin Ma, Yuxin Ma, Yongchang Peng, Duo Wang, Zhoufutu Wen, Ge Zhang, Kaiyuan Zhang, Xinyu Chen, Tianci He, Jiani Hou, Liang Hu, Ziyun Huang, Yongzhe Hui, Jianpeng Jiao, Chennan Ju, Yingru Kong, Yiran Li, Mengyun Liu, Luyao Ma, Fei Ni, Yiqing Ni, Yueyan Qiu, Yanle Ren, Zilin Shi, Zaiyuan Wang, Wenjie Yue, Shiyu Zhang, Xinyi Zhang, Kaiwen Zhao, Zhenwei Zhu, Shanshan Wu, Qi Zhao, Wenhao Huang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 6 Apr 2026
Why this rank: XpertBench makes a solid contribution with its ShotJudge calibration methodology and unusually rigorous rubric design (15-40 binary checkpoints per task), addressing real shortcomings in LLM-as-a-judge evaluation. However, benchmark construction is a well-trodden genre, and the core findings — that models specialize by domain and cluster around 50% on hard expert tasks — are confirmatory rather than surprising.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard LLM benchmarks (MMLU-Pro, GPQA, HLE, FrontierMath) are rapidly saturating and, more fundamentally, rely on a closed-form "exam" paradigm that measures static knowledge recall rather than genuine expert-level problem-solving. Even benchmarks targeting agentic or deep-research capabilities (GAIA, BrowseComp) ultimately reduce complex workflows to point-estimate answers. Meanwhile, automated LLM-as-a-judge evaluation suffers from self-enhancement bias, and human evaluation does not scale to PhD/JD-level tasks. There is no benchmark that simultaneously covers authentic professional workflows at scale, uses fine-grained objective rubrics, and provides a calibrated yet scalable evaluation method. XpertBench aims to fill this gap. Methodology: XpertBench consists of 1,346 open-ended tasks across 80 categories in seven professional domains (Finance 18.1%, Law 16.0%, Education 24.4%, Engineering & Applied Sciences 20.4%, Humanities & Social Sciences 8.6%, Computer Science 6.8%, Healthcare 5.6%). Tasks are sourced from over 1,000 credentialed domain experts (CFAs, CPAs, MDs, JDs, active researchers) via a two-stage expert qualification pipeline, then filtered through multi-stage peer review for discriminative difficulty, real-world representativeness, and objective verifiability. Each task is paired with a rubric of 15–40 weighted checkpoints with dual-level importance tags (Essential/Important/Optional) and quantitative weights 1–10, decomposed into 16 evaluation dimensions (factual accuracy, logical coherence, domain expertise, novelty, etc.). The evaluation methodology, ShotJudge, uses GPT-5 responses as anchor baselines, which domain experts score with detailed rationales. These expert-scored exemplars serve as one-shot calibration inputs to an LLM judge (Gemini 2.5 Pro), which then scores candidate models per checkpoint via binary verdicts aggregated by a weighted sum. A curated stratified gold subset of 245 tasks (XpertBench-Gold) is used for primary evaluation. Reliability is measured via Consistency minus Discordance Rate (CDR). Key Results: ShotJudge achieves a CDR of 52.0%, substantially exceeding zero-shot LLM-as-a-judge baselines. Evaluation of 14 frontier models on XpertBench-Gold reveals a pronounced expert gap: the top model, Claude-Opus-4.6-thinking, scores only 66.20%, followed by GPT-5.4-high (64.78%) and Doubao-2.0-pro (64.51%). The majority of models cluster around 50%, and lower-tier models (Gemini-2.5-Pro, Doubao-1.6-thinking, GLM-4.6) score in the 38–44% range. Domain-specific specialization is extreme: GPT-5.4-high leads Finance (84.65%) by over 10 points over the next best model but scores only 42.84% in EAS/STEM; Claude-Opus-4.6-thinking leads Law (65.54%) and Humanities (83.02%); all models struggle in EAS/STEM (best: 49.58%). No single model dominates all domains. Key identified failure modes include retrieval interference (web browsing introducing extraneous noise), principle hallucinations (early conceptual errors cascading throughout reasoning chains), and pronounced domain specialization preventing any model from being a universal expert collaborator. Strengths: - Ecological validity is high: tasks originate from actual practitioner workflows rather than academic proxies or synthetic constructions, with experts limited to three submissions to enforce diversity. - Rubric design is unusually rigorous: 15–40 atomic, binary, objectively verifiable checkpoints per task with dual-level weighting provides far more granular and reliable scoring than Likert scales or generic dimensions. - ShotJudge's one-shot expert-calibration approach directly addresses self-enhancement bias in LLM-as-a-judge evaluation and achieves measurably better human-AI alignment (CDR 52.0%) at a fraction of full human evaluation cost. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The gold evaluation subset is only 245 tasks due to the cost of expert anchoring, limiting statistical power for domain-level comparisons (especially Computer Science with 92 total tasks and Healthcare with 75, both excluded from fine-grained domain analysis in this subset). - Expert pool is heavily skewed toward Chinese institutions (61% from Project 985/211 universities) and Chinese legal/regulatory contexts appear in example tasks, raising questions about geographic and jurisdictional generalizability for Law and Finance domains. - The benchmark is a static snapshot: unlike arena-style or adaptive benchmarks, contamination risk grows over time as frontier models are trained on more internet data, and there is no mechanism described for task refresh or anti-contamination verification.
Recommended 2604.02368 PDF
#124 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Bent optical waveguide finite element analysis with a 3D envelope Maxwell model

Jaime Mora-Paz, Stefan Henneking, Leszek Demkowicz, Jacob Grosek · arxiv.org · parmetis - new results
Why this rank: This paper extends an existing envelope Maxwell FEM framework to bent fiber geometry with a novel dual-PML construction in toroidal coordinates — a genuine but domain-specific technical contribution. The DPG formulation and hp-adaptive refinement are well-executed, and verification against semi-analytic bent-slab solutions spanning 14 orders of magnitude in loss is thorough. However, it builds directly on the authors' prior straight-fiber work, lacks ground-truth validation for the 3D coiled fiber case, and the high computational cost limits practical applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Accurate computation of optical confinement losses in circularly coiled (bent) fiber waveguides is essential for high-power fiber laser design, where differential bend loss is used to suppress higher-order modes and prevent thermally induced transverse mode instability (TMI). While prior work from this group developed full-vectorial Maxwell finite element models for straight fibers, real-world fiber systems are always coiled for packaging and thermal management. Extending 3D Maxwell simulation to bent geometries is non-trivial: wave propagation must be tracked over thousands of wavelengths in a toroidal domain with simultaneous radiation conditions in the longitudinal (circumferential) and transverse (radial) directions. Semi-analytic solutions for bent slab waveguides exist but not for 3D coiled fibers, leaving a gap that only a full boundary-value-problem approach can fill. Methodology: The authors adapt the vectorial envelope Maxwell formulation of [Henneking et al., 2025] from straight to bent fiber geometry. A full envelope ansatz E(x,r,θ) = e^{-ikr₀θ} E(x,r,θ) factors out the fast circumferential oscillation, reducing the problem to slowly varying envelope fields that can be resolved with far fewer degrees of freedom. The resulting modified Maxwell system, posed in toroidal curvilinear coordinates, introduces a skew-symmetric rotation operator K = k(r₀/r)(eθ×) that couples field components. Two distinct perfectly matched layers (PMLs) are constructed: one in the longitudinal (θ) direction via complex angle stretching, and one in the transverse radial direction (ρ) via complex coordinate stretching of the toroidal coordinate system. PML pull-back uses carefully derived Jacobians (cylindrical and toroidal) with Piola maps to preserve the H(curl) structure of the Maxwell system. The resulting boundary value problem is cast in an ultraweak variational formulation and discretized with the discontinuous Petrov-Galerkin (DPG) method, which delivers automatic stability and provides an elementwise residual norm as a built-in a posteriori error indicator for hp-adaptive mesh refinement. The implementation uses the hp3D finite element library with MPI+OpenMP parallelism, run on TACC Frontera (up to 7168 cores), with MUMPS for direct solves and parMETIS for dynamic load balancing after each adaptive refinement step. Key Results: Three experiments of increasing complexity verify the methodology. Experiment 1 (vacuum waveguide with PEC walls, essentially 2D): uniform h-refinements with polynomial orders p ∈ {3,4,5} confirm optimal h^{p/2} convergence rates for both the DPG residual and relative L² field error, consistent with anisotropic refinement theory. Experiment 2 (bent step-index open slab waveguide): adaptive refinement reduces the DPG residual by at least three orders of magnitude across all six mode/bend-radius combinations. Numerically computed bend-loss exponents converge to semi-analytic reference values for modes with significant loss — e.g., first odd mode at r₀=1300: theoretical -2.74478, computed -2.74303; second even mode at r₀=1300: theoretical -16.2649, computed -16.2698; second even mode at r₀=2600: theoretical -8.9680, computed -8.9582. The sole failure case is the first even mode at r₀=2600, where Im(β) = -3.2×10⁻⁶ is so small that numerical estimates plateau around -1.2×10⁻⁴ — an inherent double-precision floor problem, not a formulation deficiency. Experiment 3 (fully 3D bent step-index fiber, LP02 input mode): adaptive refinement grows the mesh from 8192 to 46,403 elements (24.4M DOFs); the irradiance decays nearly four orders of magnitude from the fiber input to 75% of θ_max for r₀=1300, with the power loss being dramatically stronger at r₀=1300 than r₀=2600, as expected from bend-loss physics. This constitutes the first adaptive finite element simulation of the 3D Maxwell problem for a bent optical fiber using this modeling approach. Strengths: - Mathematically rigorous end-to-end framework: well-posedness of the strong and ultraweak forms is inherited from classical Maxwell theory, and DPG provides guaranteed stability with a computable error indicator that drives mesh adaptivity. - Novel PML construction simultaneously in the longitudinal (θ) and transverse (ρ) directions of a toroidal domain, with careful Piola-map treatment of the complex-stretched Jacobians — a genuinely new technical contribution to computational photonics. - Quantitative verification against semi-analytic bent-slab loss exponents spanning more than fourteen orders of magnitude (Im(β) from ~10⁻² to ~16), demonstrating both accuracy and robustness across widely differing loss regimes. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method cannot resolve extremely small loss exponents (Im(β) ~ 10⁻⁶ or smaller): numerical estimates stagnate at ~10⁻⁴ due to double-precision accumulation errors in the Poynting flux integral, a fundamental bottleneck for nearly-lossless modes at large bend radii. - No ground truth exists for the 3D coiled fiber (Experiment 3), so convergence of the extracted bend-loss value with mesh refinement is not demonstrated — the experiment shows qualitative field evolution but stops short of a quantitative loss validation. - Computational cost is very high even after static condensation and adaptive refinement: 24M DOFs for a moderate 3D geometry, and the authors explicitly identify deteriorating direct-solver efficiency as the primary obstacle to extending the model to longer fibers and to the nonlinear TMI regime. - Elasto-optical effects (bending-strain-induced refractive index anisotropy) are explicitly excluded despite being physically significant in real coiled fibers, limiting the quantitative accuracy for practical engineering predictions.
Search Results 2604.00155 PDF
#125 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Agentic-MME: What Agentic Capability Really Brings to Multimodal Intelligence? (22 ▲ )

Qianshan Wei, Yishan Yang, Siyi Wang, Jinglin Chen, Binyu Wang, Jiaming Wang, Shuang Chen, Zechen Li, Yang Shi, Yuqi Tang, Weining Wang, Yi Yu, Chaoyou Fu, Qi Li, Yi-Fan Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 6 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Agentic-MME addresses a genuine gap in evaluating tool-augmented multimodal agents with its dual-axis process-verified evaluation and unified execution harness. The stepwise checkpoint methodology and overthinking metric are meaningful contributions to benchmark design. However, benchmark papers are inherently limited in methodological novelty, and the small scale (418 tasks) constrains statistical power.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are evolving from passive image describers into active agents that invoke tools—cropping, enhancing, or transforming images—and query the web for external knowledge. Existing benchmarks fail to evaluate this agentic behavior along three axes: (1) they decouple visual tool use from web search, leaving their synergy unstudied; (2) they lack unified execution harnesses that support heterogeneous tool interfaces; and (3) they evaluate only final-answer correctness, making it impossible to diagnose whether failures stem from wrong region crops, missed tool invocations, bad search queries, or insufficient planning. Agentic-MME is introduced to close all three gaps simultaneously. Methodology: Agentic-MME is a process-verified benchmark comprising 418 real-world tasks stratified across three difficulty levels: Level 1 (single visual operation), Level 2 (visual + web search chaining within ~3 steps), and Level 3 (iterative, interleaved visual-search workflows requiring hypothesis-verification loops). Tasks span 6 domains and 35 sub-categories; 43% require recovering evidence occupying less than 10% of the image area. The benchmark provides over 2,000 human-annotated stepwise checkpoints averaging 10+ person-hours per task. Evaluation proceeds along two axes: the V-axis audits Visual Expansion by verifying both the intent to invoke tools (Vto) and artifact faithfulness (Vtr); the S-axis audits Knowledge Expansion by checking search keywords, retrieved URLs, and intermediate answers against human references. Efficiency is measured via an "overthinking" metric penalizing excess tool calls relative to the human reference trajectory (2.15 calls/task). A unified execution harness supports both sandboxed Python code generation (Gen mode) and structured function-calling APIs (Atm mode) with an AST-based tracer for canonical operation extraction, enabling fair cross-model comparison. Key Results: Human solvers achieve 93.8% overall (82.3% on Level-3). The best model, Gemini 3 Pro in Atm mode, reaches 56.3% overall but only 33.3% on Level-3—a 49-point gap to humans on the hardest split. Without tools, Gemini 3 Pro scores 42.9% on Level-1 and collapses to 7.5% on Level-3; with tool access, Level-3 rises to 33.3%, a 4.4× improvement confirming tools are essential for complex tasks. Closed-source models consistently outperform open-source models: the best open-source model, Qwen3-VL-235B (Atm), achieves 34.9% overall and 8.1% on Level-3, while Thyme-rl collapses to 2.5% on Level-3. The S-axis reveals the mechanism: open-source models score below 5-20% on search planning vs. 25-30% for closed-source models. Atm mode uniformly matches or exceeds Gen mode; models in Gen mode frequently fail to invoke tools at all (GPT-5.2 Gen: Vto ≤7.6% across levels). Fine-grained error analysis identifies "Missing visual tool use" (29-51%) and "Overthinking Collapse" (15-27%) as the dominant failure modes, with "Unfaithful visual tool execution" (22-35%) a persistent third. Strengths: - Process-verified evaluation with dual-axis (S/V) stepwise checkpoints is a genuine methodological advance over outcome-only grading, enabling precise failure mode attribution. - The unified harness with AST-based tracing eliminates spurious advantages from heterogeneous sandbox environments, making cross-model comparisons unusually rigorous. - Step-wise oracle testing during dataset construction (using SOTA models to confirm each task is solvable given ground-truth intermediate observations) ensures failures during evaluation are attributed to planning/tool-execution deficits, not unanswerable tasks. - The overthinking metric operationalizes efficiency in a principled way, surfacing a distinct failure mode (redundant loops) that accuracy alone cannot detect. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is small (418 tasks), which limits statistical power for fine-grained per-category comparisons, especially at Level 3 (≈81 tasks). - MLLM-as-a-Judge (GPT-4o-mini) is used to evaluate intermediate visual artifacts and search checkpoints; while robustness checks against human experts show consistent Acc scores, V-axis scores vary by ≈2 points across judges, introducing non-trivial uncertainty in process metrics. - Level 3 tasks require internet-accessible search at evaluation time, introducing non-determinism in retrieved content and potential temporal drift as web content changes—despite caching mitigations. - The benchmark focuses exclusively on image-based tasks; video, audio, or mixed-modality agentic scenarios are outside scope.
Recommended 2604.03016 PDF
#126 — Moderately Innovative (57)

GeoBrowse: A Geolocation Benchmark for Agentic Tool Use with Expert-Annotated Reasoning Traces

Xinyu Geng, Yanjing Xiao, Yuyang Zhang, Hanwen Wang, Xinyan Liu, Rui Min, Tianqing Fang, Yi R. Fung · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: GeoBrowse fills a specific niche by combining ambiguous visual cue extraction with multi-hop knowledge retrieval in an agentic setting, and the two-level design provides clean diagnostic separation. The expert-annotated reasoning traces and in-trajectory image registry are useful contributions. However, the domain is narrow, the benchmark is small (300 instances) with geographic skew, and the GATE framework is a straightforward ReAct-style agent — the primary novelty lies in the benchmark construction rather than methodological innovation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing benchmarks for evaluating deep research agents either focus on text-only multi-hop browsing (BrowseComp) or treat images as a single step in text-driven reasoning chains. No benchmark jointly stresses ambiguous visual cue composition and hard, evidence-grounded multi-hop knowledge retrieval — the combination that characterizes real-world geolocation tasks. Prior geolocation datasets (MP-16, GLDv2, OSV, etc.) provide only coordinate supervision without expert reasoning traces, agentic tool evaluation, or chain-of-thought annotation. The paper fills this gap by introducing GeoBrowse, a benchmark that requires agents to iteratively manipulate images to extract weak visual cues, then verify hypotheses through open-web search in a BrowseComp-style multi-hop setting. Methodology: GeoBrowse consists of 300 instances at two difficulty levels. Level 1 (199 instances) targets visual-first geolocation from weak, fragmented cues (mean 3.21 cues per image), sourced from permissively licensed geolocation videos on YouTube and annotated by five geography experts with stepwise reasoning traces. Level 2 (101 instances) extends Level 1 by constructing BrowseComp-style queries: starting from the Level 1 geolocation answer as a root entity, a multi-hop path is sampled on the Wikipedia hyperlink graph (mean 8.42 hops), and Gemini-3-Pro rewrites the query with intermediate entities obfuscated, forcing agents to retrieve each hop sequentially. Quality control uses o4-mini to filter trivially easy or unsolvable instances, followed by three-expert validation requiring verifiable evidence traces. The authors also introduce GATE (Geolocation Agentic-workflow with Tool Enhancement), a ReAct-style agentic framework with nine tools: five think-with-image tools (Crop, Rotate, Auxiliary Lines, Super-Resolution, Pixel Analysis) and four knowledge tools (Web Text Search, Web Image Search, Visit, Code Interpreter). GATE maintains an in-trajectory image registry using stable img_id references to avoid context bloat and hallucinated pointers across multi-step visual manipulation. Key Results: GATE consistently outperforms both direct inference and open-source specialized agents across 12 MLLMs. On GPT-4o, GATE improves accuracy from 23.1% to 31.8% on Level 1 and from 11.9% to 21.8% on Level 2, exceeding search-only OmniSearch (24.6%/18.8%) and vision-only PyVision (29.6%/13.9%). The best configuration — GATE with Gemini-3-Pro backbone — achieves 48.2% on Level 1 and 34.7% on Level 2. Single-tool ablations show that on Level 1 the Image Processor alone reaches 38.6% (synergy gap of only 1.1% to all-tools), while Level 2 shows a larger 3.7% synergy gap, confirming that multi-hop knowledge tasks require coordinated tool use. Trajectory analysis reveals that correct runs by strong models (GPT-5, Gemini-3-Pro) hit 78–80% of annotated milestones, while incorrect runs still reach 57–59% — indicating failure during final evidence synthesis rather than retrieval. Weak models (Qwen-2.5-VL-7B) drop to 15% milestone coverage on incorrect runs, pointing to early perception failures. Error taxonomy shows that strong models fail primarily at synthesis (E6: ~38–40%), while weak models fail at perception and querying (E1+E2: ~74–84%). Strengths: - The two-level design cleanly separates visual cue composition from knowledge-intensive multi-hop reasoning, enabling fine-grained diagnostic analysis of where agents succeed and fail. - Expert-annotated stepwise traces with verifiable evidence paths enable trajectory-level analysis beyond simple accuracy — a methodological advance over prior geolocation benchmarks. - The in-trajectory image registry is a practical engineering contribution that addresses a real failure mode in multi-step multimodal agents (hallucinated image references breaking tool calls). - Rigorous quality control (o4-mini filtering + three-expert review + inter-annotator agreement reporting) lends credibility; country-level IAA κ = 0.92 and LLM-as-judge agreement ≥91% with human experts are strong validation signals. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is small (300 instances) and expert-curated, which introduces distributional skew: 46–62% of instances target country-level answers (coarsest granularity), and city-level accuracy is very low for all models, making it hard to draw conclusions about fine-grained localization from the current data. - Geographic coverage is uneven (Asia 47%, Africa 5%, Oceania 4%), limiting generalization claims; the predominance of Asian street-view imagery may favor models with stronger multilingual OCR capabilities. - Absolute accuracy numbers are low across the board (best system reaches 48.2%/34.7%), making it difficult to distinguish between benchmark ceiling effects and genuine model capability gaps — it is unclear whether the benchmark will remain discriminative as models improve. - Level 2 construction via Wikipedia hyperlink graph sampling may systematically favor Western-centric knowledge chains that are densely linked in Wikipedia, potentially introducing subtle biases in the types of multi-hop reasoning rewarded.
Citations 2604.04017 PDF
#127 — Moderately Innovative (57)

Detecting and Correcting Reference Hallucinations in Commercial LLMs and Deep Research Agents

Delip Rao, Eric Wong, Chris Callison-Burch · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: A thorough and practically valuable large-scale empirical study covering 221K URLs across 13 commercial systems, with the hallucinated-vs-stale decomposition providing clean diagnostic insight for distinct mitigation strategies. The urlhealth agentic correction results (6-79× reduction) demonstrate real impact. However, the methodology is straightforward measurement and classification rather than methodological innovation—the technical contribution is an 83-line URL checker and careful bookkeeping.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large language models and deep research agents routinely supply citation URLs to support their claims, yet users — including legal professionals, researchers, and clinicians — treat these citations as credible evidence. Prior work has documented hallucination of scholarly references from parametric memory, but no systematic study has measured citation URL reliability across commercial retrieval-augmented systems. Real-world harms (legal sanctions in Mata v. Avianca, ICLR paper retractions, fabricated medical reviews) motivate urgent characterization and mitigation. The paper fills this gap by asking a logically prior question to the attribution literature: not "does the source support the claim?" but "does the source exist?" Methodology: The study organizes around six research questions (RQ1–RQ6) spanning prevalence, system type, domain variation, failure composition, quantity-quality tradeoffs, and mitigation. Two datasets are used: DRBench (100 multilingual research queries, pre-collected outputs from 23 models, yielding 53,090 URLs across 10 selected models from Google, OpenAI, and Anthropic) and ExpertQA (2,177 expert-curated questions across 32 academic fields, 168,021 URLs from three search-augmented models). URL classification is hierarchical: HTTP HEAD/GET requests identify non-resolving URLs, then Wayback Machine API lookups distinguish hallucinated URLs (no archive record, never existed) from stale URLs (archived but currently dead). The taxonomy is formally: Hallucinated = Non-resolving − Stale. For mitigation, the authors release `urlhealth`, an 83-line Python tool that classifies URLs as LIVE, DEAD, LIKELY_HALLUCINATED, or UNKNOWN, and evaluate it in an agentic self-correction loop where three models iteratively verify and replace their own broken citations. Key Results: Across 10 DRBench models, hallucinated URL rates range from 3.0% (claude-3-5-sonnet-with-search) to 13.3% (gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch), with non-resolving rates of 5.4%–18.5%. Deep research agents hallucinate at 10.7% versus 4.8% for search-augmented LLMs (z=15.15, p<10⁻⁵¹), despite producing far more citations per query (113.1 vs. 3–24.3). Volume and reliability are inversely related — gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch produces the most URLs and has the highest hallucination rate. Failure composition is diagnostically revealing: four OpenAI search-augmented models show zero stale URLs (every non-resolving URL is fabricated), while Claude and openai-deepresearch show substantial stale fractions (4.8–6.6%), indicating genuine web retrieval operating over a partially outdated index. Domain effects are significant: non-resolving rates range from 5.4% (Business) to 11.4% (Theology) overall, with per-model variation reaching 4.3× for Claude Sonnet 4.5 (4.0% in Mathematics vs. 17.4% in Healthcare/Medicine). In agentic self-correction experiments, `urlhealth` reduces non-resolving rates by 6–79×: GPT-5.1 from 16.0% to 0.6%, Gemini from 6.1% to 0.1%, and Claude from 4.9% to 0.8% (all p<10⁻³⁵), with post-mitigation hallucination rates below 1% across all three models. Tool-use competence matters critically: gpt-5-nano called the verification tool but failed to act on results, leaving 48 hallucinated URLs after 14 correction rounds, while GPT-5.1 reduced the NOT LIVE rate to 0.6%. Strengths: - Large-scale, rigorous empirical study covering 221,111 total URLs across 13 models/agents from three major commercial providers, with bootstrap confidence intervals throughout and careful sensitivity analyses (403 exclusion, Reddit URL treatment, headless-browser audit of UNKNOWN URLs) - The hallucinated vs. stale decomposition is a conceptually clean and practically useful contribution — it separates fabrication (requiring better generation or grounding) from link rot (requiring fresher indices or post-hoc validation), informing distinct mitigation strategies - The `urlhealth` tool is lightweight (83 lines), open-source, model-agnostic, and requires no additional LLM inference, making it straightforwardly deployable; the agentic self-correction results (near-zero hallucination after tool use) demonstrate practical, large-magnitude impact Weaknesses / Limitations: - Hallucination estimates depend on Wayback Machine coverage, which is incomplete and non-uniform; some hallucinated URLs may be classified as stale (if Wayback archived a coincidentally matching page) and some real stale URLs as hallucinated (if never crawled), so all rates are lower-bound approximations with uncertain error direction at the individual URL level - The study is restricted to URL-based citations and explicitly excludes fabricated snippets and invented bibliographic metadata (wrong authors, DOIs, venues), which are equally harmful failure modes that remain unquantified; the paper therefore characterizes only one dimension of a multi-dimensional citation reliability problem - ExpertQA evaluation covers only three models due to API cost constraints, and DRBench uses pre-collected outputs without control over prompt variation or API version drift, limiting causal attribution of failure differences to model architecture versus deployment configuration
Search Results 2604.03173 PDF
#128 — Moderately Innovative (56)

Beyond Message Passing: Toward Semantically Aligned Agent Communication

Dun Yuan, Fuyuan Lyu, Ye Yuan, Weixu Zhang, Bowei He, Jiayi Geng, Linfeng Du, Zipeng Sun, Yankai Chen, Changjiang Han, Jikun Kang, Alex Chen, Haolun Wu, Xue Liu · arxiv.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The three-layer taxonomy grounded in communication theory provides a useful organizing framework, and the systematic comparison of 18 protocols is the most comprehensive survey of this rapidly evolving space to date. However, this is fundamentally a survey and classification contribution rather than a technical advance — the paper diagnoses the semantic gap convincingly but offers no concrete mechanisms to close it, and the qualitative scoring methodology limits the rigor of the comparative claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: As LLM-powered agents increasingly operate in tool-rich, multi-agent environments, communication protocols have become critical infrastructure. Yet existing protocols — MCP, A2A, ACP, and others — have been designed largely around ensuring reliable message delivery and syntactic compatibility, while neglecting the third and most critical layer of human communication: semantic alignment. When two agents successfully exchange a syntactically valid message but interpret it differently (e.g., "Book a flight to Springfield" without knowing which Springfield), the result is silent failure. The field lacks a principled framework for evaluating protocols on this semantic dimension, and practitioners have no systematic guidance for protocol selection. Methodology: The paper proposes a three-layer taxonomy inspired by human communication theory — drawing on Shannon-Weaver information theory, Grice's Cooperative Principle, and Clark's Theory of Common Ground. The three layers are: (1) Communication layer — reliable transport, streaming, security, delivery guarantees; (2) Syntactic layer — schema definitions, capability registration, lifecycle management, error codes; and (3) Semantic layer — intent clarification, context alignment across agents, and verification of whether actions match intended meaning. Using this taxonomy, the authors systematically score 18 protocols across 9 dimensions (transport, streaming, security, schema, lifecycle, error handling, clarification, context, verification) using a three-level qualitative scale (explicit support / partial / absent). The protocols span MCP, A2A, Agora, ACP variants (AGNTCY, IBM, AgentUnion), ACN, ANP, Coral, LOKA, LMOS, AITP, agents.json, Agent Protocol, SPP, PXP, WAP, and CrowdES. The paper further catalogs forms of "technical debt" accumulating in the ecosystem, and converts the analysis into a practical decision workflow for protocol selection. Key Results: The comparative analysis (Table 1) reveals a consistent pattern: the ecosystem is mature at the communication and syntactic layers but severely underdeveloped at the semantic layer. Most protocols score full marks on transport and schema, but almost universally score absent or partial on clarification, context alignment, and verification. Only A2A, Agora, and PXP provide relatively explicit semantic-layer support. MCP — the most widely deployed protocol — scores zero on all three semantic dimensions. The paper identifies three major categories of technical debt: (1) ecosystem fragmentation debt from incompatible protocol stacks requiring costly glue code; (2) communication-layer debt including inadequate session management for long-running async tasks and insufficient error semantics; and (3) syntactic/semantic debt including schema drift, performative ambiguity introduced by LLM stochasticity, ontological grounding failures, and contextual drift across multi-agent conversations. The decision workflow maps deployment scenarios to protocols: MCP for single-tenant tool invocation, ACP variants for intra-org streaming, A2A for cross-org delegation, Agora for recurrent low-cost interactions, AITP+Coral for trust-boundary transactions, LOKA for ethical governance. Strengths: - The three-layer taxonomy is well-grounded in linguistic and communication theory and maps cleanly onto real protocol design choices, giving the framework explanatory power beyond a simple feature checklist. - Coverage of 18 protocols with a consistent analytical lens produces the most comprehensive side-by-side comparison of this rapidly evolving landscape to date, including under-studied protocols (Agora, PXP, SPP, WAP) alongside dominant ones. - The technical debt taxonomy is practically actionable: naming specific debt forms (schema drift, performative ambiguity, contextual drift, ontological grounding debt) gives protocol designers concrete targets rather than vague "improve semantics" recommendations. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The scoring in Table 1 is qualitative and based primarily on protocol specifications rather than empirical implementations; protocols may behave quite differently in practice, and the coarse three-level scale obscures important distinctions within each level. - The paper identifies the semantic gap as the central problem but offers limited concrete technical proposals for how to close it — the "research agenda" in the conclusion is aspirational rather than prescriptive, making the gap between diagnosis and remedy significant. - The survey mixes protocols with very different scopes (e.g., the theoretical Spatial Population Protocols for robot localization alongside production systems like MCP and A2A), which strains the comparability claims and dilutes the practical takeaways for LLM-agent practitioners who are the implied audience.
Search Results 2604.02369 PDF
#129 — Moderately Innovative (56)

Compiled AI: Deterministic Code Generation for LLM-Based Workflow Automation

Geert Trooskens, Aaron Karlsberg, Anmol Sharma, Lamara De Brouwer, Max Van Puyvelde, Matthew Young, John Thickstun, Gil Alterovitz, Walter A. De Brouwer · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: The 'Compiled AI' framing — shifting LLM invocation from runtime to compile time with multi-stage validation — is a clean architectural insight with honest quantitative evidence on cost and latency reduction. The Code Factory variant for semantic tasks avoids a false dichotomy. However, the core idea of generating deterministic code from specifications is well-established, the evaluation covers only two narrow tasks, and the healthcare deployment claims are unsupported by empirical evidence on real clinical workflows.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Runtime LLM agent systems (AutoGen, LangChain) invoke a model at every transaction, producing high token costs, variable latency, and non-deterministic outputs. Non-determinism persists even at temperature=0—Atil et al. found up to 15% accuracy variance across runs, and Ouyang et al. documented 18–75% output variance due to MoE routing. In healthcare specifically, prior authorization, billing, and clinical document processing require determinism, full auditability, and regulatory compliance (HIPAA, CMS) that runtime inference cannot reliably provide. The paper asks: for well-specified, high-volume enterprise workflows, can the LLM be removed from the execution loop entirely? Methodology: The paper proposes "Compiled AI," defined by three properties: (1) one-time LLM invocation at compile time only, (2) zero-token deterministic execution thereafter, and (3) mandatory multi-stage validation before deployment. The system architecture—called a Code Foundry—takes a YAML workflow specification and produces a validated Temporal activity. An Orchestrator selects from a Template Library (synchronous handlers, batch processors, etc.) and Module Library (DB access, HTTP clients), assembles a prompt with domain-specific Prompt Blocks (e.g., HIPAA rules), invokes the LLM once to generate a narrow 20–50 line business-logic function, then passes the assembled artifact through four sequential validation stages: Security (Bandit, Semgrep, CWE checks), Syntax (AST parse, mypy, ruff), Execution (sandboxed fixture testing), and Accuracy (golden dataset comparison). On failure at any stage, the system regenerates with error context rather than deploying. For tasks requiring runtime semantic judgment (e.g., noisy document extraction), a "Code Factory" variant compiles bounded LLM calls as code artifacts with defined schemas, Pydantic output validation, and automatic retry—preserving deterministic overall flow while allowing narrow agentic invocations. Security uses a three-gate architecture: Input Gate (DeBERTa-v3 prompt injection + Presidio PII), Code Gate (static CWE analysis), and Output Gate (cryptographic canary tokens). Evaluation is on two tasks: Berkeley Function-Calling Leaderboard (BFCL, n=400) and DocILE invoice extraction (n=5,680). Key Results: On BFCL, compiled AI achieves 96% task completion (384/400) with zero execution tokens and a one-time 9,600-token generation cost. Break-even vs. Direct LLM occurs at ~17 transactions; at 1,000 transactions, compiled AI uses 57× fewer tokens than Direct LLM and 84× fewer than AutoGen. At 1M transactions/month, total cost of ownership is $555 vs. $22,000 for Direct LLM (40× reduction). Latency is 4.5ms P50 vs. 2,004ms for Direct LLM (450× improvement) with near-zero jitter. Compiled AI achieves 100% output reproducibility vs. 95% for runtime inference. All 16 failures (4%) are caught at compile time—deployed workflows run with 100% reliability. On DocILE, the Code Factory variant matches Direct LLM on KILE (80.0% vs. 80.0%) and achieves the highest LIR accuracy (80.4% vs. 74.5% for Direct LLM), at 2.3× lower latency. Pure regex achieves only 20.3% KILE, showing semantic tasks require at least bounded LLM involvement. Security evaluation across 135 test cases shows 96.7% accuracy on prompt injection detection (100% precision, zero false positives) and 87.5% on static code safety; output gate canary recall is 12.5%, acknowledged as an artifact of the simulation methodology. Strengths: - The compile-once-run-many architecture cleanly addresses the fundamental reliability and cost problems of runtime agent systems, with compelling and honest quantitative evidence across two qualitatively different task types. - The four-stage validation pipeline converts silent runtime failures into detectable compile-time failures—a meaningful engineering insight: without the accuracy gate, 4% of semantically wrong artifacts would deploy with 100% execution success. - The Code Factory variant is a principled middle ground for semantic tasks, avoiding the false dichotomy between full compilation and ad-hoc prompting, and it outperforms Direct LLM on LIR while matching it on KILE. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation scope is narrow: two task types is insufficient to establish broad generalizability, and the BFCL function-calling task is structurally highly favorable to compilation (clean, typed inputs). The claims about healthcare applicability rest almost entirely on a synthetic YAML example in the appendix, not an empirical study on real clinical workflows. - The security evaluation is weak in its most critical component: output gate canary recall is 12.5%, which the authors attribute to methodology but do not fix—this undermines the security claims for high-stakes healthcare deployment. The test sets are small (30 adversarial inputs for injection, 20 vulnerable fixtures for code safety) and synthetic, making the 96.7% headline figure difficult to interpret. - The paper does not address the specification quality problem substantively: the "specification problem" (can domain users write correct YAML?) is acknowledged in one sentence as a limitation but is central to whether the system would work in practice for healthcare workflows where clinical staff, not engineers, define business logic.
Search Results 2604.05150 PDF
#130 — Moderately Innovative (56)

OntoTKGE: Ontology -Enhanced Temporal Knowledge Graph Extrapolation

Dongying Lin, Yinan Liu, Shengwei tang, Bin Wang, Xiaochun Yang · arxiv.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: OntoTKGE addresses a real gap — sparse entities in temporal KG extrapolation — with a well-engineered plug-in framework, and the empirical gains are large and consistent. However, the individual components (CompGCN, contrastive alignment, gated fusion, entailment cones) are established techniques recombined rather than fundamentally new ideas. The automated ontology construction pipeline is practical but relies heavily on GPT-4o-mini and Wikidata coverage.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Temporal knowledge graph (TKG) extrapolation — predicting future facts (subject, relation, object, timestamp) from historical KG snapshots — struggles with sparse entities that have few or no historical interactions. Existing approaches such as LLM-DA (NeurIPS'24) and ANEL (WWW'25) attempt to patch this with LLM-derived logical rules or latent neighborhood structures, but both fail for sparse entities: LLM-DA cannot extract high-confidence rules when historical paths are scarce, and ANEL produces unreliable neighbor embeddings for entities with few facts. The paper identifies an overlooked information source — ontological knowledge (concept hierarchies and entity-to-concept mappings) — as a principled remedy: sparse entities can inherit behavioral patterns from popular entities sharing the same ontological concept. Methodology: OntoTKGE is a plug-in encoder-decoder framework that wraps any existing TKG extrapolation model. It has four main components. First, an automated ontology-view KG construction pipeline uses GPT-4o-mini, Wikidata SPARQL queries, and the ReFinED entity linker to assign every TKG entity to ontological concepts, producing a concept hierarchy G_O. Second, a Global Ontology-Aware Evolutionary Encoder runs a modified CompGCN over G_O (with per-layer independent relation vectors rather than a shared weight matrix, to capture diverse hierarchical semantics) to produce ontology-enriched initial entity embeddings H_g. These are fed as initializations to the base model's temporal encoder, which evolves them across KG snapshots. A hierarchical entailment loss (via entailment cones) constrains child embeddings to lie within the cone of their parent concepts, preserving partial-order semantics. Third, a Local Ontology-Aware Relevance Encoder builds a per-query N-hop subgraph of G_O around the query subject and runs a second independent CompGCN to produce supplementary embeddings H_l, specifically targeting the risk that global ontological signal attenuates through temporal recurrence. Fourth, a Contrastive-Enhanced Gated Fusion unit combines Z_{t+1} (from the global encoder) and H_l (from the local encoder) via a sigmoid gate, and applies a contrastive loss to align embeddings of the same entity across the two views while separating different entities. The final fused embedding is passed to the base model's decoder unchanged. The total loss combines TKG prediction loss, hierarchical entailment loss, and contrastive loss with weights α₁=α₂=0.1. Key Results: OntoTKGE is evaluated on ICEWS14, ICEWS18, ICEWS05-15, and GDELT using MRR, H@1, and H@10 under time-aware filtering. It is wrapped around five base models (RE-GCN, TiRGN, RETIA, LogCL, HisRES) and compared against JOIE, HyperCL, LLM-DA, and ANEL. Representative gains: RE-GCN MRR on ICEWS14 rises from .422 to .541 (+28%); TiRGN from .446 to .562 (+26%); LogCL from .485 to .597 (+23%); HisRES from .495 to .601 (+21%). OntoTKGE uniformly outperforms all baselines across all five base models and all four datasets. Entity degree analysis on ICEWS14 shows the largest gains for the sparsest entities (degree 0–10): RE-GCN MRR rises from .278 to .564 (+103%), directly validating the sparse entity hypothesis. Efficiency overhead is minimal: e.g., RE-GCN training time increases from 17s to 19s per epoch on ICEWS14. Ablation confirms all four components contribute, with the local encoder removal causing the largest drop (e.g., RE-GCN MRR falls from .541 to .426 without L-Enc). Strengths: - Highly practical plug-in design: wraps five heterogeneous base models with minimal architectural changes and negligible computational overhead (single-digit percent increases in training and inference time). - Automated ontology construction removes the need for curated ontologies, making the approach dataset-agnostic; the hybrid LLM+entity-linking pipeline handles both linked and unlinked entities. - The dual-encoder design (global temporal + local query-specific) is well-motivated: it explicitly addresses the known attenuation of ontological signal through recurrent temporal encoders, and the contrastive alignment between views is principled. - Empirical gains are large and consistent across all five base models and four benchmarks, including dramatic improvements for the key sparse-entity regime. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The automated ontology construction depends on GPT-4o-mini and Wikidata; quality degrades for domains poorly covered by Wikidata or for entities with no historical facts, where only coarse concept assignment via a retrieval model is possible. The paper does not quantify ontology construction quality or its impact on downstream performance. - The contrastive loss treats all non-query entities in a mini-batch as negatives, which is a weak negative sampling strategy that may not scale well to very large entity sets or yield well-calibrated embeddings. - Experiments are limited to geopolitical event datasets (ICEWS, GDELT); generalization to other TKG domains (biomedical, e-commerce) with different sparsity distributions and ontology structures is unvalidated.
Search Results 2604.05468 PDF
#131 — Moderately Innovative (56)

A Logical-Rule Autoencoder for Interpretable Recommendations

Jinhao Pan, Bowen Wei, Ziwei Zhu · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The negation-via-sign mechanism and learnable AND/OR gate are genuinely clever design choices for interpretable collaborative filtering, and the ablation showing the gate's dominant contribution is compelling. However, the model builds heavily on prior gradient grafting work, the baselines are dated (nothing post-2018), and the interpretability claim lacks quantitative validation beyond a single case study.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Most deep learning recommender systems operate as black boxes—their latent representations make individual recommendation decisions opaque and unverifiable. Prior work on explainable recommendations relies on post-hoc techniques (feature attribution, attention visualization, example-based rationales) that approximate model behavior after the fact and do not faithfully expose the true decision logic. This paper targets the stronger requirement of interpretability-by-design: a model whose full reasoning chain is directly readable from its parameters, with no post-hoc approximation. Methodology: The authors propose LIA (Logical-rule Interpretable Autoencoder), a rule-based autoencoder for collaborative filtering built around a Learnable Logical Rule Layer with K rule neurons. Each rule neuron k has a weight vector w_k ∈ [−1,1]^N and a scalar gate g_k. The sign of each weight encodes negation (positive literal if w_ki > τ, negated literal if w_ki < −τ, inactive otherwise, with τ=0.5), avoiding the parameter doubling of the naive concatenation approach. The gate g_k selects AND vs. OR operator per rule during end-to-end training. Because AND/OR are non-differentiable, LIA adopts decoupled logical activation functions and gradient grafting from prior work (Wang et al., TPAMI 2024): the forward pass produces binary {0,1} activations for interpretability while the backward pass routes gradients through continuous relaxations. A linear disjunctive aggregation layer then maps the K binary rule activations to item scores. Rules that activate for all or no users are pruned post-training, yielding a compact, human-inspectable rule set. The model is trained with multinomial cross-entropy loss. Key Results: On three benchmarks (ML100K, ML1M, Yelp) evaluated by NDCG@20, LIA outperforms all baselines: MF (.3484/.3058/.0747), Autoencoder (.3394/.3102/.0951), MultVAE (.3532/.3227/.0953), versus LIA (.3578/.3263/.0982). Gains over the strongest baseline MultVAE are modest but consistent (~1.3% on ML100K, ~1.1% on ML1M, ~3.0% on Yelp). Ablation confirms the learnable gate is the critical component: replacing it with a fixed equal split of AND/OR rules drops NDCG@20 by 12.5%, 16.7%, and 40.9% on the three datasets.
