#137 — Moderately Innovative (54)

Think Twice Before You Write--an Entropy-based Decoding Strategy to Enhance LLM Reasoning

Jiashu He, Meizhu Liu, Olaitan P Olaleye, Amit Agarwal, M. Avendi, Yassi Abbasi, Matthew Rowe, Hitesh Laxmichand Patel, Paul Li, Tao Sheng, Sujith Ravi, Dan Roth · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: HN-decode applies the well-established idea of entropy-based uncertainty to guide search-time branching, concentrating compute at genuinely uncertain token positions rather than uniformly. The cost-performance results are striking, but the method relies on white-box access to token distributions, limiting deployment scenarios, and entropy-guided exploration is well-trodden territory in active learning and uncertainty sampling. The evaluation scope is narrow — only math reasoning on very small test sets — leaving generalization to other reasoning domains unverified.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard LLM decoding strategies—greedy search, beam search, top-k/nucleus sampling, and self-consistency—treat all token positions as equally important, allocating search effort uniformly regardless of the model's local uncertainty. In multi-step reasoning tasks (mathematics, logic), a single wrong token at a decision-critical point can cascade irreversibly through the entire chain. Self-consistency mitigates this by sampling many full rollouts and majority voting, but wastes computation on unambiguous positions and never targets the actual fault points. The gap motivates an approach that concentrates exploration at the moments of maximum uncertainty. Methodology: The paper proposes HN-decode, an entropy-guided parallel decoding framework. At each generation step the model computes the Shannon entropy H(p) = -∑ p_i log p_i of the next-token distribution. High-entropy positions are flagged as "vulnerable tokens"—points where the model's knowledge is insufficient to commit confidently. Rather than re-sampling a full trajectory (self-consistency style), the algorithm breaks the current rollout at each high-entropy position and stores the prefix in a job pool, branching only among the top-k alternative tokens at that point. Subsequent generation from each branch continues greedily or with standard top-k decoding until a terminal answer is reached. An Entropy After </Think> (EAT) stopping criterion evaluates whether to accept a completed rollout by computing mean (μ) and variance (σ) of token entropies at reasoning-end boundaries; only rollouts satisfying μ < τ₁ and σ < τ₂ are accepted, otherwise additional rollouts are drawn. Hyperparameters (max branching degree 3, max depth 3, max jobs 32, τ₁=2.3, τ₂=9.8) are optimized via Optuna on the GSM8K training split. Key Results: On GSM8K, Llama-3.1-8B improves from 84.2% (greedy pass@1) to 99.6% accuracy with a mean of only 1.4 jobs per problem. GPT-4o reaches 100% accuracy. On the harder AMC2023 (40 problems), Llama-3.1-8B rises from 45.0% to 97.5%; GPT-4o from 75.0% to 98.25%. On GPT-5-generated perturbed variants (2,242 GSM8K, 320 AMC problems), Llama-3.1-8B with HN-decode achieves 99.2% vs. GPT-5's 96.6% on perturbed GSM8K, at API cost of ~0.05¢ vs. 0.52¢ per question (~10× cheaper). An ablation against random-token branching confirms entropy-guided selection consistently outperforms random branching under identical generation budgets. Hyperparameter sensitivity is low across the ranges tested. Strengths: - Principled, adaptive allocation of search budget: concentrating branching at genuinely uncertain positions rather than uniformly, yielding high accuracy with very few extra rollouts (mean jobs ≤1.9 even on hard problems). - Striking cost-performance tradeoff: an 8B open-source model with HN-decode matches or exceeds proprietary frontier models at 10-33× lower API cost, a practically impactful result. - EAT stopping criterion is a clean inference-time signal that avoids training a separate verifier, leveraging the model's own internal entropy as a self-consistency proxy. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Entropy is a poor proxy for reasoning difficulty when the model is confidently wrong: systematic misconceptions or spurious statistical correlations will show low entropy at the wrong token, so HN-decode will not branch there—the fundamental failure mode is invisible to the method. - Evaluation scope is narrow and sample sizes are very small: AMC2023 contains only 40 test problems, making per-problem accuracy estimates highly variable. No evaluation on code generation, formal logic, or multi-hop QA, which are the natural next tests for a general reasoning decoding strategy. - The method requires white-box access to token-level probability distributions; it is inapplicable to black-box API models (e.g., GPT-5 cannot be used as the backbone, only as a comparison target), significantly limiting deployment scenarios.
Search Results 2604.00018 PDF
#138 — Moderately Innovative (53)

When Users Change Their Mind: Evaluating Interruptible Agents in Long-Horizon Web Navigation

Henry Peng Zou, Chunyu Miao, Wei-Chieh Huang, Yankai Chen, Yue Zhou, Hanrong Zhang, Yaozu Wu, Liancheng Fang, Zhengyao Gu, Zhen Zhang, Kening Zheng, Fangxin Wang, Yi Nian, Shanghao Li, Wenzhe Fan, Langzhou He, Weizhi Zhang, Xue Liu, Philip S. Yu · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: InterruptBench addresses a real and underexplored problem—mid-task user intent changes in grounded web environments—and the three-type interruption taxonomy (Addition, Revision, Retraction) with paired-outcome efficiency analysis is a clean methodological contribution. The finding that token overhead dominates action overhead and that model scale determines reconciliation ability is useful. However, the work is purely diagnostic with no proposed solutions, the benchmark derives entirely from WebArena-Lite's 165 tasks, and the low absolute performance ceilings make it difficult to separate interruption-specific failures from general web agent limitations.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM agents are increasingly deployed for long-horizon, multi-step tasks in dynamic environments, yet existing benchmarks assume static, uninterrupted user intent. In practice, users regularly revise goals mid-execution — adding requirements, correcting errors, or retracting constraints. Prior work on interruptions studied only short-horizon language tasks without grounded environmental state, leaving a critical gap in understanding how agents adapt when actions have persistent consequences and user intent changes mid-task. Methodology: The paper introduces InterruptBench, a benchmark derived from the 165-task WebArena-Lite suite covering five web domains (Reddit, GitLab, CMS, Map, OneStopShop). Three interruption types are formalized: Addition (user adds missing details), Revision (user corrects an erroneous initial query), and Retraction (user removes over-specified constraints). Interruption scenarios are synthesized via LLM (Claude-Opus-4.5) under strict constraints: combined initial query + interruption must be semantically equivalent to the original task and yield the same ground-truth answer; each interruption message must be individually necessary. Interruptions are injected at a relative position (default 60%) of the pre-computed baseline trajectory, preserving progress-awareness across tasks of varying length. Six LLM backbones (Claude Haiku/Sonnet/Opus-4.5, Qwen3-480B-A35B, DeepSeek-V3.1, Mistral-Large-3) are evaluated within the WebAgent-R1 scaffolding using three complementary metrics: budget-limited success rate SR(k) measuring post-interruption convergence speed, paired-outcome efficiency breakdowns (S/F, F/S, S/S, F/F quadrants tracking token and action deltas), and multi-turn performance as interruptions accumulate from n=1 to n=3. Key Results: All models show rapid SR(k) improvement within the first ~10 post-interruption actions, then plateau at model-specific ceilings. Under Addition at k=30: Opus reaches ~55%, Sonnet ~44%, Haiku ~38%, while open-weight models trail (Qwen ~26%, DeepSeek ~30%, Mistral ~23%). Revision baselines stay near zero for most models, confirming success critically depends on integrating the correction — Opus exceeds 50% by k≈15. Retraction baselines are stronger, indicating earlier task completion under tight budgets is the main benefit, not a higher ceiling. Efficiency analysis reveals token overhead dominates over action overhead: under Addition, Haiku adds +1699 tokens on average vs. Opus's +138, while action deltas are near zero (Opus: -0.99). The F/F quadrant (both fail) incurs the highest waste — Haiku burns +2624 tokens before failing. In multi-turn settings (Table 2), Claude models improve monotonically: Opus reaches 41.82% at n=3 (vs. 5.45% at n=0), roughly doubling from n=1 to n=3. DeepSeek slightly regresses at n=3, illustrating interference between successive updates. Interruption timing analysis shows large models (Opus) improve further with later interruptions while small models (Haiku, Mistral) degrade, suggesting weaker reconciliation of committed environment state. Strengths: - Rigorous benchmark design with three semantic constraints (consistency, necessity, authenticity) enforced and manually verified, ensuring each interruption is non-trivial and grounded in real task state. - Dual-axis evaluation separating effectiveness (SR) from efficiency (token/action cost) via paired-outcome quadrants — a methodologically clean decomposition that reveals failure modes invisible to aggregate accuracy alone. - Breadth of experimental coverage: six diverse models, three interruption types, single- and multi-turn settings, four interruption timing positions, and five web domains, providing a comprehensive characterization of the problem space. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is entirely derived from WebArena-Lite's 165 tasks, limiting scale and diversity; performance ceilings are low even for the strongest models (~55% for Opus), partly reflecting WebArena's inherent difficulty rather than isolating interruption-specific failure. - Interruption data is synthesized by an LLM (Claude-Opus-4.5) and then manually spot-checked, but the paper does not report inter-annotator agreement or the fraction of generated scenarios rejected, leaving data quality partially unverified at scale. - No proposed mechanisms or training interventions are offered to improve interruption handling — the paper is purely diagnostic, providing no baselines for how agents could be improved beyond the status quo.
Search Results 2604.00892 PDF
#139 — Moderately Innovative (53)

Adaptive Implicit Feedback Weighting: Addressing Signal Saturation and User Heterogeneity in Collaborative Filtering

researchsquare.com · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The Power-Lift formulation is a clean mathematical contribution that resolves real numerical instabilities in PMI-based weighting, and the Robust User-Centric strategy is well-motivated by non-parametric statistics. Evaluation across five datasets with diverse density and feedback regimes is thorough for the scope claimed. However, the work is fundamentally an engineering study of confidence weighting within classical ALS/KNN—no modern architectures are tested, and strategy selection remains dataset-dependent with no principled guidance.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard Weighted Matrix Factorization (WMF) models for implicit feedback recommender systems assume that user preference grows continuously and unboundedly with raw interaction counts, using static global formulas (linear or logarithmic scaling). This fails to account for two real-world phenomena: (1) signal saturation — beyond some threshold, additional interactions (e.g., hours played) no longer signal stronger preference but rather habit or background consumption; and (2) user heterogeneity — ten interactions from a power user may be noise, while ten from a casual user may signal strong preference. The paper formalizes these as the "Saturation Hypothesis" and proposes adaptive, per-user confidence weighting strategies to correct for both. Methodology: The paper proposes three families of confidence weight functions for WMF, evaluated on top of ALS and KNN models. (1) Global saturation: Log-IDF (log dampening with popularity penalty) and Sigmoid Propensity Weighting, which applies a sigmoid with a dynamically computed mean saturation point μ = mean(log r_ui) to create a hard asymptotic cap on confidence, multiplied by an IDF popularity penalty. (2) Local contextualization: Robust User-Centric Weighting, which normalizes each interaction against the user's own median and IQR (robust non-parametric statistics), then passes through a sigmoid — making weights relative to personal behavioral baseline rather than global averages. (3) Statistical lift: Power-Lift, a numerically stable generalization of PMI that replaces the log(Lift(u,i)) formula with Lift(u,i)^p for tunable p > 0. Unlike standard PMI, Power-Lift's base is always strictly positive, eliminating the need for zero-clipping that introduces gradient discontinuities. Hyperparameters are tuned via Optuna (20 trials per strategy) on a 81/9/10 train/val/test split, with ALS latent factors and KNN neighborhood size frozen to isolate the effect of weighting. Evaluation uses NDCG@20 and Precision@20 across five datasets spanning a density range from 6.3% (MovieLens 100k) to 0.0079% (Steam), with feedback types ranging from capped 1-5 ratings to unbounded play counts and hours played. Key Results: Power-Lift achieves a 54.3% relative improvement in NDCG@20 over the unweighted ALS baseline on Steam (0.0713 vs. 0.0462), where continuous hours-played values cause severe skew. On MovieLens 100k, Sigmoid Propensity is best for ALS (0.3623 NDCG vs. 0.3188 unweighted, +13.6%), and Power-Lift leads for KNN (0.3142 vs. 0.2650, +18.6%). On MovieLens 20M, Power-Lift wins for ALS (0.3375 vs. 0.3239 baseline, +4.2%). On Taste Profile (48M audio interactions), Power-Lift leads KNN with 0.1692 NDCG vs. 0.1197 baseline (+41.4%). On Last.fm, BM25 wins for ALS (0.1931 NDCG). Notably, strict unit normalization catastrophically degrades performance on skewed datasets (KNN on Steam: 0.0004 NDCG with Normalization, 0.0000 with Sigmoid Propensity). A secondary finding shows that a k=10 ALS model with Sigmoid weighting (0.3313 NDCG) outperforms an unweighted k=100 model (0.3188 NDCG) on MovieLens 100k, demonstrating that weighting can substitute for model capacity. Strengths: - Power-Lift cleanly resolves the mathematical instability of standard PMI (negative logs, zero-clipping, gradient discontinuities) via a power-law formulation that is always positive and continuously differentiable, with a single interpretable tuning parameter. - The Robust User-Centric strategy is well-motivated: using median and IQR instead of mean and std makes normalization resistant to bursty behavior, and normalizing against each user's own distribution directly addresses heterogeneity without any global assumptions. - Evaluation spans five datasets with very different statistical regimes (dense/sparse, capped/unbounded, small/large), and all weighting computations are reported to take under 7 seconds even at 48M interactions, confirming production scalability. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper restricts evaluation to ALS and KNN, deliberately excluding modern autoencoder (EASE^R, VaeCF) and graph-based (NGCF, LightGCN) methods. While the justification (continuous input incompatibility) is reasonable, it leaves open whether the proposed strategies benefit or could be adapted for the architectures that currently dominate leaderboards. - Optimal weighting strategy is dataset-dependent with no principled selection criterion: Power-Lift dominates on skewed datasets but underperforms BM25 on Last.fm for ALS; Sigmoid Propensity fails completely on Steam for KNN (NDCG 0.0000). The paper offers no guidance on how to choose a strategy without running all of them, which limits practical deployability. - The Robust User-Centric strategy degenerates for cold-start users with fewer than three interactions (median/IQR are uninformative), requiring a fallback to global weights — a significant limitation for real systems with high user churn, and the fallback strategy is not evaluated.
Citations
#140 — Moderately Innovative (53)

Ontology -Constrained Neural Reasoning in Enterprise Agentic Systems: A Neurosymbolic Architecture for Domain-Grounded AI Agents

Thanh Luong Tuan · arxiv.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The empirical identification and quantification of the inverse parametric knowledge effect—ontological grounding yielding ~2× improvement for non-English enterprise domains—is a useful and actionable finding for practitioners. Production deployment across 21 verticals lends credibility. However, the three-layer ontology architecture is a competent integration of known neurosymbolic ideas rather than a fundamentally new approach, and the small experiment with LLM-as-judge evaluation and a curated RAG baseline that shares the same ontology source creates circularity concerns.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Enterprise adoption of LLMs is constrained by hallucination, domain drift, and inability to enforce regulatory compliance at the reasoning level. Unlike general-purpose assistants, enterprise agents must use industry-specific terminology correctly, reason within regulatory bounds (SOX, Basel III/IV, HIPAA, GDPR), follow organizational workflows, and adapt to role-specific perspectives. Existing approaches—prompt engineering, RAG, and fine-tuning—each address individual dimensions but lack a unified formal framework. Prompt engineering is brittle; RAG retrieves but does not reason; fine-tuning is expensive and domain-locked. The paper targets this "enterprise grounding problem" as a neurosymbolic integration challenge. Methodology: The paper presents a three-layer enterprise ontology framework O = ⟨R, D, I⟩ implemented in the Foundation AgenticOS (FAOS) platform. The Role Ontology (R) encodes role-specific decision patterns, KPI focus, communication style, and approval authority. The Domain Ontology (D) captures hierarchically organized industry verticals, entity definitions, metric benchmarks with healthy ranges, and governance constraints. The Interaction Ontology (I) formalizes handoff patterns, approval chains, and escalation paths between roles. At runtime, a ContextResolver assembles ontological context into a priority-ordered prompt injection (Role > Domain > Interaction, capped at 2000 tokens). Tool discovery uses a SQL-pushdown scoring function combining semantic similarity, hierarchical domain matching, capability overlap, and role alignment—achieving sub-100ms discovery across 600+ registered skills. A "max rule" governance filter ensures multi-domain skills must meet the strictest applicable regulatory threshold. The paper introduces a taxonomy of neurosymbolic coupling levels (L0–L5), characterizes the current system as L2–L3 (input-side and partial process-side coupling), and proposes L4–L5 output-side validation via OWL-based constraint checking and supervised ontology evolution. Key Results: A 600-run controlled within-subject experiment (50 tasks × 4 conditions × 3 repetitions) across five regulated industries—FinTech, Insurance, Healthcare, Vietnamese Banking, and Vietnamese Insurance—compared Ungrounded (C1), RAG-Only (C2), Ontology-Coupled (C3), and Ontology+Process (C4) conditions. Ontology-coupled agents significantly outperformed ungrounded agents on Metric Accuracy (χ²=20.71, p<.001, W=.460), Regulatory Compliance (p=.003, W=.318), and Role Consistency (p<.001, W=.614), all with large effect sizes. The "inverse parametric knowledge effect" was empirically confirmed: Vietnamese-language domains showed ~2× the improvement of English-language domains (∆=+.29 for Banking VN vs. avg ∆=+.12 for English domains), because SBV circulars, MoF insurance decrees, and local metric thresholds are severely underrepresented in English-centric LLM training corpora. Role Consistency improvements were language-invariant (+.27 across all five industries), suggesting role-specific decision patterns are uniformly absent from parametric knowledge. A notable negative result emerged: for well-known concepts such as "combined ratio" and "persistency rate," ontology injection reduced Terminological Fidelity scores (e.g., IN-T1: .81→.50), hypothesized as a "context displacement" mechanism where injected content crowds out useful parametric recall. RAG (C2) was competitive with structured ontology (C3) on aggregate metrics, but this is attributed to the curated RAG baseline being drawn from the same ontology blueprints—a known limitation. The FAOS production system serves 21 industry verticals with 650+ agents. Strengths: - Empirically identifies and quantifies the inverse parametric knowledge effect, providing actionable guidance: ontological grounding is most valuable—and potentially mandatory—for non-English enterprise domains with sparse LLM pretraining coverage. - Honest and careful delineation between implemented (L1–L3) and proposed (L4–L5) contributions, avoiding overclaiming on unbuilt components. - Production deployment evidence across 21 verticals and 650+ agents lends credibility beyond typical research prototypes. - The SQL-pushdown tool discovery design is a practically significant engineering contribution enabling sub-100ms semantic skill selection at scale. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Single-system study (FAOS only): generalization to other agentic frameworks (CrewAI, AutoGen, standalone LangGraph) is undemonstrated, and evaluation uses only one LLM backbone (Claude Sonnet 4). - LLM-as-judge reliability is not validated against human domain experts (inter-rater κ unreported); the TF regression on well-known concepts may partially reflect judge sensitivity artifacts rather than true context displacement. - The curated RAG baseline (chunks extracted from the same ontology blueprints defining C3) is an artificially favorable comparison, understating the real ontology advantage in production settings with noisy document corpora. - Output-side validation (L4–L5)—the paper's central architectural proposal—is entirely unimplemented and unevaluated; OWL reasoning latency feasibility and the error-prone step of translating free-text LLM outputs into OWL-compatible representations remain open engineering problems. - The experiment is small (50 tasks, 600 total runs with only 3 repetitions per task-condition pair), and ground truth is ontology-derived rather than independently validated by domain experts, creating circularity risk in the evaluation design.
Search Results 2604.00555 PDF
#141 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Stable and Privacy-Preserving Synthetic Educational Data with Empirical Marginals: A Copula-Based Approach

Gabriel Diaz Ramos, Lorenzo Luzi, Debshila Basu Mallick, Richard Baraniuk · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The structural guarantee of regeneration stability via empirical marginal anchoring is a genuinely useful insight, and the integrated two-level differential privacy budget is well-designed. However, Gaussian copulas are a well-established tool, and the core methodology is a relatively straightforward non-parametric extension of an existing framework. The utility gap vs. TVAE and the absence of comparisons to modern tabular synthesizers (TabSyn, TabDDPM) weaken the empirical case.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Educational Data Mining (EDM) and Learning Analytics face a reproducibility crisis rooted in privacy constraints: audits of LAK and EDM proceedings found that fewer than 15% of papers shared or linked to open datasets. Existing synthetic tabular data generators — deep learning models (CTGAN, TVAE) and parametric copulas (PGC) — systematically distort marginal distributions, particularly for skewed, multi-modal, or low-frequency features common in educational data. A second problem, less studied in tabular synthesis, is regeneration instability: when synthetically generated data is reused to train subsequent models (a Synthetic Feedback Loop, SFL), iterative regeneration compounds marginal distortion into progressive variance collapse and loss of distributional support, analogous to model collapse in vision and language models. The paper targets both problems simultaneously. Methodology: The authors propose the Non-Parametric Gaussian Copula (NPGC), a three-stage generative pipeline grounded in Sklar's theorem. Stage 1 (Marginal Transformation): each column is mapped to (0,1) via its empirical CDF — continuous columns via histogram-based linear interpolation, integers via dequantization, and categoricals via empirical mass function sampling. Missing values are encoded as a disjoint upper interval of (0,1), preserving missingness correlations. Differential privacy (ε=1 default, split evenly as ε_m + ε_c = ε) is applied here via the Laplace mechanism on histogram or category counts before CDF normalization. Stage 2 (Correlation and Gaussian Sampling): the rank-transformed data is projected to standard Gaussian space via the probit function, and an empirical correlation matrix is estimated. Calibrated Laplace noise is added to the correlation matrix, and positive semi-definiteness is enforced via eigen-decomposition clipping. Correlated synthetic Gaussian samples are drawn via Cholesky factorization. Stage 3 (Marginal Reconstruction): synthetic Gaussian samples are mapped back through the stored empirical inverse CDFs, exactly recovering the privatized empirical marginals by construction. The method has O(pn log n + np² + p³) fitting complexity and O(mp²) typical sampling complexity, far below deep learning alternatives. Regeneration stability is structurally guaranteed because each new generation re-anchors to the same stored empirical marginals, preventing iterative drift. Key Results: Evaluated on five UCI datasets (Adult, Balance Scale, Nursery, Student Dropout, Student Performance) against CTGAN, TVAE, Copula-GAN, and PGC. Fidelity (SDMetrics Overall Score): NPGC achieves 0.962 vs. the next best PGC at 0.897, a 6.5 pp gap; Column Shapes (marginal agreement) 0.985 vs. 0.918 for CTGAN. Privacy (Discriminator AUC, closer to 0.5 is better): NPGC 0.801, best among all methods; DCR Share 0.527, also best. Downstream utility (TSTR accuracy drop): NPGC 16.99% vs. TVAE 8.66% (best) and CTGAN 25.73% (worst) — NPGC is second-best. Training time: NPGC 0.65 s vs. CTGAN 821 s and TVAE 268 s, a ~400–1200× speedup. Regeneration stability over 10 SFL iterations on Adult: NPGC Overall Score degrades only 1.16 pp (96.43% → 95.27%), while Copula-GAN collapses 39.7 pp (88.93% → 49.25%), CTGAN 28.9 pp, TVAE 23.8 pp, and PGC 5.8 pp. In a real-world deployment on 7,506 xAPI interaction records with severe imbalance (Hint events < 0.2%), NPGC faithfully preserves all six activity-type proportions including extreme minorities. Strengths: - The empirical marginal anchoring provides a structural guarantee of regeneration stability rather than an empirical claim — variance collapse cannot occur because each generation resamples from the same stored empirical CDF, making NPGC immune to SFL by construction. - Differential privacy is integrated at both the marginal and correlation levels with a principled ε-budget split, unlike most DP tabular synthesizers that address only one level. - Extreme computational efficiency (0.65 s fit, <1.2 s sample) and a simple sklearn-style API make the method practically deployable with no hyperparameter tuning, which is critical for resource-constrained EDM researchers. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The Gaussian copula captures only linear dependence in the rank-transformed space; threshold effects, ceiling/floor artifacts, and nonlinear cross-feature interactions common in educational data (e.g., prior knowledge × intervention response) are not modeled, which likely explains the 8 pp utility gap relative to TVAE. - Regeneration stability is evaluated on a single dataset (Adult), which is not an educational dataset — the central claim of stability in educational contexts rests on indirect evidence, and generalization to high-cardinality educational features (course IDs, learning objective codes) is explicitly acknowledged as an open question. - The comparison set is limited to SDV-framework baselines; newer methods such as TabSyn, TabDDPM, and GReaT are excluded, so the fidelity advantage over the current state-of-the-art in tabular synthesis is not established.
Citations 2604.04195 PDF
#142 — Moderately Innovative (52)

ID-Selection: Importance-Diversity Based Visual Token Selection for Efficient LVLM Inference

Zhaohong Huang, Wenjing Liu, Yuxin Zhang, Fei Chao, Rongrong Ji · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The iterative score-suppression mechanism via Gaussian kernel is an elegant unification of importance and diversity objectives, and the theoretical framing showing it subsumes both extremes is clean. However, it remains an incremental advance in the crowded visual token pruning space, with the strongest variant (unified score) inapplicable to the increasingly dominant architectures (Qwen, InternVL) that lack paired text encoders.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) like LLaVA and Qwen2.5-VL encode images into hundreds to thousands of visual tokens, creating substantial inference overhead — Qwen-2.5-VL handles up to 16,384 tokens. Existing token pruning approaches split into two camps: importance-based methods (e.g., FastV, FasterVLM) that retain high-attention tokens but suffer from redundancy as similar tokens cluster together, and diversity-based methods (e.g., DivPrune, DART) that maximize dissimilarity but may discard informative tokens in favor of dispersed yet low-value ones. Prior hybrid attempts (VisPruner, CDPruner) either evaluate importance and diversity in disjoint stages or resort to computationally complex DPP-based subset optimization. The gap is a simple, unified mechanism that jointly balances informativeness and coverage. Methodology: ID-Selection is a training-free, plug-and-play token selection algorithm with O(NT) complexity (N original tokens, T retained tokens). Each visual token is first assigned an importance score; for LLaVA-family models, three estimators are provided: (1) cross-modal attention from the LLM's second layer (FastV-style), (2) [CLS] attention from the CLIP visual encoder (FasterVLM-style), and (3) a unified score multiplying min-max normalized cosine similarity between instruction features and visual embeddings by [CLS] attention. For architectures lacking a paired text encoder (Qwen2.5-VL, InternVL2.5), cross-modal attention is used. The core innovation is diversity-aware iterative selection: at each step the highest-scoring token is selected, then the scores of remaining tokens are suppressed by a Gaussian kernel wij = exp(-γ·d(i,j)²) where d is cosine distance. Tokens similar to the just-selected one receive strong suppression; distant tokens are minimally affected. This progressively reduces redundancy within a single unified pass without requiring explicit clustering, matrix inversion, or global subset optimization — each iteration is a lightweight matrix operation. Key Results: Evaluated across 5 LVLM backbones (LLaVA-1.5-7B, LLaVA-Next-7B, Video-LLaVA, Qwen2.5-VL-7B, InternVL2.5-8B) and 16 benchmarks. On LLaVA-1.5-7B retaining only 16 of 576 tokens (97.2% pruning), ID-Selection (unified score) preserves 91.8% of full-model performance versus 90.9% for CDPruner, 86.2% for DivPrune, and 80.8% for VisPruner. At 88.9% pruning (64 tokens), ID-Selection achieves 97.6% average performance versus 96.6% for CDPruner. On LLaVA-Next-7B at 88.9% pruning, ID-Selection reaches 96.6% of baseline, edging CDPruner (96.2%). On text-focused benchmarks under 88.9% reduction for Qwen2.5-VL, FastV retains only 53.4% of original performance while ID-Selection preserves 70.5%. Efficiency-wise on LLaVA-Next-7B: nearly 10× FLOPs reduction, 2.5× wall-clock speedup, and 89% KV-cache reduction versus the full model, with ID-Selection running faster than CDPruner at the same FLOPs level (916s vs. 1010s on POPE). Strengths: - The iterative score-suppression mechanism is elegant and well-motivated: it subsumes importance-only and diversity-only selection as limiting cases (γ→0 recovers top-k; very high γ with uniform scores recovers greedy max-min diversity), and the Gaussian kernel provides a principled soft interpolation. - Extremely strong empirical coverage: 5 diverse model families, 16 benchmarks spanning VQA, OCR, document understanding, and video, with ablations isolating the contribution of each component (iterative selection vs. top-k, three importance estimators, γ sensitivity). - Practical: training-free, O(NT) complexity, negligible added latency over top-k methods, and architecture-agnostic (gracefully degrades to cross-modal attention when no paired text encoder exists). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The unified importance score requires a paired text encoder (CLIP/SigLIP), making the strongest variant inapplicable to many modern architectures (Qwen2.5-VL, InternVL) where only the cross-modal attention fallback is used — yet these are increasingly dominant models. - The single fixed γ=20 across all models and tasks is convenient but theoretically unsatisfying; the ablation shows performance saturates quickly, but it is unclear whether this holds for architectures with different attention distributions or at very low token budgets (e.g., 8 tokens). - The method is applied after the projector (pre-LLM), so it cannot adapt token selection to later LLM layers where cross-modal attention patterns may shift; dynamic or layer-aware pruning is left as future work.
Search Results 2604.05601 PDF
#143 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Do diacritics matter? evaluating the impact of arabic diacritics on tokenization and llm benchmarks

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: This paper fills a genuine gap by systematically quantifying how diacritization affects Arabic LLM tokenization and benchmark performance across nine models. The counterintuitive finding that stripping wild diacritics offers no benefit challenges a common preprocessing assumption. However, the study is fundamentally an evaluation rather than a new method, and the restriction to zero-shot multiple-choice limits generalizability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Arabic diacritics are orthographic marks that convey phonological, lexical, and morphosyntactic information, but their prevalence varies widely across domains — dense in children's books, poetry, and religious texts, but nearly absent in news and user-generated content. While many Arabic NLP pipelines strip diacritics during preprocessing to sidestep orthographic variation, this practice has unknown consequences for modern LLMs. The paper addresses a critical gap: no prior work had systematically studied how varying degrees of diacritization affect subword tokenization and downstream benchmark performance across a broad set of Arabic LLMs. Methodology: The authors evaluate nine LLMs — five Arabic-centric (AceGPT-v2-8B, ALLaM-7B, Fanar-9B, Jais-13B, Jais-2-8B), three multilingual (Aya-Expanse-8B, Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen3-8B), and GPT-4o — under three controlled diacritization conditions: (i) all diacritics removed (undiac), (ii) naturally occurring diacritics preserved (wild), and (iii) fully diacritized text (full*) produced by a BERT-based morphological disambiguation system with 88.9% word-level accuracy. Evaluation uses three Arabic benchmarks: ArabicMMLU (14,455 samples), ArabCulture (3,482 samples), and AraTrust (522 samples), all run zero-shot. Tokenization analysis measures fertility (subwords per word), token density (characters per subword), tokenization consistency (% equal subword counts, Jaccard similarity), and layer-wise cosine similarities of word representations across diacritization conditions using the Wild2MaxDiacs dataset. Key Results: Fertility increases monotonically from undiac to wild to full across all tokenizers. The Jais tokenizers are worst-affected under full diacritization, reaching fertilities of 9.3–10.1 subwords/word vs. 1.9 undiacritized, approaching character-level tokenization because individual diacritic Unicode characters are absent from their vocabularies. ALLaM (57.5% Arabic vocabulary) and Fanar show the best tokenization robustness to wild diacritics (24.6% and 22.3% equal subword counts undiac-wild). Jaccard similarities confirm near-zero overlap under undiac-full for all models. Layer-wise cosine similarity analysis shows representations converge across layers due to anisotropy, but undiac-full divergence persists at lower layers. On benchmarks, removing wild diacritics has minimal and mostly statistically insignificant impact on performance. Full diacritization, however, consistently and significantly degrades performance across models: macro-average drops from 62.8 (wild) to 56.2 (full*) across benchmarks. Jais Chat (13B) suffers the most — 14.4 points on ArabicMMLU, 9.7 on ArabCulture, 26.2 on AraTrust under full vs. wild. GPT-4o is the most robust, with only a 0.7-point macro drop. Counterintuitively, retaining wild diacritics often yields equal or slightly better performance than stripping them, challenging the conventional preprocessing practice. Strengths: - First systematic, multi-model study connecting tokenization behavior to benchmark performance under controlled diacritization conditions, filling a documented gap in Arabic LLM evaluation. - Rigorous statistical testing (McNemar's test) to distinguish meaningful performance differences from noise, avoiding overclaiming. - Analysis spans both surface-level tokenization metrics and internal representation similarity, providing mechanistic grounding for observed performance degradation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is strictly zero-shot multiple-choice; it is unknown whether findings generalize to generation tasks, few-shot settings, or fine-tuned models where the model has seen diacritized text in context. - The full* condition relies on an automatic diacritizer with 88.9% word-level accuracy, introducing noise that conflates true diacritization effects with diacritization errors, potentially inflating the measured performance drop. - All three benchmarks are Modern Standard Arabic; the impact of diacritics on dialectal Arabic — where diacritization tools are far less mature and diacritic conventions differ — is entirely unexplored.
Recommended
#144 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Towards a Unified Heavyweight Ontology For Therapeutic Targeting

hal.science · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The dual-ontology design separating data-view from expert-view is a principled contribution, and grounding construction in 42 weeks of semi-directive expert interviews is rigorous knowledge engineering. The UCQ/S summary-based pruning achieving 100% sub-query elimination at ~100ms is a practical result. However, the work remains preliminary with no downstream drug discovery task evaluated, and the Cypher generation benchmark measures only syntactic validity, not semantic correctness.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Drug discovery requires integrating biological data from heterogeneous sources (UniProt, Gene Ontology, Ensembl, HUGO, GDC, OpenTargets, Reactome, ChEMBL) that use incompatible data models (RDF, OWL, Neo4j, relational databases). Existing biological knowledge graphs have limited expressivity, are noisy, and lack a unified schema — making cross-database queries for therapeutic target identification error-prone and semantically incomplete. The paper targets this alignment and expressivity gap by proposing a heavyweight OWL2 ontology grounded in formal first-order logic (DL-liteR) and elicited from domain experts. Methodology: The work proceeds along two parallel tracks. First, a knowledge elicitation process rooted in professional didactics methodology: semi-directive interviews with eight domain experts (biologists, bioinformaticians, pharmacologists) held weekly over 42 weeks, producing two complementary ontologies — a "data view" ontology (AIDD4H, Figure 2) reflecting what is actually stored across 9 integrated databases, and an "expert view" ontology (Figure 4) capturing biological reality beyond available data (e.g., explicit Chromosome/Exon/Intron modeling). Second, the 9 databases (8 open-source, 1 proprietary) are loaded into Neo4j and PostgreSQL after ETL-based identifier alignment. The OWL2 TBox consists of 14 concepts and 60 roles with full relational constraints (functionality, inverse functionality, symmetry, asymmetry, reflexivity). Query answering uses Ontology-Mediated Query Answering (OMQA): conjunctive queries (CQ) are rewritten via the Rapid (v0.93) tool into unions of conjunctive queries (UCQ), then pruned using a graph-quotient-based database summary (UCQ/S) to eliminate empty sub-queries before evaluation. A secondary contribution benchmarks four LLMs (Llama3.1, Llama3.2, Mistral, Qwen2.5-Coder-7B) on natural-language-to-Cypher query translation. Key Results: The unified knowledge base contains 4,272,741 triples (1,428,146 class instances). Sixteen queries were evaluated (10 consistency checks QA0–QA9, 6 information-retrieval queries QA10–QA15). All 10 consistency queries returned zero answers, confirming database-ontology consistency. UCQ/S achieves ~100 ms median query evaluation time, outperforming UCQ/REF (up to 1s) and UCQ/DB (adds overhead for some queries), with a 100% optimization ratio — every generated sub-query was prunable via the summary. For Cypher generation, Qwen2.5-Coder-7B achieved 0.957 accuracy and 0.978 F1-score, vastly outperforming Llama3.1/3.2 (0.565 accuracy, 0.722 F1) and Mistral (0.478 accuracy, 0.647 F1). Strengths: - The dual-ontology design (data view vs. expert view) is principled: it cleanly separates what databases actually contain from what biology demands, enabling both practical querying and forward simulation. - Grounding ontology construction in professional didactics methodology (semi-directive interviews, collective confrontation sessions) is a rigorous knowledge engineering approach that captures implicit expert knowledge unavailable from databases alone. - The UCQ/S summary-based pruning achieves 100% sub-query elimination across all 16 queries, keeping reasoning overhead negligible (~100 ms) on a 4M-triple KB — a practical result for interactive expert use. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The Cypher generation evaluation measures only syntactic validity (does the query parse and execute?), not semantic correctness (does it retrieve the right answers?). This is a shallow benchmark that overstates practical usability. - The paper is largely a system description and preliminary report ("towards a unified ontology"); no downstream drug discovery prediction task is evaluated, so the claim that the ontology identifies therapeutic targets remains unsubstantiated. - The knowledge graph includes one proprietary database from partner Oncodesign Precision Medicine, limiting full reproducibility, and the expert view ontology is not formally published or made available for comparison against existing biomedical ontologies (e.g., OBO Foundry ontologies).
Search Results
#145 — Moderately Innovative (52)

NO FREE LUNCH GPT/ LLM LIMITATIONS

openreview.net · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The paper ambitiously attempts to formalize inherent LLM reasoning limitations via 0-1 laws and Borel-Cantelli arguments, and the Accumulating Errors Lemma is a clean standalone contribution. However, the central mapping from autoregressive token generation to graph path-finding is underspecified, the independence-of-faults assumption is almost certainly violated by transformer attention mechanisms, and the definition of 'literal learning' is so broad as to be nearly unfalsifiable. The absence of empirical validation or concrete complexity thresholds leaves the theoretical claims disconnected from observable LLM behavior.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: This paper addresses the question of whether GPT/LLM architectures can, in principle, generate valid rigorous mathematical proofs for sufficiently complex statements. Despite recent high-profile successes of LLMs on math benchmarks and proof assistants, the author argues that hallucinations, falsehoods, and reasoning failures are not engineering deficiencies to be overcome, but are inherent logical consequences of the stochastic autoregressive architecture. The paper claims to provide a formal theoretical basis for this limitation, generalizing and strengthening prior empirical observations (e.g., Dziri et al., 2023) from a purely logical standpoint. Methodology: The approach formalizes logical inference as path-finding in a domain of finite graphs G(ω), where nodes represent propositions and edges represent valid implications. The central concept is "literal learning" — defined broadly as inference that either (a) memorizes from training data, (b) produces vacuously true statements, (c) hallucinates from false premises, or (d) is otherwise invalid. The main theorem (No Free Lunch Limitation, NFLL) states: for almost all proofs, any learning algorithm using randomization in G(ω) is almost surely literal learning. The proof strategy combines two tools: (1) the 0-1 law for finite graphs (Fagin 1976; Blass et al. 1998), which forces the asymptotic probability of any first-order graph property to be 0 or 1, and (2) an Accumulating Errors Lemma (Lemma A.1), which shows that, assuming independent nonzero fault probabilities at each inference step, the probability of a fault-free proof of length n decays to zero exponentially. The formal proof in Appendix D uses a Borel-Cantelli argument over a product probability space of binary-encoded proof sequences to derive the two exhaustive cases: either an algorithm's failures sum to a finite series (implying it degenerates to literal learning) or the series diverges (implying almost sure failure for every algorithm). The result is claimed to extend to agentic/hierarchical LLMs, RLHF-trained models, EBMs with stochastic sampling, and standard inference-time improvements such as chain-of-thought, retrieval-augmented generation, and greedy decoding. Key Results: The paper's claims are theoretical rather than empirical. The main result is that LLM-based systems almost surely cannot discover genuinely new mathematical results of medium-to-high complexity — the failure probability tends to 1 as proof complexity grows. As a corollary, no randomization of the prediction scheme can discover non-trivial new valid statements of medium or high complexity. A secondary result (Proposition 2.5, via Gödel's second incompleteness theorem) shows that there exist statements provable instance-by-instance but undecidable universally, creating a further structural barrier to LLM generalization. The paper discusses specific recent results (AlphaGeometry, Aristotle/Harmonic solving Erdős problem 124) and reinterprets them as consistent with its thesis: these successes operate in decidable first-order theories (Euclidean geometry, elementary arithmetic) or rely on human-curated Lean libraries, both of which fall within the "literal learning" regime. No quantitative benchmarks or ablations are presented. Strengths: - Connects LLM reasoning failures to a precise mathematical framework (0-1 laws, Borel-Cantelli) rather than leaving them as empirical observations, providing a principled vocabulary for the phenomenon. - The Accumulating Errors Lemma (Lemma A.1) is a clean and independently useful probabilistic result that generalizes a result from Dziri et al. (2023) under weaker assumptions. - The scope of coverage is broad: the paper correctly argues that post-training techniques (RLHF, distillation, MoE, RAG, CoT) do not escape the fundamental randomization bottleneck, since they keep the LLM as the base inference engine. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The mapping from LLM token generation to path-finding in G(ω) is underspecified and not formally justified. LLMs do not operate by traversing a predefined graph of logical statements; the abstraction loses the mechanisms (attention, embeddings, in-context learning) that actually drive LLM behavior, making the model's applicability to real systems unclear. - The independence-of-faults assumption in Lemma A.1 is strong and almost certainly violated: consecutive reasoning steps in a transformer are highly correlated through the attention mechanism and KV cache, which could substantially alter the error accumulation rate. - "Literal learning" as defined is so broad (encompassing memorization, vacuous truths, and hallucinations) that the theorem is nearly unfalsifiable — almost any observed behavior can be classified as one of its four cases post hoc. - The paper provides no empirical validation or concrete complexity thresholds. The claim that failure probability tends to 1 for "sufficiently complex" proofs is not operationalized; the heuristic threshold ("~10K Lean lines") is introduced in the conclusion without derivation. - The formal proof in Appendix D contains gaps: the contradiction in Case 1 (asserting GPT cannot satisfy the Borel-Cantelli convergence condition) relies on the Accumulating Errors Lemma whose independence assumption is unverified for GPT, creating circularity.
Search Results
#146 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Temporal Ontology and the Architectonics of Determination

philpapers.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The logos-threshold openness distinction is a genuinely original conceptual contribution, and the conditional architectonic methodology shows unusual philosophical rigor in specifying its own defeat conditions. However, the Transcendent Observer is functionally indistinguishable from classical theism despite its secular framing, the Structural Fidelity Principle lacks non-circular grounding independent of the Observer posit, and the operationally undefined logos threshold leaves the framework's scope indeterminate across its most interesting edge cases.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: The paper addresses a fundamental question bridging philosophy of physics, metaphysics, and ethics: what distinguishes the freedom of a deliberating rational agent from quantum indeterminacy? If both are merely physical processes, human choice is illusory and moral responsibility collapses. The paper targets a specific unstable conjunction held by philosophers of physics: that spacetime is emergent from quantum entanglement and that the pre-biological universe was genuinely real. This conjunction, the paper argues, demands a non-spatiotemporal ontological ground. Existing frameworks — compatibilism, libertarian agent causation, panpsychism, B-theory — each sacrifice something irreducible. Three specific gaps motivate the work: no existing account explains why logos-capable openness differs ontologically from quantum indeterminacy; why moral acts have permanent ontological status independent of causal consequences; and what grounds the reality of a pre-biological universe if spacetime is not fundamental. Methodology: The dissertation employs a 'conditional architectonic' approach. It argues not that the Transcendent Observer demonstrably exists, but that any philosopher holding both emergent spacetime and pre-biological physical realism is already committed to something functionally equivalent. The TOAD framework (Teleological Observer, Organism/Actualization Dynamics, Determined Reality) operates at two logical levels. Level 1 advances three independent arguments for the Transcendent Observer: (1) a classical grounding argument — physical determinacy requires ontological grounding beyond causal closure; (2) a relational argument from quantum mechanics — classical determinacy is relational, requiring both terms, with the regress of systems-classical-relative-to-systems terminating in a constitutively atemporal observer for the macroscopic realist; (3) a transcendental emergent spacetime argument, triangulated across holography, loop quantum gravity, and black hole thermodynamics, showing that the pre-biological universe's reality demands a non-biological, non-spatiotemporal ground. Level 2 develops the conditional framework: if such a ground is posited with minimal features (atemporality, co-extensiveness, logos-structure), then logos-threshold openness, the Structural Fidelity Principle, and Structural Redemption follow with logical necessity. Agency's contribution to physical outcomes is routed through Ellis's top-down classical causation via boundary conditions — not quantum-level intervention — preserving the Born rule unmodified. Formal notation (SFI, IPB, Structural Radius R(e,x,t)) is introduced but explicitly labeled as heuristic logical rather than quantitative representation. Key Results: As a metaphysical architectonic, the paper's results are philosophical rather than empirical. The central achievement is the logos-threshold openness distinction: physical indeterminacy is openness within the space of logoi-consistent outcomes, while logos-threshold openness is openness to actualize against one's own logos constitution — a categorically different ontological structure. Three consequences are argued to follow necessarily: (1) the foreknowledge-freedom problem dissolves — the Observer's atemporal co-extensiveness encompasses the growing block as it grows, including the genuinely open frontier, so no B-theory pre-determination is implied; (2) the Structural Fidelity Principle (SFP) is derived — logos-decoherent actualization is permanently inscribed in the Determined block with an ontological deficit, independent of causal consequences, indexed to the intentional state at t₁ not downstream behavior; (3) a two-tier temporal structure is established where the logos threshold is cosmological, not merely biological. Convergent support is drawn from Aristotle's akrasia analysis, Frankfurt's hierarchical compatibilism, Korsgaard's self-constitution, Gazzaniga's neurological interpreter mechanism, Axelrod's folk theorem (which requires ontological record-legibility that TOAD provides), and the Augustinian privatio boni tradition — each independently identifying the same structural phenomenon. Four positions are explicitly ruled out: pure decoherence naturalism combined with emergent spacetime, B-theory as resolution to the GBT timing problem, identification of logos-threshold openness with physical indeterminacy, and evolutionary pragmatism as sufficient account of ontological significance. Strengths: - The conditional architectonic methodology is unusually rigorous: by specifying three philosophical discriminants that would defeat the framework (reducibility of SFP to causal consequences, reducibility of logos-threshold openness to quantum indeterminacy, spacetime functionalism dissolving the instantiation question), the paper creates a genuine standard of engagement rather than an unfalsifiable system - The logos-threshold openness distinction is the paper's most original contribution — prior frameworks (Frankfurt, Korsgaard) identify the self-referential structure of akrasia but lack the temporal-ontological architecture (Growing Block, permanent inscription at t₁, Structural Radius as spatiotemporal field) that TOAD provides - The paper is admirably self-aware about its limits: explicitly acknowledging the AdS/CFT vs. de Sitter cosmological gap, the unresolved logos threshold measurement problem, the tension in deploying Maximian logoi without specifying the Observer's phenomenology, and cleanly delineating necessary entailments, philosophical discriminants, and empirical adjacency Weaknesses / Limitations: - The Transcendent Observer, despite conditional framing, functions as a God-equivalent in all but name; the 'minimal functional definition' is not obviously distinguishable from classical theism, and the paper's own theological compatibility section (14.2–14.3) reinforces this — undermining its reach as secular metaphysics and making the Level 1 arguments feel like motivated reasoning toward a pre-determined theological conclusion - The SFP's claim that logos-correspondence status is permanently inscribed and ontologically real independent of consequences depends entirely on the Observer's constitutive logos; without accepting the Observer, the principle has no non-circular support — it restates the posit in ethical language rather than independently grounding it - The logos threshold remains operationally undefined — the paper explicitly concedes no current metric captures both information integration and narrative-ethical self-reference jointly, leaving the framework's scope indeterminate across AI systems, non-human animals, and edge cases, which is not a minor gap given that SFP's ontological weight depends on L(agent) being 'real and determinate at t₁' - The engagement with quantum mechanics, while philosophically sophisticated, makes leaps physicists would contest: the constitutive role attributed to the Observer in classical state realization does not seriously engage with the decoherence-without-observation literature, and the relational argument's regress move targets 'macroscopic realists' while most contemporary physicists are content with the strict relational or structural realist positions the paper explicitly exempts from its scope
Search Results
#147 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Omni-MMSI: Toward Identity-attributed Social Interaction Understanding

Xinpeng Li, Bolin Lai, Hardy Chen, Shijian Deng, Cihang Xie, Yuyin Zhou, James Matthew Rehg, Yapeng Tian · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Omni-MMSI identifies a well-motivated and practically important gap — the oracle-to-raw-input performance cliff in multi-party social interaction — and the quantification of this gap is valuable. The tool-augmented pipeline combining specialist models with LLM reasoning is sensibly engineered. However, the methodology is largely an integration of existing components (YOLO, Whisper, SpeechBrain, OSNet) with LoRA fine-tuning, the evaluation is confined to a single controlled game setting, and 43-47% absolute accuracy indicates the approach remains far from solving the problem it defines.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Prior work on multi-modal multi-party social interaction understanding (MMSI) assumes oracle-preprocessed, identity-attributed social cues (who speaks what, bounding boxes per speaker) as input. This is unrealistic: deployed AI assistants must operate on raw audio-video streams. The gap is large — prior pipelines lose 28.1% accuracy on average when oracle cues are replaced with raw inputs, and even advanced omni-modal LLMs (Gemini 2.5 Pro, Qwen2.5-Omni) drop ~9.5%. The core bottleneck is identity attribution in multi-party scenes: off-the-shelf extractors are designed for single-person scenarios, and frontier LLMs systematically confuse speakers by relying on spatial ordering heuristics that fail under occlusion. Methodology: The paper introduces Omni-MMSI, a benchmark task requiring social interaction understanding (Speaking Target Identification and Pronoun Coreference Resolution) from raw audio-video without any oracle cues. To solve it, the authors propose Omni-MMSI-R, a reference-guided pipeline with three components. First, participant-level reference pairs (one cropped upper-body image + several 5-second audio clips per person, 69 profiles total) anchor identities across modalities. Second, lightweight task-specific tools perform identity attribution: Whisper transcribes audio, SpeechBrain verifies speaker identity via cosine similarity in embedding space, YOLO detects bounding boxes, and OSNet re-identifies persons against reference images. Third, a fine-tuned Omni-LLM (Qwen2.5-Omni-7B with LoRA, rank=8) performs structured two-step chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning: (1) confirm the last speaker by cross-checking voice and mouth movement against references, then (2) infer the referent by integrating verbal turn-taking context and non-verbal cues (gaze, gesture). CoT training data is curated via Gemini 2.5 Pro generation + rejection sampling + lightweight human review, yielding ~2,000–2,100 traces per task. The model is trained with supervised fine-tuning for 3 epochs on this data. Key Results: On the Ego4D subset, Omni-MMSI-R achieves 43.06% average accuracy (STI: 40.57%, PCR: 45.54%), outperforming Gemini 2.5 Pro (37.70%), prior MMSI pipelines by +12%, and open-source Omni-LLMs by large margins (Qwen2.5-Omni-7B: 27.43%, R1-Omni: 0.29%). On YouTube, it reaches 47.04% (vs. Gemini 2.5 Pro: 44.80%; prior pipelines: ~31%). For identity attribution specifically, Omni-MMSI-R achieves 78.79% average attribution accuracy on Ego4D (verbal: 71.09%, non-verbal: 86.48%), exceeding Gemini 2.5 Pro by +23.1% average. Ablations show that references alone contribute +2%, tool-extracted cues alone +5.5%, and combining both yields the full +9.1% over baseline. The 2-step CoT (speaker confirmation + referent inference) is optimal; adding a 3rd step for explicit cue extraction degrades performance by ~8.6%. The pipeline is highly robust to audio noise (SNR 5dB causes <1% drop in STI/PCR) and moderately robust to visual occlusion (40% occlusion ratio causes ~1% STI/PCR drop despite 14% non-verbal attribution decline). Strengths: - Addresses a well-motivated and practically important gap: the oracle-to-raw-input performance cliff in multi-party social AI is clearly quantified and the proposed remedy is effective. - The tool-augmented approach is architecturally sound: lightweight specialist models (YOLO, OSNet, Whisper, SpeechBrain) handle attribution reliably and fast (0.16–0.21s), while the LLM handles reasoning — a sensible division of labor that avoids expecting frontier LLMs to do reliable cross-modal association. - The CoT data curation pipeline (generate-filter-human-review) is practical and principled; the ablation showing 2-step > 3-step reasoning is a concrete and non-obvious finding about optimal reasoning granularity for this domain. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Dataset scope is narrow: both Ego4D and YouTube subsets come from the Werewolf Among Us social deduction game, a controlled tabletop setting where all participants remain seated and visible under a fixed camera. Generalization to naturalistic social scenarios with camera cuts, person entry/exit, or heterogeneous viewpoints is undemonstrated. - The reference construction requires per-participant enrollment (manually cropped images + voice clips), which is labor-intensive at research scale and creates a dependency that may be non-trivial to automate reliably in deployment, particularly when references are noisy or unavailable for some participants. - Absolute performance remains low — 43–47% accuracy across tasks — reflecting that this is a genuinely hard problem, but also suggesting the approach is far from deployment-ready. The gap from human performance (60–90% with oracle cues) to the system's raw-input performance indicates the task remains largely unsolved.
