#148 — Moderately Innovative (52)

GPU Acceleration of TFHE-Based High-Precision Nonlinear Layers for Encrypted LLM Inference

Guoci Chen, Xiurui Pan, Qiao Li, Bo Mao, Congming Gao, Chengying Huan, Mingzhe Zhang, Jie Zhang · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: TIGER's LUT-numerical co-design for extending TFHE precision beyond the ~20-bit WoP-PBS ceiling is technically clean, achieving high-precision nonlinear layer evaluation without new cryptographic primitives. The precision motivation study demonstrating that nonlinear layers are far more quantization-sensitive than linear layers is a genuine standalone contribution. However, the end-to-end latency of ~985 seconds per Transformer block remains orders of magnitude from practical deployment, and the evaluation is limited to GPT-2 on a single GPU.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Privacy-preserving LLM inference via Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) faces a fundamental bottleneck at nonlinear layers (Softmax, LayerNorm, GELU). CKKS-based approaches approximate these functions with high-degree polynomials, making precision-accuracy tradeoffs expensive in depth and memory. TFHE's Programmable Bootstrapping (PBS) enables exact lookup-table evaluation without polynomial approximation, but existing TFHE libraries (e.g., TFHE-rs) are CPU-focused, capped at ~20-bit input precision due to noise growth, lack high-level LLM layer implementations, and fail to exploit GPU inter-input parallelism. The paper demonstrates that naively quantizing nonlinear layers to 4 bits increases GPT-2 perplexity by 36–668×, confirming that high-precision nonlinear evaluation is non-negotiable for encrypted inference quality. Methodology: TIGER is a GPU-accelerated TFHE framework structured in four layers. At the top, composite nonlinear layers (Softmax, LayerNorm) are decomposed into element-wise function evaluations and fixed-point arithmetic. The key precision innovation is a LUT-plus-numerical co-design: inputs are partitioned into a primary part (evaluated via WoP-PBS lookup) and a residual part (refined using first-order Taylor expansion with fixed-point operators), enabling precision well beyond WoP-PBS's native ~20-bit ceiling. Exp(-x) is decomposed into three sub-ranges and handled via lookup, Taylor approximation for the low bits, and logical shortcutting for large inputs. GELU uses a single lookup returning both GELU(t) and its correction factor G(t), followed by one multiply-add refinement step. InvSqrt uses piecewise lookup tables across four logarithmic intervals, each with Taylor-based refinement. At the fixed-point layer, a Multiply Scheduler separates offline partial-product pruning from online batched execution, and constant division is recast as base-4 reciprocal multiplication. At the low level, FFT is optimized with 4-radix decomposition (merging two passes), Karatsuba complex multiplication, shared-memory ping-pong buffering, and i64 arithmetic. PBS batching uses empirically determined optimal sub-batch sizes [192, 320] with a split strategy to avoid L2 cache thrashing, exploiting inter-input parallelism across all independent ciphertexts in a layer. Key Results: Against a 16-thread CPU WoP-PBS baseline, TIGER achieves 7.17× speedup on GELU, 16.68× on Softmax, and 17.05× on LayerNorm. End-to-end estimated latency for a single GPT-2 Transformer block drops from 15,321 s to 985 s (15.54× speedup), with nonlinear TFHE computation accounting for 98.5% of total runtime. WoP-PBS consistently outperforms the FBT-TFHE alternative (GPU-FBT), which requires ~65,000 LUT ciphertexts and ~10 GB of key memory for 20-bit inputs. Ablation shows the PBS split strategy contributes the largest single gain (25–30%), with 4-radix FFT adding 5–8% and Karatsuba another 5–10%. Batching without splitting degrades to 4.35× slower than optimal at hidden dimension 768, confirming the necessity of the split design. Strengths: - The LUT-numerical co-design is technically clean and well-motivated: it achieves precision beyond WoP-PBS's noise ceiling without a new cryptographic primitive, using only Taylor expansion and existing fixed-point ops. - The batching analysis is unusually rigorous—empirical profiling with ncu exposes the non-monotonic efficiency curve and motivates the split strategy with concrete L2/DRAM/warp-occupancy evidence rather than hand-waving. - The precision motivation study (Table I) is a genuine contribution: it systematically shows that nonlinear layers are far more sensitive to quantization than linear layers, with even QAT failing to recover from 4-bit nonlinear precision, establishing a strong use case for the system. Weaknesses / Limitations: - End-to-end latency remains ~985 seconds per Transformer block, which is far from practical deployment. The paper acknowledges this but does not articulate a credible path to real-time or even interactive inference, leaving the "practical" framing aspirational. - The evaluation is limited to GPT-2 and a single GPU (RTX 6000 Ada); it is unclear whether the optimal PBS batch range [192, 320] generalizes across GPU generations or larger models, and the batch-size tuning remains offline and manual. - The hybrid CKKS (linear) + TFHE (nonlinear) architecture introduces four protocol conversion steps per block, with TFHE→CKKS conversion already costing 14.6 s, suggesting that as nonlinear computation is further accelerated, protocol conversion will become the next dominant bottleneck.
Search Results 2604.04783 PDF
#149 — Moderately Innovative (51)

Compiling Code LLMs into Lightweight Executables

Jieke Shi, Junda He, Zhou Yang, Chengran Yang, Mykhailo Klymenko, Thong Hoang, Xiwei, Xu, Zhenchang Xing, David Lo · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The co-design of product-quantization and LLVM-level GEMV optimization is well-executed engineering, and the ablation convincingly demonstrates synergy rather than mere additivity. The formal per-output error bound is a useful theoretical contribution. However, both quantization and compiler-level BLAS substitution are individually well-known techniques, the evaluation is limited to a single hardware platform (Apple M2), and the approach is tightly scoped to llama2.c-style pipelines with no path to mainstream frameworks.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: State-of-the-art Code LLMs (7B+ parameters, ~28 GB in FP32) are too large to run on commodity laptops with 16–24 GB of RAM, forcing developers to rely on cloud-based tools with attendant privacy, latency, and connectivity concerns. Prior work on compressing Code LLMs has focused exclusively on reducing parameter count or bitwidth at the model level, ignoring the inference program itself. Without low-level program optimization, compressed models still underperform on CPU-only devices because unoptimized GEMV kernels—which account for over 80% of inference time—remain a bottleneck. This paper addresses both gaps jointly. Methodology: Ditto is a two-phase framework targeting C-based inference pipelines (specifically llama2.c). Phase 1 performs weight quantization inspired by product quantization: weights are sorted and partitioned into equal-population clusters, centroids are computed iteratively (K-Means-style using L1 distance), and each weight is replaced with the index of its nearest centroid stored in a bit-packed format (default 3 bits, yielding 8 centroids). The quantization provides a formal per-output error bound of ‖Δy‖∞ ≤ ε‖x‖₁, enabling layer-wise error budgeting and adaptive-precision inference. Phase 2 applies a custom LLVM IR pass that detects GEMV patterns in the synthesized inference code via new combinators extending LLVM's PatternMatch API (OneOf, PHITimesLD, AffineFunctionOfPHI), verifies legality via data-dependence analysis and memory-layout inference, and replaces matched loops with calls to hardware-optimized BLAS routines (e.g., cblas_sgemv from Apple Accelerate). The two phases are synergistic: quantization reduces memory bandwidth pressure for GEMV, allowing BLAS kernels to operate more efficiently than either technique alone. The full pipeline (quantization + compilation) takes ~17 minutes for a 7B model and produces a self-contained binary requiring only a system BLAS library. Key Results: Evaluated on Code Llama-7B, Magicoder-CL-7B, and OpenCodeInterpreter-CL-7B on an Apple M2 (24 GB RAM) against HumanEval+ and MBPP+ benchmarks. Compared to the full-precision llama2.c baseline: up to 10.5× faster inference (238 ms/token vs. 2500 ms/token), 6.4× lower memory (4.1–4.2 GB vs. ~27 GB), and 10.5× lower energy (3.8–5.1 J/token vs. 36–45 J/token), with an average pass@1 degradation of only 0.27% (worst case 1.83%). Compared to an int8 post-training quantization baseline: up to +6.96% higher pass@1, 2.2× speedup, 1.6× memory reduction, and 2.2× energy savings. Ditto also outperforms AWQ-4bit (−6.43% pass@1 drop vs. Ditto's +1.25%) by avoiding dequantization overhead on CPU. The ablation study shows quantization alone actually slows inference 0.8× due to unpacking overhead, while BLAS alone yields 4.8× speedup with no memory gain; combined, they yield 7.0× speedup and 6.4× memory reduction, confirming synergy. Generalization experiments show similar gains on CodeQwen-1.5-7B (Qwen architecture), Code Llama-13B, and TinyLLaMA-110M. Strengths: - The co-design of quantization and compiler-level optimization is the paper's core insight and is well-validated: the ablation clearly demonstrates synergy, not mere additivity, with quantization enabling BLAS kernels to run faster by reducing memory bandwidth demand. - The formal per-output error bound (‖Δy‖∞ ≤ ε‖x‖₁) is a concrete theoretical contribution that distinguishes this from naive truncation-based quantization methods and enables principled layer-wise error budgeting. - The LLVM IR pass design is technically sound: custom matchers handle real-world code variation in loop nesting, induction variables, and memory layouts, achieving 100% GEMV detection and rewriting on evaluated models with no correctness failures. - Practical deployment impact is substantial: 7B models fit in 4.2 GB (within an 8 GB budget), enabling genuine off-cloud inference on consumer hardware at sub-300 ms latency. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The approach is tightly scoped to llama2.c-style C inference pipelines and does not yet support llama.cpp, PyTorch, or TensorFlow backends, limiting immediate adoption; extending to these frameworks is deferred to future work. - Evaluation is conducted on a single hardware platform (Apple M2), using Apple Accelerate as the BLAS library. Speedup results are therefore not independently verified on x86 (Intel MKL) or other ARM platforms where BLAS quality varies; memory reduction generalizes but throughput improvement depends on the BLAS provider. - Only 3-bit quantization is evaluated as the default; while a sensitivity study covers 3–5 bits, sub-3-bit and mixed-precision strategies are not explored, and the tradeoff space at very low bitwidths (1–2 bits) where accuracy drops may become non-negligible is uncharted. - Energy estimation methodology is questionable: since CPU power draw is constant at 18 W regardless of quantization level on the Apple M2 SoC (all cores stay fully utilized), energy per token is simply 18/tok/s—a derived metric rather than a direct measurement, making the energy results less informative than presented.
Search Results 2603.29813 PDF
#150 — Moderately Innovative (51)

LIBERO-Para: A Diagnostic Benchmark and Metrics for Paraphrase Robustness in VLA Models (67 ▲ )

Chanyoung Kim, Minwoo Kim, Minseok Kang, Hyunwoo Kim, Dahuin Jung · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 Apr 2026
Why this rank: LIBERO-Para provides a systematic diagnostic benchmark with a well-designed two-axis linguistic decomposition and the useful PRIDE metric, revealing that even simple object-noun synonyms cause 19-51pp success rate drops across VLA models. However, the benchmark is built on only 10 tasks in a single simulation environment, the finding that language models overfit to training phrasings is not unexpected, and the practical prescriptive value is limited since the paper identifies the problem but offers no solution beyond diagnosis.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are typically fine-tuned on limited environment-specific demonstration data, creating a risk of overfitting to the exact instruction phrasings seen during training. The widely-used LIBERO benchmark evaluates models with identical instructions at train and test time, leaving linguistic robustness entirely unexamined. Existing benchmarks that do include linguistic variation either treat paraphrasing as one perturbation axis among many multimodal changes, conflate meaning-preserving rewording with task-level semantic changes, or lack formal quantification of paraphrase difficulty. This gap matters practically: deployed robots will receive instructions from diverse human operators who naturally rephrase commands. Methodology: The authors introduce LIBERO-Para, a controlled evaluation benchmark built on top of LIBERO-Goal. The benchmark independently varies two linguistic axes grounded in established taxonomies: (1) the Object axis (3 lexical types: addition, same-polarity contextual substitution, same-polarity habitual substitution) and (2) the Action axis (10 types spanning lexical, structural, and pragmatic variation derived from the Extended Paraphrase Typology and Ervin-Tripp's directive taxonomy). Compositional combinations yield 43 paraphrase type combinations and 4,092 total instructions generated from 10 base LIBERO-Goal tasks using a 4-stage LLM pipeline (Gemini 2.5 Pro for generation and verification). Seven VLA model configurations spanning 0.6B–7.5B parameters and four architectural families (OpenVLA-OFT, π0.5/Xiaomi-Robotics-0, X-VLA, VLA-Adapter) are evaluated. The authors also propose PRIDE (Paraphrase Robustness Index in Robotic Instructional DEviation), a task-specific metric combining keyword similarity (S_K, computed via Sentence-BERT cosine matching of content words) and structural similarity (S_T, computed via dependency-tree edit distance), weighted by a tunable α. PRIDE equals the paraphrase distance PD on success and 0 on failure, rewarding models that succeed on harder paraphrases. Failure analysis uses DTW-based trajectory classification to separate execution-level (Near-GT) from planning-level (Far-GT) failures. Key Results: All seven models show consistent success rate degradation of 22.8–51.9 percentage points under paraphrasing despite near-perfect (97.6–98.8%) LIBERO-Goal performance. Object-level lexical substitution is the dominant failure driver: replacing object nouns with common synonyms (e.g., 'stove' → 'range') causes drops of 19.8–51.0 pp across models, whereas action-axis variation causes milder degradation (lexical: 66–70%, structural: 57–63%, pragmatic question/hint: ~48%). Increasing training data diversity 4× (OpenVLA-OFTmixed vs. OFTgoal) does not improve robustness (32.4 pp vs. 33.2 pp drop). The PRIDE metric reveals 8.4–22.0% overestimation of robustness by binary success rate, exposing models that succeed mainly on easier paraphrases (VLA-Adapter: 22.0% overestimation, π0.5 expert-only: 18.2%). PRIDE correlates significantly with task success across all models (Pearson r: −0.671 to −0.877, p < 0.0001). Trajectory analysis shows 79.5–95.5% of failures are Far-GT (planning-level), meaning models generate different trajectories from the outset rather than failing during correct-task execution. Strengths: - The two-axis decomposition (action × object) with 43 linguistically grounded categories is considerably more systematic and controlled than all prior VLA linguistic robustness benchmarks, enabling precise attribution of where degradation originates. - PRIDE is a principled, task-grounded metric that outperforms generic NLP metrics (BLEU, BERTScore, METEOR) in monotonically tracking difficulty; its S_K/S_T decomposition provides diagnostic insight into whether failures stem from lexical or structural shifts. - The DTW-based planning vs. execution failure decomposition is a useful diagnostic tool, revealing that the bottleneck is instruction-to-task identification, not motor control—a distinction with direct implications for what to fix. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is constructed from only 10 base instructions in a single simulation environment (LIBERO-Goal), raising questions about generalization to richer task sets, real-world physics, and visual diversity; the authors acknowledge simulation-to-real transfer is unvalidated. - The study evaluates single-axis variation at a time; compound paraphrases where multiple variation types co-occur simultaneously (e.g., synonym substitution + indirect speech act + structural reorganization) are deferred, yet these are common in natural human speech and likely harder. - LIBERO-Goal's single-reference-per-object training convention is an artifact of that specific benchmark; findings about object-level fragility may be partially explained by this data property rather than a fundamental VLA limitation, and the results may not generalize to models trained on more lexically diverse corpora.
Recommended 2603.28301 PDF
#151 — Moderately Innovative (51)

SkillClaw: Let Skills Evolve Collectively with Agentic Evolver (143 ▲ )

Ziyu Ma, Shidong Yang, Yuxiang Ji, Xucong Wang, Yong Wang, Yiming Hu, Tongwen Huang, Xiangxiang Chu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Apr 2026
Why this rank: The concept of collective skill evolution from multi-user interaction traces is appealing, and grouping sessions by referenced skill to create natural controlled comparisons is a clever design choice. However, the evaluation is too preliminary to assess real impact — 8 users over 6 days on a proprietary unpublished benchmark with circular LLM-as-judge validation substantially limits the strength of the claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM agent systems such as OpenClaw rely on reusable skills—structured procedural artifacts—to accomplish complex tasks. However, these skills remain static after deployment: solutions discovered during one session do not persist, and the same failure modes are repeatedly rediscovered across users. This is fundamentally wasteful because users operate in overlapping task spaces with shared workflows, tools, and failure patterns. Existing memory-based approaches store past trajectories for retrieval but cannot generalize them into improved behavior, while skill-based methods treat the skill library as a fixed resource. The gap is a mechanism that converts heterogeneous multi-user interaction evidence into reliable, system-wide skill updates without user intervention. Methodology: SkillClaw is a framework for collective skill evolution in multi-user agent ecosystems. It implements a four-stage closed loop: Multi-user Interaction → Session Collection → Skill Evolution → Skill Synchronization. Each agent deployment records full causal interaction traces (prompt → action → feedback → response), which are uploaded to a centralized store and grouped by the skills they invoked. An agentic evolver—an LLM agent equipped with a structured harness—then analyzes each skill's group of sessions, reasoning jointly over successes and failures to select one of three actions: refine the existing skill, create a new skill, or skip. Crucially, successful sessions define invariants (parts that must not change) while failed sessions define update targets, preventing regression. Candidate skill updates are validated nightly in real user environments by comparing the original and updated skill on the same tasks; only improvements are accepted, guaranteeing monotonic deployment quality. The prompts driving each stage (session summarization, skill evolution, agentic workspace orchestration) are fully disclosed in the paper appendix, including detailed conservative-editing constraints that prevent the evolver from rewriting skills wholesale or conflating agent failures with skill deficiencies. Key Results: Experiments use WildClawBench, a 60-task real-world benchmark across six domains (productivity, code intelligence, social interaction, search & retrieval, creative synthesis, safety & alignment), simulating 8 concurrent users over 6 days with Qwen3-Max as the backbone. All four reported categories improve monotonically. Social Interaction rises from 54.01% to 60.34% (+11.72% relative) after a single nightly evolution pass. Search & Retrieval improves from 22.73% to 34.55% in two accepted updates (+52.00% relative). Creative Synthesis jumps from 11.57% to 21.80% on Day 2 (+88.41% relative). Safety & Alignment improves from 24.00% to 32.00% (+33.33% relative), driven by Git authentication fallback skills. A controlled ablation on three targeted queries (basic extraction, deadline parsing, save report) shows an average gain of +42.1% after one evolution round, with save report improving from 28.3% to 100.0%, demonstrating that procedural failures are the primary addressable bottleneck. Performance plateaus after early impactful updates, consistent with the validator's monotonic acceptance rule. Strengths: - The insight that grouping sessions by referenced skill creates a natural controlled comparison—revealing exactly where and why a skill succeeds or fails across diverse users—is elegant and well-motivated. - The conservative editing constraints (preserve invariants from successful sessions, distinguish skill vs. agent vs. environment failures, hard constraints on API endpoints and contracts) are unusually rigorous for an LLM-driven system and prevent the common failure mode of speculative rewrites. - Full prompt disclosure in the appendix makes the system reproducible and auditable, which is rare in agent-system papers. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation scale is very small (8 users, 6 days, 4 of 6 benchmark domains reported, with only 60 total tasks), and the paper explicitly acknowledges this is a preliminary study; it is impossible to assess whether the gains generalize or simply reflect easy early wins on a narrow task set. - The validation mechanism—using the same LLM (Qwen3-Max) to both generate candidate skills and judge whether they improve over the baseline—creates a circular evaluation loop with no human ground truth, making it unclear whether accepted skills reflect genuine capability improvement or LLM-consistent artifacts. - WildClawBench is an unpublished benchmark from the same institutional group (cited as 'Ding et al. 2026'), and the OpenClaw agent system evaluated against it is also an internal proprietary system, preventing independent replication or meaningful comparison to prior published work.
Recommended 2604.08377 PDF
#152 — Moderately Innovative (49)

GraphWalk: Enabling Reasoning in Large Language Models through Tool-Based Graph Navigation

Taraneh Ghandi, Hamidreza Mahyar, Shachar Klaiman · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: GraphWalk's strongest contribution is methodological: the synthetic non-semantic benchmark design that isolates structural reasoning from parametric knowledge is a valuable evaluation tool. The scalability finding (reasoning models collapse at 500 nodes while tool-based navigation degrades gracefully) is compelling. However, the framework itself — equipping LLMs with graph-query tools in a perceive-act loop — is a standard agentic pattern, and the low absolute accuracy (~29%) and failure on aggregation/variable-hop queries limit practical significance.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs struggle to reason over large knowledge graphs (KGs) because the full graph cannot fit in any context window, and even when it does fit, models fail to perform coherent multi-hop traversal. Existing approaches — graph textualization/RAG, subgraph retrieval, text-to-Cypher/SPARQL, hybrid LLM-GNN architectures — either hit context-window limits at enterprise scale, require domain-specific fine-tuning, or conflate tool intelligence with model reasoning ability. Standard KGQA benchmarks (Freebase, Wikidata) compound the evaluation problem because LLMs can answer 30%+ of questions from parametric memory alone, making it impossible to isolate structural reasoning. There is a gap in understanding how much pure structural graph reasoning off-the-shelf LLMs can perform when equipped with only minimal, generic tools. Methodology: GraphWalk is a training-free, problem-agnostic agentic framework that equips off-the-shelf LLMs with a minimal orthogonal toolset backed by a Neo4j graph database. Tools are thin wrappers around deterministic Cypher queries: `get_node_by_property`, `get_all_nearest_neighbors`, `get_unique_property_values`, and `think` (a chain-of-thought scratchpad). The LLM operates in a perceive-act loop (up to 30 iterations), selecting tools sequentially to gather evidence before synthesizing a final answer. No domain knowledge is encoded in the tools. To validate pure structural reasoning, experiments use entirely synthetic graphs with random, non-semantic 4–8 character labels for all node types, relationship types, and property names, eliminating parametric knowledge leakage. Evaluation covers 12 query templates spanning retrieval, aggregation, multi-hop path traversal, and first-order logic (conjunction/negation). A maze traversal problem (10×10 grid modeled as a graph) is used as an intuitive warm-up demonstration with 2 tools. Seven OpenAI models are evaluated, including non-reasoning (gpt-4o, gpt-4.1 family) and reasoning (o3-mini, o4-mini) models, across graph sizes from 100 to 500 nodes. Key Results: On maze traversal, non-reasoning models in the no-tool setting achieve near-zero accuracy (0–10%), consistent with prior work, while GraphWalk-equipped models reach 80–100% for gpt-4.1 and gpt-4.1-mini. Reasoning models (o3-mini, o4-mini) solve all 10 mazes without tools. On synthetic KG queries (120 queries per model), tool-equipped gpt-4.1 achieves 29.17% accuracy and F1=0.43, versus 17.50% / F1=0.48 without tools — the no-tool setting shows higher precision but lower recall because models answer fewer questions. False positives drop dramatically with tools (232 vs. 1006 for gpt-4.1), demonstrating hallucination suppression from grounded tool execution. Notably, gpt-4.1 with tools outperforms o3-mini (27 correct vs. 35 correct). The critical scalability finding: at 500 nodes, o4-mini collapses from 59 to 5 correct answers (F1: 0.66 → 0.09), while gpt-4.1 with tools degrades only modestly from 35 to 32 correct (F1: 0.43 → 0.35). Simple retrieval and bounded-hop path queries are handled well; aggregation (count queries), variable-hop paths (0% across all models), and logical composition (AND/NOT) remain major failure modes. Strengths: - The synthetic benchmark design is methodologically sound: non-semantic labels eliminate knowledge leakage and isolate pure structural reasoning, providing a cleaner evaluation than Freebase/Wikidata-based benchmarks. - The scalability experiment is the paper's most compelling result — demonstrating that in-context approaches (including frontier reasoning models like o4-mini) fail catastrophically at 500 nodes while tool-based navigation degrades gracefully, directly motivating the framework's design. - The minimalist tool design philosophy is principled and generalizable: the same 4-tool set applies unchanged to both maze traversal and KG QA, substantiating the domain-agnostic claim without task-specific engineering. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Overall accuracy remains low even with tools (gpt-4.1 achieves ~29% on 120 queries), and several important query types — aggregation, variable-hop paths, logical composition — are near-completely unsolved, limiting practical applicability to real enterprise KGs where these are common. - The experimental scale is modest: graphs of 100–500 nodes are tiny compared to real enterprise KGs with hundreds of thousands of nodes and relationships, and the 30-iteration tool-call cap would be hopelessly insufficient for such scales. The paper does not address how to handle graphs where even breadth-first neighborhood expansion would require thousands of tool calls. - The no-tool baseline (full graph in context) serves as a stand-in for 'perfect retrieval' from subgraph/GraphRAG methods, but real retrieval introduces error; this comparison understates the relative advantage of GraphWalk over RAG pipelines in realistic settings while potentially overstating it for reasoning models on small graphs.
Citations 2604.01610 PDF
#153 — Moderately Innovative (49)

GCNPath: introspecting drug response prediction with pathway-guided graph convolution networks

nature.com · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The Pathway Crosstalk Network design is biologically well-motivated and the benchmark methodology is unusually rigorous across four blinding scenarios. However, performance gains over competitors are modest and inconsistent across settings, and the core approach — GCN over pathway-level features — is an evolutionary refinement of existing pathway-based DRP methods rather than a fundamentally new direction.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Anticancer drug response prediction (DRP) is critical for precision oncology, yet existing deep learning models have two underaddressed shortcomings. First, they lack rigorous benchmarking under strict-blind conditions (unseen cell lines AND unseen drugs simultaneously), which is the realistic deployment scenario. Second, they are brittle to batch effects in transcriptomic data, making cross-platform generalization unreliable. Prior pathway-based models did not integrate pathway-level crosstalk through graph architectures, and no prior work systematically evaluated how preprocessing choices and model architecture interact with generalizability across heterogeneous RNA datasets. Methodology: GCNPath introduces a three-module architecture: a cell module, a drug module, and a prediction module. For cell lines, RNA-seq TPM data are first compressed to 292 BIOCARTA pathway activity scores using GSVA, which provides unsupervised batch-effect mitigation. These pathway scores are then structured into Pathway Crosstalk Networks (PCN): three directed KNN graphs (K=5) built from (1) STRING PPI separation scores, (2) RegNetwork GRN separation scores, and (3) pairwise GSVA score correlations. The cell module processes the three PCN graphs jointly via a 3-layer Relational GCN (RGCN), using DenseGCN (skip-concatenation across layers) to mitigate over-smoothing. For drugs, atom-bond graphs with 85 atom and 10 bond features are processed by a 3-layer Graph Attention Network (GAT) with 4 attention heads and DenseGCN. Cell embeddings (7,008-dim after node concatenation → 256-dim) and drug embeddings (384-dim after global max pooling → 256-dim) are concatenated and passed through two FCN layers to predict ln(IC50). Interpretability is enabled via Grad-CAM on the first RGCN layer. Benchmarking spans four test scenarios (unblinded, cell-blind, drug-blind, strict-blind) across GDSC1, GDSC2, GDSC1+2, and external ChEMBL validation, with RMSE, PCC, and SCC metrics. Key Results: Across GDSC benchmarks, GCNPath consistently achieves the lowest or tied-lowest RMSE in all four test scenarios. Critically, unlike TGDRP, TGSA, and DRPreter which excel in unblinded tests but degrade substantially in drug-blind and strict-blind scenarios, GCNPath maintains the most balanced performance under all blinding conditions. In external ChEMBL validation (237,580 drug-cell combinations, 112,942 unseen drugs), GCNPath ranks second behind HiDRA on RMSE but outperforms all models except HiDRA on PCC/SCC stability. On batch-effect robustness tests using 404 cell lines across GDSC microarray, CCLE TPM, and SANGER TPM sources, GCNPath is the top-performing model when gene expression from external platforms replaces training-platform data — demonstrating that GSVA effectively mitigates cross-platform biases compared to sample-wise standardization or raw normalization. In the TCGA clinical validation (414 responses, 11 drugs, 399 patients), GCNPath achieves the best performance, significantly distinguishing responders for 5 drugs: Fluorouracil (P=6.3×10⁻¹⁵, effect size=−0.574), Gemcitabine (P=1.5×10⁻², ES=−0.229), Sorafenib (P=4.9×10⁻⁴, ES=−0.211), Cisplatin (P=1.1×10⁻², ES=−0.150), and Temozolomide (P=1.3×10⁻⁷, ES=−0.078). In an SCLC case study using 107 clinical patients (Liu et al. 2024), GCNPath correctly recapitulates that NMF3 is more sensitive to anlotinib than NMF1, and NMF4 is more sensitive to AURKA inhibitors than NMF1, while competitor models (TGDRP_SG, DRPreter_SG) do not consistently replicate both findings. Predictions from GSVA scores derived from proteomics correlate well with those from TPM (r=0.866), indicating potential multi-omics extensibility. Strengths: - The Pathway Crosstalk Network (PCN) design is novel and biologically motivated: encoding horizontal inter-pathway relationships via separation scores on PPI/GRN networks captures functional proximity that standard pathway-score concatenation misses, and ablations confirm that perturbing graph topology degrades performance. - Benchmark methodology is unusually rigorous: four test scenarios, three GDSC datasets, external ChEMBL validation, cross-platform batch-effect evaluation, and comparison against both freshly trained and developer-pretrained models — directly addressing cherry-picked benchmarking that plagues the DRP literature. - GSVA as a preprocessing step elegantly solves the batch-effect problem without requiring sample labels or reference datasets, enabling deployment on any RNA platform (TPM, microarray, even proteomics) without retraining. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Performance gains over the best competitors are modest rather than decisive. In several scenarios (e.g., ChEMBL RMSE), GCNPath ranks second to HiDRA, and the authors acknowledge it does not achieve overwhelming improvements — raising questions about whether the architectural innovations alone justify the added complexity. - The model relies solely on monotherapy IC50 data and cannot directly address combination therapy, gene perturbation response, or clinically heterogeneous multi-drug regimens. The TCGA clinical validation, while showing partial bridging of the cell-line-to-clinic gap, covers only 11 drugs and 399 patients, and the clinical effect sizes (e.g., ES=−0.078 for Temozolomide) are very small, limiting translational confidence. - GSVA pathway scores are sensitive to sample population composition, producing unreliable scores for small or biased sample sets — a fundamental limitation acknowledged by the authors that may cause degradation when applied to rare cancer subtypes or small patient cohorts.
Citations
#154 — Moderately Innovative (48)

Efficient and Scalable Granular-ball Graph Coarsening Method for Large-scale Graph Node Classification

Guan Wang, Shuyin Xia, Lei Qian, Guoyin Wang, Yi Liu, Yi Wang, Wei Wang · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: GB-CGNN achieves the first O(N) granular-ball coarsening method by combining METIS initialization with adaptive BFS-based binary splitting—a well-engineered two-stage design that enables scaling to million-node graphs where prior coarsening methods fail. However, both constituent techniques (METIS partitioning, quality-based splitting) are established, and the purely structural quality metric ignoring node features is a notable limitation demonstrated by the Citeseer anomaly.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Training Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) on large-scale graphs is prohibitively expensive due to the "neighbor explosion" problem: as GCN layers deepen, the neighborhood to aggregate grows exponentially. Existing remedies fall into two camps — sampling-based methods (GraphSAGE, FastGCN) and graph coarsening methods — but both have shortcomings. Sampling methods struggle with scalability and miss multi-level graph structure. Prior granular-ball coarsening methods (GBGC, SGBGC) are adaptive and structure-aware but have high initialization-time complexity, making them infeasible on very large graphs (Reddit, Yelp, Amazon). This paper targets the gap: a coarsening method that is simultaneously linear-time, multi-granularity-aware, and scalable to graphs with millions of nodes. Methodology: The proposed GB-CGNN framework has two stages. Stage 1 (Adaptive Granular-ball Coarsening Graph Formation) proceeds in five steps: (1) treat the whole graph as one coarse granular-ball; (2) partition it into √N initial granular-balls using METIS, which minimizes cut edges while maintaining load balance in O(N) time for sparse graphs; (3) measure quality of each granular-ball as average degree (edges/nodes), capturing internal connectivity; (4) adaptively split each granular-ball via binary splitting — the two highest-degree nodes become centers, remaining nodes are assigned by BFS shortest-path distance, and splitting continues only if child average quality exceeds parent quality; (5) each resulting granular-ball becomes a supernode in the coarsened graph, with inter-ball edges added when any node pair crosses boundaries. This two-level design (global METIS + local BFS splitting) achieves O(N) total coarsening complexity, a first for granular-ball methods. Stage 2 trains a standard GCN on minibatches of randomly sampled granular-ball subgraphs via SGD, exploiting the block-diagonal structure of the coarsened adjacency matrix to make the GCN objective decomposable across batches. Theoretical justification via Rayleigh quotient analysis shows the coarsened graph approximately preserves the spectral properties of the original. Memory complexity is O(BLF + LF²), independent of full graph size. Key Results: Evaluated on 12 node classification datasets ranging from Cora (2,708 nodes) to Amazon (1.6M nodes). GB-CGNN achieves best Micro-F1 on 11/12 datasets. Coarsening speed is the dominant advantage: on Reddit (232K nodes), GB-CGNN coarsens in 121.6s vs. SGBGC's 30,329s (250× faster); on Yelp (716K nodes), GB-CGNN takes 307s vs. SGBGC's 74,421s (242× faster); most competing methods fail on Amazon entirely (OOM). On classification accuracy, GB-CGNN improves over the second-best by 1.1% on Cora (0.867 vs. 0.856), 2.7% on Amazon (0.739 vs. 0.712), and 1.1% on Yelp (0.627 vs. 0.617). Ablation shows both METIS initialization and binary splitting are necessary: removing initialization causes complete failure on 4 large datasets; removing binary splitting causes only mild degradation but OOM on Amazon. Strengths: - First granular-ball coarsening method with proven O(N) time complexity, enabling operation on graphs (Amazon: 1.6M nodes) where all prior coarsening methods fail or are orders of magnitude slower. - The two-stage design is well-motivated: METIS provides globally balanced, structurally coherent initial partitions that give the quality-based adaptive splitting a strong starting point, whereas prior GBGC-style methods began from a single ball and required expensive global splits. - Comprehensive empirical evaluation across 12 diverse datasets and comparison against 15 baselines (sampling, coarsening, and condensation methods), with ablation and hyperparameter sensitivity studies. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The quality metric (average degree) is purely structural and ignores node features; this may produce granular-balls that are densely connected but feature-heterogeneous, which could hurt classification when node features are the dominant signal (as seen on Citeseer where removing binary splitting actually improves F1 by 5.9%). - The theoretical spectral preservation argument (Theorem 1) relies on C being "approximately orthogonal," an assumption that is asserted empirically rather than verified with quantitative bounds for the specific METIS+BFS construction, leaving the theory somewhat informal. - The √N choice for initial granular-ball count is empirically motivated and dataset-agnostic; the paper shows sensitivity to this hyperparameter (e.g., using 4√N on PPI triples coarsening time and drops F1 by ~2%), but provides no principled guidance for selecting k on unseen graphs.
Citations 2603.29148 PDF
#155 — Moderately Innovative (48)

Autosecagent: a semi-automated AI-driven penetration testing framework through recursive memory and real-time RAG: A. Al-Sabbagh et al.

link.springer.com · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: AutoSecAgent contributes a formalized Context Loss Error metric and a three-tier hierarchical memory architecture that addresses a real limitation of prior LLM-based pentesting tools. However, the core approach — LoRA fine-tuning plus RAG plus memory — is a predictable combination of established techniques, and exploitation success still falls well short of manual operation (58.1% vs 71.3%). The small controlled testbed limits external validity claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Manual penetration testing is time-consuming, difficult to scale, and heavily reliant on individual human expertise. Existing LLM-based tools (PentestGPT, AutoAttacker, VulnBot) suffer from a critical shared flaw: context loss over long multi-step attack chains, leading to redundant actions, contradictory decisions, and poor multi-phase attack completion. The gap this work targets is not replacing traditional scanners but providing a memory-centric orchestration layer that maintains tool-grounded procedural state across extended penetration testing trajectories, enabling systematic study of context loss as a measurable failure mode. Methodology: AutoSecAgent is built on the Agent Zero orchestration framework, using DeepSeek r1 14B fine-tuned via LoRA (rank=16) on 5,916 cybersecurity instruction pairs (Metasploit scenarios, CVE-exploit mappings, Nmap interpretations, HackTheBox privilege escalation). The two primary technical contributions are: (1) Recursive Memory Embedding (RME) — a three-tier hierarchical vector store (working memory in GPU RAM for the last 10 interactions; episodic memory in Redis using sentence-transformers/all-mpnet-base-v2 at 768 dims; semantic long-term patterns in FAISS/HNSW). At each decision step, structured tuples (state, action, binary reward, next_state, context_t) are serialized and embedded; retrieval uses cosine similarity (threshold=0.75, top-k=5) to inject relevant historical context into DeepSeek prompts. Context Loss Error (CLE) is formally defined as the rate of consistency violations (contradicting prior observations, repeating failed actions, referencing non-existent artefacts) detected via a rule-based checker over immutable execution traces. (2) An RL-inspired contextual bandit for exploit parameter optimization: per-vulnerability candidate exploit configurations are re-ranked via a binary reward signal (verified session/shell), with a strict budget of K=3 iterations and failure taxonomy-conditioned parameter adjustments. The system integrates Metasploit, Nmap, and Shodan via tool adapters, with real-time RAG from NVD/CVE databases. Up to 8 concurrent Docker-containerized agents run in parallel under a priority-weighted task scheduler. Key Results: Evaluated on 49 known vulnerabilities across 9 systems (DVWA, Juice Shop, Metasploitable 2, Windows Server 2019 AD, simulated AWS stack) with 5 independent trials each. AutoSecAgent achieved 92.4% Vulnerability Detection Rate (VDR) vs. VulnBot 85.2% and AutoPenBench 83.0% (p<0.05). Exploitation Success Rate was 58.1% vs. VulnBot 45.0% and AutoPenBench 40.0%, though still below manual Metasploit operation (71.3%). Multi-step Attack Success Rate reached 61.3% vs. VulnBot 40% and AutoPenBench 33%, with average steps to completion of 14.2 vs. 23.7 (VulnBot). RME reduced CLE by 34% relative to the ablated no-RME configuration. In an Active Directory compromise case study, AutoSecAgent completed the chain in 22 minutes vs. 58 minutes (VulnBot) and 45 minutes (human team), achieving 85% IDS evasion vs. VulnBot's 40%. HPC scaling showed 83% parallel efficiency at 16 concurrent agents. Ablation revealed domain fine-tuning contributes the most (+14.0% VDR, +16.8% ESR vs. base DeepSeek), followed by iterative optimization (+8.9% ESR) and RME (+5.2% VDR). Strengths: - Formal operationalization of Context Loss Error (CLE) as a trajectory-level consistency metric with a defined rule-based detection procedure, enabling reproducible measurement of a failure mode that prior work only described qualitatively. - Three-tier hierarchical memory architecture with structured context serialization (target descriptors, tool invocation metadata, salient outputs, analytic hypotheses, governance constraints) provides more principled state retention than ad-hoc summarizer-style context used by baselines. - End-to-end semi-automated workflow with explicit human-in-the-loop escalation, auditable decision traces, and remediation guidance generation — addressing safety and accountability requirements that fully autonomous systems ignore. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation testbed is small (49 vulnerabilities, 9 systems, 5 trials) and entirely controlled; external validity to Internet-scale environments, adversarially hardened targets, or heterogeneous production enterprise stacks is explicitly undemonstrated, and no comparison against traditional scanner-only pipelines (Nmap/OpenVAS) is provided quantitatively. - The 34% CLE reduction is measured only against AutoSecAgent's own ablated variant, not cross-system; the CLE metric relies on rule-based heuristics without independent validation or inter-rater reliability, making it difficult to assess absolute context retention quality. - Exploitation success (58.1%) remains substantially below expert-manual Metasploit operation (71.3%), hallucinations from DeepSeek still cause real exploitation failures, and there is no integrated governance or misuse prevention layer beyond human oversight — a serious gap for a dual-use capability.
Search Results
#156 — Moderately Innovative (48)

Task-Centric Personalized Federated Fine-Tuning of Language Models

Gabriel U. Talasso, Meghdad Kurmanji, Allan M. de Souza, Nicholas D. Lane, Leandro A. Villas · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The conceptual pivot from client-centric to task-centric personalization is sensible and addresses real failure modes (intra-client interference and test-time generalization). However, the technical machinery—K-Means clustering on frozen embeddings, per-cluster LoRA adapters, centroid-based routing—is a relatively straightforward assembly of existing components. The experimental scope (4 FLAN tasks, 8 clients, clean task separability) leaves open whether the approach works when task boundaries are fuzzy or overlapping in realistic federated settings.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard personalized federated learning (pFL) creates client-specific models to handle data heterogeneity, but this client-centric paradigm fails in two important ways: (1) generalization — when clients encounter unseen tasks or distribution shifts at test time, client-specific models collapse (Local and FedCluster drop to ~0.25 ROUGE-1 vs. ~0.55 when trained on seen tasks); and (2) intra-client task interference — when a single client holds data from multiple tasks, optimizing one adapter against conflicting objectives degrades all tasks. Prior work largely ignores both issues simultaneously. FedRouter addresses this by shifting personalization from the client level to the task level. Methodology: FedRouter trains one LoRA adapter per task rather than per client, using a three-component pipeline. (1) Local clustering: each client computes embeddings of its training data using the frozen base LLM, then applies K-Means to partition the data into task-specific subsets and trains a separate LoRA adapter per cluster. (2) Global clustering: clients share their local cluster centroids with the server; the server runs K-Means over all centroids to identify federation-wide task groups, aggregates corresponding adapters by averaging, and distributes them back via round-robin to control communication cost. (3) Evaluation router: at inference, each test sample is embedded and matched to its nearest centroid — either local (personalized mode) or global (generalized mode) — and routed to the corresponding adapter. The Silhouette Score is used to select the number of clusters automatically. Experiments use Llama 3.2 (1B) with LoRA rank 8, 8 clients, 25 rounds, 600 training samples per client, evaluated on four FLAN tasks (QQP, WebNLG, Samsum, GigaWord) using ROUGE-1. Key Results: Under task interference scenarios (Dual and All settings where each client holds 2 or 4 tasks respectively), FedRouter achieves ROUGE-1 of 0.558/0.566 vs. the next-best FedDPA at 0.551/0.549 — a ~6.1% relative improvement in the hardest setting. For generalization (training on single tasks, testing on all tasks), FedRouter scores 0.583 ± 0.005, compared to FedIT at 0.570, FedDPA at 0.461, and Local/FedCluster/FedSA at ~0.25 — a ~136% relative improvement over client-centric personalized baselines. Clustering accuracy on local test data reaches 100%/100%/95.4% for Single/Dual/All scenarios, confirming task separation quality. Scaling experiments show consistent gains from 1B → 3B → 8B Llama models (0.518 → 0.572 → 0.650 ROUGE-1) and from 4 → 8 → 16 clients (0.549 → 0.563 → 0.570), with no saturation observed. Strengths: - Clean conceptual shift from client-centric to task-centric personalization directly addresses two previously under-studied failure modes (intra-client interference and test-time generalization) in a unified framework. - The dual-mode router (local vs. global centroid matching) elegantly handles both standard and distribution-shift evaluation without requiring separate model variants or retraining. - Silhouette Score-based automatic cluster selection works well empirically (correctly identifies k=4 tasks in all three scenarios), reducing hyperparameter burden. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experimental scope is narrow: only 4 tasks from FLAN, 8 clients, 25 rounds, and a small 1B model as the primary testbed. It is unclear whether the clean task separability observed in t-SNE (which enables near-perfect clustering) generalizes to realistic federated settings where task boundaries are fuzzy or continuously shifting. - The round-robin adapter scheduling means each adapter is trained infrequently relative to standard FL, and the paper does not analyze convergence speed or total wall-clock time relative to baselines — the per-round communication cost is reduced but total rounds to convergence is unstudied. - The method assumes the number of distinct tasks in the federation is knowable (or estimable via Silhouette Score on server-side centroids), which may be impractical when tasks are unlabeled, overlapping, or emerge dynamically.
Recommended 2604.00050 PDF
#157 — Moderately Innovative (48)

Externalization in LLM Agents: A Unified Review of Memory, Skills, Protocols and Harness Engineering (33 ▲ )

Chenyu Zhou, Huacan Chai, Wenteng Chen, Zihan Guo, Rong Shan, Yuanyi Song, Tianyi Xu, Yingxuan Yang, Aofan Yu, Weiming Zhang, Congming Zheng, Jiachen Zhu, Zeyu Zheng, Zhuosheng Zhang, Xingyu Lou, Changwang Zhang, Zhihui Fu, Jun Wang, Weiwen Liu, Jianghao Lin, Weinan Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Apr 2026
Why this rank: A well-organized conceptual survey that usefully maps the LLM agent infrastructure landscape onto a cognitive-science-grounded externalization framework, but the contribution is largely taxonomic and post-hoc rather than generative of new testable principles. The absence of any empirical validation or prescriptive design guidance limits the paper's impact beyond intellectual synthesis.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Contemporary LLM agent development increasingly improves reliability not by training better models but by restructuring the runtime environment around them. Yet this shift lacks a coherent theoretical account—practitioners accumulate memory systems, tool protocols, and skill libraries without a principled explanation of why these architectural choices work or how they relate to one another. The paper addresses this gap by proposing "externalization" as the unifying transition logic behind all major advances in LLM agent infrastructure, drawing on Donald Norman's theory of cognitive artifacts to argue that external infrastructure does not merely add components but transforms the cognitive task itself. Methodology: The paper offers a conceptual survey and framework rather than empirical experiments. Its core claim is that reliable agency emerges from systematically relocating cognitive burdens—state continuity, procedural expertise, interaction structure—from the model's internal computation into persistent, inspectable, and reusable external structures. The resulting taxonomy identifies four coupled components: (1) Memory, which externalizes state across time (architectures ranging from monolithic context to retrieval-augmented stores to hierarchical adaptive systems); (2) Skills, which externalize procedural expertise (from atomic tool primitives through large-scale primitive selection to packaged, composable capability artifacts); (3) Protocols, which externalize interaction structure (agent-tool protocols like MCP/OpenAPI, agent-agent protocols like A2A, and agent-user protocols); and (4) Harness Engineering, a unification layer that orchestrates the three dimensions through agent loop control, sandboxing, approval gates, observability, policy encoding, and context budget management. The paper traces a historical arc from weights-centric to context-centric to harness-centric design, analyzes six bidirectional interaction flows among the three modules (memory↔skill distillation, skill→protocol invocation, protocol→memory assimilation, etc.), and discusses the parametric-vs-externalized trade-off space. The theoretical anchor draws on Norman's representational transformation, Kirsh's complementary strategies, and Hutchins's distributed cognition. Key Results: As a survey and position paper, quantitative results are not reported. The primary contribution is the conceptual framework and its synthesis of the literature. The paper maps hundreds of contemporary systems (Claude Code, Voyager, BabyAGI, MemGPT, AutoGen, MCP ecosystem, etc.) onto the externalization taxonomy and identifies several non-obvious system-level dynamics: (a) the three modules compete for the same context-window budget, requiring explicit allocation management; (b) the interaction cycle is self-reinforcing—better memory enables better skill distillation, richer traces improve memory—but this positive feedback also amplifies errors and poisoned memory; (c) the parametric/externalized boundary is not fixed and shifts as models improve (larger effective context reduces need for external memory; stronger tool-use reduces need for intent-capture harness logic). Future directions discussed include plans as first-class harness objects, runtime evaluation infrastructure, self-evolving harnesses, multi-modal externalization, and the convergence of digital and embodied agent architectures via the same decomposition logic (cerebrum LLM + cerebellum VLA skills). Strengths: - Provides the most thorough theoretical grounding to date for why agent infrastructure works, anchored in cognitive science (Norman, Kirsh, Hutchins) rather than ad hoc engineering intuition, giving the framework predictive and explanatory power beyond cataloging existing systems. - The cross-cutting analysis of six bidirectional module interaction flows is genuinely novel and analytically sharp—particularly the identification of context-budget competition, timescale mismatches (fast protocol loops vs. slow skill-evolution loops), and error amplification cascades as emergent system-level risks. - The parallel between digital agent externalization and embodied robotics (cerebrum/cerebellum decomposition of VLA systems) is insightful and points toward a unified engineering stack across domains. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is entirely non-empirical: no experiments validate whether the externalization framework actually predicts which agent architectures perform better or how much each module contributes. The cognitive-artifact analogy, while evocative, remains metaphorical—there is no formal model or measurable proxy for "cognitive burden externalized." - The framework is largely post-hoc: it organizes existing work into three buckets (memory/skills/protocols) without producing new design principles with testable implications, specific quantitative tradeoffs, or prescriptive guidance for practitioners deciding when to externalize versus leave capability in the model.
Recommended 2604.08224 PDF
#158 — Moderately Innovative (47)

A Methodological Framework for Runtime Ontology Evolution in Dynamic Environments

mdpi.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The architectural separation between probabilistic LLM interpretation and deterministic symbolic validation is a sound design principle for runtime ontology evolution, and the schema-vs-instance update taxonomy addresses a recognized gap. However, the quantitative evaluation (100 hand-crafted scenarios, no baselines) is too limited to substantiate the framework's claims, and runtime performance and scalability are entirely deferred.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Intelligent and robotic systems operating in real-world environments must make decisions in contexts only partially known at design time. The dominant practice of designing, populating, and validating ontologies offline assumes static domain knowledge—an assumption that fails in dynamic environments where new entities, relations, and configurations continuously emerge. Without runtime update mechanisms, an ontology progressively loses alignment with the operating environment, degrading the quality of formal reasoning and downstream decision-making. Existing ontology evolution approaches are largely offline or semi-automated, requiring human supervision and thus unsuitable for continuously operating autonomous systems. The paper addresses the open problem of controlled, autonomous ontology updating at runtime while preserving formal OWL consistency. Methodology: The authors propose a four-layer modular pipeline architecture: (1) a Perception Layer that produces structured JSON observations from vision-based models (treated as a black box); (2) a Semantic Interpretation Layer that uses an LLM as a controlled semantic parser—not a reasoner—to generate candidate subject–predicate–object triplets from perceptual input; (3) an Ontology and Knowledge Base Management Layer that validates and normalizes candidate triplets (handling OWL symbol correctness, datatype normalization, inverse property management, and semantic degradation prevention) and distinguishes between two update types—schema-level updates introducing new OWL classes when unmodeled concepts are encountered, and instance-level updates that populate existing classes with new individuals; and (4) a Reasoning and Decision Support Layer that consumes the updated ontology. The key design principle is a strict separation between the probabilistic LLM interpretation stage and the deterministic symbolic validation stage, so that LLM-generated ambiguity cannot corrupt the formal knowledge base. Knowledge is represented as SPO triplets consistent with OWL/RDF. The implementation uses Protégé for ontology inspection and Apache Fuseki for RDF storage and SPARQL querying. Key Results: Validation is primarily qualitative, conducted in laboratory scenarios inspired by the MUSIC4D project (adaptive creative environments). Two representative scenarios are demonstrated: (1) a known concept with a new instance (e.g., observing a violin when the MusicalInstrument class already exists), where the system correctly limits the update to instance population without modifying the schema; and (2) an entirely unmodeled concept (e.g., observing a piano when no MusicalInstrument class exists), where the system identifies a conceptual gap and extends the ontological schema before populating instances. A small-scale quantitative evaluation on 100 controlled symbolic retrieval scenarios (balanced 50/50 present/absent triples) yielded 96% accuracy, a False Missing Rate of 8% (4 triples present but not retrieved), and a False Known Rate of 0% (no hallucinated knowledge inferences). No baseline comparisons are provided. Strengths: - The architectural separation between LLM-based semantic interpretation and formal symbolic validation is a sound design choice that isolates probabilistic errors from the knowledge base, preventing semantic degradation during iterative updates. - The explicit two-category update taxonomy (schema-level vs. instance-level) directly addresses a recognized gap in ontology evolution literature and enables principled, incremental knowledge growth without unstructured ontology bloat. - The domain-agnostic, modular pipeline design is well-motivated; swapping the perception or interpretation technologies does not require changes to the knowledge management layer. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The quantitative evaluation is extremely limited: 100 hand-crafted retrieval scenarios test only whether already-integrated triples can be looked up, not the LLM interpretation quality, schema-matching correctness, or the end-to-end pipeline accuracy. No comparison against alternative approaches (e.g., direct LLM-to-ontology methods, KnowRob, other neuro-symbolic systems) is provided. - Runtime performance, computational overhead, and scalability under continuous operation are explicitly deferred to future work, leaving open whether the approach is viable in real-time robotic deployments or only in episodic, task-triggered scenarios.

#159 — Moderately Innovative (46)

Safe-Unsafe Concept Separation Emerges from a Single Direction in Language Models Activation Space

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The finding that safe-unsafe separation lies along a single linear direction is confirmatory rather than surprising, as linear separability of high-level concepts in LLM activations has been repeatedly demonstrated. The causal validation via directional ablation adds value, and the data efficiency is notable, but the core methodology (LDA + linear probe on frozen activations) is standard. The acknowledged ceiling on toxicity detection (F1≈0.75) highlights exactly where the single-direction assumption breaks down.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Ensuring LLM safety is critical, yet dominant approaches—RLHF, supervised fine-tuning, and external post-hoc guardrails—are either resource-intensive, require repeated retraining, or degrade under distribution shift and adversarial inputs. The paper asks whether safety-relevant concepts are geometrically structured within a frozen model's activation space, and whether that structure can be exploited without modifying model weights. Methodology: The authors propose a five-stage pipeline centered on Concept Activation Vectors (CAVs) and lightweight linear probes. For a frozen LLM with L layers, they extract hidden activations for a small labeled set of safe/unsafe prompts (500 examples, balanced). At each layer, Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) is used to compute a CAV—the direction maximizing between-class scatter relative to within-class scatter. The layer whose CAV yields the highest AUROC on a held-out validation set (100 examples) is designated the "GuardLayer." A logistic regression or SVM probe is then trained solely on GuardLayer activations for inference-time classification. To move beyond correlation, the authors validate causality via Directional Ablation (Activation Steering): the probe weight vector is used to subtract the unsafe direction from activations of correctly-classified unsafe inputs, and Flip Rate (fraction shifting to safe) is measured as intervention strength α increases. The method is evaluated on 4 benchmarks (PolyGuard with 100K+ multi-domain prompts, ToxiGen with 250K implicit hate speech examples, a 5K Prompt Injection benchmark, and PolyGuardPrompts covering 16 non-English languages) across 8 open-source models (4 encoders, 4 decoders ranging from 86M to 3B parameters). Key Results: On PolyGuard structured domains (Finance, Law, HR), decoder models (LLaMA3.2-1B/3B, Qwen2-1.5B) achieve F1 > 0.97–0.99 with both LR and SVM probes, surpassing all guardrail baselines including Granite Guardian (5B) which peaks around F1=0.88. On Social Media—the hardest policy domain—decoders reach F1≈0.80–0.83 vs. Granite Guardian's 0.70–0.72. On Prompt Injection, the method achieves F1≈0.87–0.88 (LLaMA3B) vs. Prompt-Guard-2 (86M, task-specialized) at 0.68. On ToxiGen (adversarial implicit toxicity), F1 plateaus at 0.74–0.75 for best decoders, still outperforming Llama-Guard-3 (1B) at 0.67 and Granite Guardian at 0.19. Multilingual evaluation shows F1 > 0.80 for high-resource languages (Chinese, German, Spanish, Japanese) with LLaMA3B, and 0.63–0.76 for mid/low-resource languages (Russian, Hindi, Korean). Causal validation via activation steering produces Flip Rates approaching 1.0 at α < 4, while a random-direction baseline remains near zero. Computationally, the full pipeline (layer selection + probe training + inference on 250K ToxiGen examples) takes ~300s for BERT-base and ~970s for LLaMA3.2-3B, versus 13,000–25,000s for large guardrail decoders. Strengths: - Principled mechanistic framing: using LDA-derived CAVs to identify the GuardLayer avoids exhaustive grid search over the full test set, yet consistently lands within the optimal AUROC plateau (within 0.015 of global maximum across all tested settings). - Causal not merely correlational: directional ablation experiments provide strong evidence that the identified linear direction actively mediates safety decisions, not just correlates with labels. - Remarkably data-efficient: only 500 labeled examples (250 per class) are needed to train effective probes, with competitive performance degrading gracefully even at 100 examples per class. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Toxicity performance ceiling is low (F1≈0.70–0.75) and the paper acknowledges that implicit, contextual forms of harm partially escape a single linear direction—the core assumption of the method breaks down precisely where safety matters most. - All benchmarks consist of static single-turn prompts; multi-turn adversarial interactions, context drift, and jailbreaks spanning multiple turns are not evaluated, leaving robustness in realistic deployment scenarios uncharacterized. - The method requires a separate CAV and probe per domain and per language, making it a collection of specialized classifiers rather than a unified model; the operational cost of maintaining dozens of probes at scale is not discussed.

#160 — Moderately Innovative (46)

Beyond Symbolic Solving: Multi Chain-of-Thought Voting for Geometric Reasoning in Large Language Models

Md. Abu Bakor Siddique, Shahrin Hossain, Sadman Ahmed Siam, Syed Rifat Raiyan, Hasan Mahmud, Md Kamrul Hasan · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: Achieves strong GPS results through a well-engineered combination of known inference-time scaling techniques — ensemble voting, entropy-based confidence, self-verification, and code augmentation — but none of these components are individually novel. The training-free design is practical, and ablations are thorough, but the approach is fundamentally an assembly of existing ideas applied to a constrained multiple-choice geometry setting.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Geometric Problem Solving (GPS) requires three capabilities: diagrammatic understanding, symbolic manipulation, and logical inference. Prior work — neural, symbolic, and neuro-symbolic — has focused heavily on the first two while leaving logical inference underdeveloped, typically committing to a single chain-of-thought reasoning path. A single erroneous step in that path propagates irrecoverably to a wrong answer. This paper addresses that gap by framing GPS as an inference-time scaling problem rather than a training problem. Methodology: MARS-GPS is a training-free, inference-time framework built on GPT-OSS 120B. It decomposes GPS into two stages. Stage 1 parses the input (diagram + text) into a unified formal representation F using PGDPNet for diagrams (extracting geometric predicates) and a rule-based regex parser for text (97% accuracy vs. 67% for a BART baseline). Stage 2 performs ensemble reasoning: k independent chain-of-thought rollouts are sampled in parallel, each augmented with a live Python sandbox for numerical computation (invoked in ~42% of rollouts). Each rollout produces an answer and a mean token-level Shannon entropy score as an inverse confidence signal. Answers are aggregated through a six-step procedure: (1) early consensus if any answer exceeds k/2+1 votes, (2) hard accept at k/2 votes, (3) candidate filtering at k/4 votes, (4) entropy-ranked ordering of candidates, (5) LLM self-verification at temperature 0 for each candidate in confidence order, and (6) weighted fallback combining vote count and entropy when all candidates are rejected (triggered in <8% of cases). The raw image is never passed to the reasoning model — all visual information flows through F. Key Results: On Geometry3K (601 test problems), MARS-GPS with k=8 achieves 88.8% accuracy, an 11% absolute improvement over the prior state-of-the-art Pi-GPS (77.8%) and 30% over the original Inter-GPS (57.5%). On PGPS9K, it achieves 77.48%, outperforming Pi-GPS (69.8%) by ~8% and all general-purpose MLLMs (GPT-4o: 51.0%, Gemini 2: 56.8%) by 20%+. It also surpasses LANS (82.3% on Geometry3K), which uses ground-truth diagram annotations and trains on the larger PGPS9K dataset. Ablations show accuracy scales log-linearly from 82.0% (k=1) to 88.0% (k=16) with diminishing returns beyond k=8. Component ablation on a subset reveals self-verification contributes the most (-4.5pp when removed), followed by code augmentation (-2.5pp). Entropy-weighted voting (87.5%) outperforms naive majority voting and entropy sorting (both 85.5%) by 2pp. Execution time analysis shows wrong predictions consume 4.2x more time than correct ones, and accuracy inversely correlates with execution time (98.6% for problems resolved in <10s, 42.1% for >300s). Strengths: - Training-free design: achieves state-of-the-art by scaling inference compute, making it immediately applicable to any sufficiently capable frozen LLM without geometry-specific fine-tuning. - The entropy-based confidence signal is zero-cost (derived from per-token log probabilities already returned by the model) and demonstrably useful for breaking ties and prioritizing verification. - Careful ablation study cleanly attributes gains to each component (voting strategy, code augmentation, self-verification, rollout count), with per-category and per-execution-time breakdowns that reveal meaningful failure mode patterns. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Heavy dependence on the parsing stage (PGDPNet + rule-based text parser): errors in F* cannot be recovered downstream regardless of rollout count, making parsing quality a hard performance ceiling that the paper does not systematically characterize. - Restricted to multiple-choice plane geometry; no evaluation on open-ended or proof-based tasks, limiting generalizability claims. The multiple-choice format also inflates absolute accuracy numbers relative to more realistic open-ended settings. - Computational cost scales linearly with k and self-verification adds additional LLM calls; the paper does not report total API cost or wall-clock time comparisons against baselines, making practical deployment cost unclear.

#161 — Moderately Innovative (46)

Large eddy simulation of auto-igniting methanol/n-dodecane blend spray flames

osuva.uwasa.fi · parmetis - new results
Why this rank: A well-executed application study that systematically maps the methanol/n-dodecane blend ignition boundary using 0D screening to guide high-fidelity LES, with rigorous multi-level validation. The mechanistic decomposition of heat release into five chemistry-mode subgroups adds useful insight. However, the methodology (LES in OpenFOAM, Lagrangian spray tracking, tabulated chemistry) is entirely established, and the novelty lies in the specific fuel-blend parametric study rather than in any new computational or modeling approach.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Methanol is an attractive low-carbon fuel for compression ignition (CI) engines due to its sustainable production pathways and lower emissions, but its poor ignitability — caused by a high heat of vaporization and net OH radical consumption at low temperatures — prevents stable combustion without an ignition improver. Blending methanol with diesel (modeled here via n-dodecane surrogate) mitigates this, yet the maximum methanol fraction achievable while maintaining engine-relevant ignition delay times (IDT < 1 ms) across practical ambient temperatures remains uncharacterized. Existing LES work covers pure fuels or dual-fuel pilot configurations but not the blend ignition boundary as a function of both composition and temperature. Methodology: The study uses high-fidelity Large Eddy Simulation (LES) in OpenFOAM-12 with an implicit-LES (ILES) approach and adaptive mesh refinement (AMR), achieving 62.5 μm resolution in the spray region. The ECN Spray D injector geometry is used at 150 MPa injection pressure and 22.8 kg/m³ ambient density. Chemical kinetics are handled via DLBFoam with a modified Polimi96 mechanism (96 species, 993 reactions) augmented with extended Zeldovich reactions for NO. Lagrangian particle tracking with no-breakup model (Weber number < 12) handles spray dynamics. A preliminary 0D homogeneous reactor screening via Cantera's mixing-line concept over the full composition space (0–100% methanol in 5% steps, Z = 0.02–0.20, T = 900–1100 K) guides case selection. The four LES cases selected represent the maximum methanol fraction at each ambient temperature satisfying IDT < 1 ms: (I) 10% MeOH / 900 K, (II) 20% MeOH / 950 K, (III) 30% MeOH / 1000 K, (IV) 70% MeOH / 1100 K. Validation covers pure n-dodecane (ECN Spray A and D) and pure methanol (new Sandia ECN Spray D experiments), plus comparison with previously unpublished CRU experiments on methanol/octanol/diesel ternary blends. Key Results: The validated LES model predicts IDTs of 0.58, 0.65, 0.80, and 0.99 ms for Cases I–IV, all within the IDT < 1 ms criterion; adding 10% more methanol to any case pushes IDT beyond 1 ms. Methanol content suppresses ignition through two mechanisms: (1) larger spray temperature drops during evaporation (ΔT ≈ 150 K for Case IV vs. 84 K for Case I), and (2) net OH consumption during low-temperature chemistry. Ignition location shifts from rich (Z = 0.093–0.095, Zst ≈ 0.062 for Cases I–II) to near-stoichiometric (Case III) to lean (Z = 0.028, Zst = 0.073 for Case IV) as methanol fraction increases. The methanol-dominant Case IV exhibits the highest HTC heat release at ignition (1290 kW vs. 710 kW for Case I) due to greater fuel–air mixture accumulation before ignition, but quasi-steady HTC converges to 300–400 kW across all cases, indicating similar steady-state behavior. LTC contribution to total HRR decreases monotonically from 16.3% (Case I) to 8.3% (Case IV). NO emissions in Case IV are 2.5× higher than Case I (thermal NO pathway, temperature-driven), while C₂H₂ (soot precursor) is 2.2× lower; CO is nearly unchanged across all cases. Flame lift-off length is shortest for Case IV (26 mm) vs. ~29–30 mm for Cases I–III. Strengths: - Rigorous multi-level validation: pure n-dodecane against established ECN datasets, pure methanol against new Sandia ECN Spray D experiments, and blends against previously unpublished CRU ternary blend measurements — providing genuine experimental grounding for blend predictions. - Systematic composition-temperature parametric sweep using 0D reactor screening to rationally select LES cases, avoiding costly brute-force 3D simulation of the full parameter space. - Mechanistic clarity: the study quantitatively decomposes HRR into five chemistry-mode subgroups (early LTC, LTC, pre-HTC, HTC pre-ignition, HTC), enabling precise attribution of how methanol fraction alters each combustion stage. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Blend validation is limited to IDT only; liquid/vapor penetration and flame lift-off length for the methanol/n-dodecane blends are not experimentally validated, and the authors themselves flag this as an open gap. - The turbulence–chemistry interaction (TCI) is treated with a crude first-order closure (no subgrid-scale model for chemical source terms), relying on the assumption that high turbulence and fine mesh resolution adequately capture the broadened reaction zone — an assumption that is not independently verified. - Octanol (used as co-solvent in CRU experiments) is replaced by n-dodecane in simulations on the basis of similar bulk properties and shock-tube IDT, but the detailed combustion chemistry differs and is acknowledged to be outside scope, introducing unquantified systematic error in the blend validation.
