#176 — Incremental (38)

Understanding User Privacy Perceptions of GenAI Smartphones

Ran Jin, Liu Wang, Shidong Pan, Luona Xu, Tianming Liu, Haoyu Wang · arxiv.org · genai manufacturing - new results
Why this rank: A timely qualitative study targeting a genuinely new privacy surface (system-level GenAI on smartphones), but the contribution is confirmatory rather than generative—the design suggestions (fine-grained permissions, data desensitization, transparency overlays) are well-established in usable security literature. The small, skewed sample (n=22, mostly young IT/CS-affiliated) and absence of any technical artifact limit the paper's lasting impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: GenAI smartphones — devices with system-level integration of generative AI (e.g., Apple Intelligence, Samsung Galaxy AI, Xiaomi HyperAI) — represent a qualitatively new privacy threat compared to traditional mobile devices. Unlike app-bounded AI, these systems leverage elevated OS permissions (notably accessibility services) to operate cross-app, execute autonomous multi-step tasks, and persistently collect contextual data (screen content, voice, location, sensor readings). Despite IDC projecting 912 million shipments by 2028 (78.4% CAGR), virtually no empirical work has examined how end users perceive the privacy implications of these devices or what safeguards they expect. This gap is especially acute before design patterns become entrenched. Methodology: The authors conducted 22 semi-structured one-on-one interviews (June–July 2025) with everyday mobile users holding varying technical backgrounds (59% IT/CS, 41% non-technical; 68% current/former GenAI smartphone users). Interviews followed a five-section protocol: introduction, awareness/usage, task-flow illustration via real scenarios, privacy concerns elicitation, and design suggestion gathering. Thematic saturation was reached at interview 20; IRR (Krippendorff's alpha) reached 0.82 after reconciliation rounds, yielding a codebook of 10 themes, 24 subthemes, and 95 codes. A follow-up focus group with 8 technically experienced participants rated 7 distilled design suggestions on effectiveness, feasibility, and implementation difficulty using 5-point Likert scales, then discussed them openly. The study is purely qualitative/user-study in nature — there is no new algorithm, model, or system built. Key Results: Privacy concern scores rose from a mean of 2.9 to 4.1 (on a 5-point scale) after participants were exposed to technical details of how GenAI smartphones operate, with 41% increasing by one or two levels — demonstrating a substantial awareness gap. Most users engaged with GenAI features only occasionally and conflated app-level AI with true system-level integration. Privacy concerns spanned four clusters: (1) data lifecycle risks (opaque collection via background accessibility permissions, profile-building for targeted ads, insecure cloud storage, inability to delete data); (2) unpredictable AI inference and misoperation (models inferring sensitive attributes, hallucination-driven erroneous UI actions such as completing unintended payments); (3) lack of transparency and legal safeguards (hidden reasoning chains, "digital resignation" in the face of manufacturer power); and (4) permission abuse and adversarial attacks (accessibility service exploitation, prompt injection, model inversion). On the focus group's rated suggestions, Data Desensitization (pre-upload sensitive-data identification) scored highest on both effectiveness (4.4) and feasibility (4.4), while Intent-Based Permission Framework scored high on effectiveness (4.3) but very low on feasibility (2.3) due to ecosystem and engineering obstacles. Dedicated Data Management Space was rated the most feasible (4.5) with moderate effectiveness (3.8). Strengths: - Timely and well-scoped study targeting a genuinely new privacy surface (system-level GenAI) that prior mobile or LLM privacy literature does not cover; fills an empirically important gap at an early adoption window. - Rigorous qualitative methodology: IRR reaching 0.82, codebook with 95 codes, thematic saturation verified, and a complementary focus group that adds technical feasibility grounding to raw user suggestions. - The three-layer design framework (system, data, UI) with Likert-rated trade-offs between effectiveness and feasibility is practically actionable for designers and regulators, going beyond pure concern documentation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Sample of 22 skews toward younger, educated, IT/CS-affiliated users recruited via social media and institutional lists — severely limiting generalizability to older adults, low-literacy users, and non-Chinese contexts (study conducted at Huazhong University of Science and Technology). - The pre-interview priming document and scenario walkthroughs almost certainly inflated post-exposure concern ratings; the authors acknowledge this confound but cannot disentangle genuine attitude change from interviewer-induced salience. - The focus group (n=8, all technically experienced, all drawn from the same interview pool) is too small and homogeneous to validate the feasibility ratings as representative; the quantitative Likert scores are presented with unwarranted precision given the sample size. - The paper produces no novel technical artifact — all design suggestions (fine-grained permissions, data desensitization, transparency overlays) are already well-known in the usable security literature; the contribution is confirmatory rather than generative.

#177 — Incremental (38)

Denominal Converted Verbs with Particles in English: Unlocking the Semantic - Ontological Role of Nominal Bases and Particles through a Lexical- Semantic Approach

journals.linguistik.de · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: Pioneering in scope as the first systematic corpus study of particled denominal verbs, and the Semantic-Ontological Model integrating frame semantics with image schemas is theoretically coherent. However, the methodology is traditional corpus linguistics with manual annotation, no inter-annotator reliability, no inferential statistics, and small category sizes, limiting the strength of the claimed correlations.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: English frequently produces complex verbs like lawyer up and fence off that combine two well-studied but independently investigated phenomena: noun-to-verb conversion (denominal verbalization) and particle verb formation. Prior work has treated these as separate constructs, and the question of whether such forms are "particle verbs + noun incorporation" or "converted verbs + particle" has gone unresolved. A generativist neo-constructionist account (à la Hale & Keyser 1993) would subsume both under root incorporation, eliminating conversion entirely. This paper argues that conversion is genuinely active and that particle choice is non-arbitrary, governed by the lexical semantics of the base noun. The gap being filled is the near-complete absence of empirical investigation into particled denominal verbs (NV-Ps) as a unified construction type. Methodology: The study uses a corpus-driven, lexical-semantic approach. NV-Ps were extracted from the enTenTen21 corpus (52 billion words) via Corpus Query Language, sampling 1,000 hits per particle (up, out, off, down) for a total of 4,000 annotated instances covering 224 distinct NV-P types. Instances were manually classified into Lieber's (2004) ten frame-semantic categories (FSCs) of denominal conversion: instrumental, ornative, similative, motive, performative, locative, causative, inchoative, privative, and stative. The primary analytical tool is a Semantic-Ontological Model (SOM), adapted from Paradis (2005), which decomposes each NV-P into four variables — subject [x], object [y], base noun [z], and particle [w] — and maps the content ontologies of [z] and [w] (e.g., [artifact], [animate], [substance], [collective]) onto relational ontologies (e.g., [artifact/location_isolation]) grounded in cognitive image schemas (UP IS MORE, BODIES ARE CONTAINERS, STATES ARE LOCATIONS, CONTROL IS UP). Type-frequency heatmaps across the four particle datasets are the primary quantitative output; no inferential statistics are reported. Key Results: Nine of Lieber's ten FSC types are attested in the NV-P data (stative is absent, consistent with its known low productivity in English conversion). Clear FSC–particle correlations emerge: Instrumental and Similative frames favor out; Ornative and Inchoative frames strongly favor up (ornative: 27/45 types are NV-up; inchoative: 9/13); Performative frames also favor out (13/24); Privative frames favor off (6/14). The particles off and down show less semantic niche-specificity. Across FSC types, the particle's completive function is consistently derived indirectly from its locative/directional primary meaning via image schemas — e.g., UP IS MORE → completion of provision in ornative beer up; BODIES ARE CONTAINERS → identity transition completion in similative Hulk out; CONTROL IS UP → downward force application in instrumental jawbone down. The data also reveal productive relational ontology pairings such as [artifact/location_communication] (fax out), [artifact/location_isolation] (fence off), [human/transition] (geek out), and [substance/quantity] (juice up). The presence of nine conversion frame types in the corpus argues against the neo-constructionist analysis and for the "converted verb + particle" derivational pathway. Strengths: - Pioneering scope: this is the first systematic corpus study of the NV-P construction, filling a conspicuous gap at the intersection of two major morphological research traditions. - The SOM framework is theoretically well-grounded, integrating frame semantics, content ontologies, and cognitive image schemas into a coherent multi-layer analysis that explains both argument structure and metaphoricity of NV-Ps. - The corpus methodology is transparent and replicable, with CQL queries reported and down-sampling strategy explicitly justified; the heatmap presentation of type-frequency distributions provides clear, readable evidence for FSC–particle correlations. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The study relies entirely on type frequencies and manual annotation with no inter-annotator reliability reported; FSC assignments for novel or opaque NV-Ps (e.g., zhuzh up, MacGyver up) are interpretive and potentially contestable. - No inferential statistical testing (chi-square, logistic regression, etc.) is applied to the FSC–particle co-occurrence data, so the claimed "correlations" lack formal significance assessment — the pattern sizes are often small (e.g., inchoative n=13, locative n=13) and sampling is limited to infinitival forms only, which may systematically skew the FSC distribution. - The paper does not engage with diachronic data or productivity measurement, leaving open when these constructions grammaticalized and whether the FSC–particle alignments are stable or shifting; it also does not model competition between synonymous NV-Ps (e.g., partner up vs. partner out) with a principled predictive account.
Search Results
#178 — Incremental (38)

The Green Computing Infrastructure & Reporting Ontology : A Modular Ontology for Sustainable IT Management

scitepress.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: GCIRO's breadth across five sustainability dimensions (infrastructure, metrics, governance, interventions, provenance) in a single modular OWL 2 DL ontology fills a documented gap, and the principled reuse of W3C standards is sound engineering. However, the contribution is integrative rather than methodologically novel, and evaluation against self-authored competency questions on toy data is thin.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: The ICT sector consumes 4–6% of global electricity and generates 1.4% of GHG emissions, with projections suggesting these figures could double by 2040. Despite growing regulatory pressure (EU Taxonomy, CSRD), organizations lack a unified semantic framework for managing IT environmental footprints. Existing ontologies (EEOnt, INDL, CoCoOn, SEAS, GCO, Green AI Ontology) each address narrow slices—energy efficiency, cloud infrastructure, or building systems—but none integrates ICT infrastructure topology, multi-dimensional sustainability metrics (carbon, water, circularity, e-waste), governance/lifecycle management, intervention modeling, and provenance into a single coherent framework. This fragmentation blocks cross-domain analysis, automated optimization, and auditable regulatory reporting. Methodology: The authors propose GCIRO (Green Computing Infrastructure & Reporting Ontology), a modular OWL 2 DL ontology built using the NeOn methodology (Scenario 3: reusing ontological resources). GCIRO comprises three primary modules: (1) Infrastructure & Facilities—models physical/logical ICT topology (sites, data centers, racks, devices, cooling, power, virtual resources, network) by specializing BOT for spatial hierarchy and SOSA/SSN for sensor observations; (2) Metrics & Impacts—provides a uniform pattern for quantitative sustainability data via a `girmi:QuantifiedEntity` hierarchy covering metrics (PUE, WUE, energy use) and impacts (GHG emissions), with carbon modeling through scoped emission factors, impact assessments, and budgets aligned to time intervals; (3) Governance & Lifecycle—captures policies, standards, controls, lifecycle state transitions (acquisition through decommissioning), governance actions, and commitments with deadlines. GCIRO reuses PROV-O for provenance, QUDT/OM for units, SOSA/SSN for observations, BOT for building topology, SKOS for controlled vocabularies, and FOAF/ORG for actors. Implementation used Protégé 5.8; validation was performed via OWL reasoning and 57 SPARQL competency questions on realistic data center scenarios. Key Results: All 57 competency questions were answered successfully with SPARQL queries, demonstrating coverage of the intended use cases. Representative demonstrated queries include: identifying unmounted ICT devices, detecting under-utilized servers (<10% utilization), correlating high grid carbon intensity (>500 gCO2/kWh) with underutilized servers (<20%), and identifying worst-case instantaneous PUE with associated facility/IT power breakdowns. The paper does not report performance benchmarks (query latency, scalability at production scale) or comparison against a quantitative baseline. Validation is strictly qualitative—competency question completeness and OWL DL consistency via reasoning. Strengths: - Genuinely comprehensive scope: the only ontology in the surveyed field covering all five dimensions (ICT infrastructure, multi-dimensional metrics, governance/lifecycle, interventions, provenance) simultaneously, filling a well-documented gap. - Strong reuse of established W3C/community standards (PROV-O, QUDT, SOSA/SSN, BOT), which grounds interoperability claims in existing tool ecosystems rather than requiring bespoke tooling. - Modular OWL 2 DL design allows independent extension, selective import, and computational tractability; the 45-query SPARQL library lowers the barrier to adoption. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is thin and self-referential: 57 competency questions were written by the authors and answered by the same authors' SPARQL queries against toy data center instances—there is no independent validation, no performance benchmarking at scale, and no comparison against operational DCIM/CMDB systems. - Coverage gaps are acknowledged but significant: application-level eco-design, software engineering practices, procurement/supply-chain impacts, financial and risk dimensions are absent, limiting applicability for organizations whose footprint is dominated by software or Scope 3 emissions. - The paper is published at ICAART (agents/AI venue), which is an unusual fit for an ontology engineering contribution; the work would benefit from deeper engagement with the ontology engineering and Semantic Web communities (e.g., ISWC, ESWC) where critical reviewers would stress-test the formal axiomatization.

#179 — Incremental (38)

VICBO–An Ontology -Enhanced Blockchain for Efficient and Adaptive Vehicle Insurance Claim Management

161.53.78.90 · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: VICBO competently integrates established technologies (Hyperledger Fabric, OWL 2, SWRL, IPFS) into a four-layer architecture for insurance claim processing, but represents a standard systems-integration effort rather than a novel contribution. The jurisdiction-adaptive reasoning via SWRL rules is a reasonable differentiator, yet performance caps at ~160 TPS on a single machine with no comparison to prior blockchain insurance systems and no validation with real insurance data or practitioners.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Traditional vehicle insurance claim workflows suffer from inefficiency, information asymmetry, fraud, and inconsistent compliance across jurisdictions. While prior blockchain-based insurance systems automate transactions and record data immutably, they cannot adapt to jurisdiction-specific rules, dynamically changing policy conditions, or complex eligibility criteria that depend on semantic relationships between incidents, vehicles, documents, and regulatory entities. Smart contracts alone are procedural and static — they cannot reason about context. This paper addresses the gap by integrating semantic web technologies (ontology + rules) with blockchain to enable context-aware, adaptive claim processing. Methodology: VICBO is a four-layer hybrid architecture built on Hyperledger Fabric (permissioned blockchain, v2.5): (1) an off-chain storage layer using IPFS/Web3.Storage where documents are AES-256 encrypted and identified by cryptographic hashes/CIDs; (2) a smart contract layer (chaincode in Go) handling policy deployment, claim registration, and conditional payment execution; (3) a knowledge layer comprising the VICOn ontology (OWL 2 DL, ALCHIQ(D) expressivity; 44 classes, 61 object properties, 18 data properties, 210 individuals, 212+ axioms) developed via the NeOn methodology, reusing Dublin Core, Time Ontology, and Event Ontology, augmented with SWRL rules executed by the Pellet reasoner; and (4) a blockchain layer for immutable event logging. Semantic reasoning runs entirely off-chain in client-side middleware, which submits compliance verdicts and document-hash proofs as transaction inputs. Representative SWRL rules encode jurisdiction-dependent document requirements, incident-type-driven evidence rules, and vehicle-usage-based risk classification. GDPR compliance is addressed via off-chain encrypted storage, hybrid AES-256/public-key encryption, and cryptographic erasure (key destruction satisfying Article 17). Evaluation uses three SPARQL-based scenario tests (adaptive document requirements, subclass-based eligibility, cross-claim fraud detection) plus adversarial counter-tests, a competency question (CQ) coverage assessment (29 SPARQL queries across 7 groups), reasoning scalability benchmarks (1K–100K individuals), and Hyperledger Fabric performance experiments (throughput/latency for claim verification and compensation payment). Key Results: The VICOn ontology passes structural soundness checks (no inconsistent axioms, zero unsatisfiable classes) and achieves 93.1% average CQ coverage across seven knowledge domains, with gaps only in policy/contract management (80%) and document/evidence management (75%). Reasoning scalability is acceptable: at 100K individuals, classification takes 12.9s, consistency checking 5.8s, and SWRL rule execution 11.4s, consuming 1,420 MB peak memory — manageable for typical enterprise servers. System throughput for claim verification peaks at ~160 TPS at a 500 TPS input rate, declining thereafter due to ontology reasoning and IPFS retrieval overhead; latency rises sharply beyond 500 TPS, exceeding 14 seconds at 1,000 TPS. Compensation payment (pure on-chain) scales better, sustaining ~450 TPS throughput with latency growing only from <2s at 100 TPS to ~8s at 1,000 TPS. Adversarial SPARQL tests confirm zero false-positive inferences across all three scenarios (e.g., a DieselVan disjoint from ElectricVehicle correctly produces no eligibility match). Strengths: - The ontology-blockchain integration genuinely addresses a real limitation of prior work: static smart contracts cannot handle jurisdiction-specific, incident-type-specific, or vehicle-class-specific reasoning, and the SWRL+OWL approach handles all three with demonstrated examples. - The GDPR compliance design (off-chain encrypted storage, cryptographic erasure via key destruction, data minimization on-chain) is thoughtfully grounded in the EU Parliament STOA report and addresses the Article 17 right-to-erasure tension with blockchain immutability in a principled way. - Evaluation is multi-faceted — structural soundness, scenario-based SPARQL testing with adversarial counter-tests, CQ coverage, scalability benchmarks, and performance experiments — providing more coverage than most systems papers in this space. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Performance numbers are weak by enterprise standards: claim verification throughput caps at ~160 TPS with >14s latency at high load, and the entire experiment runs on a single local machine (Intel i7, 32 GB RAM) with only 2 organizations and 4 peers — far from production scale; no comparison to prior blockchain insurance systems is provided. - The ontology evaluation relies entirely on synthetic data and hand-crafted SPARQL queries designed by the same team that built the ontology; there is no independent validation, no real insurance data, and no user study with actual insurance practitioners testing whether VICOn captures real-world policy complexity. - The integration between the reasoning layer and the smart contract layer is architecturally loose: reasoning results are submitted as opaque inputs trusted by the smart contract without any on-chain verifiability guarantee — a malicious or misconfigured middleware could submit falsified compliance verdicts, undermining the trust model that motivated using blockchain in the first place.

#180 — Incremental (38)

OpenWorldLib: A Unified Codebase and Definition of Advanced World Models (149 ▲ )

DataFlow Team, Bohan Zeng, Daili Hua, Kaixin Zhu, Yifan Dai, Bozhou Li, Yuran Wang, Chengzhuo Tong, Yifan Yang, Mingkun Chang, Jianbin Zhao, Zhou Liu, Hao Liang, Xiaochen Ma, Ruichuan An, Junbo Niu, Zimo Meng, Tianyi Bai, Meiyi Qiang, Huanyao Zhang, Zhiyou Xiao, Tianyu Guo, Qinhan Yu, Runhao Zhao, Zhengpin Li, Xinyi Huang, Yisheng Pan, Yiwen Tang, Yang Shi, Yue Ding, Xinlong Chen, Hongcheng Gao, Minglei Shi, Jialong Wu, Zekun Wang, Yuanxing Zhang, Xintao Wang, Pengfei Wan, Yiren Song, Mike Zheng Shou, Wentao Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 38 as primarily an engineering unification and definitional exercise rather than a research contribution with novel methods or results. The explicit scoping of what qualifies as a world model is useful for field discipline, but the framework consists largely of abstract interface stubs with no quantitative evaluation — no latency benchmarks, no standardized metrics, no comparison against standalone implementations.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: The field of world models lacks a clear, broadly accepted definition, leading to confusion about which tasks genuinely constitute world model research versus tasks that merely borrow its structure. Models like Sora have been mislabeled as "world simulators," and tasks like text-to-video generation, avatar animation, and code generation are frequently conflated with world modeling. This definitional ambiguity hinders systematic progress. A secondary problem is the absence of a unified engineering framework: researchers working on interactive video generation, 3D reconstruction, multimodal reasoning, and vision-language-action (VLA) systems each build separate, incompatible codebases, making fair comparison and capability reuse difficult. Methodology: The paper makes two intertwined contributions. First, it proposes a formal definition of world models: a model or framework centered on building internal representations from perception, equipped with action-conditioned simulation and long-term memory, for understanding and predicting the dynamics of a complex world. It delineates which tasks qualify (interactive video generation, multimodal reasoning, 3D generation/simulation, VLA) and which do not (text-to-video without perceptual input, avatar animation, code generation, web search). Second, it introduces OpenWorldLib, a Python inference framework organized into five interacting modules: (1) Operator — validates and preprocesses multimodal inputs (text, images, audio, control signals) into standardized tensors; (2) Synthesis — modality-specific generative backends (visual, audio, VLA action signals) via a BaseSynthesis template with from_pretrained() and predict() interfaces; (3) Reasoning — general, spatial, and audio reasoning via a BaseReasoning template; (4) Representation — explicit 3D reconstruction (point clouds, depth, camera poses) and simulator export via BaseRepresentation; (5) Memory — persistent multimodal history with record/select/compress/manage operations via BaseMemory. A Pipeline class orchestrates single-turn (__call__) and multi-turn stateful (stream) inference. All modules share a common inheritance pattern ensuring API consistency across task types. Key Results: The paper presents qualitative demonstrations rather than benchmark numbers. In interactive navigation video generation, recent models (Lingbot-World, Hunyuan-GameCraft, YUME-1.5, Hunyuan-WorldPlay) substantially outperform the earlier Matrix-Game-2 in visual quality and temporal consistency; Hunyuan-WorldPlay achieves the best overall visual performance. For interactive manipulation video generation, Cosmos significantly outperforms WoW in generation quality and physical realism. In 3D generation, VGGT and InfiniteVGGT can produce multi-view scenes but exhibit geometric inconsistency and texture blur under large camera movement; FlashWorld trades quality for speed. VLA evaluation on LIBERO and AI2-THOR simulators demonstrates that π0, π0.5, and LingBot-VA can execute diverse manipulation tasks within the unified framework. No quantitative comparison tables against prior frameworks (e.g., latency, throughput, lines-of-code reduction) are provided. Strengths: - The definitional contribution is practically useful: the explicit exclusion of text-to-video, avatar generation, and code generation from the world model category imposes intellectual discipline on a field prone to scope inflation. - The modular, inheritance-based API design (BaseOperator, BaseSynthesis, BaseReasoning, BaseRepresentation, BaseMemory, BasePipeline) is clean and extensible; supporting both local checkpoint and cloud API backends in the same interface is a pragmatic engineering choice. - The framework spans a genuinely broad capability set — interactive video, 3D reconstruction, multimodal reasoning, VLA — within one coherent abstraction, which lowers the barrier to cross-task research and ablation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper contains essentially no quantitative evaluation: no latency benchmarks, no throughput numbers, no standardized task metrics (FVD, PSNR, action success rate) compared across methods or against prior frameworks. The "evaluation" section amounts to a qualitative visual gallery with brief prose comparisons, making it impossible to objectively assess whether OpenWorldLib introduces overhead or whether integrated models perform comparably to standalone implementations. - The base class templates shown are almost entirely abstract stubs (methods containing only `pass`), so the paper describes an interface contract rather than a functional system; the actual complexity and completeness of the concrete implementations are not exposed or analyzed, and reproducibility depends entirely on the GitHub repository.
Recommended 2604.04707 PDF
#181 — Incremental (38)

DeepServe: SLO-Aware and Cost-Aware Elastic Scheduling for Serverless Multi-Tenant LLM Inference

researchsquare.com · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: DeepServe++ combines contextual bandits with disaggregated prefill/decode and tiered KV-cache storage, addressing a real systems problem. However, the individual components (LinUCB scheduling, KV-cache tiering, warm-standby pools) are established techniques, and the unification, while useful, is engineering integration rather than conceptual novelty. The internal inconsistencies in the action space arithmetic, citation padding with unrelated works, and missing comparisons against the closest baselines (Sarathi-Serve, DistServe) significantly undermine confidence in the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Serving large language models (LLMs) in serverless, multi-tenant cloud environments involves compounding challenges: cold start latency from loading multi-billion-parameter checkpoints, KV-cache memory fragmentation (consuming up to 30% of GPU memory), inter-tenant resource contention, and tail latency spikes under bursty workloads. Existing systems (vLLM, Orca, DistServe, Mooncake, ServerlessLLM) each address individual aspects but no prior system jointly optimizes SLO compliance, cost efficiency, and multi-tenant fairness in a unified adaptive framework. DeepServe++ targets this gap. Methodology: DeepServe++ is a three-layer scheduling framework built atop vLLM. The first layer is a Request Profiler that extracts per-request feature vectors containing prompt length, predicted output length (via lightweight linear regression), and KV-cache affinity (prefix match ratio), with profiling overhead under 0.5ms. The second layer is a Contextual Bandit Scheduler (CB-Scheduler) using LinUCB that operates on 50ms epochs and selects from a composite action space covering batch size {1,2,4,8,16,32}, concurrency {1,2,4,8}, KV-cache eviction policy {LRU, LFU, prefix-aware}, and warm-up strategy {none, speculative, aggressive}. The reward signal combines an SLO-met indicator, normalized GPU-seconds consumed, and a penalty proportional to latency SLO violation magnitude. To reduce the action space, a hierarchical decomposition selects batch size and concurrency first, then conditions KV-cache and warm-up choices on those. The third layer is an Elastic GPU Execution Layer with disaggregated prefill and decode pools plus a warm-standby pool, backed by a three-tier distributed KV-cache store (GPU HBM → CPU DRAM → SSD). Experiments use LLaMA-2-13B and Mixtral-8x7B on 8×A100 GPUs with ShareGPT and BurstGPT traces. Key Results: At 20 req/s with LLaMA-2-13B and 8 tenants, DeepServe++ achieves P99 latency of 1020ms versus 2700ms for vLLM (62% reduction) and 4200ms for Static Batch (76% reduction). Effective throughput peaks at 13.5 req/s versus 9.2 for vLLM. GPU utilization is 82–91% versus 62–78% for vLLM. Cold start ratio stays below 0.18 at 20 tenants versus 0.65 for vLLM. Effective per-request cost is 0.83 GPU-s versus 1.06 for vLLM and 1.96 for Static Batch. SLO compliance rate is 96.8% versus 82.1% for vLLM. Cache hit rate reaches 0.72 versus 0.58 for vLLM. Ablation shows the bandit scheduler is the most critical component (+41% P99 when removed), followed by the profiler (+28%) and warm-standby (+3.2× cold start ratio). Scheduling overhead is 1.2ms per epoch. Strengths: - Unifies multiple interdependent scheduling decisions (batching, concurrency, KV eviction, warm-up) under a single online learning framework rather than treating them with separate heuristics. - Contextual bandit formulation is computationally lightweight compared to full RL, enabling real-time decisions at 50ms epochs with only 1.2ms overhead. - Ablation study is structured and isolates the contribution of each component with quantitative deltas, providing meaningful empirical decomposition. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The action space arithmetic is internally inconsistent: the paper claims |A|=576 but 6×4×3×3=216, and the reduction to '24+9=33 evaluations' is unexplained and algebraically unclear, undermining confidence in the implementation details. - The reference list contains numerous tangentially related or entirely unrelated works (financial fraud detection, federated learning for finance, industrial IoT fault diagnosis) that appear to be citation padding, and many cited works [11]–[35] are unpublished 2026 preprints without peer review, raising concerns about the scholarly rigor of the paper. - Sarathi-Serve and DistServe are discussed in the introduction as key prior works but are omitted from the empirical baselines, making the comparison incomplete relative to the closest related systems. - The paper is only 6 pages (Research Square preprint) and lacks details on how per-request cost is computed, how tenant SLO heterogeneity is configured, and how the system behaves under non-stationary workload shifts—a limitation the authors themselves acknowledge.
Search Results
#182 — Incremental (38)

Evaluating Small Language Models for Front-Door Routing: A Harmonized Benchmark and Synthetic-Traffic Experiment

Warren Johnson, Charles Lee · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Methodologically rigorous benchmarking study with commendable experimental design (pre-registered RCT, Bonferroni correction, transparent negative results), but the core contribution is an empirical evaluation of existing SLMs on a narrow six-label classification task with only 60 cases. The findings—that no model meets the viability threshold and that a 3B model is Pareto-dominant among self-hosted options—are operationally useful but unsurprising, and the omission of fine-tuned discriminative baselines weakens the practical conclusions.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Production LLM deployments face a model routing problem: how to select the right model at inference time while jointly optimizing output quality, cost, latency, and governance constraints. Existing approaches—learned routers trained on preference data, cascade architectures, or delegating classification to a full LLM—are themselves costly and high-latency, and reduce routing to a single optimization axis. The authors argue that small language models (SLMs, 1–4B parameters) have crossed a capability threshold enabling sub-second, zero-marginal-cost, self-hosted task classification, potentially making the routing decision negligible in the inference budget. The paper tests whether current SLMs are accurate enough to serve as production front-door classifiers across a six-label task taxonomy. Methodology: The paper presents two complementary studies. Study 1 is a harmonized offline benchmark comparing three SLMs—Phi-3.5-mini (3.8B), Qwen2.5-1.5B, and Qwen-2.5-3B—on identical Azure T4 hardware, vLLM 0.17.1 serving stack, 4-bit NF4 quantization, and a fixed 60-case corpus (6 families × 10 cases each). All models receive an identical frozen zero-shot classification prompt outputting JSON labels from a six-class taxonomy: code/simple, code/complex, CoT/simple, CoT/complex, hybrid/agentic, hybrid/generative. Pairwise McNemar tests assess significance. Study 2 is a pre-registered four-arm randomized experiment (N=400 sessions/arm) under synthetic traffic, comparing Phi-4-mini, Qwen-2.5-3B, and DeepSeek-V3 (671B MoE, commercial API) against a no-routing control, with SHA-256 session-ID randomization, Holm-Bonferroni correction, and O'Brien-Fleming interim boundaries. The effective sample size for accuracy endpoints is n_eff=60 unique cases (deterministic decoding, T=0.0), while latency and cost have N=400 independent observations. A pre-registered decision matrix maps outcomes to deployment actions via Pareto dominance over cost and accuracy. Key Results: Study 1: Qwen-2.5-3B achieves the highest exact-match accuracy (0.783, 47/60) with perfect parse rate (1.0) and 1,088 ms median latency. Phi-3.5-mini achieves 0.717 accuracy but is 5.3× slower (5,772 ms median). No significant accuracy difference between Phi-3.5-mini and Qwen-2.5-3B (McNemar p=0.503), but Qwen-2.5-3B is Pareto-dominant on the latency–accuracy tradeoff. Qwen2.5-1.5B is significantly weaker (0.400, p<0.0001 vs. both). Family-level analysis reveals split specialization: Phi excels at code/complex and CoT/complex (both 1.00); Qwen-1.5B excels at hybrid/agentic (1.00) but scores 0.0 on all CoT families; Qwen-2.5-3B is the only model with non-zero accuracy across all six families (minimum 0.60 on hybrid/generative). Qwen-2.5-3B reports confidence=1.0 on all 60 predictions, rendering self-reported confidence useless as a fallback trigger. Study 2: DeepSeek-V3 achieves the highest accuracy (0.830) but fails the pre-registered P95 latency gate (2,295 ms vs. 2,000 ms threshold). Qwen-2.5-3B achieves 0.793 accuracy at 988 ms median, $0 marginal cost—Pareto-dominant among self-hosted models. Phi-4-mini collapses to 0.518 accuracy with 8.5% parse failures. No arm meets the viable region criterion (≥0.85 accuracy, P95 ≤2,000 ms). F1 macro scores: Phi-4-mini 0.460, Qwen-2.5-3B 0.767, DeepSeek-V3 0.818. Error analysis shows 9 of 13 Qwen-2.5-3B misclassifications cluster at the code–hybrid boundary. The recommended deployment per the decision matrix is Qwen-2.5-3B as primary classifier with DeepSeek-V3 fallback for hard cases. Strengths: - Harmonized experimental design rigorously eliminates cross-study hardware, framework, and corpus confounds that plagued prior pilot studies—a meaningful methodological contribution for reproducible LLM evaluation. - Pre-registered multi-arm RCT design with Bonferroni correction, O'Brien-Fleming interim boundaries, and transparent reporting of negative results raises the bar for empirical rigor in the routing literature. - Practical operational framing: the paper correctly distinguishes classification accuracy (necessary) from routing value (sufficient), explicitly deferring the downstream quality validation as an open problem rather than overclaiming. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The 60-case evaluation corpus is extremely small, labeled by a single annotator without inter-annotator agreement statistics, and the code–hybrid boundary is acknowledged to be genuinely ambiguous—meaning the 85% viability threshold may approach or exceed human-level agreement, making it an unreliable production gate. - Study 2's synthetic-traffic design adds no independent classification observations beyond the n_eff=60 unique cases; the N=400 framing is misleading for accuracy endpoints and does not test concurrent multi-model serving, cold-start latency, or production overhead, severely limiting generalizability. - The paper omits a fine-tuned discriminative baseline (BERT/DeBERTa), which for this narrow classification task would likely outperform zero-shot generative SLMs by a substantial margin at lower latency, making the comparison set incomplete for assessing whether the generative SLM approach is genuinely competitive.
Recommended 2604.02367 PDF
#183 — Incremental (37)

A Neural Approach to Building a Lexicon for a Symbolic Minimalist Parser

par.nsf.gov · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: The neuro-symbolic integration of BERT with a formal minimalist parser is a theoretically motivated but narrow contribution. The ambiguity-preservation design via probability thresholding is a reasonable architectural choice, but evaluation on only 96 synthetic holdout sentences and 150 homograph pairs severely limits confidence in generalization. The identified frequency bias undermining the core design goal remains unresolved.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard NLP preprocessing pipelines commit to single part-of-speech (POS) assignments early, propagating cascading errors when inputs are lexically or structurally ambiguous. Existing tokenizers (Penn Treebank, WordPiece, BPE) fragment linguistically meaningful units — phrasal verbs, multi-word expressions, morphologically complex forms — at odds with how human language cognition actually works. The gap between data-driven statistical models and theoretically grounded linguistic models remains wide: LLMs require astronomical training data and lack cognitive plausibility. This paper targets the preprocessing front-end of HASPNeL (Hybrid Architecture Symbolic Parser and Neural Lexicon), a neuro-symbolic system grounded in Chomsky's Strong Minimalist Thesis (SMT), asking whether a BERT-based encoder can supply a lexicon for a formal minimalist parser while preserving genuine ambiguity rather than collapsing it. Methodology: HASPNeL's encoder is a four-stage pipeline. (1) Data Preparation: named entity recognition via spaCy consolidates multi-word units; a dual tokenization strategy runs a linguistically-aware "smart tokenizer" (preserving phrasal verbs and compounds) in parallel with WordPiece tokenization for BERT; a fine-tuned BERT (bert-base-cased) assigns softmax distributions over 27 POS categories per token (tags with probability > 0.05 are retained as valid, enabling multi-tag outputs); spaCy and Stanza perform morphological analysis and lemmatization in parallel; all outputs are merged into a unified per-token data structure. (2) Lexical Feature Retrieval or Estimation: tokens are looked up in an Excel-based lexicon organized by syntactic category, encoding SMT-compatible morphosyntactic features (phi-features, case, TAM, c-selectional features, theta roles, animacy, wh-features); unseen items fall back to rule-based feature estimation. The downstream symbolic parser implements Merge, PairMerge, FormSet, and Agree as its structure-building operations, producing all valid constituency trees; a likelihood estimator then ranks parses. The BERT tagger was fine-tuned on 1,920 manually annotated English sentences spanning six ambiguity categories, with an 80/15/5 train/validation/holdout split, AdamW at 2e-5, batch size 16, max 32 tokens. Key Results: On the 96-sentence holdout, the encoder achieves 98.41% token-level POS accuracy with consistent performance across lexical and structural ambiguity types. On a separate 150-sentence homograph dataset (75 homograph pairs targeting noun-verb and adjective-noun ambiguities), accuracy is 92.0%. The 14 error cases are strongly skewed: 11 of 14 are verb-as-noun confusions (e.g., "Produce the evidence" predicted as noun with 95.59% confidence; "Bow before the king" predicted as noun with 99.85% confidence), revealing a systematic frequency bias rather than random error. The system satisfies SMT constraints and demonstrates feasibility of bilingual (English/Spanish) single-lexicon modeling consistent with the Chomsky-Borer hypothesis. Strengths: - Principled theoretical grounding: the architecture is explicitly designed around SMT operations (Merge, Agree, phases), providing cognitive plausibility and a clear formal semantics absent from purely statistical systems. - Honest error analysis: the authors identify three concrete failure modes — frequency bias toward noun interpretations, circular dependency between lemmatization and POS tagging, and spurious sensitivity to sentence-final punctuation — rather than reporting only headline accuracy. - Ambiguity preservation by design: the 0.05 probability threshold for retaining multiple POS tags is a deliberate architectural choice to defer disambiguation to the symbolic parser, contrasting with standard taggers that force single-label commitment. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation corpus is synthetic and in-domain: both training and test data are from the same manually constructed 1,920-sentence English corpus, making the 98.41% holdout figure an optimistic measure of generalization. No evaluation on natural text (e.g., Penn Treebank WSJ, Universal Dependencies) is reported. - Frequency bias undermines the core ambiguity-preservation goal: the BERT tagger systematically suppresses verb readings of noun-frequent forms ("produce," "bow," "rebel") even when syntactic context is unambiguous, which is precisely the failure mode the architecture was designed to avoid. The proposed fix — post-hoc constraint-based filtering — is described at a high level but not implemented or evaluated.

#184 — Incremental (35)

Multi-modal graph neural networks for cross-domain educational recommendation: integrating behavioral analytics and institutional context for personalized learning

link.springer.com · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Integrating two complementary public educational datasets into a unified heterogeneous graph is a reasonable research direction, but the execution is critically undermined by weak baselines (only vanilla GCN and one prior method), a single-student qualitative case study presented as validation, and no ablation to isolate which components actually contribute. The cross-domain transfer claim lacks analysis of what knowledge meaningfully transfers between structurally incompatible datasets.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Educational recommendation systems have largely operated on single datasets with simplified learner-content interaction models, failing to capture the multi-faceted nature of learning across behavioral patterns, demographic contexts, and temporal dynamics. Existing GNN-based educational systems use static, single-modal graph representations and ignore temporal knowledge decay, cognitive load constraints, and cross-domain generalization. This paper proposes a multi-modal GNN framework that jointly integrates EdNet (a large-scale Korean language-learning behavioral dataset) with OULAD (a UK higher-education institutional dataset) to deliver personalized recommendations via cross-dataset knowledge transfer. Methodology: The proposed framework constructs a unified heterogeneous graph combining 78,431 sampled EdNet students and all 32,593 OULAD students. Node types include students, courses, skills, assessments, and temporal sessions; edge types encode behavioral interactions, academic progressions, cross-dataset alignments, temporal sequences, prerequisite dependencies, and peer collaboration links. The architecture stacks 3-layer GCN+GAT hybrid layers: GCNs aggregate structural neighborhood information via relation-specific weight matrices and attention coefficients, while multi-head GATs (8 heads) dynamically reweight edges. A cross-dataset integration layer combines per-domain embeddings with a learnable weighted sum plus cross-graph neighbor aggregation. Temporal-aware message passing uses an MLP to compute messages incorporating edge features and a temporal decay function, with GRU-based node updates. A cognitive-load-aware term is incorporated into edge weights via a `cognitive_edge_weight` field. Final recommendation scores are an ensemble of per-dataset scores plus a cross-dataset interaction term. The framework is implemented in PyTorch Geometric on Google Colab with AdamW optimization. Key Results: Three experiments are reported. (1) Individual recommendation accuracy: the framework achieves 84.7% Hit Rate@5 versus 75.8% for a standard GCN baseline and 82.4% success rate versus 79.1% for the baseline. Performance is relatively consistent across ability quartiles (89.2% top, 81.3% bottom). (2) Learning gap identification: demonstrated via a single OULAD case study (id_student 28264) showing the model detects prerequisite gaps in modules BBB and CCC with 89% confidence, correlating with an observed 74% VLE engagement deficit versus successful peers. (3) Difficulty personalization: the framework achieves 77.6% learning progress rate, 3.1/10 frustration, and 32.7 min/session engagement versus 68.9%/4.2/28.4 for an adaptive baseline and 28.7%/8.4/11.3 for a fixed-hard approach. Strengths: - Integrating two large public datasets with complementary characteristics (fine-grained behavioral logs from EdNet, rich demographics and assessment data from OULAD) is a legitimate and underexplored research direction. - The hierarchical temporal modeling that spans individual question responses up to course-level trajectories is architecturally well-motivated. - The inclusion of fairness metrics (demographic parity, equalized odds) and per-ability-quartile analysis adds educational validity beyond aggregate accuracy. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The baseline comparison is critically weak: only a standard GCN (Kipf & Welling 2016) and Study-GNN (Li et al. 2022) are compared. No modern sequential recommendation models (LightGCN, BERT4Rec, SASRec), cross-domain CF methods, or knowledge tracing baselines are included, making the claimed improvements difficult to contextualize. - The 'learning gap identification' experiment is a qualitative case study of a single student, not a statistically powered evaluation — this does not constitute scientific validation of the gap detection module. - Key claimed mechanisms lack operational detail: cognitive load modeling is described only as a graph edge weight (`cognitive_edge_weight`) with no explanation of how cognitive load is estimated from data; frustration levels in the difficulty experiment are reported as numeric scores with no description of measurement. - The two source datasets are structurally incompatible (an English-as-a-second-language quiz app vs. a UK distance-learning university), and the paper provides no analysis of what knowledge actually transfers across domains or whether cross-dataset edges encode meaningful relationships rather than noise. - No ablation study is provided to disentangle contributions of the individual components (GCN vs. GAT, temporal decay, cross-modal attention, cognitive load term), making it impossible to determine which design choices drive the performance gains.

#185 — Incremental (35)

Interpretable Fuzzy Inference Traces for Regulatory Compliance: Lukasiewicz Semantics in Neuralized Markov Logic Networks

researchsquare.com · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: The theoretical case for Lukasiewicz semantics over Gödel and product t-norms in differentiable neuro-symbolic inference is technically sound and well-argued. However, the empirical evaluation is critically weak: tiny toy datasets, no baseline comparisons whatsoever, and zero recall on the COLIEE benchmark reveals a large gap between theoretical design and practical capability. The central interpretability claim for legal professionals remains entirely unvalidated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: The EU AI Act (Articles 13–15) imposes binding transparency, oversight, and accuracy obligations on high-risk AI systems in legal, financial, and judicial domains. Prevailing responses rely on post-hoc explanation methods like LIME and SHAP, which suffer from faithfulness problems and instability. This paper argues that intrinsically interpretable models are needed instead, and proposes a neuro-symbolic framework grounded in Lukasiewicz fuzzy logic to provide auditable, by-design transparency for legal reasoning tasks. Methodology: The paper introduces LIMEN-AI (Lukasiewicz Interpretable Markov Engine for Neuralized AI), which extends Markov Logic Networks by replacing Boolean satisfiability with Lukasiewicz t-norm and implication operators: T_L(a,b) = max(0, a+b−1) and a→b = min(1, 1−a+b). These piecewise-linear operators yield differentiable energy manifolds E(I;w) = Σ w_i σ(φ_i, I) whose gradients guide gradient-based sampling (MALA/Langevin dynamics) for approximate inference. Every inference step produces a structured trace decomposing conclusions into per-rule activation degrees and weighted energy contributions. A small LLM (Qwen2.5-1.5B via Ollama) is integrated for natural language parsing to ground predicates from raw legal text. Human override is supported via a veto mechanism that pins atom truth degrees as hard constraints in the energy manifold. Weight learning uses gradient-based optimization of the energy manifold from labeled examples. Key Results: The paper reports four capability demonstrations on very small datasets. (1) Zero-shot schema induction: the system extracted 6 predicates from a 1,444-word service agreement and 8 predicates from an 892-word GDPR policy without manual ontology engineering. (2) Knowledge base evolution: across three sequential document ingestion rounds with human feedback, the KB grew from 0 to 18 predicates and 4 induced rules with no catastrophic forgetting. (3) Scalability: 220 words/second throughput on 2,500+ words of ISO 27001 text, with near-linear inference scaling to 100 query atoms in ~3 seconds on a consumer GPU. (4) Human override: a predicate truth degree was reduced from 0.9868 to 0.0172 via veto, while an unrelated predicate remained at 0.9852. No comparison to any baseline system is provided. Critically, attempts to evaluate on the COLIEE legal reasoning benchmark resulted in zero recall, revealing unimplemented query translation and retrieval mechanisms. Strengths: - The theoretical motivation for Lukasiewicz semantics is sound: piecewise linearity avoids gradient vanishing from Gödel t-norm and near-zero collapse from product t-norm, making it genuinely more suitable for differentiable neuro-symbolic inference than alternatives. - The regulatory mapping between LIMEN-AI's technical features and specific EU AI Act articles (13, 14, 15) is explicit and structurally coherent, and the paper honestly notes this is a design proposal rather than a certified compliance claim. - The open-source release (v0.2.5 on PyPI) and publicly available test suite enable independent reproducibility verification, which is unusual and commendable for a single-author preprint. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The empirical evaluation is extremely thin and self-serving: all reported experiments use 2–3 tiny documents (under 1,500 words each), the scalability plot has only four data points, and the veto validation is a single controlled experiment on one predicate. There is no comparison to any baseline—not BERT-Legal, BM25, GPT-4, LTN, PSL, or DeepProbLog. - The COLIEE benchmark evaluation produced zero recall, exposing a significant gap between the system's claimed capabilities and actual legal reasoning performance on a standard task; this failure is acknowledged but not adequately addressed. - The central interpretability claim—that Lukasiewicz inference traces are more meaningful to legal professionals than neural embeddings—is entirely unvalidated. No user study with legal experts is conducted, making the regulatory compliance arguments speculative. - The paper is a single-author preprint from relatively obscure institutions, not yet peer-reviewed, and the knowledge base used in the main case study (tort liability) is explicitly described as 'pedagogical' rather than derived from real legal practice.
Search Results
#186 — Incremental (35)

Symbolic wrappers enable precise human in the loop operation for large action models

link.springer.com · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: The symbolic wrapping concept for trapping action hallucinations before physical execution is a sound engineering principle, but the architecture assembles entirely off-the-shelf components without methodological novelty. The evaluation on 13 simple linear tasks by 3 co-author participants provides minimal external validity.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large Action Models (LAMs) aim to extend LLMs from text generation to physical task completion, but existing approaches face two critical problems: (1) massive end-to-end training requirements with quadratic inference complexity prohibitive for real-time control, and (2) "action hallucinations" — generation of invalid or physically unsafe actions — which unlike textual hallucinations can cause irreversible hardware damage. The core gap is that pure neural approaches lack interpretability and verifiability, while classic symbolic AI (PDDL planners) cannot handle open-world perception at scale. The paper asks whether competent LAM intelligence can be achieved by composing off-the-shelf models with symbolic verification, avoiding costly end-to-end training. Methodology: The authors propose a modular neuro-symbolic architecture implemented in ROS2 on a Universal Robots arm. The system has three tiers: (1) a High-Level Planner using LangChain to either directly generate structured tool-call sequences (neural-direct) or translate natural language into PDDL problem instances solved by Fast Downward (neuro-symbolic); (2) a Medium-Level Planner that maps subtasks to deterministic ROS2 API calls; and (3) a Low-Level Executor using MoveIt2 for inverse kinematics and collision avoidance. The key innovation is "symbolic wrapping": the LLM is never permitted to emit executable robot code directly — it produces only intermediate symbolic artifacts (task lists or PDDL) that are human-readable and subject to human-in-the-loop review before execution. The static PDDL domain definition (robot capabilities) is hard-coded deterministically; the LLM only generates the dynamic problem state. Perception uses SAM for segmentation, CLIP for zero-shot classification, and GraspNet-1Billion for 6-DoF grasp poses. Speech uses AssemblyAI ASR with a deterministic emergency-stop bypass that circumvents LLM reasoning entirely. Key Results: On 13 benchmark manipulation tasks (N=65 trials), the neural-direct approach achieved 100% task success at 7.20±0.25s per step with 0.96±0.032s planning latency. The neuro-symbolic PDDL approach achieved 91% success at 6.83±0.27s per step but 3.82±0.104s planning latency (growing ~3s per additional action vs ~0.1-0.2s for neural-direct). The 9% PDDL failure rate stemmed from logical hallucinations (impossible goal specifications in long sequential tasks), not syntactic errors — and critically, all failures were caught at the planning layer with no unsafe physical execution. Perception benchmarks on 25 objects: SAM segmentation AP@IoU≥0.5 = 0.741, CLIP top-1 accuracy = 0.850, GraspNet mean quality score = 0.746, scene understanding spatial accuracy = 0.980, pixel-to-real RMSE = 0.054m. ASR accuracy across 3 participants = 97.91% on 30 commands, with emergency-stop latency of 1.41±0.14s. Strengths: - The symbolic wrapping concept cleanly addresses action hallucination safety: all failures are trapped in software before physical motion, a genuinely useful safety property for deployment. - No new training required — the entire system assembles from off-the-shelf components (SAM, CLIP, GraspNet, LangChain, Fast Downward, MoveIt2), making the approach immediately practical and extensible. - The emergency-stop path is architecturally decoupled from LLM latency, providing a hard real-time safety guarantee that is correctly designed for human-robot collaboration. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is severely limited: 13 intentionally simple, short, linear tasks with no branching or conditional logic, evaluated by only 3 participants who are co-authors of the paper — constituting an obvious upper-bound bias with no external validity. - The PDDL domain is entirely static and hand-coded, meaning the system cannot generalize to new robot capabilities or object categories without manual domain engineering — directly contradicting the open-world perception capability provided by CLIP and SAM. - Perception latency (scene understanding alone averages 4.56s) makes the system unsuitable for dynamic environments or reactive manipulation, and the 0.054m pixel-to-real RMSE, while tolerated by the gripper compliance range, was tested only on large tools (>300mm), not precision tasks.

#187 — Incremental (34)

A multi-LLM explainable food recommendation system based on Deep Learning

inria.hal.science · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The multi-LLM selection idea and ontology-grounded conflict resolution are conceptually interesting, and the attention to African dietary contexts fills a genuine gap. However, the execution is weak: no actual base recommender is trained, the user study is underpowered (N=80), the LLM confidence-based selection formula lacks validation, and the 67% hallucination reduction claim has no rigorous ablation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Food recommender systems (FRS) increasingly guide dietary decisions in health-sensitive contexts—obesity, diabetes, chronic disease—yet their explanations remain opaque and poorly contextualized. Post-hoc XAI methods like SHAP and LIME produce numerical feature attributions that are static, domain-dependent, and unintuitive for non-experts. Prior LLM-based explanation systems compound this by relying on a single model with a single explanation style, introducing model-specific bias and offering no flexibility to adapt explanation type (contextual, contrastive, counterfactual, or simulation-based) to user needs. The paper also highlights a gap in cultural representation: existing ontologies and datasets underserve African and other non-Western dietary contexts. Methodology: The authors propose a modular, ontology-aware explainability overlay that sits on top of any existing deep learning FRS without retraining it. The overlay receives the base model's predicted preference score, user/item embeddings, and SHAP attribution vectors as inputs. It operates in five steps: (1) an LLM Comparator selects among GPT-4, Gemini, LLaMA-3, and Mistral-7B using a latency-confidence trade-off formula (LLM_selected = argmin_i(latency_i − λ·confidence_i), λ=0.6); (2) an Explanation Type Selector dynamically chooses among contextual, contrastive, counterfactual, or simulation-based explanation styles based on user context (e.g., dietary restrictions → counterfactual; long-term health plan → simulation); (3) a Conflict-Resolution module applies ontology consistency checks, SHAP-constrained validation, and cross-LLM majority agreement to down-rank hallucinated or inconsistent explanations; (4) a Prompt Composer builds structured prompts grounding the LLM in verified SHAP scores and ontology facts; and (5) an Explanation Ranker combines prediction score with explanation quality into a final score. Two ontologies are integrated: a User Ontology encoding health conditions, cultural dietary habits, religious restrictions, and language preference; and a Food Ontology extending the Food Explanation Ontology (FEO) with African staple foods, preparation types, ingredient-substitution graphs, and health effects. Evaluation used AllRecipesExtended (4M+ interactions, 300K+ recipes) and Food.com (1M+ interactions, 95K+ recipes), with 80 participants (20 per LLM) drawn from African, European, Middle Eastern, and North American backgrounds. Key Results: GPT-4 achieved the highest average clarity (4.7/5) and was best for simulation and contextual explanations. Gemini scored 4.5/5 and excelled at contrastive explanations. LLaMA-3 (4.2/5) was strongest for counterfactual ingredient-substitution tasks. Mistral-7B (3.9/5) had the lowest quality but the best latency (640–880 ms vs. GPT-4's 1200–1780 ms). User satisfaction followed a similar ranking: GPT-4 (4.6), Gemini (4.4), LLaMA-3 (4.1), Mistral-7B (3.8). Ontology-guided cultural contextualization improved perceived personalization by +0.4 to +0.7 Likert points. Compared to a single GPT-4 baseline, the multi-LLM system traded a marginal drop in average explanation score (4.4 vs. 4.6) for 32% lower latency (1050 ms vs. 1550 ms), a 68% reduction in failure rate (3.5% vs. 11%), higher flexibility, and lower API cost. Hallucinations were reduced by approximately 67% relative to an unconstrained LLM baseline via SHAP-constrained prompting and ontology validation. Strengths: - The decoupled overlay design is genuinely plug-and-play: it requires only a preference score and SHAP attributions from the base model, making it applicable to any existing FRS without retraining. - The multi-LLM selection strategy is practically motivated—different LLMs are empirically better at different explanation types, and the system exploits this specialization while improving latency and fault tolerance over single-LLM baselines. - Explicit attention to cultural inclusivity (African dietary contexts, multilingual user ontology) addresses a real and underserved gap in food recommendation research. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The user study (80 participants, 20 per LLM) is far too small and demographically narrow to support generalized claims; the paper acknowledges this but still draws conclusions from the data. - The base recommender model is treated as a black box providing hypothetical outputs—no actual DL model is trained or evaluated, and the recommender accuracy (RMSE, NDCG, precision) is never reported, making it impossible to assess whether prediction quality and explainability genuinely coexist. - The LLM selection formula (argmin latency − λ·confidence) and the fidelity/hallucination reduction claim of 67% lack rigorous ablation or statistical grounding; confidence scores for LLMs are notoriously miscalibrated and their use as a quality proxy is unvalidated. - Several reference mismatches and a duplicated bullet point in the ethics section indicate the manuscript was not carefully proofread before submission.
