#203 — Incremental (28)

The Workload–Router–Pool Architecture for LLM Inference Optimization

vllm-semantic-router.com · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Scores 28 as a vision paper that presents no new experiments and derives all quantitative claims from 21 self-authored prior publications. While the WRP coupling framework offers a useful conceptual lens, the contribution is taxonomic rather than technical, and the integrated gains remain speculative since compositional benefits are never validated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Production LLM inference is shaped by three variables—the workload mix, the routing strategy, and the GPU pool architecture—yet these are studied by largely separate communities that seldom interact. A routing policy tuned for one workload on one pool topology can be severely suboptimal for a different combination. The paper's central thesis ("WRP Coupling") is that Workload, Router, and Pool are coupled, not orthogonal, and that optimizing any single dimension in isolation leaves substantial efficiency on the table. The motivation is grounded in concrete prior measurements: the same GPU fleet varies 40× in energy efficiency depending on context window served (Workload × Pool), and co-designing the router's compression parameter with pool sizes yields 3.1–6.4% lower cost versus retrofitting compression onto a pre-existing fleet (Router × Pool). Methodology: This is a vision/roadmap paper, not an empirical contribution. The authors distill results from 21 prior vLLM Semantic Router publications into the Workload–Router–Pool (WRP) framework, a three-dimensional decomposition: Workload characterizes the request distribution (chat vs. agent, warm vs. cold, prefill-heavy vs. decode-heavy, and prompt-length CDF archetypes); Router determines dispatch policy (static semantic rules with 13 signal types, online bandit/feedback adaptation, and RL-based cascading); Pool specifies GPU topology (homogeneous vs. heterogeneous, disaggregated prefill/decode, KV-cache management). The paper maps prior publications onto a 3×3 WRP interaction matrix, identifies sparse cells (particularly Router × Pool and Pool × Router), and proposes 21 concrete research directions at the intersections, tiered by maturity into 6 engineering-ready and 15 research-grade opportunities. A capstone Opportunity 21 proposes a MAPE-K closed-loop meta-controller that jointly adapts all WRP knobs via composite session-outcome rewards. Key Results: No new experiments are presented. Quantitative claims are drawn from cited prior work: token-budget pool routing reduces GPU instances by 17–39% on production traces; FleetOpt achieves 6–82% GPU cost reduction vs. homogeneous fleets; two-pool routing provides ~2.5× better tokens-per-watt than homogeneous deployment while GPU generation upgrade (H100→B200) gives only ~1.7×; FastRouter achieves 98× latency reduction with sub-800 MB GPU footprint; Adaptive VLM Routing (AVR) projects 78% cost reduction staying within 2 percentage points of an all-large-model baseline; OATS improves NDCG@5 from 0.869 to 0.940 for tool selection; the 1/W law shows energy efficiency halves every time context window doubles due to KV-cache concurrency limits. Strengths: - Provides a genuinely unifying conceptual framework for a fragmented research area, with cross-dimensional observations (e.g., Observation 7 on the KV-cache sunk cost of mid-session model switching) that are novel even if not empirically validated here. - The 21 proposed opportunities are concretely specified with measurable baselines, comparison metrics, and explicit feasibility/risk assessments—unusually actionable for a vision paper. - The maturity tiering (6 engineering-ready vs. 15 research-grade) and the MAPE-K dependency graph (Figure 2) give readers a prioritized implementation roadmap grounded in prior measurements. - Safety, governance, and security are treated as first-class cross-cutting concerns integrated into the routing layer (hierarchical MLCommons safety classifiers, RBAC via bearer tokens, multi-turn cumulative risk scoring), not afterthoughts. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper presents no new experimental evidence; all quantitative claims derive from separately published prior work under different conditions, making the integrated WRP gains speculative and potentially non-compositional. - The fleet-wide cross-tenant data aggregation that underpins multiple opportunities (Opportunities 1, 4, 6, 7, 9) raises serious tenant isolation and privacy concerns that are not addressed—the same infrastructure that enables cross-session learning could leak sensitive usage patterns across organizational boundaries. - The proposed opportunities systematically assume the semantic router is the optimal enforcement point, but many mechanisms (token budget enforcement, tool catalog shaping, KV-cache retention) are already implemented or planned at the serving engine layer (vLLM, Sutradhara, Continuum), creating potential architectural conflicts and duplication not reconciled in the paper. - As a self-referential survey of the authors' own project (21 of 21 founding publications are self-authored), the framing necessarily centers vLLM-specific mechanisms and leaves under-examined how the WRP framework maps onto alternative serving stacks or multi-provider deployments beyond SIRP/MPExt proposals.

#204 — Incremental (28)

Integrating Chatbots into Virtual Reality with Generative AI: A Brief

scitepress.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: This narrative review offers a three-layer integration framework (Language, Embodiment, Interaction) for LLM-driven chatbots in VR, but the framework is entirely conceptual and untested. The synthesis is timely given post-LLM developments, yet the paper reports no new experiments and relies heavily on the first author's own prior work, limiting its contribution to an organizational scaffold over existing literature.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing VR applications suffer from interaction rigidity due to predefined dialogue trees, while standalone chatbot systems lack embodied presence and spatial context. Prior literature is fragmented — reviews either focus on VR-based learning/therapy without examining conversational intelligence, or address chatbots in isolation from immersive environments. Most existing surveys predate widespread LLM adoption. This paper aims to synthesize how LLM-driven conversational agents are embedded in VR, their impact on engagement and outcomes in education and healthcare, and the design/ethical challenges that arise. Methodology: The paper conducts a structured narrative review drawing from Scopus, Web of Science, IEEE Xplore, and Google Scholar. Inclusion criteria required studies to address chatbot integration within VR or immersive environments, report empirical findings or prototype implementations, and be published in peer-reviewed venues. Studies were analyzed qualitatively through thematic synthesis organized by application domain (education vs. healthcare), chatbot architecture (rule-based vs. LLM-based), and interaction characteristics. The paper's primary conceptual contribution is a three-layer integration framework: a Language Layer (LLM-driven dialogue), an Embodiment Layer (AI avatars with voice, gesture, spatial presence), and an Interaction Layer (dynamic coupling of dialogue and VR interactivity). A system architecture is also proposed describing the real-time pipeline from speech input through ASR → NLP → dialogue management → avatar animation → spatial audio. Key Results: Reviewed evidence consistently supports that LLM-based VR chatbots improve user engagement and learning outcomes compared to rule-based systems. In education: VR training was found 4× faster than classroom training (Bailenson 2018); studies by the first author (Cinar 2024; Cinar et al. 2024) reported statistically significant improvements in Python programming comprehension with AI-powered VR versus traditional methods; generative AI-based virtual assistants increased sense of presence and usability in anatomy education (Chheang et al. 2024). In healthcare: VR with AI companions showed clinically significant mental health improvements (Freeman et al. 2017); AI-enabled VR simulations significantly improved communication knowledge and self-efficacy in nursing students (Liaw et al. 2023); VR reduced pain intensity across diverse patient populations (Viderman et al. 2023); cognitive rehabilitation in elderly patients yielded improvements in global cognitive functioning and reduced depressive symptoms (Tortora et al. 2024). No new quantitative experiments are reported — all results are synthesized from cited works. Strengths: - The three-layer framework (Language, Embodiment, Interaction) is a useful conceptual scaffold that goes beyond mere description, offering a structured lens for system design and evaluation. - Coverage is broad and timely, synthesizing post-LLM literature across two domains where VR-chatbot integration has strong empirical grounding. - Practical challenges are treated substantively — latency, hallucination in safety-critical contexts, cognitive overload, privacy from fine-grained behavioral/spatial data, and the tension between conversational richness and real-time performance are all identified and discussed. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is primarily a narrative (not systematic) review with no meta-analysis, no PRISMA flow diagram, no explicit count of included studies, and no quantitative synthesis — making it difficult to assess coverage completeness or replication risk. - Heavy reliance on the first author's own prior work (Cinar 2024; Cinar et al. 2024) for the primary empirical claims about learning improvements creates a potential bias; independent replication of those results is not discussed. - The proposed three-layer framework and system architecture diagrams are conceptual and untested — no evaluation, user study, or prototype implementation is presented to validate their utility or correctness.

#205 — Incremental (28)

Empirical Comparison of Scalability Strategies in Density-based Clustering Algorithms for Large Datasets

cys.cic.ipn.mx · cluto - new results
Why this rank: This is a purely empirical comparison of existing density-based clustering algorithms under a unified framework, with no new methods or surprising findings — the results largely confirm expected theoretical behavior (parallelism is fast, comparison reduction is accurate but slow). The practical strategy-to-resource mapping is useful but the restriction to 2D synthetic data limits generalizability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Density-based clustering (rooted in DBSCAN) is attractive for discovering clusters of arbitrary shapes while filtering noise, but its O(n²) complexity makes scaling to large datasets a persistent challenge. Prior comparative studies either focused narrowly on parallel algorithms in distributed frameworks (MapReduce, Spark), listed algorithm characteristics without empirical comparison, or evaluated runtime and quality without isolating the effect of specific scalability strategies. No study had directly compared parallelism, sampling, and comparison-reduction strategies under a unified experimental framework — leaving practitioners without principled guidance on which strategy to choose given their computational resources and data size. Methodology: The paper evaluates five density-based clustering algorithms, each representing one or more scalability strategies: S-DBSCAN and K-DBSCAN (parallelism via random and KMeans++-based partitioning, respectively), Improved KMeans (sampling), KNN-BLOCK-DBSCAN (comparison reduction via block-based KNN structure), and RS-DBSCAN (hybrid: sampling + parallelism). All five were reimplemented in Python and run on the same hardware (dual Intel Xeon E5-2620, 256 GB RAM). Ten 2D synthetic datasets from the Tomas Barton benchmark repository (240–8,000 objects originally) were scaled 10×–50× via perturbation of existing points, yielding datasets up to 400,000 objects. Clustering quality was measured with NMI and ARI (requiring ground-truth labels); runtime excluded I/O. Parameters swept: number of cores {2, 5, 10, 15, 20} for parallel algorithms; sample fraction {10%, 20%, 30%, 40%, 50%} for sampling-based algorithms. Statistical significance was assessed via one-way ANOVA with post-hoc tests (α = 0.05). Key Results: Parallelism (S-DBSCAN, K-DBSCAN) delivers the best runtime-quality tradeoff: S-DBSCAN achieved the lowest runtime across all scales while maintaining NMI ≈ 0.6–0.7, statistically indistinguishable in quality from KNN-BLOCK-DBSCAN (p = 0.088). Parallel speedup is largest from 2→5 cores and diminishes beyond 10–15 cores. Sampling-based algorithms show high sensitivity to sample fraction: IKMEANS NMI drops near zero at 10% sampling and reaches ~0.6 only at 50%, at which point its runtime advantage largely disappears. RS-DBSCAN consistently outperforms IKMEANS in quality but at higher runtime cost (up to ~650 seconds at 50×/50% vs. ~30 seconds for IKMEANS). KNN-BLOCK-DBSCAN achieves the highest ARI values across all scales but is by far the slowest, exceeding 8,000 seconds and failing to complete on 50× datasets; its runtime disadvantage is statistically significant versus all others (p < 0.001). RS-DBSCAN's counterintuitive quality degradation with larger samples is attributed to redundant data diluting the representative structure extraction. Strengths: - Provides the first unified empirical framework explicitly isolating three scalability strategies (parallelism, sampling, comparison reduction) under identical hardware and dataset conditions, filling a clear gap in prior literature. - Rigorous experimental design with dataset scaling up to 50× (400,000 objects), multiple parameter sweeps per strategy, and ANOVA-based statistical validation of quality and runtime differences. - Practically actionable conclusions: the strategy-to-resource mapping (parallelism when cores available, sampling for approximate results, comparison reduction when quality is paramount and runtime is not) is directly usable by practitioners. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All datasets are 2D synthetic benchmarks; generalizability to high-dimensional real-world data (where density estimation degrades and distance metrics behave differently) is entirely untested and likely overstated. - The paper compares existing algorithms rather than proposing new methods, limiting its contribution to empirical analysis; the findings largely confirm expected theoretical behavior rather than surfacing surprising insights. - KNN-BLOCK-DBSCAN's inability to complete at 50× scale is treated as a finding rather than a flaw to address; no hybrid approach combining its quality advantages with tractable runtime is developed, leaving the most important open problem unresolved despite it being explicitly stated as future work.
Search Results
#206 — Incremental (27)

Hybrid Deep Learning and Knowledge Engineering Framework for Predicting Diabetes and Personalized Drug Administration Using HRV and Ontology

rjwave.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: Combining CNN, LSTM, and SVM into a cascade for HRV-based diabetes classification applies well-known components without architectural novelty. The ontology module encodes existing ADA guidelines and is evaluated circularly against those same guidelines. The critically small dataset (68 male inpatients, ~14 test samples) makes the reported metrics unreliable, and no comparison to prior HRV-based diabetes systems is provided.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Type 2 diabetes mellitus affects over 537 million adults globally, with diagnosis still relying primarily on invasive biochemical tests (HbA1c, fasting glucose). Heart rate variability (HRV) derived from ECG signals is a non-invasive autonomic biomarker linked to diabetes progression, but existing deep learning models lack clinical interpretability, while rule-based ontology systems lack predictive power. No prior work unified these two capabilities into a single end-to-end clinical decision support system covering both diagnosis and personalized pharmacotherapy. Methodology: The paper proposes ECGHybridPredictor-AI, a two-phase framework. Phase I trains a CNN–LSTM–SVM hybrid classifier on HRV features extracted from 24-hour ECG recordings (Mendeley T2DM dataset, 68 male inpatients, single-lead Holter at 250 Hz). Preprocessing uses NeuroKit2 for bandpass filtering (0.5–50 Hz), R-peak detection, and RR interval extraction; features include time-domain (SDNN, RMSSD, MeanNN), frequency-domain (LF, HF, LF/HF ratio), and nonlinear (SD1, SD2, entropy) HRV metrics. The 1D CNN extracts local morphological features, LSTM captures temporal dependencies, and an RBF-kernel SVM provides the final binary classification. Hyperparameters are tuned via Bayesian optimization and grid search; training uses Adam (lr=0.001, batch=64) with early stopping and 70/10/20 stratified splits. Phase II builds an OWL 2.0 ontology in Protégé 5.5.0 encoding ADA 2023 and WHO treatment guidelines via SWRL rules with Pellet/HermiT reasoners. The ontology filters contraindicated drugs based on comorbidity profiles (CKD, cardiovascular disease, hypoglycemia risk) and prioritizes drug classes (metformin first-line, SGLT2 inhibitors for nephropathy, GLP-1 RAs for high CV risk, insulin for severe hyperglycemia). Only Phase I predictions with confidence ≥0.85 feed into the automated ontology module. Key Results: On 68 ECG samples (test set ~14 samples), the CNN–LSTM–SVM achieves accuracy 94.2%, precision 93.5%, recall 92.8%, F1 93.1%, ROC AUC 0.96, statistically significantly outperforming CNN alone (88.3%, AUC=0.91) and CNN–LSTM (91.6%, AUC=0.94) with p=0.034 by paired t-test. Inference latency is 1.2 seconds per patient. The confusion matrix shows 1 false positive and 1 false negative out of 46 test samples. The ontology achieves >95% rule coverage across 100 ADA-compliant scenarios and filters out 26.3% of unsafe drug prescriptions across 30 simulated cases, with offline reasoning latency of ~280 ms. Strengths: - End-to-end integration of predictive modeling and semantic reasoning is a meaningful architectural contribution — bridging the gap between black-box accuracy and clinical interpretability in a single deployable system. - The three-stage CNN→LSTM→SVM pipeline is well-motivated: CNNs capture local HRV morphology, LSTMs model temporal autonomic dynamics, and SVM provides robust margin-based classification on the learned representation. - The ontology-based drug filtering that actively removes contraindicated prescriptions (26.3% reduction in unsafe recommendations) addresses a concrete patient safety concern rather than just generating recommendations. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The dataset is critically small (68 patients, all male inpatients) and demographically homogeneous, making the reported 94.2% accuracy almost certainly overfit to this population; the test set of ~14 samples is far too small to draw reliable conclusions about generalization. - The ontology evaluation is based entirely on synthetic ADA case scenarios, not real patient prescribing outcomes — no ground-truth treatment data exists, so the claim of >95% guideline compliance is circular (the ontology encodes the same guidelines it is tested against). - No comparison to any prior HRV-based diabetes prediction system is provided beyond the authors' own CNN and CNN–LSTM ablations, making it impossible to situate the contribution relative to the broader literature the paper cites.
Search Results
#207 — Incremental (27)

An Ontology -Based Architecture for Interoperable Healthcare Systems-of-Systems: Structure, Interaction Patterns, and Covenant-Based Governance

mdpi.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The Covenant class as a first-class ontological entity is a reasonable design choice, and the layered SWRL/SHACL/OWL validation architecture is technically sound. However, the work is standard ontology engineering applied to a well-studied domain, validated only on 14 hand-crafted instances with no empirical deployment or comparison against existing healthcare ontologies.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Healthcare delivery is structurally fragmented — hospitals, insurers, suppliers, and IT vendors operate independently, producing poor coordination, duplicated effort, and degraded outcomes. While the healthcare delivery system is inherently a System of Systems (SoS), existing modeling approaches (SysML, MBSE, digital twin frameworks) address structural or behavioral dimensions in isolation and lack the semantic rigor to connect governance principles — legal mandates, interoperability standards, regulatory constraints — with implementable, machine-verifiable architectures. Prior work by the same research group had established theoretical foundations (SoS classification, a three-tier governance model, an interaction taxonomy from 40+ cases, and the Confluence Interoperability Covenant), but no unifying formalism existed to make these artifacts computationally tractable and mutually consistent. Methodology: The paper develops a comprehensive OWL 2 DL ontology for healthcare SoS, constructed using the Methontology engineering methodology, implemented in Protégé 5.6.7, and validated with the HermiT reasoner. The ontology contains 35 classes, 42 object properties, 9 data properties, >280 logical axioms, and 3 SWRL validation rules. Seven primary constituent system classes are derived from functional decomposition rather than market-sector boundaries: ProviderSystem, CareRecipientSystem, InsuranceSystem, SupplierSystem, MedicalSupportServiceSystem, InfoComTechSystem, and Covenant. The interaction taxonomy distinguishes intra-category patterns (CollaborativePartnership, ResourcePooling, MarketNegotiationBlock) from inter-category patterns (ServiceDeliveryChannels, SupplyChainLinkages, TechnologyIntegration, StrategicCrossCategoryAlliances). The central innovation is the Covenant class — a first-class ontological entity embedding legal frameworks (HIPAA, GDPR), interoperability protocols (FHIR, HL7v2, HL7v3), and technical standards (SNOMED, LOINC, ICD-11, ISO 9001, ISO 13485, DICOM) as queryable objects linked to interaction properties via supportsInteraction. Governance enforcement is layered: SWRL rules for deductive compliance checking (e.g., consent validation before data sharing), SHACL shapes for structural constraint validation (e.g., ISO9001 required for equipment sharing), and OWL equivalentClass axioms for automated conflict detection (mutually exclusive covenants). The framework is further exercised via SPARQL queries for network topology: node degree computation, hub identification, and resource pooling network density. Key Results: Consistency checking via HermiT confirmed a fully satisfiable ontology with no unsatisfiable classes. Four validation scenarios demonstrate the framework's governance enforcement capabilities: (1) Scenario 1 shows automated consent and HIPAA compliance checking, with SPARQL retrieval of ConsentViolation instances when hasConsent is false; (2) Scenario 2 demonstrates SHACL detection of ISO9001 non-compliance in equipment-sharing relationships among hospitals; (3) Scenario 3 shows that a legacy EHR system lacking FHIR/HL7v2 support is automatically flagged with InteroperabilityViolation; (4) Scenario 4 shows that OWL equivalentClass axioms infer ConflictViolation when a hospital simultaneously complies with mutually exclusive covenants, and QualityManagementViolation when certifications are temporally expired. Extended SPARQL queries produce supplier compliance registries, population-level standard adoption counts (HIPAA: 10 entities, ISO13485: 4, FHIR: 2), standards coverage by system type, bidirectional relationship maps, interaction-to-covenant mappings, node degree rankings, hub entity lists, and hospital resource pooling density (Memorial and Community General Hospitals: 5 collaborations; Regional Medical Center: 4). No quantitative comparison against baseline ontology frameworks or empirical deployment in live healthcare settings is provided; all validation is proof-of-concept with 14 test individuals. Strengths: - The Covenant class as a first-class ontological entity with necessary and sufficient conditions is a principled design that shifts governance from external documentation to an intrinsic architectural element, enabling governance-as-structure rather than governance-as-policy. - The layered validation architecture combining SWRL (deductive reasoning), SHACL (structural constraints), and OWL DL axioms (conflict detection) addresses genuinely complementary compliance concerns that no single mechanism can handle, and is technically well-grounded. - The cumulative research program is coherent: each prior paper (structural typology, governance synthesis, interaction taxonomy, CIC) is explicitly operationalized in the ontology, giving the artifact a stronger theoretical foundation than typical one-off ontology proposals. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Validation is entirely demonstrative using only 14 hand-crafted instances; there is no empirical deployment, no stress test at realistic ABox scale, and no comparison against existing healthcare or SoS ontologies (GOPPRRE, OntoSoS.QM.Gov) on concrete metrics — making it impossible to assess practical effectiveness or representational adequacy. - The framework is deeply U.S.-centric (HHS mission structure, HIPAA, TEFCA, ONC) and the authors acknowledge that international application requires substantial adaptation, limiting generalizability claims; the paper also does not address how covenants would be maintained as regulations evolve in practice.
Search Results
#208 — Incremental (26)

CLARIFYING AND CONSOLIDATING GRAPH-BASED KNOWLEDGE REPRESENTATIONS IN LIS: A TERMINOLOGICAL FRAMEWORK

scielo.br · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: Fills a genuine gap by systematically comparing five graph-based representation terms within LIS via rigorous PRISMA-compliant SLR. The comparative framework is practically useful but entirely conceptual — no computational validation, no formal justification for key claims, and the corpus is heavily skewed toward ontology (64%) with only 1 study each for two of the five focal terms.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Library and Information Science (LIS) employs a diverse and often inconsistent vocabulary to describe graph-based knowledge representations — terms such as knowledge graph (KG), ontology, semantic network, semantic graph, and causality graph are used with substantial overlap and without clear definitional boundaries. This terminological fragmentation creates conceptual ambiguity that hinders cross-disciplinary communication, methodological consistency, and system interoperability. Prior reviews have addressed individual formalisms (e.g., KG surveys, semantic network reviews) but none has systematically compared the full landscape of graph-based representation terms within LIS, making a consolidated terminological framework both timely and necessary. Methodology: The authors conducted a Systematic Literature Review (SLR) following the Kitchenham (2004) protocol across three databases: Web of Science (WOS, n=156), Library, Information Science and Technology Abstracts (LISTA, n=265), and Brapci (n=53). The search string targeted title-field occurrences of the five focal terms combined with definition/concept terms. Inclusion criteria restricted the corpus to publications from 2019–2023, accessible full-text, in Portuguese, Spanish, or English, and at least 5 pages. After multi-stage PRISMA-compliant screening (deduplication, language filtering, scope exclusion), 288 of 474 initial records were retained for analysis. Qualitative content analysis coded each article across five dimensions: definition, structural characteristics, purpose, domain of application, and terminological overlap. A subset was double-coded for reliability. Key Results: Of the 288 included studies, 185 (64%) focused on ontology, 93 (32%) on knowledge graphs, 8 on semantic networks, and just 1 each on causality graphs and semantic graphs. KG publications grew markedly after 2022. Qualitatively, the analysis produced a comparative framework (Table 3) distinguishing the five constructs: (1) Ontologies — formal, axiomatized specifications of shared conceptualizations (Gruber 1993), grounded in OWL and upper-level models (BFO, UFO, DOLCE), used for semantic interoperability; (2) Knowledge Graphs — subject-predicate-object triple stores supporting AI applications (search, Q&A, recommendation), often depending on ontologies for schema; (3) Semantic Networks — informal node-link structures emphasizing conceptual co-occurrence, used in text mining and scientometrics; (4) Semantic Graphs — event-centric representations with embedded semantic depth, applied in narrative generation and data enrichment; (5) Causality Graphs — directed acyclic graphs modeling cause-effect dependencies, used in medicine and economics with closed-world or probabilistic assumptions. The study confirms (RQ1) that substantial terminological redundancy exists, especially between KG and ontologies, and (RQ2) that the constructs differ along dimensions of formality, modeling scope, temporal/causal expressiveness, and application domain — with causality graphs being the most distinctly bounded. Strengths: - Addresses a genuine and underserved gap: no prior SLR had systematically compared all five major graph-based representation terms simultaneously within LIS. - Rigorous PRISMA-compliant methodology with predefined inclusion/exclusion criteria, multi-database coverage, and double-coding for reliability, lending credibility to the qualitative synthesis. - The comparative framework (Table 3) is practically useful — it maps each construct along eight dimensions (definition, purpose, structure, semantics, applications, related concepts, temporal/causal capacity, assumptions) and provides a concrete guide for practitioners choosing among representational approaches. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The restriction to 2019–2023 and three LIS-specific databases excludes foundational CS and AI literature (e.g., ACM DL, Scopus, arXiv) where graph-based representations are extensively defined and applied; the resulting corpus is heavily skewed toward ontology (64%), which limits the depth of analysis for the other four terms. - The paper offers no empirical or computational validation of its framework — the analysis is purely conceptual/interpretive, and the practical distinctions drawn (e.g., between semantic networks and semantic graphs) remain underspecified and subjective, given that only 1 study each was retained for causality graphs and semantic graphs. - The framework's key conclusion — that all graph-based data representations can be categorized as KGs, with ontologies as a distinct formal layer — is stated without sufficient formal justification, conflating architectural flexibility with definitional equivalence.
Search Results
#209 — Incremental (25)

Extending Tabular Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models for Time-Series Data Generation

jstage.jst.go.jp · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Adding lightweight Conv1D temporal adapters to TabDDPM is a straightforward architectural extension. Evaluation on a single small dataset with only Random Forest classification, zero improvement over SMOTE on downstream accuracy, and short-range-only temporal modeling make this a standard incremental contribution with limited demonstrated practical value.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Human Activity Recognition (HAR) from wearable accelerometer sensors suffers from chronic data scarcity, class imbalance, and privacy constraints that limit training set diversity. While TabDDPM has proven effective for static tabular data synthesis, it assumes i.i.d. samples and completely ignores temporal dependencies — generating samples that may match marginal feature distributions but not the sequential dynamics of real sensor signals. Prior time-series diffusion models (TimeLDM, TimeAutoDiff) address temporality but are computationally expensive. The gap is a lightweight, temporally-aware diffusion framework suited for multivariate sensor sequences. Methodology: The paper proposes Temporal TabDDPM, which augments the original TabDDPM architecture with three additions: (1) a Context-Aware Embedding Block comprising a diffusion timestep embedding (t_emb), a conditional activity label embedding (c_emb), and a binary observed/missing mask M concatenated to the input latent; (2) Temporal Adapters — lightweight Conv1D modules with sinusoidal positional encodings inserted after the forward diffusion block, operating at O(T) cost vs. O(T²) for self-attention; and (3) the existing TabDDPM MLP denoiser, which now receives the temporally-enriched representation. Raw WISDM accelerometer data (20 Hz, triaxial, 6 activities) from 5 participants is segmented into overlapping windows of T=100 with 50-sample stride. The model is trained for 50 epochs with 1000 DDPM diffusion steps, cosine noise schedule, AdamW optimizer, and evaluated on downstream Random Forest classification alongside temporal fidelity metrics (bigram transition matrices, autocorrelation functions). Key Results: On the WISDM dataset, Temporal TabDDPM achieves accuracy 0.71 and macro F1 0.64, matching baseline and SMOTE and outperforming Original TabDDPM (accuracy 0.70, macro F1 0.60). The primary gains are in temporal fidelity: bigram transition matrices show the proposed method reproduces sharp, realistic step-by-step sensor transitions matching real data, while SMOTE and Original TabDDPM yield flat, unstructured patterns. ACF analysis confirms the model preserves short-range stochastic decay for dynamic activities (Upstairs) and near-zero autocorrelation for static activities (Sitting), whereas Original TabDDPM produces noise-like oscillations. For static minority classes, per-class precision improves substantially: Sitting rises from 0.88 (Original TabDDPM) to 0.99, Standing from 0.81 to 0.94. Dynamic minority classes (Downstairs, Upstairs) remain challenging across all methods with recall 11–16%. Strengths: - Lightweight design: Conv1D adapters add only O(T) overhead and avoid the quadratic cost of transformer-based alternatives, making the approach practical on modest hardware (Apple M2). - The observed/missing mask provides principled handling of irregular or incomplete sensor streams — a real-world concern largely ignored by prior tabular diffusion work. - Temporal quality metrics (bigram transitions, ACF) are more diagnostic than accuracy alone for evaluating generative fidelity of time-series, and the proposed model shows clear superiority on these measures. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is restricted to a single, small, lab-collected dataset (5 of 20 WISDM participants), severely limiting claims about generalizability to real-world or cross-device HAR scenarios. - Downstream classification is evaluated only with Random Forest, which cannot exploit temporal structure; the authors acknowledge that LSTM/CNN classifiers would better leverage the improved temporal coherence, but these experiments are deferred to future work — making the practical utility of the temporal improvements undemonstrated. - The Conv1D adapters capture only short-range local dependencies (kernel size 3); long-range temporal structure and activity transitions spanning multiple windows are not modeled, and the 100-sample window (~5 seconds) may be too short to capture many activity patterns. - The improvement over SMOTE on downstream classification is essentially zero (both 0.71/0.64), making the practical case for the added complexity of diffusion-based synthesis over simple interpolation weak for this task.
Search Results
#210 — Incremental (25)

Closed and Maximal Patterns on Dissimilarity-Based Graph Embedding for Supervised Classification

cys.cic.ipn.mx · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This paper provides a systematic but straightforward empirical comparison of closed and maximal pattern subsets for dissimilarity-based graph embedding. The differences are self-described as 'barely noticeable,' no statistical significance tests are reported, and evaluation is confined to four closely related molecular datasets with default hyperparameters.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Graph classification via machine learning requires converting graph-structured data into vector representations. Dissimilarity-based Graph Embedding (DGE) accomplishes this by computing, for each graph, a vector of dissimilarities to a set of mined frequent subgraph patterns. A fundamental problem is that the number of frequent patterns can be exponentially large, leading to high-dimensional, redundant feature spaces. Closed and maximal pattern subsets are well-known condensed representations that eliminate redundancy, but their impact on DGE classification quality had not been systematically evaluated. This paper fills that gap. Methodology: The pipeline mines all frequent graph patterns (both exact, δ=0, and approximate, δ∈{1,2}) from each class's training graphs using the REAFUM algorithm, which supports structural and label variations. A post-processing step then extracts closed patterns (no supergraph with equal support) and maximal patterns (no frequent supergraph) from the full pattern set. Each graph is embedded as a vector z = [d_min(G, p1), ..., d_min(G, p|P|)], where each entry is the minimum GED-based dissimilarity to all occurrences of a pattern within the graph. Experiments use four PTC benchmark collections (MM, MR, FM, FR; ~336–351 graphs each, binary carcinogenicity labels), six classifiers (KNN, Linear SVM, Decision Tree, Random Forest, Neural Networks, GBM), 5-fold cross-validation, and six combinations of dissimilarity threshold δ and support threshold σ. Performance is measured by accuracy and F1-score. Key Results: Closed and maximal patterns consistently yield slightly higher accuracy and F1-scores than the full set of frequent patterns across nearly all classifiers and parameter settings. The improvement is described as "barely noticeable" across classifiers (Fig. 1), but more visible when varying thresholds (Fig. 2). Approximate patterns (δ=2, σ=80%) substantially outperform exact patterns (δ=0), with maximal approximate patterns achieving the best overall results. Exact patterns (δ=0) produce the worst performance regardless of pattern type. Maximal patterns reduce pattern count most aggressively—e.g., for PTC-MM at δ=2, σ=80%: All=56.6, Closed=55.4, Maximal=41.6—with closed patterns providing modest reductions. The closed/maximal extraction step is negligible computationally (≤3 seconds), while pattern mining dominates total runtime (up to ~17,290 seconds for δ=2, σ=90%, vs. ~1,254 seconds for exact patterns). Decision Tree, Random Forest, and GBM achieve the highest accuracy and F1-scores; KNN and Linear SVM perform weakest. Strengths: - Clearly isolates the effect of pattern subset type (all vs. closed vs. maximal) within a controlled experimental framework, providing actionable guidance for practitioners using DGE. - Combines both exact and approximate pattern mining, revealing that approximate patterns are substantially more effective than exact ones—an important practical finding. - The post-processing approach for extracting closed/maximal subsets is computationally negligible, making it a free performance improvement over using all patterns. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is restricted to four closely related PTC graph collections (all molecular, all binary classification), making it unclear whether findings generalize to other domains (social networks, computer vision, bioinformatics) or multi-class settings. - The performance differences between all-frequent, closed, and maximal patterns are consistently described as "barely noticeable," raising questions about practical significance; no statistical significance tests are reported. - All classifiers use default scikit-learn hyperparameters, which may disadvantage certain models and conflate classifier sensitivity with pattern-type effects; hyperparameter tuning could meaningfully change conclusions.
Citations
#211 — Incremental (25)

Mitigating bias in student performance prediction in online learning through peer relative effort intensity feature

link.springer.com · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The peer-relative max-normalization idea is intuitive and well-motivated for surfacing distributed-effort students, but the proposed feature underperforms the simple cumulative baseline in 64 of 84 settings, with marginal gains (≤0.02 AUC-ROC) in favorable conditions. This is a straightforward feature engineering contribution evaluated with basic classifiers, representing an incremental extension rather than a methodological advance.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Student performance prediction in online learning relies heavily on behavioral log features (click counts, login frequency, etc.), while cognitive dimensions of engagement remain underrepresented. Existing intensity features are constructed either from subjective self-report surveys or by simply summing raw activity counts — both methods introduce underrepresentation bias: students who spread effort evenly across multiple activities are systematically undervalued relative to students who concentrate actions in a single high-count activity. The paper addresses this specific gap by proposing a peer-relative, normalization-based intensity feature that captures effort distribution across activities and time, rather than absolute volume. Methodology: The proposed feature, called Intensity Average Ratio (IA), is constructed from edX platform log data in four algorithmic steps. First, raw action counts are aggregated per student, per activity, per time point. Second, each student's count for each activity is divided by the maximum count observed for that activity across all students (max-normalization), producing a ratio in [0,1]. Third, the average ratio is computed across all K activities at each time point. Fourth, the cumulative average ratio is divided by the number of observed time points to yield a single intensity score per student. This score reflects how consistently and broadly a student engages relative to the top performer, penalizing single-activity concentration and rewarding distributed effort. The feature is appended to temporal activity-based features and evaluated under 84 experimental settings (3 learning phases × 3 feature paradigms × 3 classifiers × 2 value encodings × 2 datasets) using Naïve Bayes, Logistic Regression, and MLP in WEKA. Fairness is measured via Statistical Parity Difference (SPD). Expert interviews with three domain specialists provide qualitative validation. Key Results: The proposed IA feature improves over the classic cumulative-count baseline (CI) in 8 of 84 settings, while CI outperforms IA in 64 settings and the two tie in 12. The improvements are concentrated in middle and final learning phases with continuous values under aggregated paradigms. On Dataset 1 (120,542 KDD Cup 2015 students, 5-week course), IA achieves 86.3% accuracy (13.7% error) vs. CI's 86.0% (14% error), and AUC-ROC improves from 0.91 to 0.93 in the final phase under specific settings. On Dataset 2 (676 Stanford CAROL students, 31-month course), IA reaches 93.5% accuracy vs. CI's 92.9%, with recall improving to 0.94 vs. 0.93 under aggregated LR. Spearman correlations of IA with class label are 0.477 (Dataset 1) and 0.324 (Dataset 2), slightly below CI's 0.481 and 0.537, but paired t-tests confirm IA yields statistically significant improvements (Dataset 1: mean difference 67.67, Cohen's d=0.48; Dataset 2: mean difference 59.63, Cohen's d=1.15). SPD is unchanged by the proposed method (−0.57 for Dataset 1, 1.00 for Dataset 2), meaning group-level parity is preserved while individual-level recognition of distributed-effort students improves. Strengths: - The peer-relative normalization idea is well-motivated: max-normalization explicitly surfaces students who approach the cohort's best effort level across multiple activities, a group invisible to raw-count features. - The multi-dimensional experimental grid (84 settings per dataset, two datasets with very different scales and durations) provides honest coverage of when the feature helps and when it does not, rather than cherry-picking favorable conditions. - Statistical validation is thorough: paired t-tests with bootstrapping, Spearman correlations between IA and individual activities (ρ=0.12–0.38), and SPD fairness measurement together provide a credible picture of the feature's complementary but non-redundant information. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The proposed method underperforms the simple cumulative baseline in the majority of settings (64/84), and the claimed gains are incremental (≤0.02 AUC-ROC, ≤0.01 accuracy), making the practical significance marginal for most deployment scenarios. - Max-normalization is sensitive to outliers: a single student with pathologically high activity in one week sets the reference maximum, potentially compressing all other students' ratios toward zero and introducing its own form of distortion — this fragility is not analyzed. - The study is limited to two edX datasets from a single platform and three simple classifiers (NB, LR, MLP with default hyperparameters); tree-based or ensemble models, which are standard in educational data mining, are excluded, and generalizability to other LMS platforms or course types is unestablished.
Citations
#212 — Incremental (25)

Mapping a System of Systems Core Ontology to a Foundational Ontology

scitepress.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: This is a standard ontology alignment exercise mapping seven SoS concepts to BFO superclasses. While the surfaced ambiguities (Capability vs. Function, dual nature of Mission) are mildly interesting, the work is purely descriptive with no formal validation, no empirical evaluation, and no demonstration that the mapping improves any concrete workflow.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Systems of Systems (SoS) engineering involves coordinating independent constituent systems to achieve emergent capabilities none can accomplish alone. This complexity demands precise, shared knowledge representation to support semantic interoperability across stakeholders and tools. A previously developed core ontology for missions and capabilities in SoS (seven concepts: system of systems, system, constellation, mission, mission thread, capability, capability configuration) lacked explicit grounding in a recognized foundational ontology (FO), limiting its philosophical credibility, interoperability, and acceptance in the ontology community. The gap: core ontologies need to demonstrate connectedness to established FOs to achieve modularity, reusability, and formal precision. Methodology: The paper performs a manual, bottom-up superclass-subclass mapping of the SoS core ontology to a selected FO. The first step is FO selection: three candidates — Basic Formal Ontology (BFO), Descriptive Ontology for Linguistic and Cognitive Engineering (DOLCE), and Unified Formal Ontology (UFO) — are compared across philosophy, axiomization, language, expressiveness, and practical acceptability. BFO is selected for its Aristotelian scientific realism, ISO 21838 conformance, OWL implementation, and broad adoption (200+ ontologies). The mapping then proceeds concept-by-concept: System → BFO Independent Continuant (IC) Object; System of Systems and Constellation → IC Object Aggregate; Capability → BFO Specifically Dependent Continuant (SC) Disposition (via Realizable Entity); Mission → BFO Occurrent Planned Process (via OBI extension); Mission Thread → Generically Dependent Continuant (GC) Plan Specification (via IAO); Capability Configuration → GC Information Content Entity Design Specification (via IAO/IOF). The mapping extends beyond base BFO to include Common Core Ontologies (CCO) extensions — particularly the Information Artifact Ontology (IAO) and the Industrial Ontology Foundry (IOF) core ontology — to achieve maximum granularity for intangible concepts. Key Results: All seven core ontology concepts are successfully mapped to BFO or its recognized extensions. Tangible concepts (System, SoS, Constellation) map cleanly to BFO material entity hierarchy (Object, Object Aggregate). Intangible concepts (Capability, Mission, Mission Thread, Capability Configuration) require BFO extensions: Disposition for Capability, Planned Process for Mission, and Information Content Entity subclasses for Mission Thread and Capability Configuration. The mapping surfaces non-trivial conceptual insights: (1) Constellation and SoS share the same BFO type (Object Aggregate) despite distinct roles in the ontology; (2) Capability maps to Disposition rather than Function, since not all capabilities have cause-effect physical consequences; (3) Mission encapsulates both occurrent (process) and continuant (plan specification) aspects, suggesting the core ontology definition needs refinement. No automated validation (e.g., OWL reasoners for consistency checking) is performed; validation is limited to expert judgment. No quantitative evaluation metrics are reported. Strengths: - Principled FO selection process with explicit criteria (theoretical, technical, practical) and structured comparison of BFO, DOLCE, and UFO — not an arbitrary choice - Mapping goes beyond base BFO to CCO/IAO/IOF extensions, achieving finer-grained alignment for informationally complex concepts (Mission Thread, Capability Configuration) that base BFO cannot adequately capture - Discussion section surfaces genuinely interesting conceptual ambiguities (e.g., Capability vs. Function, dual nature of Mission as both process and plan) that have implications for ontology refinement and MBSE tool design Weaknesses / Limitations: - No formal validation: the authors explicitly exclude reasoner-based consistency and coherency checking, leaving the correctness of mappings unverified beyond expert opinion — a significant gap for a paper about ontological rigor - Constellation and SoS map to the same BFO type (Object Aggregate) with no formal mechanism to distinguish them, weakening the mapping's discriminative power and raising questions about whether the core ontology's conceptual distinctions survive the mapping - The paper is largely descriptive and conceptual with no empirical evaluation, case study application, or demonstration that the mapping improves any concrete systems engineering or MBSE workflow outcome
Search Results
#213 — Incremental (24)

Self-evolving industrial data space: a knowledge-augmented framework for adaptive production in aerospace manufacturing

link.springer.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: A survey-cum-position paper that synthesizes existing dataspace architectures (IDSA RAM, FIWARE) and applies standard STRIDE threat modeling to the energy domain. The lack of any quantitative evaluation, formal specification, or prototype implementation, combined with the conflation of survey, position paper, and case study genres, places this firmly in incremental territory.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: The energy supply chain involves multiple stakeholders—Transmission System Operators (TSOs), Distribution System Operators (DSOs), customers, and third-party service providers—who must share sensitive operational and infrastructure data across organizational and national boundaries. Existing data exchange methods lack robust privacy guarantees, data sovereignty controls, and standardized legal frameworks for multi-party collaboration. The European Common Dataspaces initiative provides a strategic response, but the concept remains ill-defined and its application to the energy sector's specific technical and regulatory requirements is underexplored. This paper targets that gap by tailoring the dataspace paradigm to energy supply chain contexts and demonstrating its security implications. Methodology: The paper proceeds in three parts. First, it synthesizes competing definitions of dataspaces from GAIA-X, Open DEI, and the European Commission into a unified characterization emphasizing data sovereignty, federated identity management, policy enforcement, and interoperability via connectors deployed on Kubernetes/Helm-based infrastructure. Second, it surveys European dataspace initiatives relevant to the energy sector, including the IDSA Reference Architecture Model (RAM)—a five-layer framework (Business, Functional, Information, Process, System) centered on connectors, certificates, and governance—and FIWARE's open-source Generic Enablers and Smart Data Models aligned with the IEC 61970 Common Information Model (CIM). Third, it presents a cross-border energy data sharing case study from the EU-funded CyberSEAS project, applying STRIDE threat modeling to a Data Flow Diagram (DFD) that captures trust boundaries among TSOs, DSOs, customers, and external service providers. Assets are mapped to STRIDE threat categories (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege), and a dynamic policy enforcement loop is proposed wherein connectors continuously ingest real-time interaction data and external threat intelligence to adaptively update and enforce security policies. Key Results: The paper does not report quantitative experimental results—it is a design/survey/case-study paper. The primary outputs are: (1) a structured architecture for energy dataspaces integrating IDSA RAM connectors, FIWARE CIM-aligned smart data models, a certification authority, data broker, clearing house, and vocabulary provider; (2) a comprehensive STRIDE threat mapping covering 14 asset classes in the energy dataspace (e.g., SCADA systems face Information Disclosure and DoS; IoT devices face Elevation of Privilege and Information Disclosure); (3) a dynamic policy enforcement loop for dataspace connectors that iteratively updates STRIDE-based threat models and enforces controls such as multi-factor authentication, attribute-based access control (ABAC), and end-to-end encryption. The case study, grounded in actual deployment experience with multiple national utility operators under CyberSEAS, demonstrates that the framework simultaneously improves operational efficiency and consumer privacy protection, including for critical billing services. Strengths: - Practical grounding in a real EU-funded multi-stakeholder deployment (CyberSEAS), lending credibility to the architectural claims beyond purely theoretical analysis. - Comprehensive STRIDE threat mapping across the full energy dataspace asset inventory, providing a reusable threat model for practitioners building similar systems. - Clear identification of the CIM/FIWARE integration as the vocabulary layer that enables machine-to-machine interoperability—a concrete and actionable technical recommendation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - No quantitative evaluation whatsoever: no performance benchmarks, security metrics, overhead measurements, or comparison against alternative privacy-preserving approaches (e.g., blockchain-based, federated learning), making it impossible to assess practical viability. - The STRIDE analysis and dynamic policy enforcement loop remain at a high conceptual level—the paper describes the loop's structure but provides no formal specification, prototype implementation, or empirical validation of its effectiveness against actual attacks. - The paper conflates survey, position paper, and case study genres without fully delivering on any one of them; the 'case study' lacks concrete deployment metrics, before/after comparisons, or reproducible details that would allow others to replicate or build on the work.

#214 — Incremental (22)

Ontology-Based Framework for Real Estate Investment Recommendation through Expert Knowledge and Market Data

scitepress.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: This is a standard application of well-known techniques (EFA, K-Means, ontology reasoning) to a specific market. The complete absence of standard recommendation metrics, no comparison against any ML baseline, and tight coupling to a single regional platform make the contribution primarily an engineering exercise rather than a research advance.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vietnamese real estate platforms connect buyers and sellers but lack personalization — they rely on predefined search filters and fail to capture complex interdependencies among decision factors (location, financial conditions, property features, living environment). The paper addresses the gap between purely data-driven recommenders (which ignore semantic relationships and lack interpretability) and pure expert knowledge systems (which lack market grounding), targeting informed, explainable investment recommendations. Methodology: The authors propose REstInves-model, built on the Rela-model relational ontology framework. The system has three main components: (1) Customer segmentation — a Likert-scale survey of real estate buyers is analyzed via Cronbach's Alpha and Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA) to identify four reliable factor groups (Location, Environment, Financial, Features), then K-Means clustering (K=6, selected by Silhouette Score and Elbow Method) segments customers into profiles such as "Young Singles with Savings" or "Wealthy Families"; (2) Ontology construction — the knowledge base K = (Cons, Rela, Ruls) ⊕ MarketData formalizes concepts (DEMOGRAPHICS, ECONOMY, PROJECT, LOCATIONS), hierarchical and semantic relations, and two rule sets: RulsCus for customer classification and RulsREst for property matching, the latter encoding survey-derived attribute weights (e.g., utility factor 87.18%, investment potential 82.63% for a specific customer group); (3) Recommendation pipeline — given a user query, inference rules assign the customer to a group, identify matching real estate attribute profiles above a scoring threshold, then query a MarketData database (7,113 listings from Nhatot platform, 63.4% in Ho Chi Minh City) to return ranked property suggestions with explanations. A chatbot interface handles natural language interaction using intent classification (11 intents, 420 samples, 5-fold cross-validation). Key Results: The entity/intent recognition component achieves Accuracy 89.8%, Precision 80.6%, and F1-score 79.0% on the chatbot classification task. Under location-search conditions, the system produces results comparable to the Nhatot commercial platform while additionally providing rule-based explanations for each recommendation. No head-to-head comparison against collaborative filtering, content-based filtering, or deep learning recommenders is reported. No offline recommendation quality metrics (precision@k, NDCG, MRR) are provided for the recommendation component itself. Strengths: - The hybrid ontology + market data architecture produces interpretable, explainable recommendations — each suggestion is backed by traceable inference rules, addressing a genuine gap versus black-box ML models. - The customer segmentation pipeline is methodologically sound: EFA with Cronbach's Alpha validation (all factors ≥ 0.804) and Silhouette/Elbow-based cluster selection provide principled grounding for the rule bases. - The system handles incomplete/ambiguous customer data through non-deterministic inference rules, a practical necessity for real-world deployment. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is severely underspecified: recommendation quality is assessed only qualitatively ("comparable to Nhatot") with no standard IR metrics (precision@k, recall, NDCG), no user study, and no ablation comparing the ontology contribution against a pure ML baseline. - The knowledge base rules (RulsREst) are derived from a single survey of undisclosed size and demographic scope; the number of survey respondents, their representativeness, and how the numeric weights (e.g., 87.18%) were derived from survey responses are never explained. - Scalability and generalizability are unaddressed: the system is tightly coupled to the Vietnamese market and the Nhatot/Batdongsan platforms, and there is no discussion of how the manually encoded ontology and rule sets would be maintained or extended as market conditions or customer behavior evolve.
Search Results
#215 — Incremental (22)

Sparse Tensor Contraction: A Comparative Benchmarking Study

mediatum.ub.tum.de · 7 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: A benchmarking thesis comparing known sparse tensor formats (COO, CSF, HiCOO) against dense baselines with no algorithmic novelty. The break-even density tables across contraction topologies provide some practical value, but non-standard implementations and single-core small-scale experiments limit the significance of the findings.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Dense tensor representations store all elements including zeros, wasting memory and compute when data is highly sparse — a common scenario in scientific computing, quantum chemistry, and machine learning. Three sparse tensor formats (COO, CSF, HiCOO) have been proposed to exploit sparsity, but there is limited systematic, format-neutral benchmarking of these formats specifically for the tensor contraction operation under controlled, identical conditions. This thesis fills that gap by conducting a reproducible empirical comparison of all three formats against the Eigen dense baseline across varying sparsity levels and contraction topologies. Methodology: The thesis implements custom C++ kernels for COO (hash-map based with FNV-1a), CSF (three-phase deferred construction via std::map), and HiCOO (block-partitioned dual-coordinate system) and integrates them into a unified benchmarking framework derived from the TCCG infrastructure. All formats are compiled under identical conditions (-O3, C++17, OpenMP, march=native) on a single Intel Core i5-1135G7. Benchmarks sweep 10 sparsity levels (1%–10% nonzero density) across 8 contraction patterns ranging from 2-mode to 6-mode tensors (e.g., ab-ac-cb, abcdef-dega-gfbc), with base dimension size 12. Performance is reported as GFLOPS using a dense FLOP count model, peak memory via getrusage RSS, and estimated Arithmetic Intensity (FLOPS/byte, using RSS as an upper-bound proxy for memory traffic). Each test runs 5 times; the minimum time is used. COO departs from the standard array-of-arrays representation, instead using an std::unordered_map keyed on index tuples — eliminating coordinate scanning at the cost of hash overhead. The CSF result tensor is constructed via an intermediate std::map accumulation followed by sorting, costing approximately 3× peak memory versus a streaming construction. Key Results: Sparse formats outperform Eigen only at extreme sparsity, and the crossover density depends heavily on contraction structure. COO is the most tolerant format, remaining competitive up to ~5–10% nonzero density for asymmetric patterns (e.g., abc-bda-dc: break-even at d≈0.10; abcde-efbad-cf: d≈0.08), while CSF and HiCOO cross Eigen much earlier (d≈0.03–0.04). For symmetric high-dimensional patterns (abcd-aebf-dfce), all sparse formats lose to Eigen by d≈0.01–0.02. At 1% density, COO achieves up to 106 GFLOPS (abcdef-dega-gfbc) versus Eigen's 5.95 GFLOPS — a ~18× advantage — but this collapses rapidly as density increases. Memory-wise, sparse formats consume ~4 MB at small scales versus Eigen's ~36 MB, maintaining a decisive advantage; at large scale (6-tensor case), sparse format memory grows linearly with nonzeros and begins converging toward Eigen's ~60 MB. Arithmetic Intensity at 1% density reaches 10³–10⁴ FLOPs/byte for sparse formats, indicating compute-bound behavior; this collapses to the low hundreds by 10% density while Eigen stays flat at ~40–540 FLOPs/byte. Strengths: - Reproducible, format-neutral benchmarking framework with controlled sparsity injection, fixed random seeds, and identical compilation flags across all formats — performance differences cleanly reflect format properties. - Comprehensive sweep across 8 contraction topologies (2–6 mode tensors drawn from quantum chemistry: CCSD, CCSD(T), AO2MO, and tensor networks), providing actionable break-even density tables for practical format selection. - Arithmetic Intensity analysis adds a second-order lens beyond raw GFLOPS and memory, revealing the compute-vs-memory-bound transition as density increases. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The COO implementation uses an unordered_map with tuple keys rather than the canonical sorted array-of-arrays representation, and CSF uses a deferred three-phase construction (3× peak memory overhead) rather than streaming insertion — these implementation choices skew the comparison away from production-quality baselines like SPLATT or TACO. - Benchmarks are conducted only on a single low-power mobile CPU (i5-1135G7, single-core, base dim=12), with no multi-core, GPU, or larger tensor experiments; generalizability to HPC or deep learning workloads is limited. - The Arithmetic Intensity metric uses peak RSS as a proxy for memory traffic, which systematically underestimates true data movement (no cache-miss profiling), rendering AI values upper-bound approximations of questionable precision.
Citations
#216 — Incremental (21)

FoodNexus: Massive Food Knowledge for Recommender Systems

link.springer.com · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The Food Product Ontology is a straightforward vocabulary contribution extending GoodRelations with food-specific classes. The E-number enumeration and CODEX Alimentarius grounding are practical but the approach is standard ontology development with no formal evaluation and a shallow nutritional model limited to four macronutrients.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: The paper addresses the lack of a common, standardized semantic vocabulary for describing food products on the Web. As manufacturers, retailers, governments, and institutions increasingly publish structured data online (RDFa, Microformats, Linked Data), interoperability requires shared ontologies. Existing food ontologies either focused on recipes, diabetes management, or general food knowledge — none provided a practical, e-commerce-ready vocabulary covering nutritional data, ingredients, food additives, and product categories in a single, reusable framework. Methodology: The authors build the Food Product Ontology (FPO) as an OWL extension of the GoodRelations e-commerce ontology, inheriting its Agent-Promise-Object model (BusinessEntity, Offering, ProductOrService, Location). New classes introduced include Food (subclass of gr:ProductOrService), Ingredient, FoodAdditive, and EAdditive — the last being an enumeration of 516 standardized E-numbers, each with a persistent URI. Nutritional attributes (carbohydrates, energy, fat, proteins per 100g) are represented both as simple datatype properties for ease of use and as object properties pointing to QuantitativeValueFloat instances for richer, range-aware descriptions. Food product categories are mapped from the CODEX Alimentarius system (16 top categories, 300+ subcategories, depth ≤4). The ontology is published at purl.org/foodontology# and evaluated via three SPARQL query examples demonstrating seller discovery, price retrieval, and additive filtering. Key Results: No quantitative benchmarks or user studies are reported. The paper demonstrates expressiveness through three SPARQL competency queries: (1) finding sellers of dried fruits with web links, (2) retrieving dairy-based drink offerings with prices and currencies, and (3) filtering bread/bakery sellers that carry no E-additives. The ontology is deployed in production in the MneMojno mobile application, backed by an RDF store containing over 500,000 triples crawled, refined, and manually reviewed. The ontology is described as moderate in size, flexible, and compatible with mainstream Semantic Web infrastructure. Strengths: - Practical reuse of GoodRelations avoids reinventing e-commerce semantics and ensures compatibility with search engines that already consume GoodRelations markup. - Inclusion of all 516 E-numbers as enumerated, URI-identified individuals enables precise, machine-readable additive queries (e.g., filtering products by specific additives), which no prior food ontology addressed. - Grounding food categories in the internationally recognized CODEX Alimentarius standard provides a principled, authoritative taxonomy rather than an ad hoc one. Weaknesses / Limitations: - No formal evaluation: there are no precision/recall measurements, no user studies, no comparison against alternative ontologies on coverage or query performance — the "evaluation" section is purely illustrative SPARQL examples. - Nutritional model is shallow: only four macronutrients (carbohydrates, fat, protein, energy) are covered; saturated fats, vitamins, minerals, cholesterol, and allergens are absent, significantly limiting real-world applicability for health or regulatory use cases.
